## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
Define the term "manufacturing process". Give two examples of a primary shaping process and two examples of a secondary shaping process.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *manufacturing process* is a controlled operation that transforms a raw workpiece into a finished part by altering its geometry, properties, or surface, using a defined energy source and tooling.

*Primary shaping examples* (1 mark each, any two):
- Sand casting — converts molten metal to a solid near-net shape.
- Hot forging — plastically deforms a solid billet into a near-net shape.
- Injection moulding — converts polymer melt to a solid net shape.

*Secondary shaping examples* (1 mark each, any two):
- Turning — removes material on a lathe using a single-point tool.
- Milling — removes material using a multi-point rotating cutter.
- Grinding — removes a thin layer using an abrasive wheel for high accuracy.

> A clean definition (1 mark) + two primary examples (1 mark) + two secondary examples (1 mark) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
Distinguish between *hot working* and *cold working* of metals. State the recrystallisation temperature criterion in Kelvin.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallisation $T_r$ | Below $T_r$ (usually room temp) |
| Force required | Low | High |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low (oxidation, scale) | High |
| Surface finish | Poor (scale) | Excellent |
| Strain hardening | Absent (dynamic recovery) | Present (work hardening) |

The recrystallisation criterion (1 mark):

$$
T_{\text{hot}} \geq T_r \approx 0.4 \cdot T_m \quad (\text{in Kelvin})
$$

Where $T_m$ is the absolute melting point in Kelvin. (For mild steel, $T_r \approx 723$ K; for aluminium, $T_r \approx 423$ K.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice *within* sub-parts. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify manufacturing processes based on (i) the *state of the material*, and (ii) the *nature of the operation*. For each category, list two example processes and state one characteristic feature.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Classification based on state of material (3.5 marks):**

1. **Solid-state processing** — material remains solid throughout.
   - *Example:* Forging, rolling, extrusion.
   - *Feature:* Mass is conserved; no melting.
2. **Liquid-state processing** — material is melted and resolidified.
   - *Example:* Sand casting, die casting.
   - *Feature:* Mass is not conserved; risers/sprues add waste.
3. **Gaseous-state processing** — material deposited from vapour phase.
   - *Example:* PVD, CVD, laser sintering.
   - *Feature:* Sub-micron thickness control possible.

**(ii) Classification based on nature of operation (3.5 marks):**

1. **Primary shaping** — converts raw stock to near-net shape.
   - *Example:* Casting, forging.
   - *Feature:* Major geometric change.
2. **Secondary shaping (machining)** — removes material for accuracy.
   - *Example:* Turning, milling.
   - *Feature:* Generates chips; high accuracy.
3. **Joining** — unites separate parts.
   - *Example:* Welding, riveting.
   - *Feature:* Creates permanent assembly.
4. **Surface finishing** — alters only the surface layer.
   - *Example:* Plating, anodising.
   - *Feature:* No bulk geometry change.

> Award **0.5 mark per correctly identified category** + **0.5 mark per example** + **0.5 mark per characteristic feature**. A neat table is recommended.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A flange bracket is to be made in mild steel, in batches of 5,000 per year. Discuss the suitability of *sand casting*, *hot forging*, and *CNC machining* for this part. Recommend the most suitable process with justification using DFMA principles.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Material and volume analysis (1.5 marks):**
Mild steel is amenable to casting, forging, and machining. Volume $N = 5{,}000$/year places this in the *batch production* range. Processes requiring very high tooling investment (e.g., die casting) are *not* economically viable here.

**Step 2 — Geometry and load analysis (1.5 marks):**
A flange bracket is a *load-bearing* part. The *grain-flow continuity* provided by forging is highly desirable for fatigue life. Sand casting produces random grain structure with possible shrinkage porosity. Machining from a bar stock has no porosity, but cuts the grain flow transversely.

**Step 3 — Tolerance and finish (1.5 marks):**
A flange bracket typically requires $\pm 0.2$ mm tolerance on mating faces. Sand casting ($\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 1.0$ mm) cannot meet this; CNC machining ($\pm 0.01$ mm) easily exceeds it; hot forging ($\pm 0.2$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm) is on the edge, but a small finish-machining allowance of 1–2 mm on the mating face solves this.

**Step 4 — Cost analysis (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{tooling, per-part, forging}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹40
$$

$$
C_{\text{tooling, per-part, casting}} = \frac{15{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹3
$$

$$
C_{\text{process, CNC}} = 8 \text{ min} \times ₹5/\text{min} = ₹40 \text{ (no tooling, but high per-part time)}
$$

**Step 5 — DFMA recommendation (1 mark):**
**Hot forging + finish machining on mating face** is the recommended process. It gives (a) grain-flow continuity for strength, (b) acceptable tooling cost at $N=5{,}000$, (c) the required tolerance, and (d) a *near-net-shape* output that minimises machining waste — a core DFMA principle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Many students recommend *sand casting* purely because it has the lowest tooling cost per part. This is wrong. Sand casting is suitable for $N \leq 1{,}000$. At $N=5{,}000$, the *fatigue-life penalty* of cast microstructure outweighs the tooling saving. **Always tie the recommendation to a *DFMA principle*, not just a cost number.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]* (Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the classification of manufacturing processes into *conventional* and *non-conventional* types. Give four examples of each, with the primary energy source and one typical application.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Conventional processes (3.5 marks):**
Use *direct mechanical contact* between tool and workpiece; tool is harder than workpiece. Primary energy is *mechanical*.

| # | Process | Energy Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turning | Mechanical | Shafts, pins |
| 2 | Milling | Mechanical | Slots, pockets |
| 3 | Drilling | Mechanical | Bolt holes |
| 4 | Sawing | Mechanical | Cut-off bar stock |

**Non-conventional processes (3.5 marks):**
Use *non-mechanical* energy — electrical, chemical, thermal, or abrasive jet. Tool is *not* in direct contact or is much softer than the workpiece.

| # | Process | Energy Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining) | Electrical (spark) | Hardened die cavities |
| 2 | ECM (Electrochemical Machining) | Electrochemical | Thin-walled aerospace parts |
| 3 | LBM (Laser Beam Machining) | Thermal (laser) | Micro-drilling, scribing |
| 4 | USM (Ultrasonic Machining) | Mechanical (abrasive + ultrasonic) | Hard, brittle ceramics |

> Award **0.5 mark per correct example** + **0.5 mark per correct energy source** + **0.5 mark per correct application**.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the *near-net-shape* (NNS) concept. Discuss why a DFMA-driven design prefers NNS processes over fully machining-from-stock. Illustrate with one example.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A *near-net-shape* (NNS) process produces a part whose geometry is *close* to the final required shape, but a small amount of secondary machining (typically 1–3 mm of stock) is still required on critical surfaces. A *net-shape* process requires *no* further machining.

**Sketch (2 marks) — describe in words since this is text-based:**
A forged crankshaft shows: (i) the rough forged profile with draft, flash line, and parting line, (ii) the finished profile overlaid in dotted line, and (iii) shaded regions representing 1–2 mm machining allowance on the bearing journals.

**Why DFMA prefers NNS (2 marks):**
1. *Material saving* — only 5–10% of stock becomes chips, compared with 60–80% in full machining-from-bar.
2. *Cycle time reduction* — forging takes seconds; rough machining from a billet may take 30+ minutes.
3. *Improved mechanical properties* — the *grain flow* follows the contour of the final shape, giving better fatigue life.
4. *Lower per-part cost at scale* — once the die is amortised, forging cost is dominated by material and energy, not labour.

**Example (1 mark):**
A connecting rod — hot forged (NNS), then finish-machined on the big-end and small-end bores and the bolt-hole threads.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Many students confuse *NNS* with *net-shape*. A sand-cast pump housing is *near-net* (the machined faces are NNS), while an injection-moulded polymer gear is *net-shape* (no machining needed). **Always state whether the example is NNS or net-shape explicitly** to earn the full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1**

- **Manufacturing** = value-adding transformation of raw material into a finished product.
- **DFMA** = co-design of the product *and* the process; design and manufacturing are not separate phases.
- **Six classification bases** are examinable: (1) state of material, (2) working temperature, (3) nature of operation, (4) production volume, (5) material flow (subtractive/additive/formative), (6) energy source (conventional/non-conventional).
- **Primary shaping** creates the *initial* geometry; **secondary shaping (machining)** refines it; **surface finishing** modifies only the *skin*; **joining** unites parts; **heat treatment** alters microstructure; **assembly** combines sub-parts.
- **Hot working** is above $T_r \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (Kelvin); **cold working** is below $T_r$ — this governs force, accuracy, and surface finish.
- **Net-shape** = no machining needed; **Near-net-shape (NNS)** = small finish-machining allowance.
- **Process selection is volume-driven**: at $N \geq 10{,}000$, invest in tooling (die casting, injection moulding, P/M); at $N \leq 10$, use job-shop processes (machining, 3D printing).
- **Subtractive** = chips (waste); **Formative** = no mass change; **Additive** = built from nothing.
- **Conventional** = mechanical contact; **Non-conventional** = electrical, chemical, thermal, or jet energy.
- **DFM Decision Rule**: Always tie your final process recommendation to a *DFMA principle* — material saving, grain-flow continuity, tolerance match, cost amortisation, or assembly reduction.
- **Answer structure for 14-mark Part B**: Material $\rightarrow$ Volume $\rightarrow$ Geometry $\rightarrow$ Tolerance $\rightarrow$ Cost $\rightarrow$ DFMA-aligned recommendation. This six-step frame is the single most reliable way to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Manufacturing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Manufacturing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Manufacturing Process?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A *manufacturing process* is a sequence of scientific and engineering operations applied to raw materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) in order to transform their **geometry, properties, or appearance** into a finished or semi-finished part having the desired shape, size, surface finish, and functional characteristics.

The American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) further categorises these operations as **shaping, joining, coating, or property-enhancing** processes. For the DFMA (Design for Manufacture and Assembly) mindset, the process is never chosen *after* the design — it is chosen *during* the design.

## 1.2 The Six Primary Families of Manufacturing

Manufacturing operations are systematically grouped into six functionally distinct families. Mastering this classification is the foundation of the entire DFMA methodology because each family carries its own **design rules, cost drivers, and tolerance ceilings**.

| # | Process Family | Primary Action | Typical Energy Source |
|---|----------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Casting** | Shape via solidification of liquid | Thermal (furnace) |
| 2 | **Forming** | Shape via plastic deformation | Mechanical / hydraulic |
| 3 | **Machining** | Shape via material removal | Mechanical (kinetic) |
| 4 | **Joining** | Permanently bond two or more parts | Thermal / chemical / mechanical |
| 5 | **Heat Treatment** | Modify microstructure & properties | Thermal (controlled) |
| 6 | **Polymer & Powder Processing** | Shape non-metallic / particulate matter | Thermal + mechanical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Metaphor"**
> Imagine cooking a meal. **Casting** is like pouring batter into a mould (think idli/doughnut). **Forming** is like rolling dough flat with a pin. **Machining** is like carving a vegetable with a knife. **Joining** is like welding two pieces of bread with a filling. **Heat treatment** is like tempering chocolate — you change its *internal structure* without changing its *outer shape*. **Polymer processing** is like squeezing toothpaste out of a tube. Once you see manufacturing this way, the abstract families become everyday intuition.

## 1.3 Why a DFMA Engineer Must Master These Processes

In a typical B.Tech product life-cycle, **70–80 % of the final product cost is committed during the design phase**, long before a single machine is switched on. A designer who does not know that a *3 mm internal corner radius* requires an *EDM operation* (cost: ₹2000) instead of a *milling operation* (cost: ₹40) will silently destroy the product's profitability. Therefore, every KTU question in this module tests not just the *what* but the *when* and *why* of process selection.

## 1.4 Process Capability at a Glance (KTU Quick Reference)

- **Dimensional accuracy** ranges from **±0.5 mm** (sand casting) to **±0.005 mm** (lapping, super-finishing).
- **Surface roughness (Ra)** ranges from **25 µm** (as-cast) down to **0.05 µm** (polishing/lapping).
- **Minimum section thickness** varies from **0.5 mm** (sheet metal) to **25 mm+** (sand casting) — a vital design rule.
- **Production rate** spans from **< 1 part/day** (job-shop) to **> 2000 parts/hour** (transfer line).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Throughout Module 1, KTU expects you to map every component feature → compatible process → design guideline → approximate cost impact. Memorise the *six families* first; everything else is a branch of this tree.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-Selection Logic — The "Decision Tree" Mental Model

A systematic process-selection approach used in DFMA follows four sequential filters:

1. **Material constraint** — Is the stock metallic, polymeric, ceramic, or composite?
2. **Geometry constraint** — Is the part *solid-and-simple* or *hollow-and-complex*?
3. **Tolerance / Surface-finish constraint** — What Ra and ±tolerance are demanded?
4. **Batch size & economic constraint** — Prototype (< 50), batch (50–10 000), or mass production (> 10 000)?

> **Logic of the tree:** If material = steel + geometry = complex + tolerance = ±0.2 mm + batch = 200 → the answer converges on *investment casting + finish machining*. Any other combination shifts the answer.

## 2.2 Detailed Family-Wise Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Casting Processes

Casting exploits the fluid-to-solid transition. The molten metal (or polymer) is poured into a mould cavity, allowed to solidify, and then ejected.

- **Sand Casting** — Most versatile; uses silica sand + binder; suitable for ferrous and non-ferrous metals; very low tooling cost (₹5 000–₹50 000) but rough surface (Ra 12–25 µm) and wide tolerances (±1.5 mm).
- **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax)** — Wax pattern is dipped in ceramic slurry; used for super-alloys, jewellery, turbine blades. Excellent surface (Ra 1.6–3.2 µm) and tight tolerance (±0.1 mm). High tooling cost.
- **Die Casting** — Molten metal is injected under high pressure (35–700 bar) into a hardened steel die. Suited to Zn, Al, Mg alloys. Very high production rates.
- **Permanent-Mould Casting** — Gravity pour into reusable metal mould — between sand and die casting in cost/finish.
- **Centrifugal Casting** — Rotating mould — used for pipes, tubes, and cylindrical bushings.
- **Continuous Casting** — For long constant-section billets/slabs used as rolling stock.

### 2.2.2 Forming Processes (Bulk & Sheet)

Bulk forming redistributes large volumes of material under compressive stresses; sheet forming redistributes thin sections.

- **Forging** (Open-die / Closed-die / Impression-die) — superior grain flow, high strength. Open-die for huge shafts; closed-die for symmetric parts like connecting rods.
- **Rolling** — passes ingot between two counter-rotating rolls; produces plate, sheet, structural sections.
- **Extrusion** (Direct / Indirect) — forces billet through a die; used for long constant cross-sections (window frames, pipes).
- **Drawing** — pulls wire/rod through a die to reduce cross-section.
- **Sheet-metal operations** — blanking, piercing, bending, deep drawing, stretch forming, spinning.

### 2.2.3 Machining Processes

Material-removal processes where a harder tool removes a softer chip. The "triad" is **Tool, Workpiece, and Relative Motion**.

- **Turning** — single-point tool, rotating workpiece → cylinders, cones, threads.
- **Milling** — multi-point rotating cutter → flat surfaces, slots, contours.
- **Drilling** — creates round holes; the most common secondary operation.
- **Grinding** — abrasive wheel removes 0.01–0.001 mm per pass; finishing operation.
- **Non-conventional** — EDM, ECM, LBM, AJM, USM, WJM (covered later in module 3).

### 2.2.4 Joining Processes

Two broad philosophies — **fusion joining** (base metal melted) and **mechanical joining** (no melting).

- **Welding** — Arc (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW), Resistance, TIG/MIG, Laser, Electron-beam, Friction-stir.
- **Brazing & Soldering** — Filler metal melts **below** 450 °C (solder) or 450–950 °C (braze) — no base-metal melting.
- **Adhesive Bonding** — Chemical/mechanical interlocking; uniform stress distribution; seals as it joins.
- **Mechanical Fastening** — Bolts, screws, rivets, snap-fits, press-fits — *the assembly engineer's bread and butter*.

### 2.2.5 Heat Treatment (Property-Enhancing, Not Shaping)

Heat treatment changes **internal structure, hardness, and strength** without altering shape.

- **Annealing** — softens, relieves stress, refines grain.
- **Normalising** — air-cool from austenitising temperature — refines grain for uniform properties.
- **Quenching (Hardening)** — rapid cool from austenitising — forms martensite (very hard, brittle).
- **Tempering** — re-heats quenched steel to 150–650 °C — toughens the martensite.
- **Case Hardening** (Carburising, Nitriding, Induction, Laser) — hard surface + tough core.

### 2.2.6 Polymer & Powder Processing

- **Injection Moulding** — molten polymer shot into mould under 700–1400 bar; cycle time 5–60 s; ideal for mass production of intricate parts.
- **Blow Moulding** — hollow plastic parts (bottles, tanks).
- **Thermoforming** — heat a sheet, drape over a mould, vacuum-form.
- **Compression Moulding** — for thermosets (phenolic, melamine) and bulk-moulding compounds.
- **Powder Metallurgy** — compact metal powder in a die + sinter in furnace; near-net-shape; minimal scrap; used for self-lubricating bearings and cemented carbide inserts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most frequently tested equations across all six process families. Master these — they appear in nearly every Part-B question.**

| Process | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turning — Material Removal Rate | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot v_f$ | $f$ = feed per rev; $d$ = depth of cut; $v_f$ = feed rate | mm³/min |
| Turning — Cutting Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $D$ = workpiece dia; $N$ = spindle rpm | m/min |
| Drilling — MRR (per hole) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2 f N}{4}$ | $D$ = drill dia; $f$ = feed/rev; $N$ = rpm | mm³/s |
| Taylor's Tool Life | $V T^n = C$ | $V$ = cutting speed; $T$ = tool life; $n, C$ = constants | m/min ; min |
| Machining Power | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_s}{60 \eta}$ | $K_s$ = specific cutting energy; $\eta$ = efficiency | kW |
| Forging Force (open die) | $F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | $K_f$ = flow stress; $A$ = contact area; $h_0, h_f$ = initial/final height | N |
| Extrusion Force | $F = A_0 \cdot k \cdot \ln(R)$ | $A_0$ = billet area; $k$ = flow stress; $R$ = extrusion ratio | N |
| Sheet Bending — Bend Allowance | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} (R + K_t s) \theta$ | $R$ = inner radius; $K_t$ = neutral-axis factor; $s$ = thickness; $\theta$ = bend angle (deg) | mm |
| Casting Yield | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{cast}}{m_{poured}} \times 100$ | $m_{cast}$ = mass of usable casting; $m_{poured}$ = total mass poured | % |
| Shrinkage Allowance | $L_{pattern} = L_{cast} (1 + S)$ | $S$ = shrinkage fraction (≈ 0.015 for steel, 0.012 for cast iron, 0.020 for Al) | mm |
| Heat Flow (Fourier's law) | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity; $dT/dx$ = temperature gradient | W/m² |
| Welding Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency; $V$ = voltage; $I$ = current; $v$ = travel speed | J/mm |
| Cycle Time (single station) | $T_c = T_h + T_s + T_i$ | $T_h$ = handling; $T_s$ = setup; $T_i$ = idle/wait | s/pc |
| Total Cost per Part | $C_p = \dfrac{C_{tool}}{N} + C_{m} + \dfrac{C_mh \cdot T_c}{3600}$ | $C_{tool}$ = tool cost; $N$ = parts/life; $C_m$ = material; $C_{mh}$ = machine-hour rate | ₹ |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU Answer Scripts** — When writing the casting-yield formula in handwriting, always enclose the fraction in a single horizontal line; examiners deduct half a mark for ambiguous division.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Process Selection

- **Automotive crank-shaft** → Forged (closed-die) + induction-hardened journals.
- **Aero-engine turbine blade** → Investment cast in Inconel + single-crystal solidification.
- **Mobile-phone housing** → Injection moulded polycarbonate + vacuum-metallised coating.
- **Pressure vessel** → Rolled plate + multi-pass arc-welded + radiographically inspected.
- **Surgical implant (hip joint)** → Investment-cast Ti-6Al-4V + passivated surface.

A single DFMA mis-step in any of these cases can result in scrap, safety failure, or legal recall.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

We begin with the geometry of a single turning cut. As the tool traverses the rotating workpiece, an annular ring of material of width $f$ (feed) and depth $d$ is removed every revolution.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The uncut chip cross-section is rectangular: width $= f$, height $= d$. The circumference of the cut per revolution is $\pi D$. Multiplying gives the volume removed per revolution.

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the chip.**

$$
A_c = f \cdot d
$$

The cross-section $A_c$ is in mm². It represents the projected area of the chip on the feed-depth plane.

**Step 3 — Length of cut per revolution.**
For one full revolution, the tool effectively sweeps a length equal to the circumference of the workpiece diameter $D$:

$$
L_{rev} = \pi D
$$

**Step 4 — Volume removed per revolution.**

$$
V_{rev} = A_c \cdot L_{rev} = f \cdot d \cdot \pi D
$$

**Step 5 — Convert revolutions to time.**
If the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), then volume per minute (which is the **Material Removal Rate**) is:

$$
MRR = V_{rev} \cdot N
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the feed rate.**
The feed rate $v_f$ (mm/min) is defined as $f \cdot N$. Substituting this directly into the expression yields the standard textbook formula:

$$
\boxed{MRR = f \cdot d \cdot v_f}
$$

> **Logic check:** If we *double the feed* and *double the depth*, the volume removed becomes *four times larger* — which matches physical intuition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forging Force in Open-Die Compression

In open-die forging, a cylindrical billet of height $h_0$ is compressed between two flat dies to a final height $h_f$. The forging force depends on the instantaneous flow stress and the instantaneous cross-sectional area.

**Step 1 — Assume constant volume (incompressibility).**

$$
A \cdot h = A_0 \cdot h_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = A_0 \dfrac{h_0}{h}
$$

This relation lets us express the instantaneous area in terms of the instantaneous height.

**Step 2 — Ideal work of deformation per unit volume.**
For a perfectly plastic material with flow stress $K_f$, the incremental plastic work is $K_f \cdot d\varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h)$ is the true (logarithmic) strain.

**Step 3 — Total ideal work.**

$$
W_{ideal} = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K_f \, V \, d\varepsilon = K_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Convert work to average force.**
Average force is work divided by displacement $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$. After some algebra (using mean-value theorem) and assuming $A_{avg} = A_0 \cdot h_0 / h_{avg}$, the practical engineering equation is:

$$
\boxed{F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)}
$$

> **Logic check:** A *taller* initial billet (larger $h_0$) and a *greater* reduction both *increase* the force — exactly what shop-floor experience confirms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Welding Heat Input

In arc welding, the heat supplied to the workpiece is the product of arc power and dwell time per unit length.

**Step 1 — Arc power.**

$$
P_{arc} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

where $\eta$ is the arc efficiency (≈ 0.7 for GMAW, 0.8 for GTAW, 0.85 for SAW).

**Step 2 — Dwell time per millimetre of weld.**
If the travel speed is $v$ (mm/s), the time spent over each millimetre of weld is $1/v$ seconds.

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length.**

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}}
$$

Units: J/mm. Typical values: 0.5–1.5 kJ/mm for thin sheets, 2–5 kJ/mm for thick sections.

> **Engineering significance:** *Too little* heat input → lack of fusion, porosity. *Too much* heat input → grain coarsening, distortion, HAZ cracking. KTU frequently tests the trade-off curve.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Process from a Feature

The following matrix is a *high-frequency* KTU evaluation tool. Read the **column** of the feature you need to manufacture, then move down to find the most cost-effective process.

| Feature to Make | Casting | Forging | Machining | Sheet Metal | Injection Moulding | Powder Metallurgy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Round hole Ø 10 mm | ✗ (need drill) | ✗ (need drill) | **★★★ Drilling** | ✗ (need punch) | **★★★ Core pin** | ✗ (need drill) |
| Complex internal cavity | **★★★ Investment** | ✗ Impossible | ✗ Impossible | ✗ Impossible | **★★★ Injection** | **★★★ P/M with core** |
| High-strength shaft | ✗ (poor grain) | **★★★ Forging** | ★ (chips away fibre) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Thin sheet panel | ✗ | ✗ | ★ (wasteful) | **★★★ Stamping** | ✗ | ✗ |
| 5000 identical gears/year | ★ (machining each) | ✗ | ★ (multi-axis) | ✗ | **★★★ Injection (nylon)** | **★★★ Sintered steel** |
| Thread M8 × 20 | ✗ | ✗ | **★★★ Tapping** | ✗ (self-tap screw) | **★★★ Moulded thread** | ✗ |
| ±0.02 mm flatness | ✗ | ✗ | **★★★ Grinding/lapping** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When the question gives a *feature* (e.g., "design a part with a 30° draft-free internal slot"), your first move is to identify *which processes* can technically make that feature, then *rank* them by cost and batch size. This 2-step reasoning is what distinguishes a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Part-B Sub-Question)

> **Question:** A turning operation on a mild-steel shaft uses cutting speed $v = 120$ m/min, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 2$ mm. The workpiece diameter is 60 mm. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) time to machine a 200 mm length.

**Step 1 — Spindle speed from cutting velocity.**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 120}{\pi \cdot 60} = \dfrac{120000}{188.50} = 636.6 \text{ rpm}
$$

We round to **637 rpm** in practice.

**Step 2 — Feed rate in mm/min.**

$$
v_f = f \cdot N = 0.25 \cdot 637 = 159.25 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate.**

$$
MRR = f \cdot d \cdot v_f = 0.25 \cdot 2 \cdot 159.25 = 79.625 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Machining time for 200 mm length.**

$$
t_m = \dfrac{L}{v_f} = \dfrac{200}{159.25} = 1.256 \text{ min} \approx 75.4 \text{ s}
$$

> **Sanity check:** 80 mm³/min × 1.256 min ≈ 100 mm³ removed — which equals $0.25 \times 2 \times 200$ mm³. The two independent computations agree, confirming the answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Manufacturing Processes

The following Mermaid tree visualises the complete hierarchy introduced in Section 1. It is the single most important diagram for the KTU viva and short-answer questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MANUFACTURING PROCESSES] --> B[Shaping]
    A --> C[JOINING]
    A --> D[PROPERTY ENHANCING]
    A --> E[COATING AND FINISHING]

    B --> B1[Casting]
    B --> B2[Forming Bulk]
    B --> B3[Forming Sheet]
    B --> B4[Machining]
    B --> B5[Polymer Processing]
    B --> B6[Powder Metallurgy]

    B1 --> B1a[Sand]
    B1 --> B1b[Investment]
    B1 --> B1c[Die Casting]
    B1 --> B1d[Centrifugal]
    B1 --> B1e[Continuous]

    B2 --> B2a[Forging Open]
    B2 --> B2b[Forging Closed]
    B2 --> B2c[Rolling]
    B2 --> B2d[Extrusion]
    B2 --> B2e[Drawing]

    B3 --> B3a[Blanking]
    B3 --> B3b[Piercing]
    B3 --> B3c[Bending]
    B3 --> B3d[Deep Drawing]
    B3 --> B3e[Spinning]

    B4 --> B4a[Turning]
    B4 --> B4b[Milling]
    B4 --> B4c[Drilling]
    B4 --> B4d[Grinding]
    B4 --> B4e[Non Conventional EDM ECM LBM]

    B5 --> B5a[Injection Moulding]
    B5 --> B5b[Blow Moulding]
    B5 --> B5c[Thermoforming]
    B5 --> B5d[Compression Moulding]

    B6 --> B6a[Press and Sinter]
    B6 --> B6b[Metal Injection Moulding]
    B6 --> B6c[Hot Isostatic Pressing]

    C --> C1[Arc Welding]
    C --> C2[Resistance Welding]
    C --> C3[Brazing and Soldering]
    C --> C4[Adhesive Bonding]
    C --> C5[Mechanical Fastening]

    D --> D1[Annealing]
    D --> D2[Normalising]
    D --> D3[Hardening and Tempering]
    D --> D4[Case Hardening]

    E --> E1[Painting]
    E --> E2[Electroplating]
    E --> E3[Anodising]
    E --> E4[Powder Coating]
```

## 4.2 Process-Selection Decision Flow (DFMA Logic)

This second diagram shows the *reasoning path* a DFMA engineer walks when choosing a process. Memorise the sequence — KTU commonly asks for a similar flow in Part-A.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start with Design Feature] --> S1{Is Material Metal or Polymer?}
    S1 -- Metal --> S2{Is Section Thin less than 6 mm?}
    S1 -- Polymer --> S3{Is Production Volume High more than 5000?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Choose Sheet Metal Forming]
    S2 -- No --> S5{Is Strength Critical?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Choose Forging]
    S5 -- No --> S7{Is Geometry Complex Internal Cavity?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Choose Casting or P and M]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Choose Machining from Bar Stock]
    S3 -- Yes --> S10[Choose Injection Moulding]
    S3 -- No --> S11[Choose Vacuum Forming or Machining Polymer]
    S4 --> S12{Is Tolerance less than 0.1 mm?}
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Add Fine Blanking or Post Machine]
    S12 -- No --> S14[Conventional Stamping]
    S6 --> S15{Is Batch more than 1000?}
    S15 -- Yes --> S16[Closed Die Forging]
    S15 -- No --> S17[Open Die Forging]
    S8 --> S18{Is Surface Finish Critical?}
    S18 -- Yes --> S19[Investment Casting]
    S18 -- No --> S20[Sand Casting]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Casting Process Chain

The end-to-end *signal/material flow* of a sand-casting production line is mapped below. Each block represents a workstation, and arrows represent material movement.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Pattern Shop] -->|Wooden Pattern| nodeB[Sand Moulding Line]
    nodeB -->|Green Sand Mould| nodeC[Melting Furnace]
    nodeC -->|Molten Metal at 1400 deg C| nodeD[Pouring Station]
    nodeD -->|Filled Mould| nodeE[Solidification and Cooling Line]
    nodeE -->|Cooled Casting| nodeF[Shakeout and Degating]
    nodeF -->|Raw Casting| nodeG[Shot Blasting]
    nodeG -->|Clean Casting| nodeH[Inspection and NDT]
    nodeH -->|Accepted Casting| nodeI[Heat Treatment Furnace]
    nodeH -->|Rejected Casting| nodeJ[Re Melting]
    nodeI -->|Treated Casting| nodeK[Final Machining Cell]
    nodeK -->|Finished Part| nodeL[Assembly and Shipping]
    nodeJ --> nodeC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Exam Writing:** When drawing such a flow in your answer script, label each block with the *workstation name* and the *input/output material*. The arrow from `nodeJ` back to `nodeC` is the **recycle loop** — examiners love seeing it because it demonstrates closed-loop material-flow thinking, a key DFMA concept.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Generic Machining Workstation

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Power Supply 415V 3Phase] --> P2[Motor Drive]
    P2 --> P3[Spindle]
    P3 --> P4[Tool Holder]
    P4 --> P5[Cutting Tool]
    P6[Workpiece] --> P7[Chuck or Fixture]
    P7 --> P8[Slideways X Y Z]
    P8 --> P9[Controller CNC or Manual]
    P9 --> P2
    P5 -->|Chips| P10[Chip Conveyor]
    P10 --> P11[Coolant Recycle Tank]
    P11 -->|Reused Coolant| P5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **controller** sends velocity commands to the **drive**, which spins the **spindle** holding the **tool**; the **tool** engages the **workpiece** held by a **fixture** mounted on **slideways**; **chips** are carried away by a **conveyor** and the **coolant** is recycled. The three vertical subsystems (power, motion, control) are the typical exam answer for "describe the architecture of a CNC turning centre."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **bulk deformation** and **sheet-metal forming** processes. Give two examples of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - *Bulk deformation* acts on large-volume stock and the change in cross-section is significant. Examples: **forging, extrusion, rolling, drawing**. (1½ marks)
> - *Sheet-metal forming* acts on thin sheets (typically < 6 mm); the change in thickness is negligible, only shape changes. Examples: **bending, deep drawing, blanking, spinning**. (1½ marks)
>
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two examples of bulk, 1 mark for two examples of sheet.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2.** List any **four design guidelines** that a DFMA engineer must follow while designing a sand-cast component.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks — list any four):**
> 1. Provide **generous fillets and rounded internal corners** (R ≥ 3 mm) to avoid stress concentration and to allow easy sand flow.
> 2. Maintain **uniform wall thickness** to avoid shrinkage cavities and hot tears.
> 3. Provide **draft angles of 1°–3°** on all faces parallel to the mould-draw direction.
> 4. Use **cores** for internal cavities and provide adequate **core prints** for support.
> 5. Avoid large flat surfaces; provide **slight crowning** to prevent warping.
> 6. Follow the **parting-line principle** to minimise machining and finishing.
>
> **Allocation:** ½ mark each for four correctly stated guidelines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **sand-casting process** step by step. List four defects encountered in sand castings and their remedies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mild-steel casting requires the final part dimensions to be 250 mm × 150 mm × 30 mm. If the shrinkage fraction for cast steel is **0.015** and the pouring mass is **9.5 kg**, find (i) the **pattern dimensions** and (ii) the **casting yield** if the final cast mass is **8.2 kg**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

1. **Pattern making** — wood or metal pattern slightly larger than final part (to compensate for shrinkage). (1 mark)
2. **Mould preparation** — silica sand + bentonite + water mixed in a muller; packed around the pattern in a flask with a parting line. (1 mark)
3. **Core setting** — sand cores are placed in the mould cavity to create internal features. (1 mark)
4. **Melting & pouring** — steel is melted in a cupola or induction furnace to 1500–1600 °C, then poured into the sprue. (1 mark)
5. **Solidification & cooling** — allowed to cool; for steel, cooling time may be several hours. (½ mark)
6. **Shakeout, degating, fettling, inspection** — mould broken, runners cut, surface cleaned. (½ mark)

**Defects and remedies (2 marks — ½ each for any four):**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Blow holes | Trapped gas/moisture in sand | Properly dry sand; add venting |
| Shrinkage cavity | Improper feeding | Add risers of correct size |
| Hot tear | Restrained contraction | Provide fillets; uniform thickness |
| Sand inclusion | Weak mould | Use proper sand binder; handle carefully |
| Cold shut | Low pouring temperature | Increase pouring temperature |
| Misrun | Insufficient fluidity | Increase pour temperature; enlarge gate |

#### Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Pattern dimensions using shrinkage rule $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$.**

For length 250 mm:

$$
L_p = 250 (1 + 0.015) = 250 \cdot 1.015 = 253.75 \text{ mm}
$$

For width 150 mm:

$$
W_p = 150 \cdot 1.015 = 152.25 \text{ mm}
$$

For thickness 30 mm:

$$
T_p = 30 \cdot 1.015 = 30.45 \text{ mm}
$$

(1.5 marks for the three dimensions)

**Step 2 — Casting yield.**

$$
\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{cast}}{m_{poured}} \times 100 = \dfrac{8.2}{9.5} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_c = 86.32\%}
$$

(1.5 marks — formula + substitution + numerical answer with units)

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
A yield above 85 % is considered *excellent* in a sand foundry; a yield of 50–60 % is typical for small castings. The high yield here indicates well-designed **gating and risering**. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in (b):**
> - Students often *forget to apply the shrinkage rule to all three dimensions* — only the longest dimension is calculated, losing 0.5 mark.
> - The **casting yield** is a percentage; some students write 0.8632 instead of 86.32 %, losing 0.5 mark.
> - Units of pattern dimensions are *mm*, not cm. Marks deducted for unit inconsistency.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **(a)** Compare **hot working and cold working** of metals across any **seven parameters**. State one example process for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A copper rod of diameter 25 mm is drawn through a die in two passes to a final diameter of 15 mm. If the flow stress of copper is constant at **250 MPa** and the die efficiency is **0.9**, calculate the **drawing force** required in the second pass. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

| # | Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature | Above recrystallisation temperature | Below recrystallisation temperature |
| 2 | Force required | Low (metal is soft) | High (metal is strain-hardened) |
| 3 | Ductility | High | Limited (may crack) |
| 4 | Surface finish | Poor (oxide scale) | Excellent (smooth, bright) |
| 5 | Dimensional accuracy | Poor | High |
| 6 | Grain structure | Refined, equiaxed | Strain-hardened, directional |
| 7 | Residual stress | Minimal | Significant (may need annealing) |
| 8 | Energy consumption | High (heating) | Low (no heating) |

(7 rows × 1 mark each = 7 marks; one mark for each correctly filled row)

Examples: Hot working → **hot rolling** or **hot forging**; Cold working → **cold drawing** or **cold rolling**.

#### Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry of the second pass.**
First pass takes rod from 25 mm → intermediate diameter $d_1$. Second pass takes $d_1$ → 15 mm.

**Step 2 — Calculate area reduction in the second pass.**
Assume uniform reduction. For a wire drawing problem, the per-pass reduction is typically 20–35 %. If the **total reduction is split equally in terms of area**:

Initial area $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (25)^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$.

Final area $A_f = \dfrac{\pi (15)^2}{4} = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2$.

Total reduction ratio $R_{total} = A_0 / A_f = 490.87 / 176.71 = 2.778$.

For two equal area passes, each pass gives $R_p = \sqrt{2.778} = 1.667$.

Therefore $A_1 = A_0 / R_p = 490.87 / 1.667 = 294.51 \text{ mm}^2$, giving $d_1 = \sqrt{4A_1/\pi} \approx 19.36 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 3 — Ideal drawing force for second pass.**

$$
F_{ideal} = A_1 \cdot K_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_f}\right)
$$

$$
F_{ideal} = 294.51 \cdot 250 \cdot \ln(1.667) = 294.51 \cdot 250 \cdot 0.5108
$$

$$
F_{ideal} = 37{,}615 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply die efficiency.**

$$
F_{actual} = \dfrac{F_{ideal}}{\eta} = \dfrac{37{,}615}{0.9} = 41{,}794 \text{ N} \approx 41.8 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{actual} \approx 41.8 \text{ kN}}
$$

(Allocation: 1.5 marks area calculations, 2 marks ideal force, 1 mark efficiency correction, 1 mark final answer, 1.5 marks units/interpretation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in (b):**
> - **Forgetting to apply the die efficiency** — examiners deduct a full 1 mark.
> - **Mixing up which area enters the log term**: it is $A_1/A_f$, **not** $A_0/A_f$ for the second pass.
> - **Unit error**: $K_f$ is in **MPa = N/mm²**, so the final force automatically comes out in **N** — do not multiply by 10⁶.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as the *last page* of your revision. Cover it daily until the exam.

- [x] **Six families** — Casting, Forming, Machining, Joining, Heat Treatment, Polymer/Powder Processing. Memorise the family tree.
- [x] **Process selection follows 4 filters** — material → geometry → tolerance → batch size.
- [x] **Casting is the only primary process for complex internal cavities** in metals.
- [x] **Forging provides the best grain flow** for fatigue-loaded components.
- [x] **Machining is a secondary process** — it is used *after* a primary shaping step to achieve tolerance and finish.
- [x] **MRR formula $f \cdot d \cdot v_f$** — appears in at least one question every year.
- [x] **Taylor's tool-life equation $VT^n = C$** — high-yield for Part-A.
- [x] **Shrinkage allowance = $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$** — values: steel 0.015, CI 0.012, Al 0.020.
- [x] **Casting yield** = (cast mass / poured mass) × 100; > 85 % is excellent.
- [x] **Welding heat input $H = \eta V I / v$** — too low → lack of fusion; too high → grain growth.
- [x] **Hot working is above recrystallisation temperature**, cold working below it.
- [x] **Forging force $F = K_f A \ln(h_0/h_f)$** — logarithmic true-strain based.
- [x] **Injection moulding is the only economical process for high-volume polymer parts**.
- [x] **Powder metallurgy is the only process that can make self-lubricating bearings**.
- [x] **Always state units** — ₹, mm, mm³/min, MPa, kN, m/min — a unit error loses a full mark.
- [x] **Internal choice is mandatory in KTU Part B** — practise writing both Question A and Question B within 45 minutes.
- [x] **Drawings are scored separately** — a clear labelled diagram carries 2–3 marks even in a theory answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam-Day Strategy:** Read the Part-B question's *process names* first. If the question asks "differentiate," make a **table** (not paragraphs) — tables are easier for the examiner to tick. If the question says "with neat sketch," *always* draw a labelled diagram, even if you are unsure of the answer — a labelled diagram alone can fetch 2–3 grace marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mechanical Properties of Materials** are the characteristic responses of a material to applied external forces, loads, or deformations. They quantify how a material behaves under mechanical, thermal, and environmental stimuli, and they form the empirical foundation upon which every design, manufacturing, and assembly decision rests within the **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)** framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET522 - Module 1):**
> Mechanical properties describe the behavior of a material under the action of external forces. They are intrinsic, structure-dependent characteristics (tensile strength, hardness, toughness, ductility, fatigue limit, etc.) that govern **form**, **function**, and **manufacturability** of engineering components.

In the context of **DFMA**, mechanical properties are not merely abstract material constants. They directly dictate:
- The **manufacturing process** chosen (e.g., casting vs. forging vs. machining)
- The **tolerances** that can be economically achieved
- The **joining/assembly methods** (welding, riveting, snap-fits, adhesive bonding)
- The **service life** and **reliability** of the product

### 1.2 Classification of Mechanical Properties

Mechanical properties can be classified into the following primary categories:

| Category | Properties | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Elastic Properties** | Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), Poisson's Ratio (ν) | Stiffness, deflection under load |
| **Strength Properties** | Yield Strength (σy), Ultimate Tensile Strength (σuts), Compressive Strength | Load-bearing capacity |
| **Plastic Properties** | Ductility (% elongation, % reduction in area) | Formability, ability to deform without fracture |
| **Toughness & Resilience** | Modulus of Resilience (Ur), Modulus of Toughness (Ut), Impact Energy | Energy absorption before fracture |
| **Hardness** | Brinell (BHN), Rockwell (HRC), Vickers (HV) | Wear resistance, machinability indicator |
| **Time-Dependent Properties** | Fatigue Strength (σf), Creep Rate (ε̇) | Long-term service behavior |
| **Fracture Properties** | Fracture Toughness (KIC) | Resistance to crack propagation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: "The Human Skeleton Test"**
> Imagine you are squeezing different fruits in your hand: a ripe mango, a green coconut, and a rubber ball.
> - The **rubber ball** bounces back to its original shape → it is **elastic**.
> - The **green coconut** does not dent at all → it is **hard and strong**.
> - The **ripe mango** dents, holds its new shape, and does not return → it is **plastic and ductile**.
> - The **stone** snaps suddenly under impact → it is **brittle**.
> - The **steel wire** that you bend back and forth many times until it breaks → it has failed due to **fatigue**.

Each fruit has a "personality" of how it responds to force. That personality, in engineering terms, is its **mechanical property profile**. A design engineer must match the component's "mechanical personality requirement" to the material's actual property profile — otherwise, the component will either fail in service or be grossly over-engineered (and thus uneconomical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFMA Perspective:** Mechanical properties are the *bridge* between *what the product must do* (functional requirement) and *how it will be made* (manufacturing process). A wrong match leads to either product failure OR an unmanufacturable/uneconomical design.

### 1.4 The Stress-Strain Curve — The Master Plot

The single most important graphical tool for understanding mechanical properties is the **Stress-Strain (σ-ε) Curve** obtained from a standardized **Tensile Test** (typically per **ASTM E8 / IS 1608**).

- **Stress (σ)**: Internal resisting force per unit area, measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Strain (ε)**: Unit deformation (dimensionless, often expressed as %).

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad \text{...(Engineering Stress)}$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \quad \text{...(Engineering Strain)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A₀ = Original cross-sectional area, L₀ = Original gauge length. These are the *initial* values, not instantaneous values, hence the term "engineering" stress/strain.

The curve reveals the entire mechanical "personality" of a material — from elastic recovery to plastic deformation to ultimate fracture — in a single plot. Every property listed above can be read off this curve.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Tensile Test — Operational Steps (ASTM E8)

The standardized tensile test sequence is as follows:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A standardized "dog-bone" specimen is machined with precise gauge length **L₀** (often 50 mm) and cross-section **A₀** (circular or rectangular).
2. **Mounting:** The specimen is gripped in a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — also called an Instron or MTS machine.
3. **Loading:** An axial tensile force **F** is applied at a controlled strain rate (typically 0.5 to 5 mm/min).
4. **Data Acquisition:** Load **F** vs. Elongation **ΔL** is continuously recorded.
5. **Conversion:** F is converted to stress (σ = F/A₀); ΔL is converted to strain (ε = ΔL/L₀).
6. **Plotting:** The σ-ε curve is plotted, and characteristic points are identified.
7. **Post-Failure Measurement:** Final gauge length **Lf** and final neck cross-section **Af** are measured to compute ductility.

### 2.2 Key Regions of the Stress-Strain Curve (Low-Carbon Steel Example)

The σ-ε curve is divided into four distinct regions:

#### **Region I — Linear Elastic Region**
- Stress is **directly proportional** to strain.
- Governed by **Hooke's Law**: σ = Eε.
- The slope of this linear portion is the **Young's Modulus (E)**.
- Deformation is **fully recoverable** — upon unloading, the material returns to its original shape.
- The upper limit of this region is the **Proportional Limit (σp)**.

#### **Region II — Elastic Limit to Yield Point**
- Beyond σp, the curve deviates from linearity, but deformation is still fully recoverable.
- The **Elastic Limit** marks the maximum stress up to which the material can return to its original shape on unloading.
- The **Yield Point (σy)** is the stress at which permanent (plastic) deformation begins.
- For low-carbon steel, there is a distinct **Upper Yield Point** and **Lower Yield Point** (Lüders bands propagate).
- For most other metals, the **0.2% Offset Method** is used to define the yield strength (σ0.2).

#### **Region III — Plastic Region (Strain Hardening)**
- Beyond σy, large deformations occur with relatively small increases in stress.
- The material undergoes **strain hardening** (also called **work hardening**).
- Stress reaches a maximum value, called the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σuts)**.
- Beyond σuts, **necking** begins — a localized reduction in cross-sectional area.

#### **Region IV — Necking and Fracture**
- After σuts, the engineering stress *appears* to drop because A₀ is no longer the true area.
- The **True Stress (σtrue = F/Ainstantaneous)** continues to rise until fracture.
- Fracture occurs at the **Fracture Point**, and the corresponding stresses/strains define the material's ductility.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a KTU examination-ready summary. Memorize these formulas verbatim — they appear in nearly every Module 1 question.**

| Property | Symbol | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Stress | σ | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** | Force per unit original area |
| Engineering Strain | ε | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless (or %) | Fractional change in length |
| Hooke's Law (Axial) | — | $\sigma = E\,\epsilon$ | — | Linear elastic constitutive law |
| Young's Modulus | E | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{F\,L_0}{A_0\,\Delta L}$ | **GPa** | Stiffness against axial loading |
| Shear Modulus | G | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | **GPa** | Stiffness against shear loading |
| Bulk Modulus | K | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_{hydro}}{-\Delta V/V_0}$ | **GPa** | Stiffness against volumetric compression |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | $\nu = -\dfrac{\epsilon_{lateral}}{\epsilon_{axial}}$ | dimensionless | Lateral contraction per unit axial extension |
| Elastic Moduli Relation | — | $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | — | Inter-relation among elastic constants |
| True Stress | σtrue | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma\,(1+\epsilon)$ | MPa | Actual stress at the deformed cross-section |
| True Strain | εtrue | $\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\epsilon)$ | dimensionless | Actual logarithmic strain |
| % Elongation | %EL | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Measure of ductility |
| % Reduction in Area | %RA | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % | Measure of ductility (more reliable than %EL) |
| Modulus of Resilience | Ur | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$ | **MJ/m³** or **MPa** | Energy stored per unit volume up to σy |
| Modulus of Toughness | Ut | $U_t = \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma\,d\epsilon$ | **MJ/m³** | Total energy absorbed per unit volume to fracture |
| Endurance Limit (Steels) | σe | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\,\sigma_{uts}$ (for steels) | MPa | Fatigue strength below which life is infinite |
| Creep Rate (Power Law) | $\dot{\epsilon}$ | $\dot{\epsilon} = A\,\sigma^{n}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | s⁻¹ | Steady-state strain rate at high T |
| Brinell Hardness | BHN | $BHN = \dfrac{2F}{\pi\,D\,(D-\sqrt{D^2-d^2})}$ | kgf/mm² | Resistance to indentation by a steel ball |
| Vickers Hardness | HV | $HV = \dfrac{1.854\,F}{d^2}$ | kgf/mm² | Resistance to diamond-pyramid indentation |
| Fracture Toughness (Mode I) | KIC | $K_{IC} = Y\,\sigma\sqrt{\pi\,a}$ | **MPa·√m** | Critical stress intensity for crack propagation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where You Will Use These Formulas in KTU Exams:**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Definitions, units, and significance of each property.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Calculation-based problems on UTM data, elastic constants, hardness conversion, %EL/%RA, resilience, and toughness.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties are not just academic — they have direct industrial application:

- **Automotive Crashworthiness:** Toughness (area under σ-ε curve) determines how much crash energy a chassis can absorb, saving lives.
- **Aerospace Fatigue Design:** Aircraft wings experience ~10⁸ cycles in their lifetime; fatigue limit determines safe operating stress.
- **Pressure Vessel Design:** Stiffness (E) governs deformation; yield strength (σy) governs plastic collapse; fracture toughness (KIC) governs brittle fracture.
- **Sheet Metal Forming (DFMA):** Ductility (%EL) and strain-hardening exponent (n) determine whether a sheet can be deep-drawn, stretched, or bent without cracking.
- **Casting Process Selection (DFMA):** Cast irons have high compressive strength but low ductility; they are *not* chosen for impact-loaded parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Inter-Relation Among Elastic Constants (E, G, K, ν)

**Why this derivation matters in KTU exams:** It is a frequently-asked derivation worth 7–8 marks. You must know it cold.

We will derive the relationship:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 1 — Define the setup.**
Consider a small cubic element of an isotropic, homogeneous material of side length $L$. Apply a uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ along the x-direction.

**Step 2 — Express strains using generalized Hooke's Law.**
For a 3D isotropic material, the strain-stress relations are:

$$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\epsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\epsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the uniaxial condition.**
Since only $\sigma_x$ is non-zero, $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$. Substituting:

$$\epsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \epsilon_y = \epsilon_z = -\frac{\nu\,\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Express pure shear as a pair of equal-and-opposite normal stresses.**
A pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acting on the x-y plane is *equivalent* (by stress transformation) to a pair of normal stresses on the 45° plane:

$$\sigma_a = +\tau_{xy} \quad \text{(tension at 45°)}, \quad \sigma_b = -\tau_{xy} \quad \text{(compression at 90° to } a\text{)}$$

So pure shear is equivalent to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = +\tau$ and $\sigma_y = -\tau$ (and $\sigma_z = 0$).

**Step 5 — Compute the shear strain from these normal strains.**
The engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is the *change in angle* between the x and y axes. Geometrically, for small strains:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \epsilon_a - \epsilon_b$$

where $\epsilon_a$ and $\epsilon_b$ are the normal strains along the 45° and 135° directions (the principal axes for pure shear).

**Step 6 — Compute $\epsilon_a$ and $\epsilon_b$ using Hooke's Law.**
Apply Hooke's Law with $\sigma_x = +\tau$ and $\sigma_y = -\tau$:

$$\epsilon_a = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu\,\sigma_y\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\epsilon_b = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu\,\sigma_x\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau)\right] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Combine to get the shear strain.**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \epsilon_a - \epsilon_b = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the Shear Modulus.**
By definition, $\tau = G\,\gamma_{xy}$. Substituting $\gamma_{xy}$ from above:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1+\nu)\,}$$

This is the most-cited elastic constant relationship in KTU exams. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> A similar (and equally important) derivation relates $E$ to $K$ and $\nu$:

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

> You can derive it by considering hydrostatic pressure $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$, computing volumetric strain, and using $K = -p / (\Delta V / V_0)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Resilience from the Stress-Strain Curve

The **Modulus of Resilience (Ur)** is the strain energy stored per unit volume in the elastic region (up to the yield point). It is the *area under the linear portion* of the σ-ε curve.

**Step 1 — Strain energy density (energy per unit volume) is:**

$$u = \int_0^{\epsilon} \sigma\,d\epsilon$$

**Step 2 — In the linear elastic region, $\sigma = E\epsilon$, so:**

$$u = \int_0^{\epsilon_y} (E\epsilon)\,d\epsilon = E\left[\frac{\epsilon^2}{2}\right]_0^{\epsilon_y} = \frac{E\,\epsilon_y^{\,2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\epsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ (from Hooke's Law at yield):**

$$U_r = \frac{E}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^2 = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$$

$$\boxed{\,U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}\, \quad \text{units: MPa or MJ/m}^3\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFMA Insight:** A high resilience means a spring can store a lot of elastic energy per unit volume before yielding. This is critical for **spring design**, **shock absorbers**, and **energy-storing assemblies**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: UTM Data Interpretation (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam Style):**
> A tensile test specimen of low-carbon steel has an original gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm and original diameter $d_0 = 12.5$ mm. At a load of $F = 49{,}000$ N, the gauge length becomes $L = 50.10$ mm. The yield load is $F_y = 49{,}000$ N. The maximum load reached is $F_{max} = 78{,}000$ N. At fracture, the final length is $L_f = 58.0$ mm and the final diameter at the neck is $d_f = 9.0$ mm. Compute: (a) Engineering stress at the given load, (b) Yield Strength, (c) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (d) % Elongation, (e) % Reduction in Area, (f) Young's Modulus (given that the load 49,000 N corresponds to a 0.1 mm extension), (g) Modulus of Resilience.

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (12.5)^2 = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Engineering stress at F = 49,000 N.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{49{,}000}{122.72} = 399.2 \text{ MPa} \approx 400 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Yield Strength (σy).**
Since $F_y = 49{,}000$ N, $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa (same as in Step 2). This is the stress at which yielding begins.

**Step 4 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (σuts).**

$$\sigma_{uts} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{78{,}000}{122.72} = 635.6 \text{ MPa} \approx 636 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Elongation.**

$$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{58.0 - 50.0}{50.0} \times 100 = 16.0\%$$

**Step 6 — Percentage Reduction in Area.**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (9.0)^2 = 63.62 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{122.72 - 63.62}{122.72} \times 100 = 48.2\%$$

**Step 7 — Young's Modulus (E).**
Strain at 49,000 N:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.10}{50.0} = 0.002$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{400}{0.002} = 200{,}000 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ GPa}$$

This is the typical Young's Modulus value for low-carbon steel — the result confirms our calculation.

**Step 8 — Modulus of Resilience.**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} = \frac{(400)^2}{2 \times 200{,}000} = \frac{160{,}000}{400{,}000} = 0.40 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This low-carbon steel can store **0.40 MJ per cubic meter** elastically before yielding. The high %EL (16%) and high %RA (48.2%) confirm it is a **ductile** material — perfectly suited for **forging, rolling, and sheet metal forming** operations in DFMA.

### 3.4 Derivation: True Stress and True Strain at Necking

**Why this matters:** Engineering stress *falsely* drops after necking because we keep dividing by the original area $A_0$. The true stress continues to rise.

**Step 1 — Definition of true stress.**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

where $A_i$ is the instantaneous cross-sectional area at load $F$.

**Step 2 — Apply constant volume assumption (plastic deformation is incompressible).**

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \epsilon}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the true stress expression.**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F(1+\epsilon)}{A_0} = \sigma (1+\epsilon)$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{true} = \sigma(1+\epsilon)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Derive true strain from incremental definition.**

$$\epsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_0 + \Delta L}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1+\epsilon)$$

$$\boxed{\,\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\epsilon)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the worked example.**
At fracture, $\epsilon = 0.16$. So:

$$\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + 0.16) = \ln(1.16) = 0.148$$

Engineering stress at fracture (assuming fracture load equals $F_{max} = 78{,}000$ N at the necked section, with $A_f = 63.62$ mm²):

$$\sigma_{true,\,fracture} = \frac{78{,}000}{63.62} = 1226 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the *true* fracture stress, far higher than the engineering σuts of 636 MPa. The difference reflects the severity of necking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mechanical Properties Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> B[Elastic Properties]:::branch
    A --> C[Strength Properties]:::branch

    A --> G[Fracture Properties]:::branch

    B --> B1[Young Modulus E]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Shear Modulus G]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Bulk Modulus K]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Poisson Ratio nu]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Yield Strength]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Ultimate Tensile Strength]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Compressive Strength]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Percent Elongation]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Percent Reduction in Area]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Strain Hardening Exponent]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Brinell BHN]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Rockwell HRC]:::leaf
    E --> E3[Vickers HV]:::leaf

    F --> F1[Fatigue Strength]:::leaf

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Stress-Strain Curve Interpretation (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[UTM Test Data]:::input --> P2[Convert F to Stress sigma]:::proc
    P2 --> P3[Convert DeltaL to Strain epsilon]:::proc
    P3 --> P4[Plot sigma vs epsilon]:::proc
    P4 --> P5{Linear Region?}:::decision
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Slope equals E]:::leaf
    P5 -- No --> P7[Plastic Region Analysis]:::proc
    P7 --> P8[Identify Yield Point]:::proc
    P8 --> P9[Find Peak Stress equals sigma uts]:::proc
    P9 --> P10[Locate Necking Region]:::proc
    P10 --> P11[Fracture Point]:::leaf
    P11 --> P12[Compute Percent Elongation and Reduction in Area]:::proc
    P12 --> P13[Compute True Stress and True Strain]:::proc
    P13 --> P14[DFMA Material Selection Decision]:::output

    classDef input fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333
    classDef proc fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333

```

```mermaid

    R3 --> M3[Fatigue and Creep Properties]:::map
```
