```mermaid
    classDef out fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Text-Based, Since Physical Drawings Cannot Be Mermaid-Rendered)

The **σ-ε Curve** for a ductile low-carbon steel has the following identifiable points (left to right):

- **O → A (Proportional Limit, σp):** Linear region, slope = E.
- **A → B (Elastic Limit):** Slight non-linearity, fully recoverable.
- **B → C (Upper Yield Point):** Stress drops at constant strain.
- **C → D (Lower Yield Point):** Lüders band propagation.
- **D → E (Strain Hardening Region):** Stress rises with plastic strain.
- **E (Ultimate Point, σuts):** Maximum engineering stress.
- **E → F (Necking Region):** Localized reduction in cross-section.
- **F (Fracture Point):** Material breaks; record final dimensions for ductility.

For a **brittle material** (e.g., gray cast iron), the curve is *linear* up to fracture with no plastic region. For a **perfectly elastic material** (e.g., rubber), the curve is *non-linear* but fully recoverable. For a **perfectly plastic material**, σ is constant (σ = σy) for all ε > 0 after yield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the KTU exam:** You may be asked to *sketch and label* the σ-ε curve. Always show: O, A (proportional limit), B (yield), C (ultimate), D (fracture), and shade the resilience area and toughness area distinctly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with their standard units:**
(a) Young's Modulus
(b) Modulus of Resilience
(c) Fracture Toughness

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Young's Modulus (E):**
It is the slope of the linear elastic portion of the stress-strain curve, defined as the ratio of uniaxial stress to the corresponding strain below the proportional limit. It measures the *stiffness* of a material.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L / L_0}$$

**Units:** **GPa** (Gigapascals) or **N/m²** in SI. For steels, $E \approx 200$ GPa; for aluminium alloys, $E \approx 70$ GPa.

**(b) Modulus of Resilience (Ur):**
It is the strain energy stored per unit volume in a material up to the elastic limit (or yield point). It is numerically equal to the area under the linear elastic portion of the σ-ε curve.

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$$

**Units:** **MJ/m³** or **MPa** (since 1 MPa = 1 MJ/m³).

**(c) Fracture Toughness (KIC):**
It is the critical value of the stress intensity factor at the tip of a sharp crack below which the crack will not propagate, causing brittle fracture. It quantifies a material's resistance to crack growth.

$$K_{IC} = Y\,\sigma_c\,\sqrt{\pi\,a_c}$$

**Units:** **MPa·√m**. For example, $K_{IC}$ for aluminium alloys is ~22–35 MPa·√m, while for maraging steels it can exceed 100 MPa·√m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between "ductility" and "brittleness" with suitable examples and σ-ε sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Plastic Deformation** | Significant plastic deformation before fracture | Negligible plastic deformation |
| **% Elongation** | High (typically > 5%) | Very low (< 2–3%) |
| **% Reduction in Area** | High (e.g., 30–60%) | Negligible |
| **Fracture Type** | Ductile (cup-and-cone) with necking | Cleavage (flat, granular surface) |
| **Energy Absorbed** | High (high toughness) | Low |
| **Examples** | Low-carbon steel, copper, aluminium | Gray cast iron, glass, ceramics |
| **σ-ε Curve** | Has a large plastic region | Linear up to fracture |
| **DFMA Implication** | Suitable for forging, rolling, extrusion, deep drawing | Suitable only for casting, grinding; not for forming |

> Ductility is measured by **%EL** and **%RA**; brittleness is the *absence* of ductility. A brittle material fractures at or near its elastic limit, while a ductile material yields and then undergoes significant plastic flow.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You attempt **one** of the two given options. Each option is 14 marks, typically split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** A cylindrical specimen of a ductile metal alloy with an original diameter of 10 mm and gauge length of 50 mm is tested in tension. The following data are recorded:

| Load (N) | Length (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 50.000 |
| 7,854 | 50.025 |
| 15,708 | 50.050 |
| 23,562 | 50.075 |
| 31,416 | 50.100 |
| 35,343 (yield) | 50.250 |
| 39,270 (max) | 53.000 |
| 35,000 (fracture) | 56.500 |

Compute:
1. Modulus of Elasticity, $E$
2. Yield Strength, $\sigma_y$
3. Ultimate Tensile Strength, $\sigma_{uts}$
4. % Elongation at fracture
5. Modulus of Resilience, $U_r$
6. Modulus of Toughness, $U_t$ (use approximate graphical integration: assume the plastic region average stress is 320 MPa over $\Delta\epsilon = 0.13$)

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define "Modulus of Toughness." How is it determined from the stress-strain curve? Why is it an important design parameter in DFMA? Explain with two engineering examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (10)^2 = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute stress and strain for the first elastic points.**

For Load = 7,854 N, Length = 50.025 mm:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{7{,}854}{78.54} = 100 \text{ MPa}, \quad \epsilon_1 = \frac{0.025}{50.000} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

For Load = 15,708 N, Length = 50.050 mm:

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{15{,}708}{78.54} = 200 \text{ MPa}, \quad \epsilon_2 = \frac{0.050}{50.000} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

The ratio $\sigma/\epsilon$ is constant (= 200,000 MPa = 200 GPa) for the first three points, confirming linear elastic behavior.

**1. Modulus of Elasticity:**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{200 \text{ MPa}}{0.001} = \boxed{200{,}000 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ GPa}}$$

**2. Yield Strength (load = 35,343 N):**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{35{,}343}{78.54} = \boxed{450 \text{ MPa}}$$

**3. Ultimate Tensile Strength (max load = 39,270 N):**

$$\sigma_{uts} = \frac{39{,}270}{78.54} = \boxed{500 \text{ MPa}}$$

**4. % Elongation at fracture (final length = 56.500 mm):**

$$\%EL = \frac{56.500 - 50.000}{50.000} \times 100 = \boxed{13.0\%}$$

**5. Modulus of Resilience:**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} = \frac{(450)^2}{2 \times 200{,}000} = \frac{202{,}500}{400{,}000} = \boxed{0.506 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$$

**6. Modulus of Toughness (approximate):**
Elastic area + plastic area:

$$U_t = U_r + (\text{average plastic stress}) \times (\text{plastic strain})$$

$$U_t = 0.506 + 320 \times 0.13 = 0.506 + 41.6 = \boxed{42.1 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$$

---

**Part (b) — Theoretical Answer**

**Definition:**
The **Modulus of Toughness (Ut)** is the *total* energy per unit volume that a material can absorb without fracturing. It is numerically equal to the *entire area* under the stress-strain curve (from zero strain up to the fracture strain $\epsilon_f$).

$$U_t = \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \, d\epsilon$$

**Determination from σ-ε curve:**
- Plot the engineering σ-ε curve up to fracture.
- Use **graphical integration** (count squares, planimeter, Simpson's rule) to find the area.
- Or use the approximate method: $U_t \approx \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_y + \sigma_{uts}) \times \epsilon_f$ (trapezoidal rule).
- The SI unit is **MJ/m³** (= MPa).

**Why it matters in DFMA:**

**Example 1 — Automotive Bumper / Crumple Zone:**
A car bumper must absorb crash energy *plastically* (not elastically) to protect occupants. A high $U_t$ material (e.g., mild steel with $U_t \approx 100$ MJ/m³) is preferred over a high-strength, low-toughness material (e.g., martensitic steel with $U_t < 20$ MJ/m³), because the latter would shatter on impact.

**Example 2 — Press-Formed Sheet Metal Panel:**
When a sheet metal panel is stamped, it undergoes large plastic deformation. If $U_t$ is too low, the sheet will crack during forming. The DFMA selection rule is: choose a material with $U_t \geq 50$ MJ/m³ for complex deep-drawing operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Many students confuse **resilience** with **toughness**. Resilience is *elastic* energy only (area up to yield). Toughness is *total* energy to fracture (area up to fracture). Marks are deducted for confusing these.
> (2) In numerical problems, students often forget to **convert kN to N** or **mm² to m²** before plugging into formulas. Always check unit consistency.
> (3) For the % Elongation calculation, use $L_f$ (final length at fracture), *not* the length at $F_{max}$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define the following elastic constants: Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's Ratio (ν). Derive the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ for an isotropic material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A material has $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$. Compute:
1. Shear Modulus, G
2. Bulk Modulus, K
3. The lateral strain when an axial stress of 100 MPa is applied
4. The volumetric strain under a hydrostatic pressure of 50 MPa
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theory + Derivation**

**Definitions:**

- **Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of uniaxial tensile (or compressive) stress to the corresponding axial strain, within the elastic limit. Indicates axial stiffness.
- **Shear Modulus (G):** The ratio of shear stress $\tau$ to shear strain $\gamma$ in the elastic range. Indicates resistance to shape change.
- **Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of hydrostatic pressure to the volumetric strain (with a negative sign). Indicates resistance to volume change.
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** The negative of the ratio of lateral strain to axial strain in a uniaxial tensile test. Typically 0.25–0.35 for most metals.

**Derivation of E = 2G(1 + ν):** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full 8-step derivation. The key steps are:)

1. Apply uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$ and use Hooke's Law for 3D to get $\epsilon_x$ and $\epsilon_y$.
2. Equate pure shear $\tau_{xy}$ to a biaxial stress state: $\sigma_a = +\tau$, $\sigma_b = -\tau$ on the 45°-rotated axes.
3. Compute principal strains $\epsilon_a$ and $\epsilon_b$ from generalized Hooke's Law.
4. The shear strain is $\gamma_{xy} = \epsilon_a - \epsilon_b = \dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$.
5. By definition $\tau = G\gamma_{xy}$, so $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$, which rearranges to:

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1+\nu)\,}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $E = 210$ GPa = $210 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$.

**1. Shear Modulus:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{210}{2.60} = \boxed{80.77 \text{ GPa}}$$

**2. Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{210}{1.20} = \boxed{175.0 \text{ GPa}}$$

**3. Lateral strain at $\sigma_{axial} = 100$ MPa:**

Axial strain:

$$\epsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma_{axial}}{E} = \frac{100}{210{,}000} = 4.762 \times 10^{-4}$$

Lateral strain:

$$\epsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \epsilon_{axial} = -0.30 \times 4.762 \times 10^{-4} = \boxed{-1.429 \times 10^{-4}}$$

(Negative sign indicates contraction.)

**4. Volumetric strain at hydrostatic pressure $p = 50$ MPa:**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = -\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{50}{175{,}000} = \boxed{-2.857 \times 10^{-4}}$$

(Negative sign indicates volume decrease under pressure.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Question B):**
> (1) The relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ is *valid only for isotropic materials*. Do not apply it to fiber-reinforced composites or wood, which are anisotropic.
> (2) In the Bulk Modulus formula $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$, watch the denominator carefully: $1 - 2\nu$, **not** $1 - 2\nu$ with sign errors. As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ (incompressible limit) — a useful sanity check.
> (3) In Q3, the *lateral* strain has the *opposite sign* of axial strain for positive $\nu$. Forgetting the negative sign will cost you 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical Properties of Materials**

- [ ] The **σ-ε curve** is the master plot; every property can be derived from it.
- [ ] **Young's Modulus (E)** = slope of linear elastic region. *Stiffness*, not strength.
- [ ] **Yield Strength (σy)** = stress at which plastic deformation begins (0.2% offset method if no clear yield point).
- [ ] **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σuts)** = maximum engineering stress; after this, necking begins.
- [ ] **Engineering vs. True:** Engineering uses $A_0$ (original area); True uses $A_i$ (instantaneous area). True stress/strain is *always* higher in the plastic region.
- [ ] **Ductility** = %EL + %RA. Higher = more formable, better for **DFMA** shaping processes.
- [ ] **Hardness** correlates *empirically* with tensile strength. For steels: $\sigma_{uts} \text{ (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$.
- [ ] **Resilience** = elastic energy storage capacity. Critical for springs, impact absorbers.
- [ ] **Toughness** = total energy to fracture. Critical for crashworthiness, dynamic loading.
- [ ] **Fatigue** = failure under cyclic loading. For steels, $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_{uts}$ (endurance limit). For non-ferrous metals, fatigue strength keeps decreasing with cycles.
- [ ] **Creep** = time-dependent deformation at high temperature (T > 0.4 × T_melting). Critical for turbines, boilers.
- [ ] **Fracture Toughness (KIC)** = resistance to crack propagation. Critical for aerospace, pressure vessels, brittle materials.
- [ ] **Elastic constant relations:** $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$. These are inter-related, not independent.
- [ ] **Poisson's Ratio range:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ (theoretical); $0 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable materials; $\nu \approx 0.5$ for incompressible materials (rubber).
- [ ] **DFMA Perspective:** Material selection for *manufacturability* (castability, formability, machinability, weldability) and *assemblability* (snap-fit, weldability, fastener compatibility) is *governed* by mechanical properties.

> **Last Word for KTU 2024 Exam Aspirants:**
> When a question on mechanical properties appears, *always* start with the σ-ε curve. It is the single visual that earns you the most marks because it allows you to anchor *every* property in its correct region of the curve. Examiners reward clarity and structure — draw the curve, label the axes, label the key points, and only then proceed to the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Materials and Material Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Materials and Material Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Material Selection** is a systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process in engineering design that identifies the optimal material (or combination of materials) from a defined universe of candidates to satisfy a product's functional, manufacturing, economic, and lifecycle requirements.

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, material selection is the **second upstream activity** (after conceptual design and before process planning) that simultaneously constrains:
- The **manufacturing process** (e.g., polymers favor injection moulding, metals favor casting/machining).
- The **joining/assembly technique** (e.g., welding for steels, snap-fits for polymers).
- The **DFM/DFA indices** (number of parts, ease of handling, fastenable features).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Material selection in DFMA is *not* a stand-alone materials science exercise — it is a **co-design** activity with the geometry and the process. Choosing CFRP commits you to lay-up/filament-winding; choosing ABS commits you to mould/tooling cost.

### 1.2 The Four Functional Requirements of a Material
Every engineering material in service must simultaneously satisfy four roles:

| Role | Property Type | Example Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Stiffness, strength, toughness | $E$, $\sigma_y$, $K_{IC}$ |
| **Thermal** | Conduction, expansion, capacity | $k$, $\alpha$, $C_p$ |
| **Chemical** | Corrosion, oxidation, degradation | Corrosion rate, $E_{corr}$ |
| **Manufacturing (Processability)** | Castability, machinability, formability | Mouldability index, chip morphology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defn — The Performance Equation:**
> A generic design specification is expressed as
> $$P = f(\text{Function},\ \text{Geometry},\ \text{Material})$$
> *P* (performance, e.g. stiffness *S* = F/δ) is split into three groups: **F** (functional demands — loads, temperature), **G** (geometric parameters — length, area), and **M** (material properties). Ashby's method exploits this decoupling.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Material Selection Kitchen"
Imagine you are a **chef** (designer) cooking a dish (product) for guests (the market). You have a **pantry** of ingredients (materials). The recipe demands *crispy outside, juicy inside* (stiffness *and* toughness). You would:
1. **Translate** the dish into attributes (crispiness → high surface modulus, juiciness → thermal resistance).
2. **Screen** the pantry (no flour if the recipe is gluten-free).
3. **Rank** the survivors (compare price, taste, cook-ability).
4. **Document** the choice (write the recipe card).

This is *exactly* the **Ashby four-step methodology** applied to materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Defn — Material Property Chart (Ashby Chart):**
> A log-log scatter plot of one material property (e.g. modulus, *E*) versus another (e.g. density, $\rho$). Each point represents a material; regions of families (foams, polymers, metals, ceramics, composites) appear as labelled *islands*. Used to visually screen and rank materials.

### 1.4 Classification of Engineering Materials (KTU-relevant)
Engineering materials are broadly grouped into **four families**, plus the modern fifth:

1. **Metals & Alloys** (Fe-alloys, Al-alloys, Ti, Cu) — high $E$, high $\sigma_y$, ductile, conductive.
2. **Polymers** (Thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers) — low $\rho$, corrosion resistant, mouldable.
3. **Ceramics & Glasses** (Al$_2$O$_3$, SiC, SiO$_2$) — very high $E$ & hardness, brittle, high-temperature.
4. **Composites** (CFRP, GFRP, MMC) — *engineered* hybrids; tailored directional properties.
5. **Hybrids / Multi-materials** (sandwich panels, foams) — exploit geometry + material jointly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ashby's Methodology — The Four Steps (Deep Dive)

**Step 1 — Translation of Design Requirements**
The design brief (e.g. "a light, stiff, cheap handle") is converted into:
- **Function**: what the part must *do* (carry load, insulate, conduct heat).
- **Constraints**: bounds on mass, cost, dimensions, temperature, environment.
- **Objectives**: maximize stiffness-to-mass, minimize cost, maximize fatigue life.
- **Free variables**: dimensions the designer is free to choose.

**Step 2 — Screening using Material Property Charts**
Eliminate materials that fail the **constraints** (e.g. if $T_{max} > 250^{\circ}C$, eliminate all polymers). Performed visually on an Ashby chart by drawing constraint boxes.

**Step 3 — Ranking using the Material Index (M$_I$)**
Among the survivors, rank by a single scalar — the **Material Index** — derived from the performance equation. The material with the highest $M_I$ (for a *maximize* objective) is the optimal.

**Step 4 — Documentation & Supporting Information**
Cross-check with secondary criteria not captured by $M_I$: availability, manufacturability, environmental impact, joining with adjacent parts, recycling pathway.

### 2.2 Deriving a Material Index — The Logic Flow
Given a performance equation, the procedure is:

1. Write the **performance equation** $P = f(F, G, M)$.
2. **Decouple** the three groups.
3. Identify the **objective** (minimize mass, maximize stiffness, etc.).
4. Identify the **free variables** (usually geometric — length, thickness).
5. **Eliminate** free variables using the *objective* and the *stiffness/strength constraint* (this is the key step — derivatives or constraints).
6. The remaining expression contains only material properties — that is the **Material Index**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Design Scenario | Functional Requirement | Geometric Form | Material Index (Maximize) | Index Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Light, **stiff** beam | $S = F/\delta$ (specified) | Solid square $b \times b$ | $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{E}}{\rho}$ |
| 2 | Light, **stiff** beam | $S$ (specified) | Thin tube ($d, t$) | $\frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | $\frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ |
| 3 | Light, **strong** beam | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_y$ (no yield) | Solid square | $\frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | $\frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ |
| 4 | Light, **strong** column | Euler buckling $P = P_{cr}$ | Thin tube | $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{E}}{\rho}$ |
| 5 | Light **spring** | Strain energy stored, $\delta$ fixed | Solid rod | $\frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{E \cdot \rho}$ | $\frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{E\rho}$ |
| 6 | **Heat sink** (min mass, fixed $Q$) | Fourier conduction | Fin/plate | $\frac{k}{\rho}$ | $\frac{k}{\rho}$ |
| 7 | **Thermal shock** resistance | $\Delta T$ fracture | Bulk ceramic | $\frac{\sigma_f \cdot k}{E \cdot \alpha}$ | $\frac{\sigma_f k}{E\alpha}$ |
| 8 | **Thermal distortion** (min) | $\varepsilon_{th}$ fixed | Bulk | $\frac{k}{\alpha}$ | $\frac{k}{\alpha}$ |
| 9 | **Torsion bar** (min mass, fixed twist $\phi$) | $GJ/L$ | Solid round | $\frac{G^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{G}}{\rho}$ |
| 10 | **Flywheel** (max KE/mass) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Disc | $\frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The three families (foams, polymers, metals, ceramics) form distinct *islands* on a $E$-$\rho$ chart. Composites occupy the **upper-left** (high $E$, low $\rho$) — the coveted "stiff-and-light" region. When the index $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ is plotted, the **best families** are at the top-left.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
| Industry | Material Selection Driver | Typical Index Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** (wing spar, fuselage) | Mass + stiffness + fatigue | $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ / $\frac{\sigma_f^{2/3}}{\rho}$ |
| **Automotive** (chassis, panels) | Mass + cost + formability | $\frac{\sigma_f}{\rho \cdot C_m}$ (cost-adjusted) |
| **Electronics** (heat sinks, PCB substrates) | Thermal dissipation | $\frac{k}{\rho}$ / $\frac{k}{\alpha}$ |
| **Biomedical implants** (hip stem) | Biocompatibility + fatigue | $\frac{\sigma_f^{3/2}}{E^{1/2} \cdot \rho}$ |
| **Civil structures** (I-beams) | Stiffness + low cost | $\frac{E}{\rho \cdot C_m}$ |

### 2.5 Design for Manufacture (DFM) Constraints on Material Choice
Material choice *drives* process choice. The matrix below is **high-yield for KTU**:

| Material | Compatible DFM Processes | Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| ABS / PP | Injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding | Heavy machining, welding |
| Mild steel | Stamping, casting, machining, welding | — (versatile) |
| Aluminium | Die casting, extrusion, CNC machining | Spark-erosion (slow) |
| CFRP | Lay-up, filament winding, RTM | Conventional machining (abrasive) |
| Magnesium | Die casting, thixoforming | Hot machining |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The three flagship derivations below are KTU's most-tested problems on this topic.** Master the procedure, not just the result.

---

### 3.1 Derivation A — Material Index for a Light, Stiff Beam (Solid Square Cross-Section)

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, square cross-section of side $b$, must support an end-point load $F$ with a maximum tip deflection $\delta \le \delta^*$. Determine the material index for **minimum mass**.

#### Step 1 — Write the deflection equation
For a cantilever with end point load, the tip deflection is:
$$\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

For a square cross-section of side $b$:
$$I = \frac{b^4}{12}$$

Substituting $I$:
$$\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E \left( b^4 / 12 \right)} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \, b^4}$$

*Comment:* This step links the functional requirement (deflection) to the material property ($E$) and the geometry ($b$).

#### Step 2 — Apply the stiffness constraint
Set $\delta = \delta^*$ (the design limit). Solve for $b^4$:
$$b^4 = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta^*}$$

Take the fourth root to get the free geometric variable $b$:
$$b = \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta^*} \right)^{1/4}$$

*Comment:* $b$ is the only **free** geometric variable; $L$, $F$, $\delta^*$ are fixed by the brief.

#### Step 3 — Write the mass of the beam
Mass = density × volume:
$$m = \rho \, V = \rho \, b^2 \, L$$

#### Step 4 — Eliminate $b$ by substitution
Substitute $b$ from Step 2 into the mass equation:
$$m = \rho L \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta^*} \right)^{1/2}$$

Rearrange to group the constant, the geometric/functional part, and the material part:
$$m = \underbrace{\left( \frac{4 F L^5}{\delta^*} \right)^{1/2}}_{\text{Geometry + Function}} \cdot \rho \cdot E^{-1/2}$$

#### Step 5 — Identify the material index
Since the first factor contains only design-given quantities ($F, L, \delta^*$), to **minimize $m$** we must minimize:
$$\rho \cdot E^{-1/2} = \frac{\rho}{\sqrt{E}}$$

Equivalently, we **maximize** the reciprocal:
$$\boxed{\,M_I = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}\,}$$

#### Step 6 — Physical interpretation
- A high $E$ (stiffness) is good — it lets $b$ shrink, cutting mass.
- A low $\rho$ is good — less weight per volume.
- The exponent $1/2$ on $E$ is *sub-linear*: doubling $E$ saves $\sqrt{2}$ in mass — but doubling $E$ typically costs far more than $2\times$ in price. This is why **foams and CFRP** (high $E/\rho$) win in aerospace, but **steel** (cheaper $E/\rho$ ratio) wins in civil construction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Material Index for a Light, Strong Beam (Solid Square Cross-Section)

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, square cross-section of side $b$, supports a tip load $F$. The maximum bending stress must not exceed the yield strength $\sigma_y$. Determine the material index for **minimum mass**.

#### Step 1 — Maximum bending stress
For a square cross-section of side $b$, the section modulus is:
$$Z = \frac{b^3}{6}$$

Maximum bending moment at the wall: $M = F L$. So:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 F L}{b^3}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the strength constraint
Set $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_y$ (no yielding permitted):
$$\sigma_y = \frac{6 F L}{b^3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad b^3 = \frac{6 F L}{\sigma_y}$$

Solve for $b$:
$$b = \left( \frac{6 F L}{\sigma_y} \right)^{1/3}$$

#### Step 3 — Mass equation
$$m = \rho \, b^2 \, L = \rho \, L \left( \frac{6 F L}{\sigma_y} \right)^{2/3}$$

#### Step 4 — Group the terms
$$m = \underbrace{\left( 6 F L^5 \right)^{2/3}}_{\text{Geometry + Function}} \cdot \rho \cdot \sigma_y^{-2/3}$$

#### Step 5 — Material index
To minimize $m$, minimize $\rho \cdot \sigma_y^{-2/3}$, or equivalently:
$$\boxed{\,M_I = \frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}\,}$$

#### Step 6 — Comparison
| Index | Optimizes For | Aerospace Winner |
|---|---|---|
| $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | Stiffness at minimum mass | CFRP, Al-Li |
| $\frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | Strength at minimum mass | Maraging steel, Ti-6Al-4V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $M_I = \sigma_y / \rho$ for *all* strength problems. This is **wrong** when the free variable is a *section dimension* — the exponent drops to $2/3$ (square) or $1/2$ (thin tube).

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Material Index for a Light Column Resisting Euler Buckling (Thin-Walled Tube)

**Problem Statement:** A thin-walled tubular column of length $L$, mean diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$ ($t \ll d$) carries an axial compressive load $P$ with $P = P_{cr}$ (the Euler buckling load). Find the material index for minimum mass.

#### Step 1 — Euler critical load
For a pin-ended column:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

For a thin-walled tube:
$$I \approx \frac{\pi d^3 t}{8}$$

So:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^3 E d^3 t}{8 L^2}$$

#### Step 2 — Mass of the column
Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi d t$. Volume: $V = A L = \pi d t L$.
$$m = \rho \, \pi d t L$$

#### Step 3 — Two free variables, one constraint
We have **two** free variables ($d$ and $t$) and **one** constraint (Step 1). We need an *additional* equation to eliminate both.

**Trick (Ashby):** For a minimum-mass design, the two should be chosen so that one is just sufficient. We treat $d$ as the free parameter and express $t$ from the constraint:
$$t = \frac{8 P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^3 E d^3}$$

#### Step 4 — Substitute into mass
$$m = \rho \, \pi L \, d \cdot \left( \frac{8 P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^3 E d^3} \right) = \frac{8 \rho \, P_{cr} L^3}{\pi^2 E d^2}$$

This still contains $d$. To *fully* minimize, we could increase $d$ (reducing mass!) — but $d$ is geometric and bounded by packaging. **Ashby's elegant resolution:** at the optimum, *both* $d$ and $t$ are free and we minimize $m$ with respect to **both**, which yields:
$$d \cdot t = \text{minimum such that } P_{cr} \text{ is met}$$

If both $d$ and $t$ are independently variable, applying a Lagrange multiplier to the full problem gives:
$$m = \left( \frac{8 P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^2} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \left( \frac{2 P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^2} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}$$

Leading (after proper treatment) to the standard index:
$$\boxed{\,M_I = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** The same index $\frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ results from buckling of a solid square column. The exponent $1/3$ appears when only *one* free variable exists (e.g. a solid rod where $d$ is fixed by the constraint and you cannot further optimize).

---

### 3.4 The Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Workbook)
A *systematic* material selection flow integrates:

| Decision Stage | Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Translate** | Free-body, load spectrum | Function, constraints, objectives |
| **2. Screen** | Ashby charts, property tables | Reduced candidate set (e.g. 6 materials) |
| **3. Rank** | Material index $M_I$ | Ordered ranking (1, 2, 3, …) |
| **4. Document** | CES (Cambridge Eng. Selector) | Final choice + back-up |
| **5. Cross-check** | DFM/DFA process compatibility | Confirm part is makeable & assemble-able |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Compliance:** Part A carries 3 marks each (short answer). Part B is 14 marks with **internal choice** (EITHER/OR). All questions below are mapped to the KTU 2024 Bloom's taxonomy weightage (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate levels for 14-mark problems).

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define material index. List three common material indices used in engineering design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *material index* is a grouping of material properties (e.g. $E^{1/2}/\rho$) that must be **maximized or minimized** to obtain the best performance of a component for a given design requirement. It is derived by decoupling the performance equation into functional, geometric, and material terms, then eliminating the free geometric variable.

Three common material indices:
1. **Light, stiff beam:** $M_I = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$
2. **Light, strong beam:** $M_I = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$
3. **Heat sink (min mass, fixed heat flow):** $M_I = \dfrac{k}{\rho}$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**State the four steps in Ashby's material selection methodology. Mention the purpose of the "Translation" step.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four steps are:
1. **Translation** — Convert the design brief into a specification: function, constraints, objectives, free variables.
2. **Screening** — Eliminate materials that violate constraints using property charts/tables.
3. **Ranking** — Order the survivors by a material index derived from the performance equation.
4. **Documentation** — Cross-check with secondary criteria (cost, availability, DFM/DFA compatibility) and finalize.

**Purpose of Translation:** It converts a *qualitative* design need (e.g. "a strong, light handle") into a *quantitative* specification (e.g. $F = 200\,\text{N}$, $\delta \le 1\,\text{mm}$, $m \le 50\,\text{g}$, $T_{max} = 60^{\circ}\text{C}$), which is the prerequisite for any analytical selection.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question — Part B]`

**(a)** Explain the role of material selection in Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA). Discuss how material choice influences (i) the manufacturing process, (ii) the number of parts in assembly, and (iii) the joining technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the material index for a light, **stiff** beam of square cross-section. Using this index, rank the four materials in the table below for minimum-mass design of a cantilever beam ($L = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $F = 500\,\text{N}$, $\delta^* = 2\,\text{mm}$). **(7 marks)**

| Material | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|---|
| Steel | 200 | 7850 |
| Aluminium 6061 | 70 | 2700 |
| CFRP | 140 | 1600 |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 110 | 4500 |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Role of Material Selection in DFMA (7 marks):**
Material selection in DFMA is the *bridge* between design intent and manufacturing reality. The role is threefold:

- **(i) Manufacturing process influence:** The selected material dictates the available forming/machining/joining processes. Example: Choosing ABS forces the designer into *injection moulding*; choosing Ti-6Al-4V forces *machining or investment casting*. The same geometry in two different materials leads to *drastically different* tooling cost, cycle time, and surface finish.
- **(ii) Number of parts in assembly:** A versatile material (e.g. steel) can carry mechanical, magnetic, and structural roles — allowing **part consolidation**. A weaker material may force the designer to add fasteners, brackets, and reinforcements — *increasing* the part count, raising the DFA index.
- **(iii) Joining technique:** Material compatibility dictates assembly methods. Polymers favour snap-fits, ultrasonic welding, and adhesives. Metals favour fasteners, welding, and riveting. Dissimilar materials (steel + plastic) limit joining to mechanical fasteners, inflating assembly time and cost.

Thus, material selection is a *cross-cutting decision* in DFMA — it co-evolves with the geometry, the process, and the assembly strategy.

**(b) Derivation and Ranking (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, square cross-section of side $b$, the tip deflection under end load $F$ is:
$$\delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b^4}$$

Setting $\delta = \delta^*$:
$$b^4 = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta^*}$$

Mass: $m = \rho b^2 L$

Substituting $b^2 = (b^4)^{1/2}$:
$$m = \rho L \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E \delta^*} \right)^{1/2} = \left( \frac{4 F L^5}{\delta^*} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \rho \cdot E^{-1/2}$$

To **minimize $m$**, maximize:
$$\boxed{\,M_I = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}\,}$$

**Ranking (substituting the values):**

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m$^3$) | $E^{1/2}$ (GPa$^{1/2}$) | $M_I = E^{1/2}/\rho$ (GPa$^{1/2}$/kg·m$^{-3}$) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CFRP | 140 | 1600 | 11.83 | $7.39 \times 10^{-3}$ | **1** |
| Aluminium 6061 | 70 | 2700 | 8.37 | $3.10 \times 10^{-3}$ | **2** |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 110 | 4500 | 10.49 | $2.33 \times 10^{-3}$ | **3** |
| Steel | 200 | 7850 | 14.14 | $1.80 \times 10^{-3}$ | **4** |

**Conclusion:** CFRP is the best material for minimum-mass, stiff beam design, followed by Aluminium. Steel ranks last because its high density penalises it despite its high modulus.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Use *consistent* units throughout. If $E$ is in GPa ($= 10^9\,\text{Pa}$), keep $\rho$ in kg/m$^3$. *Do not* convert $E$ to Pa and $\rho$ to g/cm$^3$ mid-calculation.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponent:** Many students write $M_I = E/\rho$. The *correct* exponent is $1/2$ for a solid square beam — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation:** A numerical answer without the derivation gets at most 2 of the 7 marks.

---

**Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question — Part B Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of engineering materials into four (or five) families. State two example materials and two distinguishing properties for each family. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat sink must transfer $Q = 100\,\text{W}$ of heat from a CPU in steady state, with the temperature difference $\Delta T$ limited to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. The fin has length $L = 80\,\text{mm}$, area $A = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$ (the area is fixed by packaging). Derive the material index for **minimum mass**. From the candidates below, identify the best material. **(7 marks)**

| Material | Thermal Conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminium 6061 | 167 | 2700 |
| Copper C110 | 388 | 8960 |
| AlSiC (composite) | 180 | 2900 |
| Magnesium AZ91 | 70 | 1800 |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of Engineering Materials (7 marks):**

Engineering materials are grouped by *bond type* and *microstructure* into four (or five) families:

1. **Metals & Alloys** — Metallic bonding; ductile, conductive, high stiffness.
   - *Examples:* Mild steel, Aluminium 6061.
   - *Properties:* $E \approx 70$–$200\,\text{GPa}$; $\sigma_y \approx 100$–$1500\,\text{MPa}$; $\rho \approx 2.7$–$7.8\,\text{g/cm}^3$.

2. **Polymers** — Covalent (within chain) + weak Van der Waals (between chains); low density, corrosion resistant, mouldable.
   - *Examples:* ABS, Polypropylene (PP), Nylon 6,6.
   - *Properties:* $E \approx 1$–$3\,\text{GPa}$; $\rho \approx 0.9$–$1.4\,\text{g/cm}^3$; low melting point.

3. **Ceramics & Glasses** — Ionic / covalent; high hardness, high $E$, brittle, refractory.
   - *Examples:* Al$_2$O$_3$, SiC, Soda-lime glass.
   - *Properties:* $E \approx 200$–$400\,\text{GPa}$; $\rho \approx 2$–$4\,\text{g/cm}^3$; $K_{IC}$ very low (brittle).

4. **Composites** — Two or more phases; tailored directional properties, high specific stiffness.
   - *Examples:* CFRP, GFRP, AlSiC.
   - *Properties:* $E$ (directional, 50–$500\,\text{GPa}$); $\rho$ (1.5–$2.5\,\text{g/cm}^3$); designer's choice of matrix + reinforcement.

5. **Hybrids / Multi-materials** (modern fifth family) — Sandwich panels, foams, lattice structures; exploit both material and geometry.
   - *Examples:* Honeycomb panels, Aluminium foam.
   - *Properties:* Ultra-low density ($\rho < 0.5\,\text{g/cm}^3$) with reasonable stiffness due to geometry.

**(b) Material Index for Heat Sink (7 marks):**

For 1-D steady-state conduction through a plate of area $A$, thickness $L$:
$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta T}{L}$$

The constraint $Q$ is **fixed**; $L$ and $A$ are fixed by packaging; $\Delta T$ is the design limit. **The free variable is the mass.**

Mass:
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

But $A$ and $L$ are fixed, so the *only* way to reduce mass is to reduce $\rho$ *without* compromising $k$. From the conduction equation, the **required** $k$ is:
$$k = \frac{Q L}{A \Delta T}$$

Since $Q$, $L$, $A$, and $\Delta T$ are all fixed, $k$ is fixed. So the *only* design lever is to **minimize $\rho$ for the required $k$**.

For a heat sink, the index that combines high conductivity with low density is:
$$\boxed{\,M_I = \frac{k}{\rho}\,}$$

**Ranking of candidates:**

| Material | $k$ (W/m·K) | $\rho$ (kg/m$^3$) | $M_I = k/\rho$ (W·m/kg·K) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Magnesium AZ91 | 70 | 1800 | $3.89 \times 10^{-2}$ | **1** |
| AlSiC | 180 | 2900 | $6.21 \times 10^{-2}$ | **2 (revised — see below)** |
| Aluminium 6061 | 167 | 2700 | $6.19 \times 10^{-2}$ | **3** |
| Copper C110 | 388 | 8960 | $4.33 \times 10^{-2}$ | **4** |

**Corrected interpretation:** AlSiC tops the ranking because it has slightly higher $k$ than aluminium at near-identical density, giving the highest $k/\rho$. Copper has very high $k$ but its high $\rho$ drops its index to last place. **Magnesium** is best when cost and weight dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection for the given design:** With all four candidates having $k \ge 70\,\text{W/m·K}$, all satisfy the heat-transfer constraint. So we *also* need to check the **required** $k$:
> $$k_{req} = \frac{100 \times 0.08}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} = 32\,\text{W/m·K}$$
> All four pass the screening step. The best material by $M_I = k/\rho$ is **AlSiC**, followed by **Aluminium 6061**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Skipping the constraint check:** Always verify $k_{req} \le k_{candidate}$ *before* ranking. A common error is ranking Mg first without checking if its $k = 70\,\text{W/m·K}$ is sufficient (here it is, but not always).
> 2. **Ignoring secondary criteria:** Mg corrodes easily; Cu is heavy despite high $k$. The "best" material in a *complete* DFMA analysis is rarely the one with the highest $M_I$ — cross-check with manufacturability and cost.
> 3. **Unit error in $M_I$:** $k$ in W/m·K and $\rho$ in kg/m$^3$ gives $M_I$ in W·m/kg·K — *not* dimensionless. Don't strip units for ranking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam.**

- [ ] **Ashby 4 steps:** *Translate → Screen → Rank → Document* — this is the most-tested sequence in KTU 2024 papers.
- [ ] **Performance equation:** $P = f(\text{Function},\ \text{Geometry},\ \text{Material})$ — the basis for deriving any material index.
- [ ] **Two flagship indices to memorize (derivable too):**
  - Light, stiff beam (square): $M_I = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$
  - Light, strong beam (square): $M_I = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$
- [ ] **Exponent traps:**
  - Solid beam: stiffness exponent is $1/2$ on $E$.
  - Solid beam: strength exponent is $2/3$ on $\sigma_y$.
  - Thin tube: stiffness exponent drops to $1/3$ on $E$.
- [ ] **DFMA material-process coupling:** Polymer ⇒ injection moulding; Steel ⇒ stamping/welding; CFRP ⇒ layup/filament winding; Ti ⇒ machining/investment casting.
- [ ] **Ashby chart reading:** Best region for stiffness-to-weight is **upper-left** of $E$-$\rho$ chart (CFRP, Al-Li, Mg alloys). Worst is **lower-right** (cast irons, dense steels).
- [ ] **Always verify units** before computing $M_I$ — the most common KTU error.
- [ ] **For heat sinks**, the index is $k/\rho$; for **thermal shock**, it is $\sigma_f k / (E \alpha)$.
- [ ] **Document step** includes *DFA check* — is the selected material joinable with adjacent parts? (Snapping, welding, adhesives must all be validated.)
- [ ] **One-line exam opener (worth 1 mark):** "Material selection in DFMA is a multi-criteria decision-making process that links design intent, manufacturing process choice, and assembly strategy."
- [ ] **Cambridge Engineering Selector (CES)** is the standard software implementation of Ashby's method — the KTU syllabus expects awareness, not expert use.

> **End of Module 1 — Introduction to Materials and Material Selection Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Engineering Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Engineering Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Materials** are defined as the substances, either naturally occurring or synthetically produced, that possess specific mechanical, physical, chemical, and thermal properties suitable for converting them into engineering components, structures, or systems. The **Classification of Engineering Materials** is the systematic grouping of these substances based on their atomic structure, bonding, composition, and resulting macroscopic properties to facilitate informed **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)** decisions.

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, material classification is not merely an academic exercise — it is the *first cognitive step* a design engineer performs when translating a functional requirement into a manufacturable product. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates that students understand the linkage between material class, manufacturing process selection, and assembly methodology.

### 1.2 Primary Classification Hierarchy

Engineering materials are broadly classified into **five primary families**:

| S.No | Material Family | Atomic Bonding | Typical Property Signature |
|------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Metals & Alloys** | Metallic (delocalized electron sea) | High stiffness, ductile, conductive |
| 2 | **Polymers** | Covalent + Secondary (van der Waals) | Low density, corrosion resistant, insulating |
| 3 | **Ceramics & Glasses** | Ionic / Covalent (mixed) | Hard, brittle, refractory, high melting point |
| 4 | **Composites** | Mixed (depends on matrix & reinforcement) | Tailored anisotropic property profile |
| 5 | **Advanced / Smart Materials** | Variable | Stimuli-responsive (piezoelectric, shape memory, etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFMA-Relevant Insight:** The choice of material class **dictates** the available manufacturing process envelope. For instance, selecting a thermoplastic immediately unlocks injection moulding, extrusion, and thermoforming, while constraining the assembly methods to welding, snap-fits, or adhesive bonding rather than threaded fasteners.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Construction Kit" View

Imagine you are a **chef building a sandwich** to feed a specific customer:
- The **bread** is your **metal chassis** (provides the structural backbone and shape).
- The **cheese slice** is your **polymer coating** (provides flexibility, insulation, and sealing).
- The **lettuce leaves** layered with **chicken** are your **composite laminates** (multiple thin layers combining to give strength in a particular direction).
- The **pickle** (with its sharp, brittle crunch) is your **ceramic** (hard but cracks under sudden impact).

Just as a chef selects ingredients based on *taste, texture, temperature resistance, and cost*, an engineer selects engineering materials based on *strength, ductility, thermal tolerance, manufacturability, and cost*. The **classification system** is the chef's "ingredient catalogue" — it groups materials with similar "culinary behaviours" so that informed substitutions and combinations can be made.

### 1.4 Why Classification Matters in DFMA

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module 1 learning outcome directly states: *"The student should be able to identify, classify, and select engineering materials based on design, manufacturing, and assembly requirements."*

The **three pillars** of DFMA decision-making are:
1. **Design for Manufacture (DFM):** Material must be formable by the chosen process.
2. **Design for Assembly (DFA):** Material must allow fastening, joining, or snap-fit operations.
3. **Cost Optimization:** Material class directly determines raw stock cost, machining cost, and scrap rate.

### 1.5 Key Universal Metrics in Material Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **standard KTU-board-tested properties** that any classification discussion must reference:
> - **Density ($\rho$)** — measured in **kg/m³** or **g/cm³**
> - **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — measured in **GPa**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$)** — measured in **MPa**
> - **Elongation at break ($\% \varepsilon$)** — measured in **%**
> - **Melting Point ($T_m$)** / **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** — measured in **°C**
> - **Hardness (HV / HB / HRC)** — dimensionless scale value
> - **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** — measured in **W/m·K**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Family-Wise Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Metals & Alloys

**Definition:** Crystalline solids characterized by a lattice of positively charged ions immersed in a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons, resulting in the **metallic bond**.

**Sub-Classification:**

- **Ferrous Metals** (iron-based)
  - *Carbon Steels* (Low / Medium / High carbon)
  - *Alloy Steels* (e.g., Cr, Ni, Mo additions → stainless steel, tool steel)
  - *Cast Irons* (Grey, White, Ductile, Malleable)

- **Non-Ferrous Metals**
  - *Light metals:* **Aluminium**, Magnesium, Titanium, Beryllium
  - *Heavy metals:* Copper, Lead, Zinc, Tin, Nickel
  - *Precious metals:* Gold, Silver, Platinum
  - *Refractory metals:* Tungsten, Molybdenum, Niobium
  - *Noble metals:* Platinum group

**Key Properties (Engineering Relevance):**
- **High stiffness:** $E_{steel} \approx 200$ **GPa**
- **High ductility:** $\% \varepsilon$ can exceed 30%
- **Excellent electrical & thermal conductivity** (due to free electrons)
- **Failure mode:** Ductile (yielding) → not sudden

**DFMA Implication:** Machinable, castable, forgeable, weldable. Forms the **backbone of 70%+ of mechanical assemblies** in automotive, aerospace, and heavy machinery.

#### 2.1.2 Polymers (Plastics, Elastomers, Rubbers)

**Definition:** Long-chain organic molecules (macromolecules) with repeating monomer units, bound by **covalent backbone bonds** and held together by weak **secondary (van der Waals / hydrogen) bonds**.

**Sub-Classification:**

- **Thermoplastics** (reversible softening on heating)
  - *Commodity:* PE, PP, PVC, PS
  - *Engineering:* ABS, Nylon (PA), POM (Acetal), PC
  - *High-performance:* PEEK, PPS, PEI (Ultem)

- **Thermosetting Plastics** (irreversible cross-linking)
  - Phenolics (Bakelite), Epoxy, Polyester, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine, Silicones

- **Elastomers** (high elastic recovery)
  - Natural rubber (NR), SBR, NBR, Silicone rubber, Polyurethane

**Key Properties:**
- **Low density:** $\rho \approx 900$ to $1500$ **kg/m³** (1/6 to 1/8 of steel)
- **Low $E$:** typically $1$ to $5$ **GPa**
- **High corrosion resistance**
- **$T_g$ replaces $T_m$** for amorphous thermoplastics (no true melting point)

**DFMA Implication:** Ideal for **high-volume injection moulding**, **blow moulding**, and **extrusion**. Assembly uses **snap-fits**, **ultrasonic welding**, and **adhesive bonding** instead of mechanical fasteners.

#### 2.1.3 Ceramics & Glasses

**Definition:** Inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements, typically bound by a mix of **ionic and covalent bonds**.

**Sub-Classification:**

- **Traditional Ceramics:** Clay-based (bricks, tiles, porcelain), refractories, abrasives (SiC, Al₂O₃), cements, glasses
- **Advanced / Engineering Ceramics:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄), Boron Carbide (B₄C)
- **Glasses:** Soda-lime glass, borosilicate (Pyrex), fused silica, optical fibres

**Key Properties:**
- **Very high stiffness & hardness:** $E_{Al_2O_3} \approx 380$ **GPa**
- **High melting point:** SiC sublimes near $2700$ **°C**
- **Brittle failure:** $\% \varepsilon < 0.5\%$ (no plastic deformation)
- **Excellent chemical inertness**

**DFMA Implication:** Machining is *extremely difficult* (requires diamond tooling or grinding). Forming techniques: **powder compaction + sintering**, **slip casting**, **isostatic pressing**. Assembly is typically by **adhesives**, **shrink-fit**, or **mechanical clamping** (not bolting, as ceramics crack under point loads).

#### 2.1.4 Composites

**Definition:** Engineered materials formed by combining two or more distinct phases (matrix + reinforcement) to achieve properties superior to the individual constituents.

**Sub-Classification:**

- **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC):** Epoxy + Carbon Fibre (CFRP), Glass Fibre (GFRP), Aramid (Kevlar)
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** Al-SiC, Al-Al₂O₃ (used in pistons, brake rotors)
- **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):** SiC-SiC (jet engine components)
- **Natural Composites:** Wood (cellulose + lignin), Bone (hydroxyapatite + collagen)

**Key Properties:**
- **Anisotropic:** Properties differ along fibre direction vs transverse
- **High specific strength:** $\sigma_{UTS}/\rho$ of CFRP can exceed that of steel
- **Tailorable:** Stiffness, strength, and CTE can be engineered

**DFMA Implication:** Composites are typically **near-net-shape manufactured** (filament winding, lay-up, RTM, pultrusion). Machining is limited; assembly uses **adhesive bonding** or **hybrid bolted-bonds** with great care to avoid fibre delamination.

#### 2.1.5 Advanced / Smart / Functional Materials

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA):** Nitinol (Ni-Ti) — recovers shape on heating
- **Piezoelectric materials:** PZT ceramics — generate voltage under stress
- **Magnetostrictive materials:** Terfenol-D — change shape under magnetic field
- **Biomaterials:** Ti-alloys, PEEK, hydroxyapatite — biocompatible for implants
- **Nanomaterials:** Graphene, CNTs, nano-clays — extreme surface-to-volume ratio
- **Bulk Metallic Glasses (BMG):** Amorphous metals — high strength + elasticity

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations and property definitions form the **analytical core** of any KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question on material classification.

| Property / Concept | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | Mass estimation, lightweighting |
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | Pa (or MPa) | Load-bearing capacity |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Deformation measure |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (in elastic region) | Pa (or GPa) | Stiffness of material |
| Specific Stiffness | $E/\rho$ | ratio | GPa·cm³/g | Weight-critical selection (aerospace) |
| Specific Strength | $\sigma_{UTS}/\rho$ | ratio | MPa·cm³/g | Lightweight design |
| Toughness (Modulus of Resilience) | $U$ | $U = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | J/m³ | Energy absorption before yield |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ | dimensionless | Lateral strain under axial load |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | GPa | Torsional stiffness |
| Rule of Mixtures (Composite Stiffness, Voigt) | $E_c$ | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Upper-bound estimate for composites |
| Rule of Mixtures (Composite Density) | $\rho_c$ | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | kg/m³ | Composite mass estimation |
| Ashby's Material Index (for a light stiff tie) | $M$ | $M = E/\rho$ or $E^{1/3}/\rho$ | varies | Material ranking for design |
| CTE (linear) | $\alpha$ | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | 1/°C | Thermal expansion prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** Within the KTU Formula Sheet, every mathematical absolute-value and modulus expression has been rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to prevent corruption of markdown table syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields

- **Aerospace:** Dominated by **Al-alloys**, **Ti-alloys**, and **CFRP composites** — the Boeing 787 Dreamliner is ~50% composite by mass.
- **Automotive:** AHSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel) bodies, Al-alloy engine blocks, polymer dashboards, MMC brake rotors.
- **Biomedical:** Ti-6Al-4V implants, PEEK spinal cages, ZrO₂ dental crowns.
- **Electronics:** Cu conductors, polymer PCB substrates, ceramic capacitor dielectrics (BaTiO₃), Si semiconductors.
- **Energy:** Ni-based superalloys in gas turbines, ceramic thermal barrier coatings (TBCs), polymer electrolyte membranes in fuel cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Engineering Stress, Strain, and Young's Modulus

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **gateway derivation** for all KTU mechanics-of-materials questions. Examiners frequently award full marks only when the student *writes out* the steps below.

**Step 1 — Setup: Define the test specimen and applied load.**

Consider a cylindrical test specimen of original cross-sectional area $A_0$ and original gauge length $L_0$. An axial tensile force $F$ is applied along its longitudinal axis.

**Step 2 — Define Engineering Stress.**

Engineering stress is defined as the *load per unit original cross-sectional area* (note: it uses $A_0$, not the instantaneous area).

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Engineering Strain.**

Engineering strain is the *fractional change in length* relative to the original gauge length.

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Establish the linear elastic relationship (Hooke's Law).**

For most engineering materials, within the elastic limit, stress and strain are linearly proportional.

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for Young's Modulus.**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Express the dimensions and unit of $E$.**

$$
[E] = \frac{[\text{Pa}]}{[\text{dimensionless}]} = \text{Pa}
$$

In practice, $E$ is typically expressed in **GPa** ($1$ GPa $= 10^9$ Pa).

**Step 7 — Substituting the original definitions back:**

$$
E = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L/L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}
$$

This final form is the basis of the *tensile test data reduction* — the slope of the linear portion of a stress–strain curve directly yields $E$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Fibre-Reinforced Composites

**Step 1 — Concept Setup.**

Consider a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded *parallel* to the fibre direction. The composite, fibre, and matrix share the *same strain* (iso-strain assumption, valid when the bond is perfect).

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m
$$

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium.**

The total load on the composite is shared between the fibre and the matrix:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

**Step 3 — Express each force as stress times area.**

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce volume fractions.**

Let $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c$, with $V_f + V_m = 1$.

Dividing both sides by $A_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law to each phase.**

Since $\varepsilon$ is the same throughout:

$$
\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and factor out $\varepsilon$.**

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f V_f \varepsilon + E_m V_m \varepsilon
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

This is the **Voigt upper-bound** (iso-strain) estimate of composite stiffness along the fibre direction. It is the most commonly tested KTU result for composite mechanics.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Material Selection for a Lightweight Stiff Tie

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic **Ashby material selection** problem frequently appearing in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Problem Statement:** A tensile tie in a satellite structure requires high stiffness (minimum $E = 70$ GPa) with the **minimum possible mass**. The cross-section is fixed at $A = 50$ mm² and the length at $L = 1$ m. Using the Ashby approach, rank the candidate materials.

**Step 1 — Establish the functional requirement.**

The tie must not elongate more than $\delta_{max} = 1$ mm under load $F$. The stiffness requirement:

$$
k_{req} = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E \cdot A}{L}
$$

For a given $A$ and $L$, the required $E$ is fixed — but we want to **minimize mass**.

**Step 2 — Express mass in terms of material properties.**

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

Since $A$ and $L$ are fixed geometrically, minimizing $m$ means minimizing $\rho$. But $E$ must also be satisfied. The combined material index is:

$$
M = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(or higher powers depending on objective)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E/\rho$ for typical candidates.**

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $E/\rho$ (GPa·cm³/g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel (1020) | 200 | 7850 | 25.5 |
| Aluminium (6061) | 69 | 2700 | 25.6 |
| Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) | 114 | 4430 | 25.7 |
| GFRP (unidirectional) | 38 | 1800 | 21.1 |
| CFRP (unidirectional) | 135 | 1600 | 84.4 |
| Magnesium (AZ31) | 45 | 1740 | 25.9 |

**Step 4 — Apply the index constraint.**

Since the $E$ requirement is 70 GPa:
- **Steel, Ti-6Al-4V, and CFRP** satisfy $E \geq 70$ GPa.
- Among these, **CFRP** has the highest $E/\rho$ → it minimizes mass.
- If cost is a constraint, **Al 6061** (with $E = 69$ GPa, just at the boundary) is the cheapest metallic option.

**Step 5 — DFMA decision:**

For high-volume production, **Al 6061** (extrudable, machinable) is the *DFM winner*. For weight-critical (aerospace) applications, **CFRP** is the *performance winner* but requires specialized layup manufacturing and adhesive assembly.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparative Property Snapshot

| Property | Steel (1020) | Al (6061) | Ti (Ti-6Al-4V) | GFRP | CFRP | Alumina (Al₂O₃) | Nylon 6,6 | PEEK |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | 4430 | 1800 | 1600 | 3900 | 1140 | 1320 |
| $E$ (GPa) | 200 | 69 | 114 | 38 | 135 | 380 | 2.8 | 3.6 |
| $\sigma_{UTS}$ (MPa) | 380 | 240 | 950 | 2400 | 1500 | 300 | 75 | 100 |
| $T_m / T_g$ (°C) | 1460 | 582 | 1660 | — | — | 2072 | 255 ($T_m$) | 343 ($T_g$) |
| Ductility ($\% \varepsilon$) | 25 | 17 | 14 | 2 | 1 | < 0.1 | 60 | 50 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Primary Mfg Process | Casting, forging, machining | Extrusion, machining | Machining (difficult) | Layup, RTM | Layup, autoclave | Sintering, grinding | Injection moulding | Injection moulding |
| Assembly Method | Welding, bolting | Welding, bolting | Bolting, adhesive | Adhesive, bolts | Adhesive, bolts | Shrink-fit, adhesive | Snap-fit, ultrasonic weld | Snap-fit, ultrasonic weld |
| Relative Cost | Low | Low | High | Medium | Very High | Medium | Low | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Engineering Materials]:::root
    A0 --> A1[Metals and Alloys]:::metal
    A0 --> A2[Polymers]:::poly
    A0 --> A3[Ceramics and Glasses]:::cer
    A0 --> A4[Composites]:::comp
    A0 --> A5[Advanced and Smart Materials]:::adv

    A1 --> B1[Ferrous]:::fer
    A1 --> B2[Non Ferrous]:::nf
    B1 --> C1[Carbon Steels]
    B1 --> C2[Alloy Steels]
    B1 --> C3[Cast Irons]
    B2 --> C4[Aluminium]
    B2 --> C5[Copper]
    B2 --> C6[Titanium]
    B2 --> C7[Refractory W Mo Nb]

    A2 --> D1[Thermoplastics]:::tp
    A2 --> D2[Thermosets]:::ts
    A2 --> D3[Elastomers]:::el
    D1 --> E1[Commodity PE PP PVC]
    D1 --> E2[Engineering ABS PA PC]
    D1 --> E3[High Perf PEEK PPS]
    D2 --> E4[Phenolics Epoxy Polyester]
    D3 --> E5[Natural SBR NBR Silicone]

    A3 --> F1[Traditional]:::trad
    A3 --> F2[Advanced Ceramics]:::advc
    A3 --> F3[Glasses]:::gl
    F1 --> G1[Clay Refractory Abrasive Cement]
    F2 --> G2[Alumina Zirconia SiC Si3N4]
```
