1. **Manual Assembly** — operator performs all handling and joining. Costly in developed economies, but flexible for low-volume, complex products.
2. **Hybrid Assembly** — manual loading of components, automated fastening (e.g., auto screwdrivers).
3. **Automated Assembly** — robots or dedicated machines do both handling and joining. High capital, low variable cost.

---

## 2.2 Boothroyd's Design for Assembly (DFA) — The Heart of Module 2

Geoffrey Boothroyd (MIT/University of Massachusetts) developed a **systematic worksheet-based methodology** in the 1980s. It is the most-tested DFA framework in KTU.

### The Three Boothroyd Design Criteria for a Part to be Retained

A part is **justified** in the design **only if** it satisfies **at least one** of these three criteria:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criterion 1 — Movement:** The part must move relative to other parts during operation.
> **Criterion 2 — Material / Property:** The part must be of a different material, or have isolation properties (electrical, thermal, fluidic).
> **Criterion 3 — Disassembly:** The part must be separable to allow access for service or recycling.

If a part satisfies **none** of the above → it is a **candidate for elimination** and must be combined into an adjacent part.

### The DFA Worksheet Logic

Each part is scored on three **time codes**:

| Code | Meaning | Range (seconds) |
|---|---|---|
| **H_i** | Handling Index (symmetry, size, tangling, fragility) | 1.5 – 9.0 s |
| **I_i** | Insertion Index (fastening type, alignment, tools, reorientation) | 1.8 – 12.0 s |
| **t** | Total time per part = H_i + I_i | computed |

The designer enters each part in the worksheet, sums the times, and computes the **Design Efficiency (η)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize every equation below. They are the most-tested formulas in **PEMET522 Module 2**.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_tm}{N_a} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Quantifies how close a design is to the ideal minimum part count |
| 2 | **Time-based Design Efficiency** | $\eta_t = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\%$ | **%** | When parts are not equally time-expensive |
| 3 | **Theoretical Min. Assembly Time** | $t_{min} = N_{tm} \times t^* $ | **s** | Lower bound on assembly time |
| 4 | **Total Estimated Assembly Time** | $t_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} (H_i + I_i)$ | **s** | Sum of handling + insertion over all actual parts |
| 5 | **Theoretical Min. Number of Parts** | $N_{tm} = 1 + \sum (\text{essential features})$ | integer | Boothroyd's ideal count |
| 6 | **Assembly Cost** | $C_a = t_a \times R_l$ | **currency** | Time × labour rate |
| 7 | **Minimum Time per Part (t\*)** | $t^* = $ mean of fastest 3-times in table | **s** | Normalizing constant for η_t |
| 8 | **Annual Assembly Cost Savings** | $\Delta C = C_{old} - C_{new}$ | **currency/year** | Justifying DFA redesign |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical-bar symbol `$\vert$` and `$\mid$` have been deliberately avoided in equations above to comply with the markdown rendering protocol. If you need a modulus, write it as `\lvert x \rvert`.

---

## 2.4 Why DFA Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** In the automotive industry, **70 %–80 % of total production cost** is committed at the *design* stage. Reducing part count by just 1 (e.g., eliminating a separate bracket) can save **₹10–₹50 per vehicle**, multiplied across millions of units. Ford's 2006 DFA initiative on the Focus model reportedly **removed 30+ parts** and cut assembly time by **~12 %**.

The same logic applies to:
- **Consumer electronics** (modular phones, snap-fit enclosures).
- **Medical devices** (sterilizable, tool-free disassembly).
- **Aero-engine components** (every gram, every second of cycle time matters).

---

## 2.5 Pugh's Concept (Supplementary — asked occasionally)

Stuart Pugh emphasized that DFA is **one member of a broader "Total Design"** activity that includes:
- Functional analysis,
- Concept generation,
- DFM (Design for Manufacture), and
- DFA (Design for Assembly).

The key insight: **DFA cannot be applied at the end of design — it must be concurrent with concept generation**, or the geometry of parts is already locked in.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Design Efficiency (η) Formula

### Starting Premise (from Boothroyd)

A product that is optimally designed should contain exactly the **theoretical minimum** number of parts. Any part beyond this minimum is a candidate for redesign. We want a **dimensionless index** that penalizes excess parts.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Counts

Let:

$$N_a = \text{Actual number of parts in the current design}$$

$$N_{tm} = \text{Theoretical minimum number of parts (after ideal DFA)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $N_{tm}$ is obtained by listing every *essential feature* (shaft, bearing race, etc.) and assigning one part to each. Sub-assemblies that must move, isolate, or be separated count as one part each.

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio

We want a quantity that:
- equals **1** (i.e., 100 %) when $N_a = N_{tm}$,
- is **less than 1** when $N_a > N_{tm}$,
- is **dimensionless** so that it can compare different products.

The natural candidate is the ratio of minimum to actual:

$$\eta = \dfrac{N_{tm}}{N_a}$$

### Step 3 — Express as a Percentage

Multiplying by 100 converts the ratio into the conventional efficiency used in industry and KTU answers:

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{N_{tm}}{N_a} \times 100\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical interpretation:** If $\eta = 1.0$ (100 %), your design is perfect. If $\eta = 0.30$ (30 %), only 30 % of your parts are essential; the other 70 % are waste.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact pattern** of a KTU 14-mark sub-question.

**Problem:** A gearbox design uses $N_a = 25$ parts. Boothroyd analysis shows only $N_{tm} = 8$ parts are essential. Compute $\eta$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the values.

$$N_a = 25, \quad N_{tm} = 8$$

Step 2: Substitute into the formula.

$$\eta = \dfrac{8}{25} \times 100\%$$

Step 3: Perform the division.

$$\dfrac{8}{25} = 0.32$$

Step 4: Convert to percentage.

$$\eta = 0.32 \times 100\% = 32\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only 32 % of the parts are necessary. There is massive scope for DFA redesign. ✏️

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Based Design Efficiency (η_t)

The simple $\eta$ formula treats every part as equally time-expensive. In reality, a screw is slower to install than a snap-fit. Boothroyd therefore introduced a **time-weighted efficiency**.

### Step 1 — Define the Normalizing Time t\*

Choose the **mean of the three fastest operation times** in your DFA table:

$$t^* = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2 + t_3}{3}$$

where $t_1, t_2, t_3$ are the **smallest** $(H_i + I_i)$ values in the worksheet.

### Step 2 — Compute the Theoretical Minimum Assembly Time

If every one of the $N_{tm}$ essential parts could be assembled in the fastest possible time $t^*$, then:

$$t_{min} = N_{tm} \times t^*$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Actual Total Assembly Time

Sum the handling and insertion indices for every actual part:

$$t_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} \left( H_i + I_i \right)$$

### Step 4 — Form the Time Efficiency

$$\eta_t = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both efficiencies must be reported. The KTU examiner expects **both formulas in the answer**, not just one. State clearly that $\eta$ is the *part-count* efficiency and $\eta_t$ is the *time* efficiency.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A product has $N_{tm} = 6$ essential parts. The DFA worksheet gives the 3 fastest operations as 1.5 s, 1.8 s, and 2.0 s. The actual total assembly time is $t_a = 78.5$ s. Compute $\eta_t$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $t^*$.

$$t^* = \dfrac{1.5 + 1.8 + 2.0}{3}$$

$$t^* = \dfrac{5.3}{3}$$

$$t^* = 1.7667 \text{ s}$$

Step 2: Compute $t_{min}$.

$$t_{min} = 6 \times 1.7667$$

$$t_{min} = 10.6 \text{ s}$$

Step 3: Substitute into $\eta_t$.

$$\eta_t = \dfrac{10.6}{78.5} \times 100\%$$

Step 4: Final division.

$$\eta_t = 0.1350 \times 100\% \approx 13.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only 13.5 % of available assembly time is being used productively. ✏️

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Assembly Cost from Time

Assembly cost is the simplest derivation but appears as a sub-part of nearly every Part B question.

### Step 1 — Define the Labour Rate

Let $R_l$ be the **fully-loaded labour + machine rate** in ₹/s (or $/s, etc.). This already includes overheads.

### Step 2 — Multiply by Time

$$C_a = t_a \times R_l$$

### Step 3 — Annualized Cost

If $Q$ units are produced per year, the **annual assembly cost** is:

$$C_{annual} = C_a \times Q = t_a \times R_l \times Q$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question:** Sometimes the examiner gives $R_l$ in **₹/hour**. Convert it before substituting:

$$R_l\ (\text{₹/s}) = \dfrac{R_l\ (\text{₹/hour})}{3600}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

$t_a = 78.5$ s, $R_l = ₹$ 30/hour, $Q = 100{,}000$ units/year.

Step 1: Convert rate.

$$R_l = \dfrac{30}{3600} = 0.00833\ \text{₹/s}$$

Step 2: Cost per unit.

$$C_a = 78.5 \times 0.00833 = 0.654\ \text{₹}$$

Step 3: Annual cost.

$$C_{annual} = 0.654 \times 100{,}000 = ₹\ 65{,}400\ \text{per year}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Saving from a DFA Redesign

If a redesign reduces $t_a$ from $t_{a,\text{old}}$ to $t_{a,\text{new}}$, the **per-unit saving** is:

$$\Delta C_{unit} = (t_{a,\text{old}} - t_{a,\text{new}}) \times R_l$$

The **annual saving** is:

$$\Delta C_{annual} = \Delta C_{unit} \times Q$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most powerful number a DFA engineer reports to management — a **direct, recurring, annual cost reduction** from a one-time design change.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Handling (H) and Insertion (I) Codes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the table you reproduce *verbatim* in the KTU exam if asked. It is the same Boothroyd reference table used in industry.

| Handling Operation | Symbol | Description | Time $H_i$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H = 0** | None | Part is large, not tangled, symmetry not an issue | 0.0 |
| **H = 1** | One-hand grasp | Easy, no reorientation, > 30 mm thick | 1.5 |
| **H = 2** | Two-hand / thin | Needs care, or thickness < 30 mm | 3.0 |
| **H = 3** | Tangled / fragile / nests | Requires unjamming, separation, very thin (< 5 mm) | 9.0 |

| Insertion Operation | Symbol | Description | Time $I_i$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I = 0** | Self-locating | Drop-fit, no fastener, no tool | 0.0 |
| **I = 1** | One-hand locate + light push | Snap, light clip | 1.8 |
| **I = 2** | One-hand locate + fasten | Screw or clip needing hand effort | 4.5 |
| **I = 3** | Two-hand, tool, vision, reorient | Threaded fastener, alignment critical | 12.0 |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answers, **always** state the assumption (e.g., "Assuming the part is non-tangled and the fastener requires one-hand fastening, H = 1, I = 2"). Examiners **deduct marks** for unjustified codes.

---

## 3.6 Summary Equation Set (for quick recall)

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{N_{tm}}{N_a}\times 100\% \\[4pt]
\eta_t &= \frac{N_{tm} \cdot t^*}{\sum (H_i + I_i)} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
C_a &= t_a \cdot R_l \\[4pt]
\Delta C_{annual} &= \bigl(t_{a,\text{old}} - t_{a,\text{new}}\bigr) \cdot R_l \cdot Q
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Boothroyd DFA Methodology Flow

This is the **canonical flowchart** of the Boothroyd DFA worksheet procedure. Reproduce a similar flow in 14-mark answers for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: List All Parts] --> A1[Assign a unique ID to each part]
    A1 --> A2[Apply the 3 Boothroyd Criteria: Movement / Material / Disassembly]
    A2 --> A3{Does the part satisfy at least one criterion?}
    A3 -->|Yes| A4[Retain part: record H index and I index]
    A3 -->|No| A5[Mark as candidate for elimination]
    A5 --> A6[Can it be combined with an adjacent part?]
    A6 -->|Yes| A7[Redesign: merge geometry into neighbour]
    A6 -->|No| A8[Document justification for keeping it]
    A4 --> A9[Sum t_a = sum of H_i + I_i]
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Compute N_tm and t_min]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Design Efficiency eta]
    A11 --> A12{eta greater than 60 percent?}
    A12 -->|Yes| A13[Acceptable design: proceed]
    A12 -->|No| A14[Iterate redesign loop]
    A14 --> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The flow above maps the *logical decision tree* that Boothroyd's worksheet implements. Use it as the structural backbone of any 14-mark answer that asks "Apply DFA to a given product."

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Assembly Process Classification (Hierarchical)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AP[Assembly Process] --> MJ[Mechanical Joining]
    AP --> TJ[Thermal Joining]
    AP --> CJ[Chemical Joining]
    AP --> IJ[Interference Joining]
    AP --> MD[Modular / Overmolding]

    MJ --> MJ1[Screws and Bolts]
    MJ --> MJ2[Rivets]
    MJ --> MJ3[Snap fits and Clips]

    TJ --> TJ1[MIG and TIG Welding]
    TJ --> TJ2[Resistance Spot Welding]
    TJ --> TJ3[Soldering and Brazing]

    CJ --> CJ1[Adhesive Bonding]
    CJ --> CJ2[Solvent Welding]

    IJ --> IJ1[Press fit]
    IJ --> IJ2[Shrink fit]

    MD --> MD1[Insert Molding]
    MD --> MD2[Two shot Molding]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Sequential Assembly Operation Timeline

This block diagram shows how handling and insertion concatenate per part. KTU examiners like this for "explain the meaning of the DFA worksheet" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Part 1: Pickup] --> H1[Handling H1: 1.5 s]
    H1 --> I1[Insertion I1: 1.8 s]
    I1 --> P2[Part 2: Pickup]
    P2 --> H2[Handling H2: 3.0 s]
    H2 --> I2[Insertion I2: 4.5 s]
    I2 --> P3[Part 3: Pickup]
    P3 --> H3[Handling H3: 9.0 s]
    H3 --> I3[Insertion I3: 12.0 s]
    I3 --> DONE[Finished Assembly: t_a = sum of all]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Manual vs Automated Assembly Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Production volume per year?] --> Q2{Low: less than 10000}
    Q1 --> Q3{High: more than 100000}

    Q2 --> R1[Manual Assembly]
    R1 --> R1a[Low capital cost]
    R1 --> R1b[High variable cost per unit]
    R1 --> R1c[Flexible for design changes]

    Q3 --> R2[Automated Assembly]
    R2 --> R2a[High capital cost]
    R2 --> R2b[Low variable cost per unit]
    R2 --> R2c[High repeatability and quality]

    Q2 --> Q4{Mixed volume?}
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> R3[Hybrid Assembly]
    R3 --> R3a[Manual loading]
    R3 --> R3b[Automated fastening]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The crossover point at which automation becomes cheaper than manual assembly is reached when **Annual Labour Savings ≥ Capital Cost of Automation / Payback Period**. KTU may ask a 7-mark sub-question asking you to compute the breakeven volume.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Boothroyd Three-Criterion Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Consider a single part] --> S1{Does it move during operation?}
    S1 -->|Yes| KEEP[Retain the part]
    S1 -->|No| S2{Is it made of a different material or provides isolation?}
    S2 -->|Yes| KEEP
    S2 -->|No| S3{Must it be separable for service or recycling?}
    S3 -->|Yes| KEEP
    S3 -->|No| ELIM[Eliminate or merge with neighbour]
    ELIM --> S4[Update N_a and recompute eta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most reproduced Boothroyd diagram in textbooks. Drawing it (or any equivalent flow) in the exam earns **2 bonus marks** in the presentation category.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024]`

**Define the Assembly Process. List any four joining methods used in mechanical assembly.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Assembly Process:** The sequential joining of two or more manufactured parts using defined fastening or locating operations to produce a functional product. *(1 mark)*
>
> Four joining methods: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
> 1. **Mechanical fastening** (e.g., screws, bolts, rivets).
> 2. **Welding / Thermal joining** (e.g., MIG, TIG, spot welding).
> 3. **Adhesive bonding** (e.g., epoxy, cyanoacrylate).
> 4. **Snap-fit / Press-fit** (e.g., elastic deformation join).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]`

**State Boothroyd's three criteria for retaining a part in a product design. Why is part-count reduction important in DFA?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Boothroyd's three criteria:** *(2 marks)*
> 1. The part must **move** relative to other parts during operation.
> 2. The part must be of a **different material** or provide **isolation** (thermal, electrical, fluidic).
> 3. The part must be **separable** for service, maintenance, or recycling.
>
> **Importance of part-count reduction:** *(1 mark)* Each additional part adds handling, insertion, tooling, inventory, and quality-control cost. Reducing part count lowers assembly time, reduces labour cost, simplifies supply chain, and improves reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEMET522, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd Design for Assembly (DFA) methodology** in detail. Discuss the three criteria for part retention. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mechanical assembly has **15 actual parts**. After applying DFA analysis, the **theoretical minimum number of parts** is found to be **6**. The total actual assembly time, computed from the DFA worksheet, is **92 seconds**. The three fastest operation times in the worksheet are **1.5 s, 1.8 s, and 2.0 s**. Compute the **part-count design efficiency** and the **time-based design efficiency**. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Introduction (2 marks).**
Boothroyd DFA is a *systematic, worksheet-based methodology* developed by Geoffrey Boothroyd and colleagues for minimizing the assembly cost of a product. It works on the principle that every part added to a design must justify its existence.

**Step 2: The Three Criteria (3 marks).**
A part is retained **only if** it satisfies **at least one**:

1. **Movement Criterion** — The part undergoes relative motion with another part during operation. Example: a shaft rotating inside a bearing.
2. **Material / Property Criterion** — The part is made of a different material than its neighbour, or provides necessary isolation. Example: a rubber gasket providing fluid seal.
3. **Disassembly Criterion** — The part must be separable for maintenance or recycling. Example: a replaceable filter cartridge.

**Step 3: DFA Worksheet Procedure (2 marks).**
- List every part with a unique code.
- For each part, apply the three criteria. If none apply → mark for elimination or merging.
- Assign **Handling index (H_i)** based on symmetry, size, tangling.
- Assign **Insertion index (I_i)** based on fastening type and tool requirement.
- Sum the times: $t_a = \sum (H_i + I_i)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$N_a = 15, \quad N_{tm} = 6, \quad t_a = 92 \text{ s}, \quad t_1 = 1.5 \text{ s},\ t_2 = 1.8 \text{ s},\ t_3 = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1: Compute part-count efficiency (3 marks).**

$$\eta = \dfrac{N_{tm}}{N_a} \times 100\% = \dfrac{6}{15} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 0.4 \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**Step 2: Compute the normalizing time $t^*$ (1 mark).**

$$t^* = \dfrac{1.5 + 1.8 + 2.0}{3} = \dfrac{5.3}{3} \approx 1.7667 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $t_{min}$ (1 mark).**

$$t_{min} = N_{tm} \times t^* = 6 \times 1.7667 \approx 10.6 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute time efficiency (1 mark).**

$$\eta_t = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\% = \dfrac{10.6}{92} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_t \approx 0.1152 \times 100\% \approx 11.5\%$$

**Step 5: Comment (1 mark).**
The part-count efficiency is **40 %** and the time efficiency is only **11.5 %**. This indicates that not only are there excess parts, but the retained parts are also time-expensive. The design needs a major DFA revision to combine parts, eliminate fasteners, and use self-locating snap-fits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat flowchart**, explain Boothroyd's DFA methodology. Differentiate between **manual and automated assembly** with respect to volume, cost, and flexibility. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A product has **$N_{tm} = 5$** essential parts. The DFA worksheet times (in seconds) for 12 actual parts are tabulated below. Compute the **Design Efficiency** and the **Time Efficiency** and suggest two DFA improvements.

| Part # | $H_i$ (s) | $I_i$ (s) | Retained? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.8 | Yes |
| 2 | 1.5 | 4.5 | No (merge with 1) |
| 3 | 3.0 | 4.5 | Yes |
| 4 | 1.5 | 1.8 | No (merge with 3) |
| 5 | 9.0 | 12.0 | Yes |
| 6 | 1.5 | 4.5 | Yes |
| 7 | 3.0 | 4.5 | No |
| 8 | 1.5 | 1.8 | Yes |
| 9 | 1.5 | 1.8 | No |
| 10 | 3.0 | 12.0 | Yes |
| 11 | 1.5 | 1.8 | No |
| 12 | 1.5 | 4.5 | Yes |

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: DFA Flowchart (3 marks).**
The flowchart should show the following logical steps in order:

1. **List all parts** of the product with unique IDs.
2. **Apply three criteria** to each part: Movement, Material, Disassembly.
3. **Branch decision** — If at least one criterion is satisfied → retain; else mark for elimination.
4. **Combine or eliminate** the marked part.
5. **Assign H_i and I_i** codes to the retained parts.
6. **Compute $t_a$**, $N_{tm}$, $\eta$, and $\eta_t$.
7. **Iterate** until $\eta > 60\%$.

(Refer to Diagram A in Section 4 for the full flowchart.)

**Step 2: Manual vs Automated Assembly (4 marks).**

| Parameter | Manual Assembly | Automated Assembly |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume suitability** | Low to medium (1k–100k units/yr) | High (> 100k units/yr) |
| **Capital cost** | Low (no machines) | High (robots, fixtures) |
| **Variable cost per unit** | High (labour-intensive) | Low (machine cost spread) |
| **Flexibility for design changes** | High (operator adaptable) | Low (requires reprogramming) |
| **Quality consistency** | Variable, operator-dependent | High, repeatable |
| **Best for** | Prototypes, custom products | Mass production |

**Step 3: Conclusion.**
Manual is preferred for **low volume and high mix**; automated for **high volume and low mix**. Hybrid is chosen for the mid-range.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N_{tm} = 5$.

**Step 1: Identify retained parts (1 mark).**
Retained parts (from the table): **1, 3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12** → $N_a = 7$.

**Step 2: Compute $t_a$ (2 marks).**
Sum $(H_i + I_i)$ for retained parts only:

| Part # | $H_i + I_i$ (s) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 + 1.8 = 3.3 |
| 3 | 3.0 + 4.5 = 7.5 |
| 5 | 9.0 + 12.0 = 21.0 |
| 6 | 1.5 + 4.5 = 6.0 |
| 8 | 1.5 + 1.8 = 3.3 |
| 10 | 3.0 + 12.0 = 15.0 |
| 12 | 1.5 + 4.5 = 6.0 |

$$t_a = 3.3 + 7.5 + 21.0 + 6.0 + 3.3 + 15.0 + 6.0 = 62.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $t^*$ (1 mark).**
Three fastest times: 3.3 s (Part 1), 3.3 s (Part 8), 3.3 s (others — but we need 3 unique). Using 3.3, 3.3, 3.3:

$$t^* = \dfrac{3.3 + 3.3 + 3.3}{3} = 3.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute $t_{min}$ (1 mark).**

$$t_{min} = N_{tm} \times t^* = 5 \times 3.3 = 16.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\eta$ and $\eta_t$ (1.5 marks).**

$$\eta = \dfrac{5}{7} \times 100\% \approx 71.4\%$$

$$\eta_t = \dfrac{16.5}{62.1} \times 100\% \approx 26.6\%$$

**Step 6: Suggest two DFA improvements (0.5 marks).**
1. Replace Part 5 (21 s) — likely a heavy fastener — with an **integrated snap-fit** molded into the housing.
2. Reduce reorientation on Part 10 (15 s) by adding a **chamfered lead-in** for self-alignment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PEMET522 Module 2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the three Boothroyd criteria verbatim** — Examiners *expect* the words "Movement, Material, Disassembly." Memorize them.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta$ and $\eta_t$** — $\eta$ uses *part counts*; $\eta_t$ uses *time*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** for H and I codes — Always write: *"Assuming the part is non-tangled, symmetric, and requires one-hand fastening, H = 1, I = 2."*
> 4. **Unit-conversion errors in $R_l$** — Converting ₹/hour to ₹/s is the most common arithmetic slip.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" or "interpretation" step** — KTU awards 1–2 marks for engineering judgement, not just calculation.
> 6. **Not drawing a flowchart** for the 7-mark "Explain DFA methodology" question — A neat block diagram earns 2 presentation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and read it 5 minutes before the exam.

- ✅ **Assembly Process** = sequential joining of parts to make a finished product.
- ✅ **Boothroyd's 3 Criteria** = **M**ovement, **M**aterial, **D**isassembly (M-M-D).
- ✅ **Part-count efficiency**: $\eta = (N_{tm} / N_a) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Time efficiency**: $\eta_t = (t_{min} / t_a) \times 100\%$, where $t_{min} = N_{tm} \times t^*$.
- ✅ $t^*$ = mean of the **three fastest** $(H_i + I_i)$ times in the worksheet.
- ✅ **H codes**: 0 → 1.5 → 3.0 → 9.0 s (None → One-hand → Two-hand → Tangled).
- ✅ **I codes**: 0 → 1.8 → 4.5 → 12.0 s (Self-locating → Push → Fasten → Tool).
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** for every H and I code assigned.
- ✅ **Always state the unit** of $R_l$ *before and after* conversion.
- ✅ **DFA improves** with part elimination, part combination, and self-fastening features (snap-fits, snap-clips).
- ✅ **Manual assembly** = flexible, low-capital, high variable cost.
- ✅ **Automated assembly** = high-capital, low variable cost, high repeatability.
- ✅ **Pugh's concept** = DFA must be *concurrent* with concept design, not added later.
- ✅ **Industry benchmarks**: $\eta > 60\%$ is generally acceptable; < 40 % means the design needs major DFA rework.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — THE ASSEMBLY PROCESS** ✏️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, the term **"Characteristics and Applications"** refers to the systematic study of the inherent **manufacturing process attributes** (geometric capability, dimensional tolerance, surface integrity, material compatibility, production rate, tooling cost, and economic batch size) and the corresponding **engineering applications** for which each primary and secondary manufacturing process is best suited. It is the bridge between **product design intent** and **process capability**, allowing the designer to select a process whose characteristics match the functional, geometric, and economic requirements of the part.

Formally, for any process $P_i$, we can represent its characteristics as a vector:

$$P_i = \left\{ T_i, \; R_{a,i}, \; \dot{V}_i, \; C_{t,i}, \; C_{p,i}, \; M_i, \; G_i \right\}$$

where each parameter carries specific design information. The KTU 2024 syllabus defines this concept as the foundation of *process-aware design*, meaning every geometric feature, tolerance, and material choice in a design must be validated against the **achievable characteristics** of the available manufacturing route.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMET522 / Module 2):**
> Characteristics and Applications covers the qualitative and quantitative attributes of casting, forging, powder metallurgy, welding, sheet-metal forming, machining, polymer processing, and additive manufacturing, and how these dictate part geometry, tolerance stack-up, and cost estimation in a KTU Part-B (14-mark) problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a **mode of transport** to travel from Kochi to Delhi. You could take a **train, aeroplane, car, or bicycle**. Each has its own "characteristics" (speed, cost, comfort, capacity, terrain capability, fuel type) and "applications" (a bicycle is great for a 2-km errand, useless for 1,500 km). You would *never* ride a bicycle to Delhi, just as you would *never* design a precision aerospace turbine blade using a sand-casting process.

**Manufacturing processes behave identically.** Each has:
- A **personality** (its process signature: pressure, temperature, deformation mechanism).
- A **competence zone** (size, tolerance, surface finish, material).
- A **best-use case** (application).

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU examiner will *always* expect you to justify a process selection by linking the **design requirement** (e.g., "tolerance of $\pm 0.05$ mm on a 200 mm shaft") to the **process characteristic** (e.g., "centreless grinding achieves $\pm 0.005$ mm"). Memorizing applications without linking them to measurable characteristics is the single biggest reason students lose marks.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Process Characteristics

| Pillar | Definition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Capability** | Maximum/minimum size, wall thickness, internal features | Determines if a part is *physically producible* |
| **Dimensional & Surface Quality** | Achievable tolerance $T$ and roughness $R_a$ | Determines if the part *meets specifications* |
| **Economic & Production Attributes** | Batch size, cycle time, tooling cost $C_t$, piece cost $C_p$ | Determines if the part is *commercially viable* |

A robust DFMA design maps the product specification into all three pillars simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Manufacturing Processes

Manufacturing processes are broadly classified by the **state of the material** during processing and the **mechanism of shape creation**.

**1. Solid-State Processes (Material remains solid throughout):**
- Metal Forming: Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing, Stamping, Sheet-metal forming.
- Machining: Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, EDM.
- Powder Processing: Powder Metallurgy, Sintering.
- Additive: Cold Spray, Diffusion Bonding.

**2. Liquid-State Processes (Material is molten):**
- Casting: Sand, Investment, Die, Centrifugal, Continuous.
- Polymer Moulding: Injection, Blow, Rotational.
- Welding: Arc, Gas, Resistance, Laser, Electron Beam, Friction Stir.

**3. Vapour / Particulate-State Processes:**
- Additive Manufacturing (AM): SLM, DMLS, FDM, SLA, Binder Jetting.
- Vapour Deposition: PVD, CVD.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Process Capability Evaluation

For any KTU problem, evaluate a process using the following six-step logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Material Compatibility ($M_i$):** Does the process accept the chosen material? (e.g., cast iron is forgeable; grey cast iron is *not* weldable; thermoplastics are injection-mouldable but thermosets are not).
- **Step 2 — Geometric Complexity ($G_i$):** Can the part be demoulded / unmoulded / parted? Internal undercuts eliminate gravity die-casting. Hollow closed sections need extrusion or die-casting.
- **Step 3 — Tolerance Target ($T_i$):** Compare the design tolerance to the process tolerance band. Standard ISO 2768 bands: $f$ (fine), $m$ (medium), $c$ (coarse), $v$ (very coarse).
- **Step 4 — Surface Finish Target ($R_{a,i}$):** Compare the required $R_a$ to the as-process roughness. $R_a$ for as-cast $= 12.5$ to $50\;\mu m$; for as-machined $= 0.8$ to $6.3\;\mu m$; for grinding $= 0.1$ to $0.8\;\mu m$.
- **Step 5 — Batch Size Economics ($N_{opt}$):** Identify the economic batch size. Die casting: $N_{opt} > 5{,}000$. Investment casting: $N_{opt} < 1{,}000$. CNC machining: $N_{opt}$ small to medium.
- **Step 6 — Cost Triangulation ($C_{total}$):** Sum tooling cost, setup cost, and piece cost; compare with the part budget.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **standard quantitative characteristics** of all major manufacturing processes expected in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). All values are the widely accepted industry/KTU-recommended benchmarks. No vertical-pipe absolute-value syntax is used; instead `\vert` / `\mid` is employed.

| Process | Typical Tolerance | Surface Roughness $R_a$ | Min. Wall Thickness | Economic Batch $N$ | Relative Tooling Cost | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | $\pm 0.8$ mm | $12.5$ to $25\;\mu m$ | $5$ to $6$ mm | $1$ to $10^3$ | Very Low | Large ferrous housings, engine blocks, pump bodies |
| **Investment Casting** | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $1.6$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ | $0.8$ to $1.5$ mm | $10$ to $10^3$ | Medium | Turbine blades, jewellery, medical implants |
| **Die Casting** | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $0.6$ to $1.0$ mm | $5 \times 10^3$ to $10^6$ | High | Zn/Al/Mg alloys for automobile, electronics enclosures |
| **Open-Die Forging** | $\pm 1.5$ mm | $3.2$ to $12.5\;\mu m$ | $\ge 5$ mm | $1$ to $10^2$ | Low | Large shafts, rolls, rings, flanges |
| **Closed-Die Forging** | $\pm 0.4$ mm | $1.6$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ | $\ge 2$ mm | $10^3$ to $10^5$ | High | Connecting rods, crankshafts, gears |
| **Extrusion** | $\pm 0.2$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $1.0$ to $1.5$ mm | $\ge 10^3$ | Medium | Long uniform sections, heat sinks, channels |
| **Powder Metallurgy** | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $\ge 1.5$ mm | $10^3$ to $10^6$ | High | Self-lubricating bushings, gears, soft magnetic parts |
| **Arc Welding** | $\pm 1.0$ mm | $6.3$ to $25\;\mu m$ | $\ge 2$ mm | One-off to medium | Low | Pressure vessels, ship hulls, bridges |
| **Laser / EB Welding** | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $\ge 0.5$ mm | Low to medium | Very High | Aerospace, medical, electronics |
| **Turning** | $\pm 0.025$ mm | $0.4$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | N/A (rotational) | One-off to medium | Low-Medium | Shafts, pistons, threads, rotational parts |
| **Milling** | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $0.8$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ | N/A | One-off to medium | Low-Medium | Prismatic parts, slots, pockets, 3-D contours |
| **Grinding** | $\pm 0.005$ mm | $0.1$ to $0.8\;\mu m$ | N/A | Medium to high | Medium | Hardened surfaces, precision bearings, tooling |
| **Injection Moulding (Plastics)** | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $0.4$ to $1.6\;\mu m$ | $0.5$ to $1.0$ mm | $10^4$ to $10^7$ | High | Consumer electronics, automotive interiors, toys |
| **Sheet-Metal Stamping** | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $0.5$ to $1.0$ mm | $10^4$ to $10^6$ | High | Panels, brackets, enclosures, automotive body |
| **SLM / DMLS (Metal AM)** | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.2$ mm | $5$ to $20\;\mu m$ | $0.1$ to $0.3$ mm | $1$ to $10^3$ | None (digital) | Topology-optimised brackets, lattice implants, rocket injectors |
| **FDM (Polymer AM)** | $\pm 0.2$ mm | $10$ to $25\;\mu m$ | $1.0$ mm | $1$ to $100$ | None (digital) | Prototypes, jigs, fixtures, concept models |

## 2.4 The Economic Batch Size Relation

The cost of a single part produced by a given process can be expressed as:

$$C_{piece} = \frac{C_{tool}}{N} + \frac{C_{setup}}{N} + C_{material} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$$

where the amortised tooling/setup cost $C_t/N$ dominates at low $N$ (favouring soft tooling like CNC or sand casting), while material + labour dominate at high $N$ (favouring die casting, injection moulding). The **economic batch size** $N_{opt}$ is found by equating the total cost of the candidate process to that of the alternative.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process characteristics and their applications are the heart of every industrial design decision:
- **Aerospace:** Designers pick superplastic forming + diffusion bonding for Ti-alloy wing skins because the process tolerates ultra-thin walls ($1$ to $1.5$ mm) with no porosity — characteristics unattainable in casting.
- **Automotive (high volume):** Hot-stamping of boron steel achieves 1500 MPa tensile strength with $\pm 0.1$ mm tolerance — ideal for B-pillars where both strength and dimensional repeatability are critical.
- **Medical implants:** Selective Laser Melting (SLM) of Ti-6Al-4V produces lattice-structured hip stems with $\pm 0.1$ mm accuracy and controlled porosity — impossible by any conventional process.
- **Electronics:** Die-casting of Al alloys with $R_a \le 1.6\;\mu m$ allows direct EMI shielding without secondary machining.

> [!NOTE]
> The **single most important skill** tested by KTU 2024 under this topic is the *justified mapping* of a process characteristic to a design requirement. A 14-mark question will never simply ask "list the processes". It will give a *scenario* (part description, batch, tolerance, material) and expect you to evaluate and *justify* the chosen process using the characteristic-to-requirement mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Decision Tree

The matrix below is the **canonical KTU 2024 analytical tool** for any process-selection problem. The reasoning is presented exhaustively, with every transition explained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation does *not* assume any specific numerical part. It derives the **general logic of selection**. A KTU numerical question would simply plug values into the same framework.

**Step 1 — Material Class Identification.**

The first and most restrictive filter. The allowed material class eliminates entire process families. The logic is:

$$\text{If } M \in \{\text{cast iron, bronze, Al-Si}\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{welding is forbidden (weldability check)}.$$

$$\text{If } M \in \{\text{thermoplastic}\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{all bulk metal-removal processes are uneconomical.}$$

$$\text{If } M \in \{\text{Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel}\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{casting is restricted to investment or vacuum arc remelt.}$$

**Step 2 — Required Geometry Filter.**

The geometry filter eliminates processes that cannot form the part. Three sub-tests:

- **Internal cavity test:** Is there a closed internal cavity?
  - If *Yes* → die casting, injection moulding, extrusion, investment casting, or rotational moulding are candidates.
  - If *No* → forging, sand casting, machining, AM remain.

- **Undercut test:** Are there undercuts that require side-action or cores?
  - If *Yes* → investment casting, AM, or machining (avoid simple two-part die processes like die casting / closed-die forging).

- **Symmetry test:**
  - Axisymmetric (rotational) → turning, grinding, hot forging, rotational friction welding.
  - Prismatic → milling, broaching, stamping, extrusion, AM.
  - 3-D freeform → investment casting, AM, 5-axis machining.

**Step 3 — Tolerance & Surface Filter.**

Compute the required tolerance band $T_{req}$ and surface roughness $R_{a,req}$:

$$T_{req} = \pm 0.5 \cdot (T_{upper} - T_{lower})$$

$$R_{a,req} \in [R_{a,\min}, R_{a,\max}]$$

Compare against the process band. If $T_{req} < T_{process,\min}$, the process is *disqualified* unless followed by a secondary finishing process (e.g., as-forged $+$ grinding).

**Step 4 — Batch Size Filter.**

The economic batch size filter uses the amortised cost equation:

$$C_{total}(N) = C_{tool} + N \cdot C_{piece}(N)$$

For two competing processes $A$ and $B$, the **crossover batch size** $N^*$ at which they have equal cost is:

$$N^* = \frac{C_{tool,A} - C_{tool,B}}{C_{piece,B} - C_{piece,A}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking.**

Rank survivors using a weighted scoring index:

$$S = w_1 \cdot \frac{1}{C_{total}} + w_2 \cdot \frac{1}{T} + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{R_a} + w_4 \cdot \frac{1}{Lead\;Time} + w_5 \cdot Quality$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 1$. The weights are set by the design brief (e.g., aerospace emphasises $w_4$ and $w_5$; consumer goods emphasise $w_1$).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation — Tooling-Cost Amortisation

A KTU-favourite calculation: comparing **sand casting** vs **die casting** for an aluminium housing.

**Given:**

- Tooling cost: $C_{tool,sand} = \text{₹}\;20{,}000$, $C_{tool,die} = \text{₹}\;5{,}00{,}000$.
- Piece cost (excluding tooling): $C_{p,sand} = \text{₹}\;250$, $C_{p,die} = \text{₹}\;40$.
- Required annual quantity: $N = 8{,}000$ units.

**Derivation of crossover batch $N^*$:**

$$N^* = \frac{C_{tool,die} - C_{tool,sand}}{C_{p,sand} - C_{p,die}}$$

$$N^* = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000}{250 - 40}$$

$$N^* = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{210}$$

$$N^* = 2{,}285.7 \approx 2{,}286\;\text{units}$$

**Total cost at $N = 8{,}000$:**

For sand casting:

$$C_{total,sand} = 20{,}000 + 8{,}000 \times 250 = 20{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\;20{,}20{,}000$$

For die casting:

$$C_{total,die} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}000 \times 40 = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}\;8{,}20{,}000$$

**Economic interpretation:**

Since $N = 8{,}000 \gg N^* = 2{,}286$, die casting is the **lower-cost process** despite its 25× higher tooling cost. The annual saving is:

$$\Delta C = 20{,}20{,}000 - 8{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}\;12{,}00{,}000$$

This is the canonical KTU calculation pattern. Memorise the crossover-batch formula:

$$\boxed{N^* = \frac{C_{tool,2} - C_{tool,1}}{C_{p,1} - C_{p,2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks-distribution insight (KTU):** In a 14-mark question, the *process-selection narrative* is worth 7 marks, and the *quantitative cost comparison* is worth the remaining 7. Skipping the narrative loses half the marks even if your math is correct.

## 3.3 Cross-Reference Matrix — Material vs Process Compatibility

| Material | Cast | Forge | Weld | Machine | AM (Metal) | PM | Plastic Mould |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Grey Cast Iron | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $\times$ | N/A |
| Low-C Steel | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | N/A |
| Stainless Steel 304 | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | N/A |
| Al 6061 | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | N/A |
| Ti-6Al-4V | $\checkmark$ (inv.) | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ (LBW) | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | N/A |
| ABS / PP / Nylon | N/A | N/A | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | N/A | $\checkmark$ |
| WC-Co Cemented Carbide | $\times$ | $\times$ | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ (EDM) | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | N/A |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Process Selection Engine

The mermaid diagram below captures the **decision-flow architecture** of a DFMA process-selection system as it would be applied in a KTU Module-2 scenario.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Brief Input] --> B[Identify Material Class]
    B --> C[Define Geometry Class]
    C --> D[Define Tolerance Class]
    D --> E[Define Surface Finish Class]
    E --> F[Define Batch Size]
    F --> G[Apply Process Compatibility Filter]
    G --> H{Eligible Process Pool}
    H --> I[Casting Family]
    H --> J[Forming Family]
    H --> K[Joining Family]
    H --> L[Machining Family]
    H --> M[Additive Family]
    I --> N[Cost and Tolerance Scoring]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Weighted Score Calculation]
    O --> P[Ranked Process Output]
    P --> Q[Final DFMA Recommendation]
```

## 4.2 Process Characteristic Map — Visual Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps each **process family** to its characteristic envelope. This is the mental model KTU expects you to draw in the exam (a labelled block diagram with characteristic vectors).
