# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**"List the four primary characteristics used to compare manufacturing processes. Give one example of a process selected for high geometric complexity."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four primary characteristics used to compare manufacturing processes are:
1. **Dimensional Tolerance** — the smallest variation a process can consistently achieve.
2. **Surface Finish ($R_a$)** — the as-produced surface roughness.
3. **Geometric Complexity** — the ability to form internal cavities, undercuts, and free-form surfaces.
4. **Economic Batch Size** — the production volume at which the process is commercially viable.

**Example:** Investment casting is selected for high geometric complexity because it can produce intricate internal passages, thin walls ($< 1.5$ mm), and undercuts using expendable wax patterns — a capability unmatched by most bulk-forming processes. (1 mark for example + justification)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between the typical tolerance and surface finish achievable by (i) sand casting and (ii) investment casting."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Investment Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Tolerance | $\pm 0.8$ mm (coarse) | $\pm 0.1$ mm (fine) |
| Surface $R_a$ | $12.5$ to $25\;\mu m$ | $1.6$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ |
| Reason | Mould is rigid but parting line and ramming introduce larger deviations. | Ceramic shell replicates the smooth wax pattern with high fidelity. |

(1 mark tolerance, 1 mark $R_a$, 1 mark reason.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of "process characteristics" in DFMA. Discuss, with examples, how the tolerance, surface finish, and economic batch size influence the selection of a manufacturing process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer must produce **20,000 aluminium-alloy housings per year** for an electronics enclosure. The part is non-rotational, has uniform wall thickness of $2$ mm, and requires a tolerance of $\pm 0.1$ mm with $R_a \le 3.2\;\mu m$. Two process options are being considered:
- **Process A — Sand Casting:** $C_{tool} = \text{₹}\;30{,}000$, $C_{piece} = \text{₹}\;90$.
- **Process B — Die Casting:** $C_{tool} = \text{₹}\;8{,}00{,}000$, $C_{piece} = \text{₹}\;25$.

Determine the **crossover batch size** $N^*$, the **total annual cost** for each process, and recommend the most economic process. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A:**

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Process characteristics are the quantifiable attributes of a manufacturing process — including achievable tolerance, surface finish, geometric complexity, material compatibility, batch size, and cost — that govern whether a given product design can be produced economically and to specification.

**Influence on Selection (5 marks):**

- **Tolerance:** A design requiring $\pm 0.05$ mm cannot use sand casting ($\pm 0.8$ mm); it demands grinding, die casting, or AM. *Example:* Piston pins in IC engines use grinding because the $\pm 5\;\mu m$ tolerance is unattainable by as-cast surfaces.
- **Surface Finish ($R_a$):** Components with sliding contact (e.g., journal bearings) require $R_a \le 0.4\;\mu m$, forcing the use of grinding or honing after initial forming. Cosmetic parts (e.g., phone covers) accept $R_a \le 1.6\;\mu m$ directly from injection moulding.
- **Economic Batch Size:** Sand casting has near-zero tooling cost, making it ideal for $N = 1$ to $1{,}000$ (prototypes, heavy machinery). Die casting needs a hardened die costing several lakhs, but the per-piece cost drops to a few rupees, justifying it only for $N > 5{,}000$. Selecting the wrong process inflates unit cost dramatically.
- **Geometric Complexity:** Investment casting is preferred for turbine blades with internal cooling channels; forging is restricted to simpler, near-net shapes.
- **Material Compatibility:** Grey cast iron cannot be welded; aluminium extrusions are restricted to constant cross-section profiles.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Crossover Batch $N^*$:**

$$N^* = \frac{C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}}{C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}}$$

$$N^* = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000}{90 - 25}$$

$$N^* = \frac{7{,}70{,}000}{65}$$

$$N^* = 11{,}846\;\text{units (approximately)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Cost at $N = 20{,}000$:**

For sand casting (Process A):

$$C_{total,A} = 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 \times 90$$

$$C_{total,A} = 30{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\;18{,}30{,}000$$

For die casting (Process B):

$$C_{total,B} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \times 25$$

$$C_{total,B} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\;13{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since $N = 20{,}000 > N^* = 11{,}846$, die casting is the **more economical process**. The annual saving is:

$$\Delta C = 18{,}30{,}000 - 13{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\;5{,}30{,}000$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Process B — Die Casting is recommended. It also satisfies the tolerance ($\pm 0.1$ mm) and surface finish ($R_a \le 3.2\;\mu m$) requirements directly, whereas sand casting would require a secondary machining step adding further cost.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a comparative table, list the typical tolerance, surface roughness, and economic batch size for the following processes: **sand casting, investment casting, die casting, closed-die forging, turning, and grinding.** Discuss how a designer uses this data to choose between investment casting and closed-die forging for a steel gear of batch size 5,000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **process characteristics and applications** of (i) Powder Metallurgy, (ii) Selective Laser Melting (SLM), and (iii) Injection Moulding. For each, mention one industry where it is dominantly used. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B:**

**(a) Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Process | Tolerance | Surface $R_a$ | Economic Batch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting | $\pm 0.8$ mm | $12.5$ to $25\;\mu m$ | $1$ to $10^3$ |
| Investment Casting | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $1.6$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ | $10$ to $10^3$ |
| Die Casting | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $0.8$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | $5 \times 10^3$ to $10^6$ |
| Closed-Die Forging | $\pm 0.4$ mm | $1.6$ to $6.3\;\mu m$ | $10^3$ to $10^5$ |
| Turning | $\pm 0.025$ mm | $0.4$ to $3.2\;\mu m$ | One-off to medium |
| Grinding | $\pm 0.005$ mm | $0.1$ to $0.8\;\mu m$ | Medium to high |

**Design Decision (2 marks):** For a steel gear, batch size $5{,}000$:
- **Closed-die forging** is preferred because (i) it imparts superior grain flow and fatigue strength critical for gear teeth, (ii) it lies within the economic batch band of $10^3$ to $10^5$, and (iii) post-forging hobbing achieves the final tolerance and tooth profile.
- **Investment casting** would give a smoother surface but is uneconomical above $1{,}000$ pieces and would produce inferior grain flow, reducing fatigue life.

---

**(b) Process Characteristics and Applications (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for industries):**

**(i) Powder Metallurgy (P/M):**
- **Characteristics:** Near-net-shape capability, tolerance $\pm 0.05$ mm, $R_a$ 0.8 to $3.2\;\mu m$, porosity controllable (10–30 %), no melting required.
- **Applications:** Self-lubricating bronze bushings, sintered gears, soft-magnetic pole pieces, automotive oil-pump gears.
- **Industry:** Automotive components.

**(ii) Selective Laser Melting (SLM):**
- **Characteristics:** Layer-by-layer fusion of metal powder, complex lattice and topology-optimised geometry, tolerance $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.2$ mm, $R_a$ 5 to $20\;\mu m$ (as-built), small batch viable.
- **Applications:** Topology-optimised aerospace brackets, customised orthopaedic implants with controlled porosity, rocket injector heads with internal channels.
- **Industry:** Aerospace and medical implants.

**(iii) Injection Moulding:**
- **Characteristics:** High volume, tolerance $\pm 0.1$ mm, $R_a$ 0.4 to $1.6\;\mu m$, complex 3-D geometry with inserts, thin walls ($0.5$ to $1.0$ mm).
- **Applications:** Mobile phone cases, automotive dashboards, toys, bottle caps, medical disposables.
- **Industry:** Consumer electronics and packaging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mere listing without justification ($-2$ to $-3$ marks):** Writing "die casting" without stating *why* (tolerance, finish, batch) is treated as a half-answer.
> 2. **Ignoring the crossover batch calculation ($-3$ marks):** Many students jump to the conclusion ("die casting is better") without computing $N^*$ — KTU values the *method* over the answer.
> 3. **Confusing process families ($-1$ mark each):** Powder metallurgy is *not* a casting process; investment casting is *not* suitable for steels needing fatigue life.
> 4. **Omitting units in tables ($-1$ mark):** Always specify $\mu m$ for $R_a$, mm for tolerance, units for cost (₹ / $ / €).
> 5. **Not mapping application to process in part (b) ($-2$ marks):** The application must be tied to the *characteristic* that makes the process uniquely suitable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Process characteristics** = tolerance + $R_a$ + geometric capability + material compatibility + batch size + cost.
- [ ] **Tighter tolerance** = harder, more expensive process (machining $>$ die casting $>$ sand casting).
- [ ] **Crossover batch size formula** $N^* = (C_{tool,2} - C_{tool,1}) / (C_{p,1} - C_{p,2})$ is the *single most tested* equation in this module.
- [ ] **Casting** is preferred for complex geometry, low-to-medium tolerance, and ferrous / non-ferrous parts in medium-to-high batch.
- [ ] **Forging** is preferred when grain flow, fatigue strength, and impact toughness dominate (gears, crankshafts, connecting rods).
- [ ] **Machining** is reserved for low-to-medium batch, high tolerance, hardened surfaces, and prismatic / rotational parts.
- [ ] **Powder Metallurgy** suits self-lubricating, porous, and soft-magnetic parts; **cannot** produce large structural parts.
- [ ] **Additive Manufacturing (SLM/DMLS)** is the *only* process for topology-optimised and lattice geometries; expensive per kg but tooling-free.
- [ ] **Injection Moulding** is the bulk process for thermoplastics at very high volume ($> 10^4$).
- [ ] **Sheet-metal stamping** suits thin-wall, large-area, high-volume panel-like parts (car bodies, enclosures).
- [ ] **Welding** is the dominant joining process for large structures (vessels, bridges, ships); not a primary shape-giving process.
- [ ] Always link **material** $\rightarrow$ **geometry** $\rightarrow$ **tolerance** $\rightarrow$ **batch** $\rightarrow$ **cost** in that order during justification.
- [ ] Draw a **labelled block diagram** of the process family whenever a 14-mark question includes a "compare" or "select" instruction.
- [ ] End every answer with a **single-line justified recommendation** — this is what the KTU examiner keys for when awarding the final 1 to 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Significance of Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Economic Significance of Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Economic Significance of Assembly** refers to the quantitative and qualitative impact that the assembly phase of a product's life-cycle exerts on its **total manufacturing cost, time-to-market, quality, and global competitiveness**. In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, it is the study of how strategic design decisions at the part and sub-system level determine the cost of joining components into a finished product.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, assembly is recognized as one of the **highest-value-adding yet cost-intensive activities** in discrete manufacturing. Industrial surveys consistently report that **assembly operations account for approximately 30% to 50% of total product cost** and consume a comparable share of the total production lead time. Therefore, economically intelligent assembly design is not a peripheral concern but a **central pillar of product profitability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Economic Significance of Assembly is treated as the conceptual foundation for the Design for Assembly (DFA) methodology. Students must be able to articulate (a) why assembly dominates cost, (b) how to quantify the cost of each assembly operation, and (c) how design simplification directly converts into monetary savings.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of building a **motor vehicle** on a factory assembly line. Imagine you are the chief engineer of a small car company. You can either:

- **Option A:** Design the car with **3,000 individual parts**, each requiring a separate robot to pick, orient, and fasten. (This is like cooking a five-course meal with 100 ingredients, washing a dish after every spoonful.)
- **Option B:** Redesign it with **1,500 parts**, combining functions (a single plastic molded door handle instead of five separate metal clips, springs, and screws), and use snap-fits that self-locate. (This is like cooking the same meal with 30 pre-prepped ingredients ready to be thrown in a pan.)

Both cars perform the same function, but **Option B** requires **fewer workers, less floor space, less time, fewer defects, and lower tooling investment**. The retail price stays the same, but your **profit margin explodes**. That margin difference is the **Economic Significance of Assembly** captured in one sentence.

### 1.3 Why Assembly is Economically Pivotal

The following callout summarizes the four economic levers that make assembly design a boardroom-level decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Economic Levers of Assembly Design**
> 1. **Direct Labor Cost Reduction** – Every avoided part or fastening operation removes seconds (or minutes) from the takt time of the line.
> 2. **Capital Investment Avoidance** – Fewer assembly stations means smaller factory footprint, fewer robots, and lower depreciation.
> 3. **Quality and Rework Cost** – Every joint is a potential defect location. Fewer parts ⇒ fewer joints ⇒ lower warranty exposure.
> 4. **Inventory and Logistics Cost** – Fewer part numbers reduce SKU proliferation, warehousing, and supplier management overhead.

### 1.4 Standard Economic Metrics Used

Throughout this module, the following KTU-approved metrics will be referenced. They are formally defined in Section 2.

- **Total Assembly Cost ($C_A$)** – Monetary value of all joining operations for one unit.
- **DFA Index ($I_{DFA}$)** – Ratio of theoretically minimum parts to actual parts (expressed as a percentage).
- **Handling Time ($t_h$)** and **Insertion Time ($t_i$)** – Boothroyd's elemental time benchmarks.
- **Efficiency of Assembly ($E_a$)** – Ratio of ideal minimum assembly time to actual assembly time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Economic Analysis in Assembly

The economic significance of assembly is not a single number; it is a **three-pillar model** that engineers use to size and prioritize design decisions.

#### Pillar I — Cost of Assembly
Assembly cost is the cumulative expense of all activities required to transform a set of loose components into a finished product. It includes:
- **Handling cost** – picking, orienting, and positioning each part.
- **Insertion / joining cost** – fastening, welding, snapping, or bonding.
- **Tooling and equipment cost** – amortized over the production volume.
- **Indirect cost** – supervisory overhead, floor space, utilities, rework.

#### Pillar II — Design Efficiency
This pillar asks: *Is the part count justified by its function?* The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method** assigns a *minimum part count* and quantifies the penalty for every extra part. The economic penalty is expressed in seconds of added cycle time, which is then monetized.

#### Pillar III — Process Selection Economics
Not all joining methods are equal. **Snap-fits** are cheap in high volume; **threaded fasteners** are expensive in labor but flexible in service; **welding** is fast but capital-heavy. The economic significance lies in selecting the *lowest cost-per-join* method that satisfies the design requirement.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Boothroyd Cost Model

Boothroyd's framework translates design decisions into economic numbers via the following logic chain:

1. **Enumerate** every part in the assembly.
2. For each part, ask the **three DFA questions**:
   - Does the part move relative to all other parts already assembled?
   - Does the part require a different material or material property?
   - Does the part need to be separated for assembly, service, or recycling?
3. If the answer to **all three** is *No*, the part is a candidate for **elimination or integration** — generating an economic saving.
4. For retained parts, **time** is assigned for handling ($t_h$) and insertion ($t_i$) from Boothroyd's empirical tables.
5. **Total assembly time** and **total assembly cost** are summed across all parts.
6. The **DFA efficiency** is computed and benchmarked.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield equation bank** for Module 2 examination questions.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_A$ | Total Assembly Cost per unit | $C_A = C_m + C_l + C_c$ | ₹ / unit | $C_m$=material, $C_l$=labor, $C_c$=capital |
| $t_{total}$ | Total Assembly Time per unit | $t_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (t_{h,j} + t_{i,j})$ | seconds | $n$ = number of parts |
| $C_l$ | Labor Cost per unit | $C_l = t_{total} \times r$ | ₹ | $r$ = labor rate (₹/sec) |
| $I_{DFA}$ | DFA Index | $I_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better |
| $E_a$ | Assembly Efficiency | $E_a = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_m}{t_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | $t_m$ = ideal time per part (≈ 3 s) |
| $C_s$ | Cost Saving per unit | $C_s = C_{A,old} - C_{A,new}$ | ₹ | Direct measure of redesign benefit |
| $\Delta C$ | Annual Savings | $\Delta C = C_s \times Q$ | ₹ / year | $Q$ = annual production volume |
| $PBP$ | Payback Period | $PBP = \dfrac{I_{tool}}{C_s \times Q}$ | years | $I_{tool}$ = tooling investment |
| $\eta_{join}$ | Joining Method Efficiency | $\eta_{join} = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | $C_{min}$ = lowest cost joining method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Reminder:** The minimum theoretical handling + insertion time per part in Boothroyd's DFA method is approximately **3 seconds**. This is the gold standard benchmark — the *ideal* condition.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

In high-volume industries such as **automotive, consumer electronics, and white goods**, a saving of just **2 seconds per assembly** on a product made at **5,00,000 units/year** translates to:

- **10,00,000 seconds** = **277 labor-hours saved per year**.
- At a fully-burdened labor rate of ₹500/hour, this equals **₹1.38 lakh in direct labor savings per year**, *per assembly station*, *per product variant*.

Multiply this across hundreds of stations and variants, and the **economic significance of assembly design** becomes a multi-crore annual strategic lever for the firm. This is precisely why KTU places this topic in the core of the DFMA course.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Assembly Cost from First Principles

The total cost of assembling a single product is the sum of three direct cost components plus an indirect overhead allocation. We derive it from the ground up.

**Step 1 — Define the elemental assembly time for the $j^{th}$ part.**

Every part, before it is joined, must be picked from a feeder, oriented, and brought to the join location. This is the **handling time** $t_{h,j}$. The actual mechanical joining consumes an additional **insertion time** $t_{i,j}$.

$$
t_{j} = t_{h,j} + t_{i,j}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the elemental times across all $n$ parts.**

$$
t_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (t_{h,j} + t_{i,j})
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the total time into a labor cost.**

If the fully-burdened labor rate is $r$ (in ₹ per second), then the labor cost per unit is:

$$
C_{l} = r \cdot t_{total} = r \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} (t_{h,j} + t_{i,j})
$$

**Step 4 — Add the material and capital cost contributions.**

Material cost $C_m$ is the sum of per-part raw material + purchased component costs. Capital cost $C_c$ is the amortized tooling and equipment cost per unit:

$$
C_{c} = \frac{I_{tool}}{Q}
$$

where $I_{tool}$ is the total tooling investment and $Q$ is the production volume over the tool's life.

**Step 5 — Combine all components.**

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} + C_{l} + C_{c}
$$

Substituting the labor expression:

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} + r \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} (t_{h,j} + t_{i,j}) + \frac{I_{tool}}{Q}
$$

This is the **master economic equation of assembly** and is the starting point for every quantitative DFA problem at KTU.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The DFA Index

The DFA index compares the *actual* number of parts in a design against the *theoretically minimum* number of parts required to satisfy the function.

**Step 1 — Identify the minimum number of parts $N_{min}$.**

Apply Boothroyd's three-question test to every part. Count only those that answer *Yes* to at least one question.

**Step 2 — Count the actual parts $N_a$.**

This is simply the BOM (Bill of Materials) count.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
I_{DFA} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100\%
$$

A high $I_{DFA}$ (close to 100%) indicates an **economically efficient** design with minimal unnecessary parts. A low $I_{DFA}$ (e.g., 40%) signals **massive economic waste** and a redesign opportunity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Assembly Efficiency and its Cost Interpretation

The assembly efficiency $E_a$ is a normalized measure of how close the actual design is to the ideal Boothroyd benchmark.

**Step 1 — Define the ideal minimum time.**

The minimum theoretical time per part is $t_m \approx 3$ seconds. For $N_{min}$ parts, the ideal assembly time is:

$$
t_{ideal} = N_{min} \cdot t_m
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the actual time $t_{actual}$.**

$$
E_{a} = \frac{t_{ideal}}{t_{actual}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Convert efficiency to cost.**

If the labor rate is $r$, the *cost penalty* for design inefficiency per unit is:

$$
C_{ineff} = r \cdot (t_{actual} - t_{ideal}) = r \cdot t_{ideal} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_a} - 1\right)
$$

The annual economic loss is:

$$
\Delta C_{loss} = C_{ineff} \times Q = r \cdot t_{ideal} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_a} - 1\right) \cdot Q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $E_a$ matters more than absolute part count:**
> Two products with the same $N_a$ can have very different $E_a$ because the *complexity* of handling and insertion varies. The DFA index $I_{DFA}$ rewards part *elimination*; the efficiency $E_a$ rewards part *simplification*. KTU questions frequently test both metrics in parallel.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A consumer electronics product has $N_a = 24$ parts. After applying Boothroyd's three-question test, the minimum theoretical part count is $N_{min} = 14$. The average handling time is $t_h = 2.5$ s and the average insertion time is $t_i = 4.0$ s. The labor rate is $r = ₹0.50$ per second. Annual production is $Q = 1,00,000$ units. The tooling investment for a redesigned version is $I_{tool} = ₹2,00,000$ and saves 6 parts (so the new part count is 18). Compute (i) the original DFA index, (ii) the original assembly efficiency, (iii) the annual cost saving, and (iv) the payback period.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the DFA Index (Original Design).**

$$
I_{DFA,old} = \frac{14}{24} \times 100\% = 58.33\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Actual and Ideal Assembly Times.**

$$
t_{actual} = 24 \times (2.5 + 4.0) = 24 \times 6.5 = 156 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{ideal} = 14 \times 3 = 42 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Original Assembly Efficiency.**

$$
E_{a,old} = \frac{42}{156} \times 100\% = 26.92\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Original Labor Cost per Unit.**

$$
C_{l,old} = 0.50 \times 156 = ₹78 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the New Assembly Time (Redesigned, 18 parts).**

$$
t_{new} = 18 \times 6.5 = 117 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the New Labor Cost per Unit.**

$$
C_{l,new} = 0.50 \times 117 = ₹58.5 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Per-Unit Cost Saving.**

$$
C_s = 78 - 58.5 = ₹19.5 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Annual Cost Saving.**

$$
\Delta C = 19.5 \times 1,00,000 = ₹19,50,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the Payback Period.**

$$
PBP = \frac{I_{tool}}{\Delta C} = \frac{2,00,000}{19,50,000} = 0.1026 \text{ years} \approx 37.5 \text{ days}
$$

**Interpretation:** The redesign pays for itself in **just over a month**, a textbook example of the **economic significance of intelligent assembly design**. KTU examiners love this kind of multi-part numerical because it tests all the formulas in a single coherent narrative.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Economic Path

| Design Lever | Cost Impact | Quality Impact | Time-to-Market | Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Part Elimination | **High** ↓ | Neutral | **High** ↓ | Functional regression risk |
| Part Integration (multi-function parts) | **High** ↓ | Slight ↓ | Medium ↓ | Tooling complexity ↑ |
| Snap-fit replacement for screws | Medium ↓ | Slight ↑ | Medium ↓ | Material upgrade cost |
| Modular sub-assemblies | Medium ↓ | **High** ↑ | **High** ↓ | Interface standardization needed |
| Automated assembly (robotics) | High ↑ in CAPEX, Low ↓ in OPEX | **High** ↑ | High ↓ | High break-even volume |
| Standardized fasteners | Low ↓ | Neutral | Low ↓ | None |

> [!NOTE]
> The economically optimal solution is rarely the technically maximalist one. KTU 2024 questions often test the student's ability to **select the right lever** given constraints (volume, budget, timeline) — not just to compute numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Economic Analysis Flow of an Assembly

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete economic analysis workflow that a DFMA engineer follows when evaluating the economic significance of an assembly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Product Concept] --> B[Enumerate All Parts BOM]
    B --> C[Apply Boothroyd Three-Question Test]
    C --> D{Part Retained?}
    D -- No --> E[Mark for Elimination or Integration]
    D -- Yes --> F[Assign Handling Time th]
    F --> G[Assign Insertion Time ti]
    G --> H[Sum Total Assembly Time t_total]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Compute Labor Cost Cl = r x t_total]
    I --> J[Add Material Cost Cm]
    J --> K[Add Amortized Capital Cost Cc = I_tool / Q]
    K --> L[Compute Total Assembly Cost CA]
    L --> M[Compute DFA Index I_DFA]
    L --> N[Compute Assembly Efficiency Ea]
    M --> O[Compare with Benchmark Target]
    N --> O
    O --> P{Economically Acceptable?}
    P -- No --> C
    P -- Yes --> Q[Lock Design and Release to Production]
    Q --> R[End: Cost-Optimized Assembly]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Cost Reduction Decision Tree

This second diagram captures the **decision logic** an engineer uses when selecting between joining methods on an economic basis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Required Joint Strength] --> S2{Joint Permanent or Removable?}
    S2 -- Permanent --> P1{Welding Possible?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Use Welding: Low Labor Cost]
    P1 -- No --> P3[Use Adhesive Bonding]
    S2 -- Removable --> R1{Disassembly Frequency}
    R1 -- Frequent --> R3[Use Snap-Fits: Zero Tooling Cost]
    R1 -- Occasional --> R4[Use Threaded Fasteners: High Labor Cost]
    R1 -- Never --> R5[Use Rivets or One-Way Clips]
    P2 --> Z[Compute Cost-per-Join and Select Lowest]
    P3 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    Z --> END[Economic Joining Method Selected]
```

### 4.3 Cost-Component Sankey-Style Architecture (Text Block Fallback)

The following block-level architecture describes the **flow of cost contribution** into the total assembly cost $C_A$. It is rendered as a hierarchical text map because the Mermaid engine cannot natively represent Sankey proportions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CA[Total Assembly Cost CA]
    CA --> CM[Material Cost Cm]
    CA --> CL[Labor Cost Cl]
    CA --> CC[Capital Cost Cc]
    CL --> LH[Handling Sub-Cost]
    CL --> LI[Insertion Sub-Cost]
    LH --> LH1[Pick from Feeder]
    LH --> LH2[Orient the Part]
    LH --> LH3[Position at Join Point]
    LI --> LI1[Fastening Torque]
    LI --> LI2[Locating and Seating]
    LI --> LI3[Verification Time]
    CC --> CC1[Tooling Amortization]
    CC --> CC2[Equipment Depreciation]
    CC --> CC3[Factory Space Allocation]
```

### 4.4 Economic Significance Hierarchy (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Economic Significance of Assembly]
    TOP --> L1[1. Direct Cost Reduction]
    TOP --> L2[2. Indirect Cost Avoidance]
    TOP --> L3[3. Strategic Competitiveness]
    L1 --> L1a[Lower labor minutes per unit]
    L1 --> L1b[Smaller factory footprint]
    L1 --> L1c[Lower tooling amortization]
    L2 --> L2a[Lower inventory carrying cost]
    L2 --> L2b[Lower rework and warranty cost]
    L2 --> L2c[Lower supplier management overhead]
    L3 --> L3a[Faster time-to-market]
    L3 --> L3b[Higher product reliability]
    L3 --> L3c[Improved profit margin]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
*Define the term "Economic Significance of Assembly" in the context of DFMA. State any two reasons why assembly design is considered a strategic activity in product development.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The economic significance of assembly refers to the monetary impact that the design of the assembly process and the count/complexity of parts exert on the total product cost, time-to-market, and competitive position of a firm.

Two reasons it is strategic:
1. Assembly typically accounts for **30%–50%** of total product cost, so even small per-unit savings scale into large annual profits.
2. The number of parts and the choice of joining method directly govern the **factory footprint, tooling investment, labor headcount, and warranty exposure**, all of which are boardroom-level decisions.

*(Award 1.5 marks for the definition and 1.5 marks for the two reasons.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
*What is the DFA Index? Write its formula and explain what a value of 60% indicates about a design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The DFA Index ($I_{DFA}$) is the ratio of the minimum theoretical number of parts to the actual number of parts in an assembly, expressed as a percentage.

$$
I_{DFA} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_a} \times 100\%
$$

A value of **60%** indicates that the design contains **40% more parts than functionally necessary**, representing a significant economic inefficiency and a strong candidate for redesign through part elimination or integration.

*(Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the interpretation, and 1 mark for the inference on redesign potential.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Common Stem:**
> A sub-assembly of an automotive product consists of **20 parts**. After applying Boothroyd's three-question test, the minimum theoretical part count is determined to be **11 parts**. The average handling time per part is **2.0 s** and the average insertion time is **5.0 s**. The labor rate is **₹0.40 per second**, the annual production volume is **80,000 units**, and the fully-burdened material cost per unit is **₹120**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compute the **original DFA index** and the **original assembly efficiency** of the sub-assembly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The company is considering a redesign that reduces the part count to **15 parts** with the same average handling and insertion times. Compute the **annual cost saving in labor** and comment on the **economic significance** of the redesign. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the **master economic equation for total assembly cost** and apply it to compute the **original total assembly cost per unit** for the sub-assembly described in the common stem. Assume the amortized capital cost per unit is **₹8**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the proposed redesign (with 15 parts) requires a **tooling investment of ₹1,50,000** that is amortized over the annual volume, compute the **new total assembly cost per unit** and the **payback period** for the redesign. (7 Marks)

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Compute the original DFA index:

$$
I_{DFA,old} = \frac{11}{20} \times 100\% = 55\%
$$

Step 2 — Compute the actual and ideal assembly times:

$$
t_{actual} = 20 \times (2.0 + 5.0) = 20 \times 7 = 140 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{ideal} = 11 \times 3 = 33 \text{ s}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the original assembly efficiency:

$$
E_{a,old} = \frac{33}{140} \times 100\% = 23.57\%
$$

**Comment:** An efficiency of only **23.57%** indicates that the design is using **roughly four times more time** than the theoretical minimum — a strong economic red flag.

*(Award 2 marks for the index, 3 marks for the times, and 2 marks for the efficiency + comment.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Compute the new actual assembly time:

$$
t_{new} = 15 \times 7 = 105 \text{ s}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the original and new labor costs per unit:

$$
C_{l,old} = 0.40 \times 140 = ₹56 \text{ per unit}
$$

$$
C_{l,new} = 0.40 \times 105 = ₹42 \text{ per unit}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the per-unit and annual labor savings:

$$
C_s = 56 - 42 = ₹14 \text{ per unit}
$$

$$
\Delta C = 14 \times 80,000 = ₹11,20,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Comment on Economic Significance:** A redesign that removes just 5 parts (25% reduction) yields **₹11.2 lakh in annual labor savings** at this volume. The economic significance scales non-linearly with volume: at 8 lakh units annually (e.g., a mass-market vehicle), the same redesign would save over **₹1.12 crore per year**, demonstrating the *multiplicative* power of DFA in high-volume manufacturing.

*(Award 2 marks for times, 2 marks for costs, 2 marks for the annual saving, and 1 mark for the comment.)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation:**

From Section 3.1, the master economic equation is:

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} + r \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} (t_{h,j} + t_{i,j}) + \frac{I_{tool}}{Q}
$$

**Application to the problem:**

Step 1 — Compute the labor cost:

$$
C_{l} = 0.40 \times 140 = ₹56
$$

Step 2 — Sum the components:

$$
C_{A,old} = 120 + 56 + 8 = ₹184 \text{ per unit}
$$

*(Award 3 marks for the derivation and 4 marks for the numerical application.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Compute the new labor cost:

$$
C_{l,new} = 0.40 \times 105 = ₹42
$$

Step 2 — Compute the new amortized capital cost per unit:

$$
C_{c,new} = \frac{1,50,000}{80,000} = ₹1.875
$$

Step 3 — Compute the new total assembly cost:

$$
C_{A,new} = 120 + 42 + 1.875 = ₹163.875 \text{ per unit}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the per-unit saving and annual saving:

$$
C_s = 184 - 163.875 = ₹20.125 \text{ per unit}
$$

$$
\Delta C = 20.125 \times 80,000 = ₹16,10,000 \text{ per year}
$$

Step 5 — Compute the payback period for the **tooling investment alone**:

$$
PBP_{tool} = \frac{1,50,000}{16,10,000} \approx 0.0932 \text{ years} \approx 34 \text{ days}
$$

**Comment:** The tooling investment is recovered in just over a month, confirming that assembly-driven redesigns offer some of the **fastest payback periods** in the entire product development portfolio.

*(Award 2 marks for the new cost, 2 marks for the per-unit and annual saving, 2 marks for the payback period, and 1 mark for the comment.)*

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Assembly Economic Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> 1. **Forgetting the units conversion:** Students often write $r = 0.40$ and forget to specify ₹/second. If $r$ is given as ₹/hour, divide by 3600 before substitution. Losing 1 mark here is routine.
> 2. **Using $t_m = 3$ s for $t_{ideal}$ inconsistently:** Some textbooks use 1.5 s or 2 s. Always state the assumed value of $t_m$ explicitly in your answer. KTU examiners reward clarity.
> 3. **Confusing $I_{DFA}$ and $E_a$:** The DFA index is a count ratio; the efficiency is a time ratio. They are **not interchangeable**. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Omitting the comment line:** KTU's 2024 scheme rewards **interpretation and economic reasoning** for at least 1–2 marks in every 14-mark question. A numerically correct answer with no concluding remark will be capped at 12/14.
> 5. **Not amortizing tooling over volume:** The capital cost $C_c = I_{tool}/Q$ is the most-skipped step. Examiners explicitly test this every cycle.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] Assembly accounts for **30%–50%** of total product cost — it is a strategic activity, not a downstream afterthought.
- [ ] The **Boothroyd three-question test** is the gatekeeper for part elimination: motion, material, or disassembly.
- [ ] The **DFA Index** $I_{DFA} = N_{min}/N_a$ measures part-count efficiency (ideal = 100%).
- [ ] The **Assembly Efficiency** $E_a$ measures time efficiency against the 3-second-per-part benchmark.
- [ ] The **master economic equation** is $C_A = C_m + r \cdot t_{total} + I_{tool}/Q$.
- [ ] **Always convert** labor rates to the same time unit (seconds) as the assembly time before computing $C_l$.
- [ ] **Cost saving** is $C_s = C_{A,old} - C_{A,new}$; **Annual saving** is $\Delta C = C_s \times Q$.
- [ ] **Payback period** $PBP = I_{tool}/\Delta C$ is the key decision metric for justifying a redesign investment.
- [ ] The economic significance of assembly **scales with volume** — small per-unit savings × huge volume = large annual profit.
- [ ] End every numerical answer with a **one-line economic interpretation** — this is the KTU 2024 Scheme's signature ask.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Taxonomies of Assembly Operation and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Taxonomies of Assembly Operation and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Assembly Taxonomy** is a systematic, hierarchical classification of assembly operations based on quantifiable engineering parameters such as the *number of motion directions* required for part mating, *type of fastening/joining operation*, *degree of automation*, and *handling complexity*. The taxonomy, as formalized by **Boothroyd, Dewhurst, and Knight**, provides a structured framework for evaluating manufacturability and assemblability of a product during the conceptual and embodiment design phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module expects students to be able to (i) classify assembly operations using primary, secondary, and tertiary taxonomies, (ii) compute Design for Assembly (DFA) indices, (iii) differentiate between manual, semi-automatic, and fully automatic assembly systems, and (iv) select an appropriate system based on production volume, part complexity, and economic constraints.

The **General Taxonomy of Assembly Systems** is the broader classification of the *production infrastructure* itself — i.e., how workstations, transfer mechanisms, operators, and robots are arranged to perform the assembly process. It includes:
- **Single-station systems** (one operator/robot, all operations at one place)
- **Multi-station manual lines** (workers along a conveyor)
- **Automated in-line systems** (transfer lines with dedicated equipment)
- **Rotary indexing systems** (carousel-type dial machines)
- **Flexible robotic cells** (reprogrammable, low-to-medium volume)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The LEGO Workshop

Imagine you are a child asked to build a LEGO castle. Before you start, you instinctively **sort** the bricks by:
1. **Size & Color** (which one goes where? → *secondary classification*),
2. **Type of Connection** (stud-and-tube, hinge, clip → *tertiary classification*), and
3. **How you place them** (straight down, slide-in, twist → *primary classification*).

You then choose **how** to build it: alone (**single station**), with friends along a table (**multi-station line**), or using a motorized turntable (**rotary system**). 

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A taxonomy is essentially a *decision tree* that helps a designer ask the right questions early — *"How many ways can this part be mis-oriented? Does it need to be flipped? Does it need a screwdriver, a press, or just a push?"* — saving enormous cost downstream.

### 1.3 The Three-Level Boothroyd Taxonomy (At a Glance)

| Level | Basis of Classification | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Direction & type of mating motion | Linear, rotary, complex path |
| **Secondary** | Securing / joining method | Threaded fastener, snap fit, welding, adhesive |
| **Tertiary** | Pre-/Post-assembly operations | Cleaning, lubrication, inspection, marking |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Mandatory Notation)

- **Design for Assembly Index (DFA Index)** is expressed as a percentage, ideal value = **100%**.
- **Minimum number of parts (N_min)** is an integer; cannot be lower than **1**.
- **Time metrics** are measured in **seconds (s)** — Boothroyd uses a 5-second baseline benchmark.
- **3-second rule:** A manual operation exceeding **3 seconds** in handling warrants redesign.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Assembly Operation Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Primary Taxonomy — Motion Classification

The **primary taxonomy** classifies every assembly operation by the *relative motion* required between the part being added and the sub-assembly it joins.

- **0° — No relative motion** (e.g., placing a cover plate that simply rests)
- **1° — Single linear motion** (e.g., a peg going into a hole — vertical drop)
- **2° — Linear + Rotary** (e.g., inserting and twisting a quarter-turn fastener)
- **3° — Three directions or complex path** (e.g., threading a wire through a chassis)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Rule:** Each additional motion direction roughly *doubles* the time required for insertion and *quadruples* the chance of jamming or misalignment. This is the foundation of the DFA cost penalty.

#### 2.1.2 Secondary Taxonomy — Securing / Fastening Classification

After mating, parts must often be *secured*. The Boothroyd secondary taxonomy groups these into:

| Code | Operation Type | Typical Time (s) | Tooling Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S0** | Self-securing (snap, press, interference) | 1.5 – 2.0 | None |
| **S1** | Single fastener (1 screw) | 4.0 – 5.0 | Moderate |
| **S2** | Multiple fasteners (2–4) | 6.0 – 10.0 | High |
| **S3** | Welding / brazing / soldering | 8.0 – 15.0 | Very High |
| **S4** | Adhesive / potting | 6.0 – 12.0 | Moderate |
| **S5** | Riveting | 5.0 – 8.0 | Moderate |

#### 2.1.3 Tertiary Taxonomy — Auxiliary Operations

These are *non-joining* tasks that must occur during assembly: cleaning, deburring, inspection, marking, lubrication, orientation alignment, etc. They are not part of the joining act but consume time and cost.

### 2.2 Classification of Assembly Systems (The Macro View)

Assembly systems are classified by **degree of automation** and **spatial configuration**:

#### A. By Automation Level

1. **Manual Assembly** — Human operator performs *all* operations using hand tools.
   - Suitable for: Low volume (< 10,000 units/yr), high variety, large/heavy parts.
   - Cost: Low tooling, high labor.
2. **Semi-Automatic** — Operator *loads* parts; machine performs insertion/fastening.
   - Suitable for: Medium volume (10,000 – 100,000 units/yr).
3. **Fully Automatic** — Machine (or robot) loads, mates, and fastens without human intervention.
   - Suitable for: High volume (> 100,000 units/yr), small parts, tight tolerances.

#### B. By Spatial Configuration

1. **Single Station** — One location; all operations performed sequentially at one point. Most flexible, lowest throughput.
2. **In-Line Transfer** — Workstations along a conveyor; each station does one operation. High throughput, low flexibility.
3. **Rotary Indexing (Dial)** — Workpieces mounted on a rotating table; each station performs one operation as the table indexes. Excellent for small parts, high speed.
4. **Robotic Cell** — One or more robots serving multiple stations; reprogrammable; ideal for medium-volume, high-variety production.
5. **Mixed-Model / Hybrid Lines** — Combine manual and automated stations.

### 2.3 The Manual vs. Automatic Assembly Time Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boothroyd's Two-Component Time Model** is the cornerstone of every DFA problem. Memorize it.

For **manual assembly** of a single part, the cycle time is:

$$t_{manual} = t_{handling} + t_{insertion} + t_{fastening}$$

Where:
- $t_{handling}$ = time to grasp, orient, and place the part at the insertion point (typically **1.5 – 3.0 s**).
- $t_{insertion}$ = time to perform the mating motion (depends on number of motion directions).
- $t_{fastening}$ = time for any secondary operation (screw, weld, etc.).

For **automatic assembly**, the model is:

$$t_{auto} = t_{index} + t_{mate} + t_{secure}$$

Where $t_{index}$ accounts for part feeding, orientation, and verification (typically the dominant cost).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM TIP:** In the table below, the symbol $\vert$ is written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax. Use this notation if a modulus ever appears.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DFA Index} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_{a,\,min}}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{a,\,actual}} \times 100$ | Boothroyd's DFA efficiency | **%** |
| 2 | $\text{DFA Score (Alt)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{t_{a,\,total}}$ | Simplified DFA score (target ≥ 60) | **%** |
| 3 | $t_{a} = t_{handle} + t_{insert} + t_{secure}$ | Total assembly time for one part | **s** |
| 4 | $N_{min}$ | Minimum parts the *function* requires | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{assembly} = C_{labor} \cdot t_{a} + C_{tooling} / N_{volume}$ | Total assembly cost per part | ₹ or \$ |
| 6 | $t_{min} = 1.5 \cdot N_{min}$ | Theoretical minimum assembly time | **s** |
| 7 | $\text{Handling Code} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{no symmetry needed} \\ 1 & \text{one axis symmetric} \\ 2 & \text{full symmetry / no orientation} \end{cases}$ | Difficulty of grasping/orienting | dimensionless |
| 8 | $n_{motions} = $ number of directions the part must move | Mating motion count | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_{piece\,\,part} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i)}{N}$ | Average cost per part in a system | ₹ or \$ |
| 10 | $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{t_{min,\,ideal}}{t_{actual}} \times 100$ | Time-based efficiency | **%** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A modern car has 8,000+ assembly operations; taxonomies decide which are manual (interior trim) and which are robotic (engine block).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Surface-mount technology (SMT) components use *self-securing* reflow solder — coded **S0** in Boothroyd's taxonomy — because it's fastest.
- **Aerospace:** Riveting classifications (**S2** or **S5**) dominate due to the high strength-to-weight demands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DFA Index (Boothroyd & Dewhurst)

The DFA index quantifies *how close* a design is to its theoretical optimum. We start from two fundamental ideas:

**Step 1 — Define the theoretical optimum.**
The absolute minimum time to assemble $N_{min}$ parts is governed by the fastest possible *self-securing* insertion (the **3-second rule**):

$$t_{a,\,min,\,ideal} = 1.5 \cdot N_{min} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

This 1.5 s baseline comes from empirical studies of the fastest manual snap-fit operations (Boothroyd observed experienced workers achieving ~1.5 s per part under ideal conditions).

**Step 2 — Define the actual assembly time.**
The actual time for the *real* design is the sum of handling, insertion, and securing times for each of the $N_{actual}$ parts:

$$t_{a,\,actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{actual}} \left( t_{handle,i} + t_{insert,i} + t_{secure,i} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
The DFA index is the ratio of the *theoretical minimum effort* to the *actual effort*, expressed as a percentage:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DFA Index} &= \frac{\text{Ideal Effort}}{\text{Actual Effort}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{N_{min} \cdot t_{a,\,min,\,ideal}}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{a,\,actual}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.5 \cdot N_{min}^2}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{a,\,actual}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- **DFA Index = 100%** → Design is theoretically perfect (minimum parts, minimum time). Unachievable in practice.
- **DFA Index ≥ 60%** → Considered *good* practice (Boothroyd benchmark).
- **DFA Index ≤ 30%** → Design has severe DFA violations; redesign mandatory.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Manual vs. Automatic Insertion Time

**Given:** A sub-assembly requires inserting a cylindrical peg into a blind hole. The peg must travel along **one linear direction** with no rotation. An operator can grasp it, orient it (axisymmetric → no orientation time), and insert it.

**Step 1 — Identify handling time.**
The part is *axisymmetric* (rotationally symmetric), so the operator need not worry about angular orientation. From Boothroyd's handling chart:

$$t_{handle} = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

(Reasoning: a symmetric part requires only translation to the insertion point; no flipping or reorientation.)

**Step 2 — Identify insertion time.**
For a *single-direction* motion, the base insertion time is 1.0 s. There is **no** secondary fastening (it is a press-fit, self-securing):

$$t_{insert} = 1.0 \text{ s}, \quad t_{secure} = 0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the total manual time.**

$$t_{manual} = 1.5 + 1.0 + 0 = 2.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the automatic time.**
For automatic assembly, the dominant cost is *part feeding* (vibratory bowl feeder) and *verification*. Typical values:

$$t_{feed} = 1.0 \text{ s}, \quad t_{index} = 0.5 \text{ s}, \quad t_{mate} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{auto} = 1.0 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Economic comparison.**
If labor cost = **₹1.0 / s** and machine cost = **₹0.5 / s**:

$$C_{manual} = 2.5 \times 1.0 = ₹2.50$$

$$C_{auto} = 2.0 \times 0.5 = ₹1.00$$

**Conclusion:** At high volume, automatic assembly is ~**60% cheaper** per part for this single operation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Part-Redesign Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Should I Redesign?" Matrix** is a classic 14-mark KTU question stem. Memorize the logic flow.

| DFA Index Range | Action | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| 80% – 100% | **Excellent** — Ship it | Near theoretical optimum |
| 60% – 79% | **Acceptable** — Minor tweaks | Industry benchmark achieved |
| 40% – 59% | **Reconsider** — Combine parts, reduce fasteners | Significant waste identified |
| 0% – 39% | **Redesign** — Return to concept stage | Fundamental architecture is poor |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Simplified DFA Score (Alternative Form)

A *simplified* version used in fast screening:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DFA Score} &= \frac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{t_{a,\,total}} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation logic:** The numerator represents the *theoretical best* (3 seconds × minimum parts). The denominator is the *actual* time. Multiplying by 100 converts to a percentage. This form is used when the designer wants a quick screening pass without summing every $t_{handle}$, $t_{insert}$, $t_{secure}$ individually.

**Example:** Suppose $N_{min} = 5$ and $t_{a,\,total} = 12$ s.

$$\text{DFA Score} = \frac{3 \times 5}{12} \times 100 = 125\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{capped at 100%}$$

A score **> 100%** indicates the design *exceeds* theoretical best — impossible physically, suggesting an error in measurement (a common KTU exam pitfall).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Three-Level Boothroyd Taxonomy (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ASSEMBLY OPERATION]:::root --> B[PRIMARY TAXONOMY]:::lvl1
    A --> C[SECONDARY TAXONOMY]:::lvl1
    A --> D[TERTIARY TAXONOMY]:::lvl1

    B --> B1[0 DIRECTIONS NO MOTION]:::leaf
    B --> B2[1 DIRECTION LINEAR]:::leaf
    B --> B3[2 DIRECTIONS LINEAR PLUS ROTARY]:::leaf
    B --> B4[3 DIRECTIONS COMPLEX PATH]:::leaf

    C --> C1[S0 SELF SECURING]:::leaf
    C --> C2[S1 SINGLE FASTENER]:::leaf
    C --> C3[S2 MULTIPLE FASTENERS]:::leaf
    C --> C4[S3 WELD OR BRAZE]:::leaf
    C --> C5[S4 ADHESIVE]:::leaf
    C --> C6[S5 RIVETING]:::leaf

    D --> D1[CLEANING]:::leaf
    D --> D2[DEBURRING]:::leaf
    D --> D3[INSPECTION]:::leaf
    D --> D4[MARKING]:::leaf
    D --> D5[LUBRICATION]:::leaf
    D --> D6[ORIENTATION]:::leaf

```

### 4.2 Assembly System Classification — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 -- LESS THAN 10000 --> M1[MANUAL SINGLE STATION]:::manual
    Q1 -- 10000 TO 100000 --> Q2{HIGH VARIETY}:::gate
    Q1 -- GREATER THAN 100000 --> A1[FULLY AUTOMATIC IN LINE]:::auto

    Q2 -- YES --> M2[ROBOTIC CELL FLEXIBLE]:::flex
    Q2 -- NO --> S1[ROTARY INDEXING DIAL]:::semi

    M1 --> M1D[OPERATOR + HAND TOOLS]:::note
    A1 --> A1D[TRANSFER LINE + ROBOTS]:::note
    M2 --> M2D[REPROGRAMMABLE 6 AXIS]:::note
    S1 --> S1D[CAROUSEL WITH FIXED STATIONS]:::note
```

### 4.3 The DFA Index Decision Tree — Is Redesign Required?

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    N --> T[CALCULATE T A ACTUAL]:::step
    T --> I[COMPUTE DFA INDEX]:::step
    I --> Q{DFA INDEX RANGE}:::gate

    Q -- 80 TO 100 --> R1[EXCELLENT PROCEED]:::go
    Q -- 60 TO 79 --> R2[ACCEPTABLE SHIP IT]:::go
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the **primary, secondary, and tertiary taxonomies** of assembly operations as given by Boothroyd. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Primary Taxonomy:** Classifies the assembly operation by the *type of motion* required to mate a part with the sub-assembly (e.g., linear, rotary, complex). *Example:* Inserting a peg into a hole = 1-direction linear motion.
- **Secondary Taxonomy:** Classifies the operation by the *securing/joining method* used after mating (e.g., self-securing, threaded fastener, welding, adhesive). *Example:* Tightening a screw = S1 single fastener.
- **Tertiary Taxonomy:** Classifies the *auxiliary operations* that must be performed during assembly but are not joining actions (e.g., cleaning, deburring, inspection, marking, lubrication). *Example:* Cleaning a bearing surface before mounting.

*(1 mark per correct definition + example = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** What is the **3-second rule** in Boothroyd's DFA methodology? Why is it used as a benchmark?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **3-second rule** states that if the *handling time* for a part exceeds **3 seconds**, the part's geometry should be redesigned for easier grasping, orientation, or feeding.
- **Reason for benchmark:** Empirical studies by Boothroyd showed that the *fastest possible* manual assembly operation (a self-securing, axisymmetric part) takes about **1.5 seconds**. Operations taking more than twice this baseline are considered inefficient and likely to cause bottlenecks.
- **Use:** It serves as a *quick screening tool* during the concept design phase to flag parts requiring geometric redesign (e.g., adding a chamfer, a flat grip surface, or a single locating feature).

*(1 mark for rule + 1 mark for reason + 1 mark for use-case = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q A.(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd classification of assembly systems** with reference to (i) manual assembly, (ii) semi-automatic assembly, and (iii) fully automatic assembly. For each, state **one advantage** and **one disadvantage**, and indicate the **typical production volume range**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| System Type | Typical Volume | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | < 10,000 units/yr | Highest flexibility, lowest tooling cost | Labor-intensive, ergonomic limits, variable quality |
| **Semi-automatic** | 10,000 – 100,000 units/yr | Balances flexibility with throughput; operator handles non-ergonomic loading | Requires skilled operator; system tuning needed |
| **Fully Automatic** | > 100,000 units/yr | High throughput, consistent quality, low per-unit cost at scale | High capital investment; low flexibility; expensive changeover |

*(1 mark for each correctly described system, 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for volume range = 7 marks)*

**Q A.(b)** A product consists of **12 parts** in the current design, but a design review reveals that the **minimum number of parts (N_min) required for function is 7**. The actual measured assembly time is **45 seconds** per unit. Calculate the **DFA Index** and comment on the design quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**
$N_{min} = 7$, $N_{actual} = 12$, $t_{a,\,actual} = 45$ s.

**Step 2 — Compute ideal minimum time.**

$$t_{a,\,min,\,ideal} = 1.5 \cdot N_{min} = 1.5 \times 7 = 10.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DFA Index formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DFA Index} &= \frac{N_{min} \cdot t_{a,\,min,\,ideal}}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{a,\,actual}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7 \times 10.5}{12 \times 45} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{73.5}{540} \times 100 \\
&= 13.61\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

$$\text{DFA Index} \approx 13.6\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LIES IN 0\%–39\% RANGE}$$

**Conclusion:** The design is *fundamentally poor*. It has **5 extra parts** and the assembly time is **more than 4× the theoretical minimum**. The product must be **redesigned at the conceptual stage** — combine parts using integral features, eliminate redundant fasteners, and consolidate sub-assemblies. *(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for computation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

**Q B.(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch/flowchart**, describe the **three-level Boothroyd taxonomy** of assembly operations. For each level, list **two real-world examples** from the automobile industry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Level 1 — Primary Taxonomy (Motion-based):**
- *Example 1:* Pressing a piston pin into a connecting rod → **1-direction linear motion**.
- *Example 2:* Tightening a spark plug with a torque wrench → **linear + rotary motion (2 directions)**.

**Level 2 — Secondary Taxonomy (Securing-based):**
- *Example 1:* Snap-fit mounting of a plastic bumper clip → **S0 (self-securing)**.
- *Example 2:* Spot-welding of car body panels → **S3 (welding)**.

**Level 3 — Tertiary Taxonomy (Auxiliary):**
- *Example 1:* Cleaning brake disc surfaces before caliper assembly → **cleaning**.
- *Example 2:* Laser-etching the chassis number → **marking**.

A flowchart (as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes) should accompany this answer, classifying the root "Assembly Operation" into Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary branches with their respective sub-classes. *(2 marks per level = 6 marks, 1 mark for the flowchart/structure = 7 marks)*

**Q B.(b)** A manufacturer is producing an electronic device at **150,000 units/year**. Compare the **cost per part** for:
- **Option 1:** Manual assembly station with a labor cost of **₹1.2/s** and an average manual assembly time of **8 s/part**.
- **Option 2:** Fully automatic in-line system with a machine cost of **₹0.6/s** and an automatic cycle time of **5 s/part**, but with a fixed tooling amortization cost of **₹5,00,000/year**.

Which option is **economically superior**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Variable assembly cost per part.**

**Option 1 (Manual):**
$$C_{var,1} = 1.2 \text{ ₹/s} \times 8 \text{ s} = ₹9.60 \text{ / part}$$

**Option 2 (Automatic):**
$$C_{var,2} = 0.6 \text{ ₹/s} \times 5 \text{ s} = ₹3.00 \text{ / part}$$

**Step 2 — Fixed cost per part (Option 2 only).**

$$C_{fix,2} = \frac{₹5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = ₹3.33 \text{ / part}$$

**Step 3 — Total cost per part.**

$$C_{total,1} = ₹9.60 \text{ / part}$$

$$C_{total,2} = ₹3.00 + ₹3.33 = ₹6.33 \text{ / part}$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

$$C_{total,1} = ₹9.60 \quad \text{vs.} \quad C_{total,2} = ₹6.33$$

**Option 2 (Fully Automatic) is economically superior**, saving **₹3.27 per part**. At 150,000 units/year, this is an annual saving of:

$$\text{Saving} = 3.27 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}90{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

*(1 mark for variable cost, 1 mark for fixed cost, 2 marks for totals, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for annual saving, 1 mark for final recommendation = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** in the DFA Index formula — a single, costly slip. The index is a *percentage*, not a decimal.
> 2. **Confusing $N_{min}$ with $N_{actual}$** — $N_{min}$ is the *theoretical* minimum (function-only), $N_{actual}$ is what the design *has* (often higher). Mixing them up gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 3. **Ignoring the secondary taxonomy** — Many students classify only by motion (primary) and forget the *securing* time ($t_{secure}$). This underestimates $t_{a,\,actual}$ by 30–50%.
> 4. **No unit consistency in cost problems** — Always state costs in ₹/s or \$/s and times in seconds. Mixing minutes and seconds is the #1 arithmetic error.
> 5. **Forgetting to amortize fixed tooling** — A robot is expensive; its cost must be spread over the production volume. Examiners specifically look for this in cost-comparison problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam.**

- ✅ **Boothroyd's Three Taxonomies** — Primary (motion), Secondary (securing), Tertiary (auxiliary).
- ✅ **DFA Index = $\dfrac{1.5 \cdot N_{min}^2}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{a,\,actual}} \times 100$** — always a percentage; ideal = 100%.
- ✅ **Acceptable DFA Index ≥ 60%**; below 30% mandates redesign.
- ✅ **The 3-second rule** — handling > 3 s → redesign the part geometry.
- ✅ **Self-securing (S0) = fastest**; welding (S3) = slowest but strongest.
- ✅ **Manual assembly** for < 10,000 units/yr; **rotary/in-line automatic** for > 100,000 units/yr.
- ✅ **Manual time** = $t_{handle} + t_{insert} + t_{secure}$; **Auto time** = $t_{feed} + t_{index} + t_{mate}$.
- ✅ **Cost comparison problems** must include *both* variable (per-second) and fixed (amortized) costs.
- ✅ **Axisymmetric parts** have handling time ≈ 1.5 s; **fully asymmetric** parts need 3.0 s.
- ✅ **Single-station systems** = most flexible; **rotary indexing** = best for small parts, high speed; **robotic cells** = best for high-variety, medium-volume.
- ✅ **Three primary motion types** — 0-D (no motion), 1-D (linear drop), 2-D (linear + rotary), 3-D (complex path).
- ✅ **Industry benchmark:** Modern DFA-optimized products achieve DFA Index of 70–85%.
- ✅ **Memorize the 1.5 s baseline** — it is the *theoretical minimum* per part in Boothroyd's model.
- ✅ **Examiner tip:** Always draw a *neat, labeled flowchart* (as in Section 4) for taxonomy questions — it earns easy 2–3 marks even if the explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling a Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembling a Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembly** is the process of joining two or more individual parts (sub-assemblies or components) together by a variety of means (fastening, welding, adhesives, snap-fits, etc.) to form a functional, complete product. In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, assembling a product is treated as a **systematic cost, time, and quality engineering problem** rather than a mere shop-floor joining operation.

The KTU 2024 scheme defines *Assembling a Product* as the structured engineering activity of:
1. **Planning** the assembly sequence,
2. **Selecting** appropriate joining methods,
3. **Estimating** the time and cost of bringing individual components together, and
4. **Optimising** the design so that the minimum number of parts can be assembled in the most efficient, error-free, and economical manner.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of assembling an **IKEA bookshelf** 🛠️ or a **LEGO castle** 🧱:

- In a **LEGO castle**, every block snaps onto the next in one orientation, with no screws, no glue, and no confusion. That is **excellent Design for Assembly (DFA)** — minimum parts, single-direction insertion, self-locating features.
- In a **flat-pack IKEA shelf**, however, you must identify 30+ similar-looking wooden dowels, hold a panel vertically, align a cam-lock, and turn a hex key — an assembly nightmare born of poor DFA.

The lesson: **the way a product is designed dictates how easily (or painfully) it can be assembled.** Assembly is not a downstream activity — it is a **design parameter**.

## 1.3 Why "Assembling a Product" is a Core DFMA Pillar

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry studies (Suri, Boothroyd, Dewhurst) consistently show that **assembly typically consumes 40 % to 60 % of total manufacturing time** and **20 % to 30 % of total production cost** for discrete products. Therefore, designing *for* ease of assembly is one of the highest-leverage decisions a design engineer can make.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Assembly Engineering

| Pillar | What it Asks | Engineering Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Part Reduction** | Can this part be merged, eliminated, or combined? | Fewer parts → lower cost, fewer defects |
| **Handling & Insertion** | Can the part be picked up and inserted easily? | Reduce α and β times |
| **Joining Method** | What is the most efficient way to fasten? | Snap-fit > screw > weld > adhesive |

## 1.5 Common Assembly Metrics (used throughout this module)

- **Handling time ($t_h$)** — time to grasp, orient, and present a part for joining.
- **Insertion time ($t_i$)** — time to align, mate, and secure the part.
- **Total assembly time ($T_A$)** — sum of all handling and insertion times.
- **Assembly cost ($C_A$)** — labour + equipment cost per product.
- **DFA Efficiency ($\eta_{DFA}$)** — ratio of ideal minimum parts to actual design.

> [!NOTE]
> A "good" assembly design is generally accepted as having **DFA Efficiency $\eta_{DFA} \ge 60\%$** and **fewer than 10 parts per sub-assembly** for consumer products.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA Methodology (Foundation Theory)

The **Boothroyd–Dewhurst Design for Assembly method** (1979, refined 1989, 2002) is the global industry standard and is directly referenced in the KTU PEMET522 syllabus. It is built on two sequential analysis steps:

### Step A — The Three Diagnostic Questions (Part Reduction)

For **every** individual part in the proposed design, the engineer answers:

> **Q1.** During operation of the product, does the part *move* relative to all other parts already assembled?
> **Q2.** Must the part be made of a *different material* than its neighbours?
> **Q3.** Must the part be *separated* from its neighbours to allow the assembly or disassembly of other parts?

**Rule:** If the answer to **all three** questions is **NO**, the part is a **candidate for elimination** (it is an "unnecessary" part). It should be combined with an adjacent part.

Let:
- $N_{min}$ = minimum theoretical number of parts (count of parts that passed all three questions)
- $N_m$ = total number of parts in the current (non-optimised) design

The **Number of Parts Ratio** is:

$$
N_{ratio} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_m}
$$

### Step B — Handling and Insertion Analysis

For every *essential* part, the engineer assigns two coded times:

**Handling Code ($\alpha$)** — based on part's symmetry, thickness, and grasping difficulty.

**Insertion Code ($\beta$)** — based on alignment difficulty, fastening method, and access.

The estimated assembly time for a single part is:

$$
t_{part} = t_{\alpha} + t_{\beta}
$$

And total product assembly time is:

$$
T_A = \sum_{j=1}^{N_m} \left( t_{\alpha,j} + t_{\beta,j} \right) + T_{extra}
$$

where $T_{extra}$ accounts for base/frame placement, fixturing, and reorientation operations.

## 2.2 Detailed Handling Code ($\alpha$) Table

| Thickness (mm) | Symmetrical & Easy ($\alpha_1$) | Semi-symmetrical ($\alpha_2$) | Non-symmetrical ($\alpha_3$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **> 6** | 1.5 s | 2.0 s | 3.0 s |
| **2 – 6** | 1.7 s | 2.3 s | 3.5 s |
| **< 2** | 2.0 s | 2.7 s | 4.0 s |

## 2.3 Detailed Insertion Code ($\beta$) Table

| Fastening Type | Self-locating ($\beta_1$) | Moderate alignment ($\beta_2$) | Difficult alignment ($\beta_3$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Snap-fit / press** | 1.5 s | 2.5 s | 4.0 s |
| **Screw / bolt** | 3.0 s | 4.0 s | 6.0 s |
| **Weld / adhesive** | 4.0 s | 5.5 s | 7.5 s |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you are expected to know for KTU 2024 ESE on "Assembling a Product." Memorise the units and boundary conditions.

| Equation No. | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(1)** | $T_A = \sum t_{\alpha,j} + \sum t_{\beta,j} + T_{extra}$ | Total assembly time | seconds |
| **(2)** | $C_A = T_A \cdot R_l \cdot \left(1 + O_h\right)$ | Assembly labour cost | ₹ / product |
| **(3)** | $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot 3}{T_A}$ | DFA efficiency index | dimensionless (in seconds) |
| **(4)** | $N_{ratio} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_m}$ | Part reduction ratio | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **(5)** | $T_{ideal} = N_{min} \cdot 3$ | Ideal minimum time (3 s per part) | seconds |
| **(6)** | $R_l = \dfrac{\text{Labour wage (₹/hr)}}{3600}$ | Labour rate per second | ₹ / s |
| **(7)** | $C_{total} = C_m + C_A$ | Total manufacturing + assembly cost | ₹ / product |

> **Boundary Conditions & Defaults used in KTU problems:**
> - Ideal insertion time per part = **3 s** (Boothroyd baseline).
> - Standard overhead factor $O_h = 0.4$ (40 % burden).
> - Efficiency target $\eta_{DFA} \ge 60\%$ (60 seconds per ideal part count).

> **Note on the absolute value symbol:** In LaTeX you may see expressions like $\vert t_{\alpha} \vert$ or $\mid t_{\beta} \mid$. In this KTU cheat sheet, all quantities are positive scalars, so absolute value bars are not required.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors):** Reducing one fastener per car saves **~5 minutes per vehicle** in assembly line time. Across 3 lakh units/year, this is **25,000 production hours/year** recovered.
- **Consumer Electronics (e.g., Apple AirPods):** The snap-fit charging case uses zero screws. Every gram of fastener removed reduces tooling cost and improves repair-disassembly.
- **Medical Devices (e.g., syringe):** Single-part moulded plunger + snap-fit cap → zero assembly, infinite DFA efficiency.
- **Defence (DRDO, HAL):** Field-serviceable assemblies demand **modular design** so a soldier can disassemble a radio with bare hands — a direct DFA constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> The **3-second ideal time** is not arbitrary. It represents a human operator's best-case time to **grasp, orient, insert, and release** a single, perfectly designed part. Any real part requiring more than 3 s is signalling a **design flaw**, not an operator deficiency.

## 2.6 Types of Assembly Systems (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Type | Description | Typical Industry | Cost Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Assembly** | Human operator with hand tools | Low-volume, heavy products (tractors) | Low tooling, high labour |
| **Semi-Automatic** | Operator + dedicated fixture | Medium-volume (appliances) | Moderate |
| **Automatic (Hard Automation)** | Fixed sequence, special-purpose machines | High-volume (cars, electronics) | High tooling, very low labour |
| **Robotic Assembly** | Programmable 6-axis or SCARA robots | Flexible high-volume (smartphones) | High initial, very flexible |
| **Hybrid (Lean) Assembly** | Combination with one-piece flow | Toyota Production System plants | Optimised total |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DFA Efficiency Index ($\eta_{DFA}$)

**Premise:** The theoretical best assembly design uses the minimum number of parts, and each part takes the ideal time of 3 seconds. So:

**Step 1 — Write the ideal total assembly time.**

If the design has $N_{min}$ essential parts, the minimum achievable time is one part per 3 seconds:

$$
T_{ideal} = N_{min} \times 3 \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the actual assembly time.**

For the real design with $N_m$ parts and per-part $\alpha$ and $\beta$ times:

$$
T_{actual} = \sum_{j=1}^{N_m} \left( t_{\alpha,j} + t_{\beta,j} \right) + T_{extra}
$$

**Step 3 — Define efficiency as a percentage.**

Efficiency is the ideal time expressed as a percentage of the actual time:

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

Substituting $T_{ideal} = 3 \cdot N_{min}$:

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%
$$

Or in the alternative Boothroyd form (efficiency as a time-equivalent in seconds):

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{T_A} \quad \text{[seconds per ideal part]}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation rule (KTU marker).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\eta_{DFA} \ge 60\%$, the design is **acceptable**. If $\eta_{DFA} < 50\%$, the design is **poor** and requires re-engineering.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> **Problem (Model Question):** A product has $N_m = 12$ parts. After applying the three diagnostic questions, the minimum number of parts is $N_{min} = 7$. The per-part $\alpha$ and $\beta$ times are tabulated below. Extra fixturing time is $T_{extra} = 15$ s. Labour rate is $R_l = ₹0.5/\text{s}$. Overhead is $O_h = 0.4$. Calculate (a) the total assembly time, (b) the assembly cost, and (c) the DFA efficiency.

| Part $j$ | $t_{\alpha,j}$ (s) | $t_{\beta,j}$ (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 2.5 |
| 3 | 1.7 | 3.0 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 2.5 |
| 5 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 6 | 2.3 | 3.0 |
| 7 | 1.7 | 2.5 |
| 8 | 2.7 | 4.0 |
| 9 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 10 | 2.0 | 3.0 |
| 11 | 1.7 | 2.5 |
| 12 | 2.3 | 4.0 |

### Part (a) — Total Assembly Time $T_A$

**Step 1:** Sum the handling times.

$$
\sum t_{\alpha,j} = 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.7 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 2.3 + 1.7 + 2.7 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 1.7 + 2.3
$$

$$
= 22.9 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the insertion times.

$$
\sum t_{\beta,j} = 1.5 + 2.5 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 1.5 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 4.0
$$

$$
= 34.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Add the extra fixturing time.

$$
T_A = 22.9 + 34.0 + 15 = 71.9 \text{ s}
$$

### Part (b) — Assembly Cost $C_A$

**Step 1:** Apply the cost formula.

$$
C_A = T_A \cdot R_l \cdot (1 + O_h)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
C_A = 71.9 \times 0.5 \times (1 + 0.4)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
C_A = 71.9 \times 0.5 \times 1.4 = 71.9 \times 0.7 = 50.33 \text{ ₹/product}
$$

### Part (c) — DFA Efficiency $\eta_{DFA}$

**Step 1:** Compute the ideal time.

$$
T_{ideal} = 3 \times N_{min} = 3 \times 7 = 21 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the efficiency formula.

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{T_A} \times 100\% = \frac{21}{71.9} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Final answer.

$$
\eta_{DFA} \approx 29.21\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The efficiency is **below 50 %**, indicating a **poor DFA design**. The engineer must (a) eliminate redundant parts to push $N_{min}$ higher relative to $N_m$, and (b) redesign parts to reduce $\beta$ times (e.g., replace screws with snap-fits).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Assembly Method Selection

| Design Driver | Manual | Semi-Auto | Hard Auto | Robotic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume < 1,000/yr** | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Over-spec | ❌ Wasteful | ❌ Wasteful |
| **Volume 1k – 50k/yr** | ⚠️ Slow | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Rigid | ✅ Flexible |
| **Volume > 50k/yr** | ❌ Costly | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Best | ✅ Best |
| **Product Mix High** | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Re-tool | ❌ Impossible | ✅ Best |
| **Tolerance < 0.05 mm** | ❌ Impossible | ⚠️ Difficult | ✅ Best | ✅ Best |
| **Heavy Parts > 5 kg** | ✅ Best | ✅ OK | ⚠️ Re-design | ✅ OK |
| **Low Initial Capital** | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Medium | ❌ Huge | ❌ Huge |

> **Legend:** ✅ Recommended | ⚠️ Conditional | ❌ Not recommended

## 3.4 Derivation of the Part Reduction Ratio Improvement

Suppose we eliminate $k$ parts and combine them with adjacent parts. The new part count is:

$$
N_m^{\prime} = N_m - k
$$

The new time is (assuming those $k$ parts had average $\alpha + \beta$ time $\bar{t}$):

$$
T_A^{\prime} = T_A - k \cdot \bar{t}
$$

The percentage time saving is:

$$
\Delta T \, [\%] = \frac{k \cdot \bar{t}}{T_A} \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical check:** If $k = 3$, $\bar{t} = 4$ s, $T_A = 71.9$ s:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{3 \times 4}{71.9} \times 100\% = 16.69\%
$$

So **removing just 3 redundant parts saves ~17 % of assembly time** — a powerful incentive for DFA.
