# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Assembly Process Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Product Design] --> B[Identify Total Parts Nm]
    B --> C[Apply Boothroyd 3 Diagnostic Questions]
    C --> D{All 3 Answers NO?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Mark as Candidate for Elimination]
    D -->|No| F[Keep Part, Assign Essential Status]
    E --> G[Combine with Adjacent Part]
    G --> H[Recompute Nmin]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Assign Handling Code alpha for Each Part]
    I --> J[Assign Insertion Code beta for Each Part]
    J --> K[Compute Total Assembly Time TA]
    K --> L[Compute Assembly Cost CA]
    L --> M{DFA Efficiency above 60 percent?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Accept Design, Release to Production]
    M -->|No| O[Redesign: Reduce alpha and beta Times]
    O --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Joining Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Joining Required?] --> B{Is Disassembly Needed?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Loose Joint Acceptable?}
    B -->|No| D{Permanent Joint Acceptable?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Snap-Fit or Press-Fit]
    C -->|No| F[Use Screw or Threaded Fastener]
    D -->|Yes| G{Small Series or High Volume?}
    D -->|No| H[Use Bolt + Nut + Washer Assembly]
    G -->|Small| I[Use Adhesive or Solder]
    G -->|Large| J[Use Spot Weld or Rivet]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Boothroyd DFA Analysis Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: Generate Initial Part List] --> stepB[Step 2: Apply Q1 Movement Test]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Apply Q2 Material Test]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Apply Q3 Separation Test]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Count Nmin and Nm]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Assign alpha and beta Codes]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Calculate TA and CA]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Compute Efficiency]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Decide Accept or Redesign]
    stepI -->|Redesign| stepA
    stepI -->|Accept| stepJ[Step 10: Lock Design for Production]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Assembly Cost Build-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inp1[Sum of Handling Times] --> sum1
    inp2[Sum of Insertion Times] --> sum1
    inp3[Extra Fixturing Time] --> sum1
    sum1[Total Assembly Time TA] --> cost1
    cost1 --> cost2[Multiply by Labour Rate Rl]
    cost2 --> cost3[Add Overhead Factor Oh]
    cost3 --> out1[Final Assembly Cost CA in Rupees]
    sum1 --> eff1
    eff1[Compute Ideal Time Tideal] --> eff2
    eff2[Compute Efficiency eta DFA] --> out2[DFA Efficiency Percentage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above four diagrams are designed using **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **clean uppercase text labels** (no markdown formatting or special symbols inside the labels), in strict compliance with safe Mermaid rendering protocols. They map the assembly design workflow as a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical drawing — the most reliable approach for KTU schematic questions.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Design for Assembly (DFA) and list any two DFA guidelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Design for Assembly (DFA) is a *systematic product design methodology* that seeks to *minimise the number of parts, simplify the joining operations, and reduce the total assembly time and cost* of a product, while preserving its functional performance.

**Two DFA Guidelines (1 mark):**
1. **Minimise part count** — use the Boothroyd three-question test to identify and eliminate non-essential parts.
2. **Design parts for ease of handling** — use symmetrical shapes, generous chamfers, and self-locating features to reduce $\alpha$ and $\beta$ times.

*(Full 3 marks: 2 for definition + 1 for guidelines. Examiner awards marks only for the listed key phrases — see the warning box below.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the ideal minimum assembly time per part as per Boothroyd's method? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ideal minimum time** per part in Boothroyd's DFA method is **3 seconds**. This value represents the **best achievable time** for a perfectly designed part — one that is *self-locating, self-fastening, easy to grasp, and inserted in a single direction without reorientation*. Any deviation from this 3-second benchmark signals a design inefficiency that should be corrected during the design phase itself, rather than compensated by a faster (or more skilled) operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have a **sub-part (a) for 7 marks** and a **sub-part (b) for 7 marks**. Always show full working, units, and final boxed answer.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A consumer electronic product has **$N_m = 10$ parts**. On applying the Boothroyd three-question test, **4 parts are eliminated**, leaving $N_{min} = 6$. The per-part assembly data is given below. Extra time $T_{extra} = 8$ s. Labour rate = ₹1.0 per second. Overhead factor $O_h = 0.5$.

| Part $j$ | $t_{\alpha}$ (s) | $t_{\beta}$ (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 1.7 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 2.5 |
| 4 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 5 | 2.3 | 3.0 |
| 6 | 1.7 | 2.5 |
| 7 | 2.0 | 4.0 |
| 8 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 9 | 2.3 | 3.0 |
| 10 | 1.7 | 2.5 |

**(a)** Compute the **total assembly time** $T_A$ and the **assembly cost** $C_A$. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the **DFA efficiency** $\eta_{DFA}$ and comment on the design quality. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sum the handling times (1 mark):**

$$
\sum t_{\alpha} = 1.5 + 1.7 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 2.3 + 1.7 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 2.3 + 1.7 = 18.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the insertion times (1 mark):**

$$
\sum t_{\beta} = 1.5 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 1.5 + 3.0 + 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total assembly time (1 mark):**

$$
T_A = 18.2 + 27.5 + 8 = 53.7 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply cost formula (1 mark):**

$$
C_A = T_A \cdot R_l \cdot (1 + O_h)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$
C_A = 53.7 \times 1.0 \times (1 + 0.5) = 53.7 \times 1.5
$$

**Step 6 — Final cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_A = 80.55 \text{ ₹/product}
$$

**Step 7 — Box final answers (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{T_A = 53.7 \text{ s} \quad \text{and} \quad C_A = ₹80.55 \text{ per product}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute ideal time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{ideal} = 3 \times N_{min} = 3 \times 6 = 18 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply efficiency formula (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_A} \times 100\% = \frac{18}{53.7} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Final efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{DFA} \approx 33.52\%
$$

**Step 4 — Design comment (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DFA efficiency of **33.52 %** is **well below the acceptable 60 % threshold**. The design is **inefficient** and requires re-engineering. Recommendations:
> 1. Replace screw-based $\beta$-fastenings (parts 4, 7) with **snap-fits** to reduce $\beta$ time from 4.0 s to ~1.5 s.
> 2. Apply the three-question test more rigorously to identify additional non-essential parts and reduce $N_m$ closer to $N_{min} = 6$.
> 3. Use **symmetrical, thicker parts** (where possible) to lower $\alpha$ time.

**Step 5 — Boxed final answer (1 mark — included in comment):**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{DFA} \approx 33.52\% \quad \text{— Design is POOR, redesign required.}}
$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd three-question test** for part reduction. Apply it to a hypothetical gear-shaft assembly consisting of (i) shaft, (ii) key, (iii) gear, (iv) retaining ring, and (v) housing cover. Identify the minimum number of parts. (7 marks)

**(b)** List and briefly explain the **various types of assembly systems** used in industry. State one example product for each. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the three questions (3 marks):**

1. **Q1 — Movement:** Does the part *move* relative to other parts during operation?
2. **Q2 — Material:** Must the part be made of a *different material* from its neighbour?
3. **Q3 — Separation:** Must the part be *separated* to assemble/disassemble other parts?

**Step 2 — Apply to each part (3 marks):**

| Part | Q1 (Move) | Q2 (Material) | Q3 (Separation) | Essential? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft | No (fixed) | Yes (steel) | No | ✅ Essential (passes Q2) |
| Key | No (static) | Yes (steel) | Yes (must insert gear separately) | ✅ Essential |
| Gear | No (locked by key) | Yes (steel) | Yes (must slide onto key) | ✅ Essential |
| Retaining ring | No | Yes (spring steel) | Yes (must hold gear axially) | ✅ Essential |
| Housing cover | No | Yes (cast iron) | Yes (must enclose bearing) | ✅ Essential |

**Step 3 — Count $N_{min}$ (1 mark):**

$$
N_{min} = 5, \quad N_m = 5
$$

Since every part passes at least one test, **no part can be eliminated** in this classical gear-shaft sub-assembly. The design is *already at its minimum*.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

| Type | Description (1 mark each) | Example (0.5 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Assembly** | Operator fits parts by hand using simple tools | Tractor axle assembly at M&M |
| **Semi-Automatic** | Operator + dedicated fixtures and power tools | Washing machine drum (LG) |
| **Hard Automation** | Fixed sequence, special-purpose machines | Spark-plug assembly line |
| **Robotic Assembly** | Programmable 6-axis / SCARA robots | iPhone motherboard (Foxconn) |
| **Hybrid / Lean** | One-piece flow with mixed manual + auto | Toyota engine plant |
| **Modular Assembly** | Sub-assemblies built separately, joined at final line | Maruti car dashboard |
| **Outsourced Assembly** | Vendor completes the assembly (CKD/SKD) | Two-wheeler exports from India |

(Examiner: 1 mark per correct type + 0.5 mark per example, capped at 7 marks. Quality of description matters more than rote listing.)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the +$T_{extra}$ term** — Many students compute $T_A$ as only $\sum(t_{\alpha} + t_{\beta})$ and miss the fixturing/base time. *This is a 2-mark deduction in most valuation schemes.*
> 2. **Using $N_m$ instead of $N_{min}$ in the efficiency formula** — A common error. Always use $N_{min}$ in the *numerator*; $N_m$ is used only in the *denominator* of $N_{ratio}$.
> 3. **Mixing units** — Labour rate $R_l$ is in ₹/s, but wages are quoted in ₹/hr. Convert: $R_l = \text{wage}/3600$. A unit error invalidates the cost answer.
> 4. **Skipping the design comment** — In part (b) of Question A, students give a numerical answer but **omit the qualitative comment** on whether the design is acceptable. This costs 2 marks. Always end with: *"Efficiency is below 60 %, design is poor, recommend redesign."*
> 5. **Wrong ideal time** — Using 5 s or 2 s instead of the **3 s** Boothroyd baseline. *The 3-second benchmark is sacrosanct in KTU papers.*
> 6. **Not boxing the final answer** — In KTU answer sheets, the final numerical answer *must be boxed* with units. Unboxed answers are flagged as incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — the 20 golden points on "Assembling a Product":**

- ✅ Assembly is a **design-driven** activity, not just a shop-floor process.
- ✅ The **Boothroyd three-question test** (movement, material, separation) is the *primary* tool for part reduction.
- ✅ Ideal minimum assembly time per part = **3 seconds** (Boothroyd baseline).
- ✅ $T_A = \sum t_{\alpha} + \sum t_{\beta} + T_{extra}$ — never forget the extra term.
- ✅ $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{min}}{T_A} \times 100\%$ — accept if $\ge 60\%$.
- ✅ $C_A = T_A \cdot R_l \cdot (1 + O_h)$ — convert $R_l$ to ₹/s before substituting.
- ✅ $N_{ratio} = N_{min} / N_m$ — closer to 1.0 means a more efficient design.
- ✅ **Symmetrical, thick parts** → lower $\alpha$ time.
- ✅ **Snap-fits and press-fits** → lowest $\beta$ time; preferred for high-volume consumer products.
- ✅ **Screws and bolts** → moderate $\beta$ time; reserved for disassembly-critical joints.
- ✅ **Welds and adhesives** → high $\beta$ time; permanent joints only.
- ✅ Manual assembly suits **low-volume, heavy** products; robotic suits **high-volume, light, precise** products.
- ✅ Hard automation is the **cheapest per unit** but the **most expensive to re-tool**.
- ✅ Assembly typically consumes **40–60 %** of total manufacturing time in discrete products.
- ✅ Removing just **3–5 redundant parts** can reduce total assembly time by **15–25 %**.
- ✅ DFA must be applied **before** DFM (Design for Manufacture) in the product development cycle, because assembly is downstream of manufacture.
- ✅ $\alpha$ codes range from **1.5 s to 4.0 s**; $\beta$ codes range from **1.5 s to 7.5 s**.
- ✅ $T_{extra}$ includes base placement, fixturing, and reorientation — typical value 5–20 s.
- ✅ The KTU-accepted **labour rate convention** is **₹/s**; convert hourly wages by dividing by 3600.
- ✅ Always **box the final answer** with units. Always end with a **design-quality comment** (acceptable / poor / excellent).

---

*End of Module 2 — Assembling a Product | KTU PEMET522 | 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Assembly: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design for Assembly: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Assembly (DFA)** is a concurrent engineering methodology that systematically analyses a product's design to **reduce the number of parts, simplify assembly operations, and minimize overall assembly cost and time**, while preserving the functional performance of the product.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework, DFA is treated as a **systematic design philosophy** that integrates the *assembly perspective* into the *product design phase*, so that the final product is easier, faster, and cheaper to assemble than an equivalent product designed without DFA considerations.

**Key Performance Indices Used in DFA:**
- **DFA Index (Design Efficiency Factor):** A percentage that quantifies how close the current design is to its theoretical minimum part configuration.
- **Assembly Efficiency (η_A):** Ratio of theoretical minimum assembly time to the actual assembly time.
- **Minimum Part Count (N_min):** The smallest number of parts theoretically required to satisfy the design functions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are assembling a **flat-pack book shelf** (similar to IKEA furniture).

- **Without DFA (Poor Design):** You open the box and find **47 small pieces**, **113 different screws and dowels**, and a 14-page instruction manual. You need **3 hours**, two people, and a lot of frustration to assemble it.
- **With DFA (Good Design):** You open the box and find **6 large pre-assembled sub-modules**, each held together by a single **snap-fit mechanism**. The pieces are **clearly marked**, **symmetrical**, and snap together in **one direction only**. You finish the job in **12 minutes** with one hand.

> [!NOTE]
> The shelf did the same job in both cases. The only difference is that the DFA-designed version removed every part that was *not functionally necessary* and made the *remaining parts* trivially easy to join.

This analogy is the heart of DFA: **"If a part does no useful work, eliminate it. If it is necessary, make it impossible to assemble incorrectly."**

### 1.3 Relationship between DFA and DFM

| Aspect | Design for Manufacture (DFM) | Design for Assembly (DFA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Ease of *making* individual parts | Ease of *joining* the parts together |
| **Key Question** | "Can this part be made economically?" | "Is this part even necessary?" |
| **Stage of Application** | Part-level design | Product and sub-assembly level |
| **Cost Lever** | Material & machining cost | Labour, fixturing, and time cost |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern KTU 2024 scheme pedagogy, **DFM + DFA = DFMA**, treated as a unified product development strategy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of DFA

The entire theory of DFA can be reduced to **three logical pillars**, each answering one engineering question:

**Pillar 1 — Functional Necessity (Why is this part here?)**
- Every part in an assembly must satisfy **one of two** functional criteria:
  1. The part performs a **relative motion** essential to the product's function.
  2. The part is made of a **different material** strictly required by the design.
- If a part satisfies neither criterion, it is a candidate for **elimination or integration** with another part.

**Pillar 2 — Handling Ease (Can the part be picked up easily?)**
- Parts must be:
  - **Symmetrical** (no top/bottom or left/right confusion).
  - Free of **tangling features** like long flexible appendages.
  - Designed with features that allow **single-direction insertion**.
- Each part is scored on its *handling time* — parts that require re-orientation add cost.

**Pillar 3 — Insertion/Joining Ease (Can the part be mated in a single stroke?)**
- Fasteners (screws, rivets, welds) are the **most expensive** assembly operations.
- DFA prefers **snap-fits, press-fits, and self-locating features**.
- Each joining operation must ideally be:
  - Done from **one direction** (preferably top-down).
  - Requiring **only one hand** of the operator.
  - **Self-aligning** (no fine adjustments needed).

### 2.2 The Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA Methodology (Most Tested in KTU)

The Boothroyd–Dewhurst method is the most widely taught DFA procedure in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It works in **three stages**:

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage A** | Examine each part for *functional necessity* | Reduced part count, N_min |
| **Stage B** | Analyse the *handling* requirement of each remaining part | Handling time (t_h) per part |
| **Stage C** | Analyse the *insertion/joining* requirement of each part | Insertion time (t_i) per part |

The DFA worksheet then computes an **estimated assembly time**:
$$T_{est} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min} \quad (\text{seconds})$$
$$T_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} (t_{h,i} + t_{i,i}) \quad (\text{seconds})$$

where the symbols are explained in the formula sheet below.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **every formula** a student is expected to know for a KTU 2024 ESE question on DFA Introduction.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DFA Index (Design Efficiency)** | $\text{DFA Index} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | **Dimensionless %** |
| 2 | **Assembly Efficiency (η_A)** | $\eta_{A} = \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$ | **Dimensionless %** |
| 3 | **Theoretical Minimum Assembly Time** | $T_{min} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min}$ | **seconds** |
| 4 | **Actual Assembly Time** | $T_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t}(t_{h,i} + t_{i,i})$ | **seconds** |
| 5 | **Design Simplification Ratio** | $\text{DSR} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{t}}$ | **Fraction** |
| 6 | **Annual Savings Formula** | $S = N_{saved} \cdot T_{saved} \cdot C_{labour} \cdot N_{units}$ | **Currency/Year** |

Where the standard empirical values often used in KTU problems are:
- **t_min (minimum handling + insertion time per part) ≈ 3 s** (Boothroyd's recommended benchmark).
- **C_labour (operator cost) ≈ standard rate per second** (given in question).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DFA is the invisible engineering behind every product you use daily:
- **Automotive industry:** Reducing a car's parts by just **1%** saves the manufacturer **billions of dollars** annually.
- **Consumer electronics (phones, laptops):** Snap-fit enclosures, fewer screws, integrated hinges.
- **Medical devices:** Sterile, error-proof assembly is literally a matter of life and death, making DFA *mandatory*.
- **Toy industry (LEGO, Meccano):** Mass-production economics depend entirely on DFA principles.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love testing the **interpretation** of a DFA Index value. A rough guide:
> - **< 30%** → Poor design, major redesign required.
> - **30 – 60%** → Acceptable, but room for improvement.
> - **> 60%** → Excellent, world-class design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DFA Index

The DFA Index is **not a derived formula from first principles** — it is an *engineered metric* constructed by **Boothroyd and Dewhurst** to compare a real product with its ideal counterpart. We can, however, derive it logically from the cost-based assembly model.

**Step 1: Total Assembly Cost Model**

The total cost to assemble a product is approximately proportional to the total time spent:
$$C_{assembly} = T_{actual} \cdot C_{labour}$$

where $C_{labour}$ is the cost of the operator per second, and $T_{actual}$ is the total time in seconds.

**Step 2: Lower Bound of the Assembly Time**

The **smallest possible** assembly time occurs when:
- The number of parts is reduced to the **theoretical minimum** $N_{min}$.
- Each part requires the **theoretical minimum time** $t_{min}$ to handle and insert.

Therefore, the lower bound is:
$$T_{min} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min}$$

**Step 3: Define the Design Efficiency**

The fraction of "ideal time" achieved by the current design is:
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Simplify**

Substituting $T_{min} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min}$ and $T_{actual} = N_{t} \cdot t_{avg}$:
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{N_{min} \cdot t_{min}}{N_{t} \cdot t_{avg}}$$

If we assume $t_{min} \approx t_{avg}$ (a standard simplification in introductory KTU problems), this reduces to:
$$\boxed{\;\eta_{A} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{t}} \times 100\% \equiv \text{DFA Index}\;}$$

This confirms that the DFA Index is essentially a **time-based efficiency** cast in terms of part counts.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Board-Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (14-Mark KTU Pattern):** An electric kettle assembly currently has **18 parts**. A DFA analysis concludes that the **theoretical minimum number of parts** is **9 parts**, and the **theoretical minimum assembly time** is **3 seconds per part**. The actual measured assembly time is **72 seconds**. Calculate the (a) DFA Index, (b) Assembly Efficiency, (c) DSR, and (d) comment on the result with two redesign suggestions.

**Given Data:**
- $N_t = 18$ (total current parts)
- $N_{min} = 9$ (theoretical minimum parts)
- $t_{min} = 3$ s/part
- $T_{actual} = 72$ s

#### Part (a) — DFA Index

The formula is:
$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_t} \times 100$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{9}{18} \times 100$$

Performing the division:
$$\frac{9}{18} = 0.5$$

Converting to percentage:
$$\text{DFA Index} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Result:** DFA Index = **50 %**.

#### Part (b) — Assembly Efficiency

The theoretical minimum assembly time is:
$$T_{min} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min}$$
$$T_{min} = 9 \times 3 = 27 \text{ s}$$

The Assembly Efficiency is:
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$$
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{27}{72} \times 100$$

Simplifying the fraction:
$$\frac{27}{72} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

Multiplying by 100:
$$\eta_{A} = 37.5\%$$

**Result:** $\eta_{A}$ = **37.5 %**.

#### Part (c) — Design Simplification Ratio (DSR)

The formula is:
$$\text{DSR} = 1 - \frac{N_{min}}{N_t}$$
$$\text{DSR} = 1 - \frac{9}{18}$$
$$\text{DSR} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Result:** DSR = **0.5** (i.e., **50%** of the design is "wasted complexity").

#### Part (d) — Interpretation and Redesign Suggestions

**Interpretation Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Verdict (as per KTU rubric) |
|---|---|---|
| DFA Index | 50% | **Moderate** — Acceptable but improvable |
| Assembly Efficiency | 37.5% | **Low** — Significant time savings possible |
| DSR | 0.5 | **High** — Half the parts are redundant |

**Two redesign suggestions (model answer):**
1. **Eliminate redundant fasteners:** Use a single snap-fit housing instead of separate screws and washers to join the handle to the body.
2. **Integrate the base plate with the main body** by moulding it as one piece in plastic, thereby removing 2 parts and one joining operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check for the answer script:** Notice that the **DFA Index (50%)** and the **Assembly Efficiency (37.5%)** are *not equal* even though the formula looks similar. This is because $T_{min}$ depends on *both* $N_{min}$ and $t_{min}$, while the DFA Index only uses $N_{min}$. Examiners frequently award full marks only if a student explains this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DFA Process Flow (Boothroyd–Dewhurst Method)

The following block diagram captures the full iterative DFA methodology as it must be drawn in a KTU examination answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Product Concept and Functional Specification]
    A2[Step 2: Identify All Functions of the Product]
    A3[Step 3: Generate Initial Design with Maximum Part Count]
    A4[Step 4: For Each Part, Check Functional Necessity]
    A5{Part Has Relative Motion OR Different Material?}
    A6[Step 5: KEEP the Part as Functional]
    A7[Step 5: ELIMINATE or INTEGRATE the Part]
    A8[Step 6: Estimate Handling Time for Each Remaining Part]
    A9[Step 7: Estimate Insertion or Joining Time for Each Part]
    A10[Step 8: Compute Total Assembly Time Tactual]
    A11[Step 9: Compute DFA Index and Assembly Efficiency]
    A12{DFA Index Above 60 Percent?}
    A13[Step 10: Finalise the Design]
    A14[Step 10: Return to Step 4 and Redesign]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 -- YES --> A6
    A5 -- NO --> A7
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12
    A12 -- YES --> A13
    A12 -- NO --> A14
    A14 --> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box labelled "Step X" is a mandatory stage in Boothroyd's procedure. The two decision diamonds (A5 and A12) are the **only** judgement points; everything else is mechanical. This is the exact sequence a KTU examiner expects to see in a 7-mark descriptive answer.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the DFA Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PART A: Functional Necessity Check]
    P2[PART B: Handling Difficulty Check]
    P3[PART C: Insertion and Joining Check]
    P4[Combined DFA Score]
    P5[Engineering Decision: KEEP, MODIFY, or DELETE]

    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three "pillars" shown above (A, B, C) are the **non-negotiable criteria** of any DFA review. A part that scores poorly on all three must be eliminated; a part that scores well on all three is a *core* part of the assembly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are **modelled** on the KTU 2024 ESE pattern (2-mark + 14-mark modules). They are **not** reproductions of any specific past paper but are calibrated to the same cognitive level and mark distribution.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

Define **Design for Assembly (DFA)**. List **any four** benefits of applying DFA in product development.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Design for Assembly is a systematic methodology that analyses a product design to **simplify the assembly process** by reducing part count, easing handling, and minimising joining operations, thereby lowering total assembly cost and time.

*Any four benefits (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):*
1. Reduced number of parts → lower inventory and procurement cost.
2. Faster assembly → higher production rate and lower labour cost.
3. Fewer fasteners → reduced tooling cost.
4. Improved product reliability due to fewer joint failures.
5. Easier automation of the assembly line.
6. Lower overall product cost → improved market competitiveness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

What is the **DFA Index**? State its formula and explain the significance of a value of **75%**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition + Formula (2 marks):* The DFA Index is a **quantitative measure** of design efficiency, defined as:
$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_t} \times 100$$
where $N_{min}$ is the theoretical minimum number of parts and $N_t$ is the actual number of parts in the design.

*Significance of 75% (1 mark):* A DFA Index of **75%** indicates an **excellent, world-class design** where the actual part count is very close to the theoretical minimum. The remaining 25% gap represents complexity that is genuinely required by function, and further simplification offers diminishing returns.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of the two questions below. Each question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
Discuss the **objectives and guiding principles** of Design for Assembly. Explain how DFA differs from **Design for Manufacture (DFM)**.

**Model Solution:**

*Objectives of DFA (3 marks):*
1. To reduce the number of parts in a product.
2. To simplify the assembly operations and reduce assembly time.
3. To minimise assembly cost and improve product quality.
4. To standardise parts and sub-assemblies.
5. To facilitate automation of the assembly process.

*Guiding Principles (2 marks):*
- **Minimize part count:** Eliminate any part that does not perform a function, require a different material, or move relative to another part.
- **Design for ease of handling:** Use symmetrical shapes and self-locating features.
- **Design for ease of insertion:** Prefer snap-fits and one-direction assembly.
- **Standardise fasteners:** Use identical screws/bolts throughout the product.
- **Modular design:** Group parts into sub-assemblies that can be tested independently.

*DFA vs DFM (2 marks):*

| Criterion | DFA | DFM |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Product / sub-system level | Part / feature level |
| Cost lever | Assembly labour time | Material and machining cost |
| Typical question | "Is this part necessary?" | "Can this part be made cheaper?" |
| Output | Reduced part count | Optimised part geometry |
| Relationship | Acts **after** DFM in the design sequence | Acts **before** DFA in the design sequence |

**(b) [7 Marks]**
Explain the **Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA methodology** with a neat flow diagram. List any **five design heuristics** recommended by Boothroyd for simplification.

**Model Solution:**

*Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA Methodology (4 marks):*
The Boothroyd–Dewhurst method, developed in the 1980s, is a **quantitative DFA technique** that proceeds in three stages:

- **Stage A — Functional Analysis of Each Part:** For every part in the initial design, the designer asks two questions: (i) Does the part move relative to other parts during operation? (ii) Is the part made of a different material from all adjacent parts? If the answer to both is "No," the part is a candidate for **elimination** (it can be merged with another part without changing function).

- **Stage B — Handling Analysis:** Each surviving part is analysed for the time required to **pick it up, orient it, and present it** to the assembly station. Parts that require two hands, re-orientation, or special fixturing receive a high handling time penalty.

- **Stage C — Insertion/Joining Analysis:** Each part is then analysed for the time required to **mate it with the previous parts** in the assembly. Fasteners, alignment-sensitive fits, and operations requiring downward force are penalised.

*Flow Diagram (1 mark):* (Draw the block diagram from SECTION 4.1 — it carries 1 mark.)

*Five Boothroyd Design Heuristics (2 marks):*
1. Use the **fewest possible parts** to perform each function.
2. Design parts to be **symmetrical** whenever possible to eliminate orientation problems.
3. Avoid **fasteners**; use snap-fits, clips, or press-fits.
4. Ensure that assembly is possible from **one direction only** (preferably top-down).
5. Provide **self-locating features** (chamfers, lead-ins) to reduce insertion time.
6. Use **standardised components** across product variants.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
A product has **24 parts** in its current design. After DFA analysis, the theoretical minimum number of parts is found to be **10**. The **theoretical minimum handling plus insertion time** per part is **3 seconds**.
Calculate:
(i) The **DFA Index**.
(ii) The **Design Simplification Ratio (DSR)**.
(iii) The **theoretical minimum assembly time**.

**Model Solution:**

*Given Data:*
- $N_t = 24$, $N_{min} = 10$, $t_{min} = 3$ s/part.

**(i) DFA Index (3 marks):**
$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_{min}}{N_t} \times 100$$
$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{10}{24} \times 100$$
$$\frac{10}{24} = 0.4167$$
$$\text{DFA Index} = 41.67\%$$

**Result:** DFA Index ≈ **41.67%**.

**(ii) Design Simplification Ratio (2 marks):**
$$\text{DSR} = 1 - \frac{N_{min}}{N_t}$$
$$\text{DSR} = 1 - \frac{10}{24} = 1 - 0.4167 = 0.5833$$

**Result:** DSR ≈ **0.5833** (i.e., **58.33%** of the design complexity is redundant).

**(iii) Theoretical Minimum Assembly Time (2 marks):**
$$T_{min} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min} = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** $T_{min}$ = **30 s**.

**(b) [7 Marks]**
For the same product, the **actual measured assembly time** is **96 seconds**. Calculate the **Assembly Efficiency** and the **extra time spent per product** due to the inefficient design. If the company produces **5,000 units per day** and the labour cost is **₹0.50 per second**, calculate the **daily extra cost** incurred.

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $T_{actual} = 96$ s, Production = 5,000 units/day, $C_{labour} = ₹0.50/\text{s}$.

**(i) Assembly Efficiency (2 marks):**
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$$
$$\eta_{A} = \frac{30}{96} \times 100 = 31.25\%$$

**Result:** $\eta_{A}$ = **31.25%**.

**(ii) Extra Time per Product (2 marks):**
$$T_{extra} = T_{actual} - T_{min} = 96 - 30 = 66 \text{ s/unit}$$

**Result:** Extra time = **66 seconds per product**.

**(iii) Daily Extra Cost (3 marks):**

Total extra time per day:
$$T_{total} = 66 \times 5000 = 3,30,000 \text{ s/day}$$

Daily extra cost:
$$C_{extra} = 3,30,000 \times 0.50 = ₹1,65,000 \text{ per day}$$

**Result:** **₹1,65,000 per day** is wasted due to the inefficient design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students frequently write the DFA Index as a *decimal* (e.g., 0.42) instead of a *percentage* (41.67%). This loses **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Misreading the question:** When $T_{min}$ is given in *per part* (s/part), students must multiply by $N_{min}$. Dividing by $N_{min}$ instead is a frequent error that loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the formula:** Even in numerical answers, KTU requires the formula to be written *before* the substitution. Skipping the formula line costs at least **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. **Rounding too early:** Round only the final answer. Intermediate decimal truncation (e.g., rounding 0.4167 to 0.42) leads to an incorrect final value and partial mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this for last-minute revision):**

- ✅ **DFA = reduce parts + ease handling + ease insertion.** Memorise this as the *one-line definition*.
- ✅ **DFA Index** uses *part counts* only: $\dfrac{N_{min}}{N_t} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Assembly Efficiency** uses *times*: $\dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$.
- ✅ They are **NOT equal** unless $t_{min} = t_{avg}$ — state this distinction explicitly in descriptive answers.
- ✅ **Boothroyd's two questions to eliminate a part:** *(i) Does it move? (ii) Is it a different material?* If "No" to both → **Eliminate**.
- ✅ **Standard benchmark:** $t_{min} \approx 3$ s/part (Boothroyd's empirical value) — use when a problem does not specify it.
- ✅ **Interpretation guide:** DFA Index < 30% (poor), 30–60% (acceptable), > 60% (excellent).
- ✅ **DFM ≠ DFA:** DFM is about *making* parts; DFA is about *joining* them. Together they form **DFMA**.
- ✅ **Design heuristics to remember:** symmetry, top-down assembly, snap-fits, one-hand operation, standardised fasteners, self-locating features.
- ✅ **Cost implications:** Every redundant part multiplies *part cost × assembly time × annual volume*. Always quantify savings in board answers.
- ✅ **Drawing a DFA flow diagram** in a 7-mark answer adds **at least 1 mark** — never skip it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Design Consideration" in DFMA

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, *Design Consideration* refers to the systematic set of engineering rules, geometric guidelines, material selections, tolerance specifications, and process-aware decisions that a designer must incorporate into a product's geometry and specification **right at the conceptual stage** so that the part can be **manufactured economically, machined with minimum waste, assembled effortlessly, and inspected reliably** using the chosen primary and secondary manufacturing processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *Design considerations are the proactive engineering decisions — geometry, material, tolerances, draft, fillets, parting lines, gating, draft angles, fiber orientation, grain flow, etc. — applied during product design to optimize a component for a specific manufacturing process (casting, forging, machining, sheet metal, plastics) and for ease of assembly.*

It is a **forward-integrated design philosophy** where the downstream manufacturing realities are *pulled upstream* into the design phase. The goal is to drive **DFMA's two master metrics** as low as possible:

$$
\text{Cost}_{part} = f(\text{Material}, \text{Process}, \text{Geometry}, \text{Tolerance})
$$

$$
\text{Assembly Efficiency } (AE) = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{N_m \cdot t_a + N_{a} \cdot t_{aa}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $N_m$ = **Minimum number of parts** (theoretical ideal)
- $t_a$ = theoretical assembly time
- $N_a$ = actual number of parts
- $t_{aa}$ = actual assembly time

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tailor's Rule"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Tailor's Rule:**
> Imagine you walk into a tailor to stitch a shirt. If you bring a pre-cut, oddly shaped piece of cloth with five corners and ask for a shirt, the tailor will struggle, charge more, waste fabric, and the result will be ugly. But if you **respect the tailor's rules of fabric** — straight grain, fold alignment, seam allowances, and fabric width — the tailor produces a perfect shirt cheaply and fast.
>
> In the same way, every manufacturing process (casting, forging, sheet metal, plastic molding) has its own "fabric rules." Ignoring them = waste, defects, and high cost. **Design Consideration is the engineering equivalent of respecting the tailor's rules while you sketch the dress.**

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Design Consideration

| Pillar | Core Question It Answers | Primary Process Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Alignment** | "Can my chosen process actually make this shape within tolerance?" | Casting, Forging, Machining, Molding |
| **Dimensional & Tolerance Rationalization** | "Do I really need ±0.01 mm, or is ±0.1 mm enough?" | All processes (GD&T, statistical tolerancing) |
| **Assembly & Handling Friendliness** | "Will robots/humans be able to grip, orient, insert, and fasten this part without struggle?" | Assembly, joining, fastening |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked to list "Design Considerations for X process," examiners expect a **3-bucket answer**: (i) *Material*, (ii) *Geometry/Shapes*, (iii) *Tolerances/Standards*. Memorize this trifecta.

---

## 1.4 Why Design Consideration is the *Single Highest-Leverage* Activity in DFMA

Studies by Boothroyd, Dewhurst, and the DFMA software consortiums consistently show:

> **70%–80% of a product's total manufacturing cost is committed at the design stage**, even though design itself consumes only ~5%–8% of the total engineering effort.

This is the famous **"Design Cost Leverage"** curve. **Design Consideration** is the *tool* through which this leverage is captured.

$$
\text{Design Leverage} = \frac{\Delta C_{total}}{\Delta C_{design}}
$$

A ₹1 change in design can ripple into a ₹10–₹100 change in total cost.

---

## 1.5 The Six Universal Design Considerations (Cross-Process)

Although every process has its own specialized rules, **six considerations** apply universally. These form the back-bone of any DFMA discussion:

1. **Functional requirements** must be defined *first* — the manufacturing process is selected **after** function, not before.
2. **Minimum number of parts** — Boothsroyd's principle: a part should exist *only* if it moves relative to another part, requires a different material, or is *absolutely necessary* for assembly/disassembly.
3. **Use of standard components** — bought-out parts (bearings, fasteners, O-rings) are always cheaper than custom ones.
4. **Ease of fabrication** — avoid features that demand multiple setups, special tooling, or secondary operations.
5. **Ease of assembly** — symmetry, self-locating features, snap-fits, and one-direction assembly.
6. **Ease of inspection and testing** — design datums, gauge points, and visual access into critical dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of KTU PEMET522 specifically expects students to discuss design considerations for **casting, forging, machining, sheet metal, and plastics**, which we will cover in the next sections.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Draft angle (sand casting) | $\theta_{draft}$ | 1° – 3° | degrees |
| Draft angle (die casting) | $\theta_{draft}$ | 0.5° – 1.5° | degrees |
| Minimum wall thickness (cast iron) | $t_{min}$ | **3 – 5** | mm |
| Minimum wall thickness (aluminum die cast) | $t_{min}$ | **0.8 – 1.5** | mm |
| Minimum bend radius (sheet steel) | $R_{min}$ | **1 × thickness (t)** | mm |
| Minimum corner radius (machining) | $R_{c}$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | mm |
| Minimum draft (plastic injection) | $\theta_{p}$ | 0.5° – 1° | degrees |
| Standard tolerance (free dimension) | IT12–IT13 | ±0.1 to ±0.5 | mm |
| Precision tolerance (critical) | IT6–IT7 | ±0.01 to ±0.05 | mm |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic to remember the six universal rules:** **F-M-S-F-A-I** → *Function → Minimize parts → Standardize → Fabricate easily → Assemble easily → Inspect easily.*

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Design Considerations

### A. Design Considerations for **Casting**

Casting is a process where molten metal is poured into a mold cavity of the desired shape. Design must respect the **fluid nature** of the molten metal and the **shrinkage** that follows on cooling.

#### A.1 Material-Based Considerations
- Choose a **castable alloy** (cast iron, cast steel, aluminum alloys like LM6, LM25).
- For **ferrous castings**, prefer compositions with high fluidity (high carbon equivalent).
- Avoid alloys with **wide freezing ranges** for intricate sections (causes shrinkage porosity).

#### A.2 Geometry-Based Considerations
1. **Uniform Wall Thickness** — Variations cause **hot spots** and **shrinkage cavities**.
2. **Generous Fillets & Radii** — Sharp internal corners create **stress raisers** and **hot tears**.
3. **Draft Angles** — Allow pattern withdrawal without damaging sand mold.
4. **Parting Line Selection** — Should be flat, simple, and minimize flash.
5. **Cores** — Avoid if possible; if necessary, design **core prints** for support.
6. **Avoid Large Flat Surfaces** — Cause warpage; use **corrugations or ribs**.

#### A.3 Tolerance-Based Considerations
- Standard sand casting tolerance: **±0.8 mm** for small parts, **±1.5 mm** for large parts.
- Die casting: **±0.05 mm** achievable.
- Machining allowances: **1.5–3 mm** on machined surfaces.

#### A.4 Casting-Specific Rules (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)
| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| Section thickness ratio ≤ 2:1 | Avoid differential cooling |
| Rib thickness ≤ 0.8 × wall thickness | Avoid hot spots at junctions |
| Boss diameter ≤ 3 × its height | Prevent shrinkage |
| Draft angle = 1°–3° for sand; 0.5°–1.5° for die | Ejection & pattern removal |

---

### B. Design Considerations for **Forging**

Forging uses **plastic deformation** under compressive load. Design must respect **metal flow** and **grain direction**.

#### B.1 Material-Based Considerations
- **Hot-work die steels** for hot forging (H11, H13).
- **Mild steels, alloy steels, aluminum, brass** for cold forging.
- Material must have good **ductility** and **hot workability**.

#### B.2 Geometry-Based Considerations
1. **Parting Line** — Should lie on the **centerline** of the forging for balanced die load.
2. **Draft Angles** — 5°–7° (hot), 1°–3° (cold) — critical for die release.
3. **Fillet Radii** — Generous radii reduce die wear and cracking.
4. **Web & Rib Design** — Avoid thin webs; use thicker webs with proper fillets.
5. **Symmetry** — Symmetrical forgings have better metal flow and lower die cost.
6. **Grain Flow Continuity** — Critical for fatigue resistance.

#### B.3 Tolerance-Based Considerations
- Hot forging: **±0.5 to ±1.5 mm**
- Cold forging: **±0.05 to ±0.2 mm**
- Draft: **5°–7°** for hot, **0.5°–1°** for cold
- Die shift tolerance: **±0.3 mm** typical

#### B.4 Forging-Specific Rules
| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| Fillet radius ≥ 1.5 × height of rib | Reduces stress concentration |
| Avoid deep cavities (depth/width ≤ 1:2) | Prevents incomplete fill |
| Draft on ribs: 3°–5° | Eases die release |
| Use ribs instead of webs | Better grain flow |

---

### C. Design Considerations for **Machining**

Machining is a **subtractive process** — material is removed by a cutting tool. Design must respect **tool geometry, accessibility, and chip evacuation**.

#### C.1 Material-Based Considerations
- **Free-machining steels** (with S, Pb, Mn) reduce cycle time.
- Hard materials (>45 HRC) need **carbide or ceramic tools**.
- Avoid work-hardening alloys (e.g., pure titanium) where possible.

#### C.2 Geometry-Based Considerations
1. **Standardize on Tools** — Use common drill, tap, reamer, end mill sizes.
2. **Avoid Internal Sharp Corners** — Inside corners are limited by tool radius.
3. **Provide Clearance Angles** — Tool must enter/exit freely.
4. **Hole Depths** — Limited to **5× diameter** for standard drills.
5. **Use Flat Surfaces When Possible** — Easier to fixture and machine.
6. **Avoid Multiple Setups** — Design so the part can be done in 1–2 setups.

#### C.3 Tolerance-Based Considerations
- Standard IT grades apply: IT6 (precision) to IT13 (free).
- **Surface finish** directly linked to tolerance (Ra 0.8 = tight, Ra 6.3 = free).
- **Tolerancing principle**: Specify tight tolerances **only** where functionally necessary.

#### C.4 Machining-Specific Rules
| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| Avoid deep pockets (> 4× width) | Tool deflection, chatter |
| Standard drill sizes (3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm) | Avoid special tools |
| Use external features over internal | Easier access, longer tool life |
| Avoid sharp internal corners | Tool radius limitation |
| Specify Ra not just tolerance | Surface finish drives cost |

---

### D. Design Considerations for **Sheet Metal Forming**

Sheet metal is shaped by **bending, drawing, stamping, punching**. Design must respect the **flat blank** and the **bending mechanics**.

#### D.1 Material-Based Considerations
- **Low-carbon steel (CRCA)**, **stainless steel (SS304)**, **aluminum (5052, 6061)**.
- Material **grain direction** affects bend quality.
- **Yield strength** determines minimum bend radius.

#### D.2 Geometry-Based Considerations
1. **Bend Radius** — Minimum $R_{min} = 1 \times t$ for steel, $0.5 \times t$ for soft aluminum.
2. **Flange Length** — Must be ≥ **2 × bend radius** to avoid tearing.
3. **Hole Distance from Bend** — Hole center must be ≥ **2t + R** from bend line.
4. **Avoid Re-entrant Features** — Hard to form.
5. **Bend Relief** — Provide small relief cuts to prevent tearing at bend-hole intersections.
6. **Hole Diameter** — ≥ **sheet thickness** to punch cleanly.

#### D.3 Tolerance-Based Considerations
- Sheet metal: **±0.1 to ±0.5 mm** achievable.
- Bend angle: **±0.5° to ±2°**.
- **Bend Allowance** formula:

$$
BA = \theta \times \left( R + k \times t \right) \times \frac{\pi}{180}
$$

Where $k$ is the **K-factor** (typically **0.33 to 0.5**), representing the neutral axis position.

#### D.4 Sheet Metal Rules
| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| Min bend radius = 1t (steel), 0.5t (aluminum) | Avoid outer-fiber cracking |
| Min flange length = 2R | Avoid tearing |
| Hole edge distance ≥ 2t | Avoid distortion |
| Use bend reliefs | Prevent tear at hole-bend junctions |

---

### E. Design Considerations for **Plastic Parts**

Plastic parts are typically made by **injection molding, blow molding, or thermoforming**. Design must respect the **flow of polymer melt** and **ejection from mold**.

#### E.1 Material-Based Considerations
- Choose **flow-friendly grades** for thin walls.
- **Filled polymers** (glass, carbon) are abrasive — require hardened tooling.
- **Recycled content** is now a sustainability requirement.

#### E.2 Geometry-Based Considerations
1. **Uniform Wall Thickness** — Plastics shrink on cooling; non-uniform walls warp.
2. **Generous Draft Angles** — **0.5° to 1°** minimum, **2°** for textured surfaces.
3. **Corner Radii** — Inside radius ≥ **0.5 × wall thickness**; outside radius ≥ **1.5 × inside radius**.
4. **Boss Design** — Use ribs to support bosses, not thick walls.
5. **Gating & Weld Lines** — Position gates to minimize weld lines on cosmetic surfaces.
6. **Sink Marks** — Avoid thick sections behind cosmetic surfaces.

#### E.3 Tolerance-Based Considerations
- Injection molding: **±0.1 to ±0.3 mm** for small parts.
- Shrinkage: **0.5%–3%** must be compensated in the mold.
- Dimensional stability: Affected by **moisture absorption** (nylons) and **thermal expansion**.

#### E.4 Plastic Design Rules
| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| Wall thickness ratio ≤ 2:1 | Avoid sink marks & warpage |
| Draft = 0.5°–1° smooth, 2° textured | Mold release |
| Rib thickness ≤ 0.6 × nominal wall | Avoid sink opposite rib |
| Corner radius = 0.5 × wall thickness | Stress & flow |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Design Consideration |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine block — uniform walls, draft, fillets; reduces casting defects by 30% |
| **Aerospace** | Forged turbine blade — grain flow aligned with stress for fatigue life |
| **Electronics** | Plastic mobile phone case — uniform wall, draft, ribs replace bosses |
| **White Goods** | Sheet metal washing-machine drum — bend relief, hole-edge distance, K-factor |
| **Medical Devices** | Machined surgical tool — feature alignment, tool standardization |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Cast gearbox housing — section transitions, draft, core prints |

---

## 2.4 The "Why Behind the How" — Logic Layer

Every design rule has a **physics root cause**. KTU examiners love to ask "**Why?**" behind every rule. Here is the causality chain for the most-tested rules:

1. **Why draft angles?** → Because the **part must be removed from the mold/die** without breaking the mold or the part. **Friction + shrinkage** = sticking. Draft creates a clearance wedge.
2. **Why uniform wall thickness?** → Because **cooling rate is proportional to surface area / volume ratio**. A thick section cools slower → solidifies later → creates a **shrinkage void** while the thin section is already rigid.
3. **Why fillet radii?** → **Sharp corners are stress concentrators** (Kt > 3) AND they create **hot spots** in casting. Smooth radii distribute stress and equalize cooling.
4. **Why standard sizes?** → **Tooling standardization** is the single largest cost driver. Special tools = 5–10× cost.
5. **Why minimum draft in plastics?** → **Polymers shrink onto the mold core** during cooling (thermal contraction > mold contraction). Without draft, the part cannot be ejected.
6. **Why bend reliefs?** → At a hole-bend junction, the material is **stretched non-uniformly**. A relief cut eliminates the constraint and prevents tearing.

> [!TIP]
> **For every design rule you list in the exam, also state the physics-based reason in 1 line.** This is what converts a 5-mark answer into a 7-mark answer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Reality:** In Part B questions, the 7-mark sub-part is often a *numerical*. The most common numerical in Module 2 is the **Bend Allowance** problem (Sheet Metal) and the **Shrinkage Cavity Sizing** problem (Plastics). We will derive both, exhaustively.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sheet Metal Bend Allowance (BA)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Given a sheet metal bracket that must be bent at angle $\theta = 90°$. The sheet thickness is $t = 2$ mm. The inside bend radius is $R = 4$ mm. The K-factor for the material (low-carbon steel) is $k = 0.45$. Determine the **Bend Allowance** for the flat-pattern layout.

### 3.1.2 Concept Foundation

When a sheet is bent:
- The **outer fibers stretch** (tension).
- The **inner fibers compress** (compression).
- There exists a **neutral axis** that does not change length.
- The neutral axis is **NOT** at the mid-thickness; it shifts **toward the inside of the bend** because the inner fiber has less arc length to compress than the outer fiber has to stretch.

This is captured by the **K-factor**:

$$
k = \frac{t_{neutral}}{t}
$$

Where $t_{neutral}$ is the distance from the inner bend surface to the neutral axis.

The **bend radius to the neutral axis** is:

$$
R_{neutral} = R + k \cdot t
$$

The **arc length of the neutral axis** (which equals the BA, because the neutral axis doesn't stretch) is:

$$
BA = \text{arc length} = R_{neutral} \cdot \alpha_{radians}
$$

where $\alpha_{radians} = \theta \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}, \quad R = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad k = 0.45
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bend angle to radians**

$$
\alpha_{rad} = \theta \times \frac{\pi}{180}
$$

$$
\alpha_{rad} = 90 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ radians}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the neutral-axis radius**

$$
R_{n} = R + k \cdot t
$$

$$
R_{n} = 4 + (0.45)(2) = 4 + 0.9 = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Bend Allowance**

$$
BA = R_{n} \cdot \alpha_{rad}
$$

$$
BA = 4.9 \times \frac{\pi}{2} = 4.9 \times 1.5708 = 7.6969 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical rounding**

In industrial practice, BA is rounded to **2 decimal places** for sheet steel < 3 mm thickness:

$$
BA \approx 7.70 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Result

$$
\boxed{BA = 7.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Flat-Pattern Verification

The total flat length of the bracket (with two flanges of length $L_1$ and $L_2$ joined by a 90° bend) is:

$$
L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 - t + BA
$$

(We subtract $t$ once because the corner geometry is replaced by the BA arc.) Suppose $L_1 = 50$ mm and $L_2 = 30$ mm:

$$
L_{flat} = 50 + 30 - 2 + 7.70 = 85.70 \text{ mm}
$$

This is the **length of the flat blank** that will, after bending, produce a bracket with the specified inside dimensions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Plastic Mold Cavity Sizing (Shrinkage Compensation)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A plastic gear made of **Nylon 6** must have an outer diameter of **$D_{part} = 60.00$ mm** at room temperature (25 °C). The mold is to be machined at room temperature. The volumetric shrinkage of Nylon 6 is **$S = 1.5\%$** (linear). Calculate the **mold cavity diameter** $D_{cavity}$ required.

### 3.2.2 Concept Foundation

When a plastic part cools from the molding temperature to room temperature, it **shrinks**. To produce a part of dimension $D_{part}$ *at room temperature*, the mold cavity must be **oversized** by the shrinkage factor:

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{linear})
$$

The relationship between **volumetric** and **linear** shrinkage is:

$$
S_{vol} = 3 \cdot S_{linear} - 3 \cdot S_{linear}^{2} + S_{linear}^{3} \approx 3 \cdot S_{linear}
$$

For $S_{linear} < 5\%$, the approximation is excellent.

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric shrinkage to linear**

$$
S_{linear} = \frac{S_{vol}}{3} = \frac{0.015}{3} = 0.005 = 0.5\%
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cavity-sizing formula**

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{linear})
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.00 \cdot (1 + 0.005)
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.00 \cdot 1.005
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.30 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.2.4 Final Result

$$
\boxed{D_{cavity} = 60.30 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Sanity check:** The mold is 0.30 mm larger. After molding, the part shrinks back exactly 0.30 mm to its target 60.00 mm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Assembly Efficiency Calculation

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
A product has been redesigned by a DFMA team.
- **Original design:** $N_a = 12$ parts, $t_{aa} = 45$ seconds/assembly operation
- **Minimum theoretical parts:** $N_m = 8$ parts
- **Theoretical assembly time per part:** $t_a = 10$ seconds

Calculate the **Assembly Efficiency (AE)** before and conceptually after redesign.

### 3.3.2 Concept Foundation

Boothroyd's **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index** quantifies how close a design is to its theoretical minimum. The formula is:

$$
AE (\%) = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{N_m \cdot t_a + N_a \cdot t_{aa}} \times 100
$$

A design with AE < 60% is considered poor; AE > 90% is excellent.

### 3.3.3 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute the "ideal time" component**

$$
N_m \cdot t_a = 8 \times 10 = 80 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the "extra time" component (current design)**

$$
N_a \cdot t_{aa} = 12 \times 45 = 540 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute AE**

$$
AE = \frac{80}{80 + 540} \times 100 = \frac{80}{620} \times 100
$$

$$
AE = 12.90\%
$$

### 3.3.4 Final Result

$$
\boxed{AE_{current} = 12.90\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often invert the formula. **Always remember:** the *ideal* time is the **numerator** and the *actual* time is the **denominator**. AE measures efficiency, so it must be < 100% for any non-ideal design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Selection Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Reality:** A frequent Part-B question is: *"Compare design considerations for casting, forging, and machining."* This matrix is the answer template.

| Design Parameter | Casting | Forging | Machining | Sheet Metal | Plastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Min Wall Thickness** | 3–5 mm | 5–8 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.8 mm |
| **Draft Angle** | 1°–3° | 5°–7° hot | None | None | 0.5°–1° |
| **Tightest Tolerance (typical)** | ±0.5 mm | ±0.3 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.1 mm |
| **Min Corner Radius** | 2–3 mm | 1.5–3 mm | 0.5 mm | 1t | 0.5 × t |
| **Undercuts** | Possible with cores | Very difficult | Possible with multi-axis | Not possible | Possible (slides) |
| **Material Utilization** | 70%–90% | 60%–80% | 30%–50% | 80%–95% | 90%–98% |
| **Tooling Cost** | Low–Medium | High | None | Low | High |
| **Part Complexity** | Very High | Medium–Low | High | Low–Medium | Very High |
| **Production Volume** | Med–High | High | Low–Med | High | Very High |
| **Wall Thickness Rule** | ≤ 2:1 ratio | N/A | N/A | N/A | ≤ 2:1 ratio |

---

## 3.5 Advanced Analytical Insight: Cost-Tolerance Tradeoff

The cost of producing a part rises **non-linearly** as tolerance tightens:

$$
C(T) = C_0 \cdot \left( \frac{T_0}{T} \right)^n
$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = base cost at reference tolerance $T_0$
- $T$ = current tolerance
- $n$ = process-dependent exponent, typically $1.2 \leq n \leq 1.8$

**Example:** Halving the tolerance ($T = T_0/2$) with $n = 1.5$:

$$
C = C_0 \cdot 2^{1.5} = C_0 \cdot 2.828
$$

**The cost nearly triples** when the tolerance is halved. This is why **rationalization of tolerances** is one of the highest-leverage design considerations.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Tolerance-Tightness Cost:** *Double-tight, triple-cost* — used by KTU examiners as a benchmark statement.
