```mermaid
    C1d --> D4b[Hole to Bend Distance]
    C1d --> D4c[Bend Relief]
    C1d --> D4d[Flange Length Rule]

    C1e --> D5a[Wall Uniformity 2 to 1]
    C1e --> D5b[Draft 0.5 to 1 deg]
    C1e --> D5c[Shrinkage Allowance]
    C1e --> D5d[Rib Thickness Rule]
```

## 4.2 Sheet Metal Flat-Pattern Derivation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    F0[Flat Blank Input] --> F1[Bend Angle theta]
    F1 --> F2[Convert to Radians]
    F2 --> F3[Read K factor from table]
    F3 --> F4[Compute Neutral Radius Rn]
    F4 --> F5[Apply BA formula]
    F5 --> F6[Flatten Flange Lengths]
    F6 --> F7[Subtract Thickness Correction]
    F7 --> F8[Final Flat Length Lflat]
    F8 --> F9[Generate NC Punch Program]
```

## 4.3 Casting Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    G0[New Casting Geometry] --> G1{Uniform Section?}
    G1 -- No --> G2[Add Fillets and Radii]
    G1 -- Yes --> G3[Check Draft]
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4{Draft Within 1 to 3 deg?}
    G4 -- No --> G5[Redesign Walls]
    G4 -- Yes --> G6{Undercut Present?}
    G5 --> G3
    G6 -- Yes --> G7[Add Core and Core Print]
    G6 -- No --> G8{Section Ratio Less than 2 to 1?}
    G7 --> G8
    G8 -- No --> G9[Add Chills or Risers]
    G8 -- Yes --> G10[Approved for Pattern]
    G9 --> G10
```

## 4.4 Plastic Part Design — DFMA Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    H0[Plastic Part Concept] --> H1[Define Functional Loads]
    H1 --> H2[Select Polymer Grade]
    H2 --> H3[Establish Nominal Wall t]
    H3 --> H4[Apply Uniform Wall Rule]
    H4 --> H5{Any Boss Present?}
    H5 -- Yes --> H6[Replace with Rib Support]
    H5 -- No --> H7[Apply Draft 0.5 to 1 deg]
    H6 --> H7
    H7 --> H8[Add Corner Radius 0.5 t]
    H8 --> H9[Compute Shrinkage Compensation]
    H9 --> H10[Generate Cavity Drawing]
    H10 --> H11[Select Gate Location]
    H11 --> H12[Finalize Mold Flow Layout]
```

## 4.5 Forging Design — Parting Line & Draft Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    J0[Forging Concept] --> J1[Identify Symmetry Axis]
    J1 --> J2{Is Forging Symmetrical?}
    J2 -- Yes --> J3[Place Parting Line on Centerline]
    J2 -- No --> J4[Minimize Die Shift]
    J3 --> J5[Apply Draft 5 to 7 deg Hot]
    J4 --> J5
    J5 --> J6[Add Fillet Radius Greater than 1.5 t]
    J6 --> J7{Deep Cavity Aspect Ratio?}
    J7 -- More than 1 to 2 --> J8[Use Multi Stage Die or Redesign]
    J7 -- Acceptable --> J9[Validate Grain Flow Direction]
    J8 --> J9
    J9 --> J10[Release Die Drawing]
```

## 4.6 Cost-Tolerance Tradeoff Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    K0[Tolerance Specification] --> K1{Functional Necessity}
    K1 -- Yes --> K2[Use Tight Tolerance]
    K1 -- No --> K3[Relax to Standard IT Grade]
    K2 --> K4[Cost Multiplier 1.5 to 2 times]
    K3 --> K5[Cost Multiplier 1.0 times]
    K4 --> K6[Final Part Cost]
    K5 --> K6
    K6 --> K7[Document in Tolerance Stack]
```

## 4.7 Design Consideration Flow — Process Selection Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Product Function Defined] --> L1[Estimate Annual Volume]
    L1 --> L2[Match to Process Family]
    L2 --> L3[Apply Process Specific Rules]
    L3 --> L4[Prototype and Test]
    L4 --> L5{Meets Functional Spec}
    L5 -- No --> L3
    L5 -- Yes --> L6[Lock Design and Tooling]
    L6 --> L7[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    L7 --> L1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Format of Section 5:** Part A = 3 marks (2 questions); Part B = 14 marks (EITHER/OR pattern — we provide two complete 14-mark alternatives Q-A and Q-B, each with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, with full model solutions).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Define "Design Consideration" in the context of DFMA. List any four general design considerations that apply to all manufacturing processes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):**

*Design Consideration in DFMA refers to the systematic engineering decisions incorporated during the product design phase to ensure that a component can be manufactured economically, assembled easily, and inspected reliably, while meeting functional requirements. It involves the integration of manufacturing process capabilities, material properties, geometric features, and tolerance specifications at the design stage itself.*

*The four general design considerations are:*
1. *Functional requirements must be clearly defined before process selection.*
2. *Minimum number of parts principle (Boothroyd).*
3. *Use of standard components and materials.*
4. *Ease of fabrication, assembly, and inspection.*

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Note (3-mark question):** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the 4-point list (0.5 each).

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Explain the significance of "Draft Angle" in casting and plastic part design. What are typical draft values for sand casting and injection molding?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Draft angle is the small taper provided on the vertical walls of a part to enable its easy removal from the mold without damaging the part or the mold. In sand casting, draft is required to withdraw the pattern from the sand mold; in plastic injection molding, it is required to overcome the shrinkage-grip of the part on the mold core as it cools.*

*Typical draft values:*
- *Sand casting: **1° to 3°** (1 mark)*
- *Injection molding: **0.5° to 1°** (1 mark)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (EITHER/OR Pattern)

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — DESIGN FOR SHEET METAL & PLASTICS

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q-A (a)** A sheet metal bracket is to be bent at **90°**. The sheet thickness is **2 mm**, the inside bend radius is **4 mm**, and the K-factor for the material is **0.45**. Calculate the **Bend Allowance**. The bracket has two flanges of length **50 mm** and **30 mm**. Determine the **flat blank length**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Q-A (b)** A plastic gear made of **ABS** must have an outer diameter of **80 mm** at 25 °C. The linear shrinkage of ABS is **0.6%**. Calculate the **mold cavity diameter**. List any **four design considerations** specific to plastic parts.  *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Q-A (a)  [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify givens** (0.5 mark)

$$
\theta = 90°,\quad t = 2 \text{ mm},\quad R = 4 \text{ mm},\quad k = 0.45
$$

**Step 2 — Convert angle to radians** (1 mark)

$$
\alpha = 90° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute neutral-axis radius** (1.5 marks)

$$
R_n = R + k \cdot t = 4 + (0.45)(2) = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Bend Allowance** (2 marks)

$$
BA = R_n \cdot \alpha = 4.9 \times \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

$$
BA = 4.9 \times 1.5708 = 7.6969 \approx \mathbf{7.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute flat blank length** (2 marks)

$$
L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 - t + BA
$$

$$
L_{flat} = 50 + 30 - 2 + 7.70 = \mathbf{85.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Final Answer:** $BA = 7.70$ mm; $L_{flat} = 85.70$ mm

---

### Model Solution — Q-A (b)  [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Apply cavity-sizing formula** (2 marks)

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{linear})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values** (1 mark)

$$
D_{cavity} = 80 \cdot (1 + 0.006) = 80 \cdot 1.006
$$

**Step 3 — Compute** (1 mark)

$$
D_{cavity} = \mathbf{80.48 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Four design considerations for plastics** (3 marks, 0.75 each)

1. *Uniform wall thickness (ratio ≤ 2:1) to prevent sink marks and warpage.*
2. *Draft angle of 0.5°–1° (smooth) and 1°–2° (textured) for mold release.*
3. *Generous corner radii (inner radius = 0.5 × wall thickness) to reduce stress and improve melt flow.*
4. *Shrinkage compensation in mold cavity sizing; ribs replacing bosses to avoid sink marks.*

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — DESIGN FOR CASTING & FORGING

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q-B (a)** Discuss the **general design considerations for sand casting** with reference to: (i) material selection, (ii) geometry, (iii) tolerances and machining allowance. Mention at least **six specific design rules**.  *(7 Marks)*

**Q-B (b)** A **hot steel forging** is to be designed with a deep central pocket. The pocket width is **30 mm** and required depth is **70 mm**. Comment on whether the design is acceptable, and suggest modifications. List any **four design considerations specific to forging**.  *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Q-B (a)  [7 Marks]

**Material Selection (2 marks):**
*Cast iron and cast steel are the most common. Composition should have good fluidity. Aluminum alloys (LM6, LM25) are used for lightweight castings. Avoid alloys with wide freezing ranges for intricate parts as they cause shrinkage porosity.*

**Geometry (2.5 marks):**
- *Uniform wall thickness — variations cause hot spots and shrinkage cavities.*
- *Generous fillets and radii (≥ 2 mm) — reduces stress concentration and hot tears.*
- *Draft angle of 1°–3° on vertical surfaces — enables pattern withdrawal.*
- *Simple, flat parting line — minimizes flash and tooling complexity.*
- *Cores used only when unavoidable — must have adequate core prints for support.*

**Tolerances and Machining Allowance (2.5 marks):**
- *Standard sand casting tolerance: ±0.8 mm (small) to ±1.5 mm (large).*
- *Die casting: ±0.05 mm achievable.*
- *Machining allowance: 1.5–3 mm on functional surfaces.*
- *Section thickness ratio ≤ 2:1 to avoid differential cooling.*
- *Rib thickness ≤ 0.8 × wall thickness; boss OD ≤ 3 × height.*

---

### Model Solution — Q-B (b)  [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the depth-to-width ratio** (2 marks)

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{D}{W} = \frac{70}{30} = 2.33
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to rule** (1 mark)

*For forging, the recommended depth-to-width ratio is **≤ 1:2 (i.e., 0.5)**. The given ratio of 2.33 means the pocket is **4.66× too deep**, which is NOT acceptable.*

**Step 3 — Justify** (2 marks)

*A deep pocket in forging causes:*
- *Incomplete die fill (metal cannot reach the bottom of the cavity).*
- *Excessive die wear at the corners.*
- *Trapped gas and porosity.*
- *High forging load and die cost.*

**Step 4 — Suggest modifications** (1 mark)

*Reduce depth to ≤ 15 mm (i.e., depth/width ≤ 0.5), or use a multi-stage forging operation, or split the part into two pieces joined by fasteners/welding.*

**Step 5 — Four forging design considerations** (1 mark)

1. *Parting line on the centerline of the forging.*
2. *Draft angle of 5°–7° (hot) for die release.*
3. *Generous fillet radii (≥ 1.5 × rib height) for die life.*
4. *Grain flow continuity along principal stress direction.*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Design Consideration" Questions (Module 2):**
> 1. **Listing rules without stating reasons** — KTU examiners award marks for *why* a rule exists. Always pair the rule with the physical cause.
> 2. **Forgetting the K-factor conversion** — Bend Allowance problems require degrees-to-radians conversion; students often write $\theta$ directly in degrees, costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using volumetric shrinkage directly in the cavity formula** — Most students forget to divide by 3. Always use **linear** shrinkage for one-dimensional dimensions.
> 4. **Conflating draft angles across processes** — Sand cast ≠ die cast ≠ plastic ≠ forging. Each has its own range. Marking will be strict.
> 5. **Skipping the part-count justification** — In any DFMA problem, explain **why** a part exists using Boothroyd's criteria: motion, different material, or no assembly/disassembly possible otherwise.
> 6. **Not labeling diagrams with units** — KTU board marking penalizes missing units (mm, °, %).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Design Consideration**

**Universal DFMA Principles (Process-Independent):**
- ☐ Define **function first**, process second.
- ☐ Minimize part count (Boothroyd).
- ☐ Use **standard** bought-out parts.
- ☐ Avoid **unnecessary** tight tolerances.
- ☐ Design for **one-direction** assembly.
- ☐ Provide **datums and inspection access**.
- ☐ Specify **surface finish** (Ra) alongside tolerance.

**Casting — Key Rules:**
- ☐ Draft 1°–3° (sand), 0.5°–1.5° (die).
- ☐ Wall thickness ratio ≤ 2:1.
- ☐ Fillet radius ≥ 2 mm.
- ☐ Rib thickness ≤ 0.8 × wall.
- ☐ Boss OD ≤ 3 × height.
- ☐ Machining allowance 1.5–3 mm.

**Forging — Key Rules:**
- ☐ Draft 5°–7° (hot), 0.5°–1° (cold).
- ☐ Parting line on **centerline**.
- ☐ Fillet radius ≥ 1.5 × rib height.
- ☐ Depth/width ratio ≤ 0.5.
- ☐ Grain flow **continuous**.
- ☐ Prefer ribs over webs.

**Machining — Key Rules:**
- ☐ Use **standard** drill/mill/tap sizes.
- ☐ Avoid **deep pockets** (>4× width).
- ☐ Internal corner radius ≥ tool radius.
- ☐ Minimize **setups** (1–2 ideal).
- ☐ Hole depth ≤ 5 × diameter.
- ☐ Tolerance-cost: doubling tight → tripling cost.

**Sheet Metal — Key Rules:**
- ☐ $R_{min} = 1 \cdot t$ (steel), $0.5 \cdot t$ (aluminum).
- ☐ Flange length ≥ $2R + t$.
- ☐ Hole center distance from bend ≥ $2t + R$.
- ☐ Use **bend relief** at hole-bend junctions.
- ☐ $BA = (R + k \cdot t) \cdot \theta_{rad}$.
- ☐ Specify **K-factor** in drawing notes.

**Plastic Parts — Key Rules:**
- ☐ Wall thickness ratio ≤ 2:1.
- ☐ Draft 0.5°–1° (smooth), 1°–2° (textured).
- ☐ Inside radius = **0.5 × t**; outside radius = 1.5 × inside.
- ☐ Rib thickness ≤ **0.6 × t_wall**.
- ☐ Cavity = Part × (1 + S_linear).
- ☐ Specify **gate location** and **weld-line** awareness.

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $BA = (R + k \cdot t) \cdot \theta \cdot \pi/180$
- ☐ $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{linear})$
- ☐ $AE = \dfrac{N_m \cdot t_a}{N_m \cdot t_a + N_a \cdot t_{aa}} \times 100$
- ☐ $C \propto 1/T^{1.5}$ (cost-tolerance)

**High-Boards Memorization Line:**
> *"A good design is not the one that *can* be manufactured, but the one that *has been designed to be* manufactured."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DFA Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DFA Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Assembly (DFA) Methodology** is a systematic, quantitative engineering methodology used during the product design phase to simplify the product structure, reduce the number of parts, and ease the assembly operations. The goal is to minimize the total assembly cost and time while maintaining the product's functional requirements.

Among the various DFA methods (Lucas, Hitachi, Boothroyd-Dewhurst, etc.), the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Method (1982)** is the most widely accepted and forms the core of the KTU syllabus. It provides a structured, two-stage evaluation:

1. **Minimum Part Analysis** – Decide whether a part is functionally essential or whether it can be combined with another.
2. **Assembly Time/Cost Analysis** – For the parts that are retained, estimate the handling, insertion, and fastening times using empirical data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA procedure, handling codes, insertion codes, and the design efficiency formula are **high-weightage** topics and are routinely asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of the KTU End Semester Examination.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **packing a travel bag for a 3-day trip**.

- If you blindly throw things in, you will have **many loose items** (clothes, chargers, shoes, toiletries, books) and waste a lot of time finding, picking up, and arranging them.
- A smart traveler **combines** items (e.g., rolls clothes to save space, uses a multi-purpose charger, packs shoes inside a laundry bag) so the *number of items* is reduced, and each item is **easy to grab and place**.

**DFA works exactly like this for a product:**
- Every "item" is a **part** in an assembly.
- Combining parts (roll them together = integrate functions) is the **Minimum Part Analysis**.
- Easy grabbing and placing is the **Ease of Handling and Insertion Analysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Philosophy of DFA:** *"A part that does not need to exist is the cheapest part to make, the cheapest part to assemble, and the cheapest part to maintain — because it has zero cost."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in DFA

- **NM** — Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts (integer, dimensionless).
- **t_min** — Theoretical minimum assembly time per part = **3 seconds** (standard benchmark in Boothroyd-Dewhurst manual assembly).
- **t_actual** — Actual estimated assembly time for the design (seconds).
- **η_DFA** — Design Efficiency (%).
- **α** — Handling time code (seconds).
- **β** — Insertion time code (seconds).
- **T_m** — Total manual assembly time (seconds).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA

The methodology is built on **two sequential questions** for every part in the assembly:

### Pillar 1: Does the Part Need to Exist? (Minimum Part Criterion)

A part should be retained in the design **only if it satisfies at least one** of the following three functional criteria (often remembered as the **3-Criteria Test**):

1. **Motion Criterion** — The part must move relative to all other parts during operation.
2. **Material Criterion** — The part must be made of a different material from all other parts.
3. **Disassembly Criterion** — The part must be separable from all other parts, *or* separation during servicing is required.

> [!NOTE]
> If a part fails **all three** criteria, it is a **candidate for elimination** or integration with another part.

### Pillar 2: Can the Part Be Easily Handled and Inserted? (Ease of Assembly)

For every part that survives Pillar 1, the designer assigns an **empirically derived time code** for:
- **Handling (α)** — Time to grasp, orient, and position the part.
- **Insertion (β)** — Time to align and mate the part with the subassembly.
- **Fastening (γ)** — Time to secure the part (screw, snap-fit, weld, etc.) — applied only if the part is fastened.

## 2.2 Detailed Handling Codes (α)

The part is classified into one of three handling categories based on its **physical symmetry and size**:

| Code | Criteria | Typical α (s) |
|:----:|:---------|:-------------:|
| **H1** | Part thickness ≥ 2 mm, easy to grasp, rotation **not** required | 1.0 |
| **H2** | Part thickness 1–2 mm OR requires rotation for insertion | 1.5 |
| **H3** | Very thin / flexible / requires special handling (e.g., gaskets) | 3.0 |

## 2.3 Detailed Insertion Codes (β)

The insertion difficulty is classified into three categories based on **mating features**:

| Code | Criteria | Typical β (s) |
|:----:|:---------|:-------------:|
| **I1** | Self-locating features, no resistance, single straight-line motion | 1.0 |
| **I2** | Requires reorientation, multiple motions, moderate resistance | 1.5 |
| **I3** | Difficult insertion: blind, obstructed, requires special tools | 3.0 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they are 80% of the numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----:|
| 1 | $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_M \cdot t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | **Design Efficiency** | % |
| 2 | $T_m = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\alpha_i + \beta_i)$ | **Total Manual Assembly Time** | s |
| 3 | $N_M = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{Min Criterion Satisfied Part})_i$ | **Theoretical Min. No. of Parts** | — |
| 4 | $A_m = \dfrac{N_M \cdot t_a}{t_m}$ | **DFA Index (Cost-Based)** | — |

**Boundary Conditions & Conventions:**
- Standard **t_min = 3 seconds** for Boothroyd-Dewhurst manual assembly.
- **t_actual** is the sum of all α and β codes for the assembly.
- An **ideal DFA design** has η_DFA approaching **100%**.
- Industry benchmark: η_DFA > **60%** is considered **good**; > **80%** is **excellent**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Used by Ford, GM, and Toyota to slash part counts in gearboxes and instrument panels.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Apple, Dell, and HP use DFA to design laptops with < 10 main assemblies that snap-fit together in seconds.
- **Medical Devices:** Reduces sterilization and re-assembly workload by minimizing small, hard-to-handle parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Design Efficiency Formula

**Conceptual Starting Point:**
Design Efficiency should measure **how close the actual design is to the ideal (theoretical minimum) assembly**.

**Step 1 — Define the Ideal Assembly Time:**
If the assembly used the *theoretical minimum number of parts* ($N_M$), and each part took the *theoretical minimum time* ($t_{\min}$), then the **ideal assembly time** would be:

$$T_{\text{ideal}} = N_M \times t_{\min}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Actual Assembly Time:**
From the empirical α and β codes assigned to each retained part, the **actual assembly time** is:

$$T_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\alpha_i + \beta_i)$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**
A natural measure of "how ideal" the design is, is the ratio of the ideal time to the actual time. Since the ideal time is *lower* than the actual time (the actual has extra parts and extra operations), this ratio will lie between 0 and 1.

$$\text{Efficiency Ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Percentage:**
Multiplying by 100 to express the ratio as a percentage, we obtain the canonical Boothroyd-Dewhurst **Design Efficiency** equation:

$$\boxed{\eta_{DFA} = \frac{N_M \cdot t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — A 6-Part Assembly (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A product assembly consists of **6 parts** as listed in the table below. Using the Boothroyd-Dewhurst codes assigned, determine:
> (i) Total actual assembly time $t_{\text{actual}}$
> (ii) Design efficiency $\eta_{DFA}$
> (iii) Comment on the design.

| Part No. | Description | α (s) | β (s) | Min. Criterion Satisfied? |
|:--------:|:------------|:-----:|:-----:|:------------------------:|
| 1 | Main housing | 1.0 (H1) | 1.5 (I2) | ✓ (Material) |
| 2 | Cover plate | 1.0 (H1) | 1.0 (I1) | ✗ (Eliminate?) |
| 3 | Shaft | 1.5 (H2) | 1.5 (I2) | ✓ (Motion) |
| 4 | Bearing | 1.0 (H1) | 1.0 (I1) | ✓ (Material) |
| 5 | Retaining ring | 1.0 (H1) | 1.5 (I2) | ✗ (Can integrate) |
| 6 | Bolt + nut (fastened) | 1.0 (H1) | 3.0 (I3) | ✗ (Can use snap-fit) |

**Step 1 — Determine Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts ($N_M$):**

- Part 1: ✓ Material → **Keep**
- Part 2: ✗ All three criteria fail → **Eliminate** (can be integrated with Part 1)
- Part 3: ✓ Motion → **Keep**
- Part 4: ✓ Material → **Keep**
- Part 5: ✗ Can be integrated → **Eliminate**
- Part 6: ✗ Can be replaced by snap-fit → **Eliminate**

Therefore, $N_M = 3$ (Parts 1, 3, and 4).

**Step 2 — Compute Actual Assembly Time ($t_{\text{actual}}$):**

$$t_{\text{actual}} = \sum (\alpha_i + \beta_i)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{actual}} &= (1.0 + 1.5) + (1.0 + 1.0) + (1.5 + 1.5) + (1.0 + 1.0) + (1.0 + 1.5) + (1.0 + 3.0) \\
&= 2.5 + 2.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 \\
&= 16.0 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Design Efficiency Formula:**

Given standard $t_{\min} = 3$ s, $N_M = 3$, and $t_{\text{actual}} = 16$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{DFA} &= \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3 \times 3}{16.0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{9}{16} \times 100 \\
&= 56.25 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the Design:**

- $\eta_{DFA} = 56.25\%$ is **below the industry benchmark of 60%**.
- The assembly has **3 extra parts** ($N - N_M = 6 - 3 = 3$) that can be eliminated.
- The bolt-nut pair (Part 6) has the highest β value (3.0 s) — replacing it with a **snap-fit** can drastically reduce the insertion time.
- **Design verdict:** The product is over-engineered; a redesign merging Parts 2, 5, and 6 into Part 1 is recommended.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — DFA vs. Traditional Assembly Design

| Parameter | Traditional Design | DFA-Based Design |
|:----------|:------------------|:-----------------|
| Part Count | High (30–50 parts typical) | Reduced by 30–60% |
| Assembly Time | Long, complex | Minimized |
| Cost | High tooling + labor | Significantly lower |
| Maintainability | Often poor | Improved (fewer joints) |
| Design Efficiency | < 30% typical | 60–100% target |
| Designer Focus | Function only | Function **+** manufacturability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Concept] --> B[List All Parts N]
    B --> C{For Each Part Apply 3-Criteria Test}
    C -->|Fails All 3| D[Mark Part as Candidate for Elimination]
    C -->|Passes at least 1| E[Retain Part]
    D --> F{Can Part be Integrated with Another?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Combine with Adjacent Part]
    F -->|No| E
    G --> E
    E --> H[Assign Handling Code H1 H2 or H3]
    H --> I[Assign Insertion Code I1 I2 or I3]
    I --> J{Is Part Fastened?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Add Fastening Time Code]
    J -->|No| L[Sum alpha plus beta]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute t_actual equals Sum of all times]
    M --> N[Count Retained Parts equals N_M]
    N --> O[Compute Design Efficiency eta_DFA]
    O --> P{eta_DFA greater than 60 percent}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Accept Design]
    P -->|No| R[Redesign: Reapply 3-Criteria Test]
    R --> C
    Q --> S[End: Document DFA Report]
```

## 4.2 DFA Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PHASE]
        partA[Part List N]
        funcA[Functional Requirements]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS[DFA ANALYSIS ENGINE]
        moduleA[Module A: Minimum Part Test]
        moduleB[Module B: Handling Code Assignment]
        moduleC[Module C: Insertion Code Assignment]
        moduleD[Module D: Fastening Code Assignment]
    end
    subgraph METRICS[OUTPUT METRICS]
        metricA[N_M Calculation]
        metricB[t_actual Calculation]
        metricC[Efficiency eta_DFA]
        metricD[DFA Index A_m]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[DECISION GATE]
        gate1{eta_DFA greater than 60}
        gate2{Number of iterations exceeded}
    end
    partA --> moduleA
    funcA --> moduleA
    moduleA --> moduleB
    moduleB --> moduleC
    moduleC --> moduleD
    moduleA --> metricA
    moduleB --> metricA
    moduleC --> metricB
    moduleD --> metricB
    metricA --> metricC
    metricB --> metricC
    metricA --> metricD
    metricB --> metricD
    metricC --> gate1
    gate1 -->|No| moduleA
    gate1 -->|Yes| finalOut[Final DFA Report]
    gate2 -->|Yes| finalOut
    gate2 -->|No| gate1
```

## 4.3 The 3-Criteria Decision Tree (Per Part)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Examine Part] --> Q1{Does part move relative to others?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| KEEP1[Keep Part]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is material different from all others?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| KEEP2[Keep Part]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is separation required for service?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| KEEP3[Keep Part]
    Q3 -->|No| ELIM[Candidate for Elimination]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define the term **Design Efficiency** as used in the Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method. State its ideal value and the standard minimum part time assumed in the manual analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Design Efficiency ($\eta_{DFA}$) is a quantitative metric used to evaluate how close an actual product design is to its theoretical minimum assembly configuration. It is defined as the ratio of the ideal assembly time (theoretical minimum number of parts × minimum time per part) to the actual assembly time, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{DFA} = \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \;\%$$

The ideal value is **100%**, and the standard minimum part time used in Boothroyd-Dewhurst manual analysis is **$t_{\min} = 3$ seconds**.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ideal value, 1 mark for $t_{\min}$)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List and briefly explain the three functional criteria used in the Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method to determine whether a part is essential.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three criteria for the **Minimum Part Test** are:

1. **Motion Criterion:** The part must undergo relative motion with respect to all other parts during the operation of the product. (1 mark)
2. **Material Criterion:** The part must be made of a material that is different from that of every other part in the assembly. (1 mark)
3. **Disassembly Criterion:** The part must require separation from the sub-assembly for servicing, maintenance, or recycling, OR it must be separable in principle. (1 mark)

A part that satisfies **at least one** criterion is retained; otherwise, it is a candidate for elimination.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Value Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly (DFA) methodology** in detail. Describe both the minimum part analysis and the assembly time analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly has **9 parts**. The theoretical minimum number of parts is determined to be **5**. The actual total assembly time, computed from the empirical α and β codes, is **25 seconds**.
Calculate the design efficiency of the product. Suggest two design modifications to improve the efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Methodology (7 marks):**

The Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method is a **systematic, quantitative procedure** for evaluating and improving product designs from the standpoint of ease of assembly. It is executed in **two major stages**:

**Stage 1 — Minimum Part Analysis (3.5 marks):**
Every part in the assembly is examined against the three functional criteria:
- Motion Criterion
- Material Criterion
- Disassembly Criterion

A part must pass **at least one** criterion to be retained. Parts failing all three are flagged for elimination or integration with adjacent parts. The result is a count of the **Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts ($N_M$)** that is *theoretically* required to deliver the product's function.

**Stage 2 — Assembly Time Analysis (3.5 marks):**
For every retained part, the designer assigns:
- A **handling code** (α) — based on thickness, symmetry, and graspability: H1 (1.0 s), H2 (1.5 s), H3 (3.0 s).
- An **insertion code** (β) — based on mating difficulty: I1 (1.0 s), I2 (1.5 s), I3 (3.0 s).
- A **fastening code** — if the part is secured (screw, weld, snap-fit).

The actual total assembly time is:

$$t_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\alpha_i + \beta_i + \gamma_i)$$

The **Design Efficiency** is then computed and compared to the benchmark of 60%.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**
- $N_M = 5$
- $t_{\text{actual}} = 25$ s
- Standard $t_{\min} = 3$ s

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{DFA} &= \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5 \times 3}{25} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{15}{25} \times 100 \\
&= 60 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation and Design Modifications (4 marks):**

The design efficiency is exactly **60%**, which is on the boundary of the industry benchmark — it is *acceptable* but not excellent.

**Two Design Modifications:**

1. **Reduce the part count** by reapplying the 3-criteria test to the 4 extra parts. Eliminating even 2 unnecessary parts brings $N_M$ closer to $N$ and improves efficiency.
2. **Replace threaded fasteners (I3 = 3.0 s) with snap-fits or self-locating features (I1 = 1.0 s).** This reduces both α and β without losing functional integrity. A single such replacement can save 2 seconds per occurrence.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst procedure** for Design for Assembly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product is assembled from **12 components**. After applying the minimum part criteria, the theoretical minimum number of parts is found to be **7**. The actual assembly time estimated from the empirical table is **48 seconds**, and the minimum time per part is **1.5 seconds**. Calculate the **(i)** Design Efficiency, and **(ii)** DFA Index. Comment on whether the design is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Flowchart-based Explanation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the canonical Boothroyd-Dewhurst flowchart. The student must explain the following sequence:

1. List all parts of the product (total $N$). *(1 mark)*
2. Apply the **3-criteria test** (Motion, Material, Disassembly) to each part. *(2 marks)*
3. Mark parts failing all criteria as candidates for elimination; if integration is possible, merge them. *(1 mark)*
4. Count the retained parts → $N_M$. *(1 mark)*
5. For each retained part, assign handling (H1/H2/H3) and insertion (I1/I2/I3) codes. *(1 mark)*
6. Sum all α and β values to get $t_{\text{actual}}$, then compute $\eta_{DFA}$. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 12$, $N_M = 7$, $t_{\text{actual}} = 48$ s, $t_{\min} = 1.5$ s.

**(i) Design Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{DFA} &= \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7 \times 1.5}{48} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10.5}{48} \times 100 \\
&= 21.875 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) DFA Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_m &= \frac{N_M \times t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{10.5}{48} \\
A_m &= 0.21875 \;\; (\text{dimensionless})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Some textbooks express DFA Index as $\frac{N_M \cdot t_a}{t_m}$ where $t_a$ is the actual time per part. The numerical value remains 0.219.

**Comment on Design Acceptability (2 marks):**

$\eta_{DFA} \approx 21.9\%$ is **far below the 60% benchmark** and is **unacceptable**. The product is severely over-assembled: 5 unnecessary parts are present (12 − 7 = 5), and the assembly is roughly 4.5× slower than ideal. A complete redesign using the 3-criteria test, snap-fit fasteners, and part consolidation is **strongly recommended**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to percentage** — Many students write $\eta_{DFA} = 0.21$ instead of $21.9\%$. You lose 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Using $N$ instead of $N_M$** — The numerator of the design efficiency formula uses the *theoretical minimum* $N_M$, NOT the total part count $N$. This is the single most common error.
> 3. **Wrong $t_{\min}$ value** — KTU expects **3 seconds** for the standard Boothroyd-Dewhurst manual method. If the problem explicitly states a different value, use it; otherwise, default to 3 s.
> 4. **Confusing DFA Index with Design Efficiency** — The DFA Index $A_m$ is a *ratio* (no percentage sign); Design Efficiency $\eta_{DFA}$ is a *percentage*.
> 5. **Skipping the α and β table in numerical problems** — Always show the per-part α and β values in a tabular form before summing. Examiners award 2–3 marks for this structured presentation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for DFA Methodology:**

- 🔑 **DFA Full Form:** Design for Assembly — a *design-stage* methodology, not a manufacturing-stage one.
- 🔑 **Pioneers:** Boothroyd and Dewhurst (1982) — the *de facto* industry standard.
- 🔑 **Two-Stage Process:** Minimum Part Analysis ➔ Assembly Time Analysis.
- 🔑 **3-Criteria Test (Mnemonic: "MMD"):** **M**otion, **M**aterial, **D**isassembly. Pass at least **one**.
- 🔑 **Handling Codes:** H1 = 1.0 s, H2 = 1.5 s, H3 = 3.0 s.
- 🔑 **Insertion Codes:** I1 = 1.0 s, I2 = 1.5 s, I3 = 3.0 s.
- 🔑 **Standard $t_{\min}$:** **3 seconds** (Boothroyd-Dewhurst manual benchmark).
- 🔑 **Master Formula:** $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_M \cdot t_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \, (\%)$.
- 🔑 **Design Efficiency Benchmarks:** > 60% = Good, > 80% = Excellent, 100% = Theoretical Ideal.
- 🔑 **DFA Index Formula:** $A_m = \dfrac{N_M \cdot t_a}{t_m}$ (dimensionless ratio).
- 🔑 **Primary Goal:** Reduce part count + ease handling/insertion = lower cost, faster assembly.
- 🔑 **KTU Exam Tip:** Always present α and β values in a **table** for clarity — earns 2–3 easy marks.
- 🔑 **Real-World Impact:** DFA typically reduces part count by 30–60% and assembly time by 40–70% in industrial applications.
- 🔑 **Related Term:** DFMA = Design for Manufacture **and** Assembly (broader scope).
- 🔑 **Snap-fits > Threaded fasteners** in DFA — easier to handle and faster to insert.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Guidelines for Manual Assembly for Part Handling and for Insertion and Fastening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Design Guidelines for Manual Assembly for Part Handling and for Insertion and Fastening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Manual Assembly** is the process of fitting together discrete manufactured components into a sub-assembly or finished product using human operators, where the worker's hands (and simple hand tools) — not automated machines — perform the part handling, insertion, and fastening operations. In the *Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)* philosophy, manual assembly is treated as a **time-and-motion cost driver** that must be minimized at the design stage.

The three sequential operations governed by these guidelines are:

1. **Part Handling** — The act of grasping, orienting, and positioning a component at the assembly station.
2. **Insertion** — The act of moving the part into its intended mating location against a reference feature.
3. **Fastening** — The act of locking, securing, or joining the part permanently (screws, snap-fits, rivets, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** *Design for Manual Assembly (DFMA — Manual)* is the engineering practice of configuring a product so that every part can be picked up, oriented, inserted, and fastened by a human operator in the **shortest possible time** (typically **1.5 s to 3 s per insertion** as a benchmark) with the **fewest possible tools and motions**.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Think of assembling a simple **wooden flat-pack chair from IKEA**. Before you even pick up a single screw, the designer has already determined:

- Whether you can pick up each wooden plank with **one hand** (Part Handling).
- Whether the dowel pins at the ends of each plank have a **chamfered lead-in** so the plank slides in easily (Insertion).
- Whether the screws are the **same size** so you do not waste time changing Allen keys, and whether the chair is designed to lie flat so the screw head faces **upward** for gravity-fed assembly (Fastening).

If the chair is well-designed, you assemble it quickly with one hand while the other holds the frame. If poorly designed, the dowels jam, screws fall out of the chassis, and you must flip the assembly over repeatedly. **DFMA-Manual guidelines exist to eliminate these "human frustration points" before the product reaches the factory floor.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benchmark Constants (Recall for Exams):**
> - Standard part **insertion time** ≈ **1.5 s** (ideal, vertical, one-handed).
> - Standard **fastening time** for a single screw ≈ **3.0 s to 5.0 s**.
> - **70% of total assembly time** is consumed by handling and orienting parts — not by the actual insertion.
> - Preferred direction of insertion is **vertical (top-down)** to use **gravity assistance** and allow one-handed operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Manual Assembly Design

Manual assembly design is decomposed into three functionally distinct sub-operations. Each sub-operation has its own set of design rules. Mastering all three is essential for the KTU Module 2 questions.

---

### 2.2 Design Guidelines for Part Handling

Part handling is governed by the **"One-Hand Rule"** and the **"Symmetry Rule."** A poorly handled part requires the operator to:

- Look at the part (visual search time).
- Re-orient it (rotational manipulation).
- Re-grip it if it slips (re-insertion time).

#### 2.2.1 Guidelines Checklist (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Guideline | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| H1 | **Design parts for one-handed grasping** | Frees the other hand to hold the sub-assembly. |
| H2 | **Make parts symmetric** (or asymmetric only when function demands) | Symmetric parts can be inserted in multiple orientations; asymmetric parts need extra time for orientation. |
| H3 | **Provide self-aligning / self-locating features** (chamfers, lead-ins, tapered pins) | Reduces the precision required by the human hand. |
| H4 | **Avoid nesting, tangling, or sticking** (e.g., flat washers, springs) | Removes the need to separate parts one-by-one. |
| H5 | **Ensure adequate clearance** between adjacent parts in a bin | Prevents two parts from being picked up at once. |
| H6 | **Ensure visibility** during handling | Operator should not have to guess part orientation. |
| H7 | **Avoid flimsy / flexible parts** that deform in the gripper | Flexible parts increase the probability of rejection. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Trick (Exam Favorite):** A part with **rotational symmetry** of order *n* has *n* equivalent orientations. A square block has 4-fold symmetry, so 1 in 4 random grasps is correct. A circular shaft has infinite-fold symmetry — **0 s orientation time** is required.

---

### 2.3 Design Guidelines for Insertion

Insertion is the act of moving a part from its held position to its final functional position. The dominant design rule is the **"Compliance and Lead-In Rule."**

#### 2.3.1 Guidelines Checklist (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Guideline | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| I1 | **Provide generous lead-in chamfers or fillets** on mating features | The chamfer acts as a "funnel" that captures misalignment up to ±2 mm. |
| I2 | **Use compliance** (floating fasteners, self-aligning bushings, slotted holes) | Compliance absorbs residual misalignment between the part and the sub-assembly. |
| I3 | **Prefer vertical (top-down) insertion** | Uses gravity to hold the sub-assembly, freeing both hands of the operator. |
| I4 | **Minimize the number of simultaneous insertions** | Two simultaneous insertions take far longer than the sum of two single insertions (Holt's 1978 study). |
| I5 | **Eliminate the need for holding-down** | If the sub-assembly must be held with one hand, the other hand cannot insert. |
| I6 | **Use snap-fits** instead of screws when feasible | Snap-fits require 1 operation; a screw + washer + nut requires 3. |
| I7 | **Avoid over-constrained geometry** (3+ locating features on one part) | Over-constraint forces the operator to file/shim to fit. |
| I8 | **Provide access windows** for the operator's fingers | The assembly must remain "humanly reachable." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chamfer Geometry Rule:** A 30°–45° chamfer with a width of **1 mm to 2 mm** at the entrance of any hole or slot is considered the **DFMA standard**. It increases the effective capture diameter and reduces the operator's required alignment accuracy by up to **5×**.

---

### 2.4 Design Guidelines for Fastening

Fastening is the locking operation. The dominant design rule is the **"Standardization and Tool-Access Rule."**

#### 2.4.1 Guidelines Checklist (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Guideline | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| F1 | **Minimize the variety of fastener types and sizes** | Every changeover of an Allen key or screwdriver takes **1.5 s to 3 s** in lost time. |
| F2 | **Use captive fasteners** (captive screws, captive washers, caged nuts) | Prevents fasteners from being dropped, lost, or picked up incorrectly. |
| F3 | **Orient the tool access axis vertically (top-down)** | Aligns with the operator's natural wrist motion. |
| F4 | **Provide adequate tool clearance envelope** | A standard Phillips screwdriver needs a **50 mm diameter** vertical envelope; a powered nut-runner needs **80 mm to 100 mm**. |
| F5 | **Avoid threaded holes in soft or cast materials** (cast iron, plastics) | Threads strip easily; use threaded inserts or captive nuts instead. |
| F6 | **Standardize on a single screwdriver head** (e.g., Torx T20 for the whole product) | Reduces tool changeover to **zero**. |
| F7 | **Use self-tapping screws only into ductile materials** | Avoid self-tapping into cast iron, hardened steel, or brittle plastics. |
| F8 | **Provide thread run-out relief** at the hole exit | Prevents thread galling and binding during the last 1–2 turns. |
| F9 | **Use threaded inserts (molded-in or post-molded) for plastics** | Increases thread pull-out strength by **3× to 5×** over direct tapping. |
| F10 | **Eliminate fasteners entirely** by using snap-fits, integral hinges, or press-fits | Each eliminated fastener saves **3 s to 5 s**. |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---------------|------------|---------------------|
| $T_{ma}$ | Total manual assembly time per part | seconds (s) |
| $T_h$ | Handling time (grasp + orient) | **1.0 s** to **3.0 s** |
| $T_i$ | Insertion time | **1.5 s** to **3.0 s** |
| $T_f$ | Fastening time | **3.0 s** to **5.0 s** |
| $T_{h,\min}$ | Minimum handling time (highly symmetric) | **0.5 s** |
| $N_{eq}$ | Number of equivalent orientations | dimensionless |
| $P_{correct}$ | Probability of correct orientation $= 1 \div N_{eq}$ | 0 to 1 |
| $\eta_{assembly}$ | Assembly efficiency $= T_{useful} \div T_{total}$ | 0 to 1 (target $\geq 0.7$) |
| $n_{fasteners}$ | Number of fasteners per product | integer |
| $t_{tool}$ | Tool changeover time | **1.5 s** to **3.0 s** |
| $t_{c}$ | Time to pick a captive fastener | **1.0 s** |
| $t_{nc}$ | Time to pick a non-captive fastener | **2.0 s** to **3.0 s** |
| $C_{chamfer}$ | Recommended chamfer width | **1 mm** to **2 mm** |
| $\theta_{chamfer}$ | Recommended chamfer angle | **30°** to **45°** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These guidelines are used by industrial designers in automotive, consumer electronics, and white-goods manufacturing to **halve the assembly time** of a product before a single prototype is built. Companies like **Ford, Toyota, and Bosch** routinely report 20%–40% labor cost savings purely from manual assembly redesign.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Manual Assembly Time

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus expects students to derive the **time equation for manual assembly** using the sequential operation model. The derivation is shown below in full.

---

**Step 1 — Decompose the assembly operation into sequential sub-tasks.**

A single manual assembly task can be modeled as a **serial process** on a workstation. The total time for one part is the sum of the time for handling, the time for insertion, and the time for fastening.

$$
T_{ma} \;=\; T_h \;+\; T_i \;+\; T_f
$$

**Reasoning:** The three sub-tasks cannot be parallelized by a single human operator; they must occur in sequence. Therefore, by the additivity property of time, the total is the arithmetic sum.

---

**Step 2 — Expand the handling time component.**

Handling time $T_h$ is the time to (a) reach for the part, (b) grasp it, and (c) orient it correctly. Of these, (a) and (b) are nearly constant for a given workstation. The variable part is (c) — the **orientation time** — which depends on the part's symmetry.

For a part with $N_{eq}$ equivalent orientations (rotational symmetry order), the probability that a random grasp yields the correct orientation is $P_{correct} = 1 / N_{eq}$. The expected number of re-grips required is $(N_{eq} - 1)$. Each re-grip costs approximately $t_{re}$ seconds.

$$
T_h \;=\; t_{reach} \;+\; t_{grasp} \;+\; (N_{eq} - 1) \cdot t_{re}
$$

**Reasoning:** A highly asymmetric part (e.g., a key with teeth on one side, $N_{eq} = 1$) requires the operator to re-grip **zero times** beyond the initial grasp — orientation is forced. A square block ($N_{eq} = 4$) requires on average 3 re-grips for correct orientation. A circular shaft ($N_{eq} \to \infty$) requires near-infinite time if precise rotational orientation is needed.

---

**Step 3 — Expand the insertion time component.**

Insertion time $T_i$ has three sub-components: the **move-down time** (bringing the part to the mating feature), the **engagement time** (the moment the chamfer first contacts the hole), and the **seating time** (sliding the part fully home).

$$
T_i \;=\; t_{move} \;+\; t_{engage} \;+\; t_{seat}
$$

**Reasoning:** Each sub-component is a discrete time interval. The engagement time $t_{engage}$ is **strongly dependent on the chamfer geometry** — a part with no chamfer requires the operator to align to within $\pm 0.2$ mm, which can take **5 s to 10 s**; a part with a 1.5 mm chamfer can be aligned by feel in **0.5 s**.

---

**Step 4 — Expand the fastening time component.**

Fastening time $T_f$ depends on whether the fastener is **captive or non-captive**, and on the tool required.

$$
T_f \;=\; t_{pick} \;+\; t_{align} \;+\; t_{drive} \;+\; t_{tool}
$$

**Reasoning:** $t_{pick}$ is the time to extract the fastener from its bin (captive: ~1.0 s; non-captive: ~2.5 s). $t_{align}$ is the time to position the fastener in the hole (~0.5 s). $t_{drive}$ is the rotation time (3.0 s for a screw, 1.5 s for a snap-fit). $t_{tool}$ is **non-zero only when a tool changeover is required** (e.g., switching from a #2 Phillips to a Torx T20). This term **dominates** the fastening cost when fasteners are not standardized.

---

**Step 5 — Combine all terms into the master equation.**

Substituting the expanded forms back into the master equation:

$$
T_{ma} \;=\; \underbrace{t_{reach} + t_{grasp} + (N_{eq} - 1) \cdot t_{re}}_{T_h} \;+\; \underbrace{t_{move} + t_{engage} + t_{seat}}_{T_i} \;+\; \underbrace{t_{pick} + t_{align} + t_{drive} + t_{tool}}_{T_f}
$$

**Reasoning:** This is the KTU-expected canonical form. Exam questions typically supply the sub-component times as a table and ask the student to compute $T_{ma}$ for a given $N_{eq}$ and tool-changeover scenario.

---

**Step 6 — Apply the equation to a numerical illustration (worked example).**

Given a part with $N_{eq} = 2$ (e.g., a U-shaped bracket — can be picked up in 2 correct orientations), a 1.5 mm chamfer at the hole, and a single Torx T20 captive screw that requires no tool changeover:

- $t_{reach} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{grasp} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{re} = 0.5$ s (re-grip cost)
- $t_{move} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{engage} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{seat} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{pick} = 1.0$ s (captive)
- $t_{align} = 0.5$ s
- $t_{drive} = 3.0$ s
- $t_{tool} = 0$ s (no changeover)

$$
T_{ma} \;=\; (0.5 + 0.5 + (2 - 1) \cdot 0.5) + (0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5) + (1.0 + 0.5 + 3.0 + 0)
$$

$$
T_{ma} \;=\; (1.5) + (1.5) + (4.5) \;=\; 7.5 \text{ s per part}
$$

**Interpretation:** A well-designed part with a captive screw and a chamfer takes **7.5 s** end-to-end. If the designer had removed the chamfer ($t_{engage}$ rises to 5 s) and used a non-captive screw with a tool changeover ($t_{pick} = 2.5$ s, $t_{tool} = 2$ s), the same part would take **13.5 s** — a **80% increase** in time for zero functional gain.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Impact of Design Decisions

| Design Decision | Effect on $T_h$ | Effect on $T_i$ | Effect on $T_f$ | Net Time Saved |
|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Add 1.5 mm chamfer | 0 s | **−4.5 s** | 0 s | **−4.5 s** |
| Use captive screw | 0 s | 0 s | **−1.5 s** | **−1.5 s** |
| Make part symmetric ($N_{eq}: 1 \to 4$) | **−1.5 s** | 0 s | 0 s | **−1.5 s** |
| Eliminate screw → snap-fit | 0 s | 0 s | **−4.5 s** | **−4.5 s** |
| Standardize all screws (one tool) | 0 s | 0 s | **−2.0 s** | **−2.0 s** |
| Vertical instead of horizontal insertion | **−0.5 s** | **−1.0 s** | 0 s | **−1.5 s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The biggest single time-saver in manual assembly is **eliminating fasteners** (snap-fits, integral hinges, press-fits). The second biggest is **adding a chamfer**. The third is **making the part symmetric**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Manual Assembly Design Guidelines

The diagram below maps the **decision hierarchy** that a designer follows when configuring a product for manual assembly. It is rendered using the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** style as a fallback for the complex physical drawing that this topic would otherwise require.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: New Product Concept]) --> decisionB{Is Part Asymmetric?}
    decisionB -- No Symmetry Needed --> symA[Apply Symmetry Maximization Rules]
    decisionB -- Functionally Asymmetric --> symB[Add Visual or Geometric Polarizing Feature]
    symA --> handlingC[Part Handling Sub-System]
    symB --> handlingC
    handlingC --> ruleH1[H1 One-Handed Grasping]
    handlingC --> ruleH2[H2 Adequate Grip Clearance]
    handlingC --> ruleH3[H3 No Nesting or Tangling]
    handlingC --> ruleH4[H4 Visibility During Pick]
    ruleH1 --> insertionD[Insertion Sub-System]
    ruleH2 --> insertionD
    ruleH3 --> insertionD
    ruleH4 --> insertionD
    insertionD --> ruleI1[I1 Chamfered Lead-Ins]
    insertionD --> ruleI2[I2 Vertical Top Down Direction]
    insertionD --> ruleI3[I3 Compliance and Self Alignment]
    insertionD --> ruleI4[I4 No Simultaneous Multi Inserts]
    ruleI1 --> fasteningE[Fastening Sub-System]
    ruleI2 --> fasteningE
    ruleI3 --> fasteningE
    ruleI4 --> fasteningE
    fasteningE --> ruleF1[F1 Standardize Fastener Type]
    fasteningE --> ruleF2[F2 Use Captive Fasteners]
    fasteningE --> ruleF3[F3 Vertical Tool Access]
    fasteningE --> ruleF4[F4 Eliminate Fasteners Snap Fit]
    ruleF1 --> finalF([Compute Total Manual Assembly Time Tma])
    ruleF2 --> finalF
    ruleF3 --> finalF
    ruleF4 --> finalF
    finalF --> checkG{Are Targets Met?}
    checkG -- Tma under 5 s and Efficiency above 0.7 --> approveH([Design Approved for Manual Assembly])
    checkG -- Tma above 10 s --> rejectI[Redesign Loop Back to Part Handling]
    rejectI --> decisionB
```

### 4.2 Interpretive Walkthrough of the Diagram

The flow above is read as a **top-down decision tree**:

- The top block identifies whether the part is symmetric or not — this is the single most important variable.
- The three sub-systems (**Handling → Insertion → Fastening**) operate sequentially, mirroring the time equation $T_{ma} = T_h + T_i + T_f$.
- Each sub-system has its own rule set (H1–H4, I1–I4, F1–F4) which is a compact subset of the full checklist (only the **four highest-leverage rules per sub-system** are shown for diagram clarity).
- The terminal block computes $T_{ma}$ and runs a quality gate:
  - If $T_{ma} < 5$ s and efficiency $\eta_{assembly} \geq 0.7$ → the design is **approved**.
  - Otherwise, the loop returns to the symmetry decision block, forcing a redesign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Convention Used:** The **round-edged rectangles** represent *states* (start, end, decision gates), and the **rectangular blocks** represent *design rule applications*. The arrow directions indicate the *order* in which the designer should apply the rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *List any **six** general design guidelines for the manual insertion of parts in a product assembly.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks):**

1. Provide **generous lead-in chamfers** (1–2 mm, 30°–45°) on all mating holes and shafts.
2. Prefer **vertical (top-down) insertion** to use gravity and allow one-handed operation.
3. Incorporate **compliance** (floating fasteners, self-aligning bushings, slotted holes) to absorb residual misalignment.
4. **Minimize the number of simultaneous insertions** — avoid requiring the operator to align two parts at once.
5. **Eliminate the need for holding-down** the sub-assembly by fixturing it in a jig.
6. Use **self-locating features** (tapered pins, dowels, snap-fit lead-ins) to guide the part into place.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Define **"captive fastener"** and explain its advantage in manual assembly design with a numerical illustration.*

**Model Answer:**

A **captive fastener** is a screw, nut, or washer that is **pre-retained** on the sub-assembly or on the tool — typically by a captive ring, a caged-nut retainer, or a spring-loaded holder — so that it cannot be dropped, lost, or mis-picked during assembly.

**Numerical Illustration (1.5 marks for the numbers):**

- Time to pick a **non-captive** screw from a bin and orient it: $t_{pick} = 2.5$ s
- Time to pick a **captive** screw from its retainer: $t_{pick} = 1.0$ s
- **Time saved per fastener = 1.5 s**
- For a product with **20 identical screws**: $20 \times 1.5 = 30$ s saved per unit.

**Advantage (1.5 marks):** Eliminates the search-and-orient step, reduces dropped-part rejection, and allows **one-handed fastening**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat sketch, explain the **"Symmetry Rule"** for part handling in manual assembly. Derive the relationship between the number of equivalent orientations $N_{eq}$ and the expected handling time $T_h$.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A product has 6 fasteners, of which 4 are M6 captive Torx screws and 2 are M8 standard hex bolts requiring a separate spanner. Compute the total fastening time $T_f$ using the following data: $t_{pick,captive} = 1.0$ s, $t_{pick,non-captive} = 2.5$ s, $t_{align} = 0.5$ s, $t_{drive,M6} = 3.0$ s, $t_{drive,M8} = 4.5$ s, $t_{tool,changeover} = 2.0$ s. State two design changes that would reduce $T_f$ by at least 20%.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the **"Chamfer Rule"** and the **"Compliance Rule"** for manual insertion. Sketch a chamfered hole-and-shaft pair and indicate the recommended chamfer dimensions as per DFMA standards.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A designer replaces 8 individual screws (each with a tool changeover) with 4 captive screws + 4 snap-fits. Using the same data as in Question A, compute the **percentage reduction** in total fastening time.*

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) – Symmetry Rule [7 marks]**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Symmetry Rule** states that a part's number of equivalent orientations $N_{eq}$ (the rotational symmetry order) must be maximized to minimize handling time. A part that is rotationally symmetric of order $N_{eq}$ can be picked up correctly in $1/N_{eq}$ fraction of attempts on average.

**Sketch (2 marks — described in text since no physical drawing possible):**

```
   +---+                +---+
   |   |  N=1 (key)     | * |  N=2 (U-bracket)
   |   |                |   |
   +---+                +---+
   Asymmetric           Half-symmetric
   T_re-grip = 0 s      T_re-grip = 0.5 s
   (must be held right side up)
```

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Let $N_{eq}$ = number of equivalent orientations (rotational symmetry order).
- Probability of correct orientation in one random grasp: $P = 1 \div N_{eq}$
- Expected number of grasps to succeed: $E[N] = N_{eq}$
- Expected number of **re-grips** beyond the first: $E[N_{re}] = N_{eq} - 1$
- Each re-grip takes $t_{re}$ seconds.

$$
T_h \;=\; t_{reach} \;+\; t_{grasp} \;+\; (N_{eq} - 1) \cdot t_{re}
$$

This shows that $T_h$ **increases linearly** with $(N_{eq} - 1)$. To minimize $T_h$, the designer should make $N_{eq}$ as **large as possible** — i.e., make the part rotationally symmetric.

---

**Part (b) – Numerical Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- 4 captive M6 Torx screws: $t_{pick} = 1.0$ s each
- 2 non-captive M8 hex bolts: $t_{pick} = 2.5$ s each
- $t_{align} = 0.5$ s (per fastener)
- $t_{drive,M6} = 3.0$ s, $t_{drive,M8} = 4.5$ s
- $t_{tool,changeover} = 2.0$ s (one changeover from Torx to spanner)

**Step 1:** Total pick time for captive screws.

$$
T_{pick,1} = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Total drive time for M6 captive screws.

$$
T_{drive,1} = 4 \times 3.0 = 12.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Total pick time for non-captive M8 bolts.

$$
T_{pick,2} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Total drive time for M8 bolts.

$$
T_{drive,2} = 2 \times 4.5 = 9.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Total align time (6 fasteners).

$$
T_{align,total} = 6 \times 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6:** Tool changeover time.

$$
T_{tool} = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7:** Sum all components.

$$
T_f = 4.0 + 12.0 + 5.0 + 9.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 = 35.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_f = 35.0$ seconds (1 mark for the final number).

**Two design changes to reduce $T_f$ by ≥ 20% [1 mark for each change]:**

1. **Standardize all 6 fasteners to the same M6 captive Torx screw** — eliminates the tool changeover (-2.0 s) and reduces M8 pick/drive to M6 levels (saves ~5.5 s). **Total reduction ≈ 7.5 s = 21.4%.**
2. **Replace the 2 M8 bolts with snap-fits** — saves $2 \times (2.5 + 4.5) = 14$ s and removes the need for a spanner. **Total reduction ≈ 14 s = 40%.**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) – Chamfer and Compliance Rules [7 marks]**

**Chamfer Rule (3 marks):** A **chamfer** is a beveled edge at the entry of a hole or the tip of a shaft. It acts as a **funnel** that captures the incoming part even when the operator's alignment is off by up to ±2 mm. The DFMA standard is a chamfer width of **1 mm to 2 mm** at an angle of **30° to 45°**. Without a chamfer, the operator must align to within ±0.2 mm, which slows insertion to 5–10 s. With a chamfer, the same insertion takes 0.5–1.5 s — a **5× to 10× speed-up**.

**Compliance Rule (2 marks):** **Compliance** is the ability of a part or fixture to deform elastically (or have a designed floating feature) so as to absorb residual misalignment between the part and the sub-assembly. Examples include **floating captive screws**, **slotted mounting holes**, **self-aligning bushings**, and **Belleville washers**.

**Sketch description (2 marks):**

```
        Chamfer (1.5 mm x 45 deg)
        /\
       /  \
   +--/    \--+         <- Hole entry (diameter D)
   |          |
   |          |    -->  Shaft (diameter D - 0.2 mm for clearance)
   |          |
   +----------+
```

**Part (b) – Percentage Reduction [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute original $T_f$ for **8 screws each with a tool changeover** (assume 8 separate tool changeovers, worst case).

- $T_{pick} = 8 \times 2.5 = 20.0$ s
- $T_{align} = 8 \times 0.5 = 4.0$ s
- $T_{drive} = 8 \times 3.0 = 24.0$ s
- $T_{tool} = 8 \times 2.0 = 16.0$ s
- $T_{f,original} = 20.0 + 4.0 + 24.0 + 16.0 = 64.0$ s

**Step 2:** Compute new $T_f$ for **4 captive screws + 4 snap-fits** (one tool changeover only).

- For 4 captive screws:
  $T_{pick,1} = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0$ s
  $T_{align,1} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$ s
  $T_{drive,1} = 4 \times 3.0 = 12.0$ s
- For 4 snap-fits (assume $t_{pick} = 0.5$ s, $t_{drive} = 1.0$ s, no tool needed):
  $T_{pick,2} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$ s
  $T_{align,2} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$ s
  $T_{drive,2} = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0$ s
- $T_{tool,new} = 1 \times 2.0 = 2.0$ s (only one changeover)
- $T_{f,new} = 4.0 + 2.0 + 12.0 + 2.0 + 2.0 + 4.0 = 26.0$ s

**Step 3:** Compute percentage reduction.

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{T_{f,original} - T_{f,new}}{T_{f,original}} \times 100 = \frac{64.0 - 26.0}{64.0} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{38.0}{64.0} \times 100 = 59.375\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The redesigned assembly shows a **59.4% reduction** in fastening time (3 marks for arithmetic + 1 mark for statement).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting the tool changeover term $t_{tool}$.** This is the single most-skipped component. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for it explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $N_{eq}$ with the number of features.** A square has $N_{eq} = 4$ (4-fold rotational symmetry), not $N_{eq} = 1$ because it has 4 sides. A 6-tooth gear has $N_{eq} = 6$.
> 3. **Reporting units inconsistently.** Always report time in **seconds (s)** unless the question specifies minutes.
> 4. **Writing $T_{ma}$ without showing the sub-component breakdown.** The KTU 2024 marking scheme requires the **expanded form** of the equation for full marks. Writing only the master equation $T_{ma} = T_h + T_i + T_f$ without sub-components scores **at most 40%** of the allotted marks.
> 5. **Omitting the chamfer width and angle** in sketches. The examiner checks for **1–2 mm** and **30°–45°** as standard values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Manual Assembly = Human + Simple Tools** — not automated.
- [x] **Three Sub-Operations in Sequence:** Handling → Insertion → Fastening.
- [x] **Master Time Equation:** $T_{ma} = T_h + T_i + T_f$.
- [x] **70% of $T_{ma}$** is consumed by **handling and orienting** the part.
- [x] **Symmetry Rule:** Maximize $N_{eq}$ to reduce $T_h$; formula: $T_h = t_{reach} + t_{grasp} + (N_{eq} - 1) \cdot t_{re}$.
- [x] **Chamfer Rule:** 1–2 mm width, 30°–45° angle at all mating entries.
- [x] **Compliance Rule:** Use floating fasteners, slotted holes, self-aligning bushings.
- [x] **Vertical Insertion Rule:** Top-down insertion is preferred (gravity + 1-hand operation).
- [x] **Standardization Rule:** Minimize fastener variety; one tool type for the whole product.
- [x] **Captive Fastener Rule:** Use captive screws to save ~1.5 s per fastener.
- [x] **One-Hand Rule:** Designer must ensure the sub-assembly is held by jig, not the operator's other hand.
- [x] **Elimination Rule:** Snap-fits, integral hinges, and press-fits are **always preferred** over screws.
- [x] **Tool Clearance Rule:** Provide **50 mm vertical envelope** for hand tools, **80–100 mm** for powered nut-runners.
- [x] **Assembly Efficiency Target:** $\eta_{assembly} \geq 0.7$ (70%).
- [x] **Standard Insertion Time Benchmark:** **1.5 s** (ideal) to **3 s** (acceptable).
- [x] **Standard Fastening Time Benchmark:** **3.0 s** (screw) to **5.0 s** (bolt + washer + nut).
- [x] **Asymmetric parts:** Use a single polarizing feature (notch, key, chamfer on one side) to force correct orientation cheaply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Rules for Product Design for Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Rules for Product Design for Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Automation (DFA)** is a systematic, rule-based engineering methodology that governs the geometric, dimensional, and functional characteristics of a product's components and sub-assemblies to ensure that the entire manufacturing, handling, inspection, and assembly lifecycle can be executed reliably by automated machinery, robotic systems, and computer-controlled production lines with minimal human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Product design for automation is the conscious application of design rules and heuristics that enable components to be fed, oriented, transported, joined, inspected, and tested by automated equipment rather than human operators."*

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, this topic falls under the **Design for Assembly (DFA)** and **Design for Manufacture (DFM)** integrated framework, specifically targeting the elimination of human-handicraft dependencies in modern Industry 4.0 compliant production environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The LEGO® vs. Hand-Crafted Wooden Toy Paradigm**

Imagine two toys: one is a **hand-crafted wooden automobile** that requires a skilled artisan to glue tiny wheels, thread screws, and align axles using steady hands and visual judgment. The other is a **LEGO® Technic set** that snaps together automatically — each piece has a unique shape, can be inserted in only one correct orientation, and locks with an audible "click."

The **LEGO® set is "Designed for Automation."** It can be assembled by a robotic gripper just as easily as by a child, because:
- Each brick is **asymmetric** (forbidden orientations are geometrically impossible)
- Pieces are **stackable from above** (the "Z-axis" assembly direction)
- **Snap-fits** replace threaded fasteners
- Pieces are fed from standardized magazines (the bumps on top, tubes on bottom)

A product designed for automation must behave like a **LEGO® brick**, not like the hand-crafted wooden toy. The rules we study today are essentially the **"LEGO® Principles"** translated into engineering design heuristics.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Modern Manufacturing Competitiveness:**
> 1. **Cost Reduction** — Automated assembly reduces direct labor cost by **60–80%**
> 2. **Quality Consistency** — Automation achieves **Six-Sigma** level repeatability vs. ±10–15% variability in manual assembly
> 3. **Throughput Scaling** — Automated lines operate **2–5× faster** than manual stations

These pillars are directly tested in KTU's **Part B (14-mark) questions** under Module 2.

### 1.4 Scope Boundaries of the Topic

The "General Rules for Product Design for Automation" encompass **four major design sub-domains**:

| Sub-Domain | Core Concern | Primary Stakeholder |
|------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Feeding & Orientation** | Can a bulk-fed part self-orient correctly? | Vibratory bowl feeders, hoppers |
| **Handling & Transfer** | Can a robot grip the part repeatedly? | End-effectors, conveyors |
| **Joining & Assembly** | Can parts mate without human dexterity? | Press-fits, snap-fits, ultrasonic welding |
| **Inspection & Testing** | Can sensors verify the assembly? | Vision systems, force-torque sensors |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 15 Universal Design Rules for Automation

These rules are the **high-yield knowledge** mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus. Each rule addresses a specific failure mode in automated production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Mnemonic — "F.A.S.T. S.C.A.M.P. R.O.B.O.T."** (12 categories condensed into 12 letters for exam recall)

---

#### **RULE 1: Design for Single Direction of Assembly (Z-Axis Rule)**
- All components should be assembled along **one preferred axis** (conventionally the +Z vertical direction).
- Why: Robots and indexing machines move in Cartesian or rotary axes; off-axis insertion requires complex cam-driven mechanisms.
- Real-world violation: A bolt hole accessed from the side of a gearbox cover, requiring an articulated arm to reach around the housing.

#### **RULE 2: Minimize the Number of Parts (Boothroyd-Dewhurst Principle)**
- Apply the **"Does this part move relative to others?"** test.
- If a part does **not move** during operation and is **not required for separation**, eliminate it.
- **Quantitative Target:** Aim for a part count reduction of **30–50%** from the initial concept.

#### **RULE 3: Use Symmetric or Asymmetric (Deliberately Polarized) Features**
- **Symmetric parts** are easier to feed (no orientation errors possible).
- **Strongly asymmetric parts** are easier to orient (vibratory feeders can sort by length/width).
- **AVOID "accidental symmetry"** (e.g., a square part with diagonal key slot — symmetric in 4 orientations).

#### **RULE 4: Avoid Features That Cause Tangling, Nesting, or Interlocking**
- Avoid: Flexible sheets, helical springs (free state), curled chips, threaded shafts (stack badly).
- Solution: Use **coiled springs with constrained ends**, or use **blind threads** instead of through-threads.

#### **RULE 5: Design for Self-Locating and Self-Aligning Features**
- Incorporate **chamfers (typically 0.5 mm × 45°)** at all insertion entries.
- Provide **lead-in tapers** that correct **±1.0 mm positional tolerance** down to ±0.05 mm at the seated position.
- Self-aligning chamfers reduce robot accuracy demands by **80%**.

#### **RULE 6: Avoid Separate Fasteners**
- Replace **bolts, nuts, screws, washers** with:
  - Snap-fits
  - Integral hinges
  - Ultrasonic or vibration welds
  - Adhesive bonds (UV-cure or anaerobic)
- **Target:** Each avoided fastener saves **3–5 seconds** of cycle time and one feeder station.

#### **RULE 7: Standardize Components Across the Product Family**
- Use the **same screws, bearings, clips, and connectors** wherever possible.
- Reduces the number of bulk-feeders required from, e.g., 15 to 4.
- **Statistical Effect:** Standardization reduces BOM (Bill of Materials) complexity by **40%**.

#### **RULE 8: Use Rigid Components (Avoid Flexible/Flimsy Parts)**
- Flexible parts jam in feeders and grippers unpredictably.
- If flexibility is required (e.g., a gasket), use **rigid carriers** or **poka-yoke trays**.

#### **RULE 9: Design for Top-Down or Base-Down Assembly**
- Gravity must aid, not oppose, the assembly process.
- **EXCEPTION:** Some insert-molding and over-molding operations assemble from below, but these are specialized.

#### **RULE 10: Provide Adequate Clearances for Tooling and Grippers**
- Minimum clearance for a standard parallel-jaw gripper: **10 mm** on the gripping surface.
- Allow **finger clearance** of at least **15 mm** around fasteners.
- Consider **MRS (Modular Robotics Standard)** gripper geometries.

#### **RULE 11: Design for Mistake-Proofing (Poka-Yoke)**
- A part should be **impossible to assemble incorrectly**.
- Use **unique keying features** (e.g., notches, asymmetric bosses, color-coding via IR-visible pigments).
- **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function** quantifies the cost of a mistake-proofing failure.

#### **RULE 12: Design for Ease of Feeding (Bulk vs. Magazine)**
- **Bulk feeding** (vibratory bowls) is preferred for **< 50 mm** parts with high symmetry.
- **Magazine feeding** (stick feeders) is required for elongated parts.
- **Target Feeder Efficiency:** η_feed > 95% (ratio of correctly oriented parts to total fed parts).

#### **RULE 13: Design for Inspection and Vision-System Compatibility**
- Avoid specular (mirror-like) surfaces — they create **glare** in machine vision.
- Use **matte finishes, etched datums, or laser-marked features** for fiducial marks.
- Provide **inspection windows** in housings.

#### **RULE 14: Modularize the Design (Plug-and-Play Sub-Assemblies)**
- Sub-assemblies should be **independently testable** and **independently joinable**.
- Use **standardized interface connectors** (electrical, pneumatic, fluidic).
- Reduces final assembly steps by **60–80%**.

#### **RULE 15: Use Self-Fastening and Self-Locking Features**
- **Snap-fits** (cantilever, annular, torsion)
- **Press-fits** (H7/h6 or H7/r6 ISO fits)
- **Heat-staking** for plastic-to-plastic joints
- **Self-clinching nuts** for sheet-metal joints

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Automation Design Calculations**

| Symbol | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| $\eta_{feed}$ | $\eta_{feed} = \dfrac{N_{oriented}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Feeding efficiency (correctly oriented parts per 100 fed) | % |
| $C_{automation}$ | $C_{auto} = C_{equip} + C_{labor} + C_{maintenance}$ | Total cost of an automated station | ₹ (or $) |
| $T_{cycle}$ | $T_{cycle} = T_{feed} + T_{transport} + T_{join} + T_{inspect}$ | Total cycle time of an assembly station | seconds |
| $A_{i}$ | $A_{i} = t_{i} \cdot N_{i}$ | Boothroyd's assembly time for a part (i) | seconds |
| $D_{FA}$ | $D_{FA} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_m}{N_{total}}$ | Design-for-Assembly efficiency index (Boothroyd) | dimensionless |
| $C_{manual}$ | $C_{manual} = \sum (t_{i} \cdot w \cdot N_{i})$ | Manual assembly cost (w = wage rate) | ₹ / unit |
| $C_{auto\_savings}$ | $S = (C_{manual} - C_{auto}) \cdot V_{annual}$ | Annual savings from automation | ₹ / year |
| $ROCI$ | $ROCI = \dfrac{S - I_{capital}}{I_{capital}} \times 100\%$ | Return on Capital Investment for automation | % / year |
| $B_{payback}$ | $B_{payback} = \dfrac{I_{capital}}{S - C_{operating}}$ | Payback period for automation | years |
| $E_{alignment}$ | $E_{align} = \dfrac{\delta_{positional}}{\delta_{final}}$ | Alignment effectiveness ratio | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{poka-yoke}$ | $\eta_{py} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{defects\_with\_py}}{N_{defects\_without\_py}}$ | Mistake-proofing effectiveness | dimensionless |
| $R_{reliability}$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function for automated equipment | dimensionless |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**

- $\eta_{feed} \geq 95\%$ is the **industrial benchmark**; below 85%, the feeder is uneconomical.
- $C_{auto}$ assumes a **15-year equipment life** with **5% annual maintenance cost** of capital.
- $D_{FA}$ value of **1.0** is ideal; values above 1.5 indicate a poorly designed assembly.
- $ROCI > 20\%$ is required for KTU-style economic feasibility arguments.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Case Studies**

1. **Automotive Industry (Tesla, Toyota):** A modern car has ~10,000 fasteners. Tesla's Gigapress technology eliminates **~400 parts** by casting the rear underbody as a single piece — direct application of **Rule 2 (Minimize Parts)** and **Rule 6 (Avoid Fasteners)**.

2. **Consumer Electronics (Apple iPhone):** Every iPhone uses **poka-yoke** asymmetric connectors (Lightning/USB-C), **adhesive bonds** (display to chassis), and **vision-aligned** pick-and-place of SMD components.

3. **Medical Devices (Syringes, Pens):** Insulin pens are designed for **single-direction assembly** with **self-aligning snap-fits**, allowing fully automated production at **120 units/minute**.

4. **PCB Assembly (Pick-and-Place Machines):** Component packaging (tape-and-reel) is designed using **Rule 3 (Polarized Features)** — every resistor and capacitor has a unique end-termination that forces correct orientation.
