## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Rule Selection by Failure Mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Approach a 14-Mark KTU Question on This Topic:**
> Step 1 — Identify the **manufacturing/assembly failure mode** in the given product diagram.
> Step 2 — Map the failure to the **corresponding rule(s)**.
> Step 3 — Propose a **redesign** and quantify the improvement.

| Observed Failure Mode | Applicable Rule | Recommended Redesign |
|----------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Part jams in vibratory bowl | Rule 3 + Rule 4 | Add an **asymmetric notch** to break symmetry, eliminate tangling features |
| Robot cannot grip the part | Rule 10 + Rule 8 | Add **gripper flats** (≥ 10 mm wide), use rigid material |
| Assembly is inserted upside-down | Rule 11 + Rule 3 | Add **polarizing key**, use **chamfered lead-in** |
| Multiple fastener stations slow line | Rule 6 + Rule 15 | Replace with **snap-fits** or **self-clinching fasteners** |
| Vision system misreads orientation | Rule 13 | Add **laser-etched fiducials**, use matte finish |
| Cycle time is dominated by feeding | Rule 12 | Switch from **bulk to magazine feeding** |
| Many parts, long assembly | Rule 2 + Rule 14 | **Modularize** and **eliminate** non-functional parts |

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Efficiency Index

The **Design-for-Assembly Efficiency Index** ($D_{FA}$ or $E_{A}$) is a quantitative metric that students must know how to derive and apply for 7-mark sub-questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Equation Derivation:**

The fundamental premise is that the **minimum theoretical assembly time** of any assembly is **3 seconds per part** (a value empirically established from extensive industrial studies by Boothroyd and Dewhurst). This represents the lower bound where each part is trivially added in 3 seconds with no handling difficulty.

$$
T_{min} = 3 \cdot N_{m}
$$

Where:
- $T_{min}$ = minimum theoretical assembly time (seconds)
- $N_{m}$ = number of parts that **must** be moved (i.e., parts that have a relative motion requirement OR are required for disassembly)

The actual assembly time, including handling and insertion difficulty, is computed as:

$$
T_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} t_{i} \cdot N_{i}
$$

Where:
- $t_i$ = time to handle and insert part $i$ (from Boothroyd tables, typically 1.5–30 s)
- $N_i$ = quantity of part $i$ in the assembly
- $N_{total}$ = total number of distinct parts in the BOM

**Step 1 — Define the efficiency ratio:**

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{min}}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the ratio using the explicit definitions:**

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} t_{i} \cdot N_{i}}{3 \cdot N_{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simplification for assemblies with one of each part** ($N_i = 1$ for all $i$):

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} t_{i}}{3 \cdot N_{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $N_{total} \geq N_{m}$** (since $N_m$ excludes non-functional parts that can theoretically be eliminated):

$$
D_{FA} \geq 1.0 \quad \text{(theoretical minimum)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$
D_{FA} = 1.0 \implies \text{Perfect DFA design (each part takes exactly 3 s)}
$$

$$
D_{FA} > 1.5 \implies \text{Poor design — redesign is mandatory}
$$

$$
D_{FA} < 1.2 \implies \text{Good design — suitable for automation}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering example with numerical evaluation:**

Consider a simple product with 8 total parts ($N_{total} = 8$), of which 5 must move ($N_m = 5$), and individual handling times are: $t_1 = 3, t_2 = 4, t_3 = 2, t_4 = 6, t_5 = 5, t_6 = 8, t_7 = 3, t_8 = 4$ (all in seconds).

The minimum theoretical time:

$$
T_{min} = 3 \cdot N_{m} = 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \text{ s}
$$

The actual assembly time:

$$
T_{actual} = \sum t_{i} = 3 + 4 + 2 + 6 + 5 + 8 + 3 + 4 = 35 \text{ s}
$$

The DFA efficiency index:

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{min}} = \dfrac{35}{15} = 2.33
$$

**Conclusion:** $D_{FA} = 2.33 > 1.5$ indicates **poor DFA performance**. The designer should eliminate 3 of the 8 parts (reducing $N_{total}$ to 5 = $N_m$) to achieve a perfect index of 1.0.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Economic Justification of Automation

For a 7-mark sub-question on "**Justify automation for a given product**", the student must derive the **Return on Capital Investment (ROCI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automation Economics — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the manual assembly cost per unit:**

$$
C_{manual} = t_{manual} \cdot w \cdot (1 + O_{h})
$$

Where:
- $t_{manual}$ = total manual assembly time per unit (seconds)
- $w$ = operator wage rate (₹/second)
- $O_h$ = overhead fraction (typically **1.5–2.0** for Indian SMEs, **2.5–3.0** for global OEMs)

**Step 2 — Define the automated assembly cost per unit:**

$$
C_{auto} = t_{auto} \cdot c_{energy} + \dfrac{C_{capital}}{L \cdot V_{annual}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{auto}$ = automated cycle time per unit (seconds)
- $c_{energy}$ = energy + consumables cost per second (₹/s)
- $C_{capital}$ = total capital cost of the automated station (₹)
- $L$ = equipment life (years, typically **10–15**)
- $V_{annual}$ = annual production volume (units/year)

**Step 3 — Compute the per-unit savings:**

$$
S_{unit} = C_{manual} - C_{auto}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the annual savings:**

$$
S_{annual} = S_{unit} \cdot V_{annual}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the annual operating cost of the automated system** (maintenance, spares, downtime):

$$
C_{operating} = 0.05 \cdot C_{capital} + 0.02 \cdot V_{annual} \cdot c_{consumable}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the net annual cash flow:**

$$
C_{net} = S_{annual} - C_{operating}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the payback period:**

$$
B_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{C_{net}} \quad [\text{years}]
$$

**Step 8 — Decision criterion:**

$$
\text{Automate if } B_{payback} \leq 3 \text{ years AND } S_{annual} > 0
$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Exam-Style Problem:**

Given: Manual time = 90 s, manual wage = ₹0.25/s, overhead = 1.8, automated cycle time = 30 s, energy cost = ₹0.05/s, capital cost = ₹50,00,000, equipment life = 10 years, annual volume = 1,00,000 units, consumable cost = ₹20/unit.

**Step 1 — Manual cost per unit:**

$$
C_{manual} = 90 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (1 + 1.8) = 90 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 2.8 = ₹63.00
$$

**Step 2 — Automated cost per unit (variable portion):**

$$
C_{auto\_variable} = 30 \cdot 0.05 = ₹1.50
$$

**Step 2b — Capital amortization per unit:**

$$
C_{auto\_capital} = \dfrac{50,00,000}{10 \cdot 1,00,000} = ₹5.00
$$

**Step 2c — Total automated cost per unit:**

$$
C_{auto} = 1.50 + 5.00 = ₹6.50
$$

**Step 3 — Per-unit savings:**

$$
S_{unit} = 63.00 - 6.50 = ₹56.50
$$

**Step 4 — Annual savings:**

$$
S_{annual} = 56.50 \cdot 1,00,000 = ₹5,65,00,000
$$

**Step 5 — Operating cost:**

$$
C_{operating} = 0.05 \cdot 50,00,000 + 0.02 \cdot 1,00,000 \cdot 20 = 2,50,000 + 40,000 = ₹2,90,000
$$

**Step 6 — Net cash flow:**

$$
C_{net} = 5,65,00,000 - 2,90,000 = ₹5,62,10,000
$$

**Step 7 — Payback period:**

$$
B_{payback} = \dfrac{50,00,000}{5,62,10,000} = 0.089 \text{ years} \approx 32 \text{ days}
$$

**Conclusion:** Payback of **32 days** is exceptional — automation is **strongly justified**.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Feeder Efficiency Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Vibratory Bowl Feeder Efficiency Derivation:**

Feeder efficiency is the ratio of correctly oriented parts exiting the bowl to the total parts fed per unit time.

**Step 1 — Define the basic efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{feed} = \dfrac{n_{correct}}{n_{total}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of the bowl's geometric design parameters:**

$$
\eta_{feed} = f(S, A, C, T)
$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Symmetry Number** (number of indistinguishable orientations, e.g., 4 for a square, 2 for a rectangle, 1 for a triangle)
- $A$ = **Asymmetric features** present (1 if yes, 0 if no)
- $C$ = **Chamfer presence** (1 if yes, 0 if no)
- $T$ = **Tangling features** (1 if absent, 0 if present)

**Step 3 — Empirical relationship (from industrial studies):**

$$
\eta_{feed} \approx \dfrac{A \cdot C \cdot T}{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with examples:**

A square part (S = 4) with no asymmetric feature, no chamfer, no tangling:

$$
\eta_{feed} = \dfrac{0 \cdot 0 \cdot 1}{4} = 0\% \quad \text{(catastrophic — must redesign)}
$$

Same part with one asymmetric notch (A = 1), chamfer (C = 1), no tangling (T = 1):

$$
\eta_{feed} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{4} = 25\% \quad \text{(improved but still poor)}
$$

A triangle (S = 1) with chamfer, no tangling, no asymmetric notch needed:

$$
\eta_{feed} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{1} = 100\% \quad \text{(ideal)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **symmetry number** is the **most powerful** determinant of feeder efficiency. A triangular (3-fold symmetric but non-overlapping) part has 100% self-orientation, while a square part requires deliberate asymmetric features.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define Design for Automation. List any four general rules for designing products suitable for automated assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Design for Automation (DFA) is a systematic design approach that ensures a product's components can be manufactured, fed, oriented, handled, assembled, and inspected by automated equipment and robotics with minimal human intervention, achieving high reliability, repeatability, and cost efficiency.

**Any Four Rules (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Minimize the number of parts** — Eliminate non-functional parts that do not move and are not required for disassembly.
2. **Design for single direction of assembly (Z-axis rule)** — All components should mate along one preferred axis to simplify robotic motion planning.
3. **Use symmetric or deliberately polarized features** — Either eliminate orientation ambiguity entirely, or use a unique keying feature to force correct orientation.
4. **Avoid separate fasteners** — Replace bolts, nuts, and screws with snap-fits, press-fits, or self-clinching features that are automation-friendly.

*(Any other valid rule from the 15 universal rules is also acceptable.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Explain the Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design-for-Assembly efficiency index with its formula. State the ideal value and the threshold for redesign.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} t_{i} \cdot N_{i}}{3 \cdot N_{m}}
$$

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The DFA index is the ratio of the **actual assembly time** to the **minimum theoretical assembly time**. The minimum theoretical time is taken as **3 seconds per part** that must move, based on empirical Boothroyd-Dewhurst industrial studies.

**Threshold Values (0.5 mark):**
- **Ideal value:** $D_{FA} = 1.0$ (perfect design)
- **Threshold for redesign:** $D_{FA} > 1.5$ (poor design requiring immediate intervention)
- **Acceptable range:** $D_{FA} < 1.2$ (good, automation-ready design)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain **vibratory bowl feeder** and discuss **five design features** that a part must possess to be suitable for bulk feeding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product has **10 parts** in its Bill of Materials. The Boothroyd handling times (in seconds) for each part are: 4, 6, 3, 8, 5, 7, 4, 9, 6, 5. The number of parts that **must move** during assembly is **6**. Compute the **DFA index** and **comment on the suitability for automation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Vibratory Bowl Feeder Diagram & Explanation (3 marks):**

A vibratory bowl feeder consists of a **bowl-shaped hopper** mounted on a base supported by **leaf springs**. An **electromagnetic vibrator** drives the bowl at a typical frequency of **50–100 Hz** with an amplitude of **0.5–2.0 mm**. Parts are fed from the bowl's outer rim upward along a helical track, where they pass over **selective orientation features** (notches, blades, wiper gates) that remove incorrectly oriented parts. Correctly oriented parts exit at the bottom through a discharge chute.

**Five Design Features (4 marks — 0.8 each):**

1. **Asymmetric or Polarized Geometry** — The part should have a unique orientation (or a deliberately added notch/tab) so that only one orientation is geometrically valid.

2. **No Tangling or Nesting Features** — Avoid flexible sheets, helical springs in free state, threaded shafts, and curled chips that can interlock with identical parts.

3. **Rigid Body** — The part should be made of a rigid material (metal, hard plastic) so that it does not deform during impact and orient predictably.

4. **Smooth, Non-Tacky Surfaces** — Surfaces should be free of oil films, burrs, or adhesives that could cause two parts to stick together.

5. **Adequate Mass and Size** — The part should be between **5 g and 500 g**, and between **5 mm and 100 mm** in maximum dimension, to allow the electromagnetic drive to move it efficiently.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $N_{total} = 10$, $N_{m} = 6$, $t_i = \{4, 6, 3, 8, 5, 7, 4, 9, 6, 5\}$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute the actual assembly time:**

$$
T_{actual} = 4 + 6 + 3 + 8 + 5 + 7 + 4 + 9 + 6 + 5 = 57 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the minimum theoretical assembly time:**

$$
T_{min} = 3 \cdot N_{m} = 3 \cdot 6 = 18 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the DFA index:**

$$
D_{FA} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{min}} = \dfrac{57}{18} = 3.17
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$
D_{FA} = 3.17 \gg 1.5
$$

This index is **3.17 times** the theoretical minimum and is **more than double** the redesign threshold of 1.5. The product is **NOT suitable for automation in its current form**.

**Step 5 — Recommended Redesign Actions (carry forward):**

- Eliminate 4 non-functional parts (reduce $N_{total}$ to 6 = $N_m$).
- Replace 2 high-time parts (8 s and 9 s) with snap-fit equivalents (reducing $t$ by ~50%).
- **New DFA after redesign:** Estimated $D_{FA}^{new} \approx 1.05$ — automation-ready.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] — QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** List and explain **the "Six Golden Rules" for component design** to facilitate automated assembly. Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manual assembly operation takes **120 seconds** per unit with a wage rate of **₹0.30 per second** and an overhead factor of **2.0**. The proposed automated station has a **cycle time of 35 seconds**, an energy cost of **₹0.08 per second**, and a total capital cost of **₹80 lakhs**. The annual production volume is **2,00,000 units**, and the equipment has a **service life of 10 years**. Determine the **per-unit cost savings**, **annual savings**, and the **payback period**. Recommend whether automation should be adopted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**The Six Golden Rules (4.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Design for Z-axis assembly** — All components should mate vertically, simplifying robot motion.
2. **Minimize part count** — Apply Boothroyd's "must move" test to eliminate non-functional parts.
3. **Use asymmetric or strongly polarized features** — Prevents upside-down or reversed insertion.
4. **Avoid separate fasteners** — Replace bolts and screws with snap-fits and self-clinching features.
5. **Provide self-aligning chamfers** — Lead-in tapers correct positional tolerances by 80%.
6. **Standardize across the product family** — Reduces the variety of feeder stations required.

**Illustration with Example (2.5 marks):**

Consider a **door hinge assembly**:
- **Original design:** 5 parts (two leaves, one pin, two retaining rings) requiring 4 assembly directions and 2 fasteners. Cycle time: 45 s.
- **Redesigned for automation:** 3 parts (two leaves with integral snap-pin, no rings) requiring 1 assembly direction and no fasteners. Cycle time: 12 s.
- **Improvement:** 73% reduction in cycle time, full automation-readiness.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Manual cost per unit:**

$$
C_{manual} = 120 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.0 = ₹72.00
$$

**Step 2 — Automated cost per unit (variable):**

$$
C_{auto\_variable} = 35 \cdot 0.08 = ₹2.80
$$

**Step 3 — Capital amortization per unit:**

$$
C_{auto\_capital} = \dfrac{80,00,000}{10 \cdot 2,00,000} = ₹4.00
$$

**Step 4 — Total automated cost per unit:**

$$
C_{auto} = 2.80 + 4.00 = ₹6.80
$$

**Step 5 — Per-unit savings:**

$$
S_{unit} = 72.00 - 6.80 = ₹65.20
$$

**Step 6 — Annual savings:**

$$
S_{annual} = 65.20 \cdot 2,00,000 = ₹1,30,40,00,000 \quad (\text{₹13.04 crores})
$$

**Step 7 — Annual operating cost (5% of capital):**

$$
C_{operating} = 0.05 \cdot 80,00,000 = ₹4,00,000
$$

**Step 8 — Net annual cash flow:**

$$
C_{net} = 13,04,00,000 - 4,00,000 = ₹13,00,00,000
$$

**Step 9 — Payback period:**

$$
B_{payback} = \dfrac{80,00,000}{13,00,00,000} = 0.0615 \text{ years} \approx 22.5 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 10 — Recommendation:**

Since the payback period (**22.5 days**) is **far below the 3-year threshold** and the annual savings (**₹13.04 crores**) substantially exceed operating costs, **automation is strongly recommended** for this product.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**

1. **Forgetting the "Must-Move" Definition** — In DFA calculations, students often use the **total** part count $N_{total}$ instead of the **functional** part count $N_m$. This is a **3-mark deduction** in a 7-mark sub-question.

2. **Confusing DFA with DFM** — Students sometimes write manufacturing rules (tolerances, surface finish) instead of automation rules (Z-axis, poka-yoke). Always re-read the question stem — it asks specifically for **automation** rules.

3. **Skipping the Justification Step** — When asked to "**recommend**" automation, students often stop at the calculation. The conclusion paragraph (recommend/don't recommend with payback reasoning) carries **1–2 marks**.

4. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing seconds and minutes, or ₹/hour and ₹/second, is a **1-mark deduction** per occurrence. Always convert to a single unit system.

5. **Oversimplifying Symmetry** — Students often state "use symmetric parts" without distinguishing between **rotational symmetry** (problematic) and **bilateral asymmetry** (ideal for orientation).

6. **Ignoring the CapEx Amortization** — In economic problems, the annual capital cost ($C_{capital}/L$) is frequently omitted, leading to overestimation of savings.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

#### **The 15 Universal Rules (One-Line Recall):**
- **R1** — Z-axis single direction
- **R2** — Minimize part count (Boothroyd "must move" test)
- **R3** — Asymmetry or strong polarization
- **R4** — Eliminate tangling/nesting
- **R5** — Self-aligning chamfers and lead-ins
- **R6** — Avoid separate fasteners
- **R7** — Standardize across product family
- **R8** — Use rigid components
- **R9** — Top-down gravity-assisted assembly
- **R10** — Adequate gripper clearances (≥10 mm)
- **R11** — Poka-yoke mistake-proofing
- **R12** — Efficient feeding (bulk vs. magazine)
- **R13** — Vision-system compatible surfaces
- **R14** — Modular sub-assemblies
- **R15** — Self-fastening (snap/press/heat-stake)

#### **Critical Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize:**
- **3 seconds/part** — Boothroyd minimum theoretical time
- **95%** — Industrial feeder efficiency target
- **1.0** — Ideal DFA index
- **1.5** — DFA redesign threshold
- **3 years** — Maximum acceptable payback period
- **20%** — Minimum ROCI for automation projects
- **15 mm** — Minimum gripper finger clearance
- **0.5 mm × 45°** — Standard chamfer for self-alignment

#### **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- $D_{FA} = T_{actual} / (3 \cdot N_m)$
- $C_{auto} = C_{capital}/(L \cdot V) + c_{energy} \cdot t_{auto}$
- $B_{payback} = C_{capital} / C_{net}$
- $\eta_{feed} = A \cdot C \cdot T / S$

#### **KTU Exam Tip (Last-Minute Strategy):**
- For **3-mark questions** — Define + 4 bullet points (no derivations).
- For **7-mark sub-questions** — Always include: **(i) Formula, (ii) Numerical substitution, (iii) Result, (iv) Interpretation/Justification**.
- For **14-mark questions** — Divide answer into **2 clear sub-parts** (a) and (b), each ~7 marks. Use **diagrams/sketches** wherever possible; KTU awards **1–2 marks** for neat, labeled figures.
- Always **state the rule number AND its principle** when applying (e.g., "Applying Rule 11 (Poka-Yoke): a polarizing key is added…"). Examiners reward **terminology precision**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Fasteners: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design for Fasteners: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, a **fastener** is defined as a discrete, standardised hardware component whose primary engineering function is to mechanically join, affix, or secure two or more parts together in a semi-permanent or permanent manner, either by generating a clamping force (friction-based holding) or by forming a direct geometric interlock (form-closure or force-closure).

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET522 – Module 2), the study of *Design for Fasteners* falls under the broader umbrella of **Design for Assembly (DFA)**. It deals with the rational selection, standardisation, and design integration of fastening systems to minimise the total part count, reduce assembly time, lower assembly cost, and ensure reliable service performance of the product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET522 – Module 2):**
> The scope of "Design for Fasteners" includes understanding the *types* of fasteners, their *standardisation systems*, *selection criteria*, *economic impact on assembly cost*, and the *DFA Boothroyd–Dewhurst principles* applied to fastening operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human skeleton. The **bones** are analogous to the individual parts of a product (sheet metal panels, brackets, casings, gears), and the **joints** — knees, elbows, shoulders, skull sutures — are the **fasteners**. Without these joints, the skeleton is just a useless pile of bones. The strength, flexibility, range of motion, and durability of the body depend heavily on the type of joint used (hinge joint vs. ball-and-socket).

Similarly, in a machine:
- A **bolt and nut** is like a *hinge joint* — strong, reliable, and detachable for service.
- A **rivet** is like a *fused skull suture* — permanent and lightweight.
- A **snap-fit clip** is like a *ball-and-socket joint* — quick to engage/disengage without tools.

The designer's job is to choose the *right "joint"* for the *right purpose*, balancing **strength**, **cost**, **serviceability**, and **assembly speed**.

## 1.3 Why Fasteners Matter in DFMA

In real industrial practice, fastening operations typically account for:
- **20 % to 30 % of the total assembly labour cost** in sheet-metal-dominated products (e.g., automotive bodies, white goods, electronic enclosures).
- A significant portion of the **total part count** (Boothroyd's studies showed that fasteners + standard purchased parts can constitute 60–80 % of all parts in an assembly).
- A critical *reliability* node — the fastener is often the *weakest link* in vibration, fatigue, and thermal-cycling environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> *“A fastener is a hardware device that mechanically joins two or more components, the design of which directly influences the assembly time, disassembly capability, and overall cost of the product.”*

The *Design for Fasteners* philosophy pushes the designer to:
1. **Eliminate** fasteners wherever possible (snap-fits, integral hinges, welding, adhesives).
2. **Standardise** the type and size of fasteners used (one bolt size across the product).
3. **Minimise** fastener handling and orientation issues (self-locating, self-aligning designs).
4. **Ensure accessibility** for installation tools and service operations.
5. **Reduce variety** — fewer SKUs = lower inventory, tooling, and training cost.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fasteners

Fasteners can be classified along multiple engineering axes. A clear classification is essential for KTU board answers.

### 2.1.1 By Joint Permanence

| Class | Type | Examples | Characteristics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Permanent** | Non-separable | Rivets, welds, adhesives, crimped joints | Cannot be disassembled without destruction |
| **Semi-permanent** | Limited reuse | Locknuts, self-tapping screws, key fasteners | Can be disassembled N times with effort |
| **Temporary** | Fully separable | Bolts, cap screws, studs, retaining rings | Unlimited disassembly, reusable |

### 2.1.2 By Force/Movement Type

- **Threaded fasteners** (force-closure via friction): bolts, cap screws, set screws, studs, machine screws.
- **Non-threaded mechanical fasteners** (form-closure or interference): pins, rivets, keys, circlips, snap rings.
- **Quick-release fasteners** (snap-fits, quarter-turn fasteners, clips, latches).

### 2.1.3 By Drive Type (Boothroyd Classification)

| Drive Type | Tool Required | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slotted | Flat-blade screwdriver | Low-torque, infrequent service |
| Phillips / Pozidriv | Cross-head screwdriver | Higher torque transfer |
| Hex (internal/external) | Wrench / socket | High torque, industrial |
| Torx (star) | Torx driver | Automotive, high torque |
| Robertson (square) | Square driver | Furniture, automotive |

## 2.2 The DFA Cost Penalty of Fasteners

According to **Boothroyd & Dewhurst's DFA methodology (used widely in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2)**, the inclusion of a fastener in a design adds to the assembly cost through three contribution categories:

$$
C_{\text{fastener}} = C_{\text{part}} + C_{\text{handling}} + C_{\text{insertion}} + C_{\text{extra-operations}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{part}}$ = cost of the fastener itself.
- $C_{\text{handling}}$ = cost of orienting and presenting the fastener to the assembly point.
- $C_{\text{insertion}}$ = cost of the actual joining operation (tightening, riveting).
- $C_{\text{extra-operations}}$ = cost of washers, lock-nuts, thread-locking fluid, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boothroyd's Empirical Rule:** A fastener + mating feature typically adds **2 to 3 times the cost of the fastener itself** to the total assembly cost. Therefore, *avoiding a single fastener by redesign* can save 3× the fastener cost.

## 2.3 Standardisation of Threaded Fasteners

For KTU, students must know the major international thread standards:

| Standard | Region | Common Designation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ISO metric** (ISO 261, 262) | Global | M8 × 1.25 |
| **BSP** (British Standard Pipe) | UK, Commonwealth | 1/2" BSP |
| **UNC / UNF** (Unified) | USA, Canada | 1/4"-20 UNC |
| **BSW / BSF** (British Standard Whitworth/Fine) | Legacy UK | 1/2" BSW |
| **BA** (British Association) | Instruments, watches | 2 BA |

The KTU syllabus emphasises that *metric ISO threads* are the default in most contemporary KTU exam problems. Standardisation reduces:
- Tooling variety.
- Training cost.
- Inventory and supply-chain complexity.
- Quality-control burden.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Below is the consolidated formula table for fastener design frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE:

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$ | Tightening torque | $T$ = torque (N·m), $K$ = nut factor (≈ **0.2** steel-on-steel), $F$ = preload (N), $d$ = nominal bolt dia. (m) |
| 2 | $F_i = \dfrac{T}{K \cdot d}$ | Preload from torque | Inverse of (1) |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{1.3 \cdot F}{A_s}$ | Tensile stress in bolt (incl. torsion) | $\sigma_t$ in MPa, $A_s$ = stress area (mm²) |
| 4 | $A_s \approx 0.78 \cdot d^2 \text{ to } 0.85 \cdot d^2$ | Stress area (ISO metric) | For coarse threads |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{J / r}$ | Shear stress in shaft under torsion | $J$ = polar moment, $r$ = outer radius |
| 6 | $F_{s} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot A_s}{\text{FoS}}$ | Safe design preload | $\sigma_y$ = yield strength, FoS = **1.5 to 2.5** |
| 7 | $F_{\text{clamp}} = F_i - F_{\text{ext}} \cdot \dfrac{k_b}{k_b + k_c}$ | Residual clamping force | $k_b$ = bolt stiffness, $k_c$ = joint stiffness |
| 8 | $\text{Preload utilisation} = \dfrac{F_i}{F_{s}}$ | Tightening factor check | Should be < 0.9 for reusability |
| 9 | $N_{\text{free}} = \sigma_t \cdot A_s$ | Proof load / max safe tension | Verified against ISO 898-1 property class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In tables, the absolute value notation is written using `\vert` or `\mid`, never the bare pipe symbol `\vert x \vert`, because the vertical bar `$\vert$` collides with markdown's table-cell delimiter. Examiners may deduct marks for *visual ambiguity* in scanned answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design-for-fastener principles govern every mass-produced mechanical assembly:

- **Automotive industry:** A single car contains **~2,500–3,500 threaded fasteners**. Toyota's lean DFA programme famously reduced 60 % of fasteners in the *Aisin fire* recall remediation by welding subassemblies.
- **Aerospace:** Fasteners (especially HiLok, Hi-Tite, lockbolts) account for **~20 %** of assembly cost in airframes like the Boeing 787. Composite airframes drive use of titanium blind fasteners to avoid galvanic corrosion.
- **Consumer electronics:** Apple’s iPhone assembly famously **eliminated visible external fasteners** through snap-fits and adhesive bonding — a direct application of DFA fastener reduction.
- **White goods (washing machines, refrigerators):** Welded + bolted hybrid joints dominate; standardisation to a *single bolt size* across the entire product line is a DFA benchmark.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bolt Tightening Torque (Kern's Equation)

This is the most frequently asked *Part B* derivation in KTU ESE papers for PEMET522. We derive the relationship between the applied tightening torque $T$ and the resulting preload $F_i$ in a bolted joint.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a bolt–nut assembly being tightened. Two resistive friction torques must be overcome:

1. **Thread friction torque** ($T_t$) — between the bolt threads and the nut threads.
2. **Bearing friction torque** ($T_b$) — between the nut face (or bolt head) and the clamped surface.

Let:
- $F$ = axial clamping (preload) force (N)
- $d$ = nominal major diameter of bolt (m)
- $d_m$ = mean (pitch) diameter of thread (m)
- $\alpha$ = thread half-angle (for ISO metric V-thread, $\alpha = 30°$)
- $\lambda$ = helix (lead) angle of the thread
- $\rho'$ = friction angle in the thread ($\tan \rho' = \mu_t$)
- $\mu_b$ = coefficient of friction under the nut/head bearing face
- $D_o$ = outer diameter of the bearing face
- $d_h$ = hole diameter (inner bearing diameter)
- $D_e$ = equivalent mean diameter of the bearing face

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Thread Friction Torque

The torque required to lift the load along the thread helix, while overcoming thread friction, is:

$$
T_t = F \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \rho')
$$

The helix angle $\lambda$ for a single-start thread is:

$$
\tan \lambda = \frac{L}{\pi \cdot d_m}
$$

where $L$ is the lead (= pitch for single-start threads). This is small for typical fasteners ($\lambda \approx 2°\text{ to }4°$), and for tightening we add the friction angle (positive sign because we are *ascending* the helix against friction).

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Bearing Friction Torque

The friction under the nut face is distributed over an annular ring of inner radius $d_h/2$ and outer radius $D_o/2$. Integrating the elemental friction torque over the ring yields the equivalent torque arm as the **mean radius**:

$$
D_e = \frac{D_o + d_h}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_b = F \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{D_e}{2} = F \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{D_o + d_h}{4}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Total Torque

The total applied torque equals the sum of the two resistive torques:

$$
T = T_t + T_b = F \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \rho') + F \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{D_o + d_h}{4}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Grouping into a Single Nut Factor $K$

Factorising $F \cdot d / 2$ out (since $d \approx d_m$ for coarse estimation), we obtain the well-known **Kern's nut factor form**:

$$
T = K \cdot F \cdot d
$$

where the dimensionless nut factor $K$ is:

$$
K = \frac{d_m}{2d} \tan(\lambda + \rho') + \frac{\mu_b}{2} \cdot \frac{D_o + d_h}{d}
$$

For typical ISO M-steel-on-steel fasteners with standard hexagonal nuts, $K \approx$ **0.18 to 0.22**, and is conventionally taken as **0.20** for hand calculation.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Inverting for Preload

For design purposes, we need the preload produced by a given torque. Rearranging:

$$
F_i = \frac{T}{K \cdot d}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Stress in the Bolt During Tightening

Because the bolt is simultaneously under tension and torsion, the **von-Mises equivalent stress** is computed. The combined effect is approximated by multiplying the axial tensile stress by a factor of **1.3** (a legacy factor from "Mises-equivalent" conversion of pure shear into equivalent tension in cylindrical sections):

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{1.3 \cdot F_i}{A_s}
$$

where the **stress area** $A_s$ for an ISO metric coarse thread is:

$$
A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{d_2 + d_3}{2} \right)^2
$$

with $d_2$ = pitch diameter, $d_3$ = minor diameter root. A convenient engineering approximation is:

$$
A_s \approx 0.78 \, d^2 \quad \text{(to a slightly more accurate } 0.85 d^2 \text{ for coarse threads)}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Safe Preload Criterion

To avoid yielding during tightening:

$$
F_i \leq \frac{\sigma_y \cdot A_s}{1.3 \cdot \text{FoS}}
$$

where a factor of safety (FoS) of **1.5 to 2.5** is recommended (KTU typical value **2.0** for static, non-critical joints).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A steel bolt of M12 × 1.75 grade 8.8 is to be tightened on a steel joint. Compute the tightening torque required to develop a preload of $F_i = 25\text{ kN}$. Assume nut factor $K = 0.20$. Also verify that the bolt stress is within the elastic limit. Take $\sigma_y = 640$ MPa for grade 8.8.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Identify Variables

$$
d = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}, \quad F_i = 25{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad K = 0.20, \quad \sigma_y = 640 \text{ MPa}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Compute Torque

$$
T = K \cdot F_i \cdot d = 0.20 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.012
$$

$$
T = 0.20 \times 300 = 60.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compute Stress Area (Approximate)

$$
A_s \approx 0.78 \cdot d^2 = 0.78 \times 12^2 = 0.78 \times 144 = 112.32 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Compute Tensile Stress (With Torsion Factor 1.3)

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{1.3 \cdot F_i}{A_s} = \frac{1.3 \times 25{,}000}{112.32}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{32{,}500}{112.32} \approx 289.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Compare With Yield Strength

Since $\sigma_t = 289.4 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_y = 640 \text{ MPa}$, the bolt remains elastic. The factor of safety is:

$$
\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_t} = \frac{640}{289.4} \approx 2.21
$$

This satisfies the KTU-recommended range of **1.5 to 2.5** for static joints. **Design is safe.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DFA Decision for Fastener Selection

| Design Question | KTU 2024 Decision Heuristic |
| :--- | :--- |
| Will the joint ever be serviced? | **Yes →** Use *temporary* (bolt, screw). **No →** Use *permanent* (rivet, weld). |
| Is the joint under high vibration? | Use *locknuts*, *thread-locking fluid*, *preload* ≥ 75 % of yield. |
| Is weight critical (aerospace)? | Prefer *blind rivets*, *HiLok*, or *flush-head screws*. |
| Is cost critical (consumer goods)? | Standardise on *one bolt size*; use *self-tapping screws* in plastics. |
| Is automated assembly used? | Choose fasteners that are *self-locating* and *drop-in-place*; avoid orientation-sensitive drives. |
| Will thermal cycling occur? | Match bolt/joint *CTE* (coefficient of thermal expansion); consider *flexlock* washers. |
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Classification Architecture of Fasteners

The following block-level functional diagram maps the engineering taxonomy of fastener types, which is a high-yield KTU board answer for "Classify fasteners" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootA[Fastener Hardware System]:::root --> Perma[Permanence Criterion]
    RootA --> ForceT[Force or Movement Criterion]
    RootA --> DriveT[Drive Type Criterion]
    RootA --> StdT[Standardisation Criterion]

    Perma --> Perm1[Permanent]
    Perma --> Perm2[Semi-Permanent]
    Perma --> Perm3[Temporary Reusable]

    Perm1 --> Perm1a[Rivets]
    Perm1 --> Perm1b[Welded Joints]
    Perm1 --> Perm1c[Adhesive Bonds]
    Perm1 --> Perm1d[Crimped Joints]

    Perm2 --> Perm2a[Lock Nuts]
    Perm2 --> Perm2b[Self Tapping Screws]
    Perm2 --> Perm2c[Key and Keyway]

    Perm3 --> Perm3a[Hex Bolts]
    Perm3 --> Perm3b[Cap Screws]
    Perm3 --> Perm3c[Studs]
    Perm3 --> Perm3d[Machine Screws]
    Perm3 --> Perm3e[Retaining Rings]

    ForceT --> For1[Threaded Friction Based]
    ForceT --> For2[Non Threaded Form Closure]
    ForceT --> For3[Quick Release]

    For1 --> For1a[Bolts and Screws]
    For1 --> For1b[Studs]
    For2 --> For2a[Pins]
    For2 --> For2b[Rivets]
    For2 --> For2c[Keys]
    For3 --> For3a[Snap Fits]
    For3 --> For3b[Quarter Turn Fasteners]
    For3 --> For3c[Clips and Latches]

    DriveT --> Dr1[Slotted]
    DriveT --> Dr2[Phillips or Pozidriv]
    DriveT --> Dr3[Hex Internal]
    DriveT --> Dr4[Hex External]
    DriveT --> Dr5[Torx Star]
    DriveT --> Dr6[Robertson Square]

    StdT --> St1[ISO Metric M Series]
    StdT --> St2[Unified UNC and UNF]
    StdT --> St3[BSW and BSF]
    StdT --> St4[BSP Pipe Threads]
    StdT --> St5[BA Instrument Threads]

    classDef root fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Bolted Joint Load Path

The figure below maps the engineering *load-transfer mechanism* inside a typical bolted flange joint. This is a *block-level functional schematic* showing how external force distributes across bolt and clamped members.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtLoad[External Tensile Load Fext]:::root --> JointBlock[Bolted Joint System]:::sub

    JointBlock --> StiffSplit[Stiffness Splitting]:::sub
    StiffSplit --> BoltBranch[Bolt Stiffness kb]:::sub
    StiffSplit --> ClampBranch[Clamped Member Stiffness kc]:::sub

    BoltBranch --> BoltStress[Bolt Tensile Stress sigma_t]:::leaf
    BoltBranch --> BoltTorsion[Combined Torsion From Tightening]:::leaf
    BoltStress --> BoltEquiv[Von Mises Equivalent Stress]:::leaf
    BoltTorsion --> BoltEquiv

    ClampBranch --> ClampForce[Residual Clamping Force Fclamp]:::leaf
    ClampBranch --> JointSep[Prevention of Joint Separation]:::leaf

    BoltEquiv --> CheckYield{Yield Check}:::root
    JointSep --> CheckSep{Separation Check}:::root
    ClampForce --> CheckSlip{Slip Check}:::root

    CheckYield --> AcceptYield[Factor of Safety Above 1.5]:::leaf
    CheckSep --> AcceptSep[No Gap Forms Under Load]:::leaf
    CheckSlip --> AcceptSlip[Friction Capacity Exceeds Shear]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — DFA Decision Flow for Fastener Selection

The following schematic is a *block-level functional architecture flow* showing how a designer progresses from a *joining requirement* to a *final fastener specification* under DFA principles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA[Joining Requirement Identified]:::root --> Q1{Is Disassembly Needed}:::root
    Q1 -->|Yes| Temp[Temporary Fastener Zone]:::sub
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Loading Severity}:::root

    Q2 -->|Low| Perm[Permanent Zone]:::sub
    Q2 -->|High| Sem[Semi Permanent Zone]:::sub

    Temp --> Std[Standardise Type]:::sub
    Std --> Acce{Is Tool Access Easy}:::root
    Acce -->|Yes| Drop[Drop in Self Locating Bolt]:::leaf
    Acce -->|No| Redesign[Redesign for Access]:::leaf

    Perm --> Rivet[Use Rivet or Weld]:::leaf
    Perm --> Adhesive[Adhesive Bond Option]:::leaf

    Sem --> Locknut[Lock Nut or Prevailing Torque]:::leaf
    Sem --> Key[Key and Keyway]:::leaf

    Redesign --> FinalCheck[Final DFA Audit]:::root
    Drop --> FinalCheck
    Rivet --> FinalCheck
    Adhesive --> FinalCheck
    Locknut --> FinalCheck
    Key --> FinalCheck

    FinalCheck --> Out1[Part Count Reduced]:::leaf
    FinalCheck --> Out2[Assembly Time Reduced]:::leaf
    FinalCheck --> Out3[Cost Reduced]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
