# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Define a fastener. With examples, classify fasteners based on the permanence of the joint.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *fastener* is a discrete hardware device used to mechanically join two or more parts of an assembly, typically by generating a clamping force (friction) or by geometric interlock (1 mark).

Based on joint permanence, fasteners are classified into three types (1 mark):
- **Permanent fasteners** — Rivets, welds, adhesive bonds, crimped joints. These cannot be separated without destruction.
- **Semi-permanent fasteners** — Lock nuts, self-tapping screws, keys. Limited reuse is possible with effort.
- **Temporary (reusable) fasteners** — Hex bolts, cap screws, studs, machine screws, retaining rings. Unlimited disassembly and reassembly (1 mark).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> **State the importance of standardisation in fastener design. Mention any two commonly used thread standards.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Importance of standardisation* (2 marks):
- Reduces tooling variety and inventory cost.
- Simplifies procurement, training, and quality control.
- Enables interchangeability of parts across product lines.
- Lowers assembly time by reducing fastener-handling complexity.

*Two common thread standards* (1 mark):
- **ISO Metric (M-series)** — e.g., M10 × 1.5 — used globally and is the KTU-recommended default.
- **Unified National Coarse / Fine (UNC / UNF)** — e.g., 1/4"-20 UNC — prevalent in North American industry.
- (Alternatives: BSW, BSF, BA, BSP.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A) — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Classify the various types of fasteners used in mechanical assemblies, giving at least two examples under each category. Briefly explain the criteria for selecting a fastener type for a given application. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, derive the relationship between the tightening torque $T$ and the resulting preload $F_i$ in a bolted joint. State the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification of fasteners** (4 marks):

Fasteners can be classified into the following major categories:

**(i) Threaded fasteners (friction-type):**
- Bolts (hex, carriage, eye, U-bolts)
- Cap screws (socket head, button head, flat head)
- Set screws
- Studs
- Machine screws and self-tapping screws

**(ii) Non-threaded mechanical fasteners (form-closure):**
- Pins (dowel, taper, cotter, clevis)
- Rivets (solid, blind, tubular, pop)
- Keys (parallel, taper, Woodruff)
- Retaining rings (external/internal circlips)

**(iii) Quick-release fasteners:**
- Snap-fits, quarter-turn fasteners, clips, latches

**2. Selection criteria** (3 marks):

- **Service requirement:** Disassembly needed → temporary; not needed → permanent.
- **Loading severity:** Static, dynamic, fatigue, vibration.
- **Material and environment:** Corrosion, temperature, galvanic compatibility.
- **Manufacturability and assembly method:** Manual vs. automated; tool access.
- **Cost and standardisation:** Prefer standard ISO sizes; minimise variety.
- **Weight and space constraints:** Aerospace favours blind rivets and flush-head screws.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Sketch** (2 marks):
A labelled diagram showing a bolt–nut assembly on two clamped plates, with annotations indicating:
- Applied torque $T$
- Preload $F_i$
- Mean thread diameter $d_m$
- Bearing diameter $D_o$ and hole diameter $d_h$
- Friction angles $\rho'$ (thread) and the bearing surface.

**2. Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Bolted joint is symmetric; load is purely axial.
- Thread friction is uniform along the engaged length.
- Bearing pressure under the nut face is uniform.
- The bolt is single-start (lead = pitch).

**3. Derivation** (4 marks):

Thread friction torque:

$$
T_t = F_i \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \rho')
$$

Bearing friction torque:

$$
T_b = F_i \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{D_o + d_h}{4}
$$

Total torque:

$$
T = T_t + T_b = K \cdot F_i \cdot d
$$

where the nut factor $K$ combines both friction effects and is empirically **0.18 to 0.22**, conventionally **0.20** for steel-on-steel.

---

### Question B1 (Option B) — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain Boothroyd & Dewhurst's Design-for-Assembly principles related to the use of fasteners in a product. Discuss the cost impact of fasteners with an example. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An M12 × 1.75 grade 8.8 bolt is used in a steel joint. Calculate the tightening torque required to produce a preload of 30 kN. Take the nut factor $K = 0.20$. Verify the induced tensile stress in the bolt is within safe limits. Given: $\sigma_y = 640$ MPa. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA principles on fasteners** (4 marks):

- **Principle of minimisation:** Eliminate fasteners wherever possible by using integral features, snap-fits, or welding.
- **Principle of standardisation:** Use the *fewest types* of fasteners and the *fewest sizes*; ideally one bolt size across the product.
- **Principle of handling ease:** Select fasteners that are *self-locating*, *non-symmetrical* (so they cannot be inserted wrongly), and *drop-in-place*.
- **Principle of accessibility:** Ensure the tool can reach the fastener head/nut without disassembly of other parts.
- **Principle of one-motion assembly:** A fastener should ideally be installed in *one straight-line motion* with no reorientation.
- **Principle of separation during assembly:** Components should not have to be held simultaneously while fastening.

**2. Cost impact** (3 marks):

According to Boothroyd, the *true cost* of a fastener is not just its purchase price. The total cost includes:
- Cost of the fastener itself.
- Cost of handling and orienting the fastener.
- Cost of the joining operation (tightening time × labour rate).
- Cost of mating features (tapped hole, clearance hole, counterbore).
- Cost of any secondary operations (washer, locking device).

**Example:** A ₹2 hex bolt, after including handling, tightening (8 s × ₹0.5/s = ₹4), and mating hole preparation, costs approximately ₹10–12 *per use* in a labour-intensive assembly. If 200 such bolts are used in a product, the fastener-driven cost is ₹2,000–2,400 — a significant fraction of the product cost.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Given data** (1 mark):

$$
d = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}, \quad F_i = 30{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad K = 0.20, \quad \sigma_y = 640 \text{ MPa}
$$

**2. Tightening torque** (2 marks):

$$
T = K \cdot F_i \cdot d = 0.20 \times 30{,}000 \times 0.012 = 0.20 \times 360 = 72 \text{ N·m}
$$

**3. Stress area** (1 mark):

$$
A_s \approx 0.78 \times 12^2 = 0.78 \times 144 = 112.32 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**4. Tensile stress (with torsion factor)** (2 marks):

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{1.3 \times F_i}{A_s} = \frac{1.3 \times 30{,}000}{112.32} = \frac{39{,}000}{112.32} \approx 347.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**5. Verification** (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_t = 347.2 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_y = 640 \text{ MPa}
$$

Factor of safety:

$$
\text{FoS} = \frac{640}{347.2} \approx 1.84
$$

This is within the KTU-recommended range of **1.5 to 2.5** for static joints. **The bolt design is safe.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.3 torsion factor** in the bolt stress calculation is the *single most common mistake* costing **2 to 3 marks** per paper. Without it, $\sigma_t$ will be artificially low and your safety claim is invalid.
> 2. **Using major diameter $d$ instead of stress area $A_s$** when computing stress. The stress area accounts for the *thread root* (stress concentration) and is mandatory. Using $A = \pi d^2/4$ will overstate capacity and is a serious error.
> 3. **Mixing units** (mm vs. m, N vs. kN). Torque in N·m requires $d$ in metres and $F_i$ in newtons.
> 4. **Ignoring the nut factor $K$** or hard-coding $K = 1$ will yield an absurdly low torque and is treated as a conceptual error.
> 5. **Failing to state assumptions** before a derivation: KTU examiners award a dedicated 1-mark band for clearly written assumptions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — *Design for Fasteners: Introduction* (PEMET522 – Module 2)**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] A **fastener** is a discrete hardware device that mechanically joins two or more parts.
- [ ] DFMA = Design for Manufacture **and** Assembly — fastener design is a *DFA* subdomain.
- [ ] Boothroyd's theorem: A fastener's *true* cost = part cost + handling + insertion + extra-operations (≈ 2–3× the bolt price).

### Classification (Memorise the 3×3 matrix)
- [ ] **By permanence:** Permanent, Semi-permanent, Temporary.
- [ ] **By force/movement:** Threaded (friction), Non-threaded (form), Quick-release.
- [ ] **By drive:** Slotted, Phillips, Hex, Torx, Robertson.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorise)
- [ ] Tightening torque: $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$, with $K \approx 0.20$.
- [ ] Preload: $F_i = T / (K \cdot d)$.
- [ ] Bolt stress (with torsion): $\sigma_t = 1.3 \cdot F / A_s$.
- [ ] Stress area: $A_s \approx 0.78 \, d^2$ (coarse).
- [ ] Safe preload: $F_i \le \sigma_y A_s / (1.3 \cdot \text{FoS})$, with **FoS = 1.5–2.5**.

### Design Heuristics
- [ ] Always *standardise* fastener type and size.
- [ ] Always provide *tool access* in the layout.
- [ ] Always check *vibration* and *fatigue* — use prevailing-torque features.
- [ ] Always consider *self-locating*, *non-symmetric* fasteners.
- [ ] Always *eliminate* a fastener first; joining is the *last* resort.

### Standards
- [ ] Default to **ISO Metric M-series** in all KTU problems.
- [ ] Know the symbols: M8, M10, M12, etc. — diameter in mm, pitch follows.
- [ ] Grade 8.8 → $\sigma_y = 640$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 800$ MPa. Grade 10.9 → $\sigma_y = 900$ MPa.

### Frequently Confused Concepts
- [ ] **Preload vs. External Load:** Preload is the *clamping* force from tightening; the *external* load adds/subtracts from it.
- [ ] **Tightening factor vs. Factor of Safety:** Tightening factor = $F_i / F_{s,\text{proof}}$; FoS = $\sigma_y / \sigma_t$.
- [ ] **Self-tapping vs. Machine screw:** Self-tapping cuts its own thread in soft material; machine screw needs a pre-tapped hole.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations for Fasteners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations for Fasteners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, *Design Recommendations for Fasteners* refer to a structured set of geometric, dimensional, and procedural rules that govern how threaded and non-threaded fastening elements (bolts, screws, studs, captive nuts, self-clinching fasteners, rivets, etc.) are specified, located, dimensioned, and integrated into the host component and assembly.

These recommendations are derived from three concurrent industrial objectives:
1. **Manufacturability** – ensuring the host part can be produced economically by machining, casting, sheet-metal forming, or plastic moulding.
2. **Assemblability** – ensuring the fastener can be inserted, oriented, and tightened in the minimum possible time, with the highest probability of first-time-right assembly.
3. **Serviceability / Functional Reliability** – ensuring the joint sustains the required preload, resists vibration, and can be serviced (or is permanently captive) depending on application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorise:**  
> *“Design recommendations for fasteners are engineering guidelines that minimise the variety of standard fasteners, standardise boss and clearance-hole geometry, ensure accessibility for assembly tools, and reduce assembly time and cost without compromising joint integrity.”*

### 1.2 The Hierarchy of Decisions

When designing a bolted joint in DFMA, the designer walks through a strict decision hierarchy:

1. **Do we need a fastener at all?** (Can snap-fits, adhesives, welds, integral hinges, or press-fits eliminate the fastener?)
2. **What is the minimum functional number of fasteners?**
3. **What standard, off-the-shelf fastener type and size will satisfy the load?**
4. **Where must the fastener be located for assembly-tool access and symmetry?**
5. **What boss, clearance hole, or counterbore geometry must the host part provide?**
6. **How will the part be oriented for one-direction, top-down assembly?**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are packing a large wooden bookshelf from a flat-pack furniture company. The instruction manual gives you **200 identical screws, 200 identical wooden dowels, and exactly one Allen key**.

Now imagine the *opposite* nightmare: 47 different screw types, each needing a different tool, each going into a slightly different hole, several hidden behind the back panel so you must partially disassemble to reach them, and a few that are *so close to the edge* that the wood splits the moment you tighten them.

The first design will be assembled in 20 minutes with zero frustration. The second will take 4 hours and produce a mountain of stripped screws and split panels.

> **Design Recommendations for Fasteners are, in essence, the engineering rules that ensure every product behaves like the first bookshelf and never like the second.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway for KTU Students:**  
> A well-designed fastener joint is *invisible* to the assembler – the part goes in one way, the fastener is standard, the tool fits, and the joint is reliable. A poorly designed joint announces itself through stripped threads, snapped bosses, jammed power-tools, and warranty claims.

### 1.4 Why These Recommendations Matter in DFMA

Industry surveys (Holtan, Whitney, Boothroyd–Dewhurst) consistently show that **fastener operations account for 25 %–40 % of total assembly labour time** in typical electro-mechanical products. A single non-standard fastener type can increase the part’s piece-price by an order of magnitude due to tooling, inventory, and quality-control overhead. Hence every fastener decision is both a *cost decision* and a *quality decision*.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eleven Cardinal Rules of Fastener Design in DFMA

The recommendations below are universally taught in DFMA literature and are directly tested in KTU university examinations.

#### Rule 1 — Minimise the Number of Fasteners
Use the fewest fasteners consistent with joint strength and stiffness. Each fastener is a separate assembly operation costing 3–15 seconds. Reducing from 6 screws to 4 screws (while keeping the joint safe) saves **two operations per assembly**.

#### Rule 2 — Use Standard, Off-the-Shelf Fasteners
Specify ISO metric (preferred) or inch standard fasteners. **Avoid specials**: left-hand threads, odd pitches, non-standard head styles, or proprietary drive systems. Standard fasteners cost a fraction of specials and are available globally.

#### Rule 3 — Standardise on the Fewest Possible Sizes
If the design truly needs M3, M4, and M6, use only those three. Eliminate every "in-between" size. This single rule yields the largest reduction in *parts-variety cost*.

#### Rule 4 — Use One Fastener Family Where Possible
Choose one drive type (e.g., Phillips or Torx), one head style (e.g., pan-head or hex-flange), and one material/coating. This allows a single feeder bowl, a single tool, and a single bin on the assembly line.

#### Rule 5 — Make Fastener Axes Vertical and Accessible (Z-axis Assembly)
Orient every fastener so its driving axis is vertical, with **unobstructed top-down access**. The power-tool envelope is a downward-pointing cone; any obstruction forces the assembler to reorient the part or use a slower tool.

#### Rule 6 — Provide Adequate Clearances for Tools and Fingers
Around every fastener head there must be a **tool-clearance cylinder**. The minimum diameter of this cylinder depends on the head style and is given in Section 3.

#### Rule 7 — Design Symmetric Fastener Patterns
Use square, rectangular, or pitch-circle patterns (not triangular) so the part can be flipped 90° or 180° and still mate correctly. Symmetric patterns also reduce the chance of off-by-one assembly errors.

#### Rule 8 — Use Captive or Self-Retaining Fasteners When Service is Required
If the fastener must be removable for service, use a **captive screw** (e.g., a captive-panel screw with a retaining ring) so it cannot fall into the mechanism. If the part will never be serviced, use a **self-tapping, self-clinching, or rivet-style permanent fastener** that does not need a mating nut.

#### Rule 9 — Bosses Must Be Designed for the Fastener
A *boss* is the raised cylindrical projection around a fastener hole that provides the wall thickness needed for a tapped hole, the bearing surface for a bolt head or nut, and the mould-flow geometry for plastics. The boss is the **bridge between the fastener and the host part**.

#### Rule 10 — Avoid Tapped Holes in Thin Sections
A tapped hole requires a minimum wall thickness. If the section is too thin, provide a **thicker boss**, use a **through-bolt with a nut** on the back, or use a **self-tapping screw** into a pre-formed pilot hole.

#### Rule 11 — Use Pilot Diameters, Counterbores, and Spot Faces Correctly
Counterbores for socket-head cap screws must be deep enough to clear the head. Spot faces under nuts and bolt heads ensure a flat bearing surface on cast or forged parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:**  
> The Boss design formulae, Clearance-hole chart, and Minimum edge-distance rules are *the* most frequently asked derivation topics in Part B questions. Memorise them in symbolic form before you substitute numbers.

### 2.2 Boss Design Theory

A boss is required whenever a fastener passes through (or is tapped into) a part. The boss has three primary geometric functions:

1. **Provides wall thickness for thread engagement** (when the part is tapped).
2. **Provides a flat, perpendicular bearing surface** for the underside of a bolt head or nut.
3. **Provides mould-flow continuity** in plastic parts (eliminates a sink mark on the cosmetic surface).

The boss is essentially a **short, hollow cylinder** of outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d_h$ (the clearance or tap hole), and height $H$, supported on a flat plate of thickness $t$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Governing Equation / Rule | Typical Value (ISO Metric) | Engineering Purpose |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Fastener nominal diameter | $d$ | ISO 261 preferred sizes | M2, M2.5, M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10, M12… | Sets the scale of every boss and hole |
| 2 | Boss outer diameter | $D$ | $D = 2d$ (minimum) | $2d$ to $2.5d$ | Prevents boss wall from being too thin |
| 3 | Boss wall thickness | $t_w$ | $t_w = (D - d_h)/2$ | $t_w \geq d/2$ | Provides thread or bearing strength |
| 4 | Boss height | $H$ | $H \geq 1.5d$ (steel) or $H \geq 2d$ (aluminium/plastic) | $1.5d$ to $2d$ | Ensures full thread engagement + wrench clearance |
| 5 | Clearance hole diameter | $d_h$ | $d_h = d + \delta$ | $\delta = 1$ mm for $d \leq 10$ mm; $\delta = 2$ mm for $d > 10$ mm (ISO 273 medium) | Permits bolt to pass without binding |
| 6 | Tap drill diameter | $d_t$ | $d_t = d - P$ | $P$ = ISO metric coarse pitch | Leaves correct minor diameter for thread cutting |
| 7 | Minimum thread engagement | $L_e$ | $L_e = 2d$ (steel), $3d$ (aluminium), $4d$ (plastic) | $1.5d$ to $4d$ | Prevents thread stripping failure |
| 8 | Edge distance | $e$ | $e \geq 1.5d$ | $1.5d$ to $2d$ | Prevents edge tear-out under preload |
| 9 | Pitch between fasteners | $c$ | $c \geq 3d$ | $3d$ to $5d$ | Allows tool access and prevents hole-to-hole failure |
| 10 | Tool clearance diameter (pan head) | $D_t$ | $D_t = 2 \times 1.5d = 3d$ | $3d$ | Room for screwdriver or socket |
| 11 | Tightening torque (preload control) | $T$ | $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$ | $K \approx 0.2$ for steel-on-steel | Generates axial preload $F$ in the bolt |
| 12 | Preload force | $F$ | $F = 0.75 \cdot A_s \cdot \sigma_y$ | 75 % of yield load | Prevents joint separation under external load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Every symbol above is an *independent decision variable* the designer controls. The numerical multipliers ($2$, $1.5$, $3$, $4$) are the *industry-standard design constants* you will be expected to recall in the exam even when the question does not provide them.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Application of These Rules |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| Automotive | Engine block bolted to gearbox: symmetric 10-bolt pattern, $D = 2d$, $e = 1.5d$, $H = 1.5d$ |
| Consumer Electronics | Laptop base to top-cover: M2.5 captive screws, self-tapping into plastic bosses |
| Aerospace | Composite wing-skin to spar: close-tolerance clearance holes, $e = 2d$ for fatigue life |
| Sheet Metal | Sheet-metal panel to chassis: self-clinching nuts, eliminating loose nuts in the bin |
| Plastics Moulding | ABS enclosure to PCB: M3 boss with $H = 2d$, $t_w = 1.5$ mm minimum |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Boss Outer Diameter $D = 2d$

**Statement to Prove:** For a tapped boss in a moulded plastic or cast metal part, the minimum outer diameter of the boss should be at least twice the nominal fastener diameter.

**Step 1 — Identify the load path.**
When the screw is tightened, the axial clamping force $F$ is transmitted into the boss wall as a *hoop stress* (circumferential tension) in the cylindrical wall of the boss. The wall behaves like a short pressure vessel.

**Step 2 — Apply the thin-cylinder hoop-stress formula.**
For a thin-walled cylinder of internal radius $r_i$, external radius $r_o$, and internal pressure $p$, the hoop stress is:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r_i}{t_w}
$$

where $t_w$ is the radial wall thickness of the boss.

**Step 3 — Express the internal pressure in terms of the fastener preload.**
The fastener preload $F$ is reacted by the projected bearing area of the boss internal thread. The projected thread diameter is the *mean thread diameter* $d_2 \approx 0.85 d$. Hence the equivalent pressure on the boss wall is:

$$
p = \frac{F}{\pi \cdot d_2 \cdot L_e}
$$

where $L_e$ is the effective thread-engagement length. Substituting $d_2 = 0.85 d$:

$$
p = \frac{F}{\pi \cdot 0.85 d \cdot L_e}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the design rule that wall thickness must equal at least $d/2$.**
For a thread engagement of $L_e = 1.5d$ (typical for steel into steel), the load is distributed over a sufficient length that the wall thickness can be modest. The DFMA convention sets:

$$
t_w = \frac{d}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the outer diameter.**
The internal diameter of the boss is the tap drill diameter:

$$
d_i = d - P
$$

For ISO metric coarse threads, $P \approx 0.125 d$ on average for the sizes of interest (M3 to M12), so $d_i \approx 0.875 d$. The outer diameter is then:

$$
D = d_i + 2 t_w = 0.875 d + 2 \cdot \frac{d}{2} = 0.875 d + d = 1.875 d
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to the nearest standard engineering multiple.**
The value $1.875 d$ is conservatively rounded *up* to give the design rule:

$$
\boxed{D = 2d}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The boss outer diameter is **twice** the fastener diameter. This guarantees that the boss wall can sustain the hoop stress generated by tightening the screw without cracking, splitting, or stripping the threads. For soft materials (aluminium, plastic, magnesium), use $D = 2.5 d$ to add a further safety margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boss Height $H \geq 1.5 d$

**Statement to Prove:** The axial length of the boss must be at least $1.5$ times the nominal fastener diameter for steel-to-steel joints, and $2 d$ for aluminium or plastic.

**Step 1 — Identify the two competing requirements.**
(a) The thread engagement length $L_e$ must be sufficient to prevent thread stripping.
(b) The hex-nut or socket-head wrench must have room to be applied and rotated.

**Step 2 — Translate requirement (a) into a length.**
For a steel bolt in a steel tapped boss, the empirical thread-stripping criterion (Shigley, Machinery’s Handbook) is:

$$
L_e \geq 1.5 d
$$

For aluminium or plastic (lower shear strength), the criterion is:

$$
L_e \geq 2 d
$$

**Step 3 — Translate requirement (b) into a length.**
A standard hex nut of nominal size $d$ has a thickness $m \approx 0.8 d$. If a nut is *not* used (screw into tapped hole), there must be a small clearance for the screw to bottom-out without bottoming the boss. This adds about $0.3 d$ at the bottom.

**Step 4 — Sum the two requirements and round up.**
The boss height must accommodate (a) the thread engagement length plus (b) the clearance for the screw tip. Therefore:

$$
H = L_e + 0.3 d
$$

Substituting $L_e = 1.5 d$ (steel):

$$
H = 1.5 d + 0.3 d = 1.8 d
$$

Rounding to the conservative engineering value:

$$
\boxed{H = 2 d \;\;\text{(steel)} \quad ; \quad H = 2.5 d \;\;\text{(aluminium / plastic)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Convention:**  
> Most textbooks present the rule as "$H \geq 1.5 d$" for general use, but allow $H = 2 d$ when a through-hole and nut are used. Memorise both forms.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clearance Hole Diameter (ISO 273, Medium Series)

**Statement to Prove:** The diameter of the clearance hole for a close-tolerance bolt is given by $d_h = d + \delta$, where $\delta$ depends on the size range.

**Step 1 — Define the function of the clearance hole.**
The clearance hole must (a) allow the bolt to enter without binding due to manufacturing tolerances, (b) permit a small angular misalignment of the mating parts, and (c) allow for the thickness of any plating or coating on the bolt.

**Step 2 — Quantify the required clearance $\delta$.**
ISO 273 (the international standard for clearance holes for bolts) defines three series: *close*, *medium*, and *loose*. The medium series is the most common in general engineering.

For nominal sizes **$d \leq 10$ mm**, the medium-series clearance is $\delta = 1$ mm.
For nominal sizes **$d > 10$ mm**, the medium-series clearance is $\delta = 2$ mm.

**Step 3 — Express as a single conditional formula.**

$$
\boxed{
d_h = 
\begin{cases}
d + 1 \text{ mm}, & d \leq 10 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d + 2 \text{ mm}, & d > 10 \text{ mm}
\end{cases}
}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
For an M8 bolt, $d = 8$ mm, so $d_h = 8 + 1 = 9$ mm.  
For an M12 bolt, $d = 12$ mm, so $d_h = 12 + 2 = 14$ mm.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tap Drill Diameter for ISO Metric Threads

**Statement to Prove:** The tap-drill diameter equals the nominal diameter minus the thread pitch, i.e. $d_t = d - P$.

**Step 1 — Define the tap-drill function.**
The tap drill must produce a hole whose *minor diameter* $D_1$ matches the minor diameter of the thread to be cut. The standard ISO metric thread geometry specifies:

$$
D_1 = d - \frac{5}{8} H \quad \text{where } H = 0.866 P
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the value of $H$.**

$$
D_1 = d - \frac{5}{8} \times 0.866 P = d - 0.541 P
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for engineering use.**
The factor $0.541$ is rounded to $0.5$ for ease of mental calculation in the workshop and the design office. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{d_t = d - P}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example.**
For an M6 × 1.0 coarse thread:
- Nominal diameter $d = 6$ mm
- Pitch $P = 1.0$ mm
- Tap drill diameter $d_t = 6 - 1 = 5$ mm

The standard 5.0 mm drill bit will produce an M6 thread when the M6 tap is run through it.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Tightening Torque vs. Preload

**Statement to Prove:** The torque required to produce a given bolt preload is $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$.

**Step 1 — Relate torque to preload via the nut factor.**
When a nut (or screw head) is tightened, the applied torque $T$ is opposed by friction in two places: (i) under the bearing face of the nut/head, and (ii) in the threads. The combined friction is encapsulated in the *nut factor* $K$, a dimensionless number typically between $0.10$ (well-lubricated) and $0.30$ (dry, galvanised).

**Step 2 — Write the energy / force-torque balance.**

$$
T = F \cdot \frac{d}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{\tan \beta + \mu_t}{1 - \mu_t \tan \beta} + \frac{3 \mu_b}{2} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\cos \beta}
$$

where $\beta$ is the thread helix angle, $\mu_t$ is the thread-friction coefficient, and $\mu_b$ is the bearing-face friction coefficient.

**Step 3 — Simplify for the standard steel-on-steel case.**
Substituting $\beta \approx 2.5°$ (for M6–M16 coarse threads) and $\mu_t \approx \mu_b \approx 0.15$, the bracketed expression evaluates to approximately $0.4$. The cosine of $2.5°$ is essentially $1.0$. Therefore the torque-preload relation collapses to:

$$
\boxed{T = K \cdot F \cdot d}
$$

with $K \approx 0.2$ for plain steel on steel with light oiling.

**Step 4 — Worked Example.**
An M10 bolt ($d = 10$ mm) is to be preloaded to $F = 20$ kN. The required torque is:

$$
T = 0.2 \times 20{,}000 \text{ N} \times 0.010 \text{ m} = 40 \text{ N·m}
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Boss / Tapped Hole / Through-Bolt

| Material of Host Part | Thickness of Host Part | Recommended Joint Type | Boss Outer $D$ | Boss Height $H$ | Tap Drill $d_t$ |
|-----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Steel, $t \geq 1.5 d$ | Adequate | Tapped boss | $2d$ | $1.5d$ | $d - P$ |
| Steel, $t < 1.5 d$ | Thin | Through-bolt + nut | None (use clearance hole) | N/A | N/A |
| Aluminium, $t \geq 2 d$ | Adequate | Tapped boss | $2.5 d$ | $2 d$ | $d - P$ |
| Aluminium, $t < 2 d$ | Thin | Through-bolt + nut | None | N/A | N/A |
| Plastic (ABS, PC), $t \geq 3 d$ | Adequate | Tapped boss or self-tap | $2.5 d$ | $2 d$ | $d - P$ or $0.8 d$ pilot |
| Plastic, $t < 3 d$ | Thin | Self-clinching nut or insert | N/A (insert moulded) | N/A | N/A |
| Sheet metal, $t < 1.5 d$ | Very thin | Self-clinching nut pressed in | N/A | N/A | $d + 0.2$ mm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boss Geometry — Sectional View (Mermaid Block-Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Plate of host part, thickness t] --> B[Boss base sits flush on plate]
    B --> C[Boss outer diameter D, 2d]
    C --> D[Boss inner clearance hole, d_h = d + 1 mm]
    D --> E[Tap threads cut on inner wall]
    E --> F[Boss height H, 1.5d to 2d]
    F --> G[Screw engaged L_e = 1.5d, leaves 0.3d tip clearance]
    G --> H[Edge distance e, 1.5d from boss center to plate edge]
    H --> I[Pitch c, 3d between adjacent boss centers]
    I --> J[Tool clearance cylinder, D_t = 3d around screw head]
```

### 4.2 Fastener Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Need a fastener?] --> B{Is a non-fastener alternative possible? e.g. snap-fit, weld, adhesive}
    B -- Yes --> C[Eliminate fastener. Use snap-fit or weld.]
    B -- No --> D{Fastener must be serviceable?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Service frequency high?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use captive screw with retaining ring]
    E -- No --> G[Use standard hex or Torx bolt plus nut]
    D -- No --> H{Host part is sheet metal t less than 1.5d?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use self-clinching nut pressed into sheet]
    H -- No --> J{Host part is plastic t greater than 2d?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Tap thread directly into boss. D = 2.5d, H = 2d]
    J -- No --> L[Use through-bolt with nut. Clearance hole only]
```

### 4.3 Boss Dimension Summary (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[F fastener diameter d] --> B[Boss outer D 2d]
    A --> C[Tap drill d minus P]
    A --> D[Boss height H 1.5d to 2d]
    A --> E[Edge distance e 1.5d]
    A --> F[Pitch c 3d]
    A --> G[Tool clearance 3d]
    A --> H[Counterbore depth for socket head, 0.7d]
    A --> I[Preload F 0.75 of yield load]
    I --> J[Torque T = K times F times d]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bolted Joint

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Part A, e.g. housing] --> CL1[Clearance hole d_h = d plus 1 mm]
    P2[Part B, e.g. cover] --> CL2[Clearance hole d_h = d plus 1 mm]
    CL1 --> B1[Boss or bearing surface on Part A]
    CL2 --> B2[Boss or bearing surface on Part B]
    B1 --> F1[Bolt shank passes through Part A]
    F1 --> F2[Bolt threads engage nut on Part B]
    B2 --> N1[Nut or captive threadform]
    N1 --> W1[Washer distributes preload]
    W1 --> T1[Torque applied T = K F d]
    T1 --> PR1[Preload F = 0.75 A_s sigma_y]
    PR1 --> JT1[Joint is now clamped and load-bearing]
```

### 4.5 Captive Fastener — Block View

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Retaining ring on captive screw] --> B[Screw body cannot fall out of host part]
    B --> C[Host panel can be removed for service without losing screw]
    C --> D[Insert moulded or pressed bushing receives screw threads]
    D --> E[Standard hand tool drives screw top-down along Z axis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define the term "boss" in the context of fastener design. Why is a boss preferred to a flat surface for receiving a screw in a moulded plastic part?**

**Model Answer:**
A *boss* is a raised, hollow cylindrical projection moulded (or machined) integral with the host part, whose axis coincides with the fastener axis. The internal surface of the boss is either tapped to receive a screw or bored as a clearance hole for a through-bolt.

A boss is preferred to a flat surface for **three DFMA reasons**:
1. It **provides the wall thickness** required for a full thread engagement, which a thin flat wall cannot supply without cracking.
2. It **avoids sink marks** on the cosmetic surface of the moulded plastic part – the thicker boss freezes last and is hidden from the user’s eye.
3. It **provides a flat, perpendicular bearing surface** for the screw head or nut, ensuring that the preload is axial and not eccentric. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons in compressed form.)*

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is meant by "preferred-size standardisation" of fasteners? List any four ISO metric preferred nominal sizes.**

**Model Answer:**
*Preferred-size standardisation* is the DFMA practice of restricting the design to a small, pre-selected set of fastener nominal diameters – the *preferred numbers* – rather than allowing every conceivable size. This minimises inventory, tool count, and the cognitive load on the assembler.

Four ISO metric preferred nominal sizes (ISO 261): **M3, M4, M5, M6** *(or M2.5, M3, M4, M5 – any four from the preferred series M1, M1.2, M1.6, M2, M2.5, M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10, M12… are acceptable).*

The accompanying *preferred pitch* for each is the *coarse* pitch: e.g. M3 × 0.5, M4 × 0.7, M5 × 0.8, M6 × 1.0. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the principle, 1 mark for the four sizes with pitches.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the standard **boss design for a tapped hole in a steel part** for an M8 × 1.25 bolt. Specify the boss outer diameter, height, wall thickness, tap-drill diameter, edge distance, and minimum pitch between two adjacent bosses.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Two steel plates 4 mm and 6 mm thick are to be joined by an M6 × 1.0 bolt and nut. The bolt is to be tightened to a preload of 12 kN. Determine (i) the tap-drill diameter if a tapped boss is used, (ii) the clearance-hole diameter (ISO medium), and (iii) the tightening torque assuming a nut factor $K = 0.2$.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

For an M8 × 1.25 bolt, $d = 8$ mm and $P = 1.25$ mm.

| Parameter | Formula | Computed Value |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| Boss outer diameter $D$ | $D = 2d$ | $D = 16$ mm |
| Tap drill diameter $d_t$ | $d_t = d - P$ | $d_t = 8 - 1.25 = 6.75$ mm |
| Boss height $H$ (steel) | $H = 1.5d$ to $2d$ | $H = 12$ mm to $16$ mm; choose $H = 14$ mm |
| Boss wall thickness $t_w$ | $t_w = (D - d_t)/2$ | $t_w = (16 - 6.75)/2 = 4.625$ mm |
| Edge distance $e$ | $e = 1.5d$ | $e = 12$ mm |
| Pitch between centres $c$ | $c = 3d$ | $c = 24$ mm |
| Tool-clearance cylinder $D_t$ | $D_t = 3d$ | $D_t = 24$ mm |

Neat-sketch description: a cylindrical boss of $D = 16$ mm rises $H = 14$ mm above the plate surface. Its inner bore is $6.75$ mm. The M8 × 1.25 tap cuts threads for a depth of $1.5 \times 8 = 12$ mm, leaving $2$ mm tip clearance at the bottom. The boss centre is at least $12$ mm from any free edge. The boss-to-boss centre pitch is at least $24$ mm. *(7 marks: 2 for sketch description, 3 for table of dimensions, 2 for correct computation.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: M6 × 1.0 bolt, $d = 6$ mm, $P = 1.0$ mm, preload $F = 12$ kN, $K = 0.2$.

**(i) Tap-drill diameter:**
$$
d_t = d - P = 6 - 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Clearance hole (ISO 273 medium series, $d \leq 10$ mm):**
$$
d_h = d + 1 \text{ mm} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Tightening torque:**
$$
T = K \cdot F \cdot d
$$
$$
T = 0.2 \times 12{,}000 \text{ N} \times 0.006 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\boxed{T = 14.4 \text{ N·m}}
$$

*Note:* the 4 mm and 6 mm plate thicknesses do not enter the torque calculation directly; they only affect whether a tapped boss is feasible (it is, since both thicknesses are > $1.5d = 9$ mm would be required, so a through-bolt + nut is the practical choice for these gauges). *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **eleven cardinal design recommendations for fasteners** in DFMA, with one line for each rule.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An aluminium bracket 5 mm thick is to be fastened to a steel chassis using four M5 × 0.8 captive screws in a rectangular pattern. Determine (i) the boss outer diameter and height for the aluminium bracket, (ii) the clearance-hole diameter in the steel chassis, (iii) the tap-drill diameter, and (iv) the recommended minimum edge distance and pitch.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **eleven cardinal design recommendations** for fasteners in DFMA (each in one line):

1. **Minimise the number of fasteners** – fewer operations, lower cost.
2. **Use standard off-the-shelf fasteners** – avoid specials, left-hand threads, odd pitches.
3. **Standardise on the fewest possible sizes** – typically three or fewer per assembly.
4. **Use one fastener family** – one drive type, one head style, one finish.
5. **Vertical Z-axis assembly** – orient every fastener for top-down tool access.
6. **Provide tool and finger clearances** – a clearance cylinder of $3d$ around each head.
7. **Use symmetric fastener patterns** – square or rectangular, not triangular.
8. **Use captive / self-retaining fasteners** where service is required.
9. **Design proper bosses** – $D = 2d$, $H = 1.5d$ to $2d$.
10. **Avoid tapped holes in thin sections** – provide a boss or use a through-bolt.
11. **Use counterbores and spot faces correctly** – flat bearing for heads and nuts.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for every 2 rules correctly stated; 0.5 mark each if wording is imprecise.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: M5 × 0.8 captive screw, $d = 5$ mm, $P = 0.8$ mm, host part material aluminium, thickness $t = 5$ mm.

**(i) Boss outer diameter and height for aluminium:**
Aluminium has a lower thread-shear strength than steel, so the conservative rules apply:
$$
D = 2.5 d = 2.5 \times 5 = 12.5 \text{ mm (round up to 13 mm)}
$$
$$
H = 2 d = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Clearance-hole diameter in the steel chassis (ISO 273 medium, $d \leq 10$ mm):**
$$
d_h = d + 1 \text{ mm} = 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Tap-drill diameter (or pilot diameter for captive screw):**
$$
d_t = d - P = 5 - 0.8 = 4.2 \text{ mm (use a 4.2 mm standard drill)}
$$

**(iv) Edge distance and pitch:**
$$
e = 1.5 d = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ mm (round up to 8 mm)}
$$
$$
c = 3 d = 3 \times 5 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

*Check feasibility of tapped boss:* $H = 10$ mm is achievable on a 5 mm plate by raising the boss above the plate surface (boss is *additional* to the plate, not carved out of it). Tap depth of $1.5 \times 5 = 7.5$ mm plus $0.3 \times 5 = 1.5$ mm tip clearance = $9$ mm, which is within the 10 mm boss height. Design is feasible. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.3 d tip clearance** in boss-height calculation. Many students compute $H = 1.5d$ and lose a mark because the screw tip would bottom against the closed end of the tapped hole.
> 2. **Confusing tap-drill diameter $d - P$ with clearance hole $d + 1$ mm.** These are *opposite* operations: tapping *removes* metal (so the hole is *smaller* than $d$), clearance *allows* the bolt (so the hole is *larger* than $d$). Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 3. **Using $H = 1.5 d$ for aluminium or plastic.** The rule $1.5 d$ is for *steel* only. For aluminium and plastic the answer must be $H = 2 d$.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the torque formula.** $T = K F d$ requires $F$ in newtons and $d$ in *metres* (not mm) to obtain $T$ in N·m. Many students substitute $d = 6$ and report $T = 24$ N·m, which is off by a factor of 1000. *Always convert mm to m.*
> 5. **Mixing preferred and non-preferred sizes.** Specify M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10, M12 – never M7 or M9 unless absolutely unavoidable.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Boss outer diameter** $D = 2d$ (steel) or $D = 2.5d$ (aluminium/plastic).
- [ ] **Boss height** $H = 1.5d$ (steel) or $H = 2d$ (aluminium/plastic).
- [ ] **Tap drill** $d_t = d - P$; **Clearance hole** $d_h = d + 1$ mm (small) or $+2$ mm (large).
- [ ] **Edge distance** $e \geq 1.5d$; **Pitch between fasteners** $c \geq 3d$.
- [ ] **Tool clearance cylinder** $D_t \geq 3d$ around every screw head.
- [ ] **Thread engagement** $L_e \geq 1.5d$ (steel), $\geq 2d$ (aluminium), $\geq 3d$ to $4d$ (plastic).
- [ ] **Tightening torque** $T = K F d$ with $K \approx 0.2$ for steel-on-steel.
- [ ] **Preload target** $F = 0.75 A_s \sigma_y$ (75 % of bolt yield load).
- [ ] **Always use standard ISO metric preferred sizes** – minimise variety.
- [ ] **Always orient fasteners vertically** for Z-axis top-down assembly.
- [ ] **Use symmetric patterns** (square, rectangular, pitch-circle) – not triangles.
- [ ] **Use captive / self-clinching fasteners** when access is restricted or service is required.
- [ ] **Use counterbores for socket-head cap screws** and **spot faces under nuts and bolt heads** on cast/forged parts.
- [ ] **Avoid tapped holes in thin sections** – use a thicker boss, a through-bolt, or a self-tapping screw into a pilot.
- [ ] **Bosses should be on the back (non-cosmetic) side** of plastic parts to hide sink marks.
- [ ] **Assembly direction is one-way** – design so that the part mates in a single Z-direction stroke; the fastener pattern should not require flipping the part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Disassembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design for Disassembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Design for Disassembly (DFD) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Disassembly (DFD)** is a systematic, proactive product development methodology that integrates end-of-life (EoL) considerations into the initial conceptual and detailed design stages of a product. The objective is to engineer products that can be **efficiently, economically, and safely separated** into their constituent components, subassemblies, and materials at the conclusion of their useful service life.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMET522), DFD is formally defined as:

> *"A design philosophy that enables the systematic separation of a product's components, modules, and materials using predefined, reversible joining techniques to facilitate maintenance, refurbishment, remanufacturing, recycling, and ultimately, sustainable disposal."*

The companion term **Disassembly (D)** is the inverse operation of Assembly (A). It refers to the **set of physical operations and processes** required to systematically dismantle a product into its constituent parts without damaging those parts that are designated for reuse, remanufacture, or recycling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DFD is treated as the *mirror image* of Design for Assembly (DFA). Every design decision that *eases* assembly must also be re-evaluated through a *disassembly* lens to ensure end-of-life recovery is feasible.

## 1.2 Related Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Disassembly (D)** | The systematic separation of a product into its constituent components. |
| **End-of-Life (EoL)** | The stage in a product's life cycle when it is discarded, recycled, or recovered. |
| **Design for Environment (DFE)** | A broader umbrella encompassing DFD, DFR (Recycling), DFM (Manufacturing). |
| **Design for Recycling (DFR)** | A subset of DFD focused on material recovery. |
| **Design for Remanufacture** | A subset of DFD focused on restoring components to "as-new" condition. |
| **Reuse** | Using a component again in the same application without modification. |
| **Recovery Rate (R)** | The fraction of total product mass successfully recovered through disassembly. |
| **Captive Fastener** | A fastener that remains attached to the part after removal, preventing loss. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Lego® vs. Glue" Principle**
>
> Imagine you are given two identical toy houses:
> - **House A (Designed for Disassembly):** Built entirely with Lego-style interlocking bricks. You can separate the roof, windows, walls, and base in **seconds** with bare hands. Each brick is reusable.
> - **House B (Not Designed for Disassembly):** Built by **gluing** and **welding** every joint. To separate it, you must **break** or **cut** through materials — destroying most components in the process.
>
> **House A was designed for disassembly from day one.** Its designer (you) chose reversible joints, accessible parts, and minimal fasteners.

The same principle applies to **automobiles, mobile phones, refrigerators, and aircraft engines**. A car designed for DFD allows the mechanic to swap a battery in **10 minutes**; a phone designed for DFD allows the refurbisher to replace a screen in **under 5 minutes**.

## 1.4 The Need for DFD — Why It Matters

| Driver | Description |
|---|---|
| **EU WEEE Directive** | Mandates 70–85 % recovery rate for end-of-life vehicles and electronics. |
| **Circular Economy Goals** | Promotes reuse, refurbishment, and remanufacture over landfill disposal. |
| **Corporate ESG Compliance** | Companies face legal and reputational pressure to design sustainable products. |
| **Resource Scarcity** | Critical raw materials (rare earths, cobalt) must be recovered. |
| **Reduced Lifecycle Cost** | Modular DFD products have lower maintenance and upgrade costs. |

## 1.5 Disassembly vs. Assembly — The Inverse Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Disassembly = Inverse Assembly" Principle**
>
> The fundamental rule of DFD is:
> $$\text{If a product is easy to assemble, it is not automatically easy to disassemble.}$$
>
> Example: A **snap-fit** is fast to assemble (one push) but requires a special tool to release. This **asymmetry** is a critical DFD concern.

The table below highlights key differences:

| Parameter | Assembly | Disassembly |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Build-up | Tear-down |
| Force application | Insertion | Extraction |
| Tool access | Often free | Often restricted |
| Component identification | Not critical | Critical (sorting) |
| Workspace | Open during build | Often confined |
| Operator skill | Moderate | High (sorting required) |
| Reversibility | N/A | Must be designed-in |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Design for Disassembly (KTU Framework)

The KTU 2024 Scheme organizes DFD theory around four foundational pillars. Every design decision must be evaluated against all four.

### Pillar 1: **Disassembly Strategy Selection**
Choose the most appropriate recovery strategy at the conceptual design phase.

### Pillar 2: **Joining Method Selection (Reversibility)**
Prefer **reversible joints** (mechanical fasteners, snap-fits, threads) over **permanent joints** (welds, adhesives, rivets).

### Pillar 3: **Access and Tooling Provision**
Ensure that disassembly tools (screwdrivers, wrenches, robots) can physically reach and operate on every joint.

### Pillar 4: **Identification, Sorting, and Material Compatibility**
Each component must be **identifiable** (color, label, RFID) and made of materials that can be safely **sorted** (ferrous vs. non-ferrous, plastic types).

## 2.2 Classification of Joining Methods — Reversibility Matrix

| Joining Method | Reversible? | Disassembly Time | Tool Required | DFD Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Threaded fastener (bolt/screw)** | Yes | Low | Standard | ★★★★★ |
| **Snap-fit** | Yes (with tool) | Low | Pliers/special | ★★★★ |
| **Press-fit** | Limited | Medium | Hydraulic press | ★★★ |
| **Pop rivet** | No | High | Drill | ★★ |
| **Adhesive bond** | No | Very High | Heat/solvent | ★ |
| **Weld (TIG/MIG/spot)** | No | Very High | Grinder/cutter | ★ |
| **Brazed joint** | No | Very High | Heat/torch | ★ |
| **Soldered joint** | No | High | Heat | ★ |
| **Molded-in insert** | No | Very High | Cutting | ★ |
| **Captive screw** | Yes | Very Low | Standard | ★★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Suggest a joining method for a DFD-compliant design"*, the **safest answer is always a threaded fastener with a captive design**, because it scores the highest on reversibility, tool access, and reusability.

## 2.3 Disassembly Process Models

### Model A: Sequential Disassembly (Linear)
Parts are removed **one at a time** in a specific order. Each part obstructs the next.

$$\text{Disassembly Sequence} = \{P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to \ldots \to P_n\}$$

### Model B: Parallel Disassembly (Modular)
The product is split into **independent modules** that can be separated concurrently.

$$\text{Disassembly Time}_{\text{parallel}} = \max(t_{M1}, t_{M2}, \ldots, t_{Mk})$$

### Model C: Hierarchical Disassembly
A combination — major subassemblies are separated first (parallel), then internal parts (sequential).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **board-exam tested** and form the analytical core of DFD.

### Formula 1: **Disassembly Index (DI)**

The Disassembly Index measures the **ease of disassembly** of a product.

$$DI = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{T_d}$$

Where:
- $N_m$ = Number of **manual** disassembly operations (operations requiring human intervention)
- $t_m$ = Average time per manual operation (seconds)
- $T_d$ = **Total theoretical disassembly time** for the product (seconds)

**Interpretation:**
- $DI \to 1.0$ → Highly efficient disassembly (manual operations dominate)
- $DI \to 0$ → Disassembly requires mostly automated/destructive operations

### Formula 2: **Disassembly Efficiency (η_d)**

$$\eta_d = \frac{N_{\text{recovered}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{recovered}}$ = Number of components successfully recovered
- $N_{\text{total}}$ = Total number of components in the product

### Formula 3: **Material Recovery Rate (R)**

$$R = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i^{\text{recovered}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i^{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $m_i^{\text{recovered}}$ = Mass of component $i$ recovered for reuse/recycle
- $m_i^{\text{total}}$ = Total mass of component $i$ originally
- $n$ = Total number of components

### Formula 4: **Disassembly Cost (C_d)**

$$C_d = C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{energy}} + C_{\text{sorting}}$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{tool}}$ = Tooling/tool wear cost
- $C_{\text{labor}}$ = Operator labor cost
- $C_{\text{energy}}$ = Energy cost (cutting, heating if required)
- $C_{\text{sorting}}$ = Post-disassembly sorting cost

### Formula 5: **Disassembly Time Estimate (T_d)**

For a product with $n$ parts and average time $t_i$ per part:

$$T_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i = n \cdot \bar{t}$$

For parallel disassembly of $k$ modules:

$$T_{d,\text{parallel}} = \max(t_{M1}, t_{M2}, \ldots, t_{Mk})$$

### Formula 6: **Reusability Index (RI)**

$$RI = \frac{N_{\text{reused}}}{N_{\text{recovered}}} \times 100\%$$

This measures the **quality** of recovery — what fraction of recovered parts is *good enough* to reuse without remanufacture.

### Formula 7: **Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFD Index (Simplified)**

$$DFD_{\text{index}} = \frac{N_{\text{manual ops}} \times 1.0 + N_{\text{destructive ops}} \times 5.0}{N_{\text{total ops}}}$$

The factor **5.0** penalizes destructive operations heavily because they destroy component value.

### Formula 8: **Depth of Disassembly (DoD)**

The number of hierarchical levels that must be traversed to recover a target component.

$$DoD = \text{Number of subassemblies between target component and root}$$

For a part inside a 3-level nested assembly (e.g., a screw inside a sub-cover inside a main housing), $DoD = 3$.

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $DI$ | Disassembly Index | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $\eta_d$ | Disassembly Efficiency | % |
| $R$ | Material Recovery Rate | % |
| $C_d$ | Total Disassembly Cost | ₹ / ₹ per product |
| $T_d$ | Total Disassembly Time | seconds |
| $RI$ | Reusability Index | % |
| $DoD$ | Depth of Disassembly | levels |
| $N_m$ | Manual operations | count |
| $N_{\text{total}}$ | Total components | count |

## 2.5 The 12 Golden Rules of DFD (KTU Board-Standard List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus treats these as the **"Twelve Commandments"** of DFD. Memorize them — they appear in nearly every Part A question.

1. **Minimize the number of parts** — fewer parts ⇒ fewer disassembly steps.
2. **Use a modular design** — modules can be removed as wholes.
3. **Prefer reversible fasteners** — bolts, screws, captive fasteners over welds.
4. **Standardize fasteners** — one size/type across the entire product.
5. **Provide clear tool access** — ensure screwdrivers can reach every fastener.
6. **Use a single disassembly direction** — preferably vertical (gravity-aided).
7. **Mark components for identification** — color codes, embossed codes, RFID.
8. **Avoid permanent joints** — minimize welds, adhesives, rivets.
9. **Use captive fasteners** — prevents loss during disassembly.
10. **Reduce material variety** — facilitates recycling and sorting.
11. **Design for single-tool disassembly** — one tool for the entire product.
12. **Avoid nested assemblies** — each part should ideally be accessible in ≤ 2 steps.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | DFD Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Modular dashboards, bolted battery packs (EVs). |
| **Aerospace** | Engine modules designed for hot-section inspection and refurbishment. |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Phones with pentalobe screws and modular displays. |
| **Appliances** | Refrigerators with reversible door hinges and standard fasteners. |
| **Medical Devices** | Sterilizable, serviceable surgical instruments. |
| **Defense** | Field-repairable weapon systems and electronics. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Disassembly Index from First Principles

**Objective:** Derive a single dimensionless metric that quantifies the *ease of disassembly* of a product, starting from the operational definition of disassembly.

### Step 1 — Define the Operational Universe

A product of total mass $M$ is made of $N_{\text{total}}$ components $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_{N_{\text{total}}}\}$. The disassembly process requires $N_{\text{total}}$ elementary operations.

### Step 2 — Classify Each Operation

Each operation $i$ falls into one of three classes:
- **Manual** ($M_i = 1$): reversible, hand-tool, no damage
- **Semi-destructive** ($M_i = 2$): requires cutting/drilling, partial damage
- **Destructive** ($M_i = 3$): destroys the component

### Step 3 — Assign Operation Cost

Let $t_i$ = time for operation $i$ (seconds), and $w_i$ = weight factor.

The **total weighted disassembly time** is:

$$
T_w = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{total}}} w_i \cdot t_i
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Disassembly Index

Normalize $T_w$ against the **ideal** case (all operations manual, $w_i = 1$):

$$
DI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{manual}}} t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{total}}} t_i} = \frac{N_m \cdot \bar{t_m}}{T_d}
$$

This gives the simplified formula used in the KTU formula sheet.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A consumer product contains **8 components** with the following disassembly data. Compute the Disassembly Index (DI) and Material Recovery Rate (R).

| Part | Operation Type | Time (s) | Mass (g) | Recovered Mass (g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Outer cover | Manual (4 screws) | 30 | 120 | 120 |
| 2. PCB assembly | Manual (2 connectors) | 20 | 80 | 80 |
| 3. Battery pack | Manual (captive clip) | 15 | 200 | 200 |
| 4. Motor | Manual (3 bolts) | 45 | 350 | 350 |
| 5. Gearbox | Destructive (rivets) | 90 | 180 | 90 |
| 6. Housing base | Manual (6 screws) | 60 | 250 | 250 |
| 7. Wire harness | Manual (plug) | 10 | 50 | 50 |
| 8. Display module | Manual (4 clips) | 25 | 90 | 90 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute Total Disassembly Time ($T_d$)**

$$
T_d = 30 + 20 + 15 + 45 + 90 + 60 + 10 + 25
$$

$$
T_d = 295 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Manual Disassembly Time**

Parts 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8 are manual; Part 5 is destructive.

$$
T_m = 30 + 20 + 15 + 45 + 60 + 10 + 25 = 205 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Disassembly Index**

$$
DI = \frac{T_m}{T_d} = \frac{205}{295} = 0.6949 \approx 0.695
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **69.5 %** of the disassembly operations are reversible/manual. This product has **good** DFD characteristics, but Part 5 (riveted gearbox) is a bottleneck.

**Step 4 — Compute Material Recovery Rate (R)**

Total mass:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 120 + 80 + 200 + 350 + 180 + 250 + 50 + 90 = 1320 \text{ g}
$$

Recovered mass:

$$
M_{\text{recovered}} = 120 + 80 + 200 + 350 + 90 + 250 + 50 + 90 = 1230 \text{ g}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1230}{1320} \times 100\% = 93.18\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only the riveted gearbox suffers 50 % material loss; the rest is fully recovered.

**Step 5 — Compute Reusability Index (RI)**

Of the 8 parts, 7 are fully recovered without damage (Parts 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8). Part 5 is partially damaged.

$$
RI = \frac{7}{8} \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

## 3.3 Design Improvement Analysis

To improve the DI, replace the riveted gearbox with a **bolted variant**:

| Parameter | Original (Riveted) | Improved (Bolted) |
|---|---|---|
| Operation type | Destructive | Manual |
| Time | 90 s | 60 s |
| Recovered mass | 90 g | 180 g |
| **New $T_d$** | 295 − 90 + 60 = **265 s** | |
| **New $T_m$** | 205 + 60 = **265 s** | |
| **New $DI$** | $265 / 265 = \mathbf{1.000}$ | |
| **New $R$** | $\frac{1230 - 90 + 180}{1320} \times 100\% = \mathbf{99.24\%}$ | |

**Conclusion:** A **single design change** (rivets → bolts on Part 5) raised the DI from **0.695 → 1.000** and the recovery rate from **93.18 % → 99.24 %**. This is the power of DFD.

## 3.4 Cost Analysis Derivation

The total disassembly cost is:

$$
C_d = C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{energy}} + C_{\text{sorting}}
$$

**Component-wise expressions:**

$$
C_{\text{labor}} = N_m \cdot t_m \cdot c_{\text{labor}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{energy}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{dest}}} E_i \cdot c_{\text{energy}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{sorting}} = N_{\text{types}} \cdot c_{\text{sort,per-type}}
$$

Where:
- $c_{\text{labor}}$ = cost per second of operator time (₹/s)
- $E_i$ = energy consumed by destructive operation $i$ (kWh)
- $c_{\text{energy}}$ = cost per kWh (₹/kWh)
- $N_{\text{types}}$ = number of distinct material types

**Optimization Goal:** Minimize $C_d$ subject to $R \geq R_{\text{regulatory}}$ (e.g., EU WEEE mandates $\geq 85\%$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — DFD Methodology Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[product concept] --> B[end of life strategy selection]
    B --> C1[reuse]
    B --> C2[remanufacture]
    B --> C3[recycle]
    B --> C4[dispose]
    C1 --> D[disassembly process design]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E1[identify joints and fasteners]
    D --> E2[establish disassembly sequence]
    D --> E3[provision tool access]
    D --> E4[plan material sorting]
    E1 --> F[select reversible joining methods]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[DFD compliant product]
    G --> H[low end of life cost]
    G --> I[high recovery rate]
    G --> J[regulatory compliance]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Joint Selection Decision Tree for DFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[start joint selection] --> B{is joint permanent or reversible}
    B -->|permanent| C1[weld]
    B -->|permanent| C2[adhesive]
    B -->|permanent| C3[rivet]
    B -->|reversible| D1[threaded fastener]
    B -->|reversible| D2[snap fit]
    B -->|reversible| D3[press fit]
    C1 --> E1[poor DFD score]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> F1[excellent DFD score]
    D2 --> F2[good DFD score with tool]
    D3 --> F3[moderate DFD score]
    E1 --> G[consider redesign to reversible joint]
    F1 --> H[preferred for DFD]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hierarchical Disassembly Sequence (Three-Level Product)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[level 0 product] --> M1[module A housing]
    R --> M2[module B power unit]
    R --> M3[module C control unit]
    M1 --> P1[part 1 cover]
    M1 --> P2[part 2 fastener]
    M2 --> P3[part 3 motor]
    M2 --> P4[part 4 battery]
    M3 --> P5[part 5 PCB]
    M3 --> P6[part 6 display]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — The DFD Design Loop (Continuous Improvement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[conceive design] --> B[apply 12 DFD rules]
    B --> C[compute DI]
    C --> D{DI above threshold}
    D -->|yes| E[finalize design]
    D -->|no| F[modify joints or access]
    F --> B
    E --> G[disassembly simulation]
    G --> H[validate recovery rate]
    H --> I[release to production]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — DFD vs DFA: Symmetry Analysis Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DFA[Design for Assembly]
        A1[minimize parts] --> A2[use standard parts]
        A2 --> A3[ease of insertion]
    end
    subgraph DFD[Design for Disassembly]
        B1[minimize parts] --> B2[use standard reversible fasteners]
        B2 --> B3[ease of extraction and tool access]
    end
    A3 --> C[not automatically equivalent]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[design must be evaluated for both]
```
