# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Design for Disassembly (DFD). List any four guidelines for designing a product for ease of disassembly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Design for Disassembly is a product design methodology that ensures a product can be efficiently and economically taken apart at the end of its useful life, using reversible joining methods, to enable the recovery of components, subassemblies, and materials for reuse, remanufacture, or recycling.

**Four Guidelines (1.5 marks — any four of the following):**
1. Minimize the total number of parts in the product.
2. Use reversible joining methods (bolts, screws, snap-fits) instead of welds or adhesives.
3. Standardize fasteners so that only one tool is needed for disassembly.
4. Provide adequate access for tools to reach all joints.
5. Mark components with material identification codes for easy sorting.
6. Avoid nested assemblies; design for single-direction disassembly.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Destructive and Non-Destructive disassembly. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Destructive Disassembly | Non-Destructive Disassembly |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Disassembly that **damages** one or more components during separation. | Disassembly that **preserves** all components in reusable condition. |
| Tools | Grinders, cutters, torches. | Screwdrivers, wrenches, pliers. |
| Recovery | Partial; damaged parts must be recycled, not reused. | Full; parts can be reused or remanufactured. |
| Cost | High (tooling + waste). | Low (standard hand tools). |
| Example | Cutting spot welds on a car body to remove a panel. | Unscrewing 4 bolts to remove a phone back cover. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**A product contains the following components with the given disassembly data:**

| Part | Operation Type | Time (s) | Mass (g) | Recovered Mass (g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Top cover | Manual (screws) | 25 | 150 | 150 |
| 2. Display | Manual (clips) | 20 | 90 | 90 |
| 3. Main PCB | Manual (connector) | 15 | 110 | 110 |
| 4. Battery | Manual (captive) | 10 | 250 | 250 |
| 5. Housing base | Manual (screws) | 30 | 280 | 280 |
| 6. Speaker module | Destructive (glued) | 60 | 70 | 30 |

#### (a) Calculate the Disassembly Index (DI), Material Recovery Rate (R), and Reusability Index (RI) of the product. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Disassembly Time**

$$
T_d = 25 + 20 + 15 + 10 + 30 + 60 = 160 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Manual Disassembly Time** (excluding the destructive operation of Part 6)

$$
T_m = 25 + 20 + 15 + 10 + 30 = 100 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Disassembly Index**

$$
DI = \frac{T_m}{T_d} = \frac{100}{160} = 0.625
$$

**Step 4 — Total Mass**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 150 + 90 + 110 + 250 + 280 + 70 = 950 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Recovered Mass**

$$
M_{\text{recovered}} = 150 + 90 + 110 + 250 + 280 + 30 = 910 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 6 — Material Recovery Rate**

$$
R = \frac{910}{950} \times 100\% = 95.79\%
$$

**Step 7 — Reusability Index**

Of 6 parts, 5 are fully recovered without damage (Part 6 is destroyed). Hence:

$$
RI = \frac{5}{6} \times 100\% = 83.33\%
$$

**Final Answer (Part a):**
- $DI = 0.625$
- $R = 95.79\;\text{\%}$
- $RI = 83.33\;\text{\%}$

---

#### (b) Suggest two design modifications to improve the DFD metrics, with quantitative justification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Modification 1: Replace glued speaker with bolted/snap-fit speaker module.**

| Parameter | Original (Glued) | Modified (Bolted) |
|---|---|---|
| Operation type | Destructive | Manual |
| Time | 60 s | 25 s |
| Recovered mass | 30 g | 70 g |
| New $T_d$ | $160 - 60 + 25 = 125$ s | |
| New $T_m$ | $100 + 25 = 125$ s | |
| **New $DI$** | $125 / 125 = \mathbf{1.000}$ | |
| **New $R$** | $\frac{910 - 30 + 70}{950} \times 100\% = \mathbf{100.00\%}$ | |
| **New $RI$** | $6/6 = \mathbf{100.00\%}$ | |

**Modification 2: Consolidate the top cover (Part 1) and housing base (Part 5) into a single molded clamshell.**

| Parameter | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Number of parts | 6 | 5 |
| Total time | 125 s | 105 s |
| $DI$ | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| Standardization | 2 screw types | 1 screw type |

**Justification (in words):**
1. The first modification (replacing adhesive with bolts) **eliminates the only destructive operation**, raising the DI from 0.625 to a perfect 1.000 and the recovery rate to 100 %.
2. The second modification (part consolidation) **reduces the part count by 16.7 %** and shortens disassembly time by 16 %, while improving tool standardization — a direct application of DFD Rule 1 (minimize parts) and Rule 4 (standardize fasteners).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the 12 Golden Rules of Design for Disassembly. Categorize them under the four pillars of DFD. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 12 Golden Rules of DFD, grouped under the four pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Disassembly Strategy Selection:**
- Rule 1: **Minimize the number of parts** in the product.
- Rule 2: **Use a modular design** — break the product into independent subassemblies.

**Pillar 2 — Joining Method Selection (Reversibility):**
- Rule 3: **Prefer reversible fasteners** (bolts, screws) over permanent joints.
- Rule 8: **Avoid permanent joints** (welds, adhesives, rivets) where possible.

**Pillar 3 — Access and Tooling Provision:**
- Rule 5: **Provide clear tool access** for every joint.
- Rule 9: **Use captive fasteners** to prevent loss.
- Rule 11: **Design for single-tool disassembly** — one tool for the whole product.
- Rule 12: **Avoid nested assemblies** — each part ≤ 2 access steps.

**Pillar 4 — Identification, Sorting, and Material Compatibility:**
- Rule 4: **Standardize fasteners** — one size/type.
- Rule 7: **Mark components for identification** (color codes, RFID).
- Rule 10: **Reduce material variety** — facilitates recycling.
- Rule 6: **Use a single disassembly direction** (preferably vertical, gravity-aided).

**Diagrams/Drawings (as per KTU valuation):** A tabular grouping of all 12 rules with brief explanations of how each is applied in a real product (e.g., smartphone, washing machine) scores full marks.

---

**(b) A car dashboard has 24 components joined by a mix of bolts, snap-fits, and ultrasonic welds. Of the 24 joints, 10 are bolts (avg 20 s each), 8 are snap-fits (avg 8 s each), and 6 are ultrasonic welds (avg 90 s each). Compute the Disassembly Index. If regulations require DI ≥ 0.85, suggest a remediation strategy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the time for each joint type**

- Bolts: $10 \times 20 = 200$ s
- Snap-fits: $8 \times 8 = 64$ s
- Welds: $6 \times 90 = 540$ s

**Step 2 — Total Disassembly Time**

$$
T_d = 200 + 64 + 540 = 804 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Manual Disassembly Time** (bolts and snap-fits are reversible)

$$
T_m = 200 + 64 = 264 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Disassembly Index**

$$
DI = \frac{T_m}{T_d} = \frac{264}{804} = 0.328
$$

**Interpretation:** DI = 0.328 is far below the regulatory threshold of 0.85. The product is **non-compliant**.

**Step 5 — Remediation Strategy**

Replace the **6 ultrasonic welds** with **bolted joints** (avg 25 s each, slightly higher than original 20 s for serviceability).

- New bolt time: $6 \times 25 = 150$ s
- New manual time: $T_m^{\text{new}} = 200 + 64 + 150 = 414$ s
- New total time: $T_d^{\text{new}} = 414$ s (all operations are now manual)
- New DI: $414 / 414 = 1.000$ ✓

**Justification (in words):** The substitution of 6 ultrasonic welds with bolts raises the DI from 0.328 to 1.000, exceeding the 0.85 regulatory threshold by a wide margin. Although disassembly time per joint increases slightly (20 s → 25 s for bolts), the gain in **regulatory compliance, reusability, and recovery rate** far outweighs the small time penalty. This is a textbook example of DFD-driven design modification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DFD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Disassembly Index (DI) with Disassembly Efficiency (η_d).** DI uses *time weighting*; η_d uses *part count*. They are **not interchangeable**. The KTU board deducts 1 mark for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include the destructive operation time** in $T_d$ when computing DI. The denominator must include **all** operations, not just the manual ones.
>
> 3. **Writing "DFD = reverse of assembly"** without qualification. While DFD is the *inverse operation*, it is **not the inverse in time** — disassembly is often **slower** than assembly because of tool-access constraints and sorting requirements.
>
> 4. **Suggesting "use fewer fasteners"** as a DFD improvement. The correct framing is "**standardize and make reversible**" — *not* "remove" them. Structural integrity must be preserved.
>
> 5. **Ignoring regulatory mandates** (EU WEEE, End-of-Life Vehicle Directive) when answering real-world DFD questions. Always cite the relevant regulation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design for Disassembly (DFD)**

### Core Definitions
- **DFD** = design methodology for end-of-life recovery.
- **Disassembly (D)** = the physical operation of taking a product apart.
- **Destructive disassembly** = damages parts (e.g., cutting welds).
- **Non-destructive disassembly** = preserves all parts (e.g., unscrewing).

### The 12 Golden Rules
- Minimize parts · Modular design · Reversible fasteners · Standardize fasteners · Tool access · Single direction · Mark parts · Avoid permanent joints · Captive fasteners · Reduce material variety · Single-tool design · Avoid nesting.

### The 4 Pillars
- **Pillar 1:** Strategy (reuse → remanufacture → recycle → dispose).
- **Pillar 2:** Reversibility of joints.
- **Pillar 3:** Access and tooling.
- **Pillar 4:** Identification and sorting.

### Key Formulas (must memorize)
- $DI = (N_m \cdot t_m) / T_d$
- $\eta_d = (N_{\text{recovered}} / N_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$
- $R = (M_{\text{recovered}} / M_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$
- $RI = (N_{\text{reused}} / N_{\text{recovered}}) \times 100\%$
- $C_d = C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{energy}} + C_{\text{sorting}}$
- $DoD$ = hierarchical depth to target part

### Numerical Benchmarks
- **Good DFD:** $DI \geq 0.85$, $R \geq 85\%$.
- **Excellent DFD:** $DI \to 1.0$, $R \to 100\%$.
- **Poor DFD:** $DI < 0.5$, dominated by destructive operations.

### Common Mappings (Board-Exam Favorites)
| If the question asks… | The answer is… |
|---|---|
| Best joining method for DFD | Threaded captive fastener |
| Tooling strategy for DFD | Single tool, single direction |
| Disassembly strategy hierarchy | Reuse → Remanufacture → Recycle → Dispose |
| Metric for ease of disassembly | Disassembly Index (DI) |
| Penalty for destructive operations | Disposal cost + lost reusability |
| Regulatory driver | EU WEEE / EoLV Directive |

### Final Word
DFD is **not** a post-design activity — it is a **concurrent engineering** input that must be considered from the **conceptual stage** onward. The most powerful DFD improvements (as shown in the worked example) come from a **single design change at the right joint**, not from broad redesigns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Machining ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3.1)** > **Machining** is a *subtractive manufacturing process* in which a controlled layer of unwanted material is removed from a workpiece in the form of chips (or swarf) using a harder cutting tool, governed by relative motion, tool geometry, and machine-tool kinematics. The process is governed by standard parameters—**cutting speed (V)**, **feed (f)**, and **depth of cut (d)**—and is fundamental to achieving the **dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and geometric form** required in engineered components. In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, machining is one of the most *design-decision-critical* processes because **over 80% of manufactured components** in mechanical engineering require at least one machining operation to achieve their final functional geometry. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that *a designer must think like a machinist*—selecting features, tolerances, and materials that are *machinable*, *economical*, and *assembly-friendly*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sculptor and the Chisel" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy** > Imagine a **sculptor** carving a statue from a block of marble. The sculptor uses a **chisel (cutting tool)** that is harder than the marble (workpiece), applies a **force (cutting force)**, and moves the chisel at a certain **speed (cutting velocity)** while pushing it forward a small distance per stroke (**feed**). Chips of marble fly off — just like **swarf** in machining. The sculptor chooses the angle of the chisel (**rake angle**), the depth of each cut (**depth of cut**), and even lubricates it (**cutting fluid**). Now replace the marble with a steel block and the chisel with a carbide insert on a CNC lathe — that, in essence, is **machining**. ### 1.3 Why Machining is Indispensable in Modern Manufacturing Unlike *forming* (casting, forging) or *joining* (welding, riveting), machining uniquely offers: | Capability | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **High Dimensional Accuracy** | Tolerances down to **±0.005 mm** in precision grinding | | **Excellent Surface Finish** | Surface roughness **Ra 0.1 – 1.6 µm** achievable | | **Complex 3D Geometry** | Free-form surfaces via 5-axis CNC milling | | **Material Versatility** | From aluminum alloys to hardened tool steels (HRC 60+) | | **Repeatability** | Identical parts for mass production (Cpk > 1.33) | ### 1.4 The Role of Machining in the DFMA Philosophy > [!IMPORTANT] > In **Design for Machining (DFM)**, the KTU 2024 syllabus highlights three cardinal rules: > 1. **Standardize** — Use standard tool sizes, thread pitches, and hole diameters. > 2. **Minimize** — Reduce the number of machining operations, setups, and direction changes. > 3. **Simplify** — Avoid features that require special tooling or non-standard fixturing. A part that requires **12 set-ups and 8 special tools** is a *design failure*, even if the CAD geometry looks elegant. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanics of Machining — The Chip Formation Process When a cutting tool with a sharp wedge engages the workpiece, the material ahead of the tool is subjected to intense **compressive and shear stresses**. This causes the material to undergo: 1. **Elastic deformation** — Initial contact (recoverable). 2. **Plastic deformation** — Material flows plastically along the **shear plane**. 3. **Shear failure** — Material separates as a **chip** along the shear plane. 4. **Chip sliding** — The newly formed chip slides over the **tool face (rake face)** under friction. > [!NOTE] > **Shear Angle (φ):** The angle between the shear plane and the cutting velocity direction. It is the *single most important parameter* in chip formation theory — it dictates chip thickness, cutting force, and tool life. The **Merchant's Circle Diagram** (E. H. Merchant, 1945) is the classical analytical tool that relates: - Rake angle (α) - Shear angle (φ) - Friction angle (β) - Cutting force (Fc) - Thrust force (Ft) - Shear force (Fs) - Normal force on shear plane (N) - Chip thickness ratio (r) ### 2.2 Primary Cutting Parameters — The "Machining Trinity" | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Definition | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cutting Speed** | V | m/min | Surface speed at the cutting edge | **20 – 300 m/min** (steel), 500+ (aluminum) | | **Feed** | f | mm/rev (turning), mm/tooth (milling) | Tool advance per revolution/ tooth | **0.05 – 0.5 mm/rev** | | **Depth of Cut** | d | mm | Penetration into the workpiece | **0.5 – 6 mm** (roughing), < 0.5 (finishing) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Taylor Equation** (F. W. Taylor, 1907) is the *bedrock* of tool life analysis: > **V · T^n = C**, where **T** = tool life (min), **C** = constant, **n** = Taylor exponent (typically 0.2 – 0.5 for HSS, 0.3 – 0.6 for carbide). ### 2.3 Types of Chips — Diagnostic Indicators of Machining Health | Chip Type | Appearance | Indicates | Common With | |---|---|---|---| | **Continuous Chip** | Long, ribbon-like, no break | Ductile material, sharp tool, high V, low f | Mild steel, aluminum, copper | | **Discontinuous (Segmented) Chip** | Small, separated pieces | Brittle material, low V, high f, large d | Cast iron, bronze, hard alloys | | **Built-Up Edge (BUE) Chip** | Wavy with material welded to tool tip | Sticking friction, low V, inadequate coolant | Low-carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminum | | **Serrated (Saw-tooth) Chip** | Alternate high/low thickness | Periodic cracking, high strain-hardening | Titanium, high-temp alloys | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$ | Cutting speed | $D$ = work diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle RPM | | 2 | $$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$ | Spindle speed | — | | 3 | $$MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d$$ | Material Removal Rate (mm³/min) | V in mm/min (after conversion), f in mm/rev, d in mm | | 4 | $$MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$$ | MRR using V in m/min | yields **mm³/min** | | 5 | $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000}$$ | Cutting Power (kW) | $F_c$ in N, V in m/min | | 6 | $$F_c = K_c \cdot A_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$$ | Cutting Force (N) | $K_c$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²) | | 7 | $$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$ | Chip thickness ratio | $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness, $t_2$ = chip thickness | | 8 | $$T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$$ | Taylor's Tool Life Equation | $C$ = Taylor constant, $n$ = exponent | | 9 | $$Ra \approx \frac{f^2}{8 \cdot R}$$ | Theoretical Surface Roughness (turning) | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $R$ = nose radius (mm) | | 10 | $$\eta_m = \frac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}}$$ | Machining Efficiency | dimensionless (0 < η < 1) | > [!NOTE] > **Specific Cutting Energy ($K_c$) values (N/mm²)** — *high-yield for KTU problems*: > - Mild Steel: **1500 – 2200 N/mm²** > - Aluminum Alloys: **600 – 900 N/mm²** > - Cast Iron: **1000 – 1800 N/mm²** > - Stainless Steel: **2000 – 2800 N/mm²** > - Titanium Alloys: **1800 – 2500 N/mm²** ### 2.5 Tool Geometry — The 7-Element Reference System (ASA/ISO) A single-point cutting tool (e.g., lathe tool) has the following key angles, all measured in **standard tool-in-work reference planes**: - **Rake Angle (α / γ)** — Angle of tool face above the horizontal. Positive α → lower cutting force. - **Clearance Angle (β)** — Angle preventing tool flank from rubbing the workpiece. - **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ / κ)** — Angle of cutting edge w.r.t. feed direction. - **Nose Radius (rε)** — Small radius at the tool tip (affects surface finish and tool life). - **Side Rake, Back Rake, Side Clearance, End Clearance** — Refinements in 3D. - **Planar Approach Angle** — Influences chip flow and tool strength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Insight (KTU Module 3 emphasis):** A **larger positive rake angle** reduces cutting force and power but weakens the cutting edge. A **larger nose radius** improves surface finish but increases radial force and chatter tendency. *Trade-offs define the design of a tool and the design of a part.* ### 2.6 Tool Materials — Selection Hierarchy | Tool Material | Hardness (HRC) | Cutting Speed (m/min) | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** | 60 – 65 | 20 – 50 | Drills, taps, form tools, low-volume | | **Cemented Carbide (WC-Co)** | 75 – 90 | 100 – 300 | General turning, milling inserts | | **Coated Carbide (TiN, Al₂O₃, TiAlN)** | 80 – 92 | 150 – 400 | High productivity, dry machining | | **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄)** | 90 – 95 | 300 – 800 | High-speed finishing of cast iron, Ni-superalloys | | **CBN (Cubic Boron Nitride)** | 95+ | 200 – 1000 | Hardened steel (HRC > 50), high-V finishing | | **PCD (Polycrystalline Diamond)** | 95+ | 300 – 2000 | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, composites), abrasive composites | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning The **MRR** is the volume of material removed per unit time. In a turning operation on a cylindrical workpiece: **Given:** - Workpiece diameter = $D$ (mm) - Spindle speed = $N$ (rev/min) - Feed = $f$ (mm/rev) - Depth of cut = $d$ (mm) **Step 1 — Express the cross-sectional area of the cut (uncut chip cross-section):** The tool removes an annular ring of width $f$ and depth $d$. The area $A_c$ of this ring (in mm²) is: $$A_c = f \cdot d$$ **Step 2 — Express the linear velocity of the workpiece surface:** As the workpiece rotates, every point on the surface (at diameter $D$) traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution. Over one minute, the surface moves: $$V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the volume removed per unit time:** The tool engages the workpiece continuously. In one minute, a length $V$ of the workpiece passes under the tool, and from each mm of that length, an area $f \cdot d$ of material is removed. Hence: $$MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitution using cutting speed in m/min:** Since $V$ is often quoted in m/min, we convert: $$V \text{ (m/min)} \times 1000 = V \text{ (mm/min)}$$ Therefore: $$MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$ **Step 5 — Final compact form (KTU-favored):** $$\boxed{MRR = 1000 \, V \, f \, d \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Cutting Power and Cutting Force **Given:** - Specific cutting energy $K_c$ (N/mm²) - Uncut chip area $A_c = f \cdot d$ (mm²) - Cutting speed $V$ (m/min) **Step 1 — The fundamental cutting force relation:** The tangential (cutting) force $F_c$ is the resistance offered by the workpiece to shearing. Empirically, it is proportional to the uncut chip area: $$F_c = K_c \cdot A_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$$ **Step 2 — Relate power to force and velocity:** Power is the rate of doing work. The tool travels a circumferential distance per minute equal to $V$ (m), and the force resisting motion is $F_c$ (N). Hence power in Watts: $$P = F_c \cdot v \quad \text{(N} \cdot \text{m/s)}$$ But $V$ is in m/min, so to get m/s: $$v = \frac{V}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$ **Step 3 — Combine and convert to kilowatts:** $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $F_c$:** $$P_c = \frac{K_c \cdot f \cdot d \cdot V}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of MRR:** $$\boxed{P_c = \frac{K_c \cdot MRR}{60{,}000 \times 10^3} \quad \text{kW (if MRR in mm}^3\text{/min)}}$$ Or more cleanly, using the canonical form: $$\boxed{P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} \quad \text{kW}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern > **Problem (14-mark KTU type):** > A mild steel shaft of **75 mm diameter** is being turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is **120 m/min**, the feed is **0.3 mm/rev**, and the depth of cut is **2.5 mm**. The specific cutting energy is **2000 N/mm²**. Calculate: > (a) Spindle speed > (b) Material removal rate > (c) Cutting force > (d) Cutting power in kW **Solution:** **Part (a) — Spindle speed:** $$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 75}$$ $$N = \frac{120{,}000}{235.62} = 509.3 \quad \text{RPM}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 509 \text{ rev/min}}$$ **Part (b) — Material removal rate:** $$MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d = 1000 \times 120 \times 0.3 \times 2.5$$ $$MRR = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 90 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$ $$\boxed{MRR = 9 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$ **Part (c) — Cutting force:** $$F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d = 2000 \times 0.3 \times 2.5$$ $$F_c = 1500 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_c = 1.5 \text{ kN}}$$ **Part (d) — Cutting power:** $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} = \frac{1500 \times 120}{60{,}000}$$ $$P_c = \frac{180{,}000}{60{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_c = 3.0 \text{ kW}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engine Efficiency Assumption:** Real lathes are 70 – 85% efficient. Thus, **motor power** required = $P_c / \eta_m$ = $3.0 / 0.8$ ≈ **3.75 kW**. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Chip Thickness Ratio (r) **Given:** Uncut chip thickness $t_1$ (mm), chip thickness after cutting $t_2$ (mm). **Step 1 — Definition of ratio:** $$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2}$$ **Step 2 — Geometric relation from Merchant's circle:** By geometry of the shear plane (shear angle $\phi$ and rake angle $\alpha$): $$r = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$ **Step 3 — Significance:** - If $r < 1$ → chip is **thicker** than uncut thickness → **shear strain is large** → high cutting energy. - If $r \to 1$ → **shearing is efficient** → low energy, sharp tool, ductile material. - Higher **positive rake angle** → larger $\phi$ → larger $r$ → lower cutting force. $$\boxed{r = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}}$$ > This relation is *high-yield* for KTU Module 3 problems and is regularly tested. ## 4.
