KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** > *Define the term **Machining**. List any **four conventional machining processes** with one application of each.* **Model Answer (10 lines):** **Machining** is a *subtractive manufacturing process* in which material is removed from a workpiece in the form of chips using a harder cutting tool, by controlled relative motion. It is used to achieve desired **shape, size, surface finish, and tolerance**. Four conventional machining processes: 1. **Turning** — Producing cylindrical surfaces on a lathe (e.g., shaft diameter reduction). 2. **Milling** — Generating flat or contoured surfaces using multi-tooth rotating cutters (e.g., keyway, slot). 3. **Drilling** — Making round holes using a twist drill (e.g., bolt-hole in a flange). 4. **Grinding** — Removing material using an abrasive wheel for high accuracy and finish (e.g., bearing race finishing). --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** > *Explain the **three primary cutting parameters** in machining. State their typical units.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Cutting Speed (V)** — The relative surface speed between the tool and workpiece at the cutting edge. Unit: **m/min** (meters per minute) 2. **Feed (f)** — The distance the tool advances per revolution (in turning) or per tooth (in milling). Unit: **mm/rev** (turning), **mm/tooth** (milling) 3. **Depth of Cut (d)** — The radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece. Unit: **mm** These three parameters together control the **material removal rate, surface finish, cutting force, and tool life**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the **mechanics of chip formation** in metal cutting. Explain the **types of chips** formed during machining.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Derive the expression for **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in a turning operation. A mild steel component of 80 mm diameter is turned at a cutting speed of 150 m/min, with feed 0.25 mm/rev and depth of cut 3 mm. Calculate the **MRR, cutting force, and cutting power**. Take $K_c = 2000 \, N/mm^2$.* **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative)* **(a)** *Explain the **single-point cutting tool geometry** with reference to the **seven-element ASA system**. Discuss the effect of **rake angle** and **nose radius** on cutting performance.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Derive the relation between **shear angle, rake angle, and chip thickness ratio** using the Merchant circle diagram. State the **Taylor tool life equation** and discuss the effect of cutting speed on tool life.* **(7 marks)** --- ### **Detailed Model Solutions** #### **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Chip Formation Mechanics (7 marks):** When a sharp cutting tool engages the workpiece under a cutting velocity $V$ and feed $f$, the material in front of the tool is subjected to severe **compressive and shear stresses**. A narrow zone called the **primary deformation zone** forms immediately ahead of the tool tip. Within this zone: - Material undergoes **elastic then plastic deformation**. - The material **shears** along a plane called the **shear plane** (inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting direction). - The sheared material flows up the **rake face** of the tool, forming a **chip** — this constitutes the **secondary deformation zone** (high frictional contact with the rake face). - The remaining material below the tool (the freshly machined surface) undergoes **elastic recovery**, sometimes causing rubbing against the **clearance face** — the **tertiary deformation zone**. **Types of chips:** | Type | Description | Cause | Material | |---|---|---|---| | **Continuous** | Long, unbroken ribbon | Ductile, sharp tool, high V | Mild steel, Al | | **Discontinuous** | Segmented pieces | Brittle, low V, high d | Cast iron, bronze | | **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** | Weld-like deposit on tool | Sticking friction | Low-C steel, SS | | **Serrated** | Saw-tooth profile | Periodic cracking | Titanium alloys | --- **Part (b) — MRR Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** In turning, the uncut chip cross-section area is $A_c = f \cdot d$ (mm²). The workpiece surface moves at cutting velocity $V$ (in mm/min, after multiplying by 1000). Thus volume removed per minute: $$MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$ **Numerical Solution (3 marks):** **Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $V = 150$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, $d = 3$ mm, $K_c = 2000$ N/mm². (i) Spindle speed (if needed): $$N = \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{150{,}000}{251.33} \approx 597 \text{ RPM}$$ (ii) MRR: $$MRR = 1000 \times 150 \times 0.25 \times 3 = 112{,}500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$ (iii) Cutting force: $$F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d = 2000 \times 0.25 \times 3 = 1500 \text{ N} = 1.5 \text{ kN}$$ (iv) Cutting power: $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} = \frac{1500 \times 150}{60{,}000} = 3.75 \text{ kW}$$ --- #### **Solution to Question B (Alternative):** **Part (a) — Tool Geometry (7 marks):** The **ASA (now ISO) 7-element reference system** defines tool geometry in three mutually perpendicular reference planes: - **Reference plane (π)**: parallel to the work surface. - **Tool-in-work plane (π'):** vertical, perpendicular to reference plane. - **Cutting edge plane (π''):** contains the cutting edge, perpendicular to both. The seven elements are: 1. **Back Rake Angle (α_b)** — Inclination of rake face in longitudinal section. 2. **Side Rake Angle (α_s)** — Inclination of rake face in transverse section. 3. **End Clearance Angle** — Prevents flank rubbing. 4. **Side Clearance Angle** — Prevents flank rubbing on side. 5. **End Cutting Edge Angle** — Angle of major edge with feed direction. 6. **Side Cutting Edge Angle** — Angle of minor edge with feed direction. 7. **Nose Radius (rε)** — Roundness at the tool tip. **Effect of Rake Angle (α):** - **Positive α (high)** → Reduces cutting force, power, and chip thickness — but weakens cutting edge. - **Negative α** → Strengthens the edge, suitable for hard materials (e.g., carbide tooling on hard steel). **Effect of Nose Radius (rε):** - **Larger rε** → Better surface finish ($Ra \approx f^2 / 8R$). - **Larger rε** → Higher radial (deflection) force, more chatter risk on slender shafts. - **Smaller rε** → Lower radial force, but poorer surface finish. **Part (b) — Shear Angle, Taylor Equation (7 marks):** **Merchant's Analysis (4 marks):** By geometry of the chip formation process, the **chip thickness ratio** is: $$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$ where: - $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness - $t_2$ = chip thickness after cutting - $\phi$ = shear angle - $\alpha$ = rake angle A **larger positive α** → larger $\phi$ → larger r → less shear strain → lower cutting force. **Taylor's Tool Life Equation (3 marks):** $$\boxed{V \cdot T^n = C}$$ where: - $V$ = cutting speed (m/min) - $T$ = tool life (minutes) - $n$ = Taylor's exponent (typically 0.2 to 0.5 for HSS, 0.3 to 0.6 for carbide) - $C$ = constant (depends on tool-work material combination) Rearranging: $$T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Effect of Cutting Speed on Tool Life:** - **Doubling V** can reduce tool life by **5× to 10×** depending on $n$. - Higher V → higher cutting temperature → faster flank wear → shorter T. - This is why **high-speed machining** requires ceramic, CBN, or PCD tooling. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing m/min with mm/min in MRR formula — *always use 1000 × V × f × d when V is in m/min*. > 2. **Forgetting Taylor's exponent direction:** Some students write $V = (C/T)^{1/n}$ — *correct* — but swap $\phi$ and $\alpha$ in the Merchant equation. > 3. **Not stating the chip type for the material:** A general answer like "chips are formed" gets 0; the KTU key wants *types* with *causes*. > 4. **Confusing feed units:** In turning, feed is **mm/rev**; in milling, it is **mm/tooth**. Do not interchange. > 5. **Skipping the sign convention** in rake angle — a positive rake has the chip flowing *over* the tool face; negative rake has it flowing *into* the tool. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Module 3.1: Introduction to Machining** - [ ] **Machining = Subtractive process** using a *harder tool* on a *softer workpiece*. - [ ] **Primary parameters:** $V$ (m/min), $f$ (mm/rev or mm/tooth), $d$ (mm). - [ ] **MRR Formula:** $MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$ (mm³/min). - [ ] **Spindle Speed:** $N = 1000V / \pi D$. - [ ] **Cutting Force:** $F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$. - [ ] **Cutting Power:** $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60{,}000$ kW. - [ ] **Taylor's Equation:** $V T^n = C$ → Higher $V$ ⇒ Much lower $T$. - [ ] **Chip Thickness Ratio:** $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$. - [ ] **Four chip types:** Continuous, Discontinuous, BUE, Serrated. - [ ] **Tool material hierarchy:** HSS < Carbide < Coated Carbide < Ceramics < CBN < PCD. - [ ] **$K_c$ values (must memorize):** Mild Steel ≈ 2000, Al ≈ 800, CI ≈ 1500, SS ≈ 2500 N/mm². - [ ] **DFMA rule:** Always prefer *standard tool sizes* and *minimal setups* in machined parts. - [ ] **Surface finish in turning:** $Ra \approx f^2 / 8R$ — *larger nose radius = better finish*. - [ ] **Positive rake** = lower force, weaker edge; **Negative rake** = stronger edge, higher force. - [ ] **Built-Up Edge** = bad surface finish and tool wear — overcome by ↑V, ↓f, use coolant, coated tools. - [ ] **Merchant's circle** links: α, φ, β, r, $F_c$, $F_t$ — *must know geometric relationships*. - [ ] **Real lathes are 70 – 85% efficient** — divide $P_c$ by η to get motor power required. > **End of Module 3.1 — Introduction to Machining (PEMET522, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Recommended Materials for Machinability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recommended Materials for Machinability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machinability** is defined as the relative ease or difficulty with which a given material can be machined (cut) under specified conditions to obtain an acceptable surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and tool life. It is a **comparative, qualitative-quantitative index** that integrates tool wear, cutting forces, surface integrity, chip formation, and power consumption into a single engineering metric.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET522), the concept of **Recommended Materials for Machinability** is positioned within the **Design for Manufacture (DFM)** workflow, where the design engineer must pre-select work-piece materials that minimize manufacturing cost, energy consumption, and tool replacement cycles, while simultaneously ensuring functional performance.

The standard **reference baseline** is:
- **AISI 1112 steel (resulfurized, rephosphorized) = 100% machinability rating**.
- All other materials are rated as a percentage relative to this benchmark.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Cutting a Cake vs. Cutting a Hard Cheese"**
> Imagine slicing a soft sponge cake versus cutting a frozen block of aged Parmesan cheese.
> - The cake slices cleanly, forms neat crumbs, requires almost no pressure, and your knife stays sharp for a long time — this is **high machinability** (like **free-machining brass or AISI 1112 steel**).
> - The cheese cracks unevenly, sticks to the knife, requires heavy pressure, and dulls the knife quickly — this is **low machinability** (like **titanium or Inconel alloys**).
>
> **Recommended materials for machinability** are essentially the engineering equivalents of the cake — materials engineered (or naturally possessing) micro-structural features that allow a cutting tool to glide through them easily, break chips into manageable shapes, and leave a smooth surface.

## 1.3 Why Material Selection is the First Step in DFM

In a Design for Manufacture framework, material selection **precedes** the selection of:
1. Manufacturing process
2. Cutting tool geometry
3. Cutting parameters (speed, feed, depth of cut)
4. Coolant/lubrication strategy

A material with poor inherent machinability will increase:
- Specific cutting energy (J/mm³)
- Tool wear rate (mm³ tool wear per mm³ material removed)
- Cycle time
- Scrap rate (poor surface finish, dimensional deviation)

Therefore, the KTU syllabus specifically asks the design engineer to **preferentially select materials that have high machinability ratings**, provided functional requirements (strength, corrosion resistance, fatigue life) are not compromised.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Machinability Rating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machinability Rating (MR)** is defined mathematically as:
>
> $$MR = \frac{V_{60} \text{ of test material}}{V_{60} \text{ of AISI 1112 steel (reference)}} \times 100\%$$
>
> Where **V₆₀** is the cutting speed (in m/min) at which a standard HSS tool achieves a **60-minute tool life** under standard cutting conditions.

The percentage rating directly indicates how fast one can cut the material relative to the reference.

## 1.5 Common Standards and Codes

| Standard Body | Designation | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **AISI / SAE** | 4-digit steel grades (11xx, 12xx, etc.) | Carbon & alloy steels |
| **ISO 3685** | Tool-life testing standard | Single-point turning |
| **DIN EN 10087** | Free-cutting steels | European designation |
| **JIS G 4804** | Sulfurized free-cutting steels | Japanese standard |

**Key Constants/Metrics to Remember:**
- **AISI 1112 machinability = 100%**
- **Cutting speed V₆₀ reference (AISI 1112) ≈ 30–55 m/min (HSS tool)**
- **Surface roughness unit: Ra (μm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Machinability

The machinability of a material is governed by **five interrelated factors** (KTU textbook reference: Groover, "Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing"):

1. **Tool Life** — Time before tool reaches a flank-wear criterion (e.g., VB = 0.3 mm).
2. **Cutting Forces & Power** — Lower forces → less energy, less deflection.
3. **Surface Finish** — Function of built-up-edge (BUE) formation.
4. **Chip Shape** — Continuous chips are bad (entangle tools); segmented/short chips are good.
5. **Cutting Temperature** — Affects tool wear, BUE, and dimensional accuracy.

## 2.2 Material Properties that Influence Machinability

| Property | Effect on Machinability | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** (HB / HRC) | Optimum at ~150–250 HB | Too soft = BUE; Too hard = rapid tool wear |
| **Tensile Strength** | Inversely related to MR | High strength → high cutting forces |
| **Microstructure** | Ferritic/Pearlitic > Martensitic | Hard phases (carbides) abrade tool |
| **Thermal Conductivity** | Higher = Better | Heat dissipates → tool life increases |
| **Work-Hardening Rate** | Lower = Better | Austenitic SS, Ti, Ni alloys are problematic |
| **Chemical Affinity to Tool** | Lower = Better | Ti reacts with WC tools → crater wear |

## 2.3 Free-Machining Additives (Most Important Section for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free-machining steels** are carbon or alloy steels to which one or more additives (S, Pb, P, Bi, Te, Se) have been deliberately added to enhance machinability.

### 2.3.1 Sulfur (S) — 0.08% to 0.30%
- Forms **Manganese Sulfide (MnS) inclusions** in the steel matrix.
- MnS inclusions act as **stress concentrators** that promote chip breaking.
- MnS also provides **solid lubrication** at the tool-chip interface.
- **Resulfurized steels**: AISI 1110, 1117, 1118, 1119, **1120**, **1126**, **1132**, **1137**, **1140**, **1141**, **1144**, **1146**, **1151**.

### 2.3.2 Lead (Pb) — 0.15% to 0.35%
- Lead is **insoluble in iron** and exists as discrete, finely dispersed globules.
- Melting point of Pb = **327 °C** (low for a metal).
- During cutting, the frictional heat melts the lead globules, providing **liquid-metal lubrication** at the rake face.
- Reduces **built-up edge (BUE)** formation → improves surface finish.
- **Leaded steels**: AISI 12L13, 12L14, 12L15.
- **Concern**: Lead is toxic — restricted under **RoHS / REACH** directives. Modern alternatives: **Bi** and **Te**.

### 2.3.3 Phosphorus (P) — 0.04% to 0.12%
- Strengthens the **ferrite phase** by solid-solution strengthening.
- Promotes **short, brittle chips** at higher cutting speeds.
- Often combined with sulfur: **Rephosphorized & Resulfurized (RRS)** steels.

### 2.3.4 Bismuth (Bi) — 0.05% to 0.20%
- Environmentally friendly **substitute for lead**.
- Low melting point = **271 °C**.
- Behaves similarly to Pb but is non-toxic.

### 2.3.5 Tellurium (Te) and Selenium (Se) — <0.05%
- Chemically similar to S; form MnTe / MnSe inclusions.
- Used in **free-machining stainless steels** (e.g., AISI 303, 416).

### 2.3.6 Manganese (Mn) — Critical for Sulfur Steels
- Mn must be present in **2.5× to 5× the S content** to ensure sulfur forms **MnS** and not **FeS** (which causes hot shortness / hot cracking during rolling).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** required for KTU university exam questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$MR = \frac{V_{60,\text{test}}}{V_{60,\text{ref}}} \times 100\%$$ | MR = Machinability Rating, V₆₀ = cutting speed at 60-min tool life | Comparing two materials' machinability |
| 2 | $$F_c = k_s \cdot A_c$$ | F_c = cutting force (N), k_s = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), A_c = undeformed chip area (mm²) | Calculating power |
| 3 | $$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60000}$$ | P_c = power (kW), V_c = cutting speed (m/min) | Sizing machine tool motor |
| 4 | $$\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$$ | MRR = Material Removal Rate (mm³/min), f = feed (mm/rev), d = depth of cut (mm) | Production rate estimation |
| 5 | $$\text{CT} = \frac{\text{MRR}}{V_c \cdot f \cdot d} \times \text{cycle factor}$$ | CT = cycle time | Estimating part cost |
| 6 | $$V_c \cdot T^n = C \text{ (Taylor's tool life equation)}$$ | T = tool life (min), n = exponent, C = constant | Predicting tool life at different speeds |
| 7 | $$n_{\text{op}} = 2.5 \text{ to } 5 \text{ (Mn:S ratio)}$$ | Mn, S in wt% | Ensuring MnS formation |
| 8 | $$\%S_{\max} \approx 0.30\%, \quad \%Pb_{\max} \approx 0.35\%$$ | Composition limits | Specifying free-machining steel |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Lead-free free-machining steels (Bi/Te based) for transmission gears, shafts, fasteners — reduces cycle time by **20–40%** vs. plain carbon steel.
- **Hydraulics & Pneumatics**: Free-machining steels for valve bodies, manifolds — deep drilling and threading are critical.
- **Electrical Components**: Brass C36000 (free-machining brass) is the most machined copper alloy in the world — 100% rating.
- **Aerospace**: Despite excellent strength, Ti-6Al-4V is notoriously difficult to machine (~20% MR); designers must justify the cost penalty.
- **Medical Devices**: Free-machining stainless steel 303 is preferred for surgical instruments due to **chip breakability and lower tool change frequency**.

## 2.6 Recommended Material Categories (DFM Selection Hierarchy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Priority for Machining-Based Designs (KTU DFM Rule):**
>
> 1. **Best**: Free-machining brass (C360), Aluminum 2011, AISI 12L14
> 2. **Good**: AISI 1118, 1144, 1215, 12114 (leaded resulfurized)
> 3. **Acceptable**: AISI 1040, 4140 pre-hardened (HB 250–300)
> 4. **Difficult**: 17-4 PH SS, AISI 304, AISI 316
> 5. **Very Difficult**: Inconel 718, Waspaloy, Ti-6Al-4V
> 6. **Avoid for Machining**: WC, ceramics, hardened tool steels (>55 HRC)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Machinability Rating from Tool-Life Data

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows how the **100% baseline** is established and how a new material's rating is calculated from experimental cutting data. This is a **frequently asked KTU question** (Part A 3-mark type).

### Given:
- Reference material: **AISI 1112 steel** with HSS single-point tool.
- Test material: **Free-machining steel AISI 12L14**.
- Tool-life criterion: **Flank wear VB = 0.3 mm**.
- Cutting conditions: Feed f = 0.25 mm/rev, Depth of cut d = 2.5 mm.

### Experimental Tool-Life Data (Hypothetical):

| Cutting Speed V_c (m/min) | Tool Life of AISI 1112 (min) | Tool Life of AISI 12L14 (min) |
|---|---|---|
| 30 | 60 (reference) | 120 |
| 45 | 18 | 40 |
| 60 | 5 | 14 |
| 75 | 2 | 6 |

### Step 1: Identify V₆₀ for AISI 1112
By definition, V₆₀ for AISI 1112 = **30 m/min** (the speed that gives exactly 60 min of tool life).

### Step 2: Identify V₆₀ for AISI 12L14
From the test data, the tool life drops to 60 min at a cutting speed higher than 30 m/min.
We use **Taylor's tool-life equation** to interpolate:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_c \cdot T^n &= C \\
\Rightarrow \quad \ln T &= n \cdot \ln C - n \cdot \ln V_c
\end{aligned}$$

This is a linear relationship in log-log scale. Using two points for 12L14:

$$\begin{aligned}
(30, 120) \quad &\Rightarrow \quad 30 \times 120^n = C_{12L14} \\
(75, 6) \quad &\Rightarrow \quad 75 \times 6^n = C_{12L14}
\end{aligned}$$

Equating:

$$\begin{aligned}
30 \times 120^n &= 75 \times 6^n \\
\frac{120^n}{6^n} &= \frac{75}{30} \\
20^n &= 2.5
\end{aligned}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\begin{aligned}
n \cdot \ln(20) &= \ln(2.5) \\
n &= \frac{\ln(2.5)}{\ln(20)} = \frac{0.9163}{2.9957} = 0.3059
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Calculate C for 12L14

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{12L14} &= 30 \times 120^{0.3059} \\
&= 30 \times e^{0.3059 \times \ln(120)} \\
&= 30 \times e^{0.3059 \times 4.7875} \\
&= 30 \times e^{1.4644} \\
&= 30 \times 4.326 \\
&= 129.78
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Find V₆₀ for 12L14 (i.e., the speed giving T = 60 min)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{60,12L14} \times (60)^{0.3059} &= 129.78 \\
V_{60,12L14} &= \frac{129.78}{60^{0.3059}} \\
60^{0.3059} &= e^{0.3059 \times \ln(60)} = e^{0.3059 \times 4.0943} = e^{1.2528} = 3.500 \\
V_{60,12L14} &= \frac{129.78}{3.500} = 37.08 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute Machinability Rating

$$\begin{aligned}
MR_{12L14} &= \frac{V_{60,12L14}}{V_{60,1112}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{37.08}{30} \times 100\% \\
&= 123.6\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Interpretation:
AISI 12L14 has a machinability rating of **~124%** — meaning you can cut it **24% faster** than AISI 1112 and still get the same tool life. This is why **leaded free-machining steels** are highly preferred in DFM.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection for DFM

| Material | MR (%) | Recommended? | Key Reason | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum 2011 (T3) | ~300 | ✅ Excellent | Low hardness, free-machining grade | Aerospace fittings |
| Brass C36000 | 100 (own ref) | ✅ Excellent | Free graphite-like effect, short chips | Plumbing, electrical |
| AISI 12L14 | ~124 | ✅ Excellent | Pb lubrication, short chips | Bushings, fittings |
| AISI 1144 | ~116 | ✅ Very Good | High S, no Pb (RoHS compliant) | Shafts, studs |
| AISI 1118 | ~80 | ✅ Good | Moderate S, machinable | General machined parts |
| AISI 1040 | ~60 | ⚠️ Acceptable | Medium carbon, decent tool life | Shafts, gears |
| AISI 4140 (annealed) | ~50 | ⚠️ Acceptable | Cr-Mo, higher strength | Critical structural |
| AISI 304 SS | ~25 | ❌ Poor | Work hardening, low cond. | Must use sharp tools |
| AISI 316 SS | ~20 | ❌ Poor | Mo addition worsens mach. | Corrosion-critical |
| Ti-6Al-4V | ~20 | ❌ Very Poor | High chem. reactivity, low cond. | Aerospace, medical |
| Inconel 718 | ~10 | ❌ Extremely Poor | Work hardening, hot strength | Jet engines |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Power Calculation Using k_s

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked KTU 14-mark question** combining material selection with cutting parameters.

### Problem:
A shaft is to be machined from **AISI 12L14 free-machining steel** in a turning operation. 
Cutting speed V_c = 45 m/min, feed f = 0.3 mm/rev, depth of cut d = 2.0 mm. 
Specific cutting energy k_s for 12L14 ≈ **2000 N/mm²**. 
Calculate the cutting force and required power.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate undeformed chip area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_c &= f \times d \\
A_c &= 0.3 \text{ mm} \times 2.0 \text{ mm} \\
A_c &= 0.6 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate cutting force**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_s \times A_c \\
F_c &= 2000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 0.6 \text{ mm}^2 \\
F_c &= 1200 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate material removal rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= V_c \times f \times d \\
\text{MRR} &= 45 \text{ m/min} \times 0.3 \text{ mm/rev} \times 2.0 \text{ mm} \\
\text{MRR} &= 45 \times 0.6 = 27 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \times 1000 \text{ (unit conversion)} \\
\text{MRR} &= 27{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate power required at the spindle**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{F_c \times V_c}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW, with } V_c \text{ in m/min)} \\
P_c &= \frac{1200 \text{ N} \times 45 \text{ m/min}}{60{,}000} \\
P_c &= \frac{54{,}000}{60{,}000} \\
P_c &= 0.9 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Select machine motor (with 80% efficiency)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{motor}} &= \frac{P_c}{0.80} = 1.125 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

A standard **1.5 kW or 2.0 kW** lathe motor would be sufficient.

### Comparative Note:
If the same part were machined in **AISI 304 SS** (k_s ≈ 3500 N/mm²):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= 3500 \times 0.6 = 2100 \text{ N} \quad (\uparrow 75\%) \\
P_c &= \frac{2100 \times 45}{60{,}000} = 1.575 \text{ kW} \quad (\uparrow 75\%)
\end{aligned}$$

**Design Insight**: Switching from 304 SS to 12L14 reduces both the cutting force and power demand by **~43%**, justifying the material substitution in DFM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Material Selection Logic for Machinability (DFM Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Functional Requirements] --> B{Strength Required?}
    B -- Low to Medium --> C{Corrosion Required?}
    B -- High --> D[Alloy Steels AISI 4140 4340]
    C -- No --> E[Free Machining Carbon Steels]
    C -- Yes --> F[Free Machining SS AISI 303 416]
    E --> G[Add Sulfur S 0.08 to 0.30 percent]
    G --> G1{AISI 11xx Series}
    G1 --> H{Lead Allowed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[AISI 12L14 MR 124 percent]
    H -- No --> J[AISI 1144 1215 Bi or Te]
    I --> K[Optimized for Machinability]
    J --> K
    F --> K
    D --> L{Machining Major Process?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Specify Pre Hardened 28 to 32 HRC]
    L -- No --> N[Specify Annealed for Machining]
    M --> O[Final Material Selection]
    N --> O
    K --> O
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: How Free-Machining Additives Work During Cutting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Steel Matrix] --> R2[MnS Inclusions]
    R1 --> R3[Pb Bi Globules]
    R2 --> S1[Stress Concentrators]
    S1 --> S2[Chip Fractures Easily]
    S2 --> S3[Short Discontinuous Chips]
    R3 --> T1[Heat from Friction]
    T1 --> T2[Pb Melts at 327C]
    T2 --> T3[Liquid Metal Lubrication]
    T3 --> T4[Reduces Built Up Edge]
    T4 --> T5[Better Surface Finish]
    S3 --> T6[Longer Tool Life]
    T5 --> T6
    T6 --> OUT[Optimized Machinability]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Rating Chart (Mermaid Bar Chart Equivalent via Block Width)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[AISI 12L14 MR 124 percent]:::high
    A1[Brass C360 MR 100 percent]:::high
    A2[AISI 1144 MR 116 percent]:::high
    A3[AISI 1112 MR 100 percent]:::ref
    A4[AISI 1040 MR 60 percent]:::medium
    A5[AISI 4140 MR 50 percent]:::medium
    A6[AISI 304 SS MR 25 percent]:::low
    A7[Ti6Al4V MR 20 percent]:::low
    A8[Inconel 718 MR 10 percent]:::low

    classDef high fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#27ae60,color:#fff
    classDef ref fill:#3498db,stroke:#2980b9,color:#fff
    classDef medium fill:#f39c12,stroke:#e67e22,color:#fff
    classDef low fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Architecture: Interaction Between Material Properties and Machining Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAT[Material Inputs]
        M1[Chemical Composition]
        M2[Hardness]
        M3[Microstructure]
        M4[Thermal Conductivity]
        M5[Work Hardening Tendency]
    end

    subgraph PRO[Process Parameters]
        P1[Cutting Speed Vc]
        P2[Feed f]
        P3[Depth of Cut d]
        P4[Coolant Type]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Performance Outputs]
        O1[Tool Life T]
        O2[Surface Finish Ra]
        O3[Chip Shape]
        O4[Cutting Force Fc]
        O5[Power Pc]
    end

    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O4
    M3 --> O2
    M4 --> O1
    M5 --> O1
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O4
    P4 --> O2
    O1 --> OUTfinal[Machinability Rating]
    O2 --> OUTfinal
    O3 --> OUTfinal
    O4 --> OUTfinal
    O5 --> OUTfinal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Machinability. List any four material properties that influence it.**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Machinability is the relative ease with which a material can be cut to obtain an acceptable surface finish and tool life under standard cutting conditions. (1 mark)

Four material properties that influence machinability:
1. **Hardness** — Optimum around 150–250 HB for best performance. (0.5 mark)
2. **Thermal conductivity** — Higher conductivity improves heat dissipation, increases tool life. (0.5 mark)
3. **Work-hardening tendency** — Low work-hardening improves machinability. (0.5 mark)
4. **Microstructure** — Ferritic/pearlitic steels machine better than martensitic. (0.5 mark)

---

### **Q2. What are the roles of Sulfur and Lead in free-machining steels?**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Sulfur (S):** Added up to 0.08–0.30 wt%, combines with Mn to form **MnS inclusions** that act as stress concentrators, promoting **short, brittle chip formation** and providing solid lubrication. (1.5 marks)

**Lead (Pb):** Added up to 0.15–0.35 wt% as dispersed globules. During cutting, frictional heat melts the lead (m.p. 327 °C), providing **liquid-metal lubrication** at the tool-chip interface, reducing **built-up edge (BUE)** and improving surface finish. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** (either A or B). Both are given below; students answer ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`**

> **(a)** Explain the **concept of machinability rating** with AISI 1112 steel as the reference. Show how the rating is calculated from tool-life data. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of free-machining additives** (S, Pb, P, Bi) in enhancing machinability of carbon steels. Mention the typical AISI grade designations. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept and Calculation of Machinability Rating (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Machinability rating is a comparative index expressed as a percentage of a reference material's cutting speed (V₆₀) at a standardized tool life of 60 minutes. **AISI 1112 steel is the universally accepted reference** (MR = 100%) because it is a low-carbon, resulfurized, rephosphorized steel that is easy to machine.

- **Mathematical Definition (2 marks):**
  $$MR = \frac{V_{60,\text{test}}}{V_{60,\text{AISI 1112}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Calculation Procedure (3 marks):**
  1. Perform turning tests on the candidate material at **multiple cutting speeds** while keeping feed (f) and depth of cut (d) constant.
  2. Record tool life (T) at each speed until flank wear reaches VB = 0.3 mm.
  3. Plot log(V_c) vs. log(T) — straight line (Taylor's equation).
  4. Read V₆₀ (the speed that gives 60 min tool life) by interpolation.
  5. Apply the formula above.

  *Example:* If a steel exhibits V₆₀ = 75 m/min, its MR = (75/30) × 100% = **250%** — meaning it can be cut 2.5× faster than AISI 1112.

**(b) Free-Machining Additives (7 marks):**

| Additive | Wt% Range | Function | Typical AISI Grade |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sulfur (S)** | 0.08–0.30% | Forms **MnS inclusions**; chip breaker, solid lubricant | **AISI 1117, 1119, 1126, 1141, 1144** |
| **Lead (Pb)** | 0.15–0.35% | Liquid-metal lubrication at tool-chip interface; reduces BUE | **AISI 12L13, 12L14, 12L15** |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | 0.04–0.12% | Solid-solution strengthens ferrite; produces brittle chips | Often combined with S (AISI 1212, 1215) |
| **Bismuth (Bi)** | 0.05–0.20% | Eco-friendly substitute for Pb; melts at 271 °C | Used in lead-free free-machining steels |
| **Tellurium (Te) / Se** | <0.05% | Chemically similar to S; used in SS grades | **AISI 303, 416, 420F** |

**Critical Composition Rule (1 mark):** To ensure sulfur forms MnS (and not the hot-shortness-causing FeS), the **Mn:S weight ratio must be at least 2.5:1 to 5:1**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple Paper]`**

> **(a)** "Material selection is the first and most critical step in Design for Machining." Justify the statement with reference to **AISI 12L14 vs. AISI 304 SS** as candidate materials for a precision shaft, supported by quantitative comparison. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **influence of microstructure, hardness, and work-hardening tendency** on machinability. Use cast iron and austenitic stainless steel as contrasting examples. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Justification with Comparative Quantification (7 marks):**

- **Opening Statement (1 mark):** Material selection precedes all other DFM decisions because material properties dictate the achievable cutting speed, tool life, and surface finish — directly affecting **manufacturing cost and part quality**.

- **Property Comparison (3 marks):**

| Property | AISI 12L14 (Leaded Free-Machining) | AISI 304 SS (Austenitic) |
|---|---|---|
| Machinability Rating | **~124%** | **~25%** |
| Specific Cutting Energy k_s | ~2000 N/mm² | ~3500 N/mm² |
| Work-Hardening Tendency | Low | **High (severe)** |
| Hardness (annealed) | ~130 HB | ~200 HB |
| Thermal Conductivity | ~50 W/m·K | ~16 W/m·K |
| Tool Cost (relative) | 1× | **4–6×** |

- **Quantitative Impact (3 marks):** For identical cutting parameters (V_c = 40 m/min, f = 0.2 mm/rev, d = 2 mm):
  - Chip area: A_c = 0.2 × 2 = 0.4 mm².
  - F_c (12L14) = 2000 × 0.4 = **800 N**.
  - F_c (304 SS) = 3500 × 0.4 = **1400 N** (75% higher).
  - Power (12L14) = (800 × 40)/60 000 = **0.53 kW**.
  - Power (304 SS) = (1400 × 40)/60 000 = **0.93 kW**.
  - Tool life at 40 m/min: 12L14 → ~120 min; 304 SS → ~5 min.

- **Conclusion (1 mark):** Choosing 12L14 reduces cutting force by 43%, power by 43%, and extends tool life by 24×. The statement is fully justified.

**(b) Microstructure, Hardness & Work-Hardening (7 marks):**

- **Microstructure (2.5 marks):**
  - **Gray Cast Iron**: Contains **graphite flakes** in a ferritic matrix. Graphite acts as an **internal chip-breaker and lubricant**, producing discontinuous chips and very low tool wear. **MR ≈ 50–80%** depending on pearlite content.
  - **White Cast Iron**: Fe₃C (cementite) network — **extremely abrasive** to cutting tools. MR ≈ 10–20%.
  - **Austenitic SS (304)**: Single-phase FCC austenite — **tough and ductile**; tends to form **long stringy chips** and a large built-up edge (BUE).

- **Hardness (2 marks):**
  - **Optimum**: 150–250 HB. Below 100 HB → soft material sticks to tool (BUE). Above 350 HB → rapid abrasive wear.
  - Gray cast iron typically ~180–220 HB → in the optimum range.

- **Work-Hardening (2.5 marks):**
  - Austenitic SS (304) work-hardens rapidly due to **austenite → martensite transformation** under tool pressure. The "hardened layer" beneath the cut surface can reach **400+ HV**, causing rapid flank wear.
  - Gray cast iron does not work-harden (graphite flakes interrupt dislocation motion).
  - *Engineering Implication*: For 304 SS, design engineers must specify **sharper tools, lower feed, and rigid setups** — all of which increase cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students **commonly lose marks** in the following areas:
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the baseline**: Always state **AISI 1112 = 100%** before any rating calculation. (–0.5 mark)
> 2. **❌ Confusing Sulfur and Lead roles**: Sulfur = chip breaker (mechanical). Lead = liquid lubricant (thermal). Do NOT interchange. (–1 mark)
> 3. **❌ Skipping the Mn:S ratio rule**: Forgetting that Mn must be ≥ 2.5× S to prevent hot shortness. (–0.5 mark)
> 4. **❌ Mixing units in Taylor's equation**: V_c must be in **m/min**; tool life T in **minutes** for the standard form. (–0.5 mark)
> 5. **❌ Not specifying AISI grade numbers**: KTU examiners expect **exact AISI designations** (e.g., 12L14, 1144) — not generic "free-machining steel". (–1 mark)
> 6. **❌ Ignoring RoHS compliance**: Modern exam questions may ask about **lead-free alternatives** (Bi, Te). Mention them for full marks. (–0.5 mark)
> 7. **❌ Forgetting that AISI 12L14 has 124% rating**: A common number to remember; the >100% rating reflects the **lead-induced lubricity** benefit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Print Before Exam**

- ⭐ **AISI 1112 = 100% baseline** for machinability rating.
- ⭐ **MR formula**: MR = (V₆₀,test / V₆₀,1112) × 100%.
- ⭐ **Sulfur** → MnS inclusions → chip breaking (mechanical action).
- ⭐ **Lead** → liquid-metal lubrication at 327 °C (thermal action).
- ⭐ **Mn:S ratio must be 2.5:1 to 5:1** to avoid FeS and hot shortness.
- ⭐ **Phosphorus** strengthens ferrite → brittle chips.
- ⭐ **Bismuth (Bi)** and **Tellurium (Te)** are modern **RoHS-compliant** Pb substitutes.
- ⭐ **AISI 12L14 = 124% MR** (highest among standard free-machining steels).
- ⭐ **Optimum hardness for machining = 150–250 HB**.
- ⭐ **Higher thermal conductivity = better machinability** (Al > Steel > SS > Ti).
- ⭐ **Graphite in cast iron** = natural chip-breaker & lubricant.
- ⭐ **Austenitic SS (304, 316)** has low MR (~25%) due to work-hardening.
- ⭐ **Ti-6Al-4V (~20%) and Inconel (~10%)** — avoid if machining is primary process.
- ⭐ **Free-machining brass C36000** = benchmark for non-ferrous machinability.
- ⭐ **DFM rule**: Specify free-machining grades unless functional requirements forbid it.
- ⭐ **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is the main surface-finish killer in soft ductile materials.
- ⭐ **Taylor's tool-life equation**: V_c · T^n = C (where n ≈ 0.25 for HSS, 0.5 for carbide).
- ⭐ **Specific cutting energy k_s** units = **N/mm²** (or J/mm³).
- ⭐ **Power formula**: P_c (kW) = F_c (N) × V_c (m/min) / 60 000.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining Using Single-Point Cutting Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machining Using Single-Point Cutting Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT):** A machining tool with one principal cutting edge designed to remove material from a workpiece through a controlled shearing action. It is the fundamental tool geometry from which all complex cutting tool designs (drills, milling cutters, boring bars) are kinematically and geometrically derived.

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, understanding single-point cutting tool mechanics is non-negotiable because it directly governs:

- **Cutting Force (Fc):** Determines machine tool power selection, fixture design load, and structural deflection.
- **Surface Finish (Ra):** Influences post-machining assembly tolerances and mating part fit.
- **Tool Life (T):** Controls the cost-of-quality and the "Design for Manufacturing" economics of the part.
- **Heat Generation (θ):** Affects dimensional accuracy via thermal expansion of the workpiece.

The two canonical reference configurations analyzed in KTU syllabi are:

| Configuration | Cutting Edge Orientation | Plane of Analysis | Industrial Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Orthogonal Cutting** | Perpendicular to cutting velocity | 2-D (rake plane) | Theoretical benchmarking, turning with zero inclination |
| **Oblique Cutting** | Inclined at an angle (i) to velocity | 3-D (requires force decomposition) | All real turning, drilling, milling operations |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wood Plane" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a carpenter using a hand plane on a wooden plank. The blade (single-point cutting tool) has a sharp edge that slices off a thin wood shaving (chip). The carpenter pushes the plane horizontally (cutting velocity, V), and a thin wood ribbon curls up over the blade (chip flow angle). The harder the wood, the more force the carpenter must apply (cutting force). If the blade gets dull, the carpenter must push harder and the wood gets burnt (tool wear → heat).

In machining:
- The **workpiece** is the wood.
- The **cutting tool** is the plane blade.
- The **chip** is the wood shaving curling over the tool.
- The **cutting force** is the force the carpenter applies.
- The **rake angle (α)** is the tilt of the blade — more tilt = easier cutting.
- The **feed (f)** is the thickness of the shaving.
- The **depth of cut (d)** is how deep the plane bites into the wood.

## 1.3 Tool Geometry — The Seven Standard Angles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A single-point tool is defined by **seven angles** in a standard reference system (ISO 3002/1). For DFMA design, only the **rake angle (α)** and **clearance angle (γ)** are first-order design parameters; the remaining five are secondary but govern chip flow direction and surface finish.

| Symbol | Angle | Function | Typical Range (Steel) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **α (alpha)** | Rake (positive/negative) | Reduces cutting energy, controls chip flow | $+5°$ to $+20°$ |
| **γ (gamma)** | Clearance (primary) | Prevents tool flank rubbing the workpiece | $6°$ to $15°$ |
| **i** | Inclination | Tilts edge out of the rake plane | $-5°$ to $+5°$ |
| **λs** | Cutting edge angle | Positions edge for chip breaker | $30°$ to $75°$ |
| **χr** | Side cutting edge angle | Controls feed direction force | $15°$ to $30°$ |
| **χ'r** | End cutting edge angle | Prevents tool contact on finished surface | $5°$ to $15°$ |
| **η** | Nose radius (not angle) | Affects surface finish & tool life | $0.4$ to $1.6$ mm |

## 1.4 Standard Nomenclature (ISO 3685)

- **Cutting Velocity (V):** The instantaneous surface speed of the workpiece at the cutting point. **Units: m/min.**
- **Feed (f):** The axial advance of the tool per spindle revolution (in turning). **Units: mm/rev.**
- **Depth of Cut (d):** The radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece. **Units: mm.**
- **Uncut Chip Thickness (t₁):** The thickness of material before the tool removes it. In turning, **t₁ = f** (for sharp tools).
- **Chip Thickness (t₂):** The thickness after the chip is formed. The ratio **r = t₂/t₁** is the **chip thickness ratio**.
- **Cutting Time (Tc):** Time to machine a length L, given by **Tc = L / (f · N)** where N is spindle RPM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanics of Chip Formation

When the tool advances into the workpiece, the material ahead of the tool is compressed and shears along a primary shear plane. This produces a continuous chip whose thickness increases as it slides up the rake face.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shear Zone:** Material does not shear along a single line — it shears within a finite **shear zone** of thickness ~0.025 to 0.5 mm. For KTU analytical problems, this zone is approximated as a single plane (the **shear plane**) defined by the **shear angle φ**.

### 2.1.1 Geometric Chip Thickness Relationship

The fundamental geometric identity linking chip thickness ratio (r), rake angle (α), and shear angle (φ) is:

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A larger shear angle (φ) means the material shears more easily → less cutting energy → lower temperatures → longer tool life. Designers prefer tools and cutting conditions that maximize φ.

## 2.2 The Merchant Circle — Force Analysis (KTU's Most Tested Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Merchant Force Circle** is the single most examinable diagram in Module 3. It is a 2-D free-body diagram of the chip on the tool's rake face showing four forces in equilibrium.

### 2.2.1 The Four Forces

| Symbol | Force | Acts On | Magnitude Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fc** | Cutting force (main force) | Tool, in V direction | Determines power: **P = Fc · V** |
| **Ft** | Thrust/Feed force | Tool, perpendicular to V | Drives feed mechanism sizing |
| **F** | Resultant force (Fc + Ft) | Rake face | Total load on tool |
| **Fs** | Shear force | Along shear plane | Shears the material |
| **N** | Normal force | Perpendicular to shear plane | Reaction to Fs |
| **R** | Friction resultant (= F) | Along friction plane | Friction at tool-chip interface |

### 2.2.2 Friction Angle (β) and Shear Angle (φ)

The friction angle is the angle the resultant force R makes with the normal to the rake face:

$$
\tan\beta = \frac{F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha}{F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical β range:** $25°$ to $45°$ for steel cutting with carbide tools.

## 2.3 Merchant's Equation — The Heart of the Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merchant's shear angle equation** (1945) is the analytical core of all KTU DFMA problems on chip formation:

$$
2\phi + \beta - \alpha = C
$$

where C is a constant. For **minimum energy / minimum force** assumption, C = 90° (the classical Merchant equation). The expanded form is:

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **friction angle β** with the **clearance angle γ**. They are completely different! β is at the tool-chip interface; γ is the tool's relief angle below the cutting edge.

## 2.4 Cutting Power & Energy

The **specific cutting energy (U)** is the energy per unit volume of material removed:

$$
U = \frac{F_c}{t_1 \cdot d} \quad \left[\frac{N \cdot m}{mm^3} = \frac{J}{mm^3}\right]
$$

For engineering alloys: **U ≈ 0.7 to 4.0 N·m/mm³** (steel = ~2.5, aluminum = ~0.7, cast iron = ~1.5).

**Cutting Power Required:**

$$
P_c = F_c \cdot V \quad [\text{Watts}]
$$

where V is in m/s. If V is in m/min, multiply by 60:

$$
P_c \, [kW] = \frac{F_c \, [N] \cdot V \, [m/min]}{60{,}000}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Table

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** All formulas below are KTU-board-tested. Memorize this table. No vertical pipes `|` are used — use `\vert` if absolute value is needed.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Chip thickness ratio | $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ | r = ratio, φ = shear angle, α = rake angle | dimensionless |
| 2 | Shear strain | $\gamma = \cos\alpha / (\sin\phi\cos(\phi-\alpha))$ | Strain in shear zone | dimensionless |
| 3 | Merchant's equation | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ | β = friction angle | degrees |
| 4 | Cutting force (shear) | $F_s = F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi$ | Force on shear plane | N |
| 5 | Shear stress | $\tau_s = F_s / (t_1 d / \sin\phi)$ | Shear plane area | N/mm² |
| 6 | Specific energy | $U = F_c / (t_1 d)$ | Energy per unit volume | N·m/mm³ |
| 7 | Cutting power | $P_c = F_c V / 60{,}000$ | V in m/min → P in kW | kW |
| 8 | Material removal rate | $MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | V in mm/min, f in mm, d in mm | mm³/min |
| 9 | Taylor's tool life | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | T = tool life (min), n = exponent | m/min |
| 10 | Cutting temperature | $\theta = C_\theta \cdot V^m \cdot f^p \cdot d^q$ | Empirical coefficients | °C |
| 11 | Cutting velocity | $V = \pi D N / 1000$ | D = diameter (mm), N = RPM | m/min |
| 12 | Machining time | $T_c = L / (f \cdot N)$ | L = length, f = feed/rev, N = RPM | min |

## 2.6 Tool Wear & Taylor's Tool Life Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Equation (1907)** is the empirical foundation of tool life prediction. It relates cutting velocity (V) to tool life (T):

$$
V \cdot T^n = C
$$

where:
- **n** = Taylor's exponent (typically 0.2 to 0.5 for HSS, 0.3 to 0.7 for carbide).
- **C** = a constant depending on tool-workpiece material pair, feed, depth, and tool-life criterion.

**Solved for V:**

$$
V = \frac{C}{T^{n}}
$$

**Tool Life for a Given Velocity:**

$$
T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight for DFMA:** A 20% increase in cutting speed typically reduces tool life by ~50% for n ≈ 0.25. Designers must balance cycle time against tool-change cost.

## 2.7 Cutting Parameters Selection Hierarchy

In DFMA, the selection of V, f, d follows a strict **economic hierarchy**:

1. **Depth of Cut (d):** Maximize first (uses full tool capacity, most MRR per unit time).
2. **Feed (f):** Optimize second (balances MRR with surface finish).
3. **Cutting Velocity (V):** Optimize last (governs tool life and temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machinability Rating:** A material's machinability is expressed as a **percentage of B1112 steel** (rated 100%). Higher % = easier to cut. Free-machining steels add **Sulfur (S)** or **Lead (Pb)** to break chips and lower cutting forces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Chip Thickness Ratio from Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Consider a tool with rake angle α cutting a chip of uncut thickness t₁. The shear plane is inclined at shear angle φ to the cutting direction. Prove that:
> $$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

### Step 1 — Define the uncut chip area

The uncut chip cross-section is a rectangle of thickness t₁ and width d (depth of cut). After shearing, the chip thickness becomes t₂. Mass conservation (assuming incompressible plastic flow) gives:

$$
t_1 \cdot d = t_2 \cdot d
$$

So the chip width is unchanged; only thickness changes.

### Step 2 — Draw the velocity vector diagram

The cutting velocity V is the relative velocity of the tool with respect to the workpiece. The chip slides up the rake face at velocity Vc (chip velocity). The material shears along the shear plane at velocity Vs (shear velocity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Triangle:** V, Vc, and Vs form a closed triangle. By geometry, the angle between V and Vs is (φ), and the angle between Vc and the tool reference is (φ - α).

### Step 3 — Apply the sine rule to the velocity triangle

$$
\frac{V}{\sin(90° - (\phi - \alpha))} = \frac{V_s}{\sin(90° - \alpha)} = \frac{V_c}{\sin\phi}
$$

### Step 4 — Express the chip thickness ratio

Since the material flows along the shear plane at Vs and slides up the rake face at Vc, continuity of volume gives:

$$
t_1 \cdot V = t_2 \cdot V_c
$$

Therefore:

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{V_c}{V}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute from the sine rule

From the sine rule in Step 3:

$$
\frac{V_c}{V} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine to obtain the result

$$
r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q.E.D.** This identity is the gateway formula. Every shear angle problem in KTU begins here.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Given a tool with rake angle α = +10°, friction angle β = 30°, find the shear angle φ.

### Step 1 — State Merchant's minimum-energy criterion

Merchant assumed that the shear angle φ adjusts itself to **minimize the resultant cutting force F**. Setting dF/dφ = 0 yields:

$$
2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for φ

$$
\phi = \frac{90° + \alpha - \beta}{2} = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the given values

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{10°}{2} - \frac{30°}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + 5° - 15°
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi = 35°}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the chip thickness ratio

$$
r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin 35°}{\cos(35° - 10°)} = \frac{\sin 35°}{\cos 25°}
$$

$$
r = \frac{0.5736}{0.9063} = 0.6329
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the shear strain

$$
\gamma = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{\cos 10°}{\sin 35° \cdot \cos 25°}
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{0.9848}{0.5736 \times 0.9063} = \frac{0.9848}{0.5198} = 1.894
$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Full KTU Board-Style Numerical:** A turning operation on mild steel uses a carbide tool with rake angle α = 6°, cutting speed V = 180 m/min, feed f = 0.25 mm/rev, depth of cut d = 2.0 mm. The measured cutting force is Fc = 1500 N and the thrust force is Ft = 600 N. Compute:
> (a) Shear angle, (b) Friction angle, (c) Shear force, (d) Specific energy, (e) Cutting power.

### Part (a) — Friction Angle β

The friction angle is computed from:

$$
\tan\beta = \frac{F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha}{F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha}
$$

Substitute Fc = 1500 N, Ft = 600 N, α = 6°:

**Numerator:**

$$
F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha = 1500 \times \sin 6° + 600 \times \cos 6°
$$

$$
= 1500 \times 0.1045 + 600 \times 0.9945
$$

$$
= 156.75 + 596.70 = 753.45
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha = 1500 \times \cos 6° - 600 \times \sin 6°
$$

$$
= 1500 \times 0.9945 - 600 \times 0.1045
$$

$$
= 1491.75 - 62.70 = 1429.05
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\tan\beta = \frac{753.45}{1429.05} = 0.5272
$$

$$
\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.5272) = 27.79°
$$

### Part (b) — Shear Angle φ (Merchant)

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2} = 45° + 3° - 13.90° = 34.10°
$$

### Part (c) — Shear Force Fs

$$
F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi
$$

$$
F_s = 1500 \times \cos 34.10° - 600 \times \sin 34.10°
$$

$$
F_s = 1500 \times 0.8281 - 600 \times 0.5606
$$

$$
F_s = 1242.15 - 336.36 = 905.79 \, N
$$

### Part (d) — Specific Cutting Energy U

$$
U = \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d} = \frac{1500}{0.25 \times 2.0} = \frac{1500}{0.5} = 3000 \, N/mm²
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Steel typically gives U ≈ 2000 to 4000 N/mm². Our result of 3000 N/mm² is consistent with a hardened mild steel.

### Part (e) — Cutting Power Pc

$$
P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} = \frac{1500 \times 180}{60{,}000} = \frac{270{,}000}{60{,}000} = 4.5 \, kW
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers (a) β = 27.79°, (b) φ = 34.10°, (c) Fs = 905.79 N, (d) U = 3000 N/mm², (e) Pc = 4.5 kW**

---

## 3.4 Taylor's Tool Life — Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic for Multi-Speed Tool Life Problems**

Given two cutting tests at (V₁, T₁) and (V₂, T₂), find the Taylor exponent n:

$$
V_1 T_1^n = V_2 T_2^n = C
$$

Divide the two equations:

$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^n
$$

Take natural logarithms:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = n \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
n = \frac{\ln(V_1/V_2)}{\ln(T_2/T_1)}
$$

And:

$$
C = V_1 T_1^n
$$

| Test | V (m/min) | T (min) | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 150 | 60 | Reference |
| 2 | 250 | 12 | High-speed test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Solution:** n = ln(150/250) / ln(12/60) = ln(0.6) / ln(0.2) = (-0.5108) / (-1.6094) = 0.317. C = 150 × 60^0.317 = 150 × 3.674 = 551.1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "rake angle" in a single-point cutting tool. How does a positive rake angle differ from a negative rake angle?**

**Model Answer:**
The **rake angle (α)** is the angle between the rake face of the tool and a reference plane (usually the horizontal plane through the cutting edge). It is one of the seven standard tool angles defined by ISO 3685.

- **Positive Rake Angle (+α):** The rake face slopes upward away from the cutting edge, exposing more of the cutting edge to the workpiece. It **reduces cutting force**, lowers power consumption, and is preferred for **soft, ductile materials** (e.g., aluminum, mild steel with HSS tools). Typical range: +5° to +20°.
- **Negative Rake Angle (−α):** The rake face slopes downward, making the tool tip stronger. It is used for **hard, abrasive materials** (e.g., hardened steel, cast iron) and for interrupted cuts. It increases cutting force but improves tool strength and life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag:** Definition + Comparative — KTU pattern 1A type.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is Taylor's tool life equation? State the significance of the exponent 'n'.**

**Model Answer:**
**Taylor's tool life equation (1907)** is the empirical relationship between cutting speed and tool life:

$$
V \cdot T^n = C
$$

where **V** = cutting velocity (m/min), **T** = tool life (min), **n** = Taylor's exponent, **C** = constant.

**Significance of 'n':**
- It quantifies the **sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed**.
- A **higher n** means tool life is less sensitive to speed changes (e.g., n = 0.5 for carbide).
- A **lower n** means tool life drops rapidly with speed (e.g., n = 0.1 for ceramic tools, n = 0.25 for HSS).
- Typical ranges: HSS → n ≈ 0.08 to 0.2; Carbide → n ≈ 0.2 to 0.5; Ceramic → n ≈ 0.5 to 0.7.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**
> 1. **Sign error in Merchant's equation:** Students often write $\phi = 45° - \alpha/2 + \beta/2$ — the signs of α and β are flipped! Correct: $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in power:** Mixing m/min and m/s without the 60,000 conversion factor. **Always** write the unit conversion explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in trigonometric calculations — but KTU usually accepts degrees if your calculator is in degree mode. State the assumption.
> 4. **Wrong identification of forces:** The cutting force Fc is along V (cutting direction), and Ft is along the feed (radial) direction. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Specific energy units:** KTU expects N·m/mm³ (or J/mm³). Writing N/mm² alone is incomplete — it's a stress, not an energy density.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS (CHOICE 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q: A turning operation is performed on a mild steel workpiece (BHN 200) using a carbide tool with the following data: Rake angle α = +10°, Feed f = 0.3 mm/rev, Depth of cut d = 2.5 mm, Cutting speed V = 150 m/min. The measured cutting force is Fc = 1800 N and the thrust force is Ft = 750 N.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Determine:**
(i) Friction angle β
(ii) Shear angle φ using Merchant's equation
(iii) Chip thickness ratio r

**(b) [7 Marks] Calculate:**
(i) Shear force Fs along the shear plane
(ii) Shear stress τs on the shear plane
(iii) Specific cutting energy U
(iv) Cutting power Pc in kW

#### **MODEL SOLUTION:**

##### Part (a) Sub-solutions

**(i) Friction angle β:**

$$
\tan\beta = \frac{F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha}{F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha}
$$

Compute the numerator (N):

$$
N = 1800 \times \sin 10° + 750 \times \cos 10°
$$

$$
N = 1800 \times 0.1736 + 750 \times 0.9848 = 312.48 + 738.60 = 1051.08
$$

Compute the denominator (D):

$$
D = 1800 \times \cos 10° - 750 \times \sin 10°
$$

$$
D = 1800 \times 0.9848 - 750 \times 0.1736 = 1772.64 - 130.20 = 1642.44
$$

$$
\tan\beta = \frac{1051.08}{1642.44} = 0.6400
$$

$$
\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.6400) = 32.62°
$$

**(ii) Shear angle φ (Merchant's equation):**

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + 5° - 16.31° = 33.69°
$$

**(iii) Chip thickness ratio r:**

$$
r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin 33.69°}{\cos(33.69° - 10°)} = \frac{\sin 33.69°}{\cos 23.69°}
$$

$$
r = \frac{0.5547}{0.9157} = 0.6058
$$

##### Part (b) Sub-solutions

**(i) Shear force Fs:**

$$
F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi
$$

$$
F_s = 1800 \times \cos 33.69° - 750 \times \sin 33.69°
$$

$$
F_s = 1800 \times 0.8319 - 750 \times 0.5547
$$

$$
F_s = 1497.42 - 416.03 = 1081.39 \, N
$$

**(ii) Shear stress τs:**

The shear plane area As:

$$
A_s = \frac{f \cdot d}{\sin\phi} = \frac{0.3 \times 2.5}{\sin 33.69°} = \frac{0.75}{0.5547} = 1.3521 \, mm²
$$

$$
\tau_s = \frac{F_s}{A_s} = \frac{1081.39}{1.3521} = 799.78 \, N/mm²
$$

**(iii) Specific cutting energy U:**

$$
U = \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d} = \frac{1800}{0.3 \times 2.5} = \frac{1800}{0.75} = 2400 \, N \cdot m / mm^3
$$

**(iv) Cutting power Pc:**

$$
P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000} = \frac{1800 \times 150}{60{,}000} = \frac{270{,}000}{60{,}000} = 4.5 \, kW
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Box (Q-A):**
> (a-i) β = 32.62° | (a-ii) φ = 33.69° | (a-iii) r = 0.6058
> (b-i) Fs = 1081.39 N | (b-ii) τs = 799.78 N/mm² | (b-iii) U = 2400 N·m/mm³ | (b-iv) Pc = 4.5 kW

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (CHOICE 2 — Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Re-modeled]**

**Q: In a single-point turning test on EN8 steel with HSS tool, two cutting trials were conducted:**
- **Trial 1:** V₁ = 30 m/min → T₁ = 90 min
- **Trial 2:** V₂ = 45 m/min → T₂ = 30 min

**(a) [7 Marks] Determine:**
(i) Taylor's tool life equation (exponent n and constant C)
(ii) The cutting speed for a tool life of 60 min
(iii) The tool life at a cutting speed of 50 m/min

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the following with neat sketches:**
(i) The seven standard tool angles of a single-point cutting tool
(ii) The three modes of tool wear — flank wear, crater wear, and notch wear
(iii) The effect of cutting parameters on surface finish and dimensional accuracy

#### **MODEL SOLUTION:**

##### Part (a) Sub-solutions

**(i) Finding n and C:**

$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^n
$$

$$
\frac{30}{45} = \left(\frac{30}{90}\right)^n
$$

$$
0.6667 = (0.3333)^n
$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln(0.6667) = n \cdot \ln(0.3333)
$$

$$
-0.4055 = n \cdot (-1.0986)
$$

$$
n = \frac{-0.4055}{-1.0986} = 0.3692
$$

Then:

$$
C = V_1 \cdot T_1^n = 30 \times (90)^{0.3692}
$$

Compute $90^{0.3692}$:

$$
90^{0.3692} = e^{0.3692 \times \ln 90} = e^{0.3692 \times 4.4998} = e^{1.6613} = 5.2656
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = 30 \times 5.2656 = 157.97
$$

**Taylor's Equation:** $V \cdot T^{0.3692} = 157.97$

**(ii) Cutting speed for T = 60 min:**

$$
V = \frac{C}{T^n} = \frac{157.97}{(60)^{0.3692}}
$$

Compute $60^{0.3692}$:

$$
60^{0.3692} = e^{0.3692 \times \ln 60} = e^{0.3692 \times 4.0943} = e^{1.5116} = 4.5334
$$

$$
V = \frac{157.97}{4.5334} = 34.85 \, m/min
$$

**(iii) Tool life at V = 50 m/min:**

$$
T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{157.97}{50}\right)^{1/0.3692}
$$

$$
T = (3.1594)^{2.7086}
$$

$$
T = e^{2.7086 \times \ln 3.1594} = e^{2.7086 \times 1.1506} = e^{3.1165} = 22.56 \, min
$$

##### Part (b) Sub-solutions (Descriptive)

**(i) Seven Standard Tool Angles:**

Refer to the figure below. The seven angles are:

| Symbol | Name | Reference Plane | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| α | Rake angle | Reference plane | Reduces cutting force |
| γ | Clearance angle | Tool flank | Prevents rubbing |
| i | Inclination angle | Rake plane | Tilts cutting edge |
| λs | Cutting edge angle | Reference plane | Positions edge |
| χr | Side cutting edge angle | Reference plane | Controls feed force |
| χ'r | End cutting edge angle | Tool side | Prevents finished surface damage |
| rε | Nose radius | Tool tip | Surface finish + life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch description for answer sheet:** Draw a tool tip showing (a) the cutting edge as a line, (b) the rake face inclined at α above the reference, (c) the flank with clearance γ below, and (d) the nose radius rε joining the two edges.

**(ii) Three Modes of Tool Wear:**

- **Flank Wear (VB):** Wear land develops on the clearance face due to rubbing. Most common criterion. Measured as VB width. End of tool life is typically **VB = 0.3 mm**.
- **Crater Wear (KT):** A concave depression on the rake face caused by diffusion and dissolution of tool material into the hot chip. Common with carbide tools cutting steel.
- **Notch Wear:** Localized wear at the depth-of-cut line (DCL) due to work-hardening of the workpiece surface. Particularly severe in machining austenitic stainless steels.

**(iii) Effect of Cutting Parameters:**

| Parameter | Surface Finish (Ra) | Dimensional Accuracy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increase **V** | Slight improvement (chip flow smoother) | Worse (more heat → thermal expansion) |
| Increase **f** | **Worsens** Ra (rougher, theoretical Ra ≈ f²/8rε) | Worse (larger deflection of tool) |
| Increase **d** | No direct effect | Worse (larger radial force) |
| Use cutting fluid | Improves | Improves (lower thermal distortion) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Box (Q-B):**
> (a-i) n = 0.3692, C = 157.97
> (a-ii) V (T=60) = 34.85 m/min
> (a-iii) T (V=50) = 22.56 min
> (b) Descriptive answers as per sub-parts above.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### 🧠 Conceptual Foundation
- Single-point cutting tool = tool with **one principal cutting edge**.
- Tool geometry: **7 standard angles** per ISO 3685.
- Orthogonal cutting = 2-D analysis; Oblique cutting = 3-D analysis with inclination angle i.

### 📐 Core Formulas to Memorize
- Chip thickness ratio: $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$
- Merchant's equation: $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$
- Friction angle: $\tan\beta = (F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha) / (F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha)$
- Specific energy: $U = F_c / (f \cdot d)$  → Steel ≈ 2400 N·m/mm³
- Cutting power: $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60{,}000$ (kW, V in m/min)
- MRR: $V \cdot f \cdot d$ (mm³/min)
- Taylor's tool life: $V \cdot T^n = C$

### 🎯 High-Yield Numerical Patterns (14-mark favorites)
1. **Force → β → φ → r → Fs → τs → U → Pc** (full chain, 7 sub-parts)
2. **Two test points → n and C → predict T or V** (Taylor's analysis)
3. **MRR + V + d + f → machining time Tc** (process planning)
4. **Cost minimization:** optimum V from Taylor + cost coefficients

### ⚠️ Common Exam Traps
- Sign of α in Merchant's equation (positive rake → increases φ).
- Units of specific energy: must be N·m/mm³, not N/mm².
- Tool life: T in minutes, not hours.
- V in m/min for Taylor, but in m/s for power.
- Inclination angle i ≠ Rake angle α.

### 🏭 DFMA Design Implications
- Higher **rake angle** → lower force → thinner, more flexible tool → **tool deflection increases** → set tolerance band carefully.
- Higher **nose radius rε** → better surface finish (Ra ≈ f²/8rε) → but **higher BUE tendency** at low speeds.
- Higher **cutting speed** → better finish → but exponential tool life loss.
- **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** forms at low V with ductile materials — avoid by using coated tools or increasing V.
- For **DFA**, prefer parts machinable in **single setup** (one chucking) to minimize tolerance stack-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Memory Hook:** *"Big D, Small f, Optimum V"* — Depth first, Feed second, Velocity last. This is the universal selection rule.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
