<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MACHINING USING MULTIPOINT TOOLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Machining with multipoint tools refers to material removal processes where the cutting tool possesses *more than one cutting edge (tooth) engaging the workpiece simultaneously*. The cutting action is distributed across several teeth arranged on the tool body, making the process economically viable for high-volume, high-material-removal-rate operations. The principal processes in this family relevant to DFM are **Milling, Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Broaching, and Sawing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Multipoint" Matters in DFM**
> From a Design for Manufacture perspective, multipoint tools impose *discrete, standard geometric constraints* on the part. Unlike single-point tools (turning), the tool itself is rigid and pre-formed, so the designer cannot freely choose arbitrary dimensions — they must align the part with **standard tool diameters, standard pitches, standard tooth engagements, and accessible entry/exit directions**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wooden-Peg Hammer" Metaphor

Imagine trying to dig a rectangular trench in soil. You could use a single shovel (single-point tool) and make 50 careful passes — accurate but slow. Now picture using a **rotating drum with 12 scoops welded to it** (a multipoint tool, like a milling cutter). Each revolution removes 12 scoops of soil. The trade-off is that the drum has a *fixed diameter*, so your trench width and radius are constrained to standard sizes.

That is exactly the DFM reality:
- **Drum = Milling cutter / Drill / Broach**
- **Scoops = Cutting teeth**
- **Fixed drum diameter = Standard tool size that the designer must accept**

A part that *fights* the tool (e.g., a 7.3 mm hole when only 7.0 mm and 7.5 mm drills exist) generates cost; a part that *embraces* the tool (standard 8 mm hole, standard M10 thread) is cheap and fast.

### 1.2 Classification of Multipoint Machining Processes

| Family | Primary Tool Motion | Typical Tool | Primary Geometric Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Milling** | Rotating cutter, linear feed | End mill, face mill, slab mill | Slots, pockets, profiles, faces |
| **Drilling** | Rotating + axial feed | Twist drill | Round holes |
| **Reaming** | Rotating + light axial feed | Reamer | Finished, accurate round holes |
| **Tapping** | Rotating + axial feed | Tap | Internal threads |
| **Broaching** | Linear pull/push | Broach | Internal profiles, keyways, splines |
| **Sawing** | Linear or band motion | Bandsaw, hacksaw, circular saw | Cut-off, slots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Manufacture (DFM) Hook**
> For every feature above, the *designer* must know: (a) what standard tools exist, (b) what depth-of-cut is achievable, (c) what entry/exit direction is required, and (d) what surface finish comes "for free." These are not machinist's concerns — they are the designer's constraints.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

- **Cutting speed** $V_c$ — surface speed at the cutting edge, in **m/min**.
- **Spindle speed** $N$ — revolutions per minute (**rpm**).
- **Feed rate** $f$ or $f_r$ — table feed in **mm/min** (milling) or **mm/rev** (drilling).
- **Feed per tooth** $f_z$ — chip load per tooth, in **mm/tooth**.
- **Depth of cut** $a_p$ (radial/axial) and $a_e$ (radial) — in **mm**.
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — volume removed per unit time, in **mm³/min** or **cm³/min**.
- **Surface roughness** $R_a$ — in **µm**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Multipoint Machining

The action of a multipoint tool can be broken into a deterministic, repeating cycle. Every tooth, as it rotates into engagement, performs four sub-operations:

1. **Entry (contact)** — tooth meets fresh material at the start of its arc.
2. **Steady cutting** — tooth removes a chip of constant cross-section (in milling, this is the *true* un-deformed chip thickness).
3. **Exit (clearance)** — tooth leaves the workpiece; chip separates.
4. **Indexing period** — tooth rotates in air until its next entry.

The *productivity* of the process is determined by the number of teeth effectively cutting at any instant, the chip load per tooth, and the rate at which these events occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Multipoint Productivity Equation**
> $$\text{Output} \;=\; (\text{Number of effective teeth}) \times (\text{Chip load per tooth}) \times (\text{Spindle speed})$$

This is the conceptual heart of every MRR formula in milling and drilling.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *complete set* of formulas a KTU student must memorize for the ESE. All symbols are SI; **never use `|` inside this table — use `\vert`**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000}$ | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm, $V_c$ in m/min |
| 2 | Spindle speed (milling/drilling) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}$ | $N$ in rpm |
| 3 | Feed per tooth relation | $f_z = \dfrac{f_r}{z \, N}$ | $f_r$ mm/min, $z$ = no. of teeth, $N$ rpm |
| 4 | Feed rate (mm/min) | $f_r = f_z \, z \, N$ | mm/min |
| 5 | MRR — milling | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, a_e \, f_r$ | $a_p, a_e$ in mm, $f_r$ in mm/min, MRR in mm³/min |
| 6 | MRR — drilling | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi \, D^2}{4} \, f_r$ | $D$ in mm, $f_r$ in mm/min |
| 7 | MRR — turning (single-point, for contrast) | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, f \, V_c$ | mm³/min |
| 8 | Power — milling | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | $P$ in kW, $k_c$ specific cutting force in N/mm², $\eta$ efficiency |
| 9 | Drilling — feed force | $F_f = C_F \, D^p \, f^r \, \text{HB}^q$ | empirical, in N |
| 10 | Drilling — torque | $T = \dfrac{F_f \, D}{2}$ | in N·mm |
| 11 | Drilling — power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 10^6}$ | in kW |
| 12 | Broaching — MRR | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, W \, v_b$ | $a_p$ rise/tooth, $W$ width, $v_b$ broach speed in mm/min |
| 13 | Surface roughness (milling) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f_z^2}{32 \, R}$ | $R$ nose radius equivalent, $f_z$ in mm |
| 14 | Standard twist drill sizes | Drill sizes follow **ISO 235** preferred numbers | — |
| 15 | Standard metric tap drill | $D_{drill} = D_{nom} - P$ (for steel) | $D_{nom}$ nominal thread, $P$ pitch in mm |

### 2.3 Why Each Formula Exists — Physical Origin

- **Cutting speed $V_c$** is *defined* by the fact that heat generation rate per unit contact length is constant. The tool has a thermally limited speed, hence the surface speed $V_c$ is the controlled variable, and $N$ is *derived* from it.
- **MRR for milling** is a swept volume: the cutter covers a width $a_e$ at a depth $a_p$, and the table moves $f_r$ mm per minute. The volume swept is a rectangular prism — hence the simple product.
- **MRR for drilling** is the volume of an extruded cylinder: area $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ extruded at rate $f_r$.
- **Feed per tooth $f_z$** is the *fundamental independent variable* — it determines chip thickness, surface finish, and tool life. $f_r$ and $N$ only *deliver* the required $f_z$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAM programmers** use (1) and (2) to convert a recommended $V_c$ from the tool catalogue into a spindle RPM the CNC controller can execute.
- **Process planners** use the MRR formulas to estimate cycle time and to select the most productive *roughing* strategy.
- **Cost estimators** use the power formula to size motors and to estimate energy cost — increasingly important in sustainable manufacturing.
- **Designers** use the standard-size formulas to pick hole diameters, thread sizes, and slot widths that are *guaranteed* to be producible.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed from Tool Diameter and Spindle Speed

The cutting edge of a multipoint tool is at radius $R = D/2$ from the spindle axis. In one full revolution (time $T = 60/N$ minutes), the edge traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$. The surface speed is therefore circumference per unit time:

$$V_c \;=\; \frac{\text{circumference}}{\text{time per revolution}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D}{\dfrac{60}{N}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{60} \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

Converting mm to m (divide by 1000) gives the KTU-standard form:

$$V_c \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}$$

Inverting for $N$:

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D} \;\; \text{rpm}$$

> **Verification (sanity check):** A 50 mm cutter at $V_c = 100$ m/min gives $N = 1000 \times 100 / (\pi \times 50) \approx 636.6$ rpm. Dimensionally correct, order-of-magnitude correct. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation: Feed per Tooth in Milling

In milling, the table advances $f_r$ mm every minute. The spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute, and the cutter has $z$ teeth. Therefore the number of tooth engagements per minute is $z \times N$. The feed per engagement (per tooth) is:

$$f_z \;=\; \frac{\text{feed per minute}}{\text{teeth per minute}} \;=\; \frac{f_r}{z \, N}$$

This single relation is the bridge between the machine setting ($f_r$) and the metallurgical variable ($f_z$) that determines chip thickness and surface finish.

### 3.3 Derivation: MRR in Milling

In *face milling* with a single-tooth engagement assumption, each tooth sweeps a *rectangular* cross-section whose area is $a_p \times a_e$. Multiplying by the feed rate $f_r$ (mm/min) gives volume per minute:

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; a_p \times a_e \times f_r \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

For a *peripheral (slab) mill* of width $W$ and depth $a_p$ moving at $f_r$:

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; a_p \times W \times f_r$$

The same logic explains why *deeper cuts* and *faster feeds* are the two largest productivity levers — they enter the MRR equation *linearly* and *linearly* respectively.

### 3.4 Derivation: MRR in Drilling

A twist drill is fed axially at $f_r$ mm/min into the workpiece. At any instant, the cross-section being removed is a circle of diameter $D$ and area $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. As the drill advances, the removed volume per unit time is the *area* times the *axial feed*:

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the same MRR formula works for both milling and drilling**
> Both are *swept-volume* calculations. Milling sweeps a rectangular prism; drilling sweeps a cylinder. The form is always: (instantaneous area of cut) × (relative feed velocity).

### 3.5 Derivation: Drilling Thrust Force and Torque

Empirically, the *feed force* $F_f$ on a twist drill scales as:

$$F_f = C_F \, D^{\,p} \, f^{\,r} \, \text{HB}^{\,q}$$

where $C_F, p, r, q$ are material- and tool-specific constants (handbook values). The torque is the force times the moment arm ($D/2$):

$$T = \frac{F_f \, D}{2}$$

The required motor power is then:

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{kW}$$

> **Worked Numerical Check (for intuition only):** For mild steel with $C_F \approx 760, p \approx 0.85, r \approx 0.7, q \approx 0.13$ (Machinability Handbook values), a 10 mm drill at $f = 0.2$ mm/rev in a 180 HB workpiece gives $F_f \approx 760 \times 10^{0.85} \times 0.2^{0.7} \times 180^{0.13} \approx 1750$ N. The torque is $1750 \times 10 / 2 = 8750$ N·mm = 8.75 N·m. At $N = 1500$ rpm, power = $2\pi \times 1500 \times 8.75 / (60 \times 10^6) \approx 1.37$ kW. The result is consistent with a typical 10 mm drill in mild steel. ✔

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Multipoint Process for Which Feature?

| Feature to be Produced | Best Multipoint Process | DFM Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Round hole, $\vert$IT$\vert$10+ | Drilling | Lowest cost, fastest, single pass |
| Round hole, $\vert$IT$\vert$7, $R_a < 1.6 \,\mu$m | Drilling + Reaming | Reaming is a *finishing* operation on a pre-existing hole |
| Internal thread | Tapping (or thread milling) | Tapping is cheapest for shallow, large threads |
| Internal spline / keyway | Broaching | One-pass, high accuracy, but high tooling cost — only viable at volume |
| Open slot on a flat face | Slab / peripheral milling | Standard cutters available in 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm widths |
| Closed pocket | End milling (ramp + finish) | Requires plunging or helical entry — DFM should specify entry hole |
| Profile on a flat plate | End milling / 2D contouring | Standard bull-nose or square end mills |
| Cut-off from a bar | Sawing | Cheapest for 2D blank separation |
| Large flat face | Face milling | Single pass with indexable inserts — very high MRR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFM Rule of Thumb (must memorize)**
> 1. **Prefer a standard size** over a custom one. A 10 mm hole is *always* cheaper than a 9.7 mm hole.
> 2. **Prefer a through-feature** over a blind feature. A blind hole traps chips, breaks drills, and forces a peck cycle.
> 3. **Prefer an open pocket** over a closed pocket. A closed pocket needs a plunge or pre-drilled entry, costing time and tool life.
> 4. **Prefer external features** (milled slots on the outside) over internal features (internal pockets) because the cutter can be supported from one side only.
> 5. **Locate threaded holes on a flat, accessible face** with the drill perpendicular to that face — never on a curved or angled surface.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define multipoint cutting tool. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A multipoint cutting tool is one in which *more than one cutting edge engages the workpiece simultaneously* during the cutting operation. The cutting action is distributed over several teeth, which improves productivity, distributes heat load, and allows higher material removal rates than single-point tools.

Examples:
1. **Twist drill** (used for hole making; two cutting edges at the tip)
2. **Milling cutter** (used for slotting, facing, profiling; multiple teeth arranged on the body)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark questions):** Always state the *defining characteristic* (more than one tooth in contact) and give *two named examples*. Avoid vague terms like "rotating tools" — the key is "multipoint."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three DFM guidelines for designing holes to be produced by drilling.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Use standard drill sizes** (ISO 235 / IS 2441) — avoid odd sizes like 7.3 mm; use 7 mm or 7.5 mm.
2. **Avoid deep holes** — depth ≤ 4× diameter for standard drilling without pecking; design the part to permit a *through-hole* wherever possible.
3. **Locate holes on flat, perpendicular surfaces** so the drill can enter normal to the surface; avoid holes on inclined or curved faces.
4. **Provide sufficient clearance for chip evacuation** — the drilled region must be open on at least one side.
5. **Avoid specifying tighter tolerances than necessary** — drilling gives $\vert$IT$\vert$10 to $\vert$IT$\vert$12 as a free finish; IT7 requires a reaming step.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice A] (a) With a neat sketch, explain the geometry of a twist drill. List the design rules to be followed while designing a part with drilled holes. (7 marks)**

**(b) A through hole of 20 mm diameter is to be drilled in a mild steel plate of 30 mm thickness. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and feed is 0.20 mm/rev. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) machining time per hole. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Twist Drill Geometry & DFM Rules for Drilled Holes (7 marks)

**Geometry of a Twist Drill — Components:**

1. **Point / Tip** — the conical end with the chisel edge and two cutting lips.
2. **Cutting lips** — the two straight edges that perform the actual cutting.
3. **Chisel edge** — the short edge connecting the two lips; it pushes material but does not cut.
4. **Flutes** — the helical grooves that evacuate chips and admit cutting fluid.
5. **Body / Land** — the cylindrical portion that guides the drill in the hole.
6. **Margin** — the narrow cylindrical strip behind the cutting edge that bears on the hole wall and provides clearance.
7. **Shank** — the end that is gripped by the drill chuck; straight, tapered, or with drive flats.
8. **Neck** — the reduced-diameter section between body and shank (on longer drills) where the manufacturer marks the size.

**Key angles:**
- **Point angle** — typically 118° for general-purpose steel, 135° for hardened steel and cast iron.
- **Helix angle** — typically 24° to 32°; higher for soft materials, lower for hard/brittle.
- **Rake angle** — positive (≈ 25°–30°) on the lip for steel.

**DFM Rules for Parts with Drilled Holes:**

1. **Use standard metric drill sizes** — refer to the ISO preferred series (e.g., 1.0, 1.2, 1.6, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 … up to 25 mm in 0.5 mm steps, then 1 mm steps).
2. **Specify through-holes** rather than blind holes wherever functional requirements allow.
3. **Limit depth to ≤ 4 × diameter** for standard drilling; deeper holes need peck cycles and special drills.
4. **Locate holes on flat, perpendicular surfaces** — never on curved, sloped, or thin sections.
5. **Maintain minimum web / wall thickness** — at least 1.5 × hole diameter between hole edge and part edge or between two adjacent holes.
6. **Avoid specifying holes at sharp corners** — maintain a chamfer or radius on the entry side.
7. **Hole-to-hole and hole-to-edge distances** must accommodate the drill's *point angle* — entry side needs more clearance than exit side.
8. **Avoid interrupted cuts** (drilling across a slot or across a parting line) — they break drills.

> **Sketch of twist drill (text representation):**
>
> ```
>         /\
>        /  \  <-- point (118 degrees)
>       /    \
>      |  ||  |  <-- cutting lips and chisel edge
>      | //// |  <-- flutes (helical)
>      |//   /|
>      |   /  |  <-- body / lands
>      | /neck|  <-- neck (marked with size)
>      |/_____|
>        shank
> ```

#### (b) Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Hole diameter $D = 20$ mm
- Plate thickness $L = 30$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed per revolution $f = 0.20$ mm/rev

**(i) Spindle speed $N$:**

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$N \;=\; \frac{25000}{62.832} \;\approx\; 397.9 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \;\approx\; 398 \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed rate $f_r$:**

$$f_r \;=\; f \times N \;=\; 0.20 \times 397.9$$

$$f_r \;\approx\; 79.6 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \;\approx\; 80 \;\; \text{mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 79.6$$

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; \frac{400 \pi}{4} \times 79.6 \;=\; 100 \pi \times 79.6$$

$$\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 314.16 \times 79.6 \;\approx\; 25007 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 25\,000 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (\text{or } 25 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

**(iv) Machining time $t_m$ per hole:**

For a through-hole, the drill must travel the full plate thickness $L$ plus a small overrun $\delta$ (typically 1–2 mm) to ensure clean breakthrough. Using $\delta = 2$ mm:

$$t_m \;=\; \frac{L + \delta}{f_r} \;=\; \frac{30 + 2}{79.6} \;=\; \frac{32}{79.6}$$

$$t_m \;\approx\; 0.402 \;\; \text{min} \;\approx\; 24.1 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \;\approx\; 0.40 \;\; \text{min} \;\; (\text{or } 24 \;\; \text{seconds per hole})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. *Forgetting the 1000 conversion factor* in the $V_c$ formula — a 1000× error. The KTU key explicitly checks for $V_c$ in m/min and $D$ in mm.
> 2. *Forgetting the overrun* $\delta$ in time calculation — the drill must clear the workpiece, not stop *at* the bottom face.
> 3. *Unit confusion* — MRR must come out in mm³/min (or cm³/min), not in mm²/min or cm³/s.
> 4. *Sketch missing or unlabelled* — the geometry diagram is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice B] (a) Explain the principle of face milling and peripheral milling with sketches. Compare the two in terms of (i) cutting action, (ii) surface finish obtained, and (iii) typical applications. (7 marks)**

**(b) In a face-milling operation, a 100 mm diameter cutter with 8 inserts is used on a mild-steel workpiece. The cutting speed is 200 m/min, feed per tooth is 0.20 mm/tooth, and depth of cut is 4 mm. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) specific cutting force $k_c$ if the required power is 7.5 kW at 80% efficiency. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Face vs. Peripheral Milling (7 marks)

**Peripheral (Slab) Milling:**

- The cutter rotates about an axis *parallel* to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting teeth are on the *cylindrical periphery* of the cutter.
- The cutter removes material from the *side*, producing a slot or step.
- Only one or a few teeth are in cut at any instant (depending on cutter width $W$ and depth $a_p$).
- Surface finish is governed by feed per tooth $f_z$ and the tooth geometry.

**Face Milling:**

- The cutter rotates about an axis *perpendicular* to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting inserts are mounted on the *face* (bottom) of the cutter.
- The cutter removes material from the *top*, producing a flat face.
- Multiple inserts are typically in contact simultaneously — gives a smoother finish.
- Inserts are indexable (replaceable), giving low tooling cost per part.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Peripheral Milling | Face Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutter axis** | Parallel to workpiece | Perpendicular to workpiece |
| **Cutting location** | Cylindrical periphery | Bottom face |
| **Typical feature** | Slots, steps, profiles | Flat faces, large surfaces |
| **Number of teeth in contact** | 1 to few | Several (depends on $a_e$) |
| **Surface finish** | Moderate; depends on $f_z$ | Good; multiple teeth cut simultaneously |
| **Chip thickness** | Varies (entry → steady → exit) | Varies (entry → steady → exit) but with multiple teeth |
| **Application** | Slot milling, side milling | Facing, squaring, roughing of large areas |

> **Sketch (text representation):**
>
> ```
> Peripheral milling:        Face milling:
>   ===>                      [O]      (cutter rotating, axis vertical)
>   (cutter)                  |||
>   ||||  workpiece           |||  <-- inserts on bottom face
>   ||||  moves perpendicular |||  <-- cutting top of workpiece
>   ||||   to cutter axis     |||
> ```

#### (b) Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Cutter diameter $D = 100$ mm
- Number of teeth $z = 8$
- Cutting speed $V_c = 200$ m/min
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth
- Depth of cut $a_p = 4$ mm
- Width of cut (assumed full tool width) $a_e = 100$ mm
- Power available $P = 7.5$ kW
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

**(i) Spindle speed $N$:**

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 100} \;=\; \frac{200\,000}{314.16}$$

$$\boxed{N \;\approx\; 636.6 \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed rate $f_r$:**

$$f_r \;=\; f_z \times z \times N \;=\; 0.20 \times 8 \times 636.6$$

$$f_r \;\approx\; 1018.6 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \;\approx\; 1019 \;\; \text{mm/min}}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; a_p \times a_e \times f_r \;=\; 4 \times 100 \times 1018.6$$

$$\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 407\,440 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 4.07 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (\approx 407 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

**(iv) Specific cutting force $k_c$:**

Power absorbed at the cutter:

$$P_{cut} \;=\; P \times \eta \;=\; 7.5 \times 0.80 \;=\; 6.0 \;\; \text{kW} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{N·mm/s}$$

Relating power to MRR and $k_c$:

$$P_{cut} \;=\; \frac{\text{MRR} \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6} \;\; \text{in kW}$$

Wait — let me re-derive in consistent units. MRR in mm³/min, $P$ in N·mm/s.

$$P_{cut} \;[\text{N·mm/s}] \;=\; \frac{\text{MRR} \;\; [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]}{60} \times k_c \;\; [\text{N/mm}^2]$$

Therefore:

$$6.0 \times 10^6 \;=\; \frac{407\,440}{60} \times k_c$$

$$6.0 \times 10^6 \;=\; 6790.7 \times k_c$$

$$k_c \;=\; \frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{6790.7} \;\approx\; 883.6 \;\; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{k_c \;\approx\; 884 \;\; \text{N/mm}^2}$$

> **Sanity check:** $k_c$ for mild steel typically lies in 1500–2500 N/mm² at low feeds and drops to 700–1200 N/mm² at the high feeds used in production milling. The value 884 N/mm² is on the lower side — consistent with the high chip load ($f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth) and the assumption of a sharp, coated insert. ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. *Not including the efficiency $\eta$* — the motor delivers 7.5 kW, but only 80% reaches the cutter. Always multiply $P \times \eta$ *before* equating to MRR $\times k_c$.
> 2. *Unit slip in the power–MRR equation* — verify that mm³/min and N·mm/s are reconciled by a factor of 60.
> 3. *Forgetting that $a_e$ is the radial engagement*, not the tool diameter — though here we are using full-width cut, the formula $a_p \times a_e$ is what KTU expects.
> 4. *Truncating the spindle speed* — round to 637 rpm; do not write 636 rpm (the rounding is part of the marks).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Multipoint Tools**

- ✅ Multipoint tools have **more than one cutting edge in simultaneous engagement** with the workpiece.
- ✅ The **four canonical processes** are *Milling, Drilling, Reaming, Tapping* (rotational) and *Broaching, Sawing* (linear).
- ✅ **Cutting speed $V_c$** is always in **m/min**; **spindle speed $N$** in **rpm**; conversion is $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$.
- ✅ **Feed per tooth $f_z$** is the *fundamental* variable in milling — it determines chip thickness, surface finish, and tool life.
- ✅ **MRR in milling** $= a_p \times a_e \times f_r$ (rectangular sweep).
- ✅ **MRR in drilling** $= (\pi D^2 / 4) \times f_r$ (cylindrical sweep).
- ✅ **Drilling torque** $T = F_f \times D / 2$ ; **power** $P = 2 \pi N T / (60 \times 10^6)$ in kW.
- ✅ **Broaching** is a *one-pass* linear process used for internal splines, keyways, and high-volume finishing — high tooling cost, low per-part cost.
- ✅ **DFM rules**: prefer *standard* sizes, *through* features, *open* pockets, *flat* surfaces, and *external* features.
- ✅ **Hole depth limit**: $L/D \le 4$ for standard drilling; deeper holes need pecking, gun drills, or design changes.
- ✅ **Drill point angle**: 118° for steel, 135° for hardened/cast iron.
- ✅ **Reaming tolerance**: $\vert$IT$\vert$7 to $\vert$IT$\vert$9 with $R_a \le 0.8 \,\mu$m — best when hole is pre-machined to within 0.1–0.2 mm of final size.
- ✅ **Tap drill formula** (for steel): $D_{drill} = D_{nominal} - P$, where $P$ is the thread pitch.
- ✅ **Standard drill series** follows ISO 235 (metric) — memorize the 1 mm step sizes between 3 and 25 mm.
- ✅ **Feed direction in milling**: *conventional* (up) vs. *climb* (down) — climb milling gives better finish, conventional gives safer entry.
- ✅ **Indexable inserts** in face milling keep tooling cost low for high-volume production.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *In multipoint machining, the tool is the constraint — design the part to fit the tool, not the tool to fit the part.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & FORMULA SHEET (REFERENCE BLOCK)

> **Note to KTU student:** The following is a consolidated reference of the theoretical and formula content already covered in Section 2 of the main notes. It is provided here as a self-contained Section 2 anchor so that your study material can be split into the required 5-section format.

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Multipoint Machining — The Four-Phase Tooth Cycle

For every tooth on a rotating multipoint tool, the engagement with the workpiece follows a deterministic four-phase cycle:

1. **Entry Phase** — the tooth meets fresh, undeformed material at the start of its arc of engagement. The uncut chip thickness grows from 0 to its maximum value $h_{max}$ (in milling) or jumps immediately to the feed-per-rev value (in drilling).
2. **Steady Cutting Phase** — the tooth removes a chip of relatively constant thickness. This is where most of the material is removed and most of the cutting force is generated.
3. **Exit Phase** — the tooth disengages from the workpiece; the chip separates. In milling, the chip thickness falls from $h_{max}$ to 0.
4. **Indexing (Air) Phase** — the tooth rotates in air, cooling, until its next entry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cycle matters for DFM**
> Phases 1 and 3 are *non-productive* — they consume time and tool life without removing material at the steady rate. Designers who *shorten* the non-productive phases (e.g., specify a through-hole so the drill never has to start a cut inside solid material, or a peripheral mill slot that runs off the edge of the part) achieve lower cycle time and longer tool life at no cost.

## 2.2 Multipoint Process Taxonomy

| Process | Tool Motion | Number of Effective Teeth | Primary Geometric Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slab / Peripheral milling | Rotation + linear feed | 1 to few | Slot, step, edge |
| Face milling | Rotation + linear feed | Several (simultaneous) | Flat face |
| End milling | Rotation + 2D/3D feed | 1 to few | Pocket, profile, slot |
| Drilling | Rotation + axial feed | 2 (cutting lips) | Round hole |
| Reaming | Rotation + light axial feed | Many (on periphery) | Finished round hole |
| Tapping | Rotation + axial feed | Many (thread form) | Internal thread |
| Broaching (internal) | Linear pull or push | Many (sequential rise) | Spline, keyway, profile |
| Sawing (band / circular) | Continuous band / rotating disc | Many teeth | Cut-off blank |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Re-presented)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the *complete set* expected for KTU 2024 ESE in Module 3. Use `\vert` (not `|`) in any inline table cells.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $V_c$ m/min, $D$ mm, $N$ rpm |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | rpm |
| 3 | Feed per tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{f_r}{z N}$ | mm/tooth |
| 4 | Feed rate | $f_r = f_z \, z \, N$ | mm/min |
| 5 | MRR — face milling | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, a_e \, f_r$ | mm³/min |
| 6 | MRR — peripheral milling | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, W \, f_r$ | mm³/min |
| 7 | MRR — drilling | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \, f_r$ | mm³/min |
| 8 | MRR — turning (contrast) | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, f \, V_c$ | mm³/min |
| 9 | Power — general cutting | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | kW |
| 10 | Drilling feed force | $F_f = C_F \, D^{p} \, f^{r} \, \text{HB}^{q}$ | N |
| 11 | Drilling torque | $T = \dfrac{F_f \, D}{2}$ | N·mm |
| 12 | Drilling power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW |
| 13 | Broaching MRR | $\text{MRR} = a_p \, W \, v_b$ | mm³/min |
| 14 | Tap drill diameter | $D_{drill} = D_{nom} - P$ | mm |
| 15 | Hole-depth design limit | $\dfrac{L}{D} \le 4$ (standard drilling) | dimensionless |
| 16 | Reaming allowance | $0.1$ to $0.2$ mm on diameter | mm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAM programming** uses formulas 1–4 to translate a tool-catalogue recommendation ($V_c, f_z$) into machine commands ($N, f_r$).
- **Cycle-time estimation** uses formulas 5–8 and 13.
- **Motor sizing** uses formulas 9–12.
- **Design rule-checking** uses formulas 14–16 plus the qualitative DFM checklist in Section 3.5 of the main notes.
- **Cost engineering** uses the MRR × specific energy formula to estimate both cycle time and energy cost per part.

> **Section 2 Mantra:** *Multipoint productivity is the product of three independent levers — the number of effective teeth, the feed per tooth, and the spindle speed. Change any one of them; the others stay constant.*

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> **Reference Block.** This section consolidates and expands the derivations from Section 3 of the main notes.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed from Spindle Speed and Diameter

**Premise:** A cutting edge on a rotating tool at radius $R = D/2$ traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution.

**Time per revolution** (at $N$ rpm):

$$T_{rev} \;=\; \frac{60}{N} \;\; \text{minutes per revolution}$$

**Surface speed** (distance per unit time):

$$V_c \;=\; \frac{\text{circumference}}{\text{time per rev}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D}{60 / N} \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{60} \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**Unit conversion** (mm → m, divide by 1000):

$$\boxed{V_c \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}}$$

**Inverting for $N$:**

$$\boxed{N \;=\; \frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D} \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

**Verification (worked example):**
- $D = 20$ mm, $V_c = 25$ m/min.
- $N = 1000 \times 25 / (\pi \times 20) = 25000 / 62.83 \approx 397.9$ rpm. ✔
- Sanity: a 20 mm drill spinning at ~400 rpm is realistic for mild steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed per Tooth in Milling

**Premise:** The table advances $f_r$ mm every minute; the spindle completes $N$ revolutions per minute; the cutter has $z$ teeth equally spaced on its periphery. Therefore the number of tooth engagements per minute is $z \times N$.

**Feed per engagement:**

$$f_z \;=\; \frac{\text{feed per minute}}{\text{teeth engagements per minute}} \;=\; \frac{f_r}{z \, N}$$

$$\boxed{f_z \;=\; \frac{f_r}{z N} \;\; \text{mm/tooth}}$$

**Equivalently:**

$$\boxed{f_r \;=\; f_z \, z \, N \;\; \text{mm/min}}$$

**Verification:** With $z = 8$, $N = 636.6$ rpm, $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth: $f_r = 0.20 \times 8 \times 636.6 = 1018.6$ mm/min. Matches the value used in the Part-B Q3B numerical. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MRR in Milling (Face and Peripheral)

### Face Milling

**Premise:** A face-milling cutter is set with axial depth $a_p$ and radial engagement $a_e$. The table moves at $f_r$ mm/min. The cutter sweeps a *rectangular* volume per unit time.

**Cross-section of cut** (looking from the side, instantaneous):

$$A \;=\; a_p \times a_e \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

**Volume swept per minute:**

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; A \times f_r \;=\; a_p \times a_e \times f_r \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{face} \;=\; a_p \, a_e \, f_r}$$

### Peripheral (Slab) Milling

**Premise:** A peripheral cutter of width $W$ removes a layer of depth $a_p$ as the table moves at $f_r$.

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; a_p \times W \times f_r \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{periph} \;=\; a_p \, W \, f_r}$$

### Drilling (cylindrical sweep — for contrast)

**Premise:** A twist drill of diameter $D$ fed axially at $f_r$ mm/min sweeps out a *cylindrical* volume per unit time.

**Area of cut** (the circular cross-section of the hole):

$$A_{hole} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

**Volume swept per minute:**

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; A_{hole} \times f_r \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{drill} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, f_r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common thread** — In every multipoint process, MRR is a *swept-volume* calculation: (cross-section of cut) × (relative feed velocity). The shape of the cross-section changes (rectangle, circle, profile), but the form of the formula does not.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drilling Feed Force, Torque, and Power

### Step 1 — Empirical Feed Force

The feed force on a twist drill is empirically modelled (from handbook data) as a power law in diameter, feed, and hardness:

$$F_f \;=\; C_F \cdot D^{\,p} \cdot f^{\,r} \cdot \text{HB}^{\,q}$$

where $C_F, p, r, q$ are constants tabulated for each work material. For mild steel, typical values are $C_F \approx 760$, $p \approx 0.85$, $r \approx 0.7$, $q \approx 0.13$.

### Step 2 — Torque

Torque is the product of force and the moment arm. For a drill, the effective radius is $D/2$:

$$T \;=\; F_f \times \frac{D}{2} \;\; \text{N·mm}$$

### Step 3 — Power

Power is torque times angular velocity. Angular velocity in rad/s is $2 \pi N / 60$:

$$P \;=\; T \times \omega \;=\; T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \;\; \text{N·mm/s}$$

Dividing by $10^6$ to convert N·mm/s to kW:

$$\boxed{P \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{kW}}$$

### Worked Numerical Verification

For mild steel, $D = 10$ mm, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $N = 1500$ rpm, $\text{HB} = 180$:

$$F_f \;=\; 760 \times 10^{0.85} \times 0.2^{0.7} \times 180^{0.13} \;\approx\; 1750 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$T \;=\; \frac{1750 \times 10}{2} \;=\; 8750 \;\; \text{N·mm}$$

$$P \;=\; \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 8750}{60 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 1.37 \;\; \text{kW}$$

The result is consistent with a typical 10 mm drill in mild steel at modest feed. ✔

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Specific Cutting Force from Power, MRR, and Efficiency

**Premise:** The power absorbed at the cutter $P_{cut}$ equals the product of the material removal rate, the specific cutting force $k_c$, divided by the unit-conversion factor and the machine efficiency $\eta$.

Starting from:

$$P_{cut} \;[\text{kW}] \;=\; \frac{\text{MRR} \;\;[\text{mm}^3/\text{min}] \times k_c \;\;[\text{N/mm}^2]}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$$

Solving for $k_c$:

$$k_c \;=\; \frac{P_{cut} \times 60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}{\text{MRR}}$$

**Alternative derivation from first principles** (for conceptual clarity):

The un-deformed chip cross-section in milling is $h \times b$ where $h \approx f_z \sin \kappa$ (chip thickness) and $b \approx a_p / \sin \kappa$ (chip width), with $\kappa$ the lead angle. The cutting force per unit area is $k_c$, so the tangential force is:

$$F_t \;=\; k_c \times h \times b \;\approx\; k_c \times f_z \times a_p$$

The tangential power is:

$$P_{cut} \;=\; F_t \times V_c$$

Combining:

$$P_{cut} \;=\; k_c \times f_z \times a_p \times V_c$$

Recalling that $\text{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times f_r = a_p \times f_z \times z \times N$ and that $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$, the result reduces to the compact form given above. ✔

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Designer's Process Selection

| Required Feature | Multipoint Process | DFM Justification | Watch-outs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard round hole | Drilling | Lowest cost, one operation | Specify *through* if possible |
| Accurate round hole (IT7) | Drill + Ream | Ream is a finishing op on a pre-existing hole | Leave 0.1–0.2 mm stock |
| Internal thread (large) | Tapping | Cheapest thread-making process | Needs pre-tapped hole of correct size |
| Internal thread (small / deep) | Thread milling | Better tool life than small taps | Higher machine cost |
| Internal spline / keyway | Broaching | One-pass, high accuracy | High tooling cost — only at volume |
| Open slot | Slab milling | Standard cutter widths available | None |
| Closed pocket | End milling | Plunging or helical entry | Specify entry method |
| Flat face on large surface | Face milling | High MRR with indexable inserts | Watch entry/exit marks |
| Cut-off from a bar | Sawing | Cheapest separation | Specify cut width and burrs |
| Square shoulder | End mill with square end | Sharp corner is natural | Specify corner radius = 0 if needed |

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — Combined for Practice

**Question:** A 16 mm diameter, 4-tooth end mill is used to machine a pocket in aluminium alloy. $V_c = 300$ m/min, $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 3$ mm, $a_e = 12$ mm. Calculate spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, and the time to machine a pocket 100 mm × 60 mm (assume one pass).

**Solution:**

$$N \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 16} \;=\; \frac{300\,000}{50.27} \;\approx\; 5968 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

$$f_r \;=\; f_z \times z \times N \;=\; 0.10 \times 4 \times 5968 \;\approx\; 2387 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$\text{MRR} \;=\; a_p \times a_e \times f_r \;=\; 3 \times 12 \times 2387 \;\approx\; 85\,940 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\approx\; 86 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Pocket path length** (assuming a single-pass, racetrack tool path covering the full 100 × 60 mm):

$$L_{path} \;\approx\; 2 \times (100 + 60) \;\approx\; 320 \;\; \text{mm (single loop)}$$

(For a full-area coverage, divide into stripes of width $a_e = 12$ mm — but for a single illustrative pass, the racetrack is sufficient.)

$$t_m \;\approx\; \frac{320}{2387} \;\approx\; 0.134 \;\; \text{min} \;\approx\; 8 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-of-Section-3 Mantra:** Every multipoint-machining problem reduces to *three* independent calculations — spindle speed (from $V_c, D$), feed rate (from $f_z, z, N$), and MRR (from $a_p, a_e$ or $D$, and $f_r$). Master these three and the rest is arithmetic.

# SECTION 5 — KTU EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> **Reference Block.** This section consolidates the KTU-style question bank from Section 5 of the main notes, with both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice) covered. The same model answers apply; they are re-presented here for completeness of the 5-section structure.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define multipoint cutting tool. Give two examples.

**Model Answer:**
A multipoint cutting tool is one in which **more than one cutting edge engages the workpiece simultaneously** during the cutting operation. The cutting action is distributed over several teeth, which improves productivity, distributes heat load, and allows higher material removal rates than single-point tools.

**Examples:**
1. **Twist drill** (used for hole making; two cutting edges at the tip)
2. **Milling cutter** (used for slotting, facing, profiling; multiple teeth arranged on the body)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark questions):** Always state the *defining characteristic* (more than one tooth in contact) and give *two named examples*. Avoid vague terms like "rotating tools" — the key is "multipoint."

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three DFM guidelines for designing holes to be produced by drilling.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Use standard drill sizes** (ISO 235 / IS 2441) — avoid odd sizes like 7.3 mm; use 7 mm or 7.5 mm.
2. **Avoid deep holes** — depth ≤ 4× diameter for standard drilling without pecking; design the part to permit a *through-hole* wherever possible.
3. **Locate holes on flat, perpendicular surfaces** so the drill can enter normal to the surface; avoid holes on inclined or curved faces.
4. **Provide sufficient clearance for chip evacuation** — the drilled region must be open on at least one side.
5. **Avoid specifying tighter tolerances than necessary** — drilling gives $\vert$IT$\vert$10 to $\vert$IT$\vert$12 as a free finish; IT7 requires a reaming step.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Internal Choice A)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice A] (a) With a neat sketch, explain the geometry of a twist drill. List the design rules to be followed while designing a part with drilled holes. (7 marks)**

**(b) A through hole of 20 mm diameter is to be drilled in a mild steel plate of 30 mm thickness. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and feed is 0.20 mm/rev. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) machining time per hole. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Re-presented):**

#### (a) Twist Drill Geometry & DFM Rules for Drilled Holes (7 marks)

**Geometry of a Twist Drill — Components:**

1. **Point / Tip** — the conical end with the chisel edge and two cutting lips.
2. **Cutting lips** — the two straight edges that perform the actual cutting.
3. **Chisel edge** — the short edge connecting the two lips; it pushes material but does not cut.
4. **Flutes** — the helical grooves that evacuate chips and admit cutting fluid.
5. **Body / Land** — the cylindrical portion that guides the drill in the hole.
6. **Margin** — the narrow cylindrical strip behind the cutting edge that bears on the hole wall and provides clearance.
7. **Shank** — the end that is gripped by the drill chuck; straight, tapered, or with drive flats.
8. **Neck** — the reduced-diameter section between body and shank (on longer drills) where the manufacturer marks the size.

**Key angles:**
- **Point angle** — typically 118° for general-purpose steel, 135° for hardened steel and cast iron.
- **Helix angle** — typically 24° to 32°; higher for soft materials, lower for hard/brittle.
- **Rake angle** — positive (≈ 25°–30°) on the lip for steel.

**DFM Rules for Parts with Drilled Holes:**

1. **Use standard metric drill sizes** — refer to the ISO preferred series.
2. **Specify through-holes** rather than blind holes wherever functional requirements allow.
3. **Limit depth to ≤ 4 × diameter** for standard drilling; deeper holes need peck cycles and special drills.
4. **Locate holes on flat, perpendicular surfaces** — never on curved, sloped, or thin sections.
5. **Maintain minimum web / wall thickness** — at least 1.5 × hole diameter between hole edge and part edge or between two adjacent holes.
6. **Avoid specifying holes at sharp corners** — maintain a chamfer or radius on the entry side.
7. **Hole-to-hole and hole-to-edge distances** must accommodate the drill's *point angle* — entry side needs more clearance than exit side.
8. **Avoid interrupted cuts** (drilling across a slot or across a parting line) — they break drills.

> **Sketch of twist drill (text representation):**
>
> ```
>         /\
>        /  \  <-- point (118 degrees)
>       /    \
>      |  ||  |  <-- cutting lips and chisel edge
>      | //// |  <-- flutes (helical)
>      |//   /|
>      |   /  |  <-- body / lands
>      | /neck|  <-- neck (marked with size)
>      |/_____|
>        shank
> ```

#### (b) Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm, $L = 30$ mm, $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.20$ mm/rev.

**(i) Spindle speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{25\,000}{62.832} \approx 397.9 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 398 \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed rate $f_r$:**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.20 \times 397.9 \approx 79.6 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 80 \;\; \text{mm/min}}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 79.6 = 100\pi \times 79.6 \approx 25\,007 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 25\,000 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (\text{or } 25 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

**(iv) Machining time per hole** (with overrun $\delta = 2$ mm):

$$t_m = \frac{L + \delta}{f_r} = \frac{32}{79.6} \approx 0.402 \;\; \text{min} \approx 24.1 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 0.40 \;\; \text{min} \;\; (24 \;\; \text{s per hole})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. *Forgetting the 1000 conversion factor* in the $V_c$ formula — a 1000× error.
> 2. *Forgetting the overrun* $\delta$ in the time calculation — the drill must clear the workpiece.
> 3. *Unit confusion* — MRR must come out in mm³/min, not mm²/min or cm³/s.
> 4. *Sketch missing or unlabelled* — the geometry diagram is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice B)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice B] (a) Explain the principle of face milling and peripheral milling with sketches. Compare the two in terms of (i) cutting action, (ii) surface finish obtained, and (iii) typical applications. (7 marks)**

**(b) In a face-milling operation, a 100 mm diameter cutter with 8 inserts is used on a mild-steel workpiece. The cutting speed is 200 m/min, feed per tooth is 0.20 mm/tooth, and depth of cut is 4 mm. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) specific cutting force $k_c$ if the required power is 7.5 kW at 80% efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Re-presented):**

#### (a) Face vs. Peripheral Milling (7 marks)

**Peripheral (Slab) Milling:**
- The cutter rotates about an axis *parallel* to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting teeth are on the *cylindrical periphery* of the cutter.
- The cutter removes material from the *side*, producing a slot or step.
- Only one or a few teeth are in cut at any instant.
- Surface finish is governed by feed per tooth $f_z$ and the tooth geometry.

**Face Milling:**
- The cutter rotates about an axis *perpendicular* to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting inserts are mounted on the *face* (bottom) of the cutter.
- The cutter removes material from the *top*, producing a flat face.
- Multiple inserts are typically in contact simultaneously — gives a smoother finish.
- Inserts are indexable (replaceable), giving low tooling cost per part.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Peripheral Milling | Face Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutter axis** | Parallel to workpiece | Perpendicular to workpiece |
| **Cutting location** | Cylindrical periphery | Bottom face |
| **Typical feature** | Slots, steps, profiles | Flat faces, large surfaces |
| **Number of teeth in contact** | 1 to few | Several (depends on $a_e$) |
| **Surface finish** | Moderate; depends on $f_z$ | Good; multiple teeth cut simultaneously |
| **Chip thickness** | Varies (entry → steady → exit) | Varies (entry → steady → exit) but with multiple teeth |
| **Application** | Slot milling, side milling | Facing, squaring, roughing of large areas |

#### (b) Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm, $z = 8$, $V_c = 200$ m/min, $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 4$ mm, $a_e = 100$ mm, $P = 7.5$ kW, $\eta = 0.80$.

**(i) Spindle speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{200\,000}{314.16} \approx 636.6 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 637 \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed rate $f_r$:**

$$f_r = 0.20 \times 8 \times 636.6 \approx 1018.6 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 1019 \;\; \text{mm/min}}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = 4 \times 100 \times 1018.6 \approx 407\,440 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 4.07 \times 10^5 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (\approx 407 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

**(iv) Specific cutting force $k_c$:**

$$P_{cut} = P \times \eta = 7.5 \times 0.80 = 6.0 \;\; \text{kW} = 6.0 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{N·mm/s}$$

$$\text{MRR in mm}^3/\text{s} = \frac{407\,440}{60} = 6790.7 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$P_{cut} = \text{MRR} \times k_c \;\Rightarrow\; k_c = \frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{6790.7} \approx 884 \;\; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{k_c \approx 884 \;\; \text{N/mm}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. *Not including the efficiency $\eta$* — the motor delivers 7.5 kW, but only 80% reaches the cutter.
> 2. *Unit slip in the power–MRR equation* — verify that mm³/min and N·mm/s are reconciled by a factor of 60.
> 3. *Forgetting that $a_e$ is the radial engagement*, not the tool diameter.
> 4. *Truncating the spindle speed* — round to 637 rpm; do not write 636 rpm (the rounding is part of the marks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Consolidated Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Multipoint Tools**

- ✅ Multipoint tools have **more than one cutting edge in simultaneous engagement** with the workpiece.
- ✅ The **four canonical processes** are *Milling, Drilling, Reaming, Tapping* (rotational) and *Broaching, Sawing* (linear).
- ✅ **Cutting speed $V_c$** is always in **m/min**; **spindle speed $N$** in **rpm**; conversion is $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$.
- ✅ **Feed per tooth $f_z$** is the *fundamental* variable in milling — it determines chip thickness, surface finish, and tool life.
- ✅ **MRR in milling** $= a_p \times a_e \times f_r$ (rectangular sweep).
- ✅ **MRR in drilling** $= (\pi D^2 / 4) \times f_r$ (cylindrical sweep).
- ✅ **Drilling torque** $T = F_f \times D / 2$ ; **power** $P = 2 \pi N T / (60 \times 10^6)$ in kW.
- ✅ **Broaching** is a *one-pass* linear process used for internal splines, keyways, and high-volume finishing — high tooling cost, low per-part cost.
- ✅ **DFM rules**: prefer *standard* sizes, *through* features, *open* pockets, *flat* surfaces, and *external* features.
- ✅ **Hole depth limit**: $L/D \le 4$ for standard drilling; deeper holes need pecking, gun drills, or design changes.
- ✅ **Drill point angle**: 118° for steel, 135° for hardened/cast iron.
- ✅ **Reaming tolerance**: $\vert$IT$\vert$7 to $\vert$IT$\vert$9 with $R_a \le 0.8 \,\mu$m — best when hole is pre-machined to within 0.1–0.2 mm of final size.
- ✅ **Tap drill formula** (for steel): $D_{drill} = D_{nominal} - P$, where $P$ is the thread pitch.
- ✅ **Standard drill series** follows ISO 235 (metric) — memorize the 1 mm step sizes between 3 and 25 mm.
- ✅ **Feed direction in milling**: *conventional* (up) vs. *climb* (down) — climb milling gives better finish, conventional gives safer entry.
- ✅ **Indexable inserts** in face milling keep tooling cost low for high-volume production.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *In multipoint machining, the tool is the constraint — design the part to fit the tool, not the tool to fit the part.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Turning Operation: Process Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Turning Operation: Process Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Turning** is a fundamental **subtractive manufacturing (material removal) process** in which a single-point cutting tool, having a definite geometry, is fed linearly against a workpiece rotating about a fixed axis to produce cylindrical, conical, or flat surfaces by progressive removal of chips.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Design for Manufacture and Assembly, turning is classified as a **chipless-to-chips forming process** dominated by a **continuous chip formation mechanism** under steady-state orthogonal (or oblique) cutting conditions. The operation is performed on a machine tool called a **Lathe**, which is the mother of all machine tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The turning process is the *primary reference* process for understanding DFM principles related to rotational symmetry parts, axisymmetric components, and rotational part families. Every KTU examination from this module expects the student to internalize the **three kinematic motions** and the **basic tool-workpiece interaction geometry**.

## 1.2 The Three Kinematic Motions of Turning

Turning is governed by three fundamental, simultaneous motions:

1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** Rotation of the workpiece at a controlled angular velocity about the spindle axis. This is the motion that produces the cutting velocity **V** in m/min.
2. **Secondary Motion (Feed Motion):** Linear translation of the cutting tool along (or parallel to) the axis of rotation. The magnitude per spindle revolution is the **feed rate f** in mm/rev.
3. **Tertiary Motion (Depth of Cut):** Radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece surface. The magnitude is the **depth of cut d** in mm.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Potter's Wheel Analogy:** Imagine a potter shaping clay on a spinning wheel. The **clay rotates** (primary motion), the potter's **hand moves linearly** along the rotation axis to shape the side wall (secondary motion), and the **pressure of the fingers** determines how much clay is removed/reshaped (tertiary motion). A lathe in turning is essentially the same — but the "clay" is hardened metal and the "fingers" are a hardened carbide tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Vegetable Peeler Analogy:** Think of peeling a long, cylindrical carrot while rotating it between your palms. The blade moves sideways (feed) while the carrot spins (cutting speed), and the depth you press the blade is the depth of cut. The carrot shavings (chips) curl away — exactly as a continuous chip forms in metal turning.

## 1.4 Classification of Turning Operations

Turning on a lathe encompasses a wide family of operations:

| Operation | Description | Surface Produced |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Turning** | Tool moves parallel to axis | Cylindrical surface |
| **Facing** | Tool moves radially inward | Flat end surface |
| **Taper Turning** | Tool moves at an angle to axis | Conical surface |
| **Thread Cutting** | Synchronized tool-workpiece rotation | Helical thread |
| **Parting (Cut-off)** | Tool advances radially to part off | Groove separation |
| **Grooving** | Tool plunges radially | Recessed groove |
| **Knurling** | Hardened rollers imprint pattern | Textured surface |
| **Boring** | Tool rotates inside a hole | Enlarged bore |
| **Drilling** | Axial tool rotation | Internal cylindrical hole |
| **Reaming** | Finishing of pre-drilled hole | Accurate finish bore |
| **Form Turning** | Tool shaped to contour | Complex profile |

## 1.5 Significance in DFM Context

In **Design for Manufacture and Assembly**, turning-specific design rules emerge directly from the process description:

- The **rotational symmetry requirement** means part features should be designed around the spindle axis.
- The **tool accessibility constraint** implies internal corners must allow tool nose radius clearance.
- The **continuous chip formation** dictates that deep cavities requiring chip evacuation must be designed with **through-features** wherever possible.
- The **single-point tool geometry** directly controls achievable **surface finish, dimensional tolerance, and form accuracy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Constant / Standard Metric:** The standard **cutting speed range** for mild steel with HSS tooling is **20–30 m/min**, and with carbide tooling is **150–250 m/min**. The standard **feed range** is **0.1–0.5 mm/rev** for roughing and **0.05–0.15 mm/rev** for finishing. The standard **depth of cut** is **2–5 mm** for roughing and **0.5–1.0 mm** for finishing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Process Mechanics of Turning

The turning process can be described in terms of six sequential sub-events, each of which has direct design implications:

1. **Work Holding and Centering:** The raw stock (bar, forging, casting) is clamped in a **3-jaw/4-jaw chuck, collet, or between centers**. The accuracy of centring determines the **runout (TIR – Total Indicator Reading)** and hence the **concentricity tolerance** of the turned part.
2. **Spindle Acceleration and Stabilization:** The workpiece is brought to a steady rotational speed **N (rpm)** governed by the selected cutting speed **V** and the instantaneous diameter **D**.
3. **Tool Engagement and Chip Formation:** The cutting edge engages the workpiece. A **continuous chip** (for ductile materials) or **discontinuous chip** (for brittle materials) forms by **shear deformation** along the **primary shear zone** at an angle φ (shear angle).
4. **Steady-State Material Removal:** The tool advances at feed **f**; the uncut chip thickness is **t₁ = f·sin(κᵣ)** where κᵣ is the **tool cutting edge angle**. The uncut chip width is **w = d / sin(κᵣ)**.
5. **Tool Wear Progression:** Flank wear (VB) and crater wear (KT) grow with cutting time, eventually exceeding the **tool life criterion** (typically VB = 0.3 mm for HSS, VB = 0.5 mm for carbide).
6. **Retraction and Indexing:** The tool retracts; the worn insert is indexed (multi-insert tool) or replaced; the cycle repeats.

## 2.2 The Cutting Velocity Geometry

The instantaneous cutting speed at the tool-workpiece contact point is tangential to the rotation circle:

$$V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(m/min, with D in m and N in rpm)}$$

Equivalently, solving for spindle speed:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(rpm, with V in m/min and D in mm)}$$

Because the diameter changes continuously from D₀ (initial) to D_f (final) during a turning pass, an **average diameter** D_avg = (D₀ + D_f)/2 is used for spindle speed selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the geometry matters in DFM:** A designer who specifies a **large diameter change** in a single pass forces the CNC controller to use a **constant surface speed (CSS) mode**, varying N throughout the pass. This is acceptable for modern CNC lathes, but on older machines it leads to either over-speeding (danger) or under-speeding (poor finish). **DFM rule:** Limit diameter reduction per pass to manageable ratios, and prefer multiple moderate passes over a single aggressive pass.

## 2.3 Material Removal Rate (MRR) and Process Time

The **Material Removal Rate** is the volume of metal removed per unit time:

$$\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

where V_c is in mm/min, f in mm/rev, and d in mm. For a longitudinal turning pass reducing a bar from D₀ to D_f over length L, the **actual pass time** is:

$$T_{pass} = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V \cdot f} \quad \text{(min)}$$

The **number of roughing passes** required is:

$$n_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2 \cdot d_{per\,pass}} \right\rceil$$

## 2.4 Cutting Force and Power

The **principal cutting force** Fc acts tangentially in the direction of cutting velocity. The **feed force** Ff acts along the feed direction, and the **radial (thrust) force** Fr acts perpendicular to the machined surface. Empirical relations:

$$F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(N)}$$

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

where K_s is the **specific cutting force** in N/mm² (a material-dependent constant tabulated in manufacturing handbooks).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table thoroughly** — it is the single most-tested numerical cluster in KTU ESE papers for Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \pi D N / 1000$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = 1000V / (\pi D)$ | rpm | Most frequently used |
| 3 | Feed Rate (table) | $f_t = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Linear feed velocity |
| 4 | Uncut Chip Thickness | $t_1 = f \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)$ | mm | $\kappa_r$ = tool cutting edge angle |
| 5 | Uncut Chip Width | $w = d / \sin(\kappa_r)$ | mm | d = depth of cut |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR} = V_t \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | Volume removed per minute |
| 7 | Pass Time | $T_{pass} = L / (f \cdot N)$ | min | For a pass of length L |
| 8 | Principal Cutting Force | $F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ | N | K_s = specific cutting force |
| 9 | Power at Spindle | $P_c = F_c \cdot V / (60{,}000)$ | kW | V in m/min |
| 10 | Taylor Tool Life | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | — | T = tool life in min |
| 11 | Chip Thickness Ratio | $r = t_1 / t_2$ | — | $r < 1$ (chip thickens) |
| 12 | Shear Angle | $\tan\phi = r \cos\alpha / (1 - r \sin\alpha)$ | rad | Merchant's relation |
| 13 | Surface Roughness (ideal) | $R_a = f^2 / (8 R)$ | µm | R = tool nose radius |
| 14 | Number of Passes | $n = \lceil (D_0 - D_f)/(2d) \rceil$ | — | Round up to integer |
| 15 | Volumetric Metal Rate | $Q = \pi (D_0^2 - D_f^2) L / 4$ | mm³ | Total volume to remove |

**Note on the table:** The symbol $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is rendered using the LaTeX command `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic exercises — they drive:

- **CNC Programming:** Direct input parameters (G96 CSS, G97 RPM, F feed).
- **Process Planning:** Time estimation for cost quoting in a manufacturing unit.
- **Fixture Design:** Determining clamping forces (rule of thumb: radial force Fr ≈ 0.4·Fc).
- **Tool Selection:** Taylor's equation drives the trade-off between speed and tool life.
- **Energy Audits:** Spindle power determines electricity consumption and machine tool selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Example:** For a CNC turning cell producing EN8 steel shafts, the typical values are V = 180 m/min, f = 0.25 mm/rev, d = 2.5 mm, K_s = 2000 N/mm². Computing Fc gives Fc = 2000 × 0.25 × 2.5 = **1250 N**, and Pc = 1250 × 180/60,000 = **3.75 kW**. A 5 kW spindle is selected with a 25% safety margin — a routine DFM decision driven directly by the formulas above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material Removal Rate (MRR)

The **MRR** represents the volume swept by the tool per unit time. We derive it from first principles using the cutting geometry.

**Step 1 — Identify the swept volume element per revolution.**

In one spindle revolution, the tool advances axially by a distance **f** (the feed per revolution). Simultaneously, the tool has penetrated radially by **d**. The uncut chip cross-section (projected on a plane containing the spindle axis) is a rectangle of area:

$$A_{chip} = f \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the circumferential length swept per minute.**

In one minute, the workpiece surface sweeps a circumferential length of $\pi D N$ (with N in rpm) past the tool. Equivalently, we can use the table feed velocity $f_t = f \cdot N$ and the cutting velocity $V = \pi D N / 1000$. The MRR is the chip area multiplied by the rate at which new material is presented:

$$\text{MRR} = A_{chip} \cdot f_t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\text{MRR} = (f \cdot d) \cdot (f \cdot N) \quad \text{(incorrect — this is a known student trap)}$$

**Correction:** The MRR is the chip cross-sectional area multiplied by the **cutting velocity** (or, equivalently, multiplied by the table feed per minute). Using cutting velocity:

$$\text{MRR} = (f \cdot d) \cdot V_c \quad \text{where } V_c = \pi D N \text{ in mm/min}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

Or, in the more common form using V in m/min:

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit conversion safeguard:** Always verify units before multiplying. The factor **1000** appears when V is in m/min but the chip area (f·d) is in mm², and we want mm³/min. If V is already in mm/min, no factor is needed.

## 3.2 Derivation of Taylor's Tool Life Equation

Taylor's empirical equation relates cutting speed to tool life, and is the foundation of all economic speed selection.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.**

Frederick W. Taylor (1907) observed that for a given tool-workpiece pair and fixed feed and depth, the relationship between cutting speed V and tool life T can be fitted by a power law in log-log space.

**Step 2 — Logarithmic form.**

Taking the natural log of $V \cdot T^n = C$:

$$\ln V + n \ln T = \ln C$$

This is linear in $(\ln T, \ln V)$ with slope $-n$ and intercept $\ln C$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate to find sensitivity.**

If $V_1$ and $V_2$ are two cutting speeds with corresponding tool lives $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$V_1 T_1^n = V_2 T_2^n = C$$

Dividing the two equations:

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^n$$

**Step 4 — Solve for exponent n from two data points.**

Taking logarithms:

$$n = \frac{\ln(V_1/V_2)}{\ln(T_2/T_1)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for C.**

$$C = V_1 \cdot T_1^n$$

**Example Numerical Application:**

Given: At V₁ = 200 m/min, T₁ = 30 min. At V₂ = 150 m/min, T₂ = 90 min.

$$n = \frac{\ln(200/150)}{\ln(90/30)} = \frac{\ln(1.333)}{\ln(3)} = \frac{0.2874}{1.0986} = 0.2616$$

$$C = 200 \cdot (30)^{0.2616} = 200 \cdot 2.476 = 495.2$$

So Taylor's equation for this tool-workpiece pair is:

$$V \cdot T^{0.2616} = 495.2$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Surface Roughness in Turning (Ideal Case)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the feed mark.**

The tool nose has a radius R. As the tool advances by feed f per revolution, the previously machined surface is left with a periodic corrugation. The peak-to-valley height of this corrugation is:

$$h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (f/2)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-feed approximation.**

For $f \ll R$, expand using the binomial series $\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - x^2/2$ with $x = f/(2R)$:

$$h \approx R - R\left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{f}{2R}\right)^2\right] = \frac{f^2}{8R}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Centre-Line Average (CLA / Ra).**

For a sinusoidal feed mark, $R_a = (4/\pi) \cdot h_{avg} \approx h/2$ for the geometric profile. Using the empirical factor of 1/4 (which is standard for sinusoidal geometry):

$$R_a = \frac{f^2}{32R} \quad \text{(with practical factor, commonly cited as } f^2/(8R) \text{ for peak-to-valley)}$$

The more common textbook form for peak-to-valley roughness is:

$$\boxed{R_t = \frac{f^2}{8R} \quad \text{and} \quad R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{32R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DFM Implication:** If a designer specifies a **surface finish of Ra = 0.8 µm**, with a standard tool nose radius of R = 0.8 mm, the maximum feed is:

$$f = \sqrt{32 R R_a} = \sqrt{32 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.8} = \sqrt{20.48} \approx 4.5 \text{ mm/rev}$$

Wait — this is far too high. The correct limit is obtained from the **R_t form** because real surfaces include other factors (workpiece material, built-up edge, vibrations). For practical purposes, the standard feed for Ra = 0.8 µm with R = 0.8 mm is approximately **f = 0.1 mm/rev**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Shear Angle (Merchant's Analysis)

**Step 1 — Force balance on the chip.**

The cutting force Fc, thrust force Ft, and friction force F act on the chip. The shear force Fs acts along the shear plane at angle φ to the cutting velocity.

**Step 2 — Geometry of chip formation.**

The uncut chip thickness is $t_1 = f \sin \kappa_r$ and the cut chip thickness is $t_2$. The shear angle φ relates them:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha}$$

where r = t₁/t₂ is the **cutting ratio** and α is the **rake angle**.

**Step 3 — Merchant's shear angle solution.**

Merchant equated the work done in the shear plane to the work done in the friction plane and obtained the relation for minimum energy:

$$\boxed{\phi = 45^\circ + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}$$

where β is the **friction angle** at the tool-chip interface, given by $\tan \beta = F/(N+F_c\sin\alpha)$ where N is the normal force on the rake face.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameter Interrelationships

The following matrix summarizes **how a change in one process parameter affects the others**. This is a high-yield KTU analysis tool.

| Parameter Increased ↓ affects → | Cutting Speed V | Feed f | Depth d | Spindle N | Tool Life T | Surface Finish | Power | Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed V** | — | — | — | ↑ proportionally | ↓ sharply (T^n) | Slight improvement | ↑ linearly | Slight ↑ |
| **Feed f** | — | — | — | — | ↓ moderately | Worsens (R_a ∝ f²) | ↑ linearly | ↑ linearly |
| **Depth d** | — | — | — | — | ↓ slightly | Slight effect | ↑ linearly | ↑ linearly |
| **Workpiece Hardness** | Limited by tool | — | — | — | ↓↓ drastically | Variable | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| **Tool Nose Radius R** | — | — | — | — | Slight ↑ | Improves (R_a ∝ 1/R) | Slight ↑ | Slight ↑ |
| **Rake Angle α** | — | — | — | — | ↑ with positive α | Variable | ↓ with positive α | ↓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Turning Process

This Mermaid diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of a turning operation — from input stock to finished turned component. It is the standard KTU-style architectural representation suitable for Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Stock Bar Casting Forging] --> A1[Workholding Device Chuck Collet Centers]
    A1 --> A2[Spindle Drive Motor]
    A2 --> A3[Rotational Primary Motion At Speed N rpm]
    A3 --> A4[Workpiece Rotating With Diameter D]
    B0[CNC Controller G Code Interpreter] --> B1[Feed Drive Motor]
    B1 --> B2[Linear Secondary Motion At Feed f mm per rev]
    B2 --> B3[Tool Carriage Advancing Along Z Axis]
    C0[Tool Magazine Tool Presetter] --> C1[Single Point Cutting Tool With Nose Radius R]
    C1 --> C2[Radial Tertiary Motion Depth d mm]
    C2 --> C3[Tool Engages Workpiece At Rake Angle alpha]
    A3 --> D1[Cutting Interface Shear Zone]
    B2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Chip Formation Continuous Or Discontinuous]
    D2 --> D3[Heat Generation In Shear Zone]
    D3 --> D4[Cutting Fluid Application Flood Coolant]
    D4 --> D5[Cooling Lubrication Chip Evacuation]
    A4 --> E1[Finished Turned Surface]
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Quality Inspection Surface Finish Dimensional Tolerance]
    E2 --> F1[Accept Or Reject]
    F1 --> F2[Feedback To CNC For Adjustment]
    D2 --> G1[Chip Disposal Conveyor Or Tray]
    D3 --> H1[Tool Wear Monitoring Flank And Crater]
    H1 --> I1[Tool Life Decision Replace Or Continue]
    I1 --> C0
```

```mermaid
    J0 --> J2[Feed Force Ff Along Feed Direction]
    J0 --> J3[Radial Thrust Force Fr Perpendicular To Surface]
    J1 --> K1[Power Consumption Pc Equals Fc Times V]
    J1 --> K2[Heat Generation In Primary Shear Zone]
    J2 --> K3[Table Feed Drive Loading]

```

This block diagram illustrates the **causal hierarchy** from raw material selection to final surface integrity in turning.

