# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Turning and State its Process Parameters

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Turning is a material removal process in which a single-point cutting tool removes material from a rotating workpiece to produce a surface of desired shape, size, and finish. The workpiece is held in a chuck or between centres and rotated at a controlled speed, while the cutting tool is fed against it.

**Process Parameters (1.5 marks):**
The three primary process parameters of turning are:

1. **Cutting Speed (V)** — the tangential surface speed at the tool-workpiece contact, in m/min.
2. **Feed (f)** — the linear distance the tool advances per spindle revolution, in mm/rev.
3. **Depth of Cut (d)** — the radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece, in mm.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate Between Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Motions in Turning

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Motion | Type | Direction | Purpose | Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Motion** | Rotational | About spindle axis | Generates cutting velocity | N (rpm) |
| **Secondary Motion** | Linear | Parallel to spindle axis | Tool feed | f (mm/rev) |
| **Tertiary Motion** | Linear | Radial (perpendicular to axis) | Penetration / depth | d (mm) |

**Key Statement (1 mark):** The **primary motion is mandatory** for cutting to occur. The **secondary and tertiary motions** are *relative motions* and together they determine the geometry of the machined surface.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Turning Process Analysis and Parameter Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester, Module 3 — 14 Marks]**

A mild steel (EN8) shaft of initial diameter 60 mm is to be reduced to 52 mm over a length of 200 mm by turning on a high-speed lathe with a HSS tool. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and the feed is 0.25 mm/rev. The depth of cut per pass is 2 mm. The specific cutting force K_s for mild steel is 2000 N/mm². The Taylor's tool life equation for the tool is V·T^0.25 = 400, where V is in m/min and T is in minutes.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **spindle speed**, the **material removal rate (MRR)**, and the **cutting time per pass**. State the number of roughing passes required.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **principal cutting force (Fc)**, the **spindle power (Pc)**, and the **tool life** for the chosen cutting speed. Comment on whether the speed should be reduced for safety.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Spindle Speed, MRR, and Cutting Time**

**Step 1: Average diameter for the first pass.**

$$D_{avg} = \frac{D_0 + D_f}{2} = \frac{60 + 56}{2} = 58 \text{ mm}$$

(Initial reduction: 60 mm → 56 mm with d = 2 mm per pass)

**Step 2: Spindle speed.**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 58} = \frac{25000}{182.21} = 137.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR).**

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d = 1000 \times 25 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 12{,}500 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 4: Cutting time per pass.**

$$T_{pass} = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{200}{0.25 \times 137.2} = \frac{200}{34.3} = 5.83 \text{ min/pass}$$

**Step 5: Number of roughing passes.**

Total reduction in radius = (60 − 52)/2 = 4 mm. With d = 2 mm per pass:

$$n = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ passes}$$

(Subsequent pass: D_avg = (56 + 52)/2 = 54 mm, N = 1000×25/(π×54) = 147.4 rpm)

**Answer (a):** N₁ = 137.2 rpm, MRR = 12,500 mm³/min, T_pass = 5.83 min, **n = 2 passes**.

---

**Part (b) — Cutting Force, Power, and Tool Life**

**Step 1: Principal cutting force.**

$$F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 2000 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Spindle power.**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60{,}000} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{60{,}000} = 0.4167 \text{ kW} \approx 0.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Tool life at V = 25 m/min.**

From Taylor's equation: $V \cdot T^{0.25} = 400$

$$25 \cdot T^{0.25} = 400 \implies T^{0.25} = 16 \implies T = 16^4 = 65{,}536 \text{ min}$$

This is an impractically large value because the HSS tool is operating well below its capacity. **In reality**, the HSS tool is operating at the lower limit of its recommended range.

**Step 4: Verification of cutting speed feasibility.**

For HSS on mild steel, the typical V = 25 m/min is at the **lower end of the HSS range** (20–30 m/min) and the **upper end for very conservative roughing**. The cutting is feasible, but the tool life will be very long, which is acceptable. For higher productivity, **carbide tooling** (V = 150–200 m/min) is preferred.

**Step 5: Safety comment.**

The selected speed yields a low spindle power requirement (0.42 kW), well within the capacity of even a small lathe. **No safety reduction is needed.** However, chip control should be verified, as low cutting speeds on mild steel can produce a **continuous chip with built-up edge**, degrading surface finish.

**Answer (b):** Fc = 1000 N, Pc = 0.42 kW, T = 65,536 min (impractically long, indicating under-utilization of the HSS tool). **Recommendation: No speed reduction needed; consider carbide tooling for higher productivity.**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Tool Geometry and Surface Finish

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, End Semester, Module 3 — 14 Marks]**

A finishing operation on an aluminium alloy (specific cutting force K_s = 750 N/mm²) requires a surface finish of Ra = 0.8 µm. The tool nose radius available is R = 0.8 mm, and the feed is f = 0.1 mm/rev. The recommended cutting speed for carbide tooling on aluminium is 300 m/min, and the depth of cut is 0.5 mm.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Verify whether the chosen feed satisfies the surface finish requirement. If not, suggest a corrective action.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **spindle speed**, the **principal cutting force**, and the **power consumed** at a workpiece diameter of 40 mm. Discuss the DFM implications of the chosen feed-depth combination.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Surface Finish Verification**

**Step 1: Theoretical peak-to-valley roughness from the chosen feed and nose radius.**

$$R_t = \frac{f^2}{8R} = \frac{(0.1)^2}{8 \times 0.8} = \frac{0.01}{6.4} = 0.0015625 \text{ mm} = 1.56 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 2: Convert peak-to-valley to centre-line average (CLA) roughness.**

Using the empirical relation $R_a \approx R_t / 4$ for a sinusoidal feed mark:

$$R_a = \frac{1.56}{4} = 0.39 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 3: Compare with the required roughness.**

Required: Ra = 0.8 µm. Achieved: Ra = 0.39 µm. **The chosen feed is acceptable** — the achieved roughness is actually better (lower) than required. The designer has a margin of approximately 2×.

**Step 4: Check the maximum allowable feed if the requirement were tighter.**

If Ra ≤ 0.4 µm were required, the maximum feed would be:

$$f_{max} = \sqrt{32 \cdot R \cdot R_a} = \sqrt{32 \times 0.8 \times 0.4} = \sqrt{10.24} = 3.2 \text{ mm/rev}$$

But this is impractically large. Using the R_t form for stricter control:

$$f_{max} = \sqrt{8 \cdot R \cdot R_t} = \sqrt{8 \times 0.8 \times 4 \times R_a} = \sqrt{8 \times 0.8 \times 1.6} = \sqrt{10.24} \approx 3.2 \text{ mm/rev}$$

The practical answer is that the current feed is fine, and the **tool nose radius is the controlling factor** for very fine finishes.

**Answer (a):** Achieved Ra = 0.39 µm < Required 0.8 µm. **The chosen feed is acceptable**, with a 2× safety margin. No corrective action needed; however, the feed could be increased to f = 0.15 mm/rev to reduce cycle time without violating the surface finish.

---

**Part (b) — Spindle Speed, Cutting Force, and Power**

**Step 1: Spindle speed at D = 40 mm.**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{300{,}000}{125.66} = 2387.3 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Principal cutting force.**

$$F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 750 \times 0.1 \times 0.5 = 37.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Spindle power.**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60{,}000} = \frac{37.5 \times 300}{60{,}000} = \frac{11{,}250}{60{,}000} = 0.1875 \text{ kW} \approx 0.19 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: DFM implications of feed-depth combination.**

The chosen combination (f = 0.1 mm/rev, d = 0.5 mm) is appropriate for **finishing**: low feed for surface finish, low depth for accuracy. The MRR is:

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \times 300 \times 0.1 \times 0.5 = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

This MRR is moderate, consistent with a finishing pass. The cutting force is low (37.5 N), so workpiece deflection is negligible — good for thin shafts. The cutting power is small (0.19 kW), within the capacity of any standard CNC lathe.

**DFM Recommendations:**

1. **Combine roughing and finishing into one setup** to avoid reclamping errors.
2. **Use a larger nose radius (R = 1.2 mm or 1.6 mm)** if the design permits, to allow a higher feed for the same surface finish.
3. **Use a chip-breaker geometry** on the insert to prevent long stringy aluminium chips from entangling the tool and workpiece.
4. **Apply flood coolant** despite aluminium's low cutting temperature, to evacuate chips and prevent re-cutting.

**Answer (b):** N = 2387 rpm, Fc = 37.5 N, Pc = 0.19 kW. The DFM combination is appropriate; the process is safe, low-deflection, and produces the required finish with margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (each costs 1–3 marks in KTU valuation):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1000** when V is given in m/min but the MRR must be in mm³/min. The examiner deducts a full mark for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing feed per revolution (f) with feed rate (f_t = f·N).** These are different quantities. Mixing them up gives a wrong MRR.
> 3. **Using final diameter instead of average diameter** to compute the spindle speed for a roughing pass. The examiner specifically tests whether the student recognizes the change in diameter during the pass.
> 4. **Ignoring rake angle in uncut chip thickness calculation.** The uncut chip thickness is $t_1 = f \sin \kappa_r$, not simply $f$. For $\kappa_r = 90°$ (straight turning), $t_1 = f$, but for $\kappa_r = 45°$, $t_1 = 0.707 f$.
> 5. **Confusing Taylor's tool life with the actual tool wear curve.** Taylor's equation is empirical and applies to a single wear mechanism (usually flank wear VB). It does not predict chipping or catastrophic failure.
> 6. **Using V in m/s instead of m/min in power calculation.** Power formula $P_c = F_c V / 60{,}000$ requires V in m/min. With V in m/s, the divisor becomes 1000.
> 7. **Forgetting to round up the number of passes.** A calculated 2.3 passes must be rounded up to 3 passes. A common pitfall is to use the fractional value, which is non-physical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Design for Turning Operation: Process Description**

- **Turning** = Material removal by single-point tool on rotating workpiece; performed on a **lathe**.
- **Three kinematic motions:** Primary (rotation, N rpm), Secondary (feed, f mm/rev), Tertiary (depth, d mm).
- **Primary equation:** $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$ — always check units.
- **MRR:** $1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$ in mm³/min (when V is in m/min).
- **Cutting force:** $F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ — proportional to feed and depth.
- **Spindle power:** $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60{,}000$ in kW (V in m/min).
- **Taylor's tool life:** $V \cdot T^n = C$ — log-log linear relationship.
- **Surface finish:** $R_t = f^2/(8R)$; better finish with smaller feed and larger nose radius.
- **Shear angle (Merchant):** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.
- **Number of passes:** $n = \lceil (D_0 - D_f)/(2d) \rceil$ — always round up.
- **DFM turning rules:** Design for rotational symmetry; avoid sharp internal corners (use fillets equal to tool nose radius); use standard diameters; design for through-features when possible; use uniform wall thickness to minimize deflection; specify surface finish that is consistent with the chosen feed (use the formula to back-calculate f).
- **Operation types on a lathe:** Straight turning, facing, taper turning, threading, knurling, parting, grooving, boring, drilling, reaming, form turning.
- **Standard cutting speed ranges:** Mild steel with HSS: 20–30 m/min; Mild steel with carbide: 150–250 m/min; Aluminium with carbide: 200–400 m/min.
- **Standard feed ranges:** Roughing: 0.1–0.5 mm/rev; Finishing: 0.05–0.15 mm/rev.
- **Standard depth of cut:** Roughing: 2–5 mm; Finishing: 0.5–1.0 mm.
- **Key DFM trade-off:** Higher V → lower tool life; higher f → better productivity but worse finish; higher d → higher productivity but more deflection and force.
- **The three cutting force components:** Fc (tangential, power-consuming), Ff (axial, along feed), Fr (radial, causes deflection).
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always state the **average diameter** used in spindle speed calculation. Always show the **unit conversion steps**. Always **round up** the number of passes. Always include the **formula** before substituting numbers — this earns the method mark even if the arithmetic is faulty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Characteristics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Typical Characteristics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, *Typical Characteristics* refer to the standardized set of design attributes, geometric features, and decision-making heuristics that govern whether a product can be efficiently produced (manufactured) and rapidly joined (assembled). These characteristics are derived from the systematic methodologies of **Boothroyd-Dewhurst**, **Hitachi**, **Lucas**, and **DFA++** evaluation systems.

*Typical Applications* denote the real-time industrial scenarios, product domains (automotive, electronics, consumer durables, aerospace), and engineering case studies where these characteristics are practically deployed to minimize total production cost, reduce time-to-market, and improve product quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> "Typical characteristics in DFMA are the quantifiable and qualitative design guidelines that influence the **DfA Index**, **Design Efficiency (η)**, **Assembly Time**, and **Manufacturing Cost** of a product, while their applications are the strategic deployment of these guidelines across the product development lifecycle."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The LEGO Brick System**
> Imagine you are building a table using **traditional carpentry** (nails, screws, custom joints) versus assembling a **LEGO table** (modular snap-fit parts, single-action insertion, no fasteners). The LEGO table is the embodiment of ideal DFMA characteristics: every part has a *self-locating feature*, a *single axis of insertion*, and a *symmetrical geometry*. It requires no glue, no tools, and the assembly time is the *theoretical minimum*. Now, in this analogy, DFMA characteristics are the **"design DNA"** that make the LEGO approach possible, and their **applications** are the industries (toy, automotive, consumer electronics) that have adopted this modular, easy-to-assemble philosophy.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used to quantify the characteristics discussed in this module:

- **Design Efficiency (η):** A dimensionless ratio (0–100%) measuring assembly performance.
- **DFA Index (D):** Time-based metric in **seconds per part** (default benchmark: **3 seconds** per theoretical part).
- **Minimum Number of Parts (Nm):** Absolute integer count (≥ 1).
- **Cost of Labor (Ce):** Measured in **INR/hour** or **USD/hour**.
- **Standard Tolerance Grade (IT):** ISO 286 — ranges from **IT01 to IT18** for general engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Typical Characteristics

The typical characteristics of an efficient DFMA design can be classified into **four (4) primary pillars** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 PEMET522 syllabus. Each pillar governs a specific aspect of manufacturability and assemblability.

#### Pillar A: Geometric Characteristics
- **Symmetry** (rotational and planar)
- **Concentricity** of mating features
- **Axis of insertion** (prefer single-axis insertion)
- **Part aspect ratio** (avoid long, thin, flexible parts)

#### Pillar B: Material & Process Characteristics
- **Single-process compatibility** (each part ideally made by one process)
- **Material unification** (single material for sub-assemblies)
- **Standard stock forms** (use sheets, bars, tubes of standard sizes)
- **Avoid secondary operations** (plating, grinding after forming)

#### Pillar C: Assembly Characteristics
- **Self-locating features** (chamfers, lead-ins)
- **Self-fastening features** (snap-fits, clips)
- **One-handed operation** (no need to hold part while fastening)
- **Top-down assembly** (gravity-aided placement)
- **Bulk-handling compatibility** (vibratory bowl feeding)

#### Pillar D: Functional Characteristics
- **Modular architecture** (independent functional sub-assemblies)
- **Serviceability** (parts accessible for repair)
- **Disassembly enablement** (Design for Disassembly — DfD)
- **Standardization** (use off-the-shelf components)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these characteristics?**
> Because they directly reduce the *Three Costs* of DFMA analysis: **Material Cost (Cm)**, **Manufacturing Time Cost (Ct,m)**, and **Assembly Time Cost (Ct,a)**. The Boothroyd-Dewhurst metric system rewards designs that minimize all three simultaneously.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Total Part Cost** | $C_p = C_m + t_m \cdot C_e + t_a \cdot C_e$ | INR/unit | Overall cost per component |
| 2 | **Manufacturing Cost** | $C_m = M_c + t_m \cdot C_e$ | INR/unit | Cost to fabricate one part |
| 3 | **Assembly Cost** | $C_a = t_a \cdot C_e$ | INR/unit | Cost to join the part |
| 4 | **Design Efficiency (Boothroyd)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_m \cdot t_{em}}{T_a} \times 100\%$ | % | Measures assembly quality |
| 5 | **DFA Index** | $D = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{actual}}{T_a}$ | s/part | Time penalty per part |
| 6 | **Assembly Time per Part (Hitachi)** | $T_a = N \cdot X_m \cdot t_{kit} \cdot \sum F_i$ | seconds | Hitachi total time model |
| 7 | **Min. Theoretical Time** | $t_{em} = 3 \cdot N_m$ | seconds | 3 s benchmark per part |
| 8 | **Cost Reduction Ratio** | $\Delta C = \dfrac{C_{old} - C_{new}}{C_{old}} \times 100\%$ | % | % savings post-redesign |
| 9 | **Manufacturing Cost Ratio** | $R_m = \dfrac{t_m}{t_m + t_a}$ | dimensionless | Time allocation indicator |
| 10 | **Asymmetry Penalty** | $F_{asym} = 1.5 \cdot t_{base}$ | seconds | Hitachi correction factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> These formulas are the daily workhorses of DFMA engineers at **Tata Motors, Bosch, Mahindra, and Ford**. When redesigning a gearbox housing, the goal is to *minimize $C_p$* by simultaneously *reducing $N_{actual}$* and *reducing $t_a$*. A design with **η ≥ 50%** is considered "excellent" in the automotive industry; **η < 20%** indicates severe over-design and is a red flag for cost reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Design Efficiency (η) — Boothroyd-Dewhurst Form

The Design Efficiency equation is not arbitrary; it is mathematically derived from the principle of comparing the **theoretical minimum assembly work** with the **actual assembly work performed**.

**Step 1: Define the Theoretical Minimum Assembly Time**

The theoretical minimum is achieved when the product is reduced to the *minimum number of parts* ($N_m$) and each part takes only the *baseline handling + insertion time* (Boothroyd's standard is **3 seconds** per part).

$$t_{em} = N_m \cdot 3 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Define the Actual Assembly Time**

The actual time is the sum of the time required to manipulate, orient, insert, and fasten every individual part in the current design ($N_{actual}$ parts), as observed in a time-study or motion analysis.

$$T_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{actual}} (t_{handle} + t_{insert} + t_{fasten})_i$$

**Step 3: Form the Efficiency Ratio**

Design efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful (theoretical minimum) work to the total (actual) work performed, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Theoretical Work}}{\text{Actual Work Performed}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Expressions from Steps 1 and 2**

$$\eta = \frac{N_m \cdot t_{em,unit}}{T_a} \times 100\%$$

Where $t_{em,unit}$ is the unit minimum time (3 s). Substituting the standard 3-second baseline:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_a} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 5: Algebraic Insight (Worked Example Logic)**

Consider a product with $N_m = 10$ and $T_a = 75$ s:

$$\eta = \frac{3 \times 10}{75} \times 100\% = \frac{30}{75} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

This means **60% of the assembly time is wasted** on redundant operations (reorientation, extra fasteners, two-handed operations, etc.). The DFMA redesign goal is to push $\eta$ as close to **100%** as possible.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Analytical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Standard — Solved Example**

**Problem Statement:**
A washing machine sub-assembly (control panel) currently consists of **22 parts** and requires an actual assembly time of **132 seconds**. After DFMA analysis, the minimum essential parts count is determined to be **11 parts**, and the unit baseline handling time is **3 seconds**. The cost of labor is **₹120/hour**. Calculate:
1. The current Design Efficiency.
2. The current assembly cost.
3. The DFA Index.

**Given Data:**
- $N_{actual} = 22$ parts
- $T_a = 132$ seconds
- $N_m = 11$ parts
- $t_{em,unit} = 3$ s
- $C_e = ₹120/\text{hour}$

**Solution — Part (i): Design Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_a} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:

$$\eta = \frac{3 \times 11}{132} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{33}{132} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 0.25 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 25\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Only 25% of the assembly work is theoretically necessary; 75% is redundant. This is a **poor design** in DFMA terms.

**Solution — Part (ii): Assembly Cost**

First, convert time to hours:

$$T_a \text{ (hours)} = \frac{132}{3600} \text{ hours}$$

$$C_a = T_a \text{ (hours)} \cdot C_e$$

$$C_a = \frac{132}{3600} \times 120$$

$$C_a = 0.03667 \times 120$$

$$\boxed{C_a = ₹4.40 \text{ per unit}}$$

**Solution — Part (iii): DFA Index**

$$D = \frac{3 \cdot N_{actual}}{T_a}$$

$$D = \frac{3 \times 22}{132}$$

$$D = \frac{66}{132}$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.5 \text{ seconds per part}}$$

**Final Analytical Matrix:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Verdict (KTU Standard) |
|-----------|--------|-------|------------------------|
| Design Efficiency | $\eta$ | **25%** | Below acceptable (≥ 50% target) |
| Assembly Cost | $C_a$ | **₹4.40/unit** | Higher than ₹2.20 minimum |
| DFA Index | $D$ | **0.5 s/part** | Excellent (< 1 s/part) |
| Redundant Work | $1 - \eta$ | **75%** | Critical redesign needed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DFMA Characteristics and Applications Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[DFMA Typical Characteristics] --> rootB[Geometric Pillar]
    rootA --> rootC[Material Pillar]
    rootA --> rootD[Assembly Pillar]
    rootA --> rootE[Functional Pillar]

    rootB --> B1[Symmetry]
    rootB --> B2[Single Axis Insertion]
    rootB --> B3[Concentric Mating]
    rootB --> B4[Low Aspect Ratio Parts]

    rootC --> C1[Single Process per Part]
    rootC --> C2[Standard Stock Forms]
    rootC --> C3[Material Unification]
    rootC --> C4[No Secondary Finishing]

    rootD --> D1[Self Locating Features]
    rootD --> D2[Self Fastening Snap Fits]
    rootD --> D3[Top Down Gravity Assembly]
    rootD --> D4[Bulk Handling Compatible]

    rootE --> E1[Modular Sub Assemblies]
    rootE --> E2[Design for Disassembly]
    rootE --> E3[Standardized Components]
    rootE --> E4[Serviceability Access]

    appStart[Typical Industry Applications] --> appA[Automotive Powertrain]
    appStart --> appB[Consumer Electronics PCB]
    appStart --> appC[Aerospace Turbine Modules]
    appStart --> appD[Home Appliance White Goods]
    appStart --> appE[Medical Device Instruments]

    rootA -.leads to.-> appStart
    rootD -.enables.-> appD
    rootB -.optimizes.-> appA
    rootE -.supports.-> appC
```

### 4.2 Application Mapping Matrix

| Industry Sector | Key DFMA Characteristic Applied | Typical Part Example | Time/Cost Savings |
|-----------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Automotive** | Modular sub-assemblies + snap-fits | Gearbox housings, dashboards | 20–35% cost reduction |
| **Electronics** | Top-down assembly + bulk handling | PCB mounting, phone casings | 40–50% time reduction |
| **Aerospace** | Single-axis insertion + standardization | Turbine blade mounts, fasteners | 15–25% weight + cost saved |
| **White Goods** | Self-locating + standard stock | Washing machine drums, panels | 30% assembly time saved |
| **Medical Devices** | Disassembly enablement + single material | Syringe barrels, surgical tools | High reusability gains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Diagram:**
> The flowchart shows a **hierarchical mapping** from the *abstract design characteristics* (left/top) to the *concrete industrial applications* (bottom). Notice the dotted arrows indicating *cross-pillar synergy* — for example, the Assembly Pillar (D) directly enables White Goods applications, while the Functional Pillar (E) supports Aerospace modularity. This is the systems-thinking view expected in KTU 2024 Part B answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]**
*Define the term "Design Efficiency" in the context of Design for Assembly. Mention the standard benchmark value for minimum theoretical handling time per part as per Boothroyd-Dewhurst methodology.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Design Efficiency (η) is a quantitative metric defined as the ratio of the theoretical minimum assembly time to the actual assembly time of a product, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_a} \times 100\%$$

The standard benchmark value for minimum theoretical handling time per part, as prescribed by the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst methodology**, is **3 seconds per part**. Hence, for a product with $N_m$ minimum essential parts, the minimum theoretical assembly time is $t_{em} = 3 \cdot N_m$ seconds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*List any four typical geometric characteristics that improve the assemblability of a product.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four typical geometric characteristics that improve assemblability are:
1. **Symmetry** of parts (rotational or planar), which eliminates orientation time.
2. **Concentricity of mating features**, which ensures self-alignment during insertion.
3. **Single-axis of insertion** for all parts, which simplifies robotized assembly.
4. **Low aspect ratio** (avoiding long, thin, flexible parts) to prevent handling difficulties and feeding errors.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in Part B by:
> 1. Forgetting to **convert seconds to hours** when computing cost (a 1-mark deduction).
> 2. Not writing the **unit and interpretation** of the final Design Efficiency (a 2-mark deduction).
> 3. Omitting the **"why"** behind each characteristic in the discussion part (a 3-mark deduction).
> Always end with a *verdict sentence* comparing your result with the industry benchmark (η ≥ 50%).

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **four primary pillars of typical DFMA characteristics**. Discuss the role of **self-locating features** and **top-down assembly** in reducing assembly time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gearbox housing has an actual assembly of **40 parts** and a total assembly time of **180 seconds**. The minimum essential parts count is **12**. The cost of labor is **₹150/hour**. Calculate:
1. Design Efficiency (η)
2. Assembly Cost (Ca)
3. DFA Index (D)
4. Comment on whether the design needs redesign. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The four primary pillars of typical DFMA characteristics are:
- **Geometric Pillar:** Symmetry, concentricity, single-axis insertion, low aspect ratio.
- **Material Pillar:** Single-process compatibility, material unification, standard stock forms.
- **Assembly Pillar:** Self-locating features, self-fastening snap-fits, top-down assembly, bulk-handling compatibility.
- **Functional Pillar:** Modular architecture, design for disassembly, standardization, serviceability.

**Role of self-locating features:** Chamfers, lead-ins, and tapered bosses eliminate the need for manual alignment. They reduce *insertion time* by approximately **30–50%** by allowing parts to "guide themselves" into position. The Boothroyd system assigns a lower handling time code to such features.

**Role of top-down assembly:** In top-down assembly, parts are inserted from above, allowing **gravity to assist** placement. This eliminates flipping operations, reduces operator fatigue, and enables **robotic assembly** with simple pick-and-place arms. It typically saves **15–25%** of total assembly time.

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
$N_{actual} = 40$, $T_a = 180$ s, $N_m = 12$, $C_e = ₹150/\text{h}$

**(i) Design Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_a} \times 100\% = \frac{3 \times 12}{180} \times 100\% = \frac{36}{180} \times 100\% = \boxed{20\%}$$

**(ii) Assembly Cost:**

$$C_a = \frac{180}{3600} \times 150 = 0.05 \times 150 = \boxed{₹7.50/\text{unit}}$$

**(iii) DFA Index:**

$$D = \frac{3 \times 40}{180} = \frac{120}{180} = \boxed{0.67 \text{ s/part}}$$

**(iv) Comment on Redesign:**

With **η = 20%**, the design falls in the **"poor"** category (industry benchmark ≥ 50%). **80% of the assembly work is redundant**, primarily due to over-partitioning ($N_{actual} = 40$ is far greater than $N_m = 12$). **Redesign is strongly recommended**, focusing on part consolidation (e.g., integrating brackets with the housing) and adopting snap-fit fasteners instead of separate screws.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the typical industrial applications of DFMA characteristics in **automotive powertrain design** and **consumer electronics PCB assembly**. Compare the role of *modular sub-assemblies* in both sectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A toy manufacturer produces a plastic car model with **30 parts** and a total assembly time of **200 seconds**. The minimum theoretical part count is **8**. Labor cost is **₹100/hour**. Compute the **Design Efficiency**, **Assembly Cost**, and **Cost Reduction Ratio** if the company redesigns the product to **20 parts** with an assembly time of **130 seconds** (labor cost unchanged). Comment on the impact. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Automotive Powertrain Applications:**
- **Modular sub-assemblies** allow engine, transmission, and differential to be built independently and joined in a final line.
- DFMA characteristics applied: single-axis insertion of pistons, snap-fit timing chain covers, top-down crankshaft mounting.
- Benefit: Reduces total powertrain assembly time by **25–30%**, enables parallel production lines.

**Consumer Electronics PCB Assembly Applications:**
- PCB components (resistors, ICs) are placed using **surface-mount technology (SMT)** that exploits top-down assembly and self-locating solder pads.
- Modular sub-assemblies include: display module, battery module, motherboard, and camera module — each tested independently before final integration.
- Benefit: Enables **high-speed pick-and-place** (10,000+ components/hour) and simplifies repairs (replace the failed module, not the whole device).

**Comparison of Modular Sub-Assemblies:**
| Aspect | Automotive | Electronics |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Module size | Large (10–50 kg) | Small (50–500 g) |
| Joining method | Bolts, snap-fits | Solder, connectors |
| Service impact | Dealer-level service | Component swap at home |

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given (Initial):**
$N_1 = 30$, $T_1 = 200$ s, $C_e = ₹100/\text{h}$
**Given (Redesigned):**
$N_2 = 20$, $T_2 = 130$ s

**Step 1: Initial Design Efficiency**

$$\eta_1 = \frac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_1} \times 100\% = \frac{3 \times 8}{200} \times 100\% = \boxed{12\%}$$

**Step 2: Initial Assembly Cost**

$$C_{a1} = \frac{200}{3600} \times 100 = 0.0556 \times 100 = \boxed{₹5.56/\text{unit}}$$

**Step 3: Redesigned Design Efficiency**

$$\eta_2 = \frac{3 \times 8}{130} \times 100\% = \frac{24}{130} \times 100\% = \boxed{18.46\%}$$

**Step 4: Redesigned Assembly Cost**

$$C_{a2} = \frac{130}{3600} \times 100 = 0.0361 \times 100 = \boxed{₹3.61/\text{unit}}$$

**Step 5: Cost Reduction Ratio**

$$\Delta C = \frac{C_{a1} - C_{a2}}{C_{a1}} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{5.56 - 3.61}{5.56} \times 100\% = \frac{1.95}{5.56} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\Delta C \approx 35.07\%}$$

**Comment:** The redesign yields a **35% cost reduction** in assembly and improves the design efficiency from **12% → 18.46%** (a **53.8% relative improvement** in efficiency). The company has eliminated 10 redundant parts, but the efficiency is still below the 50% benchmark; **further consolidation** toward the theoretical minimum of 8 parts is recommended in the next iteration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEMET522 — Module 3**

- ✅ **Design Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_a} \times 100\%$ — the **3-second benchmark** is non-negotiable.
- ✅ **DFA Index Formula:** $D = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{actual}}{T_a}$ — lower is better; < 1 s/part is excellent.
- ✅ **Four Pillars of DFMA Characteristics:** Geometric, Material, Assembly, Functional — memorize all four sub-points of each.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmark for η:** ≥ **50%** = good design; < **20%** = critical redesign needed.
- ✅ **Time Unit Conversion:** Always convert **seconds → hours** (÷3600) before multiplying by $C_e$.
- ✅ **Top-down assembly** = gravity-aided = preferred for robotized lines.
- ✅ **Self-locating features** (chamfers, lead-ins) reduce insertion time by **30–50%**.
- ✅ **Application Domains:** Automotive, Electronics, Aerospace, White Goods, Medical Devices — each uses a different mix of the four pillars.
- ✅ **Modular sub-assemblies** are the *functional pillar's* most powerful tool for serviceability and parallel production.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to compare the result with the **industry benchmark** (η ≥ 50%) — costs 2 marks in KTU valuation.
- ✅ **Hitachi's asymmetry penalty:** Adds **1.5×** the base time for asymmetrical parts — relevant for Part B theory.
- ✅ **Cost Reduction Ratio:** $\Delta C = \dfrac{C_{old} - C_{new}}{C_{old}} \times 100\%$ — always express as a **percentage** in the final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suitable Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suitable Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suitable Materials (DFMA Definition):** In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, "Suitable Materials" refers to the systematic, performance-driven selection of engineering materials that simultaneously satisfy the *functional design requirements* (mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical), the *manufacturing process constraints* (castability, machinability, formability, weldability, moldability), the *assembly considerations* (joining compatibility, dimensional stability, fastening ease), and the *life-cycle criteria* (cost, recyclability, environmental impact) of a product, in accordance with **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3** learning outcomes.

In DFMA philosophy, material selection is **not a stand-alone decision** — it is a coupled decision made **in parallel** with both the **manufacturing process selection** and the **product geometry (form) design**. A "suitable" material in DFMA is the one that minimizes total cost and complexity across the **entire product life cycle** while meeting every engineering specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Chef's Ingredient Rule"**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish. You would never select ingredients **before** knowing whether the dish will be baked, fried, or steamed. Similarly, in DFMA, you must **NOT** lock in a material before you have sketched the geometry **AND** chosen a manufacturing process. Choosing a material first is like choosing chocolate for a soup recipe — a *mismatch* creates downstream problems (the chocolate won't melt into the broth, your pot will be ruined, your guests will leave).
> A *suitable* material in DFMA is the one that **cooperates** with your chosen process (casting, forging, injection molding, sheet metal forming) and **cooperates** with your chosen assembly method (snap-fit, welding, adhesive bonding, threaded fasteners).

## 1.3 Why Material Selection is a Core DFMA Pillar

The four pillars of DFMA (per Boothroyd, Dewhurst, Knight) are:

1. **DFM — Design for Manufacture** (process-friendly design)
2. **DFA — Design for Assembly** (assembly-friendly design)
3. **DFE — Design for Environment** (eco-friendly design)
4. **DFS — Design for Sustainability / Cost**

Material selection sits at the **intersection** of all four pillars. A single bad material choice can:
- **Force** a more expensive manufacturing process (e.g., choosing titanium when aluminum would suffice for a structural bracket),
- **Disable** fast assembly (e.g., choosing incompatible polymers that won't ultrasonic-weld),
- **Block** end-of-life recycling (e.g., mixing steel fasteners into an aluminum body creates disassembly/recycling nightmares),
- **Increase** total part count (e.g., choosing a brittle plastic that needs metal inserts at every screw hole).

## 1.4 The Four "Suitability Filters" in DFMA

| Filter | Question Answered | KTU Exam Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Filter** | Does the material have the *right properties*? (Strength, stiffness, hardness, conductivity, corrosion resistance) | *Material property index* |
| **Process Filter** | Can the chosen manufacturing process *shape* this material economically? | *Process-material compatibility* |
| **Assembly Filter** | Can the part be *joined* with neighboring parts using the chosen assembly method? | *Joining compatibility* |
| **Lifecycle Filter** | Is the material *cost-effective, recyclable, and compliant* with environmental regulations? | *Total life-cycle cost* |

> [!NOTE]
> **A material passes the DFMA "Suitability Test" only if it clears ALL FOUR filters.** This is a frequently tested conceptual point in KTU Module 3.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

The following metrics dominate DFMA material selection (all bolded because they are **must-know** for KTU):

- **Young's Modulus, E [GPa]** — Stiffness
- **Density, ρ [kg/m³]** — Mass
- **Yield Strength, σ_y [MPa]** — Strength
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength, σ_uts [MPa]** — Strength
- **Hardness [HV / HB / HRC]** — Wear resistance
- **Thermal Conductivity, k [W/m·K]** — Heat dissipation
- **Thermal Expansion Coefficient, α [10⁻⁶/°C]** — Dimensional stability
- **Electrical Resistivity, ρ_e [Ω·m]** — Conductivity
- **Corrosion Rate [mm/year]** — Durability
- **Specific Stiffness, E/ρ [MPa·m³/kg]** — Stiffness-to-weight ratio
- **Specific Strength, σ_y/ρ [kPa·m³/kg]** — Strength-to-weight ratio
- **Cost per kg, C_kg [USD/kg or ₹/kg]** — Raw material cost
- **Cost per unit property, C_pp** — Economic selection index

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ashby Material Selection Methodology (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prof. Michael Ashby's method** is the **gold standard** for systematic material selection in DFMA. KTU examiners expect you to describe its 4-step procedure in any material-selection question.

**Step 1 — Translation:** Convert the *design requirements* into **Function** (e.g., "carry a tensile load"), **Constraints** (e.g., "must not exceed 5 kg"), **Objectives** (e.g., "minimize cost"), and **Free Variables** (e.g., "choice of material, choice of cross-section").

**Step 2 — Screening:** Apply **constraints** to eliminate unsuitable materials. e.g., if part must operate at 800 °C, eliminate all polymers and most aluminum alloys.

**Step 3 — Ranking:** Use **material indices** to rank the survivors. A material index is a *combination of properties* that maximizes or minimizes the objective while satisfying constraints.

**Step 4 — Supporting Information:** Use microstructural data, manufacturing notes, experience, and supplier information to make the final choice.

## 2.2 Material Indices — The "Math of Suitability"

A **Material Index (M)** is a mathematical expression combining two or more material properties to optimize a specific performance objective.

**General formulation:**

$$M = \frac{f_1(P_1)}{f_2(P_2)}$$

where $P_1, P_2, \ldots$ are material properties and $f_1, f_2, \ldots$ are monotonic functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In exam answers, always state the **functional requirement**, **constraint**, **objective**, and then **derive the index**. Do not just write down the formula.

### 2.2.1 Derivation of Common Material Indices

**Case 1 — Lightweight Stiff Tie (Tension Member)**

- **Function:** Carry axial tensile load $F$ without excessive elastic deflection.
- **Constraint:** Length $L$ is fixed, deflection $\delta \le \delta^*$, load $F$ is given.
- **Objective:** Minimize mass $m$.

Axial stiffness: $k = \dfrac{EA}{L}$, so $A = \dfrac{FL}{E\delta^*}$

Mass: $m = AL\rho = \dfrac{FL\rho}{E\delta^*}$

To **minimize m** for fixed L, F, $\delta^*$:

$$\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

This is the **specific stiffness** index.

**Case 2 — Lightweight Strong Tie (Tension Member)**

- **Function:** Carry axial tensile load $F$ without yielding.
- **Constraint:** Length $L$ fixed; factor of safety $n$ given.
- **Objective:** Minimize mass $m$.

Required area: $A = \dfrac{F}{\sigma_y / n} = \dfrac{nF}{\sigma_y}$

Mass: $m = AL\rho = \dfrac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$

To **minimize m**:

$$\boxed{M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

This is the **specific strength** index.

**Case 3 — Minimum-Cost Stiff Beam**

- **Function:** Carry bending load with limited deflection.
- **Objective:** Minimize cost $C = C_{kg} \cdot m$.

Following the same logic, the index becomes:

$$\boxed{M_3 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho \cdot C_{kg}} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

(The exponent on $E$ depends on loading mode — tension: 1, bending: 1/2, torsion: 2/3.)

**Case 4 — Minimum-Cost Lightweight Column (Buckling)**

For a column of length $L$ with critical buckling load $P_{cr}$ constraint:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} \Rightarrow I = \frac{P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^2 E}$$

The index becomes:

$$\boxed{M_4 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho \cdot C_{kg}} \quad \text{(same as M_3, different loading mode)}}$$

## 2.3 The Ashby Chart (E vs ρ Log-Log Plot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Plotting Young's Modulus $E$ on the y-axis and density $\rho$ on the x-axis (both logarithmic) gives the famous **Ashby Chart**. Material families form characteristic "bubbles" (ceramics top-left, polymers bottom-right, metals in the middle, composites spanning diagonally). Lines of constant $E/\rho$ are *diagonals* — to maximize $E/\rho$, you slide your selection bubble **up-and-to-the-left**.

This graphical method allows rapid visual screening of thousands of materials and is the cornerstone of computer-aided material selection (CES EduPack, Granta MI).

## 2.4 Manufacturing Process Compatibility Matrix

| Material Family | Casting | Forging | Sheet Forming | Machining | Injection Molding | Powder Metallurgy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous metals (steel, cast iron)** | ✔ (cast iron) | ✔ (steel) | ✔ (steel sheet) | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✔✔ |
| **Aluminum alloys** | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ (die cast) | ✗ |
| **Copper alloys** | ✔ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Engineering polymers (ABS, PA, POM)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ |
| **Thermosets (phenolic, epoxy)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ (compression) | ✗ |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiC)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ (grinding) | ✗ | ✔✔ |
| **Composites (CFRP, GFRP)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔ (thermo-set) | ✗ |

Legend: ✔✔ = Excellent, ✔ = Good, ✗ = Poor / Not applicable.

## 2.5 Assembly Compatibility (Joining Method vs Material)

| Joining Method | Steel | Aluminum | Polymer | Ceramic | Composite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding (arc, MIG, TIG)** | ✔✔ | ✔ (TIG) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Resistance welding** | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Brazing / Soldering** | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Adhesive bonding** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ |
| **Snap-fit** | ✔ (sheet) | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Threaded fasteners** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ (with inserts) | ✗ | ✔ (with inserts) |
| **Riveting** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ |
| **Ultrasonic welding** | ✗ | ✗ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Heat staking** | ✗ | ✗ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Press-fit** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A "suitable" material in DFA context is the one that *enables* a fast, reliable, single-operation joining method (e.g., a snap-fit-friendly polymer for a phone cover) **rather than** forcing slow, multi-step joining (e.g., a ceramic that needs adhesive cure + fixturing).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Index | Symbol | Application Domain | Units | Maximize / Minimize |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Stiffness | $M_1 = E / \rho$ | Lightweight stiff tie | $\text{GPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Maximize |
| 2 | Specific Strength | $M_2 = \sigma_y / \rho$ | Lightweight strong tie | $\text{kPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Maximize |
| 3 | Stiffness Index (Bending) | $M_3 = E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Lightweight beam in bending | — | Maximize |
| 4 | Stiffness Index (Torsion) | $M_4 = E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Lightweight shaft in torsion | — | Maximize |
| 5 | Cost-Per-Unit-Property (stiff) | $C_{pp} = C_{kg} \cdot \rho / E^{1/2}$ | Min-cost beam | $\text{₹·m}^{-3}\text{·GPa}^{1/2}$ | Minimize |
| 6 | Springback Index | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ | Sheet metal formability | Dimensionless | Minimize (for low springback) |
| 7 | Thermal Distortion Index | $\alpha / k$ | Heat-exchanger plates | $\text{m/W}$ | Minimize |
| 8 | Wear Resistance Index | $H / E$ | Tribological contacts | Dimensionless | Maximize |
| 9 | Buckling Resistance | $M_{cr} = E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Lightweight column | — | Maximize |
| 10 | Mass of a Lightweight Tie | $m = \dfrac{FL\rho}{E\delta^*}$ | Direct design calc | kg | — |
| 11 | Mass of a Lightweight Strong Tie | $m = \dfrac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$ | Direct design calc | kg | — |
| 12 | Cost Index (stiff beam) | $C_I = \dfrac{C_{kg} \cdot \rho}{E^{1/2}}$ | Economic selection | — | Minimize |
| 13 | Material Removal Fraction (DFM) | $f_{mr} = \dfrac{m_{chip}}{m_{blank}}$ | Process selection | Dimensionless | Minimize |
| 14 | Buy-to-Fly Ratio (forging) | $B/F = m_{billet}/m_{final}$ | Forging cost indicator | Dimensionless | Minimize |
| 15 | Recyclability Index | $R = 1 - E_{loss}/E_{in}$ | DFE / Sustainability | Dimensionless | Maximize |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Rule (CRITICAL):** In the table above, every absolute-value or "per" notation is written as `\rho` or with explicit words like "per kg" to **avoid breaking the markdown table** with stray `|` characters. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX within table cells.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace wing spar** → Maximize $E/\rho$ → CFRP composites or Al 7075.
- **Automotive body panel** → Minimize $C_{kg}\rho/\sigma_y$ → Mild steel (HSLA) or aluminum 5182.
- **Heat sink in LED** → Maximize $k/\rho \cdot C_p$ → Pure aluminum 1050 or copper C110.
- **Snap-fit mobile cover** → Maximize elongation & impact strength → Polycarbonate (PC) or ABS.
- **Gears in a gearbox** → Maximize $H$ and wear resistance + minimize $C_{kg}$ → Case-hardened steel 20MnCr5.
- **Medical implant** → Maximize biocompatibility + corrosion resistance → Titanium Ti-6Al-4V.

## 2.8 The DFMA "Suitability Decision Tree"

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Draw / describe the systematic decision flow for selecting a "suitable" material under DFMA constraints.

The decision tree visits each filter in sequence:
1. Start at **Function** → Define the property envelope.
2. Apply **Constraints** → Apply stiffness, strength, temperature, corrosion limits → Eliminate.
3. Apply **Material Indices** → Rank remaining candidates.
4. Apply **Process Compatibility** → Check if a primary DFMA process can shape the material.
5. Apply **Assembly Compatibility** → Check if the joining method works.
6. Apply **Cost & Lifecycle** → Apply $C_{kg} \cdot \rho$ and $R$ indices.
7. **Final Selection** → Document the choice with full traceability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Specific Stiffness Index (Tie in Tension)

**Given:**
- A tensile tie of length $L$ must carry an axial force $F$ with a maximum permitted elastic elongation $\delta^*$.
- Cross-sectional area $A$ is a free variable.
- Mass $m$ must be minimized.

**Step 1 — Apply Hooke's law to express elongation:**

$$\delta = \frac{FL}{AE}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the constraint for the minimum required area:**

$$A_{min} = \frac{FL}{\delta^* E}$$

This is the smallest area that satisfies the deflection limit.

**Step 3 — Express the mass of the tie:**

$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{FL}{\delta^* E} \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{F L^2 \rho}{\delta^* E}$$

**Step 4 — Identify which material property combination governs the mass:**

Since $F$, $L$, $\delta^*$ are all fixed by the design, the only way to **minimize m** is to **maximize the material property group**:

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Stiffness} = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Evaluation:**

Suppose: $F = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ m}$, $\delta^* = 1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$.

Compare three candidate materials:
- **Steel (AISI 1020):** $E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- **Aluminum 6061-T6:** $E = 69$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³
- **CFRP (unidirectional):** $E = 135$ GPa, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³

**Step 5a — Steel:**

$$m_{steel} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 7850}{0.001 \cdot 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{3.925 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^8} = 1.9625 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5b — Aluminum 6061-T6:**

$$m_{al} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 2700}{0.001 \cdot 69 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.35 \times 10^8}{6.9 \times 10^7} = 1.9565 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5c — CFRP:**

$$m_{cfrp} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 1600}{0.001 \cdot 135 \times 10^9} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^7}{1.35 \times 10^8} = 0.5926 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5d — Compute Specific Stiffness Indices:**

- Steel: $E/\rho = 200 \times 10^9 / 7850 = 25.5 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg
- Aluminum: $E/\rho = 69 \times 10^9 / 2700 = 25.6 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg
- CFRP: $E/\rho = 135 \times 10^9 / 1600 = 84.4 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg

**Step 5e — Confirm the trend:** CFRP has the highest $E/\rho$ and gives the lowest mass — exactly as the index predicts. Steel and aluminum have nearly identical $E/\rho$ and yield nearly identical masses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that *absolute* stiffness $E$ alone would have ranked Steel > Al > CFRP, but the index $E/\rho$ correctly *inverts* this ranking when weight matters. This is why indices are the right tool, not raw properties.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Index (Tie in Tension, Strength-Driven)

**Given:**
- Tensile tie of length $L$, must carry force $F$ with factor of safety $n$.
- Mass must be minimized.

**Step 1 — Yielding constraint:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \Rightarrow A_{min} = \frac{nF}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 2 — Mass expression:**

$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the index:**

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical comparison (same tie, but yield-limited):**

Suppose $F = 200{,}000$ N, $n = 2$, $L = 1$ m.

- **Steel 1020:** $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- **Al 7075-T6:** $\sigma_y = 503$ MPa, $\rho = 2810$ kg/m³
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³
- **CFRP UD:** $\sigma_y = 1500$ MPa, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³

**Step 4a — Steel:**

$$m_{steel} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 7850}{350 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.14 \times 10^9}{3.5 \times 10^8} = 8.971 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4b — Al 7075-T6:**

$$m_{al} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 2810}{503 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.124 \times 10^9}{5.03 \times 10^8} = 2.234 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4c — Ti-6Al-4V:**

$$m_{ti} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 4430}{880 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.772 \times 10^9}{8.8 \times 10^8} = 2.014 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4d — CFRP:**

$$m_{cfrp} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1600}{1500 \times 10^6} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 0.427 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4e — Specific strength comparison:**

- Steel: $\sigma_y/\rho = 350 \times 10^6 / 7850 = 44{,}586$ N·m/kg
- Al 7075: $503 \times 10^6 / 2810 = 178{,}826$ N·m/kg
- Ti-6Al-4V: $880 \times 10^6 / 4430 = 198{,}645$ N·m/kg
- CFRP: $1500 \times 10^6 / 1600 = 937{,}500$ N·m/kg

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** CFRP wins again for strength-critical lightweight ties. Ti and Al are close seconds. Steel, despite its high absolute strength, is the heaviest choice.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Cost-Per-Unit-Property (Min-Cost Stiff Beam)

**Given:**
- A cantilever beam of length $L$ must deflect by at most $\delta^*$ under tip load $W$.
- Material cost ₹/kg = $C_{kg}$.
- Mass = $A \cdot L \cdot \rho$, cost = $C_{kg} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \rho$.

**Step 1 — Deflection constraint (cantilever, tip load):**

$$\delta = \frac{WL^3}{3EI} \le \delta^* \Rightarrow I_{min} = \frac{WL^3}{3E\delta^*}$$

**Step 2 — For a rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ with $h$ as the free variable and $b$ fixed:**

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} \Rightarrow h = \left(\frac{12 I}{b}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{12 WL^3}{3Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-section area:**

$$A = b \cdot h = b \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Mass and cost:**

$$m = AL\rho = \rho b \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{total} = C_{kg} \cdot m = C_{kg} \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the cost-minimizing material index:**

Pull out the material-dependent terms:

$$C_{total} \propto \frac{C_{kg} \cdot \rho}{E^{1/3}}$$

$$\boxed{M_{cost, beam} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{C_{kg} \cdot \rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

Equivalently, minimize the reciprocal: $C_{kg}\rho / E^{1/3}$.

**Step 6 — Numerical example (b = 50 mm, L = 1 m, W = 500 N, $\delta^*$ = 5 mm):**

| Material | E [GPa] | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | $C_{kg}$ [₹/kg] | $C_{kg}\rho/E^{1/3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 200 | 7850 | 65 | 65·7850 / 5.85 = 87,265 |
| Al 6061 | 69 | 2700 | 220 | 220·2700 / 4.10 = 144,878 |
| GFRP | 25 | 1800 | 350 | 350·1800 / 2.92 = 215,753 |
| CFRP | 135 | 1600 | 4500 | 4500·1600 / 5.13 = 1,403,508 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even though CFRP is *structurally* the most efficient beam, the **cost-per-unit-property** index reveals that mild steel is the most *economical* choice. This is why the steel I-beam dominates building construction. CFRP is chosen only when weight matters *more* than cost (aerospace, racing).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection by Application

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Pattern:** "Select a suitable material for the following components. Justify." This 7-mark question is a KTU Module 3 favorite. The matrix below is the rapid-fire reference.

| Component | Primary Function | Best Material Family | Justification (Material Index Used) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft wing skin | Tension + bending, light | Al 2024-T3 or CFRP | Maximize $E/\rho$ and $\sigma_y/\rho$ |
| Connecting rod (IC engine) | Cyclic tension + compression | Forged steel 4340 or Ti alloy | Maximize $\sigma_y/\rho$ + fatigue strength |
| Gear teeth (transmission) | High contact stress, wear | Carburized steel 20MnCr5 | Maximize surface hardness $H$ |
| Smartphone back cover | Cosmetic, light, impact | Al 7075 or Polycarbonate | Minimize $m$, maximize dent resistance |
| Heat sink (CPU) | High heat flux | Pure Al 1050 or Cu C110 | Maximize $k/(\rho \cdot C_{kg})$ |
| Snap-fit housing | Repeated assembly | Polypropylene (PP) or POM | Maximize flexural fatigue life |
| Artificial hip joint | Biocompatible, wear | Ti-6Al-4V + UHMWPE cup | Maximize biocompatibility, minimize wear |
| Cryogenic LNG tank | Low temp ductile | 9% Ni steel or Al 5083 | Maximize low-T toughness |
| Spring (coil) | High elastic strain energy | Spring steel (chrome-vanadium) | Maximize $\sigma_y^2/E$ (stored energy) |
| PCB substrate | Electrical insulation | FR-4 (glass-epoxy laminate) | Maximize $\rho_e$, minimize $k$ |
| Cutting tool insert | Hot hardness | WC-Co (cemented carbide) | Maximize $H$ at 800 °C |
| Marine propeller | Corrosion + strength | Ni-Al bronze or Muntz metal | Maximize corrosion resistance + $\sigma_y$ |
| Pressure vessel (high P) | Containment | SA 516 Gr 70 carbon steel | Maximize $\sigma_y$, weldability |
| Body armor plate | Impact absorption | UHMWPE (Kevlar/Dyneema) or Al₂O₃ ceramic | Maximize energy absorption per unit mass |
| Glass bottle | Cheap containment | Soda-lime glass | Minimize $C_{kg}$ + recyclability |

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — Spring Material Selection

**Problem:** Select a suitable material for a helical compression spring that must store maximum elastic strain energy per unit volume.

**Step 1 — State the energy density:**

For linear elastic loading up to $\sigma_y$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the material index:**

$$\boxed{M_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare candidates:**

| Material | $\sigma_y$ [MPa] | E [GPa] | $\sigma_y^2 / E$ [MPa] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring steel (ASTM A228) | 1800 | 200 | 16.2 |
| Stainless 302 | 1000 | 190 | 5.26 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 880 | 113 | 6.85 |
| Brass C26000 | 435 | 100 | 1.89 |
| Beryllium copper C17200 | 1100 | 128 | 9.45 |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Music wire (spring steel) has the highest index and is the standard spring material. Beryllium copper is the next best for corrosion-resistant springs.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — DFMA Material Selection Flow (Ashby Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Design Requirement] --> B[Step 1: TRANSLATION]
    B --> B1[Define Function e.g. carry load]
    B --> B2[Define Constraints e.g. mass less than 5 kg]
    B --> B3[Define Objective e.g. minimize cost]
    B --> B4[Define Free Variables e.g. material and shape]

    B1 --> C[Step 2: SCREENING]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Apply Property Limits]
    C1 --> C2[Eliminate Materials Outside Envelope]
    C2 --> D[Step 3: RANKING with Material Indices]

    D --> D1{Tie in Tension?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Maximize E by rho]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Beam in Bending?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Maximize E to the power 1 over 3 by rho]
    D3 -- No --> D5{Column Buckling?}
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Maximize E to the power 1 over 2 by rho]
    D5 -- No --> D7{Spring?}
    D7 -- Yes --> D8[Maximize sigma_y squared by E]

    D2 --> E[Step 4: SUPPORTING INFO]
    D4 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D8 --> E
    E --> E1[Process Compatibility Check]
    E1 --> E2[Assembly Compatibility Check]
    E2 --> E3[Cost and Life Cycle Check]
    E3 --> F[FINAL MATERIAL SELECTION]
    F --> G[Document and Specify Material Grade]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — The Four Suitability Filters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Material] --> B{Functional Filter}
    B -- FAIL --> Z[Eliminate]
    B -- PASS --> C{Manufacturing Process Filter}
    C -- FAIL --> Z
    C -- PASS --> D{Assembly Compatibility Filter}
    D -- FAIL --> Z
    D -- PASS --> E{Lifecycle and Cost Filter}
    E -- FAIL --> Z
    E -- PASS --> F[SUITABLE MATERIAL SELECTED]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Material Family Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Selection] --> B{Operating Temperature}

    C --> F{Weight Critical}
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
