# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are written strictly in the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** — Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, sub-divided as 7+7).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEMET522]**
**"Define a 'suitable material' in the context of DFMA. List any three criteria that a material must satisfy to be considered 'suitable' under DFMA principles."**

**Model Answer:**

A "suitable material" in DFMA is one that simultaneously satisfies the **functional, manufacturing, assembly, and life-cycle** requirements of a product while minimizing total cost and complexity.

Three criteria are:
1. **Functional Suitability** — The material's mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical properties must match the design specification (e.g., $E$, $\sigma_y$, $H$, $k$, $\alpha$).
2. **Manufacturability** — The material must be compatible with the chosen primary forming process (e.g., castable, forgeable, machinable, moldable).
3. **Assembly Compatibility** — The material must be joinable using the chosen assembly method (e.g., weldable, snap-fit-capable, adhesive-bondable).

*(Any 3 of the 4 filters — functional, process, assembly, lifecycle — earn full marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEMET522]**
**"What is a 'material index'? Derive the material index for selecting a lightweight, stiff tie in tension."**

**Model Answer:**

A **material index** is a mathematical expression that combines two or more material properties into a single performance metric. It is used to rank candidate materials against a specific design objective.

**Derivation:**
For a tie of length $L$ under load $F$ with max permissible elongation $\delta^*$:

$$A = \frac{FL}{\delta^* E}, \quad m = AL\rho = \frac{FL^2 \rho}{\delta^* E}$$

To **minimize mass** (with $F$, $L$, $\delta^*$ fixed), the material property group to **maximize** is:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Stiffness)}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option I

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, PEMET522]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and a step-by-step procedure, explain **Prof. Ashby's systematic method for material selection**. Discuss the role of material property charts in this process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cantilever beam of length $L = 0.8$ m must support a tip load of $W = 1500$ N with maximum tip deflection $\delta^* = 2$ mm. The cross-section is rectangular, $b = 40$ mm, $h$ is a free variable. The candidate materials are:

- **Mild Steel:** $E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $C_{kg} = 65$ ₹/kg
- **Al 6061-T6:** $E = 69$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, $C_{kg} = 220$ ₹/kg
- **GFRP (woven):** $E = 22$ GPa, $\rho = 1800$ kg/m³, $C_{kg} = 350$ ₹/kg

Determine (i) the **lightest** material, and (ii) the **cheapest** material for this beam. Justify using material indices. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Translation Phase:** The designer converts the design requirements into:
- **Function** (e.g., "support load, conduct heat, store energy"),
- **Constraints** (e.g., "max mass = 5 kg", "min stiffness = 200 N/m"),
- **Objectives** (e.g., "minimize mass", "minimize cost", "maximize life"),
- **Free variables** (e.g., material choice, dimensions).

**Step 2 — Screening Phase:** Apply hard constraints. Materials that violate any constraint (e.g., service temperature above melting point, density too high, corrosion-incompatible) are **eliminated**. This typically reduces the universe from ~100,000 materials to a few hundred.

**Step 3 — Ranking Phase:** Define a **material index** $M$ that, when maximized (or minimized), satisfies the objective. For example, for a lightweight stiff tie, $M = E/\rho$.

**Step 4 — Supporting Information Phase:** Cross-check against process capability, supplier availability, environmental regulations, prior experience, and the designer's institutional knowledge.

**Role of Material Property Charts:**
- Charts are **log-log plots** of two (or three) material properties — e.g., Young's Modulus vs Density, Strength vs Density, Strength vs Cost.
- Each material family (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) occupies a characteristic "bubble."
- **Diagonal lines of constant material index** allow visual ranking of materials.
- A designer *sliding a window* up-and-to-the-left on the $E$-$\rho$ chart is "optimizing" for stiffness-to-weight.

*(Neat sketches of the Ashby $E$-$\rho$ chart, $E^{1/3}$-$\rho$ chart, and $\sigma_y$-$\rho$ chart would earn the diagram marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Deflection constraint for a cantilever under tip load:**

$$\delta = \frac{WL^3}{3EI} \le \delta^* \Rightarrow I_{min} = \frac{WL^3}{3E\delta^*}$$

**Step 2 — Relate moment of inertia to cross-section dimensions (b fixed, h free):**

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} \Rightarrow h = \left(\frac{12 I_{min}}{b}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4 WL^3}{E b \delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Mass and cost expressions:**

$$m = \rho \cdot b \cdot h = \rho b \left(\frac{4 WL^3}{E b \delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C = C_{kg} \cdot m = C_{kg} \cdot \rho \cdot b \left(\frac{4 WL^3}{E b \delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values.** Common constants: $W = 1500$ N, $L = 0.8$ m, $b = 0.04$ m, $\delta^* = 0.002$ m.

Compute the constant:

$$K = 4 W L^3 / (b \delta^*) = 4 \times 1500 \times 0.8^3 / (0.04 \times 0.002)$$

$$K = 4 \times 1500 \times 0.512 / 0.00008 = \frac{3072}{0.00008} = 3.84 \times 10^7$$

**Step 4a — Mild Steel:**

$$h_{steel} = (K/E)^{1/3} = (3.84 \times 10^7 / 200 \times 10^9)^{1/3} = (1.92 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3}$$

$$h_{steel} = 0.0576 \text{ m} = 57.6 \text{ mm}$$

$$m_{steel} = 7850 \times 0.04 \times 0.0576 = 18.09 \text{ kg}$$

$$C_{steel} = 65 \times 18.09 = 1175.85 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4b — Al 6061-T6:**

$$h_{al} = (3.84 \times 10^7 / 69 \times 10^9)^{1/3} = (5.565 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0823 \text{ m} = 82.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$m_{al} = 2700 \times 0.04 \times 0.0823 = 8.89 \text{ kg}$$

$$C_{al} = 220 \times 8.89 = 1955.8 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4c — GFRP:**

$$h_{gfrp} = (3.84 \times 10^7 / 22 \times 10^9)^{1/3} = (1.745 \times 10^{-3})^{1/3} = 0.1204 \text{ m} = 120.4 \text{ mm}$$

$$m_{gfrp} = 1800 \times 0.04 \times 0.1204 = 8.67 \text{ kg}$$

$$C_{gfrp} = 350 \times 8.67 = 3034.5 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5 — Summary Table:**

| Material | Mass [kg] | Cost [₹] | $E^{1/3}/\rho$ Index | $E^{1/3}/(C_{kg}\rho)$ Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | **18.09** | **1175.85** | 5.85/7850 = 7.45e-4 | 1175.85 |
| Al 6061-T6 | 8.89 | 1955.8 | 4.10/2700 = 1.52e-3 | 1955.8 |
| GFRP | **8.67** | 3034.5 | 2.80/1800 = 1.55e-3 | 3034.5 |

**Step 6 — Conclusions:**
- **(i) Lightest material:** **GFRP** at 8.67 kg (marginally lighter than Al at 8.89 kg). The $E^{1/3}/\rho$ index is highest for GFRP, confirming it.
- **(ii) Cheapest material:** **Mild Steel** at ₹1175.85, despite being the heaviest. The $C_{kg}\rho/E^{1/3}$ cost index is lowest for steel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert $L$, $b$, $\delta^*$ into **metres** before computation. The numerical answer will be off by orders of magnitude. *Always* show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the index for a tie ($E/\rho$) with the index for a beam ($E^{1/3}/\rho$). The exponent on $E$ **depends on the loading mode** — tension gives 1, bending gives 1/3, torsion gives 1/2, buckling gives 1/2. Examiners deduct 2 marks for using the wrong exponent.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Many students pick the material with highest $E$ and assume it's "best" — that is **wrong** in DFMA. Always consider $E$ *relative* to $\rho$ and $C_{kg}$.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to include the *process compatibility* filter. GFRP cannot be machined, cast, or welded using conventional methods — it can only be cut, drilled (with care), or adhesive-bonded. In a real DFMA project, GFRP might be *eliminated* at the process filter despite winning the index ranking.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option II (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, PEMET522]**

**(a)** Explain the **DFMA approach to material selection**. Discuss in detail the four "suitability filters" that a material must clear. Give one example of a manufactured product where wrong material selection led to a **manufacturing problem** and another where it led to an **assembly problem**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A helical compression spring must store maximum elastic strain energy per unit volume. The candidates are:

- **Music wire (ASTM A228):** $\sigma_y = 1800$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $C_{kg} = 120$ ₹/kg
- **Stainless 302:** $\sigma_y = 1000$ MPa, $E = 190$ GPa, $C_{kg} = 280$ ₹/kg
- **Beryllium Copper C17200:** $\sigma_y = 1100$ MPa, $E = 128$ GPa, $C_{kg} = 1500$ ₹/kg
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa, $E = 113$ GPa, $C_{kg} = 4500$ ₹/kg

Determine the **best material for energy storage per unit mass** and the **best material for energy storage per rupee**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**DFMA Approach to Material Selection:**

In DFMA, material selection is treated as a **coupled, multi-criteria decision** rather than a stand-alone engineering choice. The four filters are:

1. **Functional Filter** — Does the material have the required mechanical/physical/chemical properties? (E.g., a bridge cable needs $\sigma_y > 1500$ MPa and good fatigue life.)
2. **Manufacturing Process Filter** — Can the chosen primary process shape the material economically? (E.g., forging requires hot ductility; injection molding requires thermoplastic flow.)
3. **Assembly Compatibility Filter** — Can the part be joined to its neighbors using the chosen assembly method? (E.g., a ceramic cover cannot be ultrasonic-welded; it needs adhesive bonding.)
4. **Life-Cycle / Cost Filter** — Is the material cost-effective, recyclable, and environmentally compliant? (E.g., RoHS-banned substances like lead and cadmium must be excluded.)

**Example 1 — Manufacturing Problem from Wrong Material Selection:**
A company once designed an automotive dashboard retainer in **polypropylene (PP)** to leverage its low cost. However, the PP grade selected had **poor UV resistance**. The dashboard cracked in tropical climates within 6 months — a **manufacturing/process-related** failure traceable to a wrong *grade* (not wrong family) selection. Lesson: always check the *specific grade's datasheet* for sub-properties (UV, flame, chemical resistance), not just the family averages.

**Example 2 — Assembly Problem from Wrong Material Selection:**
A consumer electronics OEM selected a **glass-filled nylon (PA66-GF30)** for a snap-fit enclosure. The 30% glass content made the part **too stiff and brittle** to flex during snap-fit assembly, causing the clips to fracture on first insertion. Switching to **unfilled PA66** (or PC/ABS blend) restored ductility and the snap-fit worked. Lesson: the *manufacturing filter* approved glass-filled nylon (moldable), but the *assembly filter* should have vetoed it because it couldn't survive the snap-fit flexure.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Derive the energy-storage material index.**

Elastic strain energy per unit volume stored at yield:

$$u = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

To **maximize energy per unit mass**, we need to maximize $u/\rho$:

$$M_1 = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E\rho}$$

To **maximize energy per unit cost**, we need to maximize $u/(C_{kg}\rho)$:

$$M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E \cdot C_{kg} \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $M_1$ for each candidate.**

| Material | $\sigma_y^2$ [MPa²] | $E$ [GPa] | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | $M_1 = \sigma_y^2 / (2E\rho)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Music wire | $3.24 \times 10^6$ | 200 | 7850 | $\dfrac{3.24 \times 10^6}{2 \times 200 \times 10^3 \times 7.85} = 1.032$ |
| SS 302 | $1.0 \times 10^6$ | 190 | 8020 | $\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^6}{2 \times 190 \times 10^3 \times 8.02} = 0.328$ |
| Be-Cu C17200 | $1.21 \times 10^6$ | 128 | 8250 | $\dfrac{1.21 \times 10^6}{2 \times 128 \times 10^3 \times 8.25} = 0.573$ |
| Ti-6Al-4V | $7.74 \times 10^5$ | 113 | 4430 | $\dfrac{7.74 \times 10^5}{2 \times 113 \times 10^3 \times 4.43} = 0.773$ |

**Best for energy per unit mass:** **Music wire** ($M_1 = 1.032$).

**Step 3 — Compute $M_2$ for each candidate.**

| Material | $M_2 = \sigma_y^2 / (2E C_{kg} \rho)$ |
|---|---|
| Music wire | $1.032 / 120 = 8.60 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| SS 302 | $0.328 / 280 = 1.17 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Be-Cu C17200 | $0.573 / 1500 = 3.82 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Ti-6Al-4V | $0.773 / 4500 = 1.72 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Best for energy per rupee:** **Music wire** ($M_2 = 8.60 \times 10^{-3}$).

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Music wire dominates both rankings. This is consistent with its universal use as the *default* spring material in mechanical engineering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator: $\sigma_y^2 / E$ vs $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$. Since both candidates use the same factor, the *ranking* is unaffected, but the absolute numerical value will be wrong. Examiners may deduct 1 mark for the missing "1/2".
> 2. **Mixing up $M_1$ and $M_2$**: the "best per rupee" question requires the **cost dimension** to enter the index. Many students forget to multiply by $C_{kg}$ and incorrectly rank the same as $M_1$.
> 3. **Not stating the index formula before plugging in numbers** — a classic 1-mark deduction. Always *show the formula first, then substitute*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 3 — Suitable Materials**

- **Definition:** A "suitable material" in DFMA = a material that passes the **four filters** — functional, manufacturing, assembly, life-cycle.
- **Ashby's 4-step method:** Translation → Screening → Ranking (with indices) → Supporting Information.
- **Specific stiffness** $E/\rho$ — index for a **lightweight stiff tie in tension**.
- **Specific strength** $\sigma_y/\rho$ — index for a **lightweight strong tie in tension**.
- **Beam in bending** → index exponent on $E$ is **1/3**.
- **Shaft in torsion** → index exponent on $E$ is **1/2**.
- **Column buckling** → index exponent on $E$ is **1/2** (Pcrit = $\pi^2 EI / L^2$).
- **Spring** → index is $\sigma_y^2 / E$ (energy density).
- **Cost-per-unit-property** for a stiff beam → minimize $C_{kg} \rho / E^{1/3}$.
- **Ashby charts** are log-log plots; lines of constant index are diagonals; to **maximize** index, slide **up-and-left**.
- **Manufacturing compatibility** must be checked: e.g., polymers cannot be cast or forged; ceramics cannot be machined (only ground); CFRP cannot be welded.
- **Assembly compatibility** must be checked: e.g., snap-fit needs flexible polymer; welding needs similar metals; adhesive works on almost any solid.
- **Always state the four pieces** when solving a material index problem: **Function, Constraint, Objective, Free Variable**.
- **Always show units** explicitly during numerical substitution.
- **Always check the grade**, not just the family — wrong *grade* is the most common real-world failure mode.
- **Common DFMA material grades to memorize** for KTU: AISI 1020 (low-carbon steel), AISI 4340 (Ni-Cr-Mo alloy steel), Al 6061-T6 (extrusions), Al 7075-T6 (aerospace), Ti-6Al-4V (aerospace + biomedical), PC/ABS (consumer electronics), POM/acetal (gears), PP (snap-fits), WC-Co (cutting tools), GFRP/CFRP (lightweight structures).
- **Springback index** $K = \sigma_y / E$ — to minimize springback in sheet metal forming, choose a material with low yield-to-modulus ratio (e.g., aluminum alloys spring back less than HSLA steel of same strength).
- **Wear-resistance index** $H/E$ — high for tool steels and ceramics, low for soft polymers.
- **Recyclability** must be considered; mixed-material assemblies are a DFMA **anti-pattern** because they block end-of-life recycling.
- **Rule of thumb:** If the part can be made from one material, do so — multi-material parts increase manufacturing, assembly, and recycling complexity.
- **Last-line trick for ESE answers:** Always close with a sentence like *"Therefore, the selected material [GRADE] optimally satisfies all four DFMA suitability filters for the given application."* Examiners reward holistic, principle-aligned conclusions.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEMET522, Module 3, Topic: Suitable Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design Recommendations** in the context of Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) constitute a structured set of engineering heuristics, geometric rules, dimensional guidelines, and best-practice methodologies that a product designer must embed at the conceptual and embodiment stages of the product development cycle to simultaneously optimise **manufacturability**, **assemblability**, **cost**, **quality**, and **sustainability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Phrasing)**
> *"Design recommendations are systematic, process-specific engineering guidelines that translate manufacturing and assembly constraints into actionable geometric and material rules, enabling the designer to minimise life-cycle cost and maximise functional efficiency without manual iteration or prototyping."*

These recommendations are domain-specific — a design that is excellent for **investment casting** will be poor for **sheet-metal fabrication**, and vice versa. Therefore, the DFMA practitioner maintains a portfolio of recommendations tailored to each primary shaping process (casting, forging, machining, injection moulding, sheet-metal forming, additive manufacturing) and each joining method (welding, fastening, snap-fits, adhesive bonding).

## 1.2 The Hierarchy of Design Recommendations

Design recommendations are layered from **generic (process-independent)** at the top to **specific (process-dependent)** at the bottom. This hierarchy is mandatory in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

1. **Generic Recommendations (DFM in general):** Simplification, standardisation, symmetry, modularity, minimisation of part count, common datum surfaces, environment-friendly design.
2. **Process-Specific Recommendations:** Tailored to a specific manufacturing route.
3. **Assembly Recommendations (DFA):** Self-locating features, self-fastening joints, insertion-friendly geometry, symmetry to avoid reversal.
4. **Logistics and Disposal Recommendations (DFE/DFD):** Recyclability, ease of disassembly, packaging geometry.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Rulebook"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine you are an architect designing a house. You know that the foundation must be wide and flat, walls must be vertical, the roof needs a slight slope to drain rainwater, and the staircase should have uniform step heights. These are **"design recommendations"** for a house. They are not laws of physics — a flat roof is possible — but following them makes the house cheaper, stronger, and easier to build.
>
> In the **same way**, DFMA design recommendations are the "house-building rules" for engineering parts. For example, a sand-cast part must have **uniform wall thickness** (like a wall of even height) so that the molten metal solidifies evenly and doesn't crack. A plastic part must have **draft angles** (like the slope of a staircase) so that it can slide out of the mould without damage. A stamped sheet-metal part must have **generous bend radii** (like a gentle curve) so that the metal doesn't crack at the corner.
>
> An engineer who ignores these rules will end up with a perfectly drawn part on CAD that is **expensive, difficult, or even impossible** to manufacture. DFMA recommendations are therefore the *bridge between a designer's intention and the factory's reality*.

## 1.4 Why Design Recommendations Matter — The Cost Imperative

Industry studies consistently show that **70–80% of a product's life-cycle cost is locked-in at the design stage**, before any manufacturing tool is even ordered. Once a part is designed with poor recommendations:

- Tooling becomes complex and expensive.
- Cycle times increase due to extra operations.
- Scrap and rejection rates rise.
- Assembly requires more time and labour.
- Warranty and service costs balloon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric (KTU Board Favourite)**
> The **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index** introduced by Boothroyd, Dewhurst and Knight is the canonical metric used in KTU valuation:
> $$\text{DFA Index (\%)} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + N_a \cdot t_a} \times 100$$
> where $N_m$ is the theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ is the theoretical minimum assembly time, $N_a$ is the actual number of parts, and $t_a$ is the actual assembly time. A well-designed assembly has a **DFA Index ≥ 60%**.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Process-Specific Design Recommendations (Master List)

The KTU 2024 Scheme requires familiarity with at least the following six domains. A tabular summary at a high level is given below; each domain is expanded in subsequent sections.

| Sl. No | Design Domain | Primary Shaping Process | Most Critical Recommendation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Design for Sand Casting** | Sand moulds | Uniform wall thickness (no abrupt changes) |
| 2 | **Design for Forging** | Closed/Open die forging | Symmetric parting line, generous draft, draft web |
| 3 | **Design for Machining** | Turning, milling, drilling | Accessibility for standard cutting tools |
| 4 | **Design for Sheet Metal** | Bending, stamping, deep drawing | Bend radius $\geq 1T$, hole-to-edge distance $\geq 2T$ |
| 5 | **Design for Injection Moulding (Plastics)** | Thermoplastic moulding | Uniform wall thickness, draft angle $0.5°$–$2°$ |
| 6 | **Design for Assembly (DFA)** | Mechanical joining | Self-locating, self-fastening, symmetry |

## 1.6 The Four Pillars of Generic DFM Recommendations

Every KTU 2024 answer on "Design Recommendations" should mention these four pillars:

1. **MINIMISATION** — Reduce the number of parts. Combine functions into single parts.
2. **STANDARDISATION** — Use standard sizes, materials, fasteners, tolerances, and processes.
3. **SIMPLIFICATION** — Use geometric primitives (flat, cylindrical, prismatic) and avoid complex undercuts.
4. **SYMMETRY & COMMON DATUMS** — Use symmetric features and shared reference surfaces for ease of fixturing and inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams: "M-S-S-C"** — **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, make **C**ommon datums.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design for Casting — Detailed Recommendations

Cast parts must be designed to flow, solidify, and release without defect. The major failure modes are **hot tears**, **porosity**, **cold shuts**, **misruns**, and **mould-shift**.

### 2.1.1 Uniform Wall Thickness
A part with variable wall thickness will solidify at different rates. Thick sections act as **hot spots** (last to solidify) and pull material from adjacent thinner walls, causing **shrinkage cavities**. Recommendation: thickness variation should not exceed **2:1 to 3:1** in any region. If structural rigidity demands variation, use a **gradual taper** rather than an abrupt step.

### 2.1.2 Fillets and Radii
Sharp internal corners act as **stress concentrators** and impede metal flow. Recommended:
- **Internal fillet radius:** $r_i \geq \frac{T_1 + T_2}{4}$ where $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the adjoining wall thicknesses.
- **External corner radius:** $r_e = r_i + \frac{T_1}{2}$ (to keep uniform wall thickness on the outside).

### 2.1.3 Draft (Taper) Angle
The part must be wider at the parting line so that the mould can be withdrawn without damage.

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 0.5° \text{ to } 1.5° \text{ (sand cast, machined surfaces)}$$

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 1° \text{ to } 3° \text{ (die cast, investment cast, raw cast surfaces)}$$

### 2.1.4 Ribs and Cores
Ribs in castings should have:
- **Thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.7 \times T_{\text{wall}}$
- **Spacing:** $\geq 3 \times T_{\text{wall}}$
- **Tapered at end** to avoid hot spot at rib termination.

Cores (for internal cavities) should be:
- **Symmetric** (so they don't shift under metal pressure).
- **Adequately supported** by chaplets to prevent core-shift.
- Designed with **generous fillets** at core-prints.

## 2.2 Design for Forging — Detailed Recommendations

### 2.2.1 Parting Line
The parting line is the plane of separation between the upper and lower die halves. KTU-recommended guidelines:
- **Parting line at the largest cross-section** to minimise draft.
- **Symmetric** about the forging axis to avoid flash imbalance.
- **Single parting line** preferred; multiple parting lines are costlier.

### 2.2.2 Draft
Forging draft is typically **3° to 7°** (much larger than casting draft) because the die walls are deep and the forging must be ejected without sticking.

### 2.2.3 Web and Rib Thickness
- **Web thickness:** $T_{\text{web}} \leq 3 \times T_{\text{adjacent wall}}$ — thicker webs cause entrapped hot spots and die failure.
- **Rib thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \leq 1.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$ — overly thick ribs are uneconomical and cause dies to crack.

### 2.2.4 Fillet and Corner Radii
A forging fillet must satisfy both **strength** and **die-filling** criteria:
- **Inner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.15 \times T$ (where $T$ is the local wall thickness), but not less than **2.5 mm**.
- **Outer radius:** $r_o = r_i + 0.5T$ approximately.
- **Sharp corners** must be avoided: they cause **mould wear** and **die-failure by thermal fatigue**.

### 2.2.5 Material Allowance
The forging envelope must allow for:
- **Shrinkage allowance:** typically **0.5% to 1.5%** of the linear dimension.
- **Machining allowance:** **1.5 mm to 6 mm** depending on size and part complexity.

## 2.3 Design for Machining — Detailed Recommendations

The key constraint is **tool accessibility** — a feature that cannot be reached by a standard cutter is often impractical or requires expensive special tooling.

### 2.3.1 Avoid Deep Narrow Pockets
A pocket with depth $H$ and width $W$ should satisfy:

$$H \leq 4W \text{ (for standard end mills)}$$

Deeper pockets require a **larger mill** or a **side-cutting tool**; in extreme cases, a separate operation.

### 2.3.2 Drilling and Tapping
- **Drill point angle:** Standard 118° (general), 135° (deep holes) — choose the drill accordingly.
- **Hole depth:** Drilling depth should not exceed **6 × drill diameter** for standard drills; otherwise a **gun-drill or ejector drill** is required (much more expensive).
- **Tapped holes:** Avoid tapped holes in materials with hardness > 35 HRC, and prefer a **threaded insert** instead.
- **Minimum wall between holes:** $\geq 1.5 \times$ hole diameter to avoid drill-wander.

### 2.3.3 Standard Radii and Chamfers
Use **standard tool radii** (0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0 mm — ISO series). Custom radii force the purchase of special tools, increasing cost by **5–10×** standard tool cost.

### 2.3.4 Surface Finish
- For finish-machined surfaces, allow adequate **machining allowance** (typically **1.0 to 3.0 mm** on cast/forge blanks).
- Specify **Ra values in the standard series**: 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3, 12.5 µm.
- Avoid specifying Ra better than necessary (e.g., Ra 0.4 µm can cost 4× the cost of Ra 1.6 µm).

## 2.4 Design for Sheet Metal — Detailed Recommendations

Sheet-metal parts are formed by **bending, drawing, stamping, blanking, and piercing**. The dominant failure mode is **spring-back** and **cracking**.

### 2.4.1 Minimum Bend Radius
The minimum inside bend radius $R_{\min}$ depends on the material and the sheet thickness $T$:

$$R_{\min} \geq \frac{T}{2} \text{ (soft materials, e.g., aluminium, mild steel)}$$

$$R_{\min} \geq 2T \text{ (stainless steel, high-strength steel)}$$

Bends sharper than this cause **surface cracks** and **edge tearing**.

### 2.4.2 Hole-to-Bend and Edge Distance
A hole placed too close to a bend will distort during forming.

$$D_{bh} \geq 2T + R \text{ (hole-to-bend centreline distance)}$$

$$D_{eh} \geq 2T \text{ (hole-to-edge distance)}$$

### 2.4.3 Hole-to-Hole Spacing
$$D_{hh} \geq 2T + R_{\text{max}} \text{ (centre-to-centre distance between adjacent holes)}$$

### 2.4.4 Bend Allowance Formula (Bend Compensation)
When laying out a flat blank, the inside bend arc length must be compensated:

$$L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

where $L_b$ is the **bend allowance** (arc length), $\theta$ is the bend angle in degrees, $R$ is the **inside bend radius**, $T$ is the **sheet thickness**, and $K$ is the **K-factor** (neutral axis ratio, typically $K = 0.33$ for soft materials, $K = 0.5$ for hard materials).

### 2.4.5 Hem and Joggle
- **Hem (closed fold):** Used to stiffen an edge. Total hem length $\geq 4T$ for soft materials, $\geq 6T$ for hard materials.
- **Joggle (offset):** Used to bypass adjacent features. Minimum offset $\geq 1.5T$ + bend radius.

## 2.5 Design for Injection Moulding (Plastics) — Detailed Recommendations

Plastic parts are designed to **flow, fill, cool, and eject** from a mould.

### 2.5.1 Uniform Wall Thickness
**The single most important rule.** Variation in wall thickness causes:
- **Sink marks** (thicker areas shrink more).
- **Voids** (internal shrink cavities).
- **Warpage** (differential cooling stresses).
- **Residual stress** and **weld-line weakness**.

Recommended: wall-thickness variation $\leq \mathbf{25\%}$ across the part. Typical wall thickness values: **1.0 to 3.0 mm** for consumer plastics, **1.5 to 4.0 mm** for engineering plastics.

### 2.5.2 Draft Angle for Mould Release
The plastic shrinks onto the core, making ejection difficult without draft.

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 0.5° \text{ to } 1° \text{ (polished, simple cores)}$$

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 1° \text{ to } 2° \text{ (textured or deep cores)}$$

### 2.5.3 Boss and Rib Design
- **Boss outer diameter:** $D_b \approx 2.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$ (for press-fit fastener use).
- **Rib thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$ to $0.6 \times T_{\text{wall}}$.
- **Rib height:** $\leq 3 \times T_{\text{wall}}$ for balanced flow.
- **Gusset fillet radius:** $r \approx 0.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$.

### 2.5.4 Rounded Corners
- **Inner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$.
- **Outer radius:** $r_o = r_i + T_{\text{wall}}$.

### 2.5.5 Gate Location and Weld Lines
Place the gate at the **thickest section** and at a **symmetric location** to balance flow. Avoid gates that create weld lines in **highly stressed regions**.

## 2.6 Design for Assembly (DFA) — Detailed Recommendations

The DFA philosophy was formalised by **Boothroyd and Dewhurst (1983)**. Recommendations are grouped under three heuristics:

### 2.6.1 Reducing the Number of Parts
**"Eliminate, then combine."** For each part, ask:
- *Does the part move relative to others during operation?* If no → candidate for elimination.
- *Is a different material mandatory?* If no → candidate for elimination.
- *Is disassembly required for service?* If no → candidate for elimination.
- *Does the part serve a separate assembly function?* If no → candidate for elimination.

### 2.6.2 Ease of Insertion
- **Self-locating features:** Chamfers, lead-ins, tapers.
- **Asymmetric features:** Avoid the need to orient during assembly (or use symmetry deliberately to *prevent* incorrect orientation).
- **Snap-fits, press-fits, adhesives:** These eliminate the need for loose fasteners.
- **Standardised fasteners:** Use **metric ISO** or **UNC/UNF** only — no proprietary fasteners.
- **Tool access:** Ensure line-of-sight for screwdrivers and clearance for torque wrenches.

### 2.6.3 DFA Efficiency Index
$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Target:** $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$ is considered excellent (a Boeing study of cabin doors achieved 73%).

## 2.7 Design for Environment (DFE) and End-of-Life

### 2.7.1 Design for Disassembly (DFD)
- Use **captive fasteners** that cannot fall out during disassembly.
- Limit the **variety of joining methods** (e.g., one fastener type per assembly).
- Mark plastic parts with **ISO 11469** resin codes for recycling.

### 2.7.2 Design for Recycling
- **Mono-material** designs are easier to recycle than multi-material.
- Avoid inseparable bonded joints between dissimilar materials.
- Use **snap-fits** over **adhesives** for thermoplastics.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Master Table — Memorise before the KTU ESE.**

| Recommendation Domain | Key Formula or Rule | Typical Numerical Value | Engineering Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Casting fillet | $r_i \geq \frac{T_1 + T_2}{4}$ | 2–10 mm | Reduces stress concentration, aids flow |
| Casting draft | $\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 0.5°$ | 0.5°–3° | Mould release without damage |
| Forging draft | $\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 3°$ | 3°–7° | Die ejection without galling |
| Forging web | $T_{\text{web}} \leq 3 T_{\text{wall}}$ | – | Prevents entrapped hot spots |
| Forging inner radius | $r_i \geq 0.15 T$, min 2.5 mm | 2.5–6 mm | Die-filling and die-life |
| Sheet-metal bend | $R_{\min} \geq T$ (soft) | $\geq 1T$–$2T$ | Prevents surface cracking |
| Sheet-metal bend allowance | $L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \theta (R + K T)$ | $K = 0.33$ soft, $0.5$ hard | Flat-pattern unfolding |
| Hole-to-bend | $D_{bh} \geq 2T + R$ | 4–8 mm typical | Prevents hole distortion |
| Hole-to-edge | $D_{eh} \geq 2T$ | 2–6 mm | Prevents tear-out |
| Plastic wall variation | $\Delta T \leq 0.25 T_{\text{wall}}$ | $\leq 25\%$ | Prevents sink, void, warp |
| Plastic draft | $\theta_{\text{draft}} \geq 0.5°$ | 0.5°–2° | Mould release |
| Plastic rib | $T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.5 T_{\text{wall}}$ | 50%–60% | Prevents sink at rib root |
| Plastic boss | $D_b \approx 2.5 T_{\text{wall}}$ | 2.5× wall | Self-tapping screw boss |
| DFA Index | $\eta = \frac{N_m t_m}{N_m t_m + N_a t_a} \cdot 100$ | Target $\geq 60\%$ | Assembly efficiency |
| Machining pocket | $H \leq 4 W$ | – | Standard end-mill reach |
| Hole drilling | Depth $\leq 6 D_{\text{drill}}$ | – | Standard drill, no gun-drill |
| Machining finish | Ra in ISO series | 0.4–12.5 µm | Avoid over-spec |

## 2.9 The Cost Equation of DFMA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The integrated DFMA cost equation used in KTU ESE problems:**
> $$C_T = C_M + C_A + C_S + C_F + C_D$$
> where $C_T$ is total life-cycle cost, $C_M$ is manufacturing cost, $C_A$ is assembly cost, $C_S$ is service/warranty cost, $C_F$ is failure-cost (rework/scrap), and $C_D$ is disposal/recycling cost. Good design recommendations simultaneously reduce **all five** sub-costs.

**Engineering utility of these recommendations:**
- Used in **CAD-integrated DFMA software** (e.g., Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFM/DFA, Siemens NX DFM Advisor).
- Forms the **knowledge-base of design-for-X expert systems**.
- Drives **DFMEA (Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** by preempting known defects.
- Underlies **AI-driven generative design tools** (Autodesk Generative Design, nTopology) that constraint-optimise using these geometric rules.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sheet-Metal Bend Allowance (Neutral-Axis Geometry)

The bend allowance determines how long a flat sheet must be cut so that, when bent, it yields the desired inside dimensions. The derivation proceeds from the geometry of the neutral axis.

**Step 1 — Establish the geometry.** When a sheet of thickness $T$ is bent over a die of inner radius $R$ through an angle $\theta$ (in degrees), the **inside fibre** (radius $R$) is in tension, the **outside fibre** (radius $R + T$) is in compression, and the **neutral axis** (a fibre at constant length) lies at radius $r_n$ from the inside surface.

$$r_n = R + K \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — State the neutral-axis assumption.** The neutral axis is the locus of fibres that neither stretch nor compress. Its length is preserved during bending and therefore equals the bend allowance $L_b$.

$$L_b = \text{arc length of neutral axis} = \left(\frac{\theta}{360}\right) \cdot 2\pi \cdot r_n = \left(\frac{\theta}{180}\right) \cdot \pi \cdot r_n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $r_n = R + K T$ and convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians implicitly via the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ factor.**

$$L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

**Step 4 — Working Example.** A sheet of thickness $T = 2$ mm is bent through $\theta = 90°$ over a punch of inside radius $R = 3$ mm. The material is mild steel; K-factor $K = 0.33$.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_b &= \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot 90 \cdot (3 + 0.33 \cdot 2) \\
&= \left(\frac{3.1416}{2}\right) \cdot (3 + 0.66) \\
&= 1.5708 \cdot 3.66 \\
&= 5.749 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Add the straight flanges to obtain the flat blank length.**

$$L_{\text{blank}} = L_1 + L_b + L_2$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the lengths of the straight portions of the part on either side of the bend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is one of the most commonly set problems in KTU ESE. The examiner will expect the **neutral-axis definition**, the **arc-length formula**, and the **numerical evaluation with K-factor**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DFA Efficiency Index (Boothroyd–Dewhurst Method)

The DFA Index quantifies how close a design is to its theoretical optimum. The derivation is from the concept of "ideal minimum assembly."

**Step 1 — Define the theoretical-minimum assembly.** A perfect assembly uses only the minimum number of parts ($N_m$) and requires only the **theoretical minimum assembly time** per part ($t_m$).

$$t_m = 1.5 \text{ s (Boothroyd empirical baseline, includes grasp, orient, insert, release)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the actual assembly.** A real design uses $N_a$ parts, each requiring its actual individual assembly time $t_{a,i}$.

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio as the ratio of "theoretical" to "actual" assembly work.**

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{\text{Theoretical assembly work}}{\text{Actual assembly work}} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_{a,i}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.**

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} (\%)= \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_{a,i}} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Working Example (KTU-typical 7-mark problem).** A product has 12 parts in the current design, with an actual total assembly time of 90 s. The analysis reveals that the theoretical minimum number of parts is 5 (3 parts can be combined into a single moulded body) and the theoretical minimum assembly time is $5 \times 1.5 = 7.5$ s. Compute the DFA Index.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA}} &= \frac{5 \cdot 1.5}{5 \cdot 1.5 + 90} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.5}{97.5} \times 100 \\
&= 7.692\%
\end{aligned}$$

This indicates a very low-efficiency design. After redesign (eliminating 3 parts and combining the rest into a single snap-fit body with $N_a = 5$, $t_{a, \text{total}} = 15$ s):

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, new}} &= \frac{5 \cdot 1.5}{5 \cdot 1.5 + 15} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.5}{22.5} \times 100 \\
&= 33.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-distribution pattern used by KTU examiner:**
> - 1 mark — Statement of formula.
> - 2 marks — Defining $N_m$, $N_a$, $t_m$, $t_a$.
> - 2 marks — Numerical substitution.
> - 2 marks — Final result, units, and interpretation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cost Reduction Through Part-Count Reduction

**Step 1 — The assembly cost per part.** For a part with a base manufacturing cost $C_m$ and an individual assembly cost $C_{a,i}$, the per-part cost is:

$$C_{\text{part},i} = C_{m,i} + C_{a,i}$$

**Step 2 — The total assembly cost.**

$$C_{\text{assembly}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} C_{\text{part},i} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} C_{m,i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} C_{a,i}$$

**Step 3 — Fixed overheads.** Each part also incurs fixed costs: tooling $C_T$, handling $C_H$, inspection $C_I$, and purchasing/admin $C_P$. For a complex assembly, these can be the dominant cost driver.

$$C_{\text{fixed, per part}} \approx C_T + C_H + C_I + C_P$$

**Step 4 — Total cost after part-count reduction.** Eliminating $\Delta N$ parts (by combining them) reduces the fixed-cost terms by $\Delta N$ and may increase $C_{m,i}$ slightly (because the combined part is more complex). The net cost reduction is:

$$\Delta C = \Delta N \cdot (C_T + C_H + C_I + C_P) - \Delta C_m$$

**Step 5 — Working Example.** A bracket assembly has 8 parts, each with a fixed cost of ₹50 (tooling, handling, inspection, purchasing). Manufacturing cost per part averages ₹30. By combining 3 parts into one moulded part, the combined part costs ₹90 (complexity penalty). Compute the net saving.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 3 \cdot 50 - (90 - 3 \cdot 30) \\
&= 150 - 0 \\
&= ₹150 \text{ per assembly}
\end{aligned}$$

(Net savings: ₹150 per unit; tooling cost saving dominates; no penalty from moulded part since the new design is 30 + 30 + 30 = 90 = new part cost.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** This derivation is used in **make-or-buy decisions**, **modular vs. integral** trade studies, and **value-engineering workshops** in industry.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Design Recommendations by Process

| Design Criterion | Casting | Forging | Machining | Sheet Metal | Plastic Moulding | Welding/Joining |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Uniform wall thickness** | Critical | Important | Less critical | N/A | **Critical** | Important |
| **Generous fillet radii** | Critical | Critical | Moderate | Moderate | **Critical** | Important |
| **Draft angle** | 0.5°–3° | 3°–7° | N/A | N/A | **0.5°–2°** | N/A |
| **Standard tools/sizes** | Moderate | Moderate | **Critical** | **Critical** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Part symmetry** | Important | **Critical** | Moderate | Important | Important | Important |
| **Minimum bend radius** | N/A | N/A | N/A | **$\geq 1T$** | N/A | N/A |
| **Hole-to-edge distance** | Moderate | Moderate | **Critical** | **Critical** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Material marking** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | **Critical (ISO 11469)** | N/A |
| **Disassembly enablement** | Important | Important | Important | Important | **Critical (snap-fits)** | **Critical** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in the exam to write a comparative answer.** A comparative table earns full marks more reliably than narrative text.

## 3.5 Derivation 4: The Geometric Justification of Wall-Thickness Ratios

**Step 1 — Mass conservation during flow.** For a plastic part flowing through a runner into a cavity of thickness $T$, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ must be maintained. By continuity:

$$Q = v_1 \cdot A_1 = v_2 \cdot A_2$$

where $v$ is flow velocity and $A$ is cross-section.

**Step 2 — Cooling time dependence.** The cooling time $t_c$ of a plastic part scales approximately as the square of its thickness:

$$t_c \propto T^2$$

**Step 3 — Derive the effect of thickness variation.** A region of thickness $T_1$ and a region of thickness $T_2$ will cool in different times:

$$\frac{t_{c,2}}{t_{c,1}} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^2$$

If $T_2 / T_1 = 2$, then $t_{c,2} = 4 \cdot t_{c,1}$ — the thicker section takes **4× longer** to freeze, leading to sink marks and voids.

**Step 4 — Quantitative recommendation.** To keep the cooling-time ratio below **1.5** (i.e., $\leq 50\%$ variation), the thickness ratio must satisfy:

$$\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^2 \leq 1.5 \implies \frac{T_2}{T_1} \leq \sqrt{1.5} \approx 1.225$$

This gives the **25% wall-thickness variation rule** (since $1.225 - 1 = 0.225 \approx 25\%$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** The $25\%$ rule for plastics is a **derivable result**, not an arbitrary number. Writing the cooling-time argument (Step 3 above) elevates your answer from "good" to "outstanding."

## 3.6 Numerical Worked-Example Master Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern, 14 Marks)

> A product assembly currently uses 12 parts, each requiring 8 s for assembly (total $t_a = 96$ s). The theoretical minimum number of parts is 4, and the theoretical minimum assembly time per part is 1.5 s. The DFA team redesigns the product to combine parts and uses **snap-fits** to reduce the actual assembly time to 4 s per part. Compute the **DFA Index before and after redesign** and the **percentage cost saving** if the assembly labour cost is ₹1 per second.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{before}} &= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 96} \times 100 = \frac{6}{102} \times 100 = 5.88\% \\
\eta_{\text{after}} &= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 4 \cdot 4} \times 100 = \frac{6}{22} \times 100 = 27.27\%
\end{aligned}$$

Cost before: $96 \times ₹1 = ₹96$. Cost after: $4 \times 4 \times ₹1 = ₹16$. Saving: $₹80$ per assembly (83.3%).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing "number of parts" with "number of fasteners." In DFA, $N_a$ counts **parts**, not fasteners, unless fasteners are separate parts.
