# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Design for Assembly Index" and state the typical industry target value for an excellent assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index**, also called the DFA efficiency, is the ratio of the theoretical minimum assembly work to the actual assembly work, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} (\%) = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = 1.5 s), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. **Industry target: $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.** *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark target value.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six design recommendations for sand casting with a one-line justification for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 6 of the following, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Uniform wall thickness** — prevents shrinkage cavities at hot spots.
2. **Generous fillet radii** ($r_i \geq \frac{T_1 + T_2}{4}$) — reduces stress concentration.
3. **Draft angle** ($0.5°$–$3°$) — allows mould release without damage.
4. **Tapered ribs** ($T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$) — avoids hot spot at rib termination.
5. **Symmetric cores** — prevents core-shift under metal pressure.
6. **Generous parting line at the largest cross-section** — simplifies moulding.
7. **Avoid abrupt changes in section** — use gradual tapers instead.
8. **Provide machining allowance** (1.5–3 mm) — for finish-machined surfaces.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (A): (a)** Discuss the general design recommendations for **forging** with reference to parting line, draft angle, web and rib design, fillet radii, and machining allowance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel flat plate of thickness $T = 3$ mm is to be bent through an angle of $90°$ over an inside bend radius of $R = 4$ mm. The part has two straight flanges of 60 mm and 80 mm. Take the K-factor as 0.33. Calculate the **flat blank length** required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(a): Forging Design Recommendations (7 marks)

1. **Parting Line (1 mark):** The parting line should be located at the **largest cross-section** of the forging, be **symmetric** about the forging axis, and ideally be a **single plane**. A symmetric parting line ensures balanced die closure, uniform flash, and prevents die-shift. An asymmetric parting line produces uneven metal flow, uneven forging force, and die wear.

2. **Draft Angle (1 mark):** Draft is the taper provided on the side walls of the forging to allow easy ejection from the die. The standard forging draft is **3° to 7°** (significantly more than the 0.5°–3° used in casting) because forging dies are deep and the forging has to be ejected without galling. Draft is applied **perpendicular to the parting line**.

3. **Web and Rib Design (2 marks):**
   - **Web thickness:** $T_{\text{web}} \leq 3 \times T_{\text{adjacent wall}}$. Thicker webs cause **entrapped hot spots** (the web cools last, pulling material from adjacent ribs) and reduce die life. A web that is too thick also acts as a heat sink that prevents proper die-filling.
   - **Rib thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \leq 1.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$. Overly thick ribs are uneconomical (waste of material) and cause dies to crack from thermal cycling.
   - Ribs should be **tapered** at the end to avoid hot spots.

4. **Fillet Radii (2 marks):** Fillet radii are critical for both **strength** and **die-filling**.
   - **Inner corner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.15 \times T$, but **not less than 2.5 mm** (a minimum absolute value is mandatory regardless of the local thickness).
   - **Outer corner radius:** $r_o = r_i + 0.5T$ (to keep uniform stock).
   - Sharp corners cause **mould wear** and **die-failure by thermal fatigue** — forgings with sharp corners are almost always rejected in industry.

5. **Machining and Shrinkage Allowance (1 mark):**
   - **Shrinkage allowance:** 0.5% to 1.5% of the linear dimension, applied in the die (so the forged part cools to the nominal size).
   - **Machining allowance:** 1.5 mm to 6 mm (depending on size and complexity), to be removed during finish-machining to remove scale and achieve final tolerances.

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(b): Sheet-Metal Flat Blank Length (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify and list the inputs:**
- Sheet thickness: $T = 3$ mm
- Bend angle: $\theta = 90°$
- Inside bend radius: $R = 4$ mm
- K-factor: $K = 0.33$
- Flange lengths: $L_1 = 60$ mm, $L_2 = 80$ mm

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the neutral-axis radius:**

$$r_n = R + K \cdot T = 4 + 0.33 \cdot 3 = 4 + 0.99 = 4.99 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply the bend-allowance formula:**

$$L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \theta \cdot r_n$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_b &= \left(\frac{3.1416}{180}\right) \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 0.01745 \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 1.5708 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 7.838 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Add the straight flanges:**

$$L_{\text{blank}} = L_1 + L_b + L_2 = 60 + 7.838 + 80 = 147.838 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Final answer with interpretation:**

$$\boxed{L_{\text{blank}} \approx 147.84 \text{ mm} \approx 148 \text{ mm (rounded up)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The flat blank of mild-steel sheet must be cut to a length of approximately **148 mm** to produce the final bent part of nominal flange lengths 60 mm and 80 mm after bending.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians via the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ factor (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Using $R$ instead of $(R + K T)$ in the formula (loses 2 marks — the neutral-axis correction is the essence of the problem).
> 3. Omitting the straight flanges $L_1$ and $L_2$ from the final answer (loses 1 mark).
> 4. Failing to write the **units** in the final answer (loses 0.5 mark).

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (B): (a)** Explain **Boothroyd's DFA method** in detail. How is the DFA efficiency index calculated? Discuss the four Boothroyd questions used to identify candidate parts for elimination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly has 10 parts, each requiring 6 s for assembly (total actual = 60 s). After DFA analysis, the theoretical minimum number of parts is determined as 4, with a theoretical minimum assembly time of 1.5 s per part. The redesigned assembly uses 6 parts and a total actual assembly time of 18 s. Compute the **DFA Index before and after redesign** and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(a): Boothroyd's DFA Method (7 marks)

1. **Overview of Boothroyd's DFA (1 mark):** The Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly method, developed in 1983 at the University of Massachusetts, is the most widely used quantitative DFA technique. It has two complementary outputs:
   - A **two-question test** to identify parts that are candidates for elimination.
   - A **handling/insertion-time** database that estimates the assembly time for any retained part based on its geometric features (symmetry, size, thickness, gripping difficulty, etc.).

2. **The Four Boothroyd Questions (4 marks — 1 mark each question + 1 mark for the "no" → combine rule):** For every part in the assembly, the designer asks four questions in sequence. If all four answers are **"NO"**, the part is a candidate for **elimination or combination** with an adjacent part. If any answer is **"YES"**, the part is essential and must be retained as a separate component.

   - **Q1:** *Does the part need to move (rotate, translate, oscillate) relative to the other parts during operation of the product?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q2:** *Is a different material mandatory for the part (i.e., the part must be made of a different material than the next adjacent part for functional reasons)?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q3:** *Must the part be disassembled from the product for service, maintenance, or recycling?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q4:** *Does the part serve a separate assembly function (e.g., it is a fastener, a locating pin, a sealing element, or an electrical insulator that must be added separately)?* If YES, the part is essential.

3. **The DFA Efficiency Index (2 marks):** The DFA efficiency is computed as:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = **1.5 s** per part), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. A well-designed assembly has $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(b): DFA Index Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify the given data:**

| Parameter | Before Redesign | After Redesign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of parts, $N$ | 10 | 6 |
| Actual assembly time per part | 6 s | 3 s (since 18/6 = 3) |
| Total actual time, $\sum t_{a,i}$ | 60 s | 18 s |
| Theoretical min parts, $N_m$ | 4 | 4 |
| Theoretical min time per part, $t_m$ | 1.5 s | 1.5 s |
| Theoretical min total, $N_m t_m$ | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index before redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, before}} &= \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 60} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{66} \times 100 \\
&= 9.09\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index after redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, after}} &= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 18} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{24} \times 100 \\
&= 25.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comment on the result:**

The original assembly has a DFA Index of **9.09%**, which is very low and indicates significant opportunity for design improvement. After redesign (eliminating 4 parts and using snap-fits / simplified handling), the index rises to **25.0%** — a substantial improvement. However, the redesigned assembly is still below the industry target of 60%, indicating that further simplification is possible. The cycle-time reduction is from 60 s to 18 s, a **70% reduction in assembly time** and an equivalent reduction in assembly labour cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $N_m$ (minimum number of parts) with $N_a$ (actual number of parts)** in the formula (loses 2 marks).
> 2. Forgetting to convert the $t_m$ from 1.5 s to the **total** $N_m t_m$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Forgetting the $\times 100$ to express the result as a percentage (loses 0.5 mark).
> 4. **Failing to comment** on the result (loses 1 mark) — the KTU examiner expects at least two lines of qualitative interpretation in any numerical answer.
> 5. Listing the four Boothroyd questions but **forgetting to state the "all four NO → combine"** decision rule (loses 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density exam revision notes for KTU PEMET522 Module 3 — Design Recommendations:**

- **DFMA = Design for Manufacture + Design for Assembly**; the goal is to reduce life-cycle cost **at the design stage**, not at the shop floor.
- The **four pillars** of generic DFM are: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- **70–80% of life-cycle cost is committed at the design stage** — this statistic must appear in any KTU answer on DFMA importance.
- For **casting**: uniform wall thickness, fillet $r_i \geq \frac{T_1+T_2}{4}$, draft 0.5°–3°, rib thickness $\approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- For **forging**: single symmetric parting line, draft 3°–7°, web $\leq 3 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib $\leq 1.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, $r_i \geq 2.5$ mm min, shrinkage 0.5–1.5%, machining 1.5–6 mm.
- For **machining**: hole depth $\leq 6 D$, pocket depth $\leq 4 W$, use ISO tool radii (0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3 mm), use standard ISO Ra values.
- For **sheet metal**: bend radius $R \geq 1T$ (soft) or $\geq 2T$ (hard), hole-to-edge $\geq 2T$, hole-to-bend $\geq 2T + R$, bend allowance $L_b = (\pi/180) \cdot \theta (R + KT)$, K-factor = 0.33 (soft) or 0.5 (hard).
- For **plastic moulding**: wall variation $\leq 25\%$, draft 0.5°–2°, boss diameter $\approx 2.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib thickness $\approx 0.5 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- The **25% wall-thickness rule** for plastics is derivable from cooling-time scaling $t_c \propto T^2$ — write the derivation in the exam to earn full marks.
- **Boothroyd's DFA method** uses four binary questions; a part answering "NO" to all four is a candidate for elimination.
- **DFA Index** = $\frac{N_m t_m}{N_m t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$; target $\geq 60\%$.
- $t_m$ baseline (Boothroyd) = **1.5 s per part**.
- The **DFMA cost equation**: $C_T = C_M + C_A + C_S + C_F + C_D$.
- The **M-S-S-C** mnemonic: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- The **bend-allowance formula** uses the neutral-axis radius $r_n = R + KT$, **never** the inside radius $R$ alone.
- Always state the **units** in the final answer; always **comment** on the numerical result; always **define variables** before using them in a formula.
- A **symmetric part** is easier to manufacture (no orientation constraint), easier to assemble (no reversal needed), and easier to inspect (no wrong-direction error).
- **Snap-fits** and **press-fits** eliminate loose fasteners; the design gain is typically a 30–50% assembly-time reduction.
- **Mono-material** designs are preferred for recycling (DFE); multi-material inseparable bonds are the worst case.
- The **most over-asked KTU topic** in this module: numerical on the **DFA Index** and the **bend allowance** — practise both thoroughly.
- **Killer mnemonic for the bend allowance:** "Bend along the **N**eutral axis — so the formula is $R + K T$, **not** $R$ alone."

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** The most common reason students lose marks on this module is **formula-by-rote** without understanding the physical meaning. Always write a **one-line interpretation** of any numerical result (e.g., "the redesigned assembly is 70% faster, indicating a substantial DFM improvement") — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Machining Round Holes: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design for Machining Round Holes: Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Design for Machining (DFM) of Round Holes** is a sub-discipline of Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) that establishes geometric, dimensional, and tolerance guidelines to ensure that circular features (holes, bores, and recesses) can be produced using standard, cost-effective machining processes such as drilling, boring, reaming, and tapping. It addresses the selection of **nominal hole diameters**, **tolerances**, **depth-to-diameter ratios**, **surface finish**, and **feature orientation** to minimize manufacturing cost, secondary operations, and tool wear. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome CO3 — *Apply DFM principles to design components for various manufacturing processes*), designing for machined round holes involves applying standardization rules that allow the part to integrate seamlessly with off-the-shelf fasteners (bolts, screws, rivets) and standard tooling (drill bits, taps, reamers). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The objective is to enable students to recognize that the *single largest reason* machined parts are rejected, reworked, or excessively expensive is poor hole design — wrong diameters, tight tolerances, or excessive depths. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a tailor stitching a button onto a shirt. The button has **two holes** of a specific size, and the needle you use has a specific thickness. If you force a needle that is too thick through the buttonhole, you either tear the button or ruin the needle. If you use a needle that is too thin, the button flaps loosely. Now replace the buttonhole with a **machined round hole** in a steel bracket, and the needle with a **bolt or a drill bit**. The "perfect fit" is what we call *Design for Machining*. The designer must know: 1. What standard **drill bit sizes** are available (just like knowing standard thread thicknesses). 2. How **deep** the hole can reasonably go (just like knowing how far the needle can pierce). 3. What **tolerances** are affordable (just like knowing the acceptable slack between the button and the thread). > [!NOTE] > **Why Round Holes Specifically?** > - Over **60%** of all machined features in a typical mechanical assembly are round holes (used for fasteners, bearings, fluid ports, and alignment). > - Holes are inherently **non-additive** — they require material *removal*, which is more expensive than material *addition* (e.g., casting, 3D printing). > - Therefore, hole design is the **highest-leverage decision** a designer makes for manufacturability. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following industry-standard metrics govern round hole design and are highlighted in **bold** as they are direct KTU 2024 exam vocabulary: - **Standard Drill Size Series**: ISO 286 (metric), ANSI B94.11M (inch fractional, number, and letter drills). - **Preferred Hole Diameters**: ISO 3 preferred numbers (R10, R20, R40 series). - **Typical Hole Tolerances**: **IT8 to IT11** for general machined holes; **IT6 to IT7** for precision bores (bearings, gears). - **Depth-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D)**: Generally limited to **L/D ≤ 4** for standard drilling; up to **L/D = 10** with extended-reach (gun) drills. - **Typical Surface Finish (Ra)**: **Ra 0.8–3.2 µm** for drilled holes; **Ra 0.4–1.6 µm** for reamed/bored holes. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Decision Logic for Designing a Round Hole When a KTU board exam question asks "Design a round hole for a bolted joint of diameter M10," the student must walk through this structured logic. Below is the bulleted reasoning path that the KTU 2024 valuation key expects: 1. **Identify the Functional Requirement** - *Fastener hole?* → Use a clearance hole (loose tolerance, standard size). - *Threaded hole for a screw?* → Use a tap drill size (minor diameter). - *Precision bore for a bearing?* → Specify a boring or reaming operation (tight tolerance, fine finish). 2. **Select a Preferred Diameter** - Choose a diameter from the **preferred number series** (e.g., 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm). - Avoid non-standard diameters because they force the manufacturer to procure *custom* drill bits, taps, or reamers — a major cost driver. 3. **Specify the Tolerance Grade** - General clearance hole: **H11** or **H12** (loose, inexpensive). - Locating/aligning hole: **H8** or **H7** (requires secondary boring). - Press-fit hole for bearing: **H6 or H7** (requires grinding or fine boring). 4. **Determine the Depth** - Limit the depth using the **L/D ratio** rule. For blind holes, leave at least **0.5 × D** of un-drilled material at the bottom to allow chip clearance and avoid drill breakthrough. - *Why?* A drill bit that exits the bottom of the part causes **burrs, tool breakage, and poor surface finish**. 5. **Specify the Surface Finish (Ra)** - Match the Ra value to the function. A bolt clearance hole typically needs only **Ra 3.2 µm**. A hydraulic seal bore may need **Ra 0.4 µm**. 6. **Select the Manufacturing Process** - Drilling → Boring → Reaming → Honing, in increasing order of precision and cost. - Always **prefer the lowest-cost process** that meets the tolerance and finish requirements. > [!NOTE] > **The 10x Cost Rule (DFMA Heuristic):** Each successive machining operation (drill → bore → ream → grind) typically increases the cost of producing the hole by a factor of **5× to 10×**. Therefore, *over-specifying* tolerance is the most common DFM mistake in round hole design. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates all the core equations used in round hole design and machining analysis. All units are in **SI** unless otherwise noted. | # | Parameter | Formula | Description & Units | |---|-----------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | **Drilling Time** | $T = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | $T$ = time (min), $L$ = hole depth + tool approach (mm), $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $N$ = spindle speed (rev/min) | | 2 | **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = drill diameter (mm) | | 3 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot f \cdot N}{4}$ | Units: $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ | | 4 | **Thrust Force on Drill** | $F_t = K_s \cdot A_c$ | $K_s$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $A_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$ (cross-sectional area, mm²) | | 5 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V_c}{60\,000}$ | $P$ = power (kW) | | 6 | **Maximum Recommended L/D Ratio** | $\left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)_{max} \le 4$ | For standard twist drills (general guidance) | | 7 | **Tolerance Grade (IT)** | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | $i$ = standard tolerance unit (µm), $D$ in mm | | 8 | **Standard Clearance Hole (Medium fit)** | $D_{hole} = D_{bolt} + \text{gap}$ | For M6 bolt: gap ≈ 1 mm; for M10: gap ≈ 1.5 mm; for M16: gap ≈ 2 mm | | 9 | **Tap Drill Size (for internal thread)** | $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ | $D_{major}$ = nominal thread O.D., $P$ = thread pitch (mm) | | 10 | **Cost per Hole (Cumulative)** | $C_{hole} = C_{drill} + n \cdot C_{secondary}$ | $n$ = number of secondary operations (bore, ream, tap) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Equation 7 (Tolerance Unit):** The formula $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ is the ISO 286 standard tolerance unit. The total tolerance is $IT_n = k \cdot i$, where $k$ varies from 10 (IT10) to 400 (IT16). This is a high-yield KTU 2024 derivation topic. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** Cylinder bores for engine blocks are designed with **H7 tolerance** and **Ra 0.4 µm** finish to ensure piston ring sealing. The hole is produced by *rough boring* followed by *finish honing*. - **Aerospace:** Aircraft structural panels have thousands of fastener holes. Even a **0.05 mm** tolerance relaxation saves millions in manufacturing cost per year across a fleet. - **Hydraulics & Pneumatics:** O-ring seal grooves must have a controlled surface roughness (typically **Ra 0.8–1.6 µm**) — too smooth and the seal leaks; too rough and the O-ring tears. - **PCB Manufacturing:** Through-holes for component leads use a **+0.1 / -0.0 mm** tolerance — extremely tight, hence drilled with laser or plasma after mechanical pilot holes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Drilling Time for a Round Hole The drilling time equation is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU Module 3. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. **Given:** - Hole depth: $L$ (mm) - Drill feed: $f$ (mm/rev) - Spindle speed: $N$ (rev/min) **Step 1 — Define feed per revolution.** The feed $f$ is the axial distance the drill advances into the workpiece per single revolution of the spindle. Mathematically: $$f = \frac{\text{Axial advance per revolution (mm)}}{1 \text{ rev}}$$ **Step 2 — Define the total axial distance to be traveled.** The drill must travel the full hole depth $L$ **plus** a small approach distance $a$ (typically **0.3 × D** for the drill point to clear the workpiece). The total travel distance is: $$L_{total} = L + a$$ **Step 3 — Relate time to feed and spindle speed.** The number of revolutions required to cover the total distance is: $$N_{rev} = \frac{L_{total}}{f}$$ **Step 4 — Convert revolutions to time.** Since the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the time $T$ (in minutes) is: $$T = \frac{N_{rev}}{N}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.** $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N} \\ &= \frac{L + 0.3\,D}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(with approach included)} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **final drilling time formula**. Units check: $\dfrac{\text{mm}}{(\text{mm/rev}) \cdot (\text{rev/min})} = \text{min}$. ✔ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed **Given:** - Recommended cutting speed for the workpiece material: $V_c$ (m/min) - Drill diameter: $D$ (mm) **Step 1 — Define cutting speed.** Cutting speed is the tangential surface speed at the drill periphery, given by: $$V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$ where $D$ is expressed in **meters** and $N$ in rev/min for unit consistency. **Step 2 — Convert $D$ from mm to m.** $$D_{(m)} = \frac{D_{(mm)}}{1000}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the cutting speed equation.** $$V_c = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{1000}\right) \cdot N$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $N$.** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(rev/min)} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **spindle speed equation**, which is then plugged into the drilling time formula. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Combined DFM Problem) > **KTU-Style Problem:** A 12 mm diameter hole is to be drilled in a mild steel plate (recommended cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min, feed $f = 0.15$ mm/rev). The plate thickness is 30 mm. Calculate (a) the spindle speed, and (b) the drilling time. **Part (a) — Spindle Speed** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\ &= \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 12} \\ &= \frac{25\,000}{37.699} \\ &\approx 663.1 \text{ rev/min} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b) — Drilling Time** Approach distance: $$a = 0.3 \cdot D = 0.3 \cdot 12 = 3.6 \text{ mm}$$ Total travel: $$L_{total} = L + a = 30 + 3.6 = 33.6 \text{ mm}$$ Drilling time: $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N} \\ &= \frac{33.6}{0.15 \cdot 663.1} \\ &= \frac{33.6}{99.465} \\ &\approx 0.338 \text{ min} \\ &\approx 20.3 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **DFM Insight from this Calculation:** The drilling time is dominated by the *feed* and *depth*, not the spindle speed. If the designer reduces the hole depth from 30 mm to 20 mm (by redesigning the part with a counterbore or a recess), the drilling time drops by **33%** — a direct manufacturing cost saving achieved purely through design. ### 3.4 Derivation 3: Tolerance Unit (ISO 286) for Round Holes The standard tolerance unit $i$ (in micrometres) for a nominal diameter $D$ (in mm) is: $$i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$$ **Worked Example:** For $D = 50$ mm, $$\begin{aligned} i &= 0.45 \cdot (50)^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot 50 \\ &= 0.45 \cdot 3.684 + 0.05 \\ &= 1.658 + 0.05 \\ &\approx 1.708 \;\mu\text{m} \end{aligned}$$ For tolerance grade **IT8**, the multiplier is **25** (standard ISO table). Therefore: $$IT8 = 25 \cdot i = 25 \cdot 1.708 \approx 42.7 \;\mu\text{m}$$ So the tolerance band for a **50H8** hole is $\pm 21.35\;\mu\text{m}$, i.e., $50.000^{+0.042}_{-0.000}$ mm. This is the precision a *boring* operation can achieve; a *plain drill* cannot hold this. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Hole Manufacturing Process The following flowchart captures the **designer's decision tree** when specifying a round hole. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU 2024 board questions that ask "Which process should be used?" ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Round Hole Required] --> B{Functional
Requirement?} B -->|Fastener Clearance| C[Loose Tolerance:
H11 or H12] B -->|Locating / Aligning| D[Medium Tolerance:
H8 or H9] B -->|Bearing or Seal Fit| E[Tight Tolerance:
H6 or H7] B -->|Internal Thread| F[Tap Drill Size:
D_major minus P] C --> G[Operation:
Drilling Only] D --> H[Operation:
Drill then Bore or Ream] E --> I[Operation:
Drill, Bore, then Grind or Hone] F --> J[Operation:
Drill then Tap] G --> K[Cost Baseline
1x] H --> L[Cost Estimate
5x to 8x Baseline] I --> M[Cost Estimate
10x to 25x Baseline] J --> N[Cost Estimate
2x to 3x Baseline] K --> O[Final Hole Specification] L --> O M --> O N --> O O --> P[Document on Drawing:
Diameter, Tolerance,
Depth, Ra, Callouts] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Standard Twist Drill and Hole Geometry The block diagram below maps the *functional zones* of a standard twist drill, which is essential to understanding *why* hole design is constrained by the L/D ratio. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DRILL[Standard Twist Drill] A1[Shank
Held in Chuck] --> A2[Tapered Body
Transition Zone] A2 --> A3[Fluted Body
Cutting Edges and
Chip Flutes] A3 --> A4[Drill Point
118 Degree Included Angle
Chisel Edge] end subgraph HOLE[Resulting Hole Geometry] B1[Entry Side
May Have Burr] --> B2[Cylindrical Wall
Tolerance Zone H] B2 --> B3[Bottom of Blind Hole
Conical from Drill Point
Leave 0.5D Clearance] end A3 -. cuts .-> B2 A4 -. forms .-> B3 B2 --> C{Check L/D Ratio} C -->|L over D less than 4| D[Standard Drilling OK] C -->|L over D 4 to 10| E[Use Gun Drill or
Extended-Reach Tool] C -->|L over D greater than 10| F[Redesign:
Use Cross Hole or
Open Both Sides] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Cost-Sensitivity Diagram — Effect of Tolerance on Hole Cost The following diagram illustrates the **non-linear cost penalty** of tightening hole tolerance. This is a key KTU conceptual diagram — students are often asked to "explain with a sketch" why designers should not over-specify tolerance. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Loose Tolerance:
H12, plus or minus 0.2 mm] -->|Baseline| B[Drilling Only
Cost 1.0x] B -->|Add Boring| C[Medium Tolerance:
H9, plus or minus 0.05 mm
Cost 5x] C -->|Add Reaming| D[Tight Tolerance:
H7, plus or minus 0.025 mm
Cost 10x] D -->|Add Grinding| E[Ultra-Precision:
H6 or better
Cost 25x] style A fill:#d4f4dd style B fill:#fff3bf style C fill:#ffd8a8 style D fill:#ffadad style E fill:#d0bfff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway from the Diagram:** The cost grows roughly **exponentially** as tolerance tightens. A designer who specifies H6 when H10 would suffice is paying a **20× premium** for a feature that adds no functional value. This is the *single most important* Design-for-Manufacturing principle applied to round holes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *Define Design for Machining of round holes. List any four guidelines a designer should follow when specifying a round hole on a machined component.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Design for Machining of round holes is a DFM methodology that establishes geometric, dimensional, and tolerance guidelines for circular features to ensure they can be produced using standard, low-cost machining processes. **Four Guidelines (0.375 marks each):** 1. Use **preferred standard diameters** (ISO preferred number series) to enable use of off-the-shelf drills, taps, and reamers. 2. Specify the **loosest tolerance** that satisfies the functional requirement (e.g., H11 for clearance holes). 3. Limit the **depth-to-diameter ratio (L/D)** to 4 or less for standard drilling; redesign for higher L/D. 4. Specify a **realistic surface finish (Ra)** that matches the function — avoid over-specifying mirror finishes. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *State the recommended depth-to-diameter (L/D) ratio for standard twist drilling. What is the consequence of exceeding this ratio, and what design alternatives can be used to mitigate it?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **L/D Ratio (1 mark):** The recommended maximum L/D ratio for standard twist drilling is **L/D ≤ 4**. **Consequences of Exceeding (1 mark):** Drill bit wander, poor hole straightness, excessive cutting force, tool breakage, and unacceptable surface finish. **Design Alternatives (1 mark):** Use a cross-hole from the opposite side; use a gun drill or extended-reach tool; machine the part from both sides; or redesign the part with a recess/counterbore to reduce the effective depth. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice A]** *(a) Explain with a neat sketch the different zones of a standard twist drill. Discuss how the drill geometry influences the design rules for round holes. (7 marks)* *(b) A 20 mm diameter through-hole is to be drilled in an aluminium plate of thickness 40 mm. The recommended cutting speed is 60 m/min and the feed is 0.25 mm/rev. Calculate (i) the spindle speed in rev/min, and (ii) the drilling time in seconds. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Twist Drill Zones and Influence on Hole Design Rules (7 marks) A standard twist drill consists of the following functional zones (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2): 1. **Shank (1 mark):** The cylindrical upper portion gripped by the drilling machine chuck. It transmits torque and axial force. *Design influence:* The shank diameter limits the *minimum drill length* available; very deep holes require *extended* shanks. 2. **Body / Fluted Section (1 mark):** The cutting zone with two helical flutes that evacuate chips. The flutes also deliver cutting fluid. *Design influence:* The flute length sets the practical *maximum drill depth*. This is the basis of the **L/D ≤ 4 rule** (1 mark). 3. **Cutting Edges (1 mark):** Two symmetric lips at the drill periphery that perform the actual cutting. *Design influence:* If the workpiece material is too hard or the feed is too high, the cutting edges chip, ruining the hole. Therefore, the designer must respect the *workpiece material* when specifying feeds. 4. **Point / Chisel Edge (1 mark):** The conical tip (typically **118°** included angle) that initiates the hole and the central chisel edge that provides centering. *Design influence:* The point geometry leaves a *conical bottom* in blind holes. The designer must leave at least **0.5 × D** of un-machined material to provide chip clearance and avoid breakthrough. 5. **Margin / Land (1 mark):** A narrow cylindrical band along the drill that provides a bearing surface and establishes the hole diameter. *Design influence:* Worn margins cause oversized holes — a *tool wear* effect that the designer must consider when setting tolerance. 6. **Web (1 mark):** The central core of the drill connecting the flutes; its thickness affects rigidity. *Design influence:* Thin drills (small D) deflect more easily, limiting *tolerance precision* for small-diameter deep holes. #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - $D = 20$ mm - Plate thickness $L = 40$ mm - $V_c = 60$ m/min - $f = 0.25$ mm/rev **(i) Spindle Speed (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\ &= \frac{1000 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 20} \\ &= \frac{60\,000}{62.832} \\ &\approx 954.93 \text{ rev/min} \quad \text{(round to 955 rev/min)} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Drilling Time (4 marks):** Approach distance: $$a = 0.3 \cdot D = 0.3 \cdot 20 = 6 \text{ mm}$$ Total axial travel: $$L_{total} = L + a = 40 + 6 = 46 \text{ mm}$$ Drilling time: $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N} \\ &= \frac{46}{0.25 \cdot 954.93} \\ &= \frac{46}{238.73} \\ &\approx 0.1927 \text{ min} \\ &\approx 11.56 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to add the *approach distance* (0.3 D) to the hole depth. Always state this explicitly. > - **Common Mistake 2 (1 mark lost):** Using $D$ in mm inside $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$ without the 1000 conversion factor. The 1000 is needed because $V_c$ is in m/min and $D$ is in mm. > - **Common Mistake 3 (0.5 mark lost):** Reporting the spindle speed without the *unit* (rev/min or rpm). Units are mandatory in KTU 2024 valuation. > - **Common Mistake 4 (0.5 mark lost):** Not converting the final time from minutes to seconds when the question asks for seconds. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice B]** *(a) Discuss the role of preferred standard diameters in DFM of round holes. Explain the ISO preferred number system with an example. (7 marks)* *(b) For a bolted joint using an M12 standard bolt, determine (i) the recommended clearance hole diameter using the medium-fit standard, and (ii) the corresponding tolerance grade (IT) and tolerance band in micrometres. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Preferred Standard Diameters and ISO Preferred Numbers (7 marks) 1. **Need for Standardization (1.5 marks):** Machined round holes require *physical tools* (drills, reamers, taps, boring bars). Every non-standard diameter forces the manufacturer to procure a *special* tool, which: - Increases tooling cost (a custom 10 mm reamer costs ~10× a standard one). - Increases lead time (special tools are *made-to-order*). - Reduces the pool of available *substitute* tools on the shop floor. 2. **ISO Preferred Number Series (2 marks):** The ISO 3 standard defines *preferred numbers* in three series: - **R10** — 10 steps per decade (geometric ratio $\sqrt[10]{10} \approx 1.25$) - **R20** — 20 steps per decade (geometric ratio $\sqrt[20]{10} \approx 1.12$) - **R40** — 40 steps per decade (geometric ratio $\sqrt[40]{10} \approx 1.06$) 3. **Example (2 marks):** For diameters in the 10–100 mm range, the **R20 series** gives: - 10, 11.2, 12.5, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22.4, 25, 28, 31.5, 35.5, 40, 45, 50, 56, 63, 71, 80, 90, 100 mm. - A designer who needs a "supporting boss with a 17 mm hole" should **round up to 18 mm** (R20) rather than specify 17 mm, because 18 mm is a standard drill size. 4. **Application to Fastener Standards (1.5 marks):** Bolts, screws, and dowel pins follow their own preferred diameters (M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, M20...). The hole diameter is calculated from the bolt nominal diameter plus a standard clearance gap (see Part b below). Hence, *bolt standards and hole standards are interlocked* — using non-standard bolts creates non-standard holes, doubling the cost penalty. #### Part (b) — Clearance Hole for M12 Bolt (7 marks) **(i) Recommended Clearance Hole Diameter (3 marks):** For an **M12 standard bolt** under the *medium-fit* (ISO 273 medium), the recommended clearance is **1.0 mm** on the diameter (i.e., 0.5 mm radial gap per side). $$D_{hole} = D_{bolt} + \text{clearance} = 12 + 1.0 = 13.0 \text{ mm}$$ The preferred nominal hole size is therefore **13 mm** (a standard drill size in the R20 series). For the *close-fit* (tight assembly) variant, the clearance is 0.5 mm → hole = 12.5 mm; for the *loose-fit* variant, clearance is 1.5 mm → hole = 13.5 mm. **(ii) Tolerance Grade and Band (4 marks):** For a *general clearance hole*, the recommended ISO tolerance grade is **H11** (loose, drill-only achievable). Standard tolerance unit at $D = 13$ mm: $$\begin{aligned} i &= 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D \\ &= 0.45 \cdot (13)^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot 13 \\ &= 0.45 \cdot 2.351 + 0.013 \\ &= 1.058 + 0.013 \\ &\approx 1.071 \;\mu\text{m} \end{aligned}$$ For IT11 grade, the multiplier is **100** (from the ISO 286 standard table). Therefore: $$IT11 = 100 \cdot i = 100 \cdot 1.071 \approx 107 \;\mu\text{m} \approx 0.107 \text{ mm}$$ **Tolerance Band:** - Lower deviation = **0 µm** (H is a *hole-basis* system, so the lower limit is always 0). - Upper deviation = **+107 µm** = **+0.107 mm**. - **Final hole specification: 13.000 $^{+0.107}_{-0.000}$ mm** (or simply **13 H11**). This tolerance is achievable by a *plain drilling operation alone*, meaning the part requires **no secondary finishing** — the lowest-cost manufacturing route. ✔ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout for Question B):** > - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Using the *wrong* clearance for the fit class. Students often quote 1.5 mm for all fits, but ISO 273 distinguishes close, medium, and loose fits. Always state the fit class. > - **Common Mistake 2 (1 mark lost):** Confusing the H-system *hole basis* with the *shaft basis*. For H-grades, the **lower deviation is always zero**; the entire tolerance is in the positive direction. > - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Using the wrong IT multiplier. The IT multipliers (IT6=10, IT7=16, IT8=25, IT9=40, IT10=64, IT11=100, IT12=160) must be memorized or correctly referenced. A wrong multiplier by even one grade gives a wrong answer. > - **Common Mistake 4 (0.5 mark lost):** Failing to write the **final drawing callout** (e.g., "13 H11"). The KTU 2024 rubric explicitly awards marks for the *practical specification*, not just the intermediate calculation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Design for Machining Round Holes** - ☐ **Round holes are the most common machined feature** — over 60% of all machined features in a typical assembly. Their design has the highest leverage on manufacturing cost. - ☐ Always specify **preferred standard diameters** from the ISO R10/R20/R40 series to enable use of off-the-shelf drills, reamers, and taps. - ☐ The **depth-to-diameter ratio (L/D) for standard drilling ≤ 4**. Beyond this, drill wander, poor straightness, and tool breakage become likely. - ☐ For **blind holes**, leave a minimum of **0.5 × D** un-drilled material at the bottom to allow chip clearance and avoid breakthrough. - ☐ Specify the **loosest tolerance** that satisfies function. Tolerance grade **H11** is achievable by drilling alone; **H7 or tighter** requires boring, reaming, or grinding. - ☐ The **10× cost rule**: each successive finishing operation (drill → bore → ream → grind) increases cost by ~5–10×. Over-specification is the most expensive design error. - ☐ For **fastener clearance holes**, use ISO 273 standards: medium fit adds 1.0 mm to M12, 1.5 mm to M16, and 2.0 mm to M20 bolt diameters (memorize these three reference points). - ☐ For **internal threads (tapped holes)**, the tap drill diameter is **$D_{major} - P$** (major diameter minus pitch). Example: M10 × 1.5 → tap drill = 8.5 mm. - ☐ The **ISO tolerance unit** $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ is the foundation of all IT-grade calculations; commit this formula to memory. - ☐ The **drilling time equation** $T = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f \cdot N}$ must always include the **0.3 D approach distance**; omitting it is a common KTU mark-loss pitfall. - ☐ **Spindle speed** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ — the factor of 1000 converts mm to m for unit consistency with $V_c$ in m/min. - ☐ **Surface finish (Ra)** must match function: Ra 3.2 µm for clearance, Ra 1.6 µm for locating, Ra 0.4–0.8 µm for seal/bearing fits. - ☐ When the **L/D ratio exceeds 10**, redesign the part (use a cross hole, open both sides, or add a counterbore) rather than specifying an exotic drill. - ☐ In drawing callouts, always state **diameter, tolerance grade, depth, and surface finish** together — the KTU 2024 valuation key awards marks for *complete* specifications. - ☐ The **"manufacturing process selection"** decision (drill → bore → ream → grind) should be the *last* step in design, driven by the tolerance and finish requirements — not the first.

topics.title: Suitable Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suitable Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suitable Materials (DFMA Definition):** In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, "Suitable Materials" refers to the systematic, performance-driven selection of engineering materials that simultaneously satisfy the *functional design requirements* (mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical), the *manufacturing process constraints* (castability, machinability, formability, weldability, moldability), the *assembly considerations* (joining compatibility, dimensional stability, fastening ease), and the *life-cycle criteria* (cost, recyclability, environmental impact) of a product, in accordance with **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3** learning outcomes.

In DFMA philosophy, material selection is **not a stand-alone decision** — it is a coupled decision made **in parallel** with both the **manufacturing process selection** and the **product geometry (form) design**. A "suitable" material in DFMA is the one that minimizes total cost and complexity across the **entire product life cycle** while meeting every engineering specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Chef's Ingredient Rule"**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish. You would never select ingredients **before** knowing whether the dish will be baked, fried, or steamed. Similarly, in DFMA, you must **NOT** lock in a material before you have sketched the geometry **AND** chosen a manufacturing process. Choosing a material first is like choosing chocolate for a soup recipe — a *mismatch* creates downstream problems (the chocolate won't melt into the broth, your pot will be ruined, your guests will leave).
> A *suitable* material in DFMA is the one that **cooperates** with your chosen process (casting, forging, injection molding, sheet metal forming) and **cooperates** with your chosen assembly method (snap-fit, welding, adhesive bonding, threaded fasteners).

## 1.3 Why Material Selection is a Core DFMA Pillar

The four pillars of DFMA (per Boothroyd, Dewhurst, Knight) are:

1. **DFM — Design for Manufacture** (process-friendly design)
2. **DFA — Design for Assembly** (assembly-friendly design)
3. **DFE — Design for Environment** (eco-friendly design)
4. **DFS — Design for Sustainability / Cost**

Material selection sits at the **intersection** of all four pillars. A single bad material choice can:
- **Force** a more expensive manufacturing process (e.g., choosing titanium when aluminum would suffice for a structural bracket),
- **Disable** fast assembly (e.g., choosing incompatible polymers that won't ultrasonic-weld),
- **Block** end-of-life recycling (e.g., mixing steel fasteners into an aluminum body creates disassembly/recycling nightmares),
- **Increase** total part count (e.g., choosing a brittle plastic that needs metal inserts at every screw hole).

## 1.4 The Four "Suitability Filters" in DFMA

| Filter | Question Answered | KTU Exam Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Filter** | Does the material have the *right properties*? (Strength, stiffness, hardness, conductivity, corrosion resistance) | *Material property index* |
| **Process Filter** | Can the chosen manufacturing process *shape* this material economically? | *Process-material compatibility* |
| **Assembly Filter** | Can the part be *joined* with neighboring parts using the chosen assembly method? | *Joining compatibility* |
| **Lifecycle Filter** | Is the material *cost-effective, recyclable, and compliant* with environmental regulations? | *Total life-cycle cost* |

> [!NOTE]
> **A material passes the DFMA "Suitability Test" only if it clears ALL FOUR filters.** This is a frequently tested conceptual point in KTU Module 3.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

The following metrics dominate DFMA material selection (all bolded because they are **must-know** for KTU):

- **Young's Modulus, E [GPa]** — Stiffness
- **Density, ρ [kg/m³]** — Mass
- **Yield Strength, σ_y [MPa]** — Strength
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength, σ_uts [MPa]** — Strength
- **Hardness [HV / HB / HRC]** — Wear resistance
- **Thermal Conductivity, k [W/m·K]** — Heat dissipation
- **Thermal Expansion Coefficient, α [10⁻⁶/°C]** — Dimensional stability
- **Electrical Resistivity, ρ_e [Ω·m]** — Conductivity
- **Corrosion Rate [mm/year]** — Durability
- **Specific Stiffness, E/ρ [MPa·m³/kg]** — Stiffness-to-weight ratio
- **Specific Strength, σ_y/ρ [kPa·m³/kg]** — Strength-to-weight ratio
- **Cost per kg, C_kg [USD/kg or ₹/kg]** — Raw material cost
- **Cost per unit property, C_pp** — Economic selection index

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ashby Material Selection Methodology (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prof. Michael Ashby's method** is the **gold standard** for systematic material selection in DFMA. KTU examiners expect you to describe its 4-step procedure in any material-selection question.

**Step 1 — Translation:** Convert the *design requirements* into **Function** (e.g., "carry a tensile load"), **Constraints** (e.g., "must not exceed 5 kg"), **Objectives** (e.g., "minimize cost"), and **Free Variables** (e.g., "choice of material, choice of cross-section").

**Step 2 — Screening:** Apply **constraints** to eliminate unsuitable materials. e.g., if part must operate at 800 °C, eliminate all polymers and most aluminum alloys.

**Step 3 — Ranking:** Use **material indices** to rank the survivors. A material index is a *combination of properties* that maximizes or minimizes the objective while satisfying constraints.

**Step 4 — Supporting Information:** Use microstructural data, manufacturing notes, experience, and supplier information to make the final choice.

## 2.2 Material Indices — The "Math of Suitability"

A **Material Index (M)** is a mathematical expression combining two or more material properties to optimize a specific performance objective.

**General formulation:**

$$M = \frac{f_1(P_1)}{f_2(P_2)}$$

where $P_1, P_2, \ldots$ are material properties and $f_1, f_2, \ldots$ are monotonic functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In exam answers, always state the **functional requirement**, **constraint**, **objective**, and then **derive the index**. Do not just write down the formula.

### 2.2.1 Derivation of Common Material Indices

**Case 1 — Lightweight Stiff Tie (Tension Member)**

- **Function:** Carry axial tensile load $F$ without excessive elastic deflection.
- **Constraint:** Length $L$ is fixed, deflection $\delta \le \delta^*$, load $F$ is given.
- **Objective:** Minimize mass $m$.

Axial stiffness: $k = \dfrac{EA}{L}$, so $A = \dfrac{FL}{E\delta^*}$

Mass: $m = AL\rho = \dfrac{FL\rho}{E\delta^*}$

To **minimize m** for fixed L, F, $\delta^*$:

$$\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

This is the **specific stiffness** index.

**Case 2 — Lightweight Strong Tie (Tension Member)**

- **Function:** Carry axial tensile load $F$ without yielding.
- **Constraint:** Length $L$ fixed; factor of safety $n$ given.
- **Objective:** Minimize mass $m$.

Required area: $A = \dfrac{F}{\sigma_y / n} = \dfrac{nF}{\sigma_y}$

Mass: $m = AL\rho = \dfrac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$

To **minimize m**:

$$\boxed{M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

This is the **specific strength** index.

**Case 3 — Minimum-Cost Stiff Beam**

- **Function:** Carry bending load with limited deflection.
- **Objective:** Minimize cost $C = C_{kg} \cdot m$.

Following the same logic, the index becomes:

$$\boxed{M_3 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho \cdot C_{kg}} \quad \text{(Maximize)}}$$

(The exponent on $E$ depends on loading mode — tension: 1, bending: 1/2, torsion: 2/3.)

**Case 4 — Minimum-Cost Lightweight Column (Buckling)**

For a column of length $L$ with critical buckling load $P_{cr}$ constraint:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} \Rightarrow I = \frac{P_{cr} L^2}{\pi^2 E}$$

The index becomes:

$$\boxed{M_4 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho \cdot C_{kg}} \quad \text{(same as M_3, different loading mode)}}$$

## 2.3 The Ashby Chart (E vs ρ Log-Log Plot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Plotting Young's Modulus $E$ on the y-axis and density $\rho$ on the x-axis (both logarithmic) gives the famous **Ashby Chart**. Material families form characteristic "bubbles" (ceramics top-left, polymers bottom-right, metals in the middle, composites spanning diagonally). Lines of constant $E/\rho$ are *diagonals* — to maximize $E/\rho$, you slide your selection bubble **up-and-to-the-left**.

This graphical method allows rapid visual screening of thousands of materials and is the cornerstone of computer-aided material selection (CES EduPack, Granta MI).

## 2.4 Manufacturing Process Compatibility Matrix

| Material Family | Casting | Forging | Sheet Forming | Machining | Injection Molding | Powder Metallurgy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous metals (steel, cast iron)** | ✔ (cast iron) | ✔ (steel) | ✔ (steel sheet) | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✔✔ |
| **Aluminum alloys** | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ (die cast) | ✗ |
| **Copper alloys** | ✔ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Engineering polymers (ABS, PA, POM)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ |
| **Thermosets (phenolic, epoxy)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ (compression) | ✗ |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiC)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ (grinding) | ✗ | ✔✔ |
| **Composites (CFRP, GFRP)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔ (thermo-set) | ✗ |

Legend: ✔✔ = Excellent, ✔ = Good, ✗ = Poor / Not applicable.

## 2.5 Assembly Compatibility (Joining Method vs Material)

| Joining Method | Steel | Aluminum | Polymer | Ceramic | Composite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding (arc, MIG, TIG)** | ✔✔ | ✔ (TIG) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Resistance welding** | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Brazing / Soldering** | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Adhesive bonding** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ |
| **Snap-fit** | ✔ (sheet) | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Threaded fasteners** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ (with inserts) | ✗ | ✔ (with inserts) |
| **Riveting** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ |
| **Ultrasonic welding** | ✗ | ✗ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Heat staking** | ✗ | ✗ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Press-fit** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A "suitable" material in DFA context is the one that *enables* a fast, reliable, single-operation joining method (e.g., a snap-fit-friendly polymer for a phone cover) **rather than** forcing slow, multi-step joining (e.g., a ceramic that needs adhesive cure + fixturing).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Index | Symbol | Application Domain | Units | Maximize / Minimize |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Stiffness | $M_1 = E / \rho$ | Lightweight stiff tie | $\text{GPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Maximize |
| 2 | Specific Strength | $M_2 = \sigma_y / \rho$ | Lightweight strong tie | $\text{kPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Maximize |
| 3 | Stiffness Index (Bending) | $M_3 = E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Lightweight beam in bending | — | Maximize |
| 4 | Stiffness Index (Torsion) | $M_4 = E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Lightweight shaft in torsion | — | Maximize |
| 5 | Cost-Per-Unit-Property (stiff) | $C_{pp} = C_{kg} \cdot \rho / E^{1/2}$ | Min-cost beam | $\text{₹·m}^{-3}\text{·GPa}^{1/2}$ | Minimize |
| 6 | Springback Index | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ | Sheet metal formability | Dimensionless | Minimize (for low springback) |
| 7 | Thermal Distortion Index | $\alpha / k$ | Heat-exchanger plates | $\text{m/W}$ | Minimize |
| 8 | Wear Resistance Index | $H / E$ | Tribological contacts | Dimensionless | Maximize |
| 9 | Buckling Resistance | $M_{cr} = E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Lightweight column | — | Maximize |
| 10 | Mass of a Lightweight Tie | $m = \dfrac{FL\rho}{E\delta^*}$ | Direct design calc | kg | — |
| 11 | Mass of a Lightweight Strong Tie | $m = \dfrac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$ | Direct design calc | kg | — |
| 12 | Cost Index (stiff beam) | $C_I = \dfrac{C_{kg} \cdot \rho}{E^{1/2}}$ | Economic selection | — | Minimize |
| 13 | Material Removal Fraction (DFM) | $f_{mr} = \dfrac{m_{chip}}{m_{blank}}$ | Process selection | Dimensionless | Minimize |
| 14 | Buy-to-Fly Ratio (forging) | $B/F = m_{billet}/m_{final}$ | Forging cost indicator | Dimensionless | Minimize |
| 15 | Recyclability Index | $R = 1 - E_{loss}/E_{in}$ | DFE / Sustainability | Dimensionless | Maximize |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Rule (CRITICAL):** In the table above, every absolute-value or "per" notation is written as `\rho` or with explicit words like "per kg" to **avoid breaking the markdown table** with stray `|` characters. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX within table cells.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace wing spar** → Maximize $E/\rho$ → CFRP composites or Al 7075.
- **Automotive body panel** → Minimize $C_{kg}\rho/\sigma_y$ → Mild steel (HSLA) or aluminum 5182.
- **Heat sink in LED** → Maximize $k/\rho \cdot C_p$ → Pure aluminum 1050 or copper C110.
- **Snap-fit mobile cover** → Maximize elongation & impact strength → Polycarbonate (PC) or ABS.
- **Gears in a gearbox** → Maximize $H$ and wear resistance + minimize $C_{kg}$ → Case-hardened steel 20MnCr5.
- **Medical implant** → Maximize biocompatibility + corrosion resistance → Titanium Ti-6Al-4V.

## 2.8 The DFMA "Suitability Decision Tree"

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Draw / describe the systematic decision flow for selecting a "suitable" material under DFMA constraints.

The decision tree visits each filter in sequence:
1. Start at **Function** → Define the property envelope.
2. Apply **Constraints** → Apply stiffness, strength, temperature, corrosion limits → Eliminate.
3. Apply **Material Indices** → Rank remaining candidates.
4. Apply **Process Compatibility** → Check if a primary DFMA process can shape the material.
5. Apply **Assembly Compatibility** → Check if the joining method works.
6. Apply **Cost & Lifecycle** → Apply $C_{kg} \cdot \rho$ and $R$ indices.
7. **Final Selection** → Document the choice with full traceability.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Specific Stiffness Index (Tie in Tension)

**Given:**
- A tensile tie of length $L$ must carry an axial force $F$ with a maximum permitted elastic elongation $\delta^*$.
- Cross-sectional area $A$ is a free variable.
- Mass $m$ must be minimized.

**Step 1 — Apply Hooke's law to express elongation:**

$$\delta = \frac{FL}{AE}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the constraint for the minimum required area:**

$$A_{min} = \frac{FL}{\delta^* E}$$

This is the smallest area that satisfies the deflection limit.

**Step 3 — Express the mass of the tie:**

$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{FL}{\delta^* E} \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{F L^2 \rho}{\delta^* E}$$

**Step 4 — Identify which material property combination governs the mass:**

Since $F$, $L$, $\delta^*$ are all fixed by the design, the only way to **minimize m** is to **maximize the material property group**:

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Stiffness} = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Evaluation:**

Suppose: $F = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ m}$, $\delta^* = 1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$.

Compare three candidate materials:
- **Steel (AISI 1020):** $E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- **Aluminum 6061-T6:** $E = 69$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³
- **CFRP (unidirectional):** $E = 135$ GPa, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³

**Step 5a — Steel:**

$$m_{steel} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 7850}{0.001 \cdot 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{3.925 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^8} = 1.9625 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5b — Aluminum 6061-T6:**

$$m_{al} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 2700}{0.001 \cdot 69 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.35 \times 10^8}{6.9 \times 10^7} = 1.9565 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5c — CFRP:**

$$m_{cfrp} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.0^2 \cdot 1600}{0.001 \cdot 135 \times 10^9} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^7}{1.35 \times 10^8} = 0.5926 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5d — Compute Specific Stiffness Indices:**

- Steel: $E/\rho = 200 \times 10^9 / 7850 = 25.5 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg
- Aluminum: $E/\rho = 69 \times 10^9 / 2700 = 25.6 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg
- CFRP: $E/\rho = 135 \times 10^9 / 1600 = 84.4 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg

**Step 5e — Confirm the trend:** CFRP has the highest $E/\rho$ and gives the lowest mass — exactly as the index predicts. Steel and aluminum have nearly identical $E/\rho$ and yield nearly identical masses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that *absolute* stiffness $E$ alone would have ranked Steel > Al > CFRP, but the index $E/\rho$ correctly *inverts* this ranking when weight matters. This is why indices are the right tool, not raw properties.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Index (Tie in Tension, Strength-Driven)

**Given:**
- Tensile tie of length $L$, must carry force $F$ with factor of safety $n$.
- Mass must be minimized.

**Step 1 — Yielding constraint:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \Rightarrow A_{min} = \frac{nF}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 2 — Mass expression:**

$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \frac{nFL\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the index:**

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical comparison (same tie, but yield-limited):**

Suppose $F = 200{,}000$ N, $n = 2$, $L = 1$ m.

- **Steel 1020:** $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- **Al 7075-T6:** $\sigma_y = 503$ MPa, $\rho = 2810$ kg/m³
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³
- **CFRP UD:** $\sigma_y = 1500$ MPa, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³

**Step 4a — Steel:**

$$m_{steel} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 7850}{350 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.14 \times 10^9}{3.5 \times 10^8} = 8.971 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4b — Al 7075-T6:**

$$m_{al} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 2810}{503 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.124 \times 10^9}{5.03 \times 10^8} = 2.234 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4c — Ti-6Al-4V:**

$$m_{ti} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 4430}{880 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.772 \times 10^9}{8.8 \times 10^8} = 2.014 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4d — CFRP:**

$$m_{cfrp} = \frac{2 \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1600}{1500 \times 10^6} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 0.427 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4e — Specific strength comparison:**

- Steel: $\sigma_y/\rho = 350 \times 10^6 / 7850 = 44{,}586$ N·m/kg
- Al 7075: $503 \times 10^6 / 2810 = 178{,}826$ N·m/kg
- Ti-6Al-4V: $880 \times 10^6 / 4430 = 198{,}645$ N·m/kg
- CFRP: $1500 \times 10^6 / 1600 = 937{,}500$ N·m/kg

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** CFRP wins again for strength-critical lightweight ties. Ti and Al are close seconds. Steel, despite its high absolute strength, is the heaviest choice.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Cost-Per-Unit-Property (Min-Cost Stiff Beam)

**Given:**
- A cantilever beam of length $L$ must deflect by at most $\delta^*$ under tip load $W$.
- Material cost ₹/kg = $C_{kg}$.
- Mass = $A \cdot L \cdot \rho$, cost = $C_{kg} \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \rho$.

**Step 1 — Deflection constraint (cantilever, tip load):**

$$\delta = \frac{WL^3}{3EI} \le \delta^* \Rightarrow I_{min} = \frac{WL^3}{3E\delta^*}$$

**Step 2 — For a rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ with $h$ as the free variable and $b$ fixed:**

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} \Rightarrow h = \left(\frac{12 I}{b}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{12 WL^3}{3Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-section area:**

$$A = b \cdot h = b \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Mass and cost:**

$$m = AL\rho = \rho b \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{total} = C_{kg} \cdot m = C_{kg} \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot \left(\frac{4WL^3}{Eb\delta^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the cost-minimizing material index:**

Pull out the material-dependent terms:

$$C_{total} \propto \frac{C_{kg} \cdot \rho}{E^{1/3}}$$

$$\boxed{M_{cost, beam} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{C_{kg} \cdot \rho} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

Equivalently, minimize the reciprocal: $C_{kg}\rho / E^{1/3}$.

**Step 6 — Numerical example (b = 50 mm, L = 1 m, W = 500 N, $\delta^*$ = 5 mm):**

| Material | E [GPa] | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | $C_{kg}$ [₹/kg] | $C_{kg}\rho/E^{1/3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 200 | 7850 | 65 | 65·7850 / 5.85 = 87,265 |
| Al 6061 | 69 | 2700 | 220 | 220·2700 / 4.10 = 144,878 |
| GFRP | 25 | 1800 | 350 | 350·1800 / 2.92 = 215,753 |
| CFRP | 135 | 1600 | 4500 | 4500·1600 / 5.13 = 1,403,508 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even though CFRP is *structurally* the most efficient beam, the **cost-per-unit-property** index reveals that mild steel is the most *economical* choice. This is why the steel I-beam dominates building construction. CFRP is chosen only when weight matters *more* than cost (aerospace, racing).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection by Application

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Pattern:** "Select a suitable material for the following components. Justify." This 7-mark question is a KTU Module 3 favorite. The matrix below is the rapid-fire reference.

| Component | Primary Function | Best Material Family | Justification (Material Index Used) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft wing skin | Tension + bending, light | Al 2024-T3 or CFRP | Maximize $E/\rho$ and $\sigma_y/\rho$ |
| Connecting rod (IC engine) | Cyclic tension + compression | Forged steel 4340 or Ti alloy | Maximize $\sigma_y/\rho$ + fatigue strength |
| Gear teeth (transmission) | High contact stress, wear | Carburized steel 20MnCr5 | Maximize surface hardness $H$ |
| Smartphone back cover | Cosmetic, light, impact | Al 7075 or Polycarbonate | Minimize $m$, maximize dent resistance |
| Heat sink (CPU) | High heat flux | Pure Al 1050 or Cu C110 | Maximize $k/(\rho \cdot C_{kg})$ |
| Snap-fit housing | Repeated assembly | Polypropylene (PP) or POM | Maximize flexural fatigue life |
| Artificial hip joint | Biocompatible, wear | Ti-6Al-4V + UHMWPE cup | Maximize biocompatibility, minimize wear |
| Cryogenic LNG tank | Low temp ductile | 9% Ni steel or Al 5083 | Maximize low-T toughness |
| Spring (coil) | High elastic strain energy | Spring steel (chrome-vanadium) | Maximize $\sigma_y^2/E$ (stored energy) |
| PCB substrate | Electrical insulation | FR-4 (glass-epoxy laminate) | Maximize $\rho_e$, minimize $k$ |
| Cutting tool insert | Hot hardness | WC-Co (cemented carbide) | Maximize $H$ at 800 °C |
| Marine propeller | Corrosion + strength | Ni-Al bronze or Muntz metal | Maximize corrosion resistance + $\sigma_y$ |
| Pressure vessel (high P) | Containment | SA 516 Gr 70 carbon steel | Maximize $\sigma_y$, weldability |
| Body armor plate | Impact absorption | UHMWPE (Kevlar/Dyneema) or Al₂O₃ ceramic | Maximize energy absorption per unit mass |
| Glass bottle | Cheap containment | Soda-lime glass | Minimize $C_{kg}$ + recyclability |

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — Spring Material Selection

**Problem:** Select a suitable material for a helical compression spring that must store maximum elastic strain energy per unit volume.

**Step 1 — State the energy density:**

For linear elastic loading up to $\sigma_y$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the material index:**

$$\boxed{M_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E} \quad \uparrow \text{ Maximize}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare candidates:**

| Material | $\sigma_y$ [MPa] | E [GPa] | $\sigma_y^2 / E$ [MPa] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring steel (ASTM A228) | 1800 | 200 | 16.2 |
| Stainless 302 | 1000 | 190 | 5.26 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 880 | 113 | 6.85 |
| Brass C26000 | 435 | 100 | 1.89 |
| Beryllium copper C17200 | 1100 | 128 | 9.45 |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Music wire (spring steel) has the highest index and is the standard spring material. Beryllium copper is the next best for corrosion-resistant springs.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — DFMA Material Selection Flow (Ashby Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Design Requirement] --> B[Step 1: TRANSLATION]
    B --> B1[Define Function e.g. carry load]
    B --> B2[Define Constraints e.g. mass less than 5 kg]
    B --> B3[Define Objective e.g. minimize cost]
    B --> B4[Define Free Variables e.g. material and shape]

    B1 --> C[Step 2: SCREENING]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Apply Property Limits]
    C1 --> C2[Eliminate Materials Outside Envelope]
    C2 --> D[Step 3: RANKING with Material Indices]

    D --> D1{Tie in Tension?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Maximize E by rho]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Beam in Bending?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Maximize E to the power 1 over 3 by rho]
    D3 -- No --> D5{Column Buckling?}
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Maximize E to the power 1 over 2 by rho]
    D5 -- No --> D7{Spring?}
    D7 -- Yes --> D8[Maximize sigma_y squared by E]

    D2 --> E[Step 4: SUPPORTING INFO]
    D4 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D8 --> E
    E --> E1[Process Compatibility Check]
    E1 --> E2[Assembly Compatibility Check]
    E2 --> E3[Cost and Life Cycle Check]
    E3 --> F[FINAL MATERIAL SELECTION]
    F --> G[Document and Specify Material Grade]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — The Four Suitability Filters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Material] --> B{Functional Filter}
    B -- FAIL --> Z[Eliminate]
    B -- PASS --> C{Manufacturing Process Filter}
    C -- FAIL --> Z
    C -- PASS --> D{Assembly Compatibility Filter}
    D -- FAIL --> Z
    D -- PASS --> E{Lifecycle and Cost Filter}
    E -- FAIL --> Z
    E -- PASS --> F[SUITABLE MATERIAL SELECTED]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Material Family Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Selection] --> B{Operating Temperature}
    B -- Less than 200 deg C --> C{Mechanical Load Dominant}
    B -- 200 to 600 deg C --> D[High Temp Alloys: Ni Inconel Ti]
    B -- Greater than 600 deg C --> E[Refractory: Ceramics Mo W]

    C --> F{Weight Critical}

    G --> K{Conductivity Needed}

    I --> N{Forming Process}
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Material Selection Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Property Based Selection] --> A1[Uses Ashby Charts]
    A1 --> A2[Strength: Maximize sigma_y by rho]
    A1 --> A3[Stiffness: Maximize E by rho]
    A1 --> A4[Cost: Minimize C kg times rho per property]

    B[Process Based Selection] --> B1[Starts from chosen process]
    B1 --> B2[Casting: Cast iron, Al, Mg]
    B1 --> B3[Injection Molding: Polymers]
    B1 --> B4[Sheet Metal: Steel, Al]

    C[Assembly Based Selection] --> C1[Starts from joining method]
    C1 --> C2[Snap fit: Flexible polymer]
    C1 --> C3[Welding: Weldable steel or Al]
    C1 --> C4[Adhesive: Almost any solid]

    D[Hybrid Selection] --> D1[DFMA philosophy]
    D1 --> D2[Considers all four filters together]
    D2 --> D3[Iterative optimization until convergence]
```
