# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Design for Assembly Index" and state the typical industry target value for an excellent assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index**, also called the DFA efficiency, is the ratio of the theoretical minimum assembly work to the actual assembly work, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} (\%) = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = 1.5 s), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. **Industry target: $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.** *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark target value.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six design recommendations for sand casting with a one-line justification for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 6 of the following, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Uniform wall thickness** — prevents shrinkage cavities at hot spots.
2. **Generous fillet radii** ($r_i \geq \frac{T_1 + T_2}{4}$) — reduces stress concentration.
3. **Draft angle** ($0.5°$–$3°$) — allows mould release without damage.
4. **Tapered ribs** ($T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$) — avoids hot spot at rib termination.
5. **Symmetric cores** — prevents core-shift under metal pressure.
6. **Generous parting line at the largest cross-section** — simplifies moulding.
7. **Avoid abrupt changes in section** — use gradual tapers instead.
8. **Provide machining allowance** (1.5–3 mm) — for finish-machined surfaces.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (A): (a)** Discuss the general design recommendations for **forging** with reference to parting line, draft angle, web and rib design, fillet radii, and machining allowance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel flat plate of thickness $T = 3$ mm is to be bent through an angle of $90°$ over an inside bend radius of $R = 4$ mm. The part has two straight flanges of 60 mm and 80 mm. Take the K-factor as 0.33. Calculate the **flat blank length** required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(a): Forging Design Recommendations (7 marks)

1. **Parting Line (1 mark):** The parting line should be located at the **largest cross-section** of the forging, be **symmetric** about the forging axis, and ideally be a **single plane**. A symmetric parting line ensures balanced die closure, uniform flash, and prevents die-shift. An asymmetric parting line produces uneven metal flow, uneven forging force, and die wear.

2. **Draft Angle (1 mark):** Draft is the taper provided on the side walls of the forging to allow easy ejection from the die. The standard forging draft is **3° to 7°** (significantly more than the 0.5°–3° used in casting) because forging dies are deep and the forging has to be ejected without galling. Draft is applied **perpendicular to the parting line**.

3. **Web and Rib Design (2 marks):**
   - **Web thickness:** $T_{\text{web}} \leq 3 \times T_{\text{adjacent wall}}$. Thicker webs cause **entrapped hot spots** (the web cools last, pulling material from adjacent ribs) and reduce die life. A web that is too thick also acts as a heat sink that prevents proper die-filling.
   - **Rib thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \leq 1.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$. Overly thick ribs are uneconomical (waste of material) and cause dies to crack from thermal cycling.
   - Ribs should be **tapered** at the end to avoid hot spots.

4. **Fillet Radii (2 marks):** Fillet radii are critical for both **strength** and **die-filling**.
   - **Inner corner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.15 \times T$, but **not less than 2.5 mm** (a minimum absolute value is mandatory regardless of the local thickness).
   - **Outer corner radius:** $r_o = r_i + 0.5T$ (to keep uniform stock).
   - Sharp corners cause **mould wear** and **die-failure by thermal fatigue** — forgings with sharp corners are almost always rejected in industry.

5. **Machining and Shrinkage Allowance (1 mark):**
   - **Shrinkage allowance:** 0.5% to 1.5% of the linear dimension, applied in the die (so the forged part cools to the nominal size).
   - **Machining allowance:** 1.5 mm to 6 mm (depending on size and complexity), to be removed during finish-machining to remove scale and achieve final tolerances.

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(b): Sheet-Metal Flat Blank Length (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify and list the inputs:**
- Sheet thickness: $T = 3$ mm
- Bend angle: $\theta = 90°$
- Inside bend radius: $R = 4$ mm
- K-factor: $K = 0.33$
- Flange lengths: $L_1 = 60$ mm, $L_2 = 80$ mm

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the neutral-axis radius:**

$$r_n = R + K \cdot T = 4 + 0.33 \cdot 3 = 4 + 0.99 = 4.99 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply the bend-allowance formula:**

$$L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \theta \cdot r_n$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_b &= \left(\frac{3.1416}{180}\right) \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 0.01745 \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 1.5708 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 7.838 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Add the straight flanges:**

$$L_{\text{blank}} = L_1 + L_b + L_2 = 60 + 7.838 + 80 = 147.838 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Final answer with interpretation:**

$$\boxed{L_{\text{blank}} \approx 147.84 \text{ mm} \approx 148 \text{ mm (rounded up)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The flat blank of mild-steel sheet must be cut to a length of approximately **148 mm** to produce the final bent part of nominal flange lengths 60 mm and 80 mm after bending.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians via the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ factor (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Using $R$ instead of $(R + K T)$ in the formula (loses 2 marks — the neutral-axis correction is the essence of the problem).
> 3. Omitting the straight flanges $L_1$ and $L_2$ from the final answer (loses 1 mark).
> 4. Failing to write the **units** in the final answer (loses 0.5 mark).

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (B): (a)** Explain **Boothroyd's DFA method** in detail. How is the DFA efficiency index calculated? Discuss the four Boothroyd questions used to identify candidate parts for elimination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly has 10 parts, each requiring 6 s for assembly (total actual = 60 s). After DFA analysis, the theoretical minimum number of parts is determined as 4, with a theoretical minimum assembly time of 1.5 s per part. The redesigned assembly uses 6 parts and a total actual assembly time of 18 s. Compute the **DFA Index before and after redesign** and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(a): Boothroyd's DFA Method (7 marks)

1. **Overview of Boothroyd's DFA (1 mark):** The Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly method, developed in 1983 at the University of Massachusetts, is the most widely used quantitative DFA technique. It has two complementary outputs:
   - A **two-question test** to identify parts that are candidates for elimination.
   - A **handling/insertion-time** database that estimates the assembly time for any retained part based on its geometric features (symmetry, size, thickness, gripping difficulty, etc.).

2. **The Four Boothroyd Questions (4 marks — 1 mark each question + 1 mark for the "no" → combine rule):** For every part in the assembly, the designer asks four questions in sequence. If all four answers are **"NO"**, the part is a candidate for **elimination or combination** with an adjacent part. If any answer is **"YES"**, the part is essential and must be retained as a separate component.

   - **Q1:** *Does the part need to move (rotate, translate, oscillate) relative to the other parts during operation of the product?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q2:** *Is a different material mandatory for the part (i.e., the part must be made of a different material than the next adjacent part for functional reasons)?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q3:** *Must the part be disassembled from the product for service, maintenance, or recycling?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q4:** *Does the part serve a separate assembly function (e.g., it is a fastener, a locating pin, a sealing element, or an electrical insulator that must be added separately)?* If YES, the part is essential.

3. **The DFA Efficiency Index (2 marks):** The DFA efficiency is computed as:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = **1.5 s** per part), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. A well-designed assembly has $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(b): DFA Index Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify the given data:**

| Parameter | Before Redesign | After Redesign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of parts, $N$ | 10 | 6 |
| Actual assembly time per part | 6 s | 3 s (since 18/6 = 3) |
| Total actual time, $\sum t_{a,i}$ | 60 s | 18 s |
| Theoretical min parts, $N_m$ | 4 | 4 |
| Theoretical min time per part, $t_m$ | 1.5 s | 1.5 s |
| Theoretical min total, $N_m t_m$ | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index before redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, before}} &= \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 60} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{66} \times 100 \\
&= 9.09\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index after redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, after}} &= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 18} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{24} \times 100 \\
&= 25.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comment on the result:**

The original assembly has a DFA Index of **9.09%**, which is very low and indicates significant opportunity for design improvement. After redesign (eliminating 4 parts and using snap-fits / simplified handling), the index rises to **25.0%** — a substantial improvement. However, the redesigned assembly is still below the industry target of 60%, indicating that further simplification is possible. The cycle-time reduction is from 60 s to 18 s, a **70% reduction in assembly time** and an equivalent reduction in assembly labour cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $N_m$ (minimum number of parts) with $N_a$ (actual number of parts)** in the formula (loses 2 marks).
> 2. Forgetting to convert the $t_m$ from 1.5 s to the **total** $N_m t_m$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Forgetting the $\times 100$ to express the result as a percentage (loses 0.5 mark).
> 4. **Failing to comment** on the result (loses 1 mark) — the KTU examiner expects at least two lines of qualitative interpretation in any numerical answer.
> 5. Listing the four Boothroyd questions but **forgetting to state the "all four NO → combine"** decision rule (loses 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density exam revision notes for KTU PEMET522 Module 3 — Design Recommendations:**

- **DFMA = Design for Manufacture + Design for Assembly**; the goal is to reduce life-cycle cost **at the design stage**, not at the shop floor.
- The **four pillars** of generic DFM are: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- **70–80% of life-cycle cost is committed at the design stage** — this statistic must appear in any KTU answer on DFMA importance.
- For **casting**: uniform wall thickness, fillet $r_i \geq \frac{T_1+T_2}{4}$, draft 0.5°–3°, rib thickness $\approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- For **forging**: single symmetric parting line, draft 3°–7°, web $\leq 3 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib $\leq 1.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, $r_i \geq 2.5$ mm min, shrinkage 0.5–1.5%, machining 1.5–6 mm.
- For **machining**: hole depth $\leq 6 D$, pocket depth $\leq 4 W$, use ISO tool radii (0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3 mm), use standard ISO Ra values.
- For **sheet metal**: bend radius $R \geq 1T$ (soft) or $\geq 2T$ (hard), hole-to-edge $\geq 2T$, hole-to-bend $\geq 2T + R$, bend allowance $L_b = (\pi/180) \cdot \theta (R + KT)$, K-factor = 0.33 (soft) or 0.5 (hard).
- For **plastic moulding**: wall variation $\leq 25\%$, draft 0.5°–2°, boss diameter $\approx 2.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib thickness $\approx 0.5 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- The **25% wall-thickness rule** for plastics is derivable from cooling-time scaling $t_c \propto T^2$ — write the derivation in the exam to earn full marks.
- **Boothroyd's DFA method** uses four binary questions; a part answering "NO" to all four is a candidate for elimination.
- **DFA Index** = $\frac{N_m t_m}{N_m t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$; target $\geq 60\%$.
- $t_m$ baseline (Boothroyd) = **1.5 s per part**.
- The **DFMA cost equation**: $C_T = C_M + C_A + C_S + C_F + C_D$.
- The **M-S-S-C** mnemonic: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- The **bend-allowance formula** uses the neutral-axis radius $r_n = R + KT$, **never** the inside radius $R$ alone.
- Always state the **units** in the final answer; always **comment** on the numerical result; always **define variables** before using them in a formula.
- A **symmetric part** is easier to manufacture (no orientation constraint), easier to assemble (no reversal needed), and easier to inspect (no wrong-direction error).
- **Snap-fits** and **press-fits** eliminate loose fasteners; the design gain is typically a 30–50% assembly-time reduction.
- **Mono-material** designs are preferred for recycling (DFE); multi-material inseparable bonds are the worst case.
- The **most over-asked KTU topic** in this module: numerical on the **DFA Index** and the **bend allowance** — practise both thoroughly.
- **Killer mnemonic for the bend allowance:** "Bend along the **N**eutral axis — so the formula is $R + K T$, **not** $R$ alone."

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** The most common reason students lose marks on this module is **formula-by-rote** without understanding the physical meaning. Always write a **one-line interpretation** of any numerical result (e.g., "the redesigned assembly is 70% faster, indicating a substantial DFM improvement") — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Milling: Process Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Milling: Process Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Milling** is a fundamental **material removal process** in which a **rotating multi-point (multi-tooth) cutting tool** progressively removes material from a stationary or linearly-fed workpiece to produce a desired geometry, surface, or feature. Unlike turning (where the workpiece rotates), in milling the **cutter rotates as the primary motion** while the **workpiece provides the secondary (feed) motion**.

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, "Design for Milling" refers to a set of engineering guidelines and geometric rules that a product designer must follow so that a component's features (slots, pockets, holes, profiles, contours) can be **efficiently, economically, and accurately produced** on standard milling machines using standard milling cutters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Milling is an **interrupted cutting process** where each tooth of the cutter engages and disengages the workpiece periodically. This intermittent nature gives milling its unique thermal, mechanical, and surface-finish characteristics — distinguishing it fundamentally from continuous cutting processes like turning.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **circular saw cutting through a wooden plank**:
- The **saw blade rotates** (primary motion) at high speed.
- The **plank is pushed into the saw** (secondary feed motion).
- Each **tooth of the saw** bites into the wood, cuts a small chip, and exits — then the next tooth comes in.
- The wood is shaped/separated as the saw advances.

A **milling machine works on exactly the same principle**, except:
- The "saw" (cutter) can have many different shapes — flat, ball-ended, tapered, etc.
- The "plank" (workpiece) is held on a movable table that can travel in **X, Y, and Z directions**.
- The cuts are far more precise and can create **3D sculpted features**, not just straight cuts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Distinction for Students:**
> - **Turning** = Workpiece rotates, tool is stationary/linear → Lathe
> - **Milling** = Tool rotates, workpiece feeds linearly → Milling Machine
> - **Drilling** = Tool rotates AND feeds axially → Drill Press / Milling Machine

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard manufacturing metrics** used universally in milling (and recognized by KTU examiners):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | m/min | Surface speed of the cutter periphery |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | rpm | Rotational speed of the cutter |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | mm/tooth | Chip thickness per tooth engagement |
| Feed Rate (Table) | $v_f$ | mm/min | Linear speed of the workpiece/table |
| Depth of Cut | $a_p$ | mm | Radial engagement of the cutter |
| Width of Cut (Engagement) | $a_e$ | mm | Axial width the cutter is engaged |
| Number of Teeth | $Z$ | — | Effective cutting edges on cutter |
| Material Removal Rate | $Q$ / MRR | cm³/min | Volume of metal removed per minute |
| Average Chip Thickness | $h_m$ | mm | Mean undeformed chip thickness |
| Surface Roughness | $R_a$ | μm | Arithmetic mean roughness of cut surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** The five parameters you will see in *every* milling numerical problem are: **$V_c$, $N$, $f_z$, $v_f$, and $a_p$**. Master their inter-relationships.

### 1.4 Classification of Milling Operations

Milling operations are broadly classified by the **relative orientation of the cutter axis to the workpiece feed direction**:

**A. Based on Direction of Feed vs. Cutter Rotation:**

- **Up Milling (Conventional Milling):** The cutter rotates **opposite** to the feed direction. The chip thickness starts at **zero** and grows to a maximum at the end of the cut. The tooth tends to "rub" before biting.
- **Down Milling (Climb Milling):** The cutter rotates **in the same direction** as the feed. The chip thickness starts at a **maximum** and decreases to zero. The tooth slices cleanly into the material from the start.

**B. Based on Cutter Orientation:**

- **Peripheral Milling (Slab Milling):** The cutter axis is parallel to the workpiece surface. Cutting is done by the **peripheral teeth**.
- **Face Milling:** The cutter axis is perpendicular to the workpiece surface. Cutting is done primarily by the **end face teeth**, with the peripheral teeth providing a finishing action.
- **End Milling:** Combines both peripheral and face cutting — used for slots, pockets, and profiling.
- **Profile Milling:** Using a **shaped cutter** to produce a matching contour (e.g., gear teeth, T-slots, dovetails).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Primary Cutting Parameters — Conceptual Breakdown

Milling is governed by four independent parameters that the designer/machinist sets before a cut begins. Understanding the **"why"** behind each is essential for KTU's application-level questions.

#### (i) Cutting Speed ($V_c$) — The "How Fast the Teeth Scrape" Parameter

**Definition:** $V_c$ is the **linear speed at which the outermost cutting edge of a tooth travels through the air/workpiece material**, measured at the cutter's effective diameter.

**Why it matters:**
- $V_c$ is **NOT** a free variable — it is selected from **machining data handbooks** based on the **workpiece material** and **cutter material** (HSS, carbide, coated carbide, ceramic, CBN, PCD).
- Higher $V_c$ → higher productivity BUT shorter tool life and more heat.
- Lower $V_c$ → better surface finish, longer tool life, but slower.

**Tool life equation (Taylor's Tool Life Equation for Milling):**
The empirical relationship used by KTU examiners for tool-life based problems:

$$V_c \cdot T^n = C$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (minutes)
- $n$ = Taylor's exponent (typically $0.1$ to $0.4$ for HSS, $0.2$ to $0.5$ for carbide)
- $C$ = constant depending on tool-workpiece combination

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Automotive production lines use Taylor's equation to balance **tool-change downtime vs. cutting speed**. A high $V_c$ reduces cycle time per part but increases the frequency (and cost) of tool changes.

#### (ii) Spindle Speed ($N$) — The "How Fast the Spindle Turns" Parameter

The spindle speed is **derived from $V_c$**, not independently chosen. This is the most common calculation in KTU Part A questions.

**The Logic:**
- $V_c$ is the linear speed at the cutter's outer edge.
- The cutter's outer edge traces a circle of circumference $\pi \cdot D$ (where $D$ is the cutter diameter).
- In one revolution, the edge travels $\pi \cdot D$ mm.
- In $N$ revolutions per minute, the edge travels $N \cdot \pi \cdot D$ mm/min.
- Setting this equal to $V_c$:

$$\boxed{V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V_c}{\pi \cdot D}}$$

Where $V_c$ is in m/min, $D$ in mm, and $N$ in rpm. **Unit conversion:** multiply $V_c$ by **1000** when $D$ is in mm.

#### (iii) Feed per Tooth ($f_z$) — The "How Much Each Tooth Bites" Parameter

**Definition:** $f_z$ is the **distance the table/workpiece advances between the engagement of two consecutive teeth**. It is the parameter that directly determines the **chip thickness** and therefore the cutting forces, surface finish, and power consumption.

**Why it matters:**
- Higher $f_z$ → bigger chips, more material removed per tooth pass, but rougher surface and higher cutting force.
- $f_z$ is chosen based on the **strength of the cutter tooth**, the **rigidity of the setup**, and the **desired surface finish**.

**Surface Finish Relationship (very high-yield for KTU):**

For a **end mill** of diameter $D$ with a **corner radius $r_\epsilon$**, the theoretical surface roughness is:

$$R_a = \dfrac{f_z^2}{8 \cdot r_\epsilon}$$

For a **ball-nose end mill** (semicircular tip of radius $R$):

$$R_a \approx \dfrac{f_z^2}{8 \cdot R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer takeaway:** If a part specification requires $R_a = 1.6\ \mu m$, the designer must either (a) use a smaller $f_z$, (b) use a larger corner-radius cutter, or (c) plan for a separate **finishing pass** with reduced feed.

#### (iv) Feed Rate ($v_f$) — The "How Fast the Table Moves" Parameter

The feed rate is the **linear speed of the table** (or the equivalent linear speed of the workpiece). It is **derived** from $N$, $f_z$, and $Z$ (number of effective teeth):

**The Logic:**
- In one spindle revolution, the table moves $f = f_z \cdot Z$ mm (because $Z$ teeth each engage once per revolution, and each engagement advances the table by $f_z$).
- In $N$ revolutions per minute, the table moves $N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ mm/min.

$$\boxed{v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N}$$

#### (v) Depth of Cut ($a_p$) and Width of Cut ($a_e$)

- $a_p$ = axial depth (how deep the cutter plunges into the workpiece).
- $a_e$ = radial width of engagement (how much of the cutter's diameter is in contact with the workpiece).

**In a standard slotting operation:** $a_e = D$ (full cutter diameter is engaged).
**In a peripheral cut (slab milling):** $a_e$ is much smaller than $D$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 1000$ | Cutting speed (when D in mm, Vc in m/min) | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle speed | rpm |
| 3 | $v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | Table feed rate | mm/min |
| 4 | $f = f_z \cdot Z$ | Feed per revolution | mm/rev |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}{1000}$ | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | cm³/min |
| 6 | $P_c = \dfrac{Q \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot \eta \cdot 10^6}$ | Cutting Power (if $k_c$ in N/mm²) | kW |
| 7 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | Cutting Power from cutting force | kW |
| 8 | $F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m$ | Tangential cutting force | N |
| 9 | $h_m = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{D}}$ | Average chip thickness | mm |
| 10 | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_{app} + L_{ov}}{v_f}$ | Machining time per pass | min |
| 11 | $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ | Taylor's tool life equation | — |
| 12 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f_z^2}{8 \cdot r_\epsilon}$ | Theoretical surface finish (end mill) | μm |

Where:
- $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) — depends on workpiece material and chip thickness
- $\eta$ = machine efficiency (typically 0.7 to 0.9)
- $\kappa$ = tool entering angle (commonly $90°$ for end mills)
- $L$ = length of workpiece, $L_{app}$ = tool approach distance, $L_{ov}$ = tool over-travel
- $F_c$ = tangential cutting force component
- $h_m$ = average (mean) undeformed chip thickness

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU Board Exam:**
> Formulas 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10 are asked almost every semester. Learn them with the **unit relationships** — examiners deduct marks when units are missing or wrong.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Milling Process Analysis

In modern manufacturing:
- **CNC Machining Centers** use these exact formulas in their post-processors. A CAM software (e.g., Mastercam, Fusion 360) computes $V_c$, $N$, $v_f$ for every toolpath segment.
- **Cost Estimation:** The MRR formula (5) directly determines cycle time, which is the dominant cost driver in milling.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Power formula (6) helps factories monitor and reduce specific energy consumption (kWh per kg of chip).
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Force calculations (8) feed into digital twins that predict tool wear and prevent breakage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed from Spindle Speed and Cutter Diameter

**Statement to Prove:** The cutting speed $V_c$ at the periphery of a rotating cutter of diameter $D$ spinning at $N$ rpm is given by $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 1000$ (in m/min when $D$ is in mm).

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry:**
A milling cutter is a cylinder. As it rotates, the outermost surface (periphery) traces a circle. The diameter of this circle is $D$.

**Step 2 — Circumference of the Path:**
The circumference of any circle is $2\pi r$ where $r$ is the radius, or equivalently $\pi D$.

$$\text{Circumference} = \pi D$$

**Step 3 — Distance Travelled in One Minute:**
If the cutter makes $N$ revolutions in one minute, the total distance travelled by the outer edge in one minute is:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = N \times \pi D$$

**Step 4 — Unit Conversion:**
By definition, $V_c$ is measured in **meters per minute (m/min)**. But $\pi D$ gives the circumference in the same unit as $D$ (which is typically **mm**). To convert mm/min to m/min, divide by 1000.

$$V_c \text{ [m/min]} = \dfrac{N \times \pi D \text{ [mm/min]}}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Milling

**Statement to Prove:** For a milling operation, the volume of material removed per unit time is $Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f$ (with appropriate unit conversion).

**Step 1 — Visualize the Cutting Zone:**
Imagine a cutter of diameter $D$ engaged in a workpiece to a depth $a_p$ and width $a_e$. The cutter is moving forward at $v_f$ mm/min. In one minute, the cutter sweeps out a "swept volume" of material.

**Step 2 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area of the Cut:**
The cut cross-section is essentially a rectangle of height $a_p$ and width $a_e$:

$$A_{\text{cut}} = a_p \times a_e \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Linear Travel Per Minute:**
In one minute, the cutter's tool path (assuming it traverses once) has length $v_f$ mm. The volume removed in one minute is:

$$Q = A_{\text{cut}} \times v_f = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Standard Engineering Unit:**
The standard unit for MRR in production engineering is **cm³/min** (because the resulting numbers are more convenient, typically 10–1000 cm³/min).

$$Q \text{ [cm}^3\text{/min]} = \dfrac{a_p \text{[mm]} \cdot a_e \text{[mm]} \cdot v_f \text{[mm/min]}}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}{1000} \quad \text{cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** If $a_p = 5$ mm, $a_e = 10$ mm, $v_f = 200$ mm/min, then $Q = (5 \times 10 \times 200)/1000 = 10$ cm³/min. This is a realistic figure for an end-milling pass on steel.

### 3.3 Derivation of Machining Time $T_m$ for a Milling Pass

**Statement to Prove:** The time taken for a single milling pass over a workpiece of length $L$ is:

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f}$$

**Step 1 — Tool Approach Distance $L_{\text{app}}$:**
The cutter must engage the workpiece after a small lead-in distance to allow for full tooth immersion and stable cutting. This is the **approach length** $L_{\text{app}}$.

**Step 2 — Tool Over-Travel $L_{\text{ov}}$:**
The cutter must continue past the workpiece end to complete the cut on the far edge. This is the **over-travel** $L_{\text{ov}}$.

**Step 3 — Total Path Length:**
$$\text{Total length} = L_{\text{app}} + L + L_{\text{ov}}$$

**Step 4 — Time = Distance ÷ Speed:**
Since the table moves at $v_f$ mm/min uniformly:

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f}$$

$$\boxed{T_m = \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f} \quad \text{minutes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget $L_{\text{app}}$ and $L_{\text{ov}}$, calculating $T_m = L / v_f$ only. **Always include them** in the final answer.

### 3.4 Derivation of Cutting Power Requirement

**Statement to Prove:** The cutting power required to drive a milling operation is:

$$P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{1000 \cdot 60} \quad \text{kW}$$

(or equivalently, $P_c = Q \cdot k_c / (60 \cdot 10^6)$ kW when $Q$ is in cm³/min and $k_c$ in N/mm²).

**Step 1 — Mechanical Power Definition:**
Power = Force × Velocity. The tangential cutting force $F_c$ acts at the cutter periphery, which moves at speed $V_c$.

$$P = F_c \cdot V_c \quad [\text{N} \cdot \text{m/min}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert Velocity to m/s:**
$V_c$ is in m/min. To get power in Watts (= N·m/s), we need velocity in m/s.

$$V_c \text{ [m/s]} = \dfrac{V_c \text{ [m/min]}}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Power in Watts:**
$$P = F_c \cdot \dfrac{V_c}{60} \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kW:**
$$P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000} \quad \text{kW}$$

**Alternative Form (using MRR):**
Since $F_c = k_c \cdot A_{\text{chip}}$ and the chip cross-section area relates to MRR, for a milling pass:

$$\boxed{P_c = \dfrac{Q \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6} \quad \text{kW}}$$

Where $Q$ is in cm³/min and $k_c$ is in N/mm².

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern Style)

> **Problem (14-mark style):**
> A mild-steel workpiece (length 200 mm, width 80 mm) is to be machined on a horizontal milling machine using a 6-tooth HSS end mill of diameter 20 mm. The cutting parameters recommended are: $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 3$ mm (full slot), $a_e = 20$ mm, $k_c = 2000$ N/mm², machine efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Approach and over-travel distances are 5 mm each. Calculate:
> (a) Spindle speed and table feed rate
> (b) Material removal rate
> (c) Cutting power required
> (d) Machining time

**Given:**
- $L = 200$ mm, $W = 80$ mm
- $D = 20$ mm, $Z = 6$
- $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 3$ mm, $a_e = 20$ mm
- $k_c = 2000$ N/mm², $\eta = 0.8$
- $L_{\text{app}} = L_{\text{ov}} = 5$ mm

#### (a) Spindle Speed and Table Feed Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \dfrac{25 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 20} \\
&= \dfrac{25000}{62.832} \\
&= 397.9 \text{ rpm} \quad \approx \mathbf{398 \text{ rpm}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_f &= f_z \cdot Z \cdot N \\
&= 0.10 \cdot 6 \cdot 397.9 \\
&= \mathbf{238.7 \text{ mm/min}}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (b) Material Removal Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \cdot 20 \cdot 238.7}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{14322}{1000} \\
&= \mathbf{14.32 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (c) Cutting Power Required

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{Q \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6} \\
&= \dfrac{14.32 \cdot 2000}{60 \cdot 10^6} \\
&= \dfrac{28640}{60 \cdot 10^6} \\
&= 4.773 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW} \quad \text{(this is the net cutting power)}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this seems too low. The MRR formula above gives the *gross* volume removed; for power we should use the chip cross-section properly. Let me re-derive using the force approach:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m \\
h_m &\approx f_z = 0.10 \text{ mm (simplified for end mill, $\kappa = 90°$)} \\
F_c &= 2000 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.10 = 600 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \cdot 25}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.8} \\
&= \dfrac{15000}{48000} \\
&= 0.3125 \text{ kW} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.31 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note for Students:** The MRR-based power formula uses $Q$ in mm³/s and includes the $10^6$ conversion for $k_c$ — which yields the *gross* mechanical power. The force-based formula directly gives the spindle power. In KTU, the **force-based** form is preferred for milling because chip thickness varies cyclically.

#### (d) Machining Time

$$\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f} \\
&= \dfrac{200 + 5 + 5}{238.7} \\
&= \dfrac{210}{238.7} \\
&= \mathbf{0.88 \text{ minutes}} \quad \text{(or about 52.8 seconds)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Milling Process System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Designer Inputs<br/>Material and Geometry] --> B[Process Parameter Selection<br/>Vc fz ap ae]
    B --> C[Spindle Drive System<br/>N rpm]
    C --> D[Milling Cutter<br/>D mm, Z teeth]
    D --> E{Cutter Workpiece<br/>Engagement}
    A --> F[Workpiece Holding<br/>Vice Fixture]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Chip Formation Zone<br/>h_m average chip thickness]
    G --> H[Material Removal Rate Q<br/>cm3 per min]
    G --> I[Cutting Force Fc<br/>Newtons]
    H --> J[Spindle Power Pc<br/>kW]
    I --> J
    I --> K[Surface Finish Ra<br/>micrometres]
    D --> K
    B --> L[Tool Life T<br/>minutes]
    C --> L
    L --> M[Cost per Part<br/>Rs per piece]
    H --> N[Machining Time Tm<br/>minutes]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Design Validation<br/>Pass or Modify Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Block Diagram:** The diagram shows that the **Designer's Choice of Geometry** (block A) flows into **Process Parameter Selection** (block B), which then propagates through the entire chain — affecting Power (J), Tool Life (L), Cycle Time (N), and ultimately **Cost per Part** (M). This is why a DFM-aware designer optimizes geometry upstream to reduce costs downstream.

### 4.2 Classification of Milling Operations (Block Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Milling Process] --> B[Based on Cutter Axis]
    A --> C[Based on Feed Direction]
    A --> D[Based on Tool Shape]
    
    B --> B1[Peripheral Milling<br/>Cutter axis parallel to surface]
    B --> B2[Face Milling<br/>Cutter axis perpendicular]
    
    C --> C1[Up Milling Conventional<br/>Chip thickness zero to max]
    C --> C2[Down Milling Climb<br/>Chip thickness max to zero]
    
    D --> D1[Slab Mill<br/>Cylindrical cutter]
    D --> D2[End Mill<br/>Bottom and side cutting]
    D --> D3[Ball Nose Mill<br/>3D contouring]
    D --> D4[Form Cutter<br/>T slots dovetails]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — From Design Drawing to Finished Milled Part

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Read Engineering Drawing<br/>Identify milling features] --> S2[Step 2 Select Work Holding<br/>Vice or fixture or clamping]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Mount Cutter<br/>Collet chuck or arbor]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Set Spindle Speed N<br/>From Vc and D]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Set Feed Rate vf<br/>From fz Z and N]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Set Depth of Cut ap<br/>Roughing or finishing]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Execute Roughing Pass<br/>High ap moderate fz]
    S7 --> S8{Check Dimensions<br/>and Surface Finish}
    S8 -->|Not OK| S9[Adjust Parameters<br/>Reduce fz or ap]
    S9 --> S7
    S8 -->|OK| S10[Execute Finishing Pass<br/>Low ap low fz]
    S10 --> S11[Final Inspection<br/>CMM or gauge]
    S11 --> S12[Part Approved]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to *"explain the milling process"* (a 7-mark question), use this block diagram to structure your answer. Walk the examiner through **Step 1 → Step 12**. This guarantees a complete answer.

### 4.4 Chip Thickness Variation Across Cutter Engagement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Tooth Engagement<br/>Position angle 0 deg] --> B[Chip Thickness h starts small]
    B --> C[Mid Engagement<br/>Position angle 45 deg]
    C --> D[Chip Thickness h maximum<br/>h max equals fz]
    D --> E[End of Engagement<br/>Position angle 90 deg]
    E --> F[Chip Thickness h returns to small]
    F --> G[Tooth Exits Workpiece<br/>Free travel]
    G --> H[Next Tooth Engages<br/>Cycle repeats]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Because $h$ varies cyclically, the **cutting force on a milling cutter is NOT constant** — it pulses with each tooth engagement. This is why milling machines need robust spindles, and why **up-milling vs down-milling** affects tool life differently (down-milling gives a more uniform force profile when the machine has backlash-free drives).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q1. (3 Marks)**
> Define milling and distinguish between **up milling** and **down milling** with neat sketches.

**Model Answer:**

**Milling** is a material removal process in which a **rotating multi-tooth cutter** removes material from a workpiece that is fed linearly into the cutter. The cutter provides the **primary (rotary) motion**, and the workpiece provides the **secondary (feed) motion**.

**Distinction Table:**

| Feature | Up Milling (Conventional) | Down Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter rotation vs. feed | Opposite direction | Same direction |
| Chip thickness at start | Zero (rubbing) | Maximum (clean bite) |
| Chip thickness at end | Maximum | Zero |
| Force on workpiece | Tends to lift workpiece | Tends to pull workpiece into cutter |
| Surface finish | Slightly poorer at entry | Better at entry |
| Machine backlash tolerance | Forgiving (works with backlash) | Requires backlash-free drives |
| Tool wear pattern | Uniform | More uniform at start |
| Holding force required | Higher (workpiece wants to lift) | Lower |

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. (3 Marks)**
> List **any six design guidelines** for components intended to be produced by milling.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Avoid deep narrow pockets** — Use a **pocket floor** that is reachable by a standard end mill of diameter $\geq 3$ mm; otherwise specify EDM or specify a stepped pocket.
2. **Provide adequate corner radii** — Internal corners should have radii $\geq$ cutter radius (typically $1$ mm minimum) to avoid internal sharp corners that need small-diameter cutters.
3. **Standardize slot widths** — Slot widths should match **standard end mill diameters** (e.g., 6, 8, 10, 12 mm) — avoid non-standard widths.
4. **Make features accessible** — Avoid features that require cutters to enter at sharp angles; provide **ramped or pre-drilled entry** for tool engagement.
5. **Avoid enclosed pockets without tool access holes** — Either open the pocket to an edge or include a **tool entry hole** in the design.
6. **Keep floor and wall thickness uniform** — Minimize vibration and chatter; use **ribs and gussets** for stiffness rather than thick walls.
7. **Specify realistic tolerances** — Standard milling achieves $\pm 0.1$ mm; tighter tolerances should be reserved for **finishing passes** or specific features.
8. **Choose milling-friendly materials** — Aluminium, brass, free-machining steel are preferred; avoid hardened tool-steel for milling unless necessary.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted) - 14 Marks]**
>
> **Q.A.** (a) **Explain the milling process with a neat block diagram. List the four primary cutting parameters and state the formula connecting them.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **A CNC vertical machining center is to mill a pocket of length 120 mm, width 60 mm, and depth 4 mm in an aluminium alloy workpiece ($k_c = 750$ N/mm²). A 4-tooth carbide end mill of diameter 12 mm is used. The recommended cutting parameters are: $V_c = 120$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 4$ mm, $a_e = 12$ mm. Approach distance = 3 mm, Over-travel = 3 mm, machine efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Determine: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Table feed rate, (iii) Material removal rate, (iv) Cutting power, (v) Machining time.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Milling Process Explanation (7 Marks)

**Milling Process Description:**

Milling is a **material removal process** in which a **multi-tooth rotary cutter** progressively removes chips from a workpiece. The **cutter rotates** at a high speed (primary motion), while the **workpiece is held on a table** that moves linearly in one or more axes (secondary motion). Each tooth of the cutter engages the workpiece, removes a chip, and then disengages — making milling an **interrupted cutting process**.

**Key Components of a Milling System (refer to block diagram in Section 4.3):**
1. **Spindle** — rotates the cutter at programmed RPM
2. **Cutter** — multi-tooth tool, geometry depends on operation
3. **Work-holding device** — vice, fixture, or clamp
4. **Table** — provides X-Y-Z linear feed
5. **Coolant system** — reduces heat and improves surface finish
6. **Controller (in CNC)** — coordinates spindle and axes

**Four Primary Cutting Parameters and Their Inter-Relationship:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | m/min | Peripheral speed of cutter |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | rpm | Rotational speed of cutter |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | mm/tooth | Chip thickness per tooth |
| Feed Rate | $v_f$ | mm/min | Table travel speed |

**Connecting Formula:**

$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{and} \quad v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$$

These formulas show that **$N$ links $V_c$ to cutter diameter $D$**, and **$v_f$ links $N$ to $f_z$ and $Z$**. Knowing any three of the four allows computation of the fourth.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 120$ mm, $W = 60$ mm, depth = 4 mm
- Material: Aluminium alloy, $k_c = 750$ N/mm²
- Cutter: $D = 12$ mm, $Z = 4$ teeth, carbide
- $V_c = 120$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 4$ mm, $a_e = 12$ mm
- $L_{\text{app}} = 3$ mm, $L_{\text{ov}} = 3$ mm, $\eta = 0.8$

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 12} \\
&= \dfrac{120000}{37.699} \\
&= \mathbf{3183 \text{ rpm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Table Feed Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_f &= f_z \cdot Z \cdot N \\
&= 0.08 \cdot 4 \cdot 3183 \\
&= \mathbf{1018.6 \text{ mm/min}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{4 \cdot 12 \cdot 1018.6}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{48893}{1000} \\
&= \mathbf{48.89 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Cutting Power (using force approach):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \\
&= 750 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.08 \\
&= 240 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{240 \cdot 120}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.8} \\
&= \dfrac{28800}{48000} \\
&= \mathbf{0.60 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(v) Machining Time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f} \\
&= \dfrac{120 + 3 + 3}{1018.6} \\
&= \dfrac{126}{1018.6} \\
&= \mathbf{0.1237 \text{ minutes}} \quad \text{(or about 7.42 seconds)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error** — Most common. $D$ in mm and $V_c$ in m/min → multiply $V_c$ by 1000 before dividing by $\pi D$. Students often write $N = V_c / \pi D$ and get answers off by 1000×.
> 2. **Forgetting Approach/Over-Travel** — Costs 1 mark on the time calculation.
> 3. **Efficiency Misplacement** — $\eta$ is in the **denominator** of the power formula. Some students put it in the numerator.
> 4. **Chip thickness simplification** — For KTU board, the assumption $h_m \approx f_z$ is acceptable for end mills with $\kappa = 90°$ and full radial engagement. Do not write the full trigonometric expression unless asked.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted) - 14 Marks]**
>
> **Q.B.** (a) **Describe the various types of milling operations with neat sketches. Compare face milling and peripheral milling.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **For a horizontal milling operation on a mild steel workpiece using a 50 mm diameter HSS slab mill with 8 teeth, the following data is given: $V_c = 20$ m/min, $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 4$ mm, $a_e = 30$ mm, $k_c = 2200$ N/mm², machine efficiency $\eta = 0.75$. Workpiece length = 300 mm, approach = 10 mm, over-travel = 10 mm. Determine: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Cutting power, (v) Machining time.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Types of Milling Operations (7 Marks)

**1. Peripheral (Slab) Milling:**
- Cutter axis is **parallel** to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting is done by the **outer periphery** of a cylindrical cutter.
- Used for producing **flat surfaces** and **stepped features**.

**2. Face Milling:**
- Cutter axis is **perpendicular** to the workpiece surface.
- Cutting is done primarily by the **bottom face** of the cutter teeth.
- Used for producing large **flat surfaces** with high surface finish.

**3. End Milling:**
- Combines both peripheral and face cutting.
- Used for **slots, pockets, profiles, and contours**.
- Available as square-end, ball-end, bull-nose, etc.

**4. Profile / Form Milling:**
- Cutter has a **specially shaped profile** (T-slot cutter, dovetail cutter, gear cutter).
- Used for producing **complex profiles** in one pass.

**5. Straddle Milling:**
- Two cutters mounted on the same arbor to machine **two parallel vertical surfaces** simultaneously.

**6. Gang Milling:**
- **Multiple cutters** of different shapes mounted on one arbor to perform several operations in a single pass.

**Comparison: Face vs. Peripheral Milling:**

| Feature | Face Milling | Peripheral Milling |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter axis | Vertical (⊥ to surface) | Horizontal (∥ to surface) |
| Cutting action | Mainly by face teeth | Mainly by peripheral teeth |
| Typical cutter | Insert-type face mill | Slab mill / side mill |
| Surface finish | Better (insert geometry optimized) | Slightly lower |
| Productivity | Higher for large flat areas | Lower for large areas |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower |
| Chip thickness | Variable, depends on insert geometry | Varies with cutter rotation |
| Application | Machining the top of a large casting | Cutting a slot or step |

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm, $Z = 8$, HSS
- $V_c = 20$ m/min, $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 4$ mm, $a_e = 30$ mm
- $k_c = 2200$ N/mm², $\eta = 0.75$
- $L = 300$ mm, $L_{\text{app}} = 10$ mm, $L_{\text{ov}} = 10$ mm

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \dfrac{20 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 50} \\
&= \dfrac{20000}{157.08} \\
&= \mathbf{127.3 \text{ rpm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_f &= f_z \cdot Z \cdot N \\
&= 0.20 \cdot 8 \cdot 127.3 \\
&= \mathbf{203.7 \text{ mm/min}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{4 \cdot 30 \cdot 203.7}{1000} \\
&= \mathbf{24.44 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Cutting Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \\
&= 2200 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.20 \\
&= 1760 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{1760 \cdot 20}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.75} \\
&= \dfrac{35200}{45000} \\
&= \mathbf{0.782 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(v) Machining Time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \dfrac{L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}}{v_f} \\
&= \dfrac{300 + 10 + 10}{203.7} \\
&= \dfrac{320}{203.7} \\
&= \mathbf{1.571 \text{ minutes}} \quad \text{(about 94.3 seconds)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Zero marks if units are missing** — Always write the unit (mm/min, cm³/min, kW, min) immediately after the numerical value. The KTU answer key deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Significant figures** — Use 3 to 4 significant figures. Do not write $N = 127.32387$ rpm. Round to one decimal place.
> 3. **Distinguishing roughing vs. finishing** — When a problem says "finishing pass", you must use **lower $f_z$ and $a_p$** (typically $f_z \leq 0.05$ mm/tooth, $a_p \leq 0.5$ mm). Students using the same parameters for both passes lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Choice of formula for power** — KTU accepts both the MRR-based and the force-based power formulas. Use the one whose given data better matches (force-based if $k_c$, $a_p$, $f_z$ are given; MRR-based if $Q$ and $k_c$ alone are given).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Design for Milling: Process Description**

**✅ Conceptual Fundamentals:**
- ☐ Milling = rotating multi-tooth cutter + linearly fed workpiece
- ☐ Primary motion = cutter rotation; Secondary motion = table feed
-  Milling is an **interrupted cutting process** (each tooth engages periodically)
- ☐ Up Milling (Conventional) vs. Down Milling (Climb) — distinguish by chip thickness profile
- ☐ Peripheral vs. Face vs. End vs. Profile milling — distinguish by cutter axis and tool shape

**✅ The Four Primary Parameters (must memorize):**
- ☐ $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min) → **selected from handbook** based on material + tool
- ☐ $N$ = spindle speed (rpm) → **derived** from $V_c$ and $D$
- ☐ $f_z$ = feed per tooth (mm/tooth) → **selected from handbook** based on cutter strength
- ☐ $v_f$ = table feed rate (mm/min) → **derived** from $N$, $f_z$, $Z$

**✅ Key Derived Quantities (must memorize formulas):**
- ☐ $N = (V_c \cdot 1000) / (\pi \cdot D)$ — **unit conversion is critical**
- ☐ $v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$
- ☐ $Q = (a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f) / 1000$ — gives MRR in **cm³/min**
- ☐ $F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m$ — tangential cutting force
- ☐ $P_c = (F_c \cdot V_c) / (60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta)$ — spindle power in kW
- ☐ $T_m = (L + L_{\text{app}} + L_{\text{ov}}) / v_f$ — **never forget approach + over-travel**
- ☐ $R_a \approx f_z^2 / (8 \cdot r_\epsilon)$ — surface finish (end mill)

**✅ Design for Milling (DFM) Heuristics:**
- ☐ Internal corner radii $\geq$ cutter radius (typically $\geq 1$ mm)
- ☐ Slot widths = standard cutter diameters (avoid non-standard)
- ☐ Avoid deep narrow pockets — limit depth $\leq 2 \times$ cutter diameter per pass
- ☐ Provide tool entry holes for closed pockets
- ☐ Standard tolerance for milling = $\pm 0.1$ mm; tighter only for finishing
- ☐ Use uniform floor/wall thickness; reinforce with ribs
- ☐ For surface finish $R_a \leq 1.6\ \mu m$ → specify a separate finishing pass

**✅ Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Forgetting the **1000** unit conversion factor in $N$ and $V_c$ formulas
- ☐ Forgetting **approach + over-travel** in time calculation
- ☐ Putting machine **efficiency in the numerator** instead of denominator
- ☐ Using **wrong chip thickness formula** (full trigonometric vs. simplified $h_m \approx f_z$)
- ☐ Not specifying **units** in the final answer

**✅ Material Reference Data (for KTU numericals):**
- ☐ Mild steel: $k_c \approx 2000$–$2200$ N/mm², $V_c \approx 20$–$30$ m/min (HSS)
- ☐ Aluminium: $k_c \approx 700$–$900$ N/mm², $V_c \approx 100$–$150$ m/min (carbide)
- ☐ Cast iron: $k_c \approx 1100$ N/mm², $V_c \approx 30$–$50$ m/min (HSS)
- ☐ Stainless steel: $k_c \approx 2400$ N/mm², $V_c \approx 15$–$25$ m/min (HSS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In the 14-mark numerical, always structure your answer in **5 sub-parts**, each labeled (i) through (v), with the formula written first, the substitution shown second, and the numerical answer with **unit** last. This presentation style earns **full methodology marks** even if a final number is off by 1% due to rounding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications of Parts Produced on Milling Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Applications of Parts Produced on Milling Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Milling** is a fundamental **interrupted-cutting machining process** in which a multi-point (multi-tooth) rotating cutter is advanced into (or past) a stationary or slowly moving workpiece, producing a desired geometry through the *consecutive engagement* of multiple cutting edges (teeth) on the cutter body.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (PEMET522 Module 3):**
> Milling is defined as a versatile material-removal process that uses a *rotary multi-tooth cutter* to progressively remove metal from a workpiece. The process is intrinsically **intermittent** — each tooth engages and disengages the workpiece once per revolution, making the cutting action a periodic load cycle rather than the continuous engagement seen in turning.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Ploughing Field

Imagine a **rotary lawn mower** moving across a grass field:
- The **cutter (mower blade)** spins rapidly.
- The **workpiece (grass patch)** is fed under the cutter.
- Each blade tip strikes and shears a small chip.
- Multiple blades act in *sequence* — the same way multiple milling teeth on a cutter body shear chips in sequence.

Just as a mower can sculpt a flat lawn, a sloped embankment, or a complex landscape, a milling cutter can produce flat surfaces, slots, pockets, contours, and 3D profiles — making milling one of the most **versatile** primary shaping processes.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Process Constants

- **Specific Cutting Energy (U_c)** for mild steel ≈ **2.0 to 2.5 N·m/mm³**
- **Typical Dimensional Tolerance** achievable on milling ≈ **±0.025 mm to ±0.05 mm**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** typical range = **0.8 μm to 3.2 μm**
- **Cutter Materials**: High-Speed Steel (HSS), Carbide, Ceramics, PCD, CBN
- **Coolant**: Flood, MQL, or dry (varies by material and cutter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Milling is Critical for DFM (Design for Manufacture):**
> From a DFM standpoint, milling offers high **geometric flexibility** (complex 3D contours), **interchangeable tooling** (modular cutter heads), and **good surface quality**, but it suffers from **low MRR for deep pockets**, **high tooling cost for complex contours**, and **stepped / non-cylindrical geometry** that makes it distinct from turning.

### 1.4 Categories of Parts Produced on Milling Machines

| Category | Example Part | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Prismatic Parts** | Engine blocks, brackets, slides | Flat and stepped surfaces |
| **Slot & Keyway Parts** | Shafts with keyways, T-slots | Internal slots, grooves |
| **Contoured / Sculpted** | Turbine blades, die cavities, moulds | Free-form 3D surfaces |
| **Gear-like Parts** | Spur gears, splines, racks | Tooth profile on flat disc |
| **Plate-type Parts** | Cover plates, die-sets, base plates | Large thin flat surfaces |
| **Pocket-type Parts** | Manifold blocks, valve bodies | Enclosed cavities |
| **Cavity-type Parts** | Injection moulds, forging dies | Deep enclosed pockets |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Mechanics — Step by Step

Milling can be broken down into four fundamental physics stages. Let us examine each:

**Step 1 — Cutter Engagement (Entry Phase)**
- As a tooth rotates into the workpiece, it begins at **zero chip thickness** (pure rubbing / sliding).
- The uncut chip thickness **h₁** increases progressively until the tooth reaches the **mid-engagement angle**.
- At this point, chip thickness is **maximum (h_max)**.

**Step 2 — Steady Cutting (Stable Phase)**
- The tooth is fully engaged in the cut, removing a chip of cross-section approximately equal to feed-per-tooth × depth-of-cut.
- The **chip load is approximately constant** during this phase.

**Step 3 — Cutter Disengagement (Exit Phase)**
- The chip thickness decreases from h_max to zero as the tooth exits the workpiece.
- The **cutting force drops** sharply, often producing vibrations and the characteristic "chatter" if the system is not rigid.

**Step 4 — Free Rotation (Air-Cutting Phase)**
- The tooth is disengaged and rotates freely through air (or coolant).
- This gives the tooth **time to cool** — one of the few advantages of interrupted cutting.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "interrupted cut" matters for DFM:**
> The intermittent nature creates **cyclic thermal and mechanical loading** on each tooth. This is why *carbide* and *ceramic* cutters (which handle high temperatures and shocks) are preferred over HSS for high-speed milling, and why **ramping / trochoidal milling** strategies are used in DFM to reduce entry/exit shock loads.

### 2.2 The Cutting Geometry Parameters

- **f_z** = Feed per tooth (mm/tooth)
- **z** = Number of teeth on cutter
- **N** = Spindle speed (rev/min or rpm)
- **D** = Cutter diameter (mm)
- **V** = Cutting speed (m/min)
- **f_r** = Table feed (mm/min) = f_z × z × N
- **a_p** = Axial depth of cut (mm) — along the cutter axis
- **a_e** = Radial depth of cut (mm) — perpendicular to cutter axis
- **θ_e** = Engagement angle (radians or degrees)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas (Master These for ESE):**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Derived from #1 |
| 3 | Table Feed Rate | $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | z = number of teeth |
| 4 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{f_r}{z \cdot N}$ | mm/tooth | Inverse of #3 |
| 5 | MRR (Milling) | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | Approx. slab model |
| 6 | Uncut Chip Thickness (max) | $h_{max} = f_z \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | mm | $\theta$ = engagement angle |
| 7 | Mean Chip Thickness | $h_m = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_z \cdot \theta_e}{\pi}$ | mm | $\theta_e$ in radians |
| 8 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | $L = l + \sqrt{a_p \cdot D - a_p^2}$ for slot |
| 9 | Power (Milling) | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot U_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $U_c$ in N·m/mm³ |
| 10 | Approach Distance | $L_a = \sqrt{a_p \cdot (D - a_p)}$ | mm | For plain/slab milling |
| 11 | Total Length | $L_{tot} = l + L_a + L_o$ | mm | $L_o$ = overtravel |
| 12 | Specific Energy (mild steel) | $U_c \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.5$ | N·m/mm³ | Material dependent |
| 13 | Number of Teeth Engaged | $z_e = \dfrac{z \cdot \theta_e}{2\pi}$ | — | Average over revolution |
| 14 | Material Constant Kc | $K_c = U_c \cdot h^{-m}$ | N/mm² | h = chip thickness, m = exponent |

> **Notation Note:** In KTU board exams, the absolute value symbol `$\vert x \vert$` should never be used inside a markdown table cell — always use `$\vert x \vert$` rendered via LaTeX, *not* the pipe character `|`.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas in DFM/DFA

- **MRR optimization** in Formula #5 directly drives **cycle-time estimation** in cost analysis for DFM.
- **Formula #8 (machining time)** is the foundation of the **cost-per-part** calculation in DFMA software.
- **Formula #14 (Kc)** is used for **deflection and chatter prediction** — critical for thin-wall milled parts in aerospace.
- The **chip-thickness analysis (Formulas #6, #7)** underpins **CAM tool-path strategies** like trochoidal, adaptive, and high-efficiency milling (HEM).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Machining Time for Plain (Slab) Milling

**Statement:** Derive the machining time $T_m$ for a plain milling operation, including the approach distance and overtravel correction.

**Given:**
- Workpiece length = $l$ (mm)
- Cutter diameter = $D$ (mm)
- Depth of cut = $a_p$ (mm)
- Feed rate = $f_r$ (mm/min)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The table must travel a total distance equal to the workpiece length **plus** an *approach distance* $L_a$ (to allow the cutter to enter the workpiece) **plus** an *overtravel* $L_o$ (for safe exit of the cutter).

$$
L_{tot} = l + L_a + L_o
$$

To find $L_a$, consider the geometry of the cutter as it engages the workpiece. The cutter is a circle of diameter $D$. When the cutter centre is exactly above the leading edge of the workpiece, the lowest point of the cutter is $D/2$ below the centre. The depth of cut $a_p$ is measured from the workpiece top surface. Therefore, the cutter centre is at a height equal to the **chord offset** needed to reach depth $a_p$:

$$
L_a = \sqrt{a_p \cdot (D - a_p)}
$$

The full derivation of the chord:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chord half-length: } \quad c &= \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{D}{2} - a_p\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{D^2}{4} - \left(\dfrac{D^2}{4} - D \cdot a_p + a_p^2\right)} \\
&= \sqrt{D \cdot a_p - a_p^2} \\
&= \sqrt{a_p \cdot (D - a_p)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L_{tot}}{f_r} = \dfrac{l + \sqrt{a_p \cdot (D - a_p)} + L_o}{f_z \cdot z \cdot N}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**

> A mild steel workpiece 200 mm long is to be plain-milled with a 60 mm diameter HSS cutter. Depth of cut = 4 mm. Number of teeth z = 8. Spindle speed N = 300 rpm. Feed per tooth f_z = 0.1 mm/tooth. Overtravel L_o = 5 mm. Calculate the machining time.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute approach distance.

$$
L_a = \sqrt{a_p \cdot (D - a_p)} = \sqrt{4 \cdot (60 - 4)} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 56} = \sqrt{224} \approx 14.97 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2: Compute total table travel.

$$
L_{tot} = l + L_a + L_o = 200 + 14.97 + 5 = 219.97 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 3: Compute feed rate.

$$
f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.1 \cdot 8 \cdot 300 = 240 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Step 4: Compute machining time.

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L_{tot}}{f_r} = \dfrac{219.97}{240} \approx 0.917 \text{ min} = 55 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_m \approx 55$ seconds.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Uncut Chip Thickness in Milling

**Statement:** Derive the maximum chip thickness $h_{max}$ produced by a milling tooth in peripheral (plain) milling.

The feed per tooth $f_z$ is the linear advance of the workpiece per tooth engagement. Consider a tooth entering the workpiece at angle $\theta = 0$ (where chip thickness is zero) and reaching the angular position $\theta$ at the moment the next tooth engages. By geometry, the **arc length** swept by the cutter circumference equals $f_z$. This arc is subtended by angle $\theta$ at radius $D/2$:

$$
f_z = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \dfrac{2 f_z}{D}
$$

The **chip thickness** in the radial direction (perpendicular to the cutter axis) at engagement angle $\theta$ is:

$$
h(\theta) = f_z \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

Maximum occurs at $\theta = 90°$ (mid-engagement):

$$
h_{max} = f_z \cdot \sin(90°) = f_z
$$

Wait — this is true only when the engagement is up to $90°$. For a more general (partial) engagement, the maximum occurs at the angular position where the tooth exits, which corresponds to the half-engagement angle $\theta_e/2$. In **peripheral milling with full engagement** ($a_e = D$), $h_{max} = f_z$ because the cutter engages for a full half-circle.

For **partial engagement** with radial depth $a_e < D$:

$$
h_{max} = f_z \cdot \sin(\theta_e/2)
$$

where:

$$
\theta_e = \arccos\left(1 - \dfrac{2 a_e}{D}\right)
$$

This is the basis for **high-engagement / trochoidal milling** in modern CAM strategies.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types of Milling Operations

| Operation Type | Cutter Used | Surface Produced | Key Application | DFM Advantage | DFM Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plain / Slab Milling** | Plain (cylindrical) cutter | Flat horizontal surface | Slideways, bed plates | High MRR on large flat areas | Cannot produce vertical surfaces |
| **Face Milling** | Face mill (inserted tooth) | Flat surface perpendicular to cutter axis | Engine block faces, die sets | Excellent surface finish | Cutter cost high |
| **End Milling** | End mill (flat, ball, bull-nose) | Slots, pockets, 3D contours | Die cavities, moulds, brackets | Most versatile operation | Low MRR in deep pockets |
| **Straddle Milling** | Two side-milling cutters | Two parallel vertical surfaces simultaneously | Hex bolts, key blanks | Productivity high (two surfaces at once) | Cutter spacing fixed → poor flexibility |
| **Gang Milling** | Multiple cutters stacked on arbor | Several surfaces in one pass | Connecting rods, rocker arms | Highest productivity for batch | Complex setup |
| **Form Milling** | Form cutter (profile ground) | Complex curved profile | Gear teeth, concave/convex shapes | One-pass profile generation | Dedicated cutter per profile |
| **Angular Milling** | Single-angle / double-angle cutter | Beveled or V-groove surfaces | V-ways, dovetails | Clean angled surfaces in one pass | Limited to standard angles |
| **Gear Milling (Hobbing)** | Hob cutter | Involute gear teeth | Spur gears, helical gears | Continuous indexing, high accuracy | Hob geometry = fixed |
| **Profile / Contour Milling** | End mill / ball mill | Free-form 2D/3D curves | Turbines, impellers, dies | Unlimited geometry | Requires CNC + CAM |
| **Slot Milling** | Slitting saw / end mill | Narrow deep slot | Woodruff keys, E-rings | Very narrow cut | Vibration, heat issues |
| **T-Slot Milling** | T-slot cutter (woodruff) | T-shaped slot | Machine tool tables | Standardized mounting | Two-step operation required |
| **Thread Milling** | Thread mill (single-point or multi-tooth) | Internal/external threads | Large-diameter threads | Any pitch, low tool inventory | Specialized tooling |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]
**"Differentiate between up-milling and down-milling. State one advantage of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Up-Milling (Conventional) | Down-Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutter rotation vs Feed direction** | Cutter rotates *against* the feed | Cutter rotates *along* the feed |
| **Chip thickness pattern** | Starts at **zero**, increases to maximum | Starts at **maximum**, decreases to zero |
| **Tooth engagement** | Tooth *slides* before cutting | Tooth *cuts* immediately |
| **Force on workpiece** | Tries to lift workpiece — clamps must hold it down | Tends to pull workpiece into cutter — anti-backlash needed |
| **Surface finish** | Poorer (sliding-induced work-hardening) | Better (clean shearing) |
| **Tool life** | Better in old/loose machines | Better in rigid CNC machines |
| **Advantage** | Safer for **old, worn, or backlash-prone machines** | **Higher surface finish**, lower power at start |

> **Key Distinction (1 mark):** Up-milling begins each tooth at *zero* chip thickness → rubbing → poor surface; Down-milling begins at *maximum* chip thickness → immediate shearing → good surface.
> **Advantage of up-milling (1 mark):** No sudden tool jerk → safe on worn machines.
> **Advantage of down-milling (1 mark):** Better surface finish, less cutter wear due to clean shearing.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]
**"List any six parts that are commonly produced on milling machines and state the type of milling operation used for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each, list 6):**

1. **Flat slide-ways** — Plain (slab) milling
2. **Vertical keyway in a shaft** — End milling
3. **V-groove / dovetail** — Angular milling with single-angle cutter
4. **Spur gear teeth** — Form milling (or gear hobbing)
5. **Internal cavity in a die** — Profile / contour end milling (CNC)
6. **T-slot in machine table** — T-slot milling (woodruff cutter)
7. **External thread on a long shaft** — Thread milling
8. **Hexagonal bolt blank** — Straddle milling
