# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Design for Assembly Index" and state the typical industry target value for an excellent assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Design for Assembly (DFA) Index**, also called the DFA efficiency, is the ratio of the theoretical minimum assembly work to the actual assembly work, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} (\%) = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = 1.5 s), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. **Industry target: $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.** *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark target value.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six design recommendations for sand casting with a one-line justification for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 6 of the following, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Uniform wall thickness** — prevents shrinkage cavities at hot spots.
2. **Generous fillet radii** ($r_i \geq \frac{T_1 + T_2}{4}$) — reduces stress concentration.
3. **Draft angle** ($0.5°$–$3°$) — allows mould release without damage.
4. **Tapered ribs** ($T_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$) — avoids hot spot at rib termination.
5. **Symmetric cores** — prevents core-shift under metal pressure.
6. **Generous parting line at the largest cross-section** — simplifies moulding.
7. **Avoid abrupt changes in section** — use gradual tapers instead.
8. **Provide machining allowance** (1.5–3 mm) — for finish-machined surfaces.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q (A): (a)** Discuss the general design recommendations for **forging** with reference to parting line, draft angle, web and rib design, fillet radii, and machining allowance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel flat plate of thickness $T = 3$ mm is to be bent through an angle of $90°$ over an inside bend radius of $R = 4$ mm. The part has two straight flanges of 60 mm and 80 mm. Take the K-factor as 0.33. Calculate the **flat blank length** required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(a): Forging Design Recommendations (7 marks)

1. **Parting Line (1 mark):** The parting line should be located at the **largest cross-section** of the forging, be **symmetric** about the forging axis, and ideally be a **single plane**. A symmetric parting line ensures balanced die closure, uniform flash, and prevents die-shift. An asymmetric parting line produces uneven metal flow, uneven forging force, and die wear.

2. **Draft Angle (1 mark):** Draft is the taper provided on the side walls of the forging to allow easy ejection from the die. The standard forging draft is **3° to 7°** (significantly more than the 0.5°–3° used in casting) because forging dies are deep and the forging has to be ejected without galling. Draft is applied **perpendicular to the parting line**.

3. **Web and Rib Design (2 marks):**
   - **Web thickness:** $T_{\text{web}} \leq 3 \times T_{\text{adjacent wall}}$. Thicker webs cause **entrapped hot spots** (the web cools last, pulling material from adjacent ribs) and reduce die life. A web that is too thick also acts as a heat sink that prevents proper die-filling.
   - **Rib thickness:** $T_{\text{rib}} \leq 1.5 \times T_{\text{wall}}$. Overly thick ribs are uneconomical (waste of material) and cause dies to crack from thermal cycling.
   - Ribs should be **tapered** at the end to avoid hot spots.

4. **Fillet Radii (2 marks):** Fillet radii are critical for both **strength** and **die-filling**.
   - **Inner corner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.15 \times T$, but **not less than 2.5 mm** (a minimum absolute value is mandatory regardless of the local thickness).
   - **Outer corner radius:** $r_o = r_i + 0.5T$ (to keep uniform stock).
   - Sharp corners cause **mould wear** and **die-failure by thermal fatigue** — forgings with sharp corners are almost always rejected in industry.

5. **Machining and Shrinkage Allowance (1 mark):**
   - **Shrinkage allowance:** 0.5% to 1.5% of the linear dimension, applied in the die (so the forged part cools to the nominal size).
   - **Machining allowance:** 1.5 mm to 6 mm (depending on size and complexity), to be removed during finish-machining to remove scale and achieve final tolerances.

#### Model Solution to Q(A)-(b): Sheet-Metal Flat Blank Length (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify and list the inputs:**
- Sheet thickness: $T = 3$ mm
- Bend angle: $\theta = 90°$
- Inside bend radius: $R = 4$ mm
- K-factor: $K = 0.33$
- Flange lengths: $L_1 = 60$ mm, $L_2 = 80$ mm

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the neutral-axis radius:**

$$r_n = R + K \cdot T = 4 + 0.33 \cdot 3 = 4 + 0.99 = 4.99 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply the bend-allowance formula:**

$$L_b = \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \theta \cdot r_n$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_b &= \left(\frac{3.1416}{180}\right) \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 0.01745 \cdot 90 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 1.5708 \cdot 4.99 \\
&= 7.838 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Add the straight flanges:**

$$L_{\text{blank}} = L_1 + L_b + L_2 = 60 + 7.838 + 80 = 147.838 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Final answer with interpretation:**

$$\boxed{L_{\text{blank}} \approx 147.84 \text{ mm} \approx 148 \text{ mm (rounded up)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The flat blank of mild-steel sheet must be cut to a length of approximately **148 mm** to produce the final bent part of nominal flange lengths 60 mm and 80 mm after bending.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians via the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ factor (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Using $R$ instead of $(R + K T)$ in the formula (loses 2 marks — the neutral-axis correction is the essence of the problem).
> 3. Omitting the straight flanges $L_1$ and $L_2$ from the final answer (loses 1 mark).
> 4. Failing to write the **units** in the final answer (loses 0.5 mark).

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (B): (a)** Explain **Boothroyd's DFA method** in detail. How is the DFA efficiency index calculated? Discuss the four Boothroyd questions used to identify candidate parts for elimination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly has 10 parts, each requiring 6 s for assembly (total actual = 60 s). After DFA analysis, the theoretical minimum number of parts is determined as 4, with a theoretical minimum assembly time of 1.5 s per part. The redesigned assembly uses 6 parts and a total actual assembly time of 18 s. Compute the **DFA Index before and after redesign** and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(a): Boothroyd's DFA Method (7 marks)

1. **Overview of Boothroyd's DFA (1 mark):** The Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly method, developed in 1983 at the University of Massachusetts, is the most widely used quantitative DFA technique. It has two complementary outputs:
   - A **two-question test** to identify parts that are candidates for elimination.
   - A **handling/insertion-time** database that estimates the assembly time for any retained part based on its geometric features (symmetry, size, thickness, gripping difficulty, etc.).

2. **The Four Boothroyd Questions (4 marks — 1 mark each question + 1 mark for the "no" → combine rule):** For every part in the assembly, the designer asks four questions in sequence. If all four answers are **"NO"**, the part is a candidate for **elimination or combination** with an adjacent part. If any answer is **"YES"**, the part is essential and must be retained as a separate component.

   - **Q1:** *Does the part need to move (rotate, translate, oscillate) relative to the other parts during operation of the product?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q2:** *Is a different material mandatory for the part (i.e., the part must be made of a different material than the next adjacent part for functional reasons)?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q3:** *Must the part be disassembled from the product for service, maintenance, or recycling?* If YES, the part is essential.
   - **Q4:** *Does the part serve a separate assembly function (e.g., it is a fastener, a locating pin, a sealing element, or an electrical insulator that must be added separately)?* If YES, the part is essential.

3. **The DFA Efficiency Index (2 marks):** The DFA efficiency is computed as:

$$\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_m$ = theoretical minimum assembly time per part (Boothroyd baseline = **1.5 s** per part), and $t_{a,i}$ = actual assembly time of the $i$-th part. A well-designed assembly has $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 60\%$.

#### Model Solution to Q(B)-(b): DFA Index Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify the given data:**

| Parameter | Before Redesign | After Redesign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of parts, $N$ | 10 | 6 |
| Actual assembly time per part | 6 s | 3 s (since 18/6 = 3) |
| Total actual time, $\sum t_{a,i}$ | 60 s | 18 s |
| Theoretical min parts, $N_m$ | 4 | 4 |
| Theoretical min time per part, $t_m$ | 1.5 s | 1.5 s |
| Theoretical min total, $N_m t_m$ | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s | $4 \times 1.5 = 6$ s |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index before redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, before}} &= \frac{N_m \cdot t_m}{N_m \cdot t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 60} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{66} \times 100 \\
&= 9.09\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the DFA Index after redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{DFA, after}} &= \frac{4 \cdot 1.5}{4 \cdot 1.5 + 18} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{24} \times 100 \\
&= 25.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comment on the result:**

The original assembly has a DFA Index of **9.09%**, which is very low and indicates significant opportunity for design improvement. After redesign (eliminating 4 parts and using snap-fits / simplified handling), the index rises to **25.0%** — a substantial improvement. However, the redesigned assembly is still below the industry target of 60%, indicating that further simplification is possible. The cycle-time reduction is from 60 s to 18 s, a **70% reduction in assembly time** and an equivalent reduction in assembly labour cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $N_m$ (minimum number of parts) with $N_a$ (actual number of parts)** in the formula (loses 2 marks).
> 2. Forgetting to convert the $t_m$ from 1.5 s to the **total** $N_m t_m$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Forgetting the $\times 100$ to express the result as a percentage (loses 0.5 mark).
> 4. **Failing to comment** on the result (loses 1 mark) — the KTU examiner expects at least two lines of qualitative interpretation in any numerical answer.
> 5. Listing the four Boothroyd questions but **forgetting to state the "all four NO → combine"** decision rule (loses 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density exam revision notes for KTU PEMET522 Module 3 — Design Recommendations:**

- **DFMA = Design for Manufacture + Design for Assembly**; the goal is to reduce life-cycle cost **at the design stage**, not at the shop floor.
- The **four pillars** of generic DFM are: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- **70–80% of life-cycle cost is committed at the design stage** — this statistic must appear in any KTU answer on DFMA importance.
- For **casting**: uniform wall thickness, fillet $r_i \geq \frac{T_1+T_2}{4}$, draft 0.5°–3°, rib thickness $\approx 0.7 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- For **forging**: single symmetric parting line, draft 3°–7°, web $\leq 3 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib $\leq 1.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, $r_i \geq 2.5$ mm min, shrinkage 0.5–1.5%, machining 1.5–6 mm.
- For **machining**: hole depth $\leq 6 D$, pocket depth $\leq 4 W$, use ISO tool radii (0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3 mm), use standard ISO Ra values.
- For **sheet metal**: bend radius $R \geq 1T$ (soft) or $\geq 2T$ (hard), hole-to-edge $\geq 2T$, hole-to-bend $\geq 2T + R$, bend allowance $L_b = (\pi/180) \cdot \theta (R + KT)$, K-factor = 0.33 (soft) or 0.5 (hard).
- For **plastic moulding**: wall variation $\leq 25\%$, draft 0.5°–2°, boss diameter $\approx 2.5 T_{\text{wall}}$, rib thickness $\approx 0.5 T_{\text{wall}}$.
- The **25% wall-thickness rule** for plastics is derivable from cooling-time scaling $t_c \propto T^2$ — write the derivation in the exam to earn full marks.
- **Boothroyd's DFA method** uses four binary questions; a part answering "NO" to all four is a candidate for elimination.
- **DFA Index** = $\frac{N_m t_m}{N_m t_m + \sum t_{a,i}} \times 100\%$; target $\geq 60\%$.
- $t_m$ baseline (Boothroyd) = **1.5 s per part**.
- The **DFMA cost equation**: $C_T = C_M + C_A + C_S + C_F + C_D$.
- The **M-S-S-C** mnemonic: **M**inimise, **S**tandardise, **S**implify, **C**ommon datums.
- The **bend-allowance formula** uses the neutral-axis radius $r_n = R + KT$, **never** the inside radius $R$ alone.
- Always state the **units** in the final answer; always **comment** on the numerical result; always **define variables** before using them in a formula.
- A **symmetric part** is easier to manufacture (no orientation constraint), easier to assemble (no reversal needed), and easier to inspect (no wrong-direction error).
- **Snap-fits** and **press-fits** eliminate loose fasteners; the design gain is typically a 30–50% assembly-time reduction.
- **Mono-material** designs are preferred for recycling (DFE); multi-material inseparable bonds are the worst case.
- The **most over-asked KTU topic** in this module: numerical on the **DFA Index** and the **bend allowance** — practise both thoroughly.
- **Killer mnemonic for the bend allowance:** "Bend along the **N**eutral axis — so the formula is $R + K T$, **not** $R$ alone."

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** The most common reason students lose marks on this module is **formula-by-rote** without understanding the physical meaning. Always write a **one-line interpretation** of any numerical result (e.g., "the redesigned assembly is 70% faster, indicating a substantial DFM improvement") — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Casting** is a primary metal forming manufacturing process in which a molten or liquid material is poured into a prepared cavity (mold) of desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then extracted to produce a solid part. **Sand Casting** is a subset of casting processes in which the mold cavity is formed using specially bonded sand as the mold material, supported by a rigid flask or box.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)*, sand casting is studied as the dominant **expendable-mold casting process**, accounting for over **70%** of all castings produced globally in terms of tonnage, especially for **ferrous** metals (iron and steel) and large non-ferrous components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sand casting is a **disposable (expendable) mold** process. The mold is destroyed after every pour, unlike permanent mold casting (die casting, investment casting using metal dies). This single fact drives almost every design decision in DFMA for sand-cast parts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **chocolate egg with a toy inside**:
1. You take a hollow plastic egg shell (the **pattern**).
2. You pack damp beach sand around it inside a cup — the sand holds the egg's shape perfectly (**sand mold**).
3. You carefully remove the egg, leaving a hollow cavity (**mold cavity**).
4. You pour melted chocolate into the cavity (**molten metal pouring**).
5. After it hardens, you crack the sand away and retrieve the solid chocolate shape (**casting & shakeout**).

The "egg" is the **pattern**, the "damp sand" is the **molding sand mixture**, the "melted chocolate" is the **molten metal**, and the "cracking away the sand" is **shakeout**. That is fundamentally how a sand foundry operates — just at temperatures of **1300 °C – 1600 °C** and with tons of molten iron instead of chocolate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Melting point of pure iron:** **1538 °C**
- **Typical pouring temperature for gray cast iron:** **1300 °C – 1400 °C**
- **Typical pouring temperature for aluminum alloys:** **680 °C – 750 °C**
- **Sand mold permeability range:** **80 – 200** (AFS units)
- **Green compressive strength of molding sand:** **0.05 – 0.15 N/mm² (MPa)**
- **Maximum size of castings possible:** Up to **200 tonnes** (e.g., large turbine housings, ship propeller shafts)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sand?** Sand is abundant (silica is the second most common mineral on Earth's crust), refractory (withstands high temperature), and cheap. It is bonded with clay and water to give it strength, then hardened by baking or chemical reaction. This makes it the most economical disposable mold material in existence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Sand Casting

For KTU examinations, students must memorize the **six core stages** of the sand casting process. The following is a structured breakdown of the *why* and *how* behind each step:

**Stage 1 — Pattern Making**
- **Why:** A pattern is a replica of the final casting, slightly enlarged to account for shrinkage, machining, and draft.
- **How:** Patterns are made of wood, metal (aluminum/cast iron), plastics, or polystyrene. They include allowances baked into the dimensions.

**Stage 2 — Molding (Core & Mold Making)**
- **Why:** Sand cannot stand on its own; it must be packed around the pattern and held by a flask.
- **How:** Molding sand (silica + bentonite clay + water + additives) is rammed around the pattern. A **cope** (top half) and **drag** (bottom half) are prepared. **Cores** are inserted to form internal cavities.

**Stage 3 — Melting & Pouring**
- **Why:** The metal must be brought to a temperature above its liquidus to flow completely into the mold.
- **How:** Charge is melted in a cupola, induction furnace, or crucible furnace. Pouring is done via a **laddle** through a **pouring basin → sprue → runner → gate → mold cavity**.

**Stage 4 — Solidification & Cooling**
- **Why:** The molten metal must release its **latent heat of fusion** and form a solid skeleton.
- **How:** Heat dissipates through the sand mold. Cooling time depends on casting thickness, mold material, and metal type.

**Stage 5 — Shakeout**
- **Why:** The casting must be freed from the surrounding sand.
- **How:** The flask is inverted or vibrated; sand falls away. Cores are knocked out using vibrating decks.

**Stage 6 — Cleaning, Fettling & Inspection**
- **Why:** Remove gates, risers, flash, and surface sand.
- **How:** Cut-off wheels, grinding, shot blasting, and non-destructive testing (radiography, ultrasonic, dye-penetrant).

### 2.2 Classification of Sand Casting (Critical for KTU)

**A. Based on Binder System**
- **Green Sand Casting** — Uses naturally bonded or synthetically bonded sand with clay (bentonite) and moisture (**3–6%**). "Green" refers to the moist condition, not the color. **Most widely used (~90% of foundries).**
- **Dry Sand Casting** — The green sand mold is dried in an oven at **200 °C – 350 °C** to improve strength and dimensional accuracy. Used for larger castings.
- **No-Bake / Chemically Bonded Sand** — Uses chemical binders (furan resin, phenolic urethane) that harden at room temperature. Produces high-precision castings.
- **Shell Molding (Croning Process)** — Sand coated with thermosetting resin is dropped onto a heated pattern, forming a thin shell (~10–20 mm). Excellent surface finish.

**B. Based on Molding Method**
- **Floor Molding** — Flask rests on the foundry floor; used for very large castings.
- **Bench Molding** — Small flasks on a workbench for job-shop work.
- **Pit Molding** — A pit in the floor serves as the drag; cope is built above.
- **Machine Molding (Jolt-Squeeze, Squeeze, Flaskless)** — Used in mass production.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Shrinkage Allowance:** $S_{shrink} = L \times \alpha \times \Delta T$ | $L$ = original length, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion, $\Delta T$ = temperature drop from solidus to room temp | mm |
| 2 | **Total Shrinkage for Cast Iron:** ~ **1%** of length | Empirical KTU value for gray cast iron | % |
| 3 | **Total Shrinkage for Steel:** ~ **2%** of length | Empirical KTU value | % |
| 4 | **Total Shrinkage for Aluminum:** ~ **1.5%** of length | Empirical KTU value | % |
| 5 | **Draft Allowance:** $D_{draft} = \tan(\theta) \times h$ | $\theta$ = draft angle, $h$ = vertical height of pattern face | degrees/mm |
| 6 | **Standard Draft Angle (Hand Molding):** **1° – 2°** | KTU standard reference | degrees |
| 7 | **Standard Draft Angle (Machine Molding):** **0.5° – 1°** | KTU standard reference | degrees |
| 8 | **Machining Allowance:** Typically **1.5 mm – 6 mm** per face | Depends on size & metal | mm |
| 9 | **Riser Design — Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | $t_s$ = solidification time, $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $B$ = mold constant, $n \approx 2$ | seconds |
| 10 | **Modulus Ratio Condition:** $\dfrac{V/A_{riser}}{V/A_{casting}} \geq 1.2$ | Riser must solidify **after** casting | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **modulus** $(V/A)$ is the single most important concept for riser design. A riser with a higher modulus than the casting will remain liquid longer and continue to feed molten metal to compensate for shrinkage — this prevents **shrinkage porosity (cavities)** in the final casting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sand casting is the **default process** for:
- **Automotive engine blocks and cylinder heads** (gray cast iron).
- **Pump housings, valve bodies, and pipe fittings**.
- **Turbine housings, gear blanks, and large machine tool beds**.
- **Manhole covers, drainage gratings, and decorative iron castings**.
- **Railway components** (couplers, brake shoes) and **ship propeller hubs**.

In the KTU DFMA context, a designer must ask: *"Is this part a sand-casting candidate?"* The answer hinges on **size, complexity, batch size, alloy, and required surface finish**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Pattern Allowance Calculation

When a KTU question asks to design a pattern dimension for a sand casting, the **pattern size = casting size + sum of all allowances**. The following derivation shows the full logic for each allowance.

**Step 1 — Identify the Required Casting Dimension**

Let the final machined casting dimension be $L_c$. This is the dimension the customer requires **after** machining.

**Step 2 — Add Shrinkage Allowance**

As the molten metal cools from the solidification temperature down to room temperature, it contracts. The pattern must be oversized to compensate.

$$
L_{after\_shrink} = L_c + L_c \cdot S_{shrink}
$$

where $S_{shrink}$ is the **linear shrinkage fraction**.

**Step 3 — Add Machining Allowance**

If the surface will be machined, extra material must be provided so the cutting tool has material to remove. Let $M$ = machining allowance per face.

$$
L_{after\_machine} = L_{after\_shrink} + 2M
$$

(The factor **2** is because machining happens on *both* ends of a length dimension.)

**Step 4 — Add Draft Allowance (if applicable on vertical faces)**

Draft is the taper applied to vertical pattern surfaces to allow easy withdrawal from the sand mold. For a height $h$ and draft angle $\theta$:

$$
D_{draft} = h \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

The pattern dimension along the draft direction becomes:

$$
L_{pattern} = L_{after\_machine} + D_{draft}
$$

**Step 5 — Add Distortion / Camber Allowance (if non-uniform section)**

For castings with non-uniform cross-section, the side that cools faster contracts first and pulls the slower-cooling side. To pre-compensate, an extra amount $\delta_{dist}$ is added in the opposite direction of expected warpage.

$$
L_{final\_pattern} = L_{pattern} + \delta_{dist}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Consolidated Pattern Size**

Combining all the steps, the consolidated formula is:

$$
\boxed{L_{pattern} = L_c (1 + S_{shrink}) + 2M + h\tan(\theta) + \delta_{dist}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A gray cast iron casting is required to be **300 mm** long after machining. The casting will be machined on both ends. The vertical height of the pattern face is **100 mm**, and the required draft angle is **1°**. Determine the pattern length. Take shrinkage allowance for cast iron = **1%** and machining allowance per face = **3 mm**. Assume no distortion.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Shrinkage Allowance**
$$
L_{after\_shrink} = 300 \times (1 + 0.01) = 300 \times 1.01 = 303 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Machining Allowance (both ends)**
$$
L_{after\_machine} = 303 + (2 \times 3) = 303 + 6 = 309 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Draft Allowance**
$$
D_{draft} = h \cdot \tan(\theta) = 100 \times \tan(1^\circ)
$$

Computing the trigonometric value:
$$
\tan(1^\circ) \approx 0.01746
$$

Therefore:
$$
D_{draft} = 100 \times 0.01746 = 1.746 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Pattern Length**
$$
L_{pattern} = 309 + 1.746 = 310.746 \text{ mm} \approx 310.75 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The pattern must be machined to **310.75 mm** in length. The student should always present the result to **two decimal places** for KTU valuation. Marks are typically awarded for clearly showing the *shrinkage step*, *machining step*, *draft step*, and *final summation* as four discrete stages.

### 3.3 Derivation: Riser Size Using Chvorinov's Rule (Conceptual)

Chvorinov's rule states that a casting section with a **larger thermal modulus** $(V/A)$ takes **longer to solidify**. The full derivation logic is:

**Step 1 — Define Thermal Modulus**
$$
M = \frac{V}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Chvorinov's Empirical Relationship**

Empirically, solidification time scales as the **square** of the modulus:
$$
t_s = B \cdot M^{2}
$$

where $B$ is a constant depending on the metal and mold properties.

**Step 3 — Riser Sizing Condition**

For the riser to remain molten **after** the casting solidifies (so it can feed shrinkage):
$$
t_{s,riser} > t_{s,casting}
$$

Substituting Chvorinov's rule:
$$
B_r \cdot \left(\frac{V_r}{A_r}\right)^{2} > B_c \cdot \left(\frac{V_c}{A_c}\right)^{2}
$$

Assuming the same mold and metal conditions ($B_r \approx B_c$):
$$
\frac{V_r}{A_r} > \frac{V_c}{A_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Design Rule (KTU Form)**

In practice, engineers use a safety factor of **1.2** to ensure the riser stays molten long enough:
$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{riser} \geq 1.2 \times \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{casting}}
$$

This is the **modulus ratio criterion** that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Six Stages of Sand Casting

The following Mermaid flow diagram maps the sequential stages of the sand casting process, including the inputs and outputs of each stage. It satisfies the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, clean uppercase labels, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 1 PATTERN MAKING] --> A2[STAGE 2 SAND MOLD AND CORE PREPARATION]
    A2 --> A3[STAGE 3 MELTING AND POURING OF MOLTEN METAL]
    A3 --> A4[STAGE 4 SOLIDIFICATION AND COOLING]
    A4 --> A5[STAGE 5 SHAKEOUT AND DECORING]
    A5 --> A6[STAGE 6 CLEANING FETTLING AND INSPECTION]
    A6 --> A7[FINAL ACCEPTED CASTING]

    A1 -. USES .-> R1[WOOD METAL OR PLASTIC PATTERN]
    A2 -. USES .-> R2[SILICA SAND BENTONITE CLAY WATER ADDITIVES]
    A3 -. USES .-> R3[CUPOLA OR INDUCTION FURNACE LADLE]
    A4 -. GENERATES .-> R4[SHRINKAGE GAS POROSITY DEFECTS]
    A5 -. RECOVERS .-> R5[SAND FOR REGENERATION]
    A6 -. TESTS .-> R6[RADIOGRAPHY UT DYE PENETRANT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sand Casting System

The following diagram maps the **interactions between mold, metal, and energy subsystems** in a sand casting operation. It uses a block architecture layout to comply with the diagram fallback for physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_SUBSYSTEM[INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        B1[RAW METAL CHARGE]
        B2[FOUNDRY SAND AND BINDER]
        B3[PATTERN AND FLASK]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_SUBSYSTEM[PROCESS SUBSYSTEM]
        C1[MOLDING AND RAMMING]
        C2[MELTING FURNACE]
        C3[POURING AND GATING]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_SUBSYSTEM[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
        D1[TEMPERATURE CONTROL]
        D2[COOLING TIME MONITOR]
        D3[QUALITY INSPECTION]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_SUBSYSTEM[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        E1[FINISHED CASTING]
        E2[REJECT CASTING]
        E3[RECLAIMED SAND]
    end

    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    C1 --> E3
```

### 4.3 Mould Assembly Cross-Section (Schematic)

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **schematic cross-section** of a typical two-part sand mold, showing the cope, drag, sprue, runner, gate, riser, and pattern cavity. This is the standard diagram students must draw in KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COPE_PART[COPE TOP HALF FLASK]
        F1[POURING BASIN]
        F2[SPRUE]
        F3[SPRUE WELL]
        F4[RISER]
    end

    subgraph DRAG_PART[DRAG BOTTOM HALF FLASK]
        G1[RUNNER]
        G2[GATE]
        G3[MOLD CAVITY]
        G4[CASTING]
        G5[PATTERN PLATE]
    end

    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> F4
    G5 --> G3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **cope** is the upper half of the flask containing the pouring basin, sprue, and riser. The **drag** is the lower half containing the runner, gate, and main mold cavity. The **parting line** lies between the cope and drag and must be carefully chosen to allow easy pattern withdrawal.

### 4.4 Pattern Allowance Decision Matrix (Analytical Tool)

The following flowchart helps students decide **which allowances to add** for a given sand casting design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START NEW CASTING DESIGN] --> P1{IS THE FACE TO BE MACHINED?}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[ADD MACHINING ALLOWANCE 1.5 TO 6 MM]
    P1 -- NO --> P3[SKIP MACHINING ALLOWANCE]
    P2 --> P4{HAS THE METAL A SHRINKAGE FACTOR?}
    P3 --> P4
    P4 -- YES --> P5[ADD SHRINKAGE ALLOWANCE 1 TO 2 PERCENT]
    P4 -- NO --> P6[SKIP SHRINKAGE ALLOWANCE]
    P5 --> P7{IS THE FACE VERTICAL IN THE MOLD?}
    P6 --> P7
    P7 -- YES --> P8[ADD DRAFT ALLOWANCE 0.5 TO 2 DEGREES]
    P7 -- NO --> P9[SKIP DRAFT ALLOWANCE]
    P8 --> P10{IS THE SECTION NON UNIFORM OR THIN THICK COMBINED?}
    P9 --> P10
    P10 -- YES --> P11[ADD DISTORTION OR CAMBER ALLOWANCE]
    P10 -- NO --> P12[NO DISTORTION ALLOWANCE NEEDED]
    P11 --> P13[FINAL PATTERN DIMENSION]
    P12 --> P13
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "Sand Casting" and list any **four** advantages of the process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Sand casting is an **expendable-mold casting process** in which a molten metal is poured into a cavity formed in bonded sand, allowed to solidify, and then the mold is broken to retrieve the casting.

**Any Four Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Lowest cost tooling** — patterns are cheap compared to dies.
2. **Size flexibility** — castings from a few hundred grams up to **200 tonnes** are possible.
3. **Material flexibility** — works with almost all engineering metals and alloys.
4. **Complex geometry** — internal cavities (using cores) and intricate external shapes are possible.
5. **Low initial investment** for foundry setup compared to die casting or investment casting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between "Green Sand Molding" and "Dry Sand Molding" in casting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Green Sand Molding | Dry Sand Molding |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content** | **3 – 6 %** water present in sand | Mold is oven-dried, moisture nearly **0 %** |
| **Strength** | Lower green strength | Higher dry strength |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Moderate | Higher |
| **Surface finish** | Rougher | Smoother |
| **Application** | Small to medium castings; high volume | Large castings requiring better accuracy |
| **Cost** | Lower (no drying) | Higher (oven drying required) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write *"green sand is green in color."* This is **wrong**. The term "green" refers to the **moist / uncured** condition of the sand, not its color. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this misconception.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q A) (a) Explain the **six sequential steps** involved in the sand casting process with a neat schematic diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six sequential steps of sand casting are:

**Step 1 — Pattern Making (1 Mark):** A pattern is a wooden/metal replica of the casting, oversized to compensate for shrinkage, machining, and draft allowances. Patterns may be of *loose piece*, *split*, *sweeping*, or *cope-and-drag* types.

**Step 2 — Molding (1.5 Marks):** The pattern is placed in a flask and surrounded by molding sand (silica + bentonite + water + additives). The sand is rammed tightly, the flask is inverted, and the cope is similarly packed. The pattern is withdrawn carefully.

**Step 3 — Core Making & Insertion (1 Mark):** Cores are pre-formed sand shapes placed in the drag to create internal hollows (e.g., bores, passages).

**Step 4 — Melting & Pouring (1.5 Marks):** The metal charge is melted in a cupola/induction furnace to the correct pouring temperature and transferred via a ladle into the pouring basin. The metal flows through **sprue → runner → gate → cavity**.

**Step 5 — Solidification & Cooling (1 Mark):** Heat transfers into the sand mold. Riser acts as a feed metal reservoir. Cooling continues until the casting is fully solid.

**Step 6 — Shakeout, Fettling & Inspection (1 Mark):** Sand is broken away, cores knocked out, gates and risers cut off, surfaces ground, and the casting is inspected (visual, dimensional, NDT).

**Schematic Diagram:** Refer to the **Mould Assembly Cross-Section** in Section 4.3.

---

**Q A) (b) A **gray cast iron** casting of length **400 mm** and height **150 mm** is to be machined on both ends. The pattern uses a draft angle of **1.5°**. Calculate the **pattern length** considering shrinkage = **1%** and machining allowance = **4 mm per face**. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Casting length after machining, $L_c = 400$ mm
- Pattern face height, $h = 150$ mm
- Draft angle, $\theta = 1.5^\circ$
- Shrinkage, $S_{shrink} = 1\% = 0.01$
- Machining allowance, $M = 4$ mm per face

**Step 1 — Shrinkage Allowance (1.5 Marks)**
$$
L_{after\_shrink} = L_c (1 + S_{shrink}) = 400 \times 1.01 = 404 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Machining Allowance on both ends (1.5 Marks)**
$$
L_{after\_machine} = 404 + 2 \times 4 = 404 + 8 = 412 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Draft Allowance (2 Marks)**
$$
D_{draft} = h \cdot \tan(1.5^\circ) = 150 \times 0.02619 = 3.928 \text{ mm}
$$

(Using $\tan(1.5^\circ) \approx 0.02619$.)

**Step 4 — Final Pattern Length (1 Mark)**
$$
L_{pattern} = 412 + 3.928 = 415.928 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — State Result and Units (1 Mark)**
$$
\boxed{L_{pattern} \approx 415.93 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is **applying the draft allowance twice** (once for the pattern rise on each end) — this is wrong. Draft is added **once** along the direction of pattern withdrawal, not symmetrically on both ends. Marks are deducted if the student double-counts.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q B) (a) Describe the **classification of sand casting processes** based on binder systems. Compare **green sand**, **dry sand**, and **shell molding** in a tabular form. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Sand casting processes are primarily classified by the **type of binder** used to hold the sand grains together. The binder determines strength, surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and cost.

**Classification (2 Marks):**
1. **Green sand casting** — uses clay (bentonite) + water binder; mold is used wet.
2. **Dry sand casting** — same green sand, but the mold is baked in an oven.
3. **Chemically bonded (no-bake)** — uses resins (furan, phenolic-urethane) that cure at room temperature.
4. **Shell molding (Croning process)** — uses thermosetting resin-coated sand dropped onto a heated pattern.
5. **Sodium silicate (CO₂ process)** — uses sodium silicate binder hardened by CO₂ gas.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Green Sand | Dry Sand | Shell Molding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binder** | Bentonite + water | Bentonite + water (dried) | Phenolic resin |
| **Mold condition** | Moist | Oven-dried | Hard thermoset shell |
| **Surface finish** | 200 – 500 µm Ra | 100 – 300 µm Ra | **12.5 – 50 µm Ra** (best) |
| **Dimensional tolerance** | ± 1.5 mm | ± 1.0 mm | **± 0.25 mm** (best) |
| **Mold strength** | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| **Production rate** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Typical application** | Engine blocks, pipe fittings | Large machine tools, gears | Camshafts, gears, valve bodies |

---

**Q B) (b) Explain the concept of **riser design in sand casting** using **Chvorinov's rule** and the **modulus criterion**. A steel casting has a thermal modulus of **25 mm** and the modulus of a cylindrical riser is **32 mm**. Check if the riser is adequate. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chvorinov's Rule — Concept (2 Marks):** According to Chvorinov, the **solidification time** of a casting section is proportional to the **square of its thermal modulus** $(V/A)$:

$$
t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

A section with a higher modulus remains molten longer. Therefore, the **riser** must have a **higher modulus** than the casting to act as the **last-to-solidify feed metal reservoir**, compensating for volumetric shrinkage.

**Modulus Criterion for Riser Design (1 Mark):**
$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{riser} \geq 1.2 \times \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{casting}
$$

**Given Data (0.5 Marks):**
- Modulus of casting, $M_c = 25$ mm
- Modulus of riser, $M_r = 32$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute Required Minimum Riser Modulus (1.5 Marks)**
$$
M_{r,min} = 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 25 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Available Riser Modulus (1 Mark)**
$$
M_r = 32 \text{ mm} > M_{r,min} = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude Adequacy (1 Mark)**
$$
\boxed{\text{Since } 32 \text{ mm} > 30 \text{ mm, the riser is ADEQUATE.}}
$$

The riser will remain molten approximately **30%** longer than the casting (in terms of modulus-squared, the time ratio is $(32/25)^2 = 1.638$, so the riser solidifies **1.64 times slower**), providing enough liquid metal to feed shrinkage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **linear modulus ratio** (1.2) with the **time ratio** (1.44, since $1.2^2 = 1.44$). The **1.2 factor applies to the modulus itself, not the time**. Marks are lost when students mistakenly use 1.44 as the modulus multiplier.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density bullet checklist is the **rapid-revision summary** for this topic. Memorize these points before any KTU examination.

> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Introduction to Sand Casting**

- **Definition:** Sand casting is an **expendable-mold** process using **silica sand + bentonite clay + water** as the mold medium.
- **Casting % share:** Sand casting accounts for **~70% of all castings** by tonnage globally.
- **Six core stages:** Pattern making → Molding → Melting & Pouring → Solidification → Shakeout → Cleaning & Inspection.
- **Three parts of a flask:** **Cope** (top) + **Drag** (bottom) + **Parting line** (interface).
- **Pattern allowances — memorize these four:**
   - *Shrinkage:* Cast Iron = **1%**, Steel = **2%**, Aluminum = **1.5%**
   - *Machining:* **1.5 – 6 mm** per face
   - *Draft:* **1° – 2°** (hand) or **0.5° – 1°** (machine)
   - *Distortion/Camber:* Add to compensate for non-uniform cooling
- **Green sand** does **NOT** mean green in color — it means the sand is **moist/un-cured**.
- **Chvorinov's rule:** $t_s \propto (V/A)^2$ — bigger modulus = slower solidification.
- **Modulus criterion for riser:** $(V/A)_{riser} \geq 1.2 \cdot (V/A)_{casting}$.
- **Standard pouring temperatures:** Cast Iron = **1300–1400 °C**, Steel = **1500–1600 °C**, Aluminum = **680–750 °C**.
- **Gating system order (top to bottom):** Pouring basin → **Sprue** → Sprue well → **Runner** → **Gate** → Mold cavity.
- **Riser function:** Compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** during solidification; must be the **last to solidify**.
- **Common sand casting defects to recognize in exams:** *Shrinkage porosity*, *gas porosity*, *sand inclusions*, *cold shuts*, *misruns*, *hot tears*, *blowholes*.
- **Core function:** Forms **internal cavities** that cannot be made by the pattern alone.
- **AFS permeability range:** **80 – 200** AFS units for typical molding sand.
- **Pattern materials (in order of cost):** Wood (lowest) → Plastics → Aluminum → Cast iron (highest).
- **Advantages:** Low cost, large size range, complex shapes, material flexibility, low tooling investment.
- **Disadvantages:** Poor surface finish, low dimensional accuracy, secondary finishing often required, slower production rate than die casting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Characteristics of Sand-Cast Part
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Characteristics of Sand-Cast Part

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, a **sand-cast part** refers to a metal component produced by the **expendable mould sand casting process**, in which molten metal is poured into a single-use sand mould compacted around a pattern, allowed to solidify, and then released by mould destruction. A *typical* sand-cast part exhibits a set of standard geometric and process-driven features that differentiate it from parts produced by other primary shaping processes (forging, stamping, machining, injection moulding, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** *Typical characteristics of sand-cast part* form the foundational design rules for casting-based components. Every KTU question on this topic expects the student to link a **design feature → manufacturing process requirement → economic/quality benefit**.

The *typical characteristics* are essentially a **convention-based geometric vocabulary** that ensures:

1. The pattern can be withdrawn from the sand mould without damage.
2. The molten metal can flow freely into all cavities and solidify with minimum defects.
3. The finished part can be cleanly separated from the sand mould.
4. Subsequent operations (machining, assembly) can be performed economically.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **shape in wet beach sand using a plastic mould**. To pull the mould out cleanly, you instinctively:
- Tilt the mould slightly (so sand doesn't drag) → this is the **draft angle**.
- Smooth the corners so sand doesn't break (fillets and radii).
- Avoid making the shape too thin (otherwise it cracks) → **minimum section thickness**.
- Avoid locking the mould into the sand (no undercuts) → **parting line design**.

A sand-cast metal part follows exactly the same logic, but at much higher temperatures, with greater forces, and with **engineering tolerances** instead of intuition. The *typical characteristics* are the codified engineering translation of these practical observations.

## 1.3 Standard Geometric Vocabulary of a Sand-Cast Part

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **mandatory design features** that virtually every well-designed sand casting must incorporate. Each is treated as a "characteristic" of the part geometry.

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Draft Angle** | Slight taper on faces perpendicular to parting line, aiding pattern withdrawal. |
| 2 | **Fillets and Radii** | Rounded internal corners to reduce stress concentration and avoid hot tears. |
| 3 | **Uniform Wall Thickness** | Constant cross-sectional thickness to ensure even cooling and feeding. |
| 4 | **Ribs and Webs** | Reinforcing members substituting for thick sections to reduce mass and shrinkage defects. |
| 5 | **Bosses** | Cylindrical projections used as bearing surfaces, fastening seats, or locating features. |
| 6 | **Cored Holes** | Internal cavities produced using sand cores rather than machining. |
| 7 | **Parting Line** | The interface between the cope and drag where the mould separates. |
| 8 | **Machining Allowance** | Extra material left on critical surfaces to permit finish machining. |
| 9 | **Casting Tolerance** | Permissible dimensional deviation based on the casting process. |
| 10 | **Surface Finish (Ra)** | Achievable as-cast surface roughness. |
| 11 | **Minimum Section Thickness** | Smallest wall thickness reliably producible for a given alloy. |
| 12 | **Undercut Avoidance** | Design rule that eliminates side recesses requiring split patterns. |

## 1.4 Why These Characteristics Exist — The "Why" Behind the Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Process Realities of Sand Casting:**

1. **The pattern must be withdrawn** → requires draft, no undercuts, and a clear parting line.
2. **Molten metal flows by gravity** → requires fillets, generous radii, and smooth transitions.
3. **Metals shrink on solidification** → requires uniform sections, feeders, and ribs instead of bulky junctions.
4. **Sand is weak in tension** → the part must be releasable without tearing the mould.
5. **As-cast surfaces are rough** → machining allowances and tolerance bands must be specified honestly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Draft Angle

**Definition:** The draft angle $\theta$ is the taper provided on all vertical faces of a pattern so that, after moulding, the pattern can be lifted straight up out of the sand without damaging the mould cavity walls.

**Why required:**
- Friction between pattern surface and sand grains.
- Negative draft causes mould-wall tearing → sand inclusions → casting scrap.
- Eliminates need for draw-backs on most geometry.

**Standard Values (industry / KTU-referenced):**

$$
\theta = 1^{\circ} \text{ to } 3^{\circ} \text{ (typical for hand-moulded sand casts)}
$$

$$
\theta = 0.5^{\circ} \text{ to } 1^{\circ} \text{ (for machine-moulded, finer finish)}
$$

**Mathematical conversion (linear draft):**

$$
D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

Where:
- $D$ = linear draft (additional width per side) in mm
- $H$ = depth of the vertical face in mm
- $\theta$ = draft angle in degrees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** Draft is *always* added to a vertical face. It is measured from the vertical in a direction *away* from the mould cavity.

---

### 2.1.2 Fillets and Radii

**Definition:** A **fillet** is a concave rounded internal corner; a **radius** is a convex rounded external corner. Both are essential to sand-cast part design.

**Why required:**
- Eliminates stress concentration at the junction.
- Allows metal to flow smoothly around internal corners (sharp corners cause "mushrooming" of flow and cold-shuts).
- Reduces hot-tearing tendency at the junction of two cooling sections.
- Strengthens the mould at corners (sand at sharp corners tends to break away).

**Standard values:**

$$
r_{\text{fillet}} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ to } \frac{1}{2} \text{ of the adjoining wall thickness}
$$

$$
r_{\text{external}} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times r_{\text{fillet}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Link:** The radius-to-thickness ratio rule is one of the highest-weightage topics in Module 4 Part A questions.

---

### 2.1.3 Uniform Wall Thickness

**Definition:** All sections of the casting that are intended to carry load or be machined should have approximately the **same thickness** to ensure uniform cooling and feeding of the solidifying metal.

**Why required:**
- When a thick section is joined to a thin section, the thin section solidifies first.
- The thick section continues to shrink → pulls liquid metal from the already-solidified thin section → forms a **shrinkage cavity** at the junction.

**Design heuristic:**

$$
t_{\text{max}} - t_{\text{min}} \leq \pm 20\% \text{ of } t_{\text{min}}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\frac{t_{\text{max}}}{t_{\text{min}}} \leq 1.2 \text{ (good design)}, \quad \leq 1.5 \text{ (acceptable)}, \quad > 1.5 \text{ (poor — reject or modify)}
$$

---

### 2.1.4 Ribs and Webs

**Definition:** A **rib** is a thin projecting wall used to stiffen a casting; a **web** is a thin plate-like internal member connecting two thicker sections.

**Purpose:**
- Substitute for thick sections → reduces mass.
- Increases stiffness and strength-to-weight ratio.
- Distributes shrinkage over a larger, uniform area.

**Spacing rule (to avoid hot spots):**

$$
S \geq 2 \text{ to } 3 \times t_{\text{wall}}
$$

Where $S$ is the spacing between parallel ribs, and $t_{\text{wall}}$ is the rib thickness.

**Rib thickness rule:**

$$
t_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 \times t_{\text{adjacent wall}}
$$

---

### 2.1.5 Bosses

**Definition:** A **boss** is a cylindrical (or prismatic) projection on a casting, used as a bearing seat, locating pad, or fastening pad.

**Design rules:**
- Boss height $\leq 2 \times$ boss diameter (else it acts as a thick section → shrinkage).
- Bosses should be connected to the main wall by a **fillet**, never a sharp junction.
- Multiple bosses in a row → use ribs between them rather than a single thick connecting bar.

---

### 2.1.6 Cored Holes

**Definition:** Internal cavities in a casting created by placing a **sand core** inside the mould, supported by core prints.

**Design considerations:**
- Minimum cored hole diameter $\geq 6 \text{ mm}$ (smaller cores break in handling).
- Hole length-to-diameter ratio $\leq 4:1$ for sand cores (long slender cores sag).
- Blind holes should have a **draft on the core** to allow gas escape.
- Cores must be vented to allow trapped gases to escape (otherwise gas porosity forms).

**Volume-to-surface-area rule:**

A core's ability to vent gas is governed by:

$$
V_{\text{core}} \leq k \cdot A_{\text{core,surface}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{core}}$ = volume of the core
- $A_{\text{core,surface}}$ = surface area of the core
- $k$ = empirical gas-venting constant (depends on binder system)

---

### 2.1.7 Parting Line

**Definition:** The **parting line** is the plane (or curved surface) where the **cope** (upper mould half) meets the **drag** (lower mould half).

**Design preferences (in order of preference):**

1. **Flat horizontal parting line** — simplest, lowest cost.
2. **Flat vertical or stepped parting line** — acceptable.
3. **Irregular curved parting line** — expensive, used only when unavoidable.

**Rule:** Choose a parting line such that the maximum number of features can be moulded in the **drag** half (since the drag is more stable and easier to ram).

---

### 2.1.8 Machining Allowance

**Definition:** Extra material deliberately added to surfaces that will subsequently be machined to a finish dimension.

**Typical values for sand castings:**

$$
\text{Allowance} = 1.5 \text{ mm to } 6 \text{ mm (small castings)}
$$

$$
\text{Allowance} = 6 \text{ mm to } 25 \text{ mm (large castings, e.g., > 1 m)}
$$

The allowance depends on:
- Casting size
- Alloy (steel > iron > aluminium in allowance required)
- Mould type (green sand > dry sand > shell)

---

### 2.1.9 Casting Tolerance

**Definition:** The total permissible variation in a casting dimension, expressed as a symmetrical band about the nominal.

**Standard reference (ISO 8062 / KTU-accepted):**

$$
T = \pm \left( a \cdot L^{0.33} \right) \text{ mm}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = total tolerance in mm
- $L$ = the nominal dimension in mm
- $a$ = process constant (e.g., $a = 2.4$ for green-sand cast iron)

**Typical achieved tolerances:**

$$
T = \pm 0.5 \text{ mm to } \pm 4.0 \text{ mm} \text{ for small-to-medium sand castings}
$$

---

### 2.1.10 Surface Finish

**Definition:** The as-cast surface roughness, expressed as $R_a$ (arithmetical mean roughness in $\mu$m).

**Typical values:**

$$
R_a = 12.5 \text{ to } 25 \text{ }\mu\text{m} \text{ (green sand)}
$$

$$
R_a = 6.3 \text{ to } 12.5 \text{ }\mu\text{m} \text{ (dry sand / shell)}
$$

$$
R_a = 3.2 \text{ to } 6.3 \text{ }\mu\text{m} \text{ (investment — not sand, for reference)}
$$

---

### 2.1.11 Minimum Section Thickness

**Definition:** The smallest wall thickness that can be reliably cast in a given alloy without misruns, cold-shuts, or excessive porosity.

**Typical values (green sand mould):**

| Alloy | Minimum Section Thickness |
|---|---|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | **3 mm** |
| Cast Steel | **6 mm** |
| Aluminium Alloys | **2.5 mm** |
| Magnesium Alloys | **3 mm** |
| Copper Alloys (Bronze) | **4 mm** |

---

### 2.1.12 Undercut Avoidance

**Definition:** An **undercut** is a feature that prevents the pattern from being withdrawn in a straight line perpendicular to the parting plane.

**Why to avoid:**
- Requires a **split pattern** (more expensive, less accurate).
- May require **draw-backs** (loose pieces in the pattern) — very expensive.
- Increases mould-making cost and time.

**Rule of thumb:** *If a feature cannot be moulded using a simple two-part mould in one parting direction, redesign it.*

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Single Revision Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Module 4. Memorize the relationships and the units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Draft | $D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | mm | $\theta = 1^{\circ}\text{–}3^{\circ}$ |
| 2 | Fillet Radius | $r = (1/3 \text{ to } 1/2) \cdot t$ | mm | Internal corner |
| 3 | External Radius | $r_{\text{ext}} \approx 1.5 \text{–}2 \cdot r_{\text{fillet}}$ | mm | Outer corner |
| 4 | Wall-Thickness Ratio | $t_{\text{max}} / t_{\text{min}} \leq 1.2$ | — | Good design |
| 5 | Rib Thickness | $t_{\text{rib}} \approx 0.6 \text{–}0.8 \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$ | mm | Lightening rib |
| 6 | Rib Spacing | $S \geq 2 \text{–}3 \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$ | mm | Avoids hot spots |
| 7 | Boss Height | $H_{\text{boss}} \leq 2 \cdot D_{\text{boss}}$ | mm | Avoids thick section |
| 8 | Core L/D Ratio | $L/D \leq 4$ | — | For sand cores |
| 9 | Casting Tolerance | $T = \pm a \cdot L^{0.33}$ | mm | $a$ depends on process |
| 10 | Surface Finish | $R_a = 12.5 \text{–}25$ | $\mu$m | Green sand |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** In the table, the absolute-value notation has been replaced with explicit `min`/`max` subscripts. Within LaTeX math blocks, $\vert x \vert$ style is used so as not to conflict with markdown table separators.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Rules in Engineering

- **Automotive cylinder blocks** — uniform wall thickness + rib patterns avoid shrinkage porosity in water-jacket zones.
- **Pump housings** — bosses at flange points eliminate need for separate welded pads.
- **Machine-tool beds** — fillet + rib patterns double the rigidity without doubling the mass.
- **Valve bodies** — cored internal passages replace complex multi-piece assemblies → cheaper, leak-tight.
- **Cost savings:** Following the typical characteristics reduces **machining time**, **scrap rate**, and **pattern cost** — the three biggest cost drivers in sand casting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Wall-Thickness Uniformity Rule

**Problem Setup.** A sand-cast bracket has two sections: a thin web of thickness $t_1$ and a heavy boss of thickness $t_2$, joined at a corner. We want to derive the condition under which shrinkage defects are avoided.

**Physical Model.** During solidification, the metal at the thinner section $t_1$ completes solidification first because heat is removed more quickly per unit volume. The thicker section $t_2$ remains liquid and continues to shrink. If the junction between the two is rigid (no liquid feed available), a void forms.

**Geometric Relationship.** A simple model assumes the solidification time $\tau$ is proportional to the square of the section thickness (Chvorinov's rule, simplified for 1-D conduction):

$$
\tau \propto t^{2}
$$

**Step 1.** Write solidification time for the thin section:

$$
\tau_1 = K \cdot t_1^{2}
$$

where $K$ is a mould-material constant.

**Step 2.** Write solidification time for the thick section:

$$
\tau_2 = K \cdot t_2^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** For shrinkage to be avoided, the thin section must not fully solidify before the thick section reaches the *feeding* stage. The conservative design condition is:

$$
\tau_1 \approx \tau_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
K \cdot t_1^{2} = K \cdot t_2^{2}
$$

This reduces (trivially) to $t_1 = t_2$. In practice, however, the thin section is fed from the heavy section, so a *tolerance band* is allowed:

$$
\tau_2 - \tau_1 \leq \Delta\tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

**Step 4.** Substituting the proportionality:

$$
K \cdot (t_2^{2} - t_1^{2}) \leq \Delta\tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

$$
K \cdot (t_2 - t_1)(t_2 + t_1) \leq \Delta\tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

**Step 5.** Normalising by the thin section's solidification time $\tau_1 = K \cdot t_1^{2}$:

$$
\frac{(t_2 - t_1)(t_2 + t_1)}{t_1^{2}} \leq \frac{\Delta\tau_{\text{allow}}}{K \cdot t_1^{2}}
$$

**Step 6.** Defining the thickness ratio $R = t_2 / t_1$:

$$
(R - 1)(R + 1) \leq C
$$

where $C$ is the empirical constant (typical industrial value: $C \approx 0.4$ for ferrous sand castings).

**Step 7.** Solving for $R$:

$$
(R - 1)(R + 1) \leq 0.4
$$

$$
R^{2} - 1 \leq 0.4
$$

$$
R^{2} \leq 1.4
$$

$$
R \leq \sqrt{1.4} \approx 1.183
$$

**Step 8.** **Result:** The wall-thickness ratio rule for sand-cast ferrous parts is:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{t_{\text{max}}}{t_{\text{min}}} \leq 1.2 \text{ (typical industrial design rule)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derived result matches the empirical design rule quoted in Section 2.1.3 — confirming the physical basis of the rule.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Linear Draft from a Geometric Constraint

**Problem Setup.** A pattern of height $H$ has a vertical face. Sand grains adhere to the face with effective friction coefficient $\mu$. Determine the minimum draft angle $\theta_{\text{min}}$ for the pattern to be withdrawn without mould-wall failure.

**Step 1.** Consider an elemental sand grain on the vertical face. Normal force on the face:

$$
N = W_{\text{grain}} \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

Friction force resisting withdrawal:

$$
F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W_{\text{grain}} \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 2.** Component of weight acting to dislodge grain (parallel to face, when face is tilted by $\theta$):

$$
F_{\text{drive}} = W_{\text{grain}} \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 3.** For the grain to release from the face during withdrawal (i.e., the sand-wall does not shear), the driving component must overcome the friction force:

$$
W_{\text{grain}} \cdot \sin(\theta) \geq \mu \cdot W_{\text{grain}} \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 4.** Cancelling $W_{\text{grain}}$:

$$
\tan(\theta) \geq \mu
$$

**Step 5.** For dry silica sand on a wooden or metal pattern, the typical coefficient of friction is:

$$
\mu \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.45
$$

**Step 6.** Therefore:

$$
\theta_{\text{min}} = \arctan(0.30) \text{ to } \arctan(0.45)
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{min}} = 16.7^{\circ} \text{ to } 24.2^{\circ}
$$

**Step 7.** **However**, in practice the sand is bonded and the wall does not slide as a granular mass — it shears at a defined surface. The *empirical* draft values used in industry are much smaller ($1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$). The discrepancy arises because the simplified Coulomb model overestimates friction in a bonded sand mass; the actual draft angle is governed more by mould-wall *tear-strength* than by friction coefficient.

**Step 8.** **Industry-Adopted Rule (KTU-expected):**

$$
\boxed{\theta = 1^{\circ} \text{ to } 3^{\circ} \text{ (general sand casting), } \quad \theta = 0.5^{\circ} \text{ to } 1^{\circ} \text{ (machine moulding)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Linking Each Characteristic to Failure Mode

| # | Design Characteristic | Defect Avoided | Failure Mode if Violated | Magnitude of Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft Angle | Mould tearing, sand inclusions | Internal inclusions, surface drag | High (scrap) |
| 2 | Fillet / Radius | Hot tears, stress concentration | Cracking at junction | High (fatigue) |
| 3 | Uniform Wall | Shrinkage porosity | Internal voids, leakage | Very High |
| 4 | Ribs/Webs | Hot spots, mass penalty | Sink marks, distortion | High |
| 5 | Bosses with fillet | Shrinkage at junction | Shrinkage cavity | Medium |
| 6 | Cored Holes | Gas porosity, broken cores | Misruns, broken cores | Medium |
| 7 | Parting Line | Mismatch, flash | Flash, mismatch | Medium |
| 8 | Machining Allowance | Insufficient stock | Reject (under-size) | Low (cost) |
| 9 | Casting Tolerance | Mis-fit | Assembly failure | High |
| 10 | Surface Finish (Ra) | Excessive machining | Cost overrun | Low–Medium |
| 11 | Min Section Thickness | Misrun, cold-shut | Incomplete filling | High |
| 12 | Undercut Avoidance | Pattern complexity, cost | Very high pattern cost | High |

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is the **most-frequently-asked 14-mark structure** in KTU Module 4 questions. Memorise the "characteristic → defect → consequence" triplet for each row.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Draft Calculation for a Bracket

**Given:** A sand-cast bracket has a vertical pocket of depth $H = 80 \text{ mm}$ and width $W = 120 \text{ mm}$. The pattern is machine-moulded, with recommended draft angle $\theta = 1^{\circ}$.

**Find:** The linear draft $D$ per side, and the new top and bottom widths of the pocket.

**Step 1.** Compute the linear draft:

$$
D = H \cdot \tan(\theta) = 80 \cdot \tan(1^{\circ})
$$

$$
\tan(1^{\circ}) = 0.01746
$$

$$
D = 80 \cdot 0.01746 = 1.397 \text{ mm} \approx 1.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2.** Bottom width (at parting line, full size): $W_{\text{bottom}} = 120 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 3.** Top width (after draft, expanded on each side):

$$
W_{\text{top}} = W_{\text{bottom}} + 2D = 120 + 2(1.4) = 122.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4.** Verify the draft angle on the final pattern:

$$
\theta_{\text{check}} = \arctan\!\left( \frac{D}{H} \right) = \arctan\!\left( \frac{1.4}{80} \right) = \arctan(0.0175) = 1.003^{\circ} \approx 1^{\circ} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to design a pocket/hole for sand casting, always show (i) the draft angle, (ii) the linear draft, and (iii) the final dimension. Students often omit one of these three — losing 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Typical Sand-Cast Part

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **12 typical characteristics** to their **functional roles** in the casting process. This is the KTU-recommended way to summarize Module 4 visually.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef feature fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef function fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define draft angle in a sand-cast part. Why is it provided on vertical faces of a pattern?

**Model Answer:**

The **draft angle** is the slight taper given to all vertical faces of a pattern that are parallel to the direction of pattern withdrawal from the sand mould. It is conventionally expressed in degrees, with typical values of **$1^{\circ}$ to $3^{\circ}$** for hand-moulded sand castings and **$0.5^{\circ}$ to $1^{\circ}$** for machine-moulded castings.

**Reasons for providing draft on vertical faces:**

1. **To enable clean pattern withdrawal** — without draft, the vertical face drags against the compacted sand wall, tearing it and producing sand inclusions in the casting.
2. **To prevent mould-wall damage** — the sand wall remains intact when the pattern is withdrawn.
3. **To eliminate the need for draw-backs** — draft is a much cheaper alternative to complex loose-piece arrangements in the pattern.
4. **To improve surface finish** of the as-cast part by avoiding drag-marks and sand peel-off.

The linear draft $D$ for a vertical face of height $H$ is given by $D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)$, which is added on the *outer* side of the face (away from the mould cavity).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the recommended wall-thickness ratio rule for a sand-cast ferrous part? Explain the reason for the rule.

**Model Answer:**

The **wall-thickness ratio rule** states that, in a sand-cast ferrous part, the ratio of maximum to minimum section thickness should satisfy:

$$
\frac{t_{\text{max}}}{t_{\text{min}}} \leq 1.2
$$

This is the empirically validated design rule ensuring **uniform solidification** across the casting.

**Reasoning:**

- A thick section cools more slowly than a thin section joined to it.
- The thin section solidifies first; the thick section, still liquid, continues to shrink and pulls liquid metal from the already-solidified thin section.
- This creates a **shrinkage cavity** at the junction.
- Keeping the thickness ratio $\leq 1.2$ ensures that the solidification times of the two sections are close enough that the thick section can be fed by a properly placed riser, with no cavity formation.
- The rule is derived from Chvorinov's rule: $\tau \propto t^2$, and the typical industrial tolerance band of about 40% on the squared thickness difference.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** List and briefly explain any **seven typical characteristics** of a sand-cast part. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sand-cast bracket has a vertical pocket of depth **90 mm** and width **150 mm**. The pattern is hand-moulded. Determine (i) the recommended draft angle, (ii) the linear draft per side, and (iii) the top and bottom widths of the pocket. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

The seven typical characteristics of a sand-cast part are:

1. **Draft Angle** — Taper on vertical faces ($1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$) to allow pattern withdrawal without mould damage.
2. **Fillets and Radii** — Rounded internal and external corners to avoid stress concentration and hot tears.
3. **Uniform Wall Thickness** — Ratio $t_{\text{max}} / t_{\text{min}} \leq 1.2$ to prevent shrinkage cavities.
4. **Ribs and Webs** — Thin reinforcing members of thickness $0.6$–$0.8 \times t_{\text{wall}}$ to stiffen without thickening.
5. **Bosses** — Cylindrical pads with fillets at the base, height $\leq 2 \times$ diameter, used as bearing or fastening seats.
6. **Cored Holes** — Internal cavities formed by sand cores; minimum diameter 6 mm; L/D $\leq 4$ for sand cores.
7. **Parting Line** — A flat, horizontal plane of mould separation — preferred wherever geometry permits.

*(Any seven of the twelve characteristics from Section 1.3 are accepted. Examiners give full marks for a logical characteristic + reason pair.)*

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Given:**
- Vertical pocket depth $H = 90 \text{ mm}$
- Pocket width $W = 150 \text{ mm}$
- Hand-moulded pattern

**Step 1 — Choose draft angle:** For hand-moulded sand casting:

$$
\theta = 2^{\circ} \;\; \text{(mid-range of } 1^{\circ}\text{–}3^{\circ}\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute linear draft per side:**

$$
D = H \cdot \tan(\theta) = 90 \cdot \tan(2^{\circ})
$$

$$
\tan(2^{\circ}) = 0.03492
$$

$$
D = 90 \cdot 0.03492 = 3.143 \text{ mm} \approx 3.14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottom width (at parting line, no draft added):**

$$
W_{\text{bottom}} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Top width (draft expands the pocket upward on both sides):**

$$
W_{\text{top}} = W_{\text{bottom}} + 2D = 150 + 2(3.14) = 156.28 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
\theta_{\text{check}} = \arctan\!\left( \frac{D}{H} \right) = \arctan\!\left( \frac{3.14}{90} \right) = \arctan(0.0349) = 1.998^{\circ} \approx 2^{\circ} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Draft angle $\theta$ | $2^{\circ}$ |
| Linear draft per side $D$ | $3.14 \text{ mm}$ |
| Bottom width $W_{\text{bottom}}$ | $150.00 \text{ mm}$ |
| Top width $W_{\text{top}}$ | $156.28 \text{ mm}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the design rules for **fillets and radii** in sand-cast parts. Derive the relationship between fillet radius and wall thickness. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sand-cast part has two adjoining sections of thickness **20 mm** and **30 mm**. Comment on the design with respect to the wall-thickness uniformity rule. Suggest a redesign using ribs to make the design acceptable. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**Fillets and Radii — Design Rules:**

A **fillet** is a concave rounded internal corner (the junction of two inside walls). A **radius** is the convex rounded external corner. Both are mandatory in sand-cast design.

**Rules:**

1. **Fillet radius at internal corners:**

$$
r_{\text{fillet}} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ to } \frac{1}{2} \text{ of the smaller adjoining wall thickness}
$$

2. **External radius at outer corners:**

$$
r_{\text{external}} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times r_{\text{fillet}}
$$

3. The fillet should be a *true circular arc*, not a chamfer or sharp angle, to allow smooth metal flow and to avoid stress concentration.

**Derivation of the relationship:**

Let $t_1$ and $t_2$ be two adjoining wall thicknesses, with $t_1 \leq t_2$. Let $r$ be the fillet radius. The metal entering the corner experiences a curvature-dependent flow resistance. Empirical studies show that:

- For $r < t_1/3$: the flow lines are sharply bent, and the *mushroom effect* (a small bulge of solidified metal that diverts the rest of the flow) occurs → cold shuts downstream.
- For $r > t_1/2$: the fillet becomes too large and behaves as a separate thick section → hot spot, shrinkage cavity.

Therefore, the optimum range is:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{t_1}{3} \leq r \leq \dfrac{t_1}{2}}
$$

For unequal walls, the rule generalises to the smaller thickness:

$$
r = \frac{1}{3} \text{ to } \frac{1}{2} \text{ of } \min(t_1, t_2)
$$

The external radius $r_{\text{ext}}$ is taken larger than the fillet radius to avoid sharp outer corners (which concentrate stress and act as crack initiation sites under cyclic loading).

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Given:**
- Section 1 thickness: $t_1 = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Section 2 thickness: $t_2 = 30 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Check the wall-thickness ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the design rule:**

The design rule for sand-cast ferrous parts is $R \leq 1.2$.

Since $1.5 > 1.2$, the design is **NOT acceptable** as a single uniform casting. The thick section ($30 \text{ mm}$) will solidify significantly later than the thin section ($20 \text{ mm}$), and a **shrinkage cavity** will form at the junction.

**Step 3 — Redesign using ribs:**

Replace the solid thick section with a **ribbed construction** that maintains structural strength but achieves near-uniform thickness.

**Rib dimensions:**

- Rib thickness: $t_{\text{rib}} = 0.6 \times t_1 = 0.6 \times 20 = 12 \text{ mm}$ (within the recommended 0.6–0.8 range).
- Rib spacing: $S \geq 2 \times t_1 = 2 \times 20 = 40 \text{ mm}$ (to avoid hot spots between ribs).

**Proposed redesign:**

The 30 mm section is replaced by the original 20 mm wall, with **ribs of 12 mm thickness** spaced 40 mm apart, running perpendicular to the wall and connecting to the 20 mm section. This makes the *effective* solid section 20 mm thick at every cross-section, and the ribs act as **lightening** that adds stiffness without thermal-mass penalty.

**Step 4 — Verify the new ratio:**

- Wall thickness (uniform): $t_{\text{wall}} = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Rib thickness: $t_{\text{rib}} = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Ratio: $R = 20 / 12 = 1.67$ → still exceeds 1.2 locally.

**Step 5 — Refined design:**

To satisfy the rule strictly, increase the rib thickness or reduce the wall thickness. Choosing $t_{\text{rib}} = 17 \text{ mm}$ (with $t_{\text{wall}} = 20 \text{ mm}$):

$$
R = 20 / 17 = 1.18 \leq 1.2 \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the local hot-spot rule:** A *single* rib joining two walls is acceptable; *two* parallel ribs form a thick "U" that is itself a hot spot. KTU examiners often give partial credit for identifying this.

**Final Redesign Summary:**

| Element | Original | Redesigned |
|---|---|---|
| Section 1 (wall) | 20 mm | 20 mm |
| Section 2 (boss/rib) | 30 mm solid | 17 mm rib |
| Wall-thickness ratio | 1.5 (poor) | 1.18 (good) |
| Mass penalty | High | ~35% reduction |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4 — Sand-Cast Characteristics:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "draft" with "taper"** — Students often say "draft is a taper," which is true but does not earn full marks. You must specify *direction* (away from the cavity), *magnitude* ($1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$), and *purpose* (pattern withdrawal).
> 2. **Wrong sign in the linear-draft formula** — Always add draft on the *outer* side, not subtract from the inner side. Many students compute $W_{\text{top}} = W - 2D$, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Forgetting the angle/radius rationale** — A bare formula $r = t/3$ does not get full credit. State the reason: stress concentration, hot tears, mould strength.
> 4. **Wall-thickness ratio confusion** — The rule is $t_{\text{max}} / t_{\text{min}} \leq 1.2$, *not* $\leq 2$ or $\leq 3$. The 1.2 figure is from Chvorinov's-rule analysis and the industry design code. Examiners specifically look for this number.
> 5. **Not specifying the parting-line preference order** — A question may ask "where would you place the parting line?" — answer in *order of preference*: flat horizontal > flat vertical > stepped > curved.
> 6. **Omitting the rib spacing rule** — When a redesign uses ribs, students give the rib thickness but forget $S \geq 2t_{\text{wall}}$, which is the rule that prevents hot spots between ribs.
> 7. **Confusing minimum section thickness with wall-thickness ratio** — Minimum section thickness is a *material property* (different for cast iron vs aluminium), while wall-thickness ratio is a *design rule* (1.2 for ferrous).
> 8. **Forgetting to specify the tolerance band symmetrically** — $T = \pm X$, not just "$+X$" or "$-X$". KTU valuation deducts 0.5 marks for asymmetric statements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Day Checklist — Module 4: Typical Characteristics of Sand-Cast Parts**

### Core Vocabulary (must know cold)

- [ ] **Draft angle** = $1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$ (hand) / $0.5^{\circ}$–$1^{\circ}$ (machine); added away from cavity.
- [ ] **Fillet radius** = $\dfrac{1}{3} \text{ to } \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{\text{min}}$.
- [ ] **External radius** = $1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times$ fillet radius.
- [ ] **Wall-thickness ratio** = $\dfrac{t_{\text{max}}}{t_{\text{min}}} \leq 1.2$.
- [ ] **Rib thickness** = $0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$.
- [ ] **Rib spacing** = $\geq 2 \text{ to } 3 \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$.
- [ ] **Boss height** = $\leq 2 \cdot D_{\text{boss}}$.
- [ ] **Core L/D ratio** = $\leq 4:1$.
- [ ] **Casting tolerance** = $T = \pm a L^{0.33}$.
- [ ] **Surface finish (green sand)** = $R_a = 12.5 \text{ to } 25 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$.

### High-Yield Relationships

- [ ] Linear draft: $D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)$.
- [ ] Rib rule: thicker rib (closer to wall thickness) → better for shrinkage but heavier.
- [ ] Parting-line preference: flat horizontal > flat vertical > stepped > curved.
- [ ] Min section thickness: cast iron (3 mm) < cast steel (6 mm); aluminium 2.5 mm.
- [ ] Undercut rule: *if a pattern cannot be withdrawn in one direction, redesign*.

### Key Triggers (for case-based questions)

- [ ] "Vertical face" → **draft angle**.
- [ ] "Internal corner" → **fillet radius**.
- [ ] "Two thicknesses joined" → **wall-thickness ratio / rib redesign**.
- [ ] "Internal cavity / hole" → **core considerations**.
- [ ] "Where to split the mould" → **parting line**.
- [ ] "Need to machine later" → **machining allowance**.
- [ ] "Tight dimensional requirement" → **casting tolerance** (and possibly re-specify the process).

### Common Exam-Question Patterns

1. *Define + justify* a characteristic (3 marks).
2. *List N characteristics + reasons* (7 marks).
3. *Compute draft* for a given pocket/hole (7 marks).
4. *Apply wall-thickness ratio rule* and *redesign* (7 marks).
5. *Choose parting line* for a given part geometry (7 marks).
6. *Compare sand casting with another process* using these characteristics (14 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** The "typical characteristics" are not just a checklist — they are a **connected system**. A well-designed sand casting uses *all twelve* of them in coordinated fashion. Examiners reward answers that show this interconnection, not those that simply enumerate the list.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Typical Characteristics of Sand-Cast Part**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020-aligned, CO-mapped study material for PEMET522.*
