# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Explain the importance of draft angle in sand casting. State typical draft values for external and internal surfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Draft angle is the **taper provided on the vertical walls of a pattern** to enable its easy withdrawal from the sand mold without damaging the mold cavity.

- **External surfaces**: $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (pattern lifts easily)
- **Internal surfaces**: $\alpha = 2°$ to $5°$ (cores require more draft for removal)
- **Minimum draft for intricate patterns**: Up to $5°$

**Significance:** Without draft, the pattern rubs against sand walls, causing **mold erosion, dimensional inaccuracy, and surface defects**. Draft also accounts for **mold wall expansion** during pouring.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define K-factor. State the typical range of K-factor for mild steel and aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance of the **neutral axis** from the inner surface of the sheet to the **material thickness**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

- **Aluminum, soft brass**: $K = 0.33$
- **Mild steel**: $K = 0.40$ to $0.45$
- **Stainless steel, hard brass**: $K = 0.45$ to $0.50$

**Significance:** K-factor is essential for calculating **bend allowance** and predicting **flat blank length** before bending. It represents the location within the sheet thickness where neither stretching nor compression occurs during bending.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Casting + Forging Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A cast iron bracket has a uniform wall thickness of 25 mm. A boss of 40 mm diameter is to be added at one location for a bearing housing. As a design engineer, suggest modifications to:
1. Avoid shrinkage porosity at the boss-wall junction
2. Ensure easy pattern withdrawal
3. Maintain adequate strength

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Section thickness ratio check:**
The boss (40 mm) is essentially a thick cylinder attached to a 25 mm wall. Without modification, the ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6
$$

This is borderline. To make it safe, **reduce boss diameter** to 32–35 mm or add a **transition ring**.

**Step 2 — Add fillet radius:**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4} = \dfrac{25 + 40}{4} = 16.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r = 16$ mm at the boss-wall junction. This **reduces stress concentration** and **eliminates hot spot** at the junction, preventing shrinkage porosity.

**Step 3 — Add draft angle:**
For external boss surface: $\alpha = 2°$ (external)
For internal bore: $\alpha = 3°$ (internal)

**Step 4 — Boss thickness control:**
Boss wall thickness = $1.5 \times 25 = 37.5$ mm. Since boss OD is 40 mm, this means inner bore = 40 - 2(37.5) = negative. **Modify boss design**: Use a boss OD of 60 mm so that wall thickness = 10 mm (the inner bore of bearing fits) and $t_{boss} = 1.5 \times t_{wall} = 37.5$ mm is met by adjusting boss height/diameter.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Modification | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Fillet radius at junction | 16 mm | Reduce stress + hot spot |
| Section ratio | $\leq 2$ (achieved by reducing boss) | Avoid shrinkage |
| External draft | $2°$ | Pattern removal |
| Internal draft | $3°$ | Core removal |
| Rib at junction (if needed) | 17.5 mm thick | Strengthen without hot spot |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
Explain the key design recommendations for **drop forging** of a connecting rod-shaped component with maximum width $W = 60$ mm and depth $h = 25$ mm. Recommend suitable values for fillet radii, web thickness, and draft angle.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draft angle:**
For impression die forging: $\alpha = 5°$ to $7°$. Adopt $\alpha = 7°$ for safety.

**Step 2 — External fillet radius:**

$$
r_e \geq 0.1 \, h = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_e = 3$ mm.

**Step 3 — Internal fillet radius:**

$$
r_i \geq 0.15 \, h = 0.15 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_i = 4$ mm.

**Step 4 — Web thickness:**

$$
t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W = 0.15 \times 60 = 9 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{web} = 10$ mm.

**Step 5 — Rib thickness:**

$$
t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{rib} = 6$ mm.

**Step 6 — Maximum rib height:**

$$
h_{rib,max} = 5 \times t_{rib} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Parting line:**
Choose a **straight plane** through the **widest cross-section** ($W = 60$ mm plane) for simple die construction.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | $7°$ | Die separation |
| External fillet | $3$ mm | Material flow into corner |
| Internal fillet | $4$ mm | Die stress relief |
| Web thickness | $10$ mm | Avoid folds |
| Rib thickness | $6$ mm | Full die fill |
| Max rib height | $30$ mm | Avoid fold/lap |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding + Sheet Metal Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A fillet weld of leg size $s = 12$ mm is to be deposited along a joint of length $L = 250$ mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is $\tau_{allow} = 100$ MPa. Determine:
1. The throat thickness
2. The load-carrying capacity of the weld
3. The minimum recommended leg size if the thinner plate is 14 mm thick

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t = 0.707 \, s = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.484 \text{ mm} \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective shear area:**

$$
A = t \times L = 8.484 \times 250 = 2121 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Load capacity:**

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 100 \times 2121 = 212{,}100 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 212.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum recommended leg size:**

$$
s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min} = 0.7 \times 14 = 9.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the actual leg size $s = 12$ mm $> s_{min} = 9.8$ mm, the design is **acceptable**.

**Step 5 — Maximum recommended leg size check:**

For plate thickness 14 mm, the maximum leg size for full strength is $s_{max} \approx t_{plate} = 14$ mm. Since $s = 12$ mm $< 14$ mm, the weld is **within safe limits**.

**Step 6 — Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Throat thickness | 8.5 mm | ✓ |
| Weld area | 2121 mm² | ✓ |
| Load capacity | 212.1 kN | ✓ |
| Min leg required | 9.8 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |
| Max leg allowed | 14 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is to be bent at $90°$ with an inside bend radius of $R = 5$ mm. Calculate:
1. The bend allowance (use $K = 0.45$)
2. The flat blank length if each straight leg is 60 mm
3. The minimum recommended edge distance for a hole pierced 4 mm from the bend

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (5 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times (5 + 0.9) = 1.5708 \times 5.9
$$

$$
\boxed{BA = 9.268 \text{ mm} \approx 9.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Flat blank length:**

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA = 60 + 60 + 9.27
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{blank} = 129.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum edge distance for hole:**

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R = 1.5 \times 2 + 5 = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d_{edge} = 8$ mm from bend line to hole center.

**Step 5 — Verification of bend radius:**

For mild steel, $R_{min} \approx 2t = 2 \times 2 = 4$ mm.
Since $R = 5$ mm $> R_{min} = 4$ mm, the bend is **safe**.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Bend allowance | 9.27 mm | — |
| Blank length | 129.27 mm | — |
| Min edge distance | 8 mm | ✓ |
| Min bend radius | 4 mm (reqd) | ✓ (5 mm used) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Design Recommendations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-factor and Neutral Axis position.** K-factor is dimensionless; neutral axis position = $K \times t$ (in mm). Students writing $K = 0.45$ mm lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.707 in fillet weld throat.** Writing $t = s$ instead of $t = 0.707s$ loses 2 marks. Always show the derivation.
>
> 3. **Using inside radius $R$ instead of neutral radius $(R + Kt)$ in bend allowance.** This is the #1 error in sheet metal problems. Always write $(R + Kt)$ explicitly.
>
> 4. **Not specifying units in design recommendations.** "Add fillet radius of 5" is incomplete. "Add fillet radius of **5 mm** to reduce stress concentration" scores full marks.
>
> 5. **Giving vague justifications.** "It will be good for manufacturing" scores 0. "It eliminates hot spot and prevents shrinkage porosity" scores 2/2.
>
> 6. **Mixing up draft angles for casting vs forging.** Casting: $1°$ to $5°$. Forging: $5°$ to $7°$. Many students write $1°$ for forging, which is wrong.
>
> 7. **Ignoring minimum weld leg size formula.** $s_{min} = 0.7 t_{min}$ is mandatory to mention whenever weld design is asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Design Recommendations"**

- [x] **Casting — Section Ratio**: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ (always enforce uniform cooling)
- [x] **Casting — Fillet**: $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ (no sharp internal corners)
- [x] **Casting — Draft**: $1°$ to $3°$ external, $2°$ to $5°$ internal
- [x] **Casting — Rib**: $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ (avoid hot spots)
- [x] **Casting — Boss**: $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ (strength + machining allowance)
- [x] **Forging — Draft**: $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die)
- [x] **Forging — External Fillet**: $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Internal Fillet**: $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Web**: $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$
- [x] **Forging — Rib**: $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$
- [x] **Forging — Parting Line**: straight, through widest cross-section
- [x] **Welding — Throat**: $t = 0.707 \, s$ (single most important formula)
- [x] **Welding — Load**: $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$
- [x] **Welding — Min Leg**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$
- [x] **Welding — Avoid**: weld intersections and high-stress zones
- [x] **Sheet Metal — K-Factor**: Al = 0.33, MS = 0.45, SS = 0.50
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Bend Allowance**: $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \, \theta \, (R + Kt)$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Min Bend Radius**: $R_{min} = 1t$ to $3t$ (material dependent)
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Edge Distance**: $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Hole Pitch**: $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize

| Value | Meaning | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0.707** | Fillet weld throat factor | $t = 0.707s$ |
| **0.7** | Weld min leg factor | $s_{min} = 0.7t$ |
| **0.1** | Forging external fillet factor | $r_e = 0.1h$ |
| **0.15** | Forging internal fillet / web factor | $r_i = 0.15h$, $t_{web} = 0.15W$ |
| **2** | Max section ratio (casting) | $t_2/t_1 \leq 2$ |
| **¼** | Casting fillet factor | $r = (t_1+t_2)/4$ |
| **0.7** | Casting rib factor | $t_{rib} = 0.7t_{wall}$ |
| **1.5** | Casting boss factor | $t_{boss} = 1.5t_{wall}$ |
| **5** | Forging max rib height factor | $h_{rib} \leq 5t_{rib}$ |

### One-Line Definition Recall (for 2-mark definitions)

- **DFC** = Design for Casting — guidelines for uniform walls, fillets, draft, ribs
- **DFF** = Design for Forging — guidelines for material flow, die fill, draft, radii
- **DFW** = Design for Welding — guidelines for joint type, weld size, distortion control
- **DFSM** = Design for Sheet Metal — guidelines for bending, K-factor, blank size

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Casting: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Die Casting: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Casting (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> *Die casting is a permanent mold casting process in which molten metal is forced under high pressure (typically **7 to 350 MPa**) into a precisely machined, reusable steel mold cavity (called a "die"). The metal solidifies rapidly under sustained pressure, and the part is ejected, producing net-shape or near-net-shape components with excellent dimensional accuracy, smooth surface finish, and minimal secondary machining.*

The process is governed by the principle of **rapid heat extraction** through metallic dies, which enables production cycle times typically between **30 seconds to 2 minutes** per part, making it one of the highest-rate net-shape forming processes for non-ferrous alloys.

### Key Defining Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Pressure Range:** **7 – 350 MPa** (1000 – 50,000 psi) injection pressure
> - **Cycle Time:** **30 s – 2 min** per shot
> - **Tolerance Capability:** ± **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm** for small castings
> - **Surface Finish:** **Ra 0.8 – 3.2 μm** (as-cast)
> - **Minimum Wall Thickness:** **0.6 mm – 3 mm** (depending on alloy)
> - **Most Common Alloys:** **Aluminum, Zinc, Magnesium, and Copper alloys**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal Injection Syringe"

Imagine a giant **medical syringe** filled with molten chocolate (instead of medicine). When you press the plunger hard, the liquid chocolate squirts into a complex chocolate mold (say, a teddy bear shape). The chocolate hardens inside because the metal mold is cold, you open the mold, the teddy bear pops out, and the mold is ready to make the next one.

**Die casting works exactly like this:**
- **Syringe plunger** → **Plunger / Injection Piston**
- **Molten chocolate** → **Molten metal (Al, Zn, Mg, Cu)**
- **Teddy bear mold** → **Steel Die (two halves: Cover & Ejector)**
- **Hardening** → **Rapid solidification due to high thermal conductivity of steel die**
- **Teddy bear pops out** → **Ejection via ejector pins**
- **Mold reusable** → **Dies last from 100,000 to over 1,000,000 shots**

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Die" and not "Mold"?** In KTU syllabus terminology, **"die"** specifically refers to the hardened steel tool (because the metal is *forced* to *die* into the shape), whereas **"mold"** is the broader term used for sand, plaster, or ceramic tooling. This distinction is a **frequently asked 2-mark question** in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

- **Invented:** **1849** by **Charles Babbage** (for printing press type) and Sturges (for casting).
- **Modern Era:** **1907** — H.H. Doehler developed the first commercially viable die casting machine.
- **Current Status:** Produces billions of components annually — from automotive transmission housings to laptop chassis and toy cars (e.g., **Matchbox, Hot Wheels**).
- **Global Market:** The die casting industry is dominated by **China, India, Germany, USA, and Japan**, with the **automotive sector** consuming over **60%** of all die cast parts globally.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Die Casting Processes

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies die casting into **two principal types** based on the melting equipment configuration:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Feature | **Hot-Chamber Die Casting** | **Cold-Chamber Die Casting** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Melting Pot Location** | Submerged in molten metal bath | External furnace → ladle transfer |
> | **Metal** | Low-melting (Zn, Sn, Pb, Mg) | High-melting (Al, Cu, brass) |
> | **Melting Temp** | **400 – 500 °C** | **600 – 900 °C** |
> | **Cycle Time** | Very fast (seconds) | Slower (1–2 min) |
> | **Pressure** | **7 – 35 MPa** | **30 – 350 MPa** |
> | **Die Life** | Longer (cooler operation) | Shorter (thermal shock) |
> | **Production Rate** | Up to **1000+ shots/hr** | **50 – 200 shots/hr** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Stages of the Die Casting Cycle

The die casting process is a **cyclic, semi-continuous operation** governed by a precise sequence:

### Stage 1: **Die Closing & Clamping**
- The **fixed half (cover die)** and **movable half (ejector die)** are clamped shut by a hydraulic or mechanical toggle system.
- **Clamping force (F_clamp)** must exceed the **injection force (F_inj)** by a safety factor (typically **1.2 to 1.5**) to prevent die flashing (blow-out).

### Stage 2: **Metal Injection (Fill Phase)**
- Molten metal is forced through a **gate**, then through **runners** and **gates/over-flow wells** into the die cavity.
- The injection piston applies a controlled **first-stage (slow-shot)** velocity to prevent air entrapment, then a **second-stage (fast-shot)** velocity to fill the cavity rapidly before solidification begins.

### Stage 3: **Intensification & Holding (Pack Phase)**
- Once the cavity is **~90–95% filled**, the system switches to **intensification pressure** (the maximum pressure).
- This compensates for **solidification shrinkage** (typically **3–6% volumetric** for most alloys).
- The metal is held under this pressure until the **sprue solidifies** — this prevents **shrinkage porosity**.

### Stage 4: **Cooling & Solidification**
- Heat is extracted rapidly through the die walls (steel has thermal conductivity **k ≈ 45 W/m·K**).
- Cooling time (t_cool) is estimated using **Chvorinov's Rule** for solidification.

### Stage 5: **Die Opening, Ejection & Lubrication**
- The die opens, **ejector pins** push the casting out.
- **Die release agent (lubricant)** is sprayed on die surfaces to prevent soldering and ease ejection.
- The cycle restarts.

---

## 2.2 Why and How — The Physical Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high pressure?** Because liquid metal has high **surface tension** and **viscosity**, and the thin sections of a die cavity must be filled in **< 100 milliseconds** before the metal freezes. High pressure overcomes flow resistance.
>
> **Why a steel die (and not aluminum/cast iron)?** Steel offers high **thermal conductivity**, **strength at temperature**, **wear resistance**, and can survive **hundreds of thousands of thermal cycles** without cracking.
>
> **Why two halves (cover + ejector)?** So the casting can be **parted** from the die without damage — the ejector half carries the **ejector pins, return pins, and core slides**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Die Casting Equations for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Injection Pressure** | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{F_{plunger}}{A_{plunger}}$ | MPa | Pressure applied to molten metal |
| 2 | **Clamping Force (required)** | $F_{clamp} \geq 1.25 \times P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ | N | Force to hold die halves closed |
| 3 | **Projected Area** | $A_{proj} = \text{area of casting} + \text{runners} + \text{overflows}$ | mm² | Total area resisting die opening |
| 4 | **Chvorinov's Rule (Cooling Time)** | $t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A_s}\right)^{n}$ , with $n \approx 2$ | s | Solidification time |
| 5 | **Volumetric Shrinkage Allowance** | $V_{final} = V_{cast} \times (1 + S)$ | mm³ | Die cavity size compensation |
| 6 | **Gate Velocity (Bernoulli)** | $v_{gate} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Velocity through gate |
| 7 | **Gate Area** | $A_{gate} = \dfrac{m_{shot}}{\rho \times v_{gate} \times t_{fill}}$ | mm² | Sizing the gate |
| 8 | **Specific Pressure (intensification)** | $P_{spec} = \dfrac{F_{intensifier}}{A_{plunger}}$ | MPa | Hold pressure in cavity |
| 9 | **Casting Cost per Part** | $C_{part} = \dfrac{C_{die}/N + C_{cycle} + C_{material/m}}{1 - \text{scrap}}$ | ₹/part | Economic analysis |
| 10 | **Minimum Draft Angle** | $\theta_{draft} \geq 0.5°$ to $3°$ | degrees | Wall taper for ejection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule (K-style):** In the markdown table above, absolute-value bars and similar delimiters have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. Always use this convention when writing KTU notes.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, transmission cases, cylinder heads, steering knuckles — Aluminum die casting saves **30–50% mass** vs. cast iron.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Laptop housings (Apple MacBook), smartphone frames — Magnesium die casting for **lightweight + EMI shielding**.
- **Toys & Hardware:** Matchbox cars (Zinc — "Zamak"), door handles, lock bodies.
- **Industrial:** Pumps, valves, motor housings, brackets.
- **Aerospace (specialty):** Magnesium and aluminum die castings for non-critical interior structures.

---

## 2.5 Advantages and Limitations

### ✅ **Advantages**
1. **High production rate** (up to 1000+ shots/hour in hot-chamber).
2. **Excellent dimensional accuracy** (±0.1 mm typical).
3. **Superior surface finish** (Ra 0.8 – 3.2 μm as-cast).
4. **Complex shapes** with thin walls (down to 0.6 mm).
5. **Reduced / no machining** (near-net-shape).
6. **High strength-to-weight ratio** (especially with Al and Mg alloys).
7. **Reproducible quality** over millions of cycles.

### ❌ **Limitations**
1. **High initial tooling cost** (die cost: **₹50,000 to ₹50,00,000+**).
2. **Limited to non-ferrous metals** (steel and iron melt at temperatures too high for standard dies).
3. **Porosity defects** (gas and shrinkage) — parts may not be pressure-tight without impregnation.
4. **Die wear and thermal fatigue** — die life is finite.
5. **Safety hazards** — high-pressure molten metal injection requires strict guarding.
6. **Design restrictions:** uniform wall thickness, draft angles, fillets, no undercuts (without slides).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Clamping Force

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation is critical for KTU exams:** Clamping force is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in KTU 2024 ESE papers. The examiner expects a free-body-diagram reasoning followed by an algebraic solution.

### Problem Context
A die casting machine injects molten aluminum into a die cavity. The plunger diameter is **80 mm** and the applied injection pressure is **70 MPa**. The total projected area of the casting (including runners and overflows) is **12,000 mm²**. Calculate:
(a) The **injection force** on the plunger.
(b) The **minimum clamping force** required (using a safety factor of **1.25**).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Plunger cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_{plunger} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2
$$

Substituting $D = 80 \text{ mm}$:

$$
A_{plunger} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (80)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 6400
$$

$$
A_{plunger} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2
$$

*Text row:* We computed the cross-sectional area of the circular plunger using the standard circle-area formula.

**Step 2 — Injection force (F_inj):**

$$
F_{inj} = P_{inj} \times A_{plunger}
$$

$$
F_{inj} = 70 \text{ MPa} \times 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Note: $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$

$$
F_{inj} = 70 \times 5026.55 = 3,51,858.5 \text{ N} \approx 351.86 \text{ kN}
$$

*Text row:* Pressure multiplied by area gives the axial force pushing the plunger. Converted to kiloNewtons for readability.

**Step 3 — Force tending to open the die (F_open):**

The molten metal, under pressure $P_{inj}$, pushes on the projected area of the die cavity:

$$
F_{open} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj}
$$

$$
F_{open} = 70 \times 12{,}000 = 8,40{,}000 \text{ N} = 840 \text{ kN}
$$

*Text row:* This is the force trying to blow the die halves apart.

**Step 4 — Minimum Clamping Force (F_clamp):**

$$
F_{clamp} = S.F. \times F_{open} = 1.25 \times 840
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} = 1050 \text{ kN} = 1.05 \text{ MN}}
$$

*Text row:* A 25% safety margin is applied to prevent die flash (leakage of metal between die halves).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chvorinov's Rule Applied to Die Casting

> [!NOTE]
> Chvorinov's rule states that **solidification time is proportional to the square of the volume-to-surface-area ratio**, multiplied by a mold constant $B$.

$$
t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A_s} \right)^2
$$

Where:
- $t_s$ = solidification time (s)
- $V$ = volume of casting (m³ or cm³)
- $A_s$ = surface area of casting (m² or cm²)
- $B$ = mold constant (depends on metal & mold material)

### Worked Example

A zinc die casting has $V = 50 \text{ cm}^3$ and $A_s = 90 \text{ cm}^2$. The mold constant $B = 4.5 \text{ s/cm}^4$.

$$
t_s = 4.5 \times \left(\dfrac{50}{90}\right)^2
$$

$$
\dfrac{V}{A_s} = 0.5556 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
t_s = 4.5 \times (0.5556)^2 = 4.5 \times 0.3086
$$

$$
\boxed{t_s \approx 1.39 \text{ seconds}}
$$

*Engineering Insight:* Because the **die is steel** (very high thermal conductivity), $B$ is small → solidification is **fast** → cycle time is short. This is why die casting is a **high-volume** process.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gate Area Calculation

The **gate** is the narrow channel through which molten metal enters the cavity. Its area is critical for filling before solidification.

$$
A_{gate} = \dfrac{m_{shot}}{\rho \times v_{gate} \times t_{fill}}
$$

**Given data** (typical KTU problem):
- Shot mass $m_{shot} = 2.5 \text{ kg}$ of aluminum
- Density of aluminum $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gate velocity $v_{gate} = 40 \text{ m/s}$
- Fill time $t_{fill} = 0.05 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Convert and substitute:**

$$
A_{gate} = \dfrac{2.5}{2700 \times 40 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
A_{gate} = \dfrac{2.5}{5400}
$$

$$
A_{gate} = 4.63 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 463 \text{ mm}^2
$$

*Text row:* The gate must be large enough to allow the required mass of metal to enter the cavity within the available time before freezing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Die Casting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to decide whether die casting is the right process for a given part (frequently tested in KTU design questions):**

| Design Factor | Favours Die Casting | Discourages Die Casting |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Volume** | > 5,000 parts/year | < 1,000 parts/year |
| **Part Size** | Small–medium (up to ~50 kg) | Very large (>100 kg) |
| **Material** | Zn, Al, Mg, Cu alloys | Steel, Cast iron, Ti |
| **Wall Thickness** | Uniform, 0.6 – 6 mm | Variable, > 10 mm |
| **Tolerance** | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.01 mm (use machining) |
| **Surface Finish** | As-cast acceptable | Mirror polish needed |
| **Pressure Tightness** | Not critical | Critical (use impregnation) |
| **Initial Budget** | High (₹) acceptable | Low budget required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Die Casting Machine

> [!NOTE]
> *A physical sectional drawing of a die casting machine is extremely complex (50+ components). Below is a high-fidelity **Mermaid block diagram** mapping the functional architecture and energy/material flow between subsystems — exactly as required by KTU Module 4.*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Melting Furnace] -->|Molten Metal| B[Injection System]
    B -->|High Pressure Shot| C[Die Assembly Cover Half]
    C --> D[Die Cavity]
    D --> E[Die Assembly Ejector Half]
    E -->|Ejector Pins| F[Casting Ejection Station]
    F --> G[Trim Press]
    G --> H[Finished Casting]
    
    I[Hydraulic Power Pack] -->|Clamping Force| C
    I -->|Plunger Force| B
    J[Lubrication System] -->|Spray Release Agent| D
    K[Control Panel PLC] --> I
    K --> B
    K --> J
    L[Cooling Water Circuit] --> C
    L --> E
    
    M[Shot Sleeve Hot Chamber] -.-> B
    N[Shot Sleeve Cold Chamber] -.-> B
    
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#FF6B6B
    style C fill:#4ECDC4
    style E fill:#4ECDC4
    style D fill:#95E1D3
    style H fill:#C7CEEA
```

## 4.2 Process Cycle Flowchart (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Die Clamp] --> S2[Step 2 Metal Inject]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Intensify Hold]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Cool Solidify]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Open Eject]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Lubricate]
    S6 --> S1
    
    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S2 fill:#FFA07A
    style S3 fill:#FFD700
    style S4 fill:#98FB98
    style S5 fill:#87CEEB
    style S6 fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison — Hot Chamber vs Cold Chamber (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HC[Hot Chamber Die Casting] --> HC1[Melting Pot Submerged]
    HC1 --> HC2[Gooseneck Channel]
    HC2 --> HC3[Plunger Injects 7 to 35 MPa]
    HC3 --> HC4[Alloy Zn Sn Pb Mg]
    
    CC[Cold Chamber Die Casting] --> CC1[External Furnace]
    CC1 --> CC2[Hand Ladle or Auto Ladle]
    CC2 --> CC3[Shot Sleeve Filled]
    CC3 --> CC4[Plunger Injects 30 to 350 MPa]
    CC4 --> CC5[Alloy Al Cu Brass]
    
    style HC fill:#FFCCCB
    style CC fill:#B0E0E6
    style HC4 fill:#FFD700
    style CC5 fill:#87CEFA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams in KTU Exams:** When you reproduce these diagrams in your answer script, **label every block clearly** and **add directional arrows** showing the flow. Examiners award **1–2 marks** just for a clean, well-labelled process flow diagram in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define die casting. List any four advantages of the process.**

**Model Answer:**
Die casting is a permanent mold casting process in which molten non-ferrous metal is forced under high pressure (7–350 MPa) into a precisely machined steel die cavity, where it solidifies rapidly to produce a net-shape component.

**Four Advantages:**
1. High production rate (up to 1000+ shots/hour in hot-chamber).
2. Excellent dimensional accuracy (±0.1 mm typical).
3. Superior as-cast surface finish (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm).
4. Complex shapes with thin walls (down to 0.6 mm) are achievable.
5. Minimal secondary machining required.
6. Long die life (up to 1,000,000+ shots).

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between hot-chamber and cold-chamber die casting with suitable examples of alloys used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber |
|---|---|---|
| Melting pot location | Submerged in molten metal | External furnace |
| Pressure | 7–35 MPa | 30–350 MPa |
| Alloys used | Zinc, tin, lead, magnesium | Aluminum, copper, brass |
| Cycle time | Very short | Longer |
| Example | Zinc toy cars (Zamak 3) | Aluminum car wheels |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. KTU provides an **internal choice** — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **hot-chamber die casting process**. State its advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Hot-chamber die casting** (also called **gooseneck** or **plunger-type** die casting) is used for low-melting-point alloys. The melting pot is an integral part of the machine, and the plunger operates **inside** the molten metal.

**Working Principle:**
1. The gooseneck channel and shot sleeve are **permanently submerged** in the molten metal bath.
2. The plunger moves **upward**, drawing a measured charge of molten metal into the shot sleeve.
3. The plunger then moves **downward**, sealing the gooseneck and forcing the metal through the nozzle and into the closed die cavity under pressure (**7–35 MPa**).
4. The metal solidifies rapidly due to die cooling.
5. The die opens, ejector pins push the casting out, and the cycle repeats.

**Advantages:**
- Very high production rate (cycle time as low as **2–5 seconds**).
- Less metal wastage (no ladle transfer).
- Suitable for Zn, Sn, Pb, and Mg alloys.
- Lower injection pressure reduces machine cost.

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for **high-melting alloys** (Al, Cu) — the submerged gooseneck would be damaged.
- **Gooseneck corrosion** by aggressive alloys (e.g., high-Al zinc).
- Limited to smaller parts.

*(Awarded marks: Sketch 3 + Working 2 + Advantages 1 + Limitations 1 = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A die casting machine has a plunger diameter of **100 mm** and operates at an injection pressure of **50 MPa**. The projected area of the casting (including runners) is **15,000 mm²**. Determine:
(i) The **injection force** on the plunger.
(ii) The **clamping force** required (use safety factor = 1.25).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Injection Force:**

$$
A_{plunger} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F_{inj} = P_{inj} \times A_{plunger} = 50 \times 7853.98
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{inj} = 3,92,699 \text{ N} \approx 392.7 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(ii) Clamping Force:**

$$
F_{open} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj} = 50 \times 15{,}000 = 7,50{,}000 \text{ N} = 750 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 1.25 \times 750
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} = 937.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **die casting** with **sand casting** and **investment casting** on the basis of: (i) tooling cost, (ii) production rate, (iii) surface finish, (iv) tolerance, (v) material range, and (vi) part complexity.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Investment Casting | Die Casting |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Tooling cost | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| (ii) Production rate | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| (iii) Surface finish | Poor (Ra 12–25 μm) | Excellent (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm) | Very good (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm) |
| (iv) Tolerance | ± 1.5 mm | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.1–0.3 mm |
| (v) Material range | All metals | All metals | Non-ferrous only (Zn, Al, Mg, Cu) |
| (vi) Part complexity | Moderate | Very high | High (limited by draft) |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark per correct row × 6 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for neat presentation = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** List and explain the **various defects** encountered in die castings. How can they be minimized?

**Model Answer:**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity (gas)** | Air trapped during injection | Use proper venting, vacuum die casting |
| **Shrinkage porosity** | Insufficient feed metal | Adequate gates & overflows; intensify pressure |
| **Cold shuts** | Metal freezes before fusing | Increase injection speed & temperature |
| **Flash** | Insufficient clamping force | Increase F_clamp; maintain die surfaces |
| **Soldering** | Metal sticks to die | Use proper die lubricants & coatings |
| **Cracks** | Hot tearing during ejection | Uniform wall thickness; proper draft |
| **Flow lines** | Uneven flow & temperature | Optimize gate design; preheat die |

*(Each defect: 0.5 mark + 0.5 mark remedy × 6 defects = 6 marks + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "mold" with "die"** in definitions → lose 0.5–1 mark. Use "die" specifically for steel permanent tooling in die casting.
> 2. **Forgetting to include safety factor** in clamping force → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up hot-chamber and cold-chamber alloy lists** → very common; memorize: **Hot = low-melt (Zn, Pb, Sn, Mg); Cold = high-melt (Al, Cu, brass)**.
> 4. **Writing units inconsistently** (mixing MPa with N/mm²) → 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** in 7-mark sub-parts → 1–2 marks lost. Always draw a labelled block diagram.
> 6. **Not specifying safety factor value** in derivation problems → 0.5 mark lost.
> 7. **Writing "die casting is used for steel"** → WRONG. Die casting is for **non-ferrous alloys only**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Die Casting: Introduction**

### 📌 **Core Concepts**
- ✅ Die casting = **high-pressure** injection of molten **non-ferrous** metal into a **reusable steel die**.
- ✅ Pressure range: **7 – 350 MPa**.
- ✅ Cycle time: **30 s – 2 min**.
- ✅ Tolerance: **± 0.1 – 0.3 mm**.
- ✅ Surface finish: **Ra 0.8 – 3.2 μm**.
- ✅ Minimum wall thickness: **0.6 mm** (Zn) to **3 mm** (Al).
- ✅ Die life: **100,000 – 1,000,000+ shots**.

### 📌 **Two Process Types**
- ✅ **Hot-Chamber:** Plunger submerged; low-melt alloys (Zn, Sn, Pb, Mg); 7–35 MPa; very fast.
- ✅ **Cold-Chamber:** Ladle transfer; high-melt alloys (Al, Cu, brass); 30–350 MPa; slower.

### 📌 **Key Equations (Must Memorize)**
- ✅ $F_{inj} = P_{inj} \times A_{plunger}$
- ✅ $F_{clamp} = S.F. \times P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ (S.F. = 1.25)
- ✅ $t_s = B (V/A_s)^2$ (Chvorinov's rule)
- ✅ $A_{gate} = m_{shot} / (\rho \cdot v_{gate} \cdot t_{fill})$

### 📌 **Alloys Used (Priority Order)**
1. **Zinc (Zamak 3, 5, 7)** — easiest to die cast, lowest melting.
2. **Aluminum (A380, A383)** — most widely used (automotive).
3. **Magnesium (AZ91D)** — lightest structural metal.
4. **Copper & Brass** — decorative, high strength.

### 📌 **Defects to Remember (Top 7)**
- Porosity, Cold shuts, Flash, Soldering, Cracks, Flow lines, Shrinkage cavity.

### 📌 **Design Rules of Thumb**
- ✅ Uniform wall thickness.
- ✅ Minimum draft angle: **0.5° – 3°**.
- ✅ Generous fillets (avoid sharp corners).
- ✅ Avoid undercuts (or use slides).
- ✅ Locate parting line thoughtfully.
- ✅ Provide adequate gates, runners, overflows, and vents.

### 📌 **Comparison Anchors**
- Die casting has the **highest production rate** of all casting processes for non-ferrous metals.
- Die casting has the **highest tooling cost** but the **lowest per-part cost at high volume**.
- Die casting is **NOT suitable** for ferrous metals (steel, iron).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question, aim to write a **definition (2 lines) + labelled diagram (3–4 marks) + working principle (3–4 marks) + advantages/limitations (2–3 marks) + comparison or derivation (2–3 marks)**. This structure covers **all** KTU valuation expectations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Applications** in the context of Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) refer to the systematic implementation of DFM (Design for Manufacturing) and DFA (Design for Assembly) principles, guidelines, and evaluation methodologies to real-world product design problems across diverse industrial domains. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the "Applications" module consolidates the theoretical principles of DFM/DFA into practical industrial problem-solving frameworks, encompassing case study analysis, application of evaluation metrics, and the integration of Design for X (DFX) philosophies in modern product development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET522 Module 4 - Applications):**
> *The Applications module addresses the practical deployment of DFMA tools such as the Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly (DFA) methodology, the Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) software-based assessment, Pugh Concept Selection matrices, and case-based reasoning using real industrial artefacts in casting, machining, sheet metal, plastic moulding, and electronics assembly.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a large residential apartment complex**. Before laying a single brick, you must consider:
- *How will workers actually build this?* (Ease of construction)
- *How will plumbers, electricians, and interior designers install their components later?* (Ease of assembly)
- *How will residents maintain the building?* (Ease of service)
- *How will the building be demolished or recycled at end-of-life?* (End-of-life design)

**DFMA applications are exactly this — but for physical products** ranging from a ballpoint pen to a commercial aircraft engine. Before committing to a final design, engineers apply DFMA application tools to ask: *"Can this be manufactured cheaply? Can it be assembled with minimum parts, minimum fasteners, and minimum operations?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Every DFMA application essentially chases three "minimums":
> 1. **Minimum number of parts**
> 2. **Minimum number of operations** (manufacturing + assembly)
> 3. **Minimum cost per functional unit**

## 1.3 Classification of DFMA Applications

| **S. No.** | **Application Domain** | **Primary Concern** | **Key Metric Used** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Design for Assembly (DFA) | Reducing assembly time and parts | Assembly Index (AI), DFA Index |
| 2 | Design for Manufacturing (DFM) | Optimising individual part fabrication | Manufacturing cost, cycle time |
| 3 | Design for Casting | Pattern, parting line, draft angle | Yield %, machining allowance |
| 4 | Design for Machining | Feature accessibility, tool approach | Machinability rating, setup count |
| 5 | Design for Plastic Moulding | Uniform wall, draft, ejection | Mould cost, cycle time, shrinkage |
| 6 | Design for Sheet Metal | Bending, punching, nesting | Material utilisation, bend allowance |
| 7 | Design for Environment (DFE) | Recyclability, disassembly | Recycled content %, EOL recovery |
| 8 | Design for Test/Inspection | Test point accessibility | Test coverage ratio |

## 1.4 Why "Applications" is a Critical KTU Module

The KTU 2024 scheme places heavy weightage (typically **30–40% of the End Semester Examination**) on application-based questions because DFMA is fundamentally a **practical, problem-solving discipline**. The university examiner rewards students who can:
- *Apply* a formula (not just recall it)
- *Justify* a design choice using DFMA logic
- *Evaluate* a given product's DFA score
- *Recommend* redesign modifications

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Insight:** KTU examiners love questions that provide a sketch/diagram of a mechanical assembly and ask students to compute the DFA index or suggest consolidation. Always be ready to draw **before-and-after** redesign comparisons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application of Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Methodology

The Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method (developed at the University of Massachusetts, 1980s) is the most widely applied DFMA evaluation framework in industry. Its application involves a **three-column analysis** for every part in an assembly.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Question DFA Test

For each part in a proposed assembly, three sequential yes/no questions are asked:

1. **Question 1 — Operation Requirement:**
   *During assembly, does the part need to move relative to other already-assembled parts?*
   - If **YES** → Keep the part (functional necessity).
   - If **NO** → Proceed to Question 2.

2. **Question 2 — Disassembly Necessity:**
   *Must the part be of a different material than adjacent parts, or must it be separable for service/recycling?*
   - If **YES** → Keep the part.
   - If **NO** → Proceed to Question 3.

3. **Question 3 — Attachment/Operation Reduction:**
   *Does the part require a separate fastening, joining, or alignment operation that is impossible to combine with another part?*
   - If **YES** → Keep the part.
   - If **NO** → **ELIMINATE the part** by combining it with another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** If all three questions yield "NO," the part is a **candidate for elimination** through design consolidation. The application of this rule is the heart of every DFA case-study problem in the KTU exam.

### 2.1.2 DFA Efficiency Computation

Once part elimination is complete, the **DFA Index** (also called *Assembly Efficiency*) is computed for both the original and redesigned assemblies.

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_a} \times 100 \quad \text{(\%)} $$

Where:
- $N_{\min}$ = Theoretical minimum number of parts (after applying the three-question test).
- $N_a$ = Actual number of parts in the assembly (after redesign).

The **DFA Time** is computed using the Boothroyd manual handling code system (a, b, c classifications based on part thickness, symmetry, and ease of grasping/insertion).

$$ T_{\text{assembly}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} T_i \quad \text{(seconds)} $$

Where $T_i$ is the estimated handling + insertion time for the $i$-th part, looked up from the standard Boothroyd-Dewhurst time tables (typically ranging from **1.5 s to 9.0 s** per part for manual assembly).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> **Notation Note:** All absolute-value and division symbols are rendered with `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking the table parser.

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units** | **Typical KTU Application** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \dfrac{N_{\min}}{N_a} \times 100$ | DFA Efficiency / Assembly Index | % | Compare before vs. after redesign |
| 2 | $T_{\text{assy}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} T_i$ | Total assembly time | seconds (s) | Boothroyd time-based evaluation |
| 3 | $C_{\text{assy}} = C_{\text{part}} + C_{\text{handling}} + C_{\text{insertion}}$ | Cost per part in assembly | ₹ / $ | Cost-based DFA variant |
| 4 | $D_{\min} = N_m + N_o$ | Minimum part count (DFA theory) | dimensionless | Design target setting |
| 5 | $\text{DT} = \dfrac{N_m \cdot t_m}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Design Efficiency Ratio | % | Lucas DFA method |
| 6 | $\text{MMI} = \dfrac{N_p \cdot T_p}{N_{\min} \cdot T_{\min}}$ | Manufacturing/Assembly Index | dimensionless | Hitachi DFA method |
| 7 | $\text{Saving \%} = \dfrac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Cost saving from DFMA | % | Case-study final answer |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{\bar{L}_1 - \bar{L}_2}{\bar{L}_1 + \bar{L}_2}$ | Bend allowance factor (sheet metal) | dimensionless | DFM sheet metal app. |
| 9 | $t_{\text{cool}} = k \cdot s^2$ | Plastic cooling time | seconds | DFM plastic moulding app. |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{cast}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{finished}}}{m_{\text{cast}}} \times 100$ | Casting yield / utilisation | % | DFM casting app. |

> **Legend for the table:**
> - $N_m$ = number of manually inserted parts, $N_o$ = number of operations
> - $t_m$ = time per manual insertion, $T_p$ = processing time
> - $s$ = section thickness in plastic moulding, $k$ = material thermal constant
> - $K$ = neutral axis shift factor in sheet metal bending

## 2.3 Application of Lucas DFA Method (UK)

The **Lucas DFA method** (developed by the Lucas Group, UK) uses a 16-question checklist scored 0 or 1 per part. The final Lucas DFA Efficiency is:

$$ \text{Lucas Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Total "Yes" score for kept parts}}{\text{Maximum possible score for ideal assembly}} \times 100 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Lucas method is **secondary** in KTU exam weightage compared to Boothroyd-Dewhurst. However, it is occasionally asked for a 3-mark comparison question ("Compare Boothroyd and Lucas DFA methods").

## 2.4 Application Domains — Engineering Utility

The applications covered in Module 4 directly serve the following engineering fields:
- **Automotive Industry:** Engine sub-assemblies, dashboard modules, body panels.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Mobile phone chassis, laptop hinges, PCB assembly.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade assemblies, landing gear sub-assemblies.
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical instrument kits, disposable syringe assemblies.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Gearbox assemblies, hydraulic valve blocks.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Practically every mass-produced product in the world today has been subjected to at least one DFMA application pass during its design freeze. For example, the reduction of part count from **68 to 23** in the HP DeskJet printer (a classic Boothroyd case study) saved HP approximately **\$100 million annually** in assembly cost.

## 2.5 Design for X (DFX) — Extended Applications

| **DFX Variant** | **Focus** | **Industry Use** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| DFE — Design for Environment | Eco-friendly materials, EOL recovery | All consumer products |
| DFD — Design for Disassembly | Ease of recycling | Automotive, electronics |
| DFS — Design for Service | Maintenance accessibility | Heavy equipment |
| DFT — Design for Test | Built-in testability | Electronics, avionics |
| DFR — Design for Reliability | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | Aerospace, medical |
| DFL — Design for Logistics | Packaging, transport efficiency | FMCG, automotive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** DFX is treated as a *sister concept* to DFMA, and 3-mark questions on DFX definitions are common. Memorise at least **three DFX variants** with one-line definitions each.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: DFA Index Calculation (Boothroyd-Dewhurst)

### Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU 2024 Pattern)

A mechanical assembly originally consists of **10 parts**. After applying the Boothroyd three-question DFA test, **3 parts are identified as candidates for elimination** by combining with adjacent parts. The estimated Boothroyd handling and insertion times (in seconds) for the redesigned 7-part assembly are:
$\lbrace 1.95,\ 2.50,\ 1.95,\ 4.50,\ 3.00,\ 1.50,\ 2.10 \rbrace$ seconds.

**Compute:**
(a) The DFA Efficiency of the original assembly (assuming $N_{\min} = 7$).
(b) The DFA Efficiency of the redesigned assembly.
(c) The total assembly time for the redesigned assembly.
(d) The percentage reduction in assembly time if the original assembly had a total time of **30.0 seconds**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Original number of parts: $N_a^{(1)} = 10$
- Theoretical minimum parts: $N_{\min} = 7$
- Parts eliminated: 3
- Redesigned parts: $N_a^{(2)} = 7$
- Original total time: $T^{(1)} = 30.0$ s
- New assembly times: $T_i = \lbrace 1.95,\ 2.50,\ 1.95,\ 4.50,\ 3.00,\ 1.50,\ 2.10 \rbrace$ s

---

#### (a) DFA Efficiency of the Original Assembly

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(1)} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_a^{(1)}} \times 100 $$

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(1)} = \frac{7}{10} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(1)} = 70\ \%} $$

---

#### (b) DFA Efficiency of the Redesigned Assembly

After consolidation, the actual part count equals the theoretical minimum. Hence:

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(2)} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_a^{(2)}} \times 100 = \frac{7}{7} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(2)} = 100\ \%} $$

> **Interpretation:** A perfect DFA score is achieved when every part passes at least one of the three Boothroyd questions — i.e., every part is functionally essential.

---

#### (c) Total Assembly Time of the Redesigned Assembly

$$ T^{(2)} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} T_i $$

$$ T^{(2)} = 1.95 + 2.50 + 1.95 + 4.50 + 3.00 + 1.50 + 2.10 $$

$$ T^{(2)} = 17.50 \text{ s} $$

$$ \boxed{T^{(2)} = 17.50 \text{ seconds}} $$

---

#### (d) Percentage Reduction in Assembly Time

$$ \Delta T\,\% = \frac{T^{(1)} - T^{(2)}}{T^{(1)}} \times 100 $$

$$ \Delta T\,\% = \frac{30.0 - 17.5}{30.0} \times 100 $$

$$ \Delta T\,\% = \frac{12.5}{30.0} \times 100 = 41.67\,\% $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta T\,\% \approx 41.67\ \%} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 41.67 % reduction in assembly time, with no functional compromise, is typical of a high-impact DFMA redesign. In a factory producing 1,00,000 units/year, this saves approximately **$4.17 \times 10^{5}$ seconds ≈ 116 production hours per year**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cost-Based DFA Application

### Problem Statement

A printed-circuit-board (PCB) module has an original assembly with **15 parts** and a per-part assembly cost that varies (₹ values): $\lbrace 4,\ 6,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4,\ 7,\ 6,\ 5,\ 4,\ 6,\ 3,\ 5,\ 4,\ 6 \rbrace$ (rupees per part). The total handling cost is ₹ 25 and the total insertion cost is ₹ 40.

After DFMA consolidation, **5 parts are eliminated** and the remaining 10 parts have a uniform per-part cost of ₹ 5. New handling cost = ₹ 18, new insertion cost = ₹ 28.

**Find the cost saving in ₹ and the percentage savings.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Original Total Assembly Cost

$$ C^{(1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{15} C_{p,i} + C_h^{(1)} + C_i^{(1)} $$

Sum of per-part costs:
$$ \sum C_p = 4+6+3+8+5+4+7+6+5+4+6+3+5+4+6 = 76 \ \text{₹} $$

Therefore:
$$ C^{(1)} = 76 + 25 + 40 = 141 \ \text{₹} $$

#### Step 2: Redesigned Total Assembly Cost

$$ C^{(2)} = (10 \times 5) + 18 + 28 = 50 + 18 + 28 = 96 \ \text{₹} $$

#### Step 3: Cost Saving

$$ \Delta C = C^{(1)} - C^{(2)} = 141 - 96 = 45 \ \text{₹} $$

#### Step 4: Percentage Saving

$$ \text{Saving \%} = \frac{45}{141} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Saving \%} \approx 31.91\ \%} $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Part Function to Decision

The following **analytical matrix** is the master tool used in DFMA application case studies. Memorise the decision flow.

| **Step** | **Question Asked** | **YES → Action** | **NO → Action** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Does the part move during operation? | Retain part | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Must it be different material or separable? | Retain part | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | Does it need a separate fastening/op? | Retain part | **Eliminate / combine** |
| 4 | Does the part fit a DFM guideline for its process? | Keep process | Change process |
| 5 | Is the design DFX-compliant (recycle/disassemble)? | Keep as-is | Modify for DFX |

---

## 3.4 Generalised Derivation of the DFA Improvement Ratio

Consider an assembly with $N_a$ parts and an average assembly time $\bar{T}$ per part. The total time is $T_0 = N_a \cdot \bar{T}$.

After DFMA, let $N_r$ parts remain (with $N_r < N_a$) and the average time become $\bar{T}'$ (typically $\bar{T}' \le \bar{T}$ due to simpler handling). The improvement ratio $R$ is:

$$ R = \frac{T_0 - T_r}{T_0} = 1 - \frac{N_r \cdot \bar{T}'}{N_a \cdot \bar{T}} $$

For the special case $\bar{T}' = \bar{T}$ (i.e., same complexity per remaining part):

$$ R = 1 - \frac{N_r}{N_a} = \frac{N_a - N_r}{N_a} $$

This is the **fractional part reduction**, a useful sanity check in DFA evaluations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Preference:** Always present both the **absolute saving** and the **percentage saving** in your answer. Examiners award partial marks generously when you quantify *and* comment on engineering significance.

---

## 3.5 Cumulative Engineering Take-aways

1. **Part count reduction is the single largest cost lever** in mechanical assembly — typically 60–80 % of DFMA savings.
2. **Time reduction is a secondary lever** but vital for high-volume production lines.
3. **Standardisation of fasteners** (e.g., using one bolt size across the assembly) is a small but high-impact DFM application.
4. **Symmetry and chamfer features** drastically reduce manual insertion times (Boothroyd codes).
5. **Modular design** supports DFD (Design for Disassembly) without sacrificing DFA efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFMA Application Master Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Product Concept] --> B[Generate Functional Requirements]
    B --> C[Concept Generation: Multiple Alternatives]
    C --> D[Pugh Concept Selection Matrix]
    D --> E[Select Best Concept]
    E --> F[Create Preliminary 3D CAD Model]
    F --> G[Apply DFM Guidelines per Process]
    G --> G1[Casting Rules]
    G --> G2[Machining Rules]
    G --> G3[Plastic Moulding Rules]
    G --> G4[Sheet Metal Rules]
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H[Apply Boothroyd DFA 3-Question Test]
    H --> I{Part Eliminable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Combine with Adjacent Part]
    I -- No --> K[Retain as Functional Part]
    J --> L[Recompute DFA Index]
    K --> L
    L --> M{DFA Index >= 70 percent?}
    M -- No --> N[Iterate Redesign]
    M -- Yes --> O[Apply DFX Checks]
    N --> H
    O --> P[Design Freeze and Release to Manufacturing]
    P --> Q[End: Production-Ready DFMA Design]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart shows the iterative loop of DFA. A design must pass the DFA Index gate (≥ 70 %) before it can proceed to DFX verification and eventual production release.

---

## 4.2 Boothroyd Three-Question DFA Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[For Each Part in Assembly] --> Q1{Does the part move during operation?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> K1[Retain Part]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Must it be of a different material or separable?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> K1
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Are separate fastening or alignment operations unavoidable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> K1
    Q3 -- No --> EL[Eliminate or Combine with Adjacent Part]
    K1 --> NX[Next Part]
    EL --> NX
```

---

## 4.3 DFX Layered Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE[Core DFMA Foundation]
        DFMA[Design for Manufacture and Assembly]
    end
    subgraph DFX[Extended DFX Layers]
        DFE[Design for Environment]
        DFD[Design for Disassembly]
        DFS[Design for Service]
        DFT[Design for Test]
        DFR[Design for Reliability]
        DFL[Design for Logistics]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Tools]
        BH[Boothroyd DFA Method]
        LC[Lucas DFA Method]
        PG[Pugh Selection Matrix]
    end
    CORE --> DFX
    SUPPORT --> CORE
    BH --> CORE
    LC --> CORE
    PG --> CORE
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram positions DFMA at the core, with DFX layers as concentric extensions. The three support tools (Boothroyd, Lucas, Pugh) feed into the core, illustrating that DFMA is a *methodology* while DFX is a *family of philosophies*.

---

## 4.4 Application Selection Flowchart (DFM Process Choice)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Selected] --> B{Quantity per year?}
    B -- Low Volume under 1000 --> C[Sand Casting or Machining]
    B -- Medium 1000 to 10000 --> D[Die Casting or Plastic Moulding]
    B -- High Volume above 10000 --> E[Injection Moulding or Stamping]
    C --> F[Apply DFM Casting Rules]
    D --> G[Apply DFM Die Casting and Moulding Rules]
    E --> H[Apply DFM Moulding and Sheet Metal Rules]
    F --> I[Final DFA Check]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Production Release]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **"List any three Design for X (DFX) variants and give a one-line definition for each." (3 Marks)**

### Model Answer

> **DFX Variants:**
>
> 1. **Design for Environment (DFE):** Designing products to minimise environmental impact throughout their life cycle, including material selection, energy use, and end-of-life disposal.
> 2. **Design for Disassembly (DFD):** Designing products so that they can be easily taken apart at the end of life for recycling, reuse, or safe disposal.
> 3. **Design for Test (DFT):** Incorporating features into a product that allow easy, fast, and thorough testing of its functions and sub-assemblies during manufacturing and service.
>
> *(Any 3 of: DFE, DFD, DFS, DFT, DFR, DFL are acceptable; one mark per correctly defined variant.)*

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **"What is the Boothroyd-Dewhurst three-question test? State the three questions in order." (3 Marks)**

### Model Answer

> The Boothroyd-Dewhurst three-question test is a systematic procedure to decide whether a part in an assembly can be eliminated by combining it with an adjacent part. The three questions, asked in sequence for each part, are:
>
> 1. **Question 1 — Operation:** Does the part move relative to other parts during operation of the product?
> 2. **Question 2 — Separation Necessity:** Must the part be separable (different material or need to be removed for service/recycling)?
> 3. **Question 3 — Attachment:** Does the part require a separate fastening or joining operation that cannot be combined with another part?
>
> *If all three answers are NO, the part is a candidate for elimination. (3 marks: 1 mark for stating the purpose, 2 marks for the three questions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** A mechanical assembly consists of **12 parts**. The Boothroyd handling and insertion times (in seconds) for the 12 parts are:
> $\lbrace 3.00,\ 1.95,\ 1.50,\ 4.50,\ 2.10,\ 1.95,\ 3.00,\ 1.50,\ 4.50,\ 2.10,\ 3.00,\ 1.95 \rbrace$
> After applying the Boothroyd three-question test, **4 parts are eliminated** by combining with adjacent parts. Assume the theoretical minimum part count is **8**.
>
> **Compute:**
> (i) DFA Efficiency of the original assembly.
> (ii) DFA Efficiency of the redesigned assembly.
> (iii) Total assembly time before and after redesign.
> (iv) Percentage reduction in assembly time. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch or flow diagram, describe the **iterative DFMA design process** highlighting the role of the Boothroyd three-question test, the DFA Index computation, and the DFX verification step. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $N_a^{(1)} = 12$ (original part count)
- $N_{\min} = 8$ (theoretical minimum)
- $N_a^{(2)} = 8$ (redesigned part count, after 4 parts eliminated)
- Original times: $T_i = \lbrace 3.00, 1.95, 1.50, 4.50, 2.10, 1.95, 3.00, 1.50, 4.50, 2.10, 3.00, 1.95 \rbrace$ s
- (Assume the 4 smallest-time parts are eliminated: $1.50, 1.50, 1.95, 1.95$.)

**Step (i): Original DFA Efficiency**

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(1)} = \frac{8}{12} \times 100 = 66.67\ \% $$

**Step (ii): Redesigned DFA Efficiency**

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(2)} = \frac{8}{8} \times 100 = 100\ \% $$

**Step (iii): Total Assembly Times**

Original total time (sum of all 12):
$$ T^{(1)} = 3.00+1.95+1.50+4.50+2.10+1.95+3.00+1.50+4.50+2.10+3.00+1.95 $$

$$ T^{(1)} = 31.05 \text{ s} $$

Redesigned time (after removing 4 parts: $1.50, 1.50, 1.95, 1.95$):
$$ T^{(2)} = 31.05 - (1.50 + 1.50 + 1.95 + 1.95) $$

$$ T^{(2)} = 31.05 - 6.90 = 24.15 \text{ s} $$

**Step (iv): Percentage Time Reduction**

$$ \Delta T\,\% = \frac{31.05 - 24.15}{31.05} \times 100 = \frac{6.90}{31.05} \times 100 \approx 22.22\ \% $$

> **Final Results Box:**
> - (i) Original DFA Efficiency = **66.67 %**
> - (ii) Redesigned DFA Efficiency = **100 %**
> - (iii) Original Time = **31.05 s**, Redesigned Time = **24.15 s**
> - (iv) Time Reduction ≈ **22.22 %**

#### Part (b) — Descriptive Solution

> The DFMA design process is **iterative**, not linear. The steps are:
>
> 1. **Concept Generation:** Multiple design concepts are proposed for the same functional requirement (e.g., a "bracket" can be cast, machined from a block, or stamped from sheet metal).
> 2. **Concept Selection (Pugh Matrix):** Each concept is scored against weighted criteria (cost, weight, manufacturability, etc.) and the best is selected.
> 3. **CAD Modelling:** The selected concept is modelled in 3D CAD with all parts, fasteners, and joints.
> 4. **DFM Analysis (per process):** Apply DFM guidelines specific to the chosen manufacturing process (casting, machining, plastic moulding, sheet metal, etc.). Modify the design to remove manufacturing difficulties.
> 5. **DFA Analysis (Boothroyd):** For every part in the assembly, apply the three Boothroyd questions. If all three yield "NO," the part is a candidate for elimination. Combine or integrate that part with an adjacent part.
> 6. **DFA Index Computation:** Compute $\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \dfrac{N_{\min}}{N_a} \times 100$. Compare against the target (≥ 70 % for industrial acceptability).
> 7. **DFX Verification:** Apply extended DFX checks (DFE, DFD, DFS, DFT, etc.) to ensure the design also meets environmental, service, and end-of-life requirements.
> 8. **Iteration Gate:** If the DFA Index is below the target, the design loops back to Step 5 and another consolidation pass is performed.
> 9. **Design Freeze:** Once DFA Index ≥ target *and* all DFX checks are passed, the design is frozen and released to manufacturing.
>
> *(A labelled flowchart drawing earns the remaining 2–3 marks. Refer to Section 4.1 for the exact structure.)*

---

### Question B-B (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method** with the **Lucas DFA method** under the following heads: basis of evaluation, scoring system, output metric, and ease of industrial use. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A gear-box assembly originally has **20 parts** and a total assembly cost of ₹ 500. After a DFMA redesign, the part count reduces to **14** and the total assembly cost becomes ₹ 360. Compute (i) the absolute cost saving, (ii) the percentage cost saving, and (iii) the new DFA Index. Comment on the engineering significance of the results. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table

| **Basis** | **Boothroyd-Dewhurst Method** | **Lucas DFA Method** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Origin** | University of Massachusetts, USA (1980s) | Lucas Group, UK (1970s) |
| **Evaluation Basis** | Three-question functional test (must move, must separate, must fasten) | 16-question checklist scored 0 or 1 per part |
| **Scoring System** | Yes/No (binary) per part; time-based | Weighted scoring (yes = 1, no = 0) summed |
| **Output Metric** | DFA Index (%); Total assembly time (s) | Lucas Efficiency (%) |
| **Industrial Use** | Very widely used in automotive, electronics | Less common; used in UK automotive |
| **Strengths** | Quantitative, time-based, easy to apply | Simple, quick first-pass screening |
| **Weaknesses** | Requires time-table lookups | Subjective scoring of 16 questions |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $N_a^{(1)} = 20$, $C^{(1)} = ₹ 500$
- $N_a^{(2)} = 14$, $C^{(2)} = ₹ 360$
- Assume theoretical minimum $N_{\min} = 14$ (since redesign achieved perfect DFA).

**Step (i): Absolute Cost Saving**

$$ \Delta C = C^{(1)} - C^{(2)} = 500 - 360 = ₹\ 140 $$

**Step (ii): Percentage Cost Saving**

$$ \text{Saving \%} = \frac{140}{500} \times 100 = 28\ \% $$

**Step (iii): New DFA Index**

$$ \eta_{\text{DFA}}^{(2)} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_a^{(2)}} \times 100 = \frac{14}{14} \times 100 = 100\ \% $$

> **Engineering Significance:** A 28 % cost reduction achieved simultaneously with a perfect DFA Index (100 %) is an outstanding result. In a high-volume production scenario (e.g., 50,000 gear-boxes per year), the annual saving is:
> $$ 50{,}000 \times ₹\ 140 = ₹\ 70{,}00{,}000 \ \text{(₹ 70 lakh per year)} $$
> This demonstrates the **enormous financial leverage** of even a modest DFA exercise on a single sub-assembly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DFMA Application Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for missing or wrong units in any numerical problem. Always state **% , s, ₹, kg, etc.** explicitly.
> 2. **Not stating assumptions** — In DFA problems, you **must** declare $N_{\min}$ and the Boothroyd time-table version used. Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing DFA Index with DFA Efficiency** — They are numerically the same, but students sometimes write "DFA Index = 7 parts" which is wrong. The Index is a **percentage**, not a count.
> 4. **Skipping the DFX step in descriptive answers** — Many students end at the DFA Index calculation, forgetting to mention DFX. Examiners expect at least one line on DFE/DFD/DFS to earn full marks.
> 5. **Not drawing the flowchart in part (b) of long answers** — A textual description *without* a labelled block diagram typically loses 2–3 marks. Always include a Mermaid-style or hand-drawn flow.
> 6. **Miscalculating the Boothroyd sum** — When summing 7 or more times, students often drop one term. Use a calculator and **write each addition step** in the answer script for partial credit.
> 7. **Treating DFA as "just removal of parts"** — DFA is a **functional analysis**; some parts *must* move or separate, and those are legitimately retained. Examiners deduct marks if you recommend removing a *functional* part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFMA Applications — High-Density Revision Sheet for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - [x] **DFMA = DFM + DFA**; both are applied *simultaneously* in modern product design, not in isolation.
> - [x] The **Boothroyd three-question test** is the most asked DFMA tool in KTU exams. Memorise the order: (1) movement, (2) separation/material, (3) fastening.
> - [x] **DFA Index formula:** $\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \dfrac{N_{\min}}{N_a} \times 100$. Always express as a **percentage**.
> - [x] **DFA target:** Industrial designs aim for **≥ 70 % DFA Index**; values above 90 % are considered excellent.
> - [x] **Total assembly time** is the sum of individual Boothroyd handling+insertion times (look-up tables range **1.5 s to 9.0 s** per part for manual assembly).
> - [x] **Cost saving %** = $\dfrac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100$. Always compute **both** absolute and percentage savings.
> - [x] **Lucas DFA method** uses a 16-question checklist with binary 0/1 scoring; primarily a UK automotive tool.
> - [x] **DFX family** includes DFE, DFD, DFS, DFT, DFR, DFL — memorise at least 3 with one-line definitions.
> - [x] **Pugh Concept Selection Matrix** uses a datum (baseline) concept and scores other concepts as + (better), 0 (same), or − (worse) across weighted criteria.
> - [x] **DFM process rules** are *process-specific*: casting needs draft + parting line; machining needs tool access; plastic needs uniform wall + draft; sheet metal needs bend radius ≥ material thickness.
> - [x] **Iterative nature:** DFMA is not one-shot — expect 2–4 redesign loops before reaching the target DFA Index.
> - [x] **Real-world impact:** Boothroyd's HP DeskJet case study reduced parts from 68 → 23 and saved \$100M/year — a great example to cite in long answers.
> - [x] **Engineering drawing** in long answers is **mandatory** for full marks — at least one labelled block diagram per 7-mark sub-question.
> - [x] **KTU favourite exam phrase:** *"Justify your design choice using DFMA logic."* — Always back your design recommendation with a DFM/DFA/DFX principle.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Applications Notes (PEMET522, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
