topics.title: Suitable Material Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suitable Material Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Suitable Material Consideration in DFMA** is the systematic, concurrent engineering process of selecting, evaluating, and specifying engineering materials during the product design phase by simultaneously weighing **manufacturability constraints** (formability, machinability, castability, weldability), **functional performance requirements** (mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical), **assembly compatibility** (joining methods, surface finish, dimensional stability), **life-cycle factors** (cost, availability, recyclability, environmental impact), and **standardization** to achieve an optimum balance between part cost, quality, lead time, and service performance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is treated as a **decision-making framework** that bridges the conceptual design stage with the detailed process planning stage of the Product Development Process (PDP).

## 1.2 Key Material Properties That Drive DFMA Decisions

A designer must interrogate **four families of material attributes** before freezing a material in the Bill of Materials (BOM):

1. **Physical & Chemical Properties** — density ($\rho$), melting point ($T_m$), thermal conductivity ($k$), corrosion resistance, electrical resistivity.
2. **Mechanical Properties** — Young's modulus ($E$), yield strength ($\sigma_y$), ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$), hardness, fatigue limit ($\sigma_f$), toughness ($K_{IC}$), Poisson's ratio ($\nu$).
3. **Manufacturing Process Compatibility** — formability, castability, machinability rating, weldability index, heat-treatability, and response to surface treatments.
4. **Commercial & Environmental Properties** — raw-material cost per kg, scrap value, lead time, ISO/ASME grade availability, REACH/RoHS compliance, embodied carbon, and end-of-life recyclability.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe & Kitchen" View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — Material is to a Design Engineer what Ingredients are to a Chef:**
> A chef does not just ask *"Is tomato tasty?"* — they ask *"Is tomato available in season, within budget, fast to cook, and will it complement the other ingredients on the plate?"* In the same way, a DFMA engineer does not just ask *"Is aluminium strong?"* — they ask *"Can aluminium be cast, machined, welded, and assembled in our factory, at the target cost, within the deadline, while meeting the in-service load?"*

A wrong material choice is exactly like using **kitchen-grade salt in an industrial chemical plant** — it looks similar, it is cheap, but it fails catastrophically because it was never engineered for that operating envelope.

## 1.4 The KTU "Three Cs" Material Selection Triangle

Every material decision in the KTU 2024 syllabus can be mapped onto a triangle whose vertices are:

- **C₁ — Cost** (per piece, not per kg)
- **C₂ — Capability** (can it be made and assembled at the required rate and tolerance?)
- **C₃ — Compliance** (does it satisfy the in-service function and regulatory constraints?)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "best" material is **never** the one with the highest property on any single axis — it is the one that sits closest to the centroid of the triangle when weighted by the design brief.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Systematic Material Selection Procedure (Ashby Methodology Adapted to KTU 2024)

The selection of a suitable material in a DFMA project follows a **four-stage funnel**, originally formalised by **Prof. Michael F. Ashby** and adopted by KTU for the PEMET522 module.

### Stage 1 — Translation of Design Requirements
- Identify **Function** — what the part must *do* (e.g., support a load, conduct heat, store energy).
- Identify **Constraints** — must satisfy conditions such as $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$, $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$, $T \leq T_{max}$.
- Identify **Objectives** — minimise mass, minimise cost, maximise life, or a combination.
- Identify **Free Variables** — choices the designer is free to make (material, geometry, process).

### Stage 2 — Screening on Property Limits
- Eliminate all materials that **fail** any property constraint (e.g., $T_m < 200°C$ for a kitchen-handle, $\sigma_y < 250$ MPa for a structural bracket).
- Use a **material property database** (CES EduPack, GRANTA MI, ASM Handbooks) to draw short-lists.

### Stage 3 — Ranking on Material Indices
- Combine the remaining constraints and objectives into a single **material index** $M$.
- The material with the **highest (or lowest, depending on definition) value of $M$** is the optimal candidate.

### Stage 4 — Documentation of the Final Choice
- Cross-check with manufacturing capability, supplier availability, environmental directives, and previous project history (DFMA house-style data).
- Document the rationale in the **Material Selection Report (MSR)** — a mandatory KTU deliverable.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Material Index — The "Tie-Rod" Classic

Consider a **lightweight tie-rod of length $L$** carrying a tensile load $F$ without yielding and without excessive elastic stretch.

- **Stiffness constraint:** $\delta = \dfrac{F L}{A E} \leq \delta^*$
- **Strength constraint:** $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$
- **Mass to be minimised:** $m = \rho A L$

From the stiffness constraint: $A \geq \dfrac{F L}{E \delta^*}$

Substituting into the mass expression:
$$m \geq \dfrac{F L^2 \rho}{E \delta^*}$$

To **minimise $m$** for fixed $F$, $L$, and $\delta^*$, we must **maximise the ratio $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Index for a light, stiff tie-rod:** $\boxed{M_1 = \dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ — this is the **Specific Stiffness**.

By repeating the analysis with a different objective (e.g., minimise cost) or a different constraint (e.g., buckling), we obtain the family of indices listed in the cheat-sheet below.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the pipe-free delimiters `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table layout.

| # | Design Function | Governing Constraint | Objective | Material Index $M$ | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tie-rod** (tensile) | Stiffness $\delta \leq \delta^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E / \rho$ | Specific stiffness |
| 2 | **Tie-rod** (tensile) | Strength $\sigma \leq \sigma_y / n$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | Specific strength |
| 3 | **Beam** (bending) | Deflection $\delta \leq \delta^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Light-stiff beam |
| 4 | **Beam** (bending) | Strength $\sigma \leq \sigma_y / n$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$ | Light-strong beam |
| 5 | **Plate** (bending) | Stiffness $S \leq S^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Light-stiff plate |
| 6 | **Column** (Euler) | Buckling $P \leq P_{cr}$ | Min mass $m$ | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Light column |
| 7 | **Torsion shaft** | Angle of twist $\theta \leq \theta^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $G^{1/2} / \rho$ | Light torsional shaft |
| 8 | **Spring** (energy storage) | Strain energy $U \geq U^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $\sigma_y^{2} / (E \rho)$ | Best spring |
| 9 | **Thermal insulation** | Heat flux $q \leq q^*$ | Min cost $C$ | $1 / (k \cdot C_m)$ | Cheap insulator |
| 10 | **Heat sink** | $\Delta T \leq \Delta T^*$ | Min mass $m$ | $k / (\rho \cdot C_p)$ | Thermal mass index |

**Subscript meanings in the table above:**
- $E$ = Young's modulus, $G$ = Shear modulus, $\rho$ = density
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength, $n$ = Factor of safety
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity, $C_p$ = Specific heat capacity
- $C_m$ = Cost per kg of material

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ashby methodology is used industry-wide in:

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Toyota):** selecting Al-alloys and HSS for body-in-white to reduce mass and improve crashworthiness.
- **Aerospace (e.g., HAL, Boeing, Airbus):** choosing CFRP and Ti-alloys for wing skins and landing-gear beams.
- **Consumer electronics (e.g., Apple, Samsung):** Mg-alloys and glass-fibre reinforced polymers for thin, stiff laptop chassis.
- **Medical implants (e.g., Stryker, TTK Healthcare):** Ti-6Al-4V and PEEK for biocompatible, fatigue-resistant implants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Choosing the correct material index early in DFMA can **reduce part cost by 15–30 %** and **mass by 20–50 %** without changing the production process. Conversely, choosing the wrong index is the **#1 reason** for design iterations in the KTU 2024 capstone project.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Specific Stiffness Index for a Light Stiff Beam

We want a beam of **square cross-section $b \times b$** and length $L$ that supports a transverse end load $F$ with maximum deflection $\delta^*$.

### Step 1 — Write the governing equation for deflection
For a cantilever beam with end load:
$$\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area.

### Step 2 — Substitute the second moment of area
For a square section of side $b$:
$$I = \dfrac{b^{4}}{12}$$

So the deflection becomes:
$$\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E \cdot \dfrac{b^{4}}{12}} = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b^{4}}$$

### Step 3 — Impose the deflection constraint $\delta \leq \delta^*$

$$\dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b^{4}} \leq \delta^*$$

Rearranging for the minimum required side $b$:
$$b^{4} \geq \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E \delta^*}$$

$$b \geq \left( \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E \delta^*} \right)^{1/4}$$

### Step 4 — Write the mass of the beam
$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot b^{2} \cdot L$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the minimum value of $b$ from Step 3

$$m \geq \rho L \left( \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E \delta^*} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$m \geq \rho L \cdot (4 F)^{1/2} \cdot L^{3/2} \cdot (E \delta^*)^{-1/2}$$

### Step 6 — Collect the constant terms
Constants for a given design brief: $F$, $L$, $\delta^*$, and the numerical $4^{1/2} = 2$.

$$m \geq (2 F^{1/2} L^{5/2} (\delta^*)^{-1/2}) \cdot \rho \cdot E^{-1/2}$$

### Step 7 — Identify the material index
The bracketed constants are **fixed for the design**. To minimise $m$, we must **minimise the term $\rho \cdot E^{-1/2}$**, which is equivalent to **maximising the inverse term $E^{1/2} / \rho$**.

$$\boxed{M_{\text{light-stiff beam}} = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}}$$

### Step 8 — Verification with a numerical example
Consider two candidate materials:

- **Material A — Aluminium 6061-T6:** $E = 69 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- **Material B — Steel A36:** $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

Calculate the index $M$:

$$M_A = \dfrac{(69 \times 10^{9})^{1/2}}{2700} = \dfrac{2.627 \times 10^{5}}{2700} \approx 97.3 \;\text{Pa}^{1/2}\cdot\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

$$M_B = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^{9})^{1/2}}{7850} = \dfrac{4.472 \times 10^{5}}{7850} \approx 56.9 \;\text{Pa}^{1/2}\cdot\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

**Conclusion:** $M_A > M_B$ — Aluminium 6061-T6 gives a **lighter beam** for the same stiffness, even though steel is *stiffer in absolute terms*. This is the essence of the material index methodology: **always optimise the ratio, never the individual property.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Manufacturing Process Compatibility

> [!NOTE]
> This compatibility matrix is a **high-yield table for KTU ESE Part A questions.** Memorise the relative trends, not exact numbers.

| Material Family | Castability | Weldability | Machinability | Formability | Heat-Treatable | Recyclability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Carbon Steel** | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Yes | High |
| **Stainless Steel (304)** | Fair | Good | Fair | Good | Limited | High |
| **Aluminium 6061** | Good | Good | Excellent | Good | Yes (T6) | High |
| **Aluminium Cast (A380)** | Excellent | Poor | Good | Poor | No | High |
| **Grey Cast Iron** | Excellent | Poor (pre-heat) | Fair | None | No | High |
| **Copper / Brass** | Good | Good | Fair | Excellent | No | High |
| **Magnesium AZ91** | Good | Fair | Excellent | Fair (warm) | No | High |
| **Engineering Plastics (ABS, PA66)** | N/A | Heat-weldable | Excellent (moulded) | N/A | N/A | Medium |
| **CFRP Composite** | Lay-up only | Adhesive bond | Diamond tool | Lay-up | N/A | Poor |

## 3.3 Cost-Per-Piece Calculation Framework

KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask for the **cost-per-finished-piece** rather than raw material cost. The complete formula is:

$$C_{\text{piece}} = C_m \cdot m + C_{\text{process}} + C_{\text{tooling}} + C_{\text{assembly}} + C_{\text{reject}}$$

where each term is itself a function of the chosen material:

- $C_m \cdot m$ — **Material cost** (kg × ₹/kg); aluminium is ~3× cheaper per kg than titanium, but the lower density of Al can flip the per-piece cost equation.
- $C_{\text{process}}$ — **Process cost**; e.g., machining Ti-6Al-4V is 8–10× slower than mild steel.
- $C_{\text{tooling}}$ — **Tool wear**; abrasive composites wear carbide tools rapidly.
- $C_{\text{assembly}}$ — **Joining cost**; dissimilar materials may need bi-metallic fasteners or specialised adhesives.
- $C_{\text{reject}}$ — **Quality cost**; high-defect-rate processes inflate this term exponentially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Always normalise the material decision to **cost-per-piece**, never to **cost-per-kg**. A material that is 5× costlier per kg may actually be **cheaper per piece** if it allows a 70 % reduction in machining time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The DFMA Material Consideration Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Brief: Function Constraints Objectives] --> B[Stage 1: Translation of Requirements]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Screening on Property Limits]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Ranking on Material Index M]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Cross-Check with Process and Assembly Capability]
    E --> F[Final Material Selected and Documented in MSR]
    F --> G[Concurrent Loop: Cost, Manufacturability, Compliance]
    G --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The "Three Cs" Material Selection Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Cost per Piece] --> Triangle((Material Choice Decision Centroid))
    C2[Capability: Form Cast Machine Weld Assemble] --> Triangle
    C3[Compliance: Function Reliability Regulation] --> Triangle
    Triangle --> Out[Optimum Material Selection]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Influence of Material Choice Across the Product Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph L0[Product Lifecycle Stages]
        L1[Concept Design]
        L2[Detailed Design]
        L3[Manufacturing]
        L4[Assembly]
        L5[In Service Use]
        L6[End of Life Recycling]
    end
    subgraph L1A[Material Decision Influences]
        M1[Mechanical Property Match]
        M2[Process Window Selection]
        M3[Joining Method Selection]
        M4[Corrosion Wear Fatigue Life]
        M5[Disassembly and Recyclability Code]
    end
    L1 --> M1
    L2 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L3 --> M2
    L3 --> M3
    L4 --> M3
    L5 --> M4
    L6 --> M5
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Material Family Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the part load-bearing under dynamic load?] -->|Yes| Q2[Is corrosion resistance critical?]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Is thermal insulation or electrical isolation needed?]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Stainless Steel or Titanium Family]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[High Strength Steel or Aluminium Alloy]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6[Engineering Plastics or Composites]
    Q3 -->|No| Q7[Cost Sensitive Non-Load Part: Mild Steel or Cast Iron]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Every rectangular node is alphanumeric (e.g., `Q1`, `M5`) to remain compatible with the Mermaid rendering engine. All labels are pure uppercase text, with no bold markers, italics, or HTML tables embedded.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]
**Q: List any three material properties that must be considered during the DFMA stage of product design, with one-sentence justification for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Machinability** (1 mark) — directly determines cycle time, tool wear, and hence the per-piece cost of every machined part; poor machinability inflates the assembly cost through tighter tolerance stacks.
2. **Weldability / Joinability** (1 mark) — governs whether the part can be economically assembled with the mating components; a non-weldable material may force a costly redesign to mechanical fastening.
3. **Recyclability / Environmental compliance** (1 mark) — mandated by E-Waste Management Rules 2022 and RoHS; an unsuitable material may render the entire product non-compliant for the EU export market.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]
**Q: What is a "material index" in the Ashby methodology? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **material index** is a combination of material properties (and only material properties) that **maximises or minimises the performance metric** of a component when all design constants and geometric parameters are fixed (1 mark).
- It is derived by **substituting design constraints into the objective function** and grouping the material-dependent terms together (1 mark).
- **Example:** For a light, stiff, tensile tie-rod, the material index is $M_1 = E / \rho$ (specific stiffness). The material with the highest $E/\rho$ gives the lightest tie-rod for a prescribed stiffness (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]

**A(a)** Define **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**. Explain the role of **material selection** in achieving the goals of DFMA. List the **four families of material properties** a designer must evaluate. **(7 marks)**

**A(b)** For a **lightweight cantilever beam of square cross-section**, derive the material index $M$ that should be maximised to obtain minimum mass under a stiffness constraint. Use the index to **compare Aluminium 6061-T6** ($E = 69$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³) **with Steel A36** ($E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³). Conclude which material is preferred and why. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)
1. **Definition of DFMA (2 marks):** DFMA is a concurrent engineering methodology that integrates design for manufacture (DFM) and design for assembly (DFA) to simultaneously optimise part design for ease of **fabrication, assembly, testing, and end-of-life disassembly** while meeting functional and quality requirements at minimum total cost.
2. **Role of material selection in DFMA (2 marks):** Material choice is the **upstream parameter** that fixes every downstream cost — process selection, tool life, joining method, inspection cost, in-service life, and recycling revenue. A correct early-stage material decision amplifies all later DFMA gains; a wrong one nullifies them.
3. **Four families of material properties (3 marks, 0.75 each):**
   - **Physical & Chemical** — density, melting point, thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance.
   - **Mechanical** — Young's modulus, yield strength, fatigue limit, hardness, toughness.
   - **Manufacturing Process Compatibility** — formability, castability, machinability, weldability, heat-treatability.
   - **Commercial & Environmental** — cost per kg, availability, lead time, recyclability, regulatory compliance (REACH, RoHS).

#### Model Solution — A(b)
1. **Setup of the problem (1 mark):** Let the beam have square cross-section $b \times b$ and length $L$, with end load $F$ and allowable deflection $\delta^*$.
2. **Deflection equation (1 mark):** $\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b^{4}}$
3. **Constraint and minimum $b$ (2 marks):** $\delta \leq \delta^* \;\Rightarrow\; b \geq \left( \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E \delta^*} \right)^{1/4}$
4. **Mass expression and substitution (2 marks):** $m = \rho b^{2} L \;\Rightarrow\; m \geq \rho L \left( \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E \delta^*} \right)^{1/2}$, so the material index is $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$.
5. **Numerical comparison (1 mark):** As derived in Section 3.1, $M_A \approx 97.3$ and $M_B \approx 56.9$, hence **Aluminium 6061-T6 is the preferred material** for minimum-mass light-stiff beam design.

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]

**B(a)** Explain the **four-stage Ashby methodology** for systematic material selection. State the output deliverable for each stage. **(7 marks)**

**B(b)** A **bracket** is to be designed for a mass-critical aerospace application. The bracket is a **flat plate of fixed length $L$ and width $W$**, with thickness $h$ as the free variable. It must support a **bending load** such that the **bending stiffness $S \geq S^*$**. Using $S = \dfrac{E W h^{3}}{12 L^{3}}$ and $m = \rho W h L$, derive the material index and conclude which material property combination should be maximised. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)
1. **Stage 1 — Translation (1.75 marks):** Convert the design brief into a formal statement of *function*, *constraints*, *objectives*, and *free variables*. **Output:** a clear, quantified design specification.
2. **Stage 2 — Screening (1.75 marks):** Eliminate all materials that violate any property limit (e.g., $T_m < T_{max}$, $E < E_{min}$). **Output:** a short-list of candidate materials (typically 5–15).
3. **Stage 3 — Ranking (1.75 marks):** Rank the short-list using a **material index** $M$ derived analytically. **Output:** a ranked list and a **material property chart** (Ashby bubble chart).
4. **Stage 4 — Documentation (1.75 marks):** Cross-check with process capability, supplier availability, and environmental directives; document the choice in the **Material Selection Report (MSR)**. **Output:** a finalised material grade and a one-page rationale.

#### Model Solution — B(b)
1. **State the constraint (1.5 marks):** $S = \dfrac{E W h^{3}}{12 L^{3}} \geq S^*$, so $h^{3} \geq \dfrac{12 S^* L^{3}}{E W}$, hence $h \geq \left( \dfrac{12 S^* L^{3}}{E W} \right)^{1/3}$.
2. **Mass expression (1.5 marks):** $m = \rho W h L$.
3. **Substitute the minimum $h$ (2 marks):**

$$m \geq \rho W L \left( \dfrac{12 S^* L^{3}}{E W} \right)^{1/3}$$

4. **Simplify and identify the index (1.5 marks):** $m \geq (12 S^* L^3 W^{-1})^{1/3} \cdot L W \cdot \rho \cdot E^{-1/3}$. The constants in parentheses are fixed; minimise $\rho \cdot E^{-1/3}$ by maximising its inverse.
5. **Final material index (0.5 mark):** $\boxed{M_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}}$ — this is the **light-stiff plate index** listed in the KTU formula sheet.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Do not write $M = E/\rho$ for a plate** — students frequently confuse the tie-rod and the plate indices. The plate index has a **cube root on $E$** because $h$ enters the stiffness equation with the **third power**, not linearly. *Loss: 2–3 marks per occurrence.*
> 2. **Never omit the mass equation** before substituting the geometric constraint — examiners award a full mark for the mass line and another full mark for the substitution step. A direct jump from constraint to index is treated as *incomplete derivation*.
> 3. **Do not quote raw-material cost per kg** as the final answer in cost questions — KTU 2024 expects **cost-per-piece**, which includes process and tooling. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 4. **Always quote units** of the material index (e.g., $\text{Pa}^{1/3}\cdot\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$). Omitting units is treated as a *dimensional-analysis oversight*.
> 5. **Mention the "Why" alongside the "How"** in 14-mark answers — pure derivations without an engineering commentary score ~10/14. A concluding sentence such as *"Hence, the material with the highest $E^{1/3}/\rho$ minimises plate mass under bending stiffness"* earns the remaining 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as the **last 5-minute glance** before walking into the KTU ESE hall.

- [ ] **DFMA material consideration** = systematic trade-off between **Cost, Capability, and Compliance** (the *Three Cs* triangle).
- [ ] The **Ashby four-stage funnel**: *Translation → Screening → Ranking → Documentation*.
- [ ] **Material Index $M$** is *always* a combination of material properties (and only material properties); the geometric and load terms are factored out as constants.
- [ ] **Tie-rod (tensile + stiffness):** $M = E / \rho$ — Specific Stiffness.
- [ ] **Tie-rod (tensile + strength):** $M = \sigma_y / \rho$ — Specific Strength.
- [ ] **Beam (bending + stiffness):** $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Beam (bending + strength):** $M = \sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Plate (bending + stiffness):** $M = E^{1/3} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Column (Euler buckling):** $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Torsion shaft (twist):** $M = G^{1/2} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Cost-per-piece formula:** $C_{\text{piece}} = C_m m + C_{\text{process}} + C_{\text{tooling}} + C_{\text{assembly}} + C_{\text{reject}}$.
- [ ] **Always normalise cost to per-piece, never per-kg.**
- [ ] **Property families to remember:** Physical/Chemical, Mechanical, Process, Commercial/Environmental.
- [ ] **Engineering plastics and composites** are usually chosen for **non-structural, mass-critical, or corrosive-environment** applications; **steels and Ti-alloys** dominate **high-temperature, high-fatigue, safety-critical** applications.
- [ ] **Material Selection Report (MSR)** is the **mandatory KTU 2024 deliverable** to close the design loop.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** A material with the *highest* single property is *not* the optimal choice — **the ratio is what matters**.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** Plate and beam indices are *different* — beam has $E^{1/2}$, plate has $E^{1/3}$ in the numerator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Design Recommendations" in DFMA?

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, the term **"Design Recommendations"** refers to a structured set of **empirical rules, geometric guidelines, tolerance principles, and material-process alignment strategies** that a designer must follow to ensure a component can be produced economically, accurately, and reliably using a specific manufacturing process. These recommendations form the bridge between the conceptual CAD model and a shop-floor manufacturable part.

Formally, design recommendations are **process-specific heuristics** derived from:
- **Dimensional capability curves** of machines
- **Tool wear and accessibility constraints**
- **Shrinkage, draft, and distortion behaviors**
- **Metallurgical phenomena** (grain flow, porosity, residual stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Design recommendations for **Casting, Forging, Welding, and Sheet Metal Working** form the core of this module. The emphasis is on translating *practical shop-floor constraints* into *upstream design decisions* so that cost of production, cycle time, and rejection rates are minimized **without sacrificing functional performance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

Imagine you are writing a recipe to bake a cake. The recipe says: *"use a round tin, do not fill more than 2/3rd, grease the sides lightly, bake at 180°C"*. You can ignore these rules and bake freely, but you will end up with a sticky, broken, or undercooked cake.

**Design recommendations are EXACTLY these "recipes" for manufacturing processes.**

- A *casting* needs a draft angle, just like a cake needs a greased tin — otherwise the part cannot be extracted.
- A *forging* needs generous fillet radii, just like a cake needs a smooth batter — sharp corners cause cracks.
- A *weld joint* needs proper edge preparation, just like a cake needs leveled batter — to avoid weak zones.
- A *sheet metal bend* needs a minimum bend radius, just like a cake needs proper proportions — to avoid tearing.

The genius of DFMA is that it shifts these "recipe rules" **upstream into the design stage**, so the product is *born manufacturable* rather than being "forced" into manufacture.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Design Recommendations?

| Problem in Industry | Root Cause | Recommendation That Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| Castings crack at sharp corners | Stress concentration + shrinkage restraint | Use generous **fillet radii** (≥ 3 mm) at all internal corners |
| Sheet metal tears at bends | Fiber strain > limiting strain | Maintain bend radius **r ≥ t (sheet thickness)** |
| Welds distort the assembly | Uneven heating/cooling | Use **symmetric joint design** and balanced welding sequence |
| Forgings develop folds/laps | Improper material flow | Provide **draft, radii, and proper web thickness** |
| Machined parts are rejected | Tolerance tighter than machine capability | Design within **process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Design Recommendations are process-specific geometric and tolerance guidelines formulated to ensure that a component is producible with minimum cost, minimum scrap, and consistent quality using the chosen manufacturing route."*

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Module 4

```
Module 4 - Design Recommendations
        │
        ├──► Pillar 1: Design for Casting (DFC)
        ├──► Pillar 2: Design for Forging (DFF)
        ├──► Pillar 3: Design for Welding (DFW)
        └──► Pillar 4: Design for Sheet Metal (DFSM)
```

Each pillar addresses:
1. **Process capability envelope**
2. **Geometric rules** (draft, radii, web/rib ratios)
3. **Tolerance guidance**
4. **Common defects and their design remedies**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Design for Casting (DFC)

Casting involves pouring molten metal into a mold. The designer must account for **fluid flow, solidification shrinkage, and pattern removal**.

### 2.1.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Wall Thickness** | As **uniform** as possible; ideal range **5–25 mm** for sand casting | Uneven walls cause **hot spots** → shrinkage porosity |
| **Section Thickness Ratio** | Adjacent sections: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2:1$ to $1.5:1$ | Prevents thermal gradient → reduces stress |
| **Fillet Radius** | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ at junctions | Avoids stress concentration |
| **Draft Angle** | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (external), $2°$ to $5°$ (internal) | Allows easy pattern withdrawal |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall}$ to $0.8 \, t_{wall}$ | Prevents hot spot at rib base |
| **Boss Thickness** | $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ | Adequate strength for fastening |

### 2.1.2 The "I-Section" Rule

For a flat plate requiring rigidity, instead of increasing thickness, use a **Tee or I-section**:

$$
t_{rib} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \times t_{wall}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks *"Suggest a design modification to eliminate shrinkage porosity in a cast bracket."* The model answer: *Replace uniform thick section with a T-section having rib thickness = 0.7 × wall thickness, and add fillet radii at all junctions.*

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Design for Forging (DFF)

Forging plastically deforms metal between dies. Material flow is paramount.

### 2.2.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Draft Angle** | $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die forging) | Die separation; punch clearance |
| **Fillet Radius (External)** | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ where $h$ = depth | Material flow into corner |
| **Fillet Radius (Internal)** | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Reduces die stress, prevents crack |
| **Web Thickness** | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ where $W$ = width | Avoid folding/lapping |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$ | Ensures full die fill |
| **Corner Radius Ratio** | $\dfrac{r_i}{h} \leq 0.15$ | Die life optimization |
| **Parting Line** | Should be a **straight plane** through widest cross-section | Simplifies die making |

### 2.2.2 Grain Flow Principle

In forging, **grain flow follows the contour** of the part. Design should ensure that:
- Fiber direction is **parallel to the principal stress** (tensile axis)
- Avoid abrupt changes in cross-section
- Avoid **sharp corners** that interrupt grain flow

$$
\text{Maximum allowable rib height} = 5 \times t_{rib}
$$

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Design for Welding (DFW)

Welding creates a **localized metallurgical bond** through fusion. Heat input, joint geometry, and accessibility govern weld quality.

### 2.3.1 Key Joint Design Rules

| Joint Type | Symbol | Strength Efficiency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Butt Joint (Square) | — | 70–80 % | Thin sheets (< 3 mm) |
| Butt Joint (V-groove) | V | 95–100 % | Plates > 3 mm |
| T-Joint (Fillet) | ⊥ | 60–70 % | Structural brackets |
| Lap Joint (Fillet) | ⫽ | 50–65 % | Sheet assemblies |
| Corner Joint | ⌐ | 70–80 % | Boxes, frames |

### 2.3.2 Fillet Weld Geometry

For a fillet weld of leg size $s$ and throat thickness $t$:

$$
t = 0.707 \, s
$$

> This is the **single most important formula** in welding design — KTU loves asking it.

### 2.3.3 Weld Strength Formula

For a fillet weld of length $L$, leg size $s$, in shear:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}
$$

where:
- $P$ = load capacity (N)
- $s$ = leg size (mm)
- $L$ = weld length (mm)
- $\tau_{allow}$ = allowable shear stress (MPa)

### 2.3.4 Critical Design Recommendations

- **Minimum weld leg size**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ where $t_{min}$ is the thinner plate
- **Maximum weld leg size**: For plates $\leq$ 6 mm, $s_{max} = t_{plate}$
- **Pitch of intermittent welds**: $p \leq 16 \, t$ for compression; $p \leq 24 \, t$ for tension (avoid)
- **Weld should not be located at high stress concentration zones**
- **Avoid weld intersection** — causes 4-direction stress

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Design for Sheet Metal Working (DFSM)

Sheet metal operations include **bending, drawing, piercing, blanking, and stretching**.

### 2.4.1 The K-Factor (Neutral Axis)

When a sheet is bent, the **outer surface stretches** and the **inner surface compresses**. The **neutral axis** lies somewhere between them, defined by the **K-factor**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

where $t_{neutral}$ is the distance from inner surface to neutral axis.

**Standard K-factor values:**

| Material | K-factor |
|---|---|
| Soft brass, aluminum | 0.33 |
| Mild steel | 0.40–0.45 |
| Stainless steel | 0.45–0.50 |
| Hard brass, bronze | 0.50 |

### 2.4.2 Bend Allowance (BA) — The Master Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

where:
- $\theta$ = bend angle (degrees)
- $R$ = inside bend radius
- $K$ = K-factor
- $t$ = sheet thickness

### 2.4.3 Minimum Bend Radius

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t \cdot E_{outer}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}} \quad \text{(theoretical, no safety factor)}
$$

**Practical rule** (used in industry and KTU exams):

$$
R_{min} \approx 1 \times t \quad \text{(for soft materials like aluminum)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 2 \times t \quad \text{(for mild steel)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 3 \times t \quad \text{(for stainless steel, hard brass)}
$$

### 2.4.4 Hole-to-Edge and Hole-to-Hole Distances

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum distance from hole center to edge)}
$$
$$
d_{hole} \geq 3 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum pitch between hole centers)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** If the edge distance is too small, the sheet **tears** during piercing. If holes are too close, the **inter-hole web distorts**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Process | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Casting | $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ | Adjacent thicknesses | mm |
| 2 | Casting | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1+t_2}{4}$ | Fillet radius | mm |
| 3 | Casting | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ | Draft angle | degrees |
| 4 | Forging | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ | External fillet | mm |
| 5 | Forging | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Internal fillet | mm |
| 6 | Forging | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ | Web thickness | mm |
| 7 | Welding | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | Fillet throat | mm |
| 8 | Welding | $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$ | Weld load | N |
| 9 | Welding | $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ | Min leg size | mm |
| 10 | Sheet Metal | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \theta (R + K t)$ | Bend allowance | mm |
| 11 | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = n \cdot t$ | Min bend radius | mm |
| 12 | Sheet Metal | $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ | Edge distance | mm |
| 13 | Sheet Metal | $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$ | Hole pitch | mm |
| 14 | Casting | $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ | Rib design | mm |
| 15 | Forging | $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$ | Rib height | mm |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These recommendations are not academic luxuries — they save **billions of dollars** annually:

- **Automotive industry**: An engine block with improper rib ratios can crack within weeks. A bracket with wrong fillet can fail in fatigue.
- **Aerospace**: Wing ribs in forged aluminum must follow $r \geq 0.1 \, h$ to maintain grain flow for fatigue life.
- **Pressure vessels**: Welding of LPG cylinders follows $s = 0.7 t$ to ensure full penetration without burn-through.
- **Consumer electronics**: Sheet metal laptop chassis use $K = 0.45$ for aluminum to predict bend behavior in stamping simulation.
- **Power transmission**: Cast transformer housings use section thickness ratio $\leq 2$ to prevent shrinkage porosity at junction of body and flange.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"How would you improve the manufacturability of this component?"*, the answer should ALWAYS quote at least one **numerical recommendation** with units, plus a one-line justification. Vague answers like "add fillets" score 1/7. "Add fillet radius ≥ (t1+t2)/4 to reduce stress concentration and shrinkage porosity" scores 4/7.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fillet Weld Throat Thickness

**Problem Statement:** A fillet weld of leg size **s = 10 mm** is deposited on a structural bracket. Derive the throat thickness and the load it can carry if the weld length is **L = 200 mm** and allowable shear stress is **$\tau_{allow}$ = 110 MPa**.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

A fillet weld has a triangular cross-section with two equal legs of length $s$ meeting at a right angle. The **throat** is the shortest distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (the weld face).

### Step 2: Apply Trigonometry

The throat $t$ is the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse. The hypotenuse length is:

$$
h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

For a right-angled isosceles triangle, the perpendicular from the right angle to the hypotenuse equals:

$$
t = \dfrac{s \cdot s}{h} = \dfrac{s^2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Rationalize the Denominator

$$
t = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, s
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Load Capacity

The weld is in shear along the throat plane of area $A = t \times L$:

$$
A = 7.07 \times 200 = 1414 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 110 \times 1414
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 155.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Step 6: Verification

Check using the master formula directly:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow} = 0.707 \times 10 \times 200 \times 110
$$
$$
P = 0.707 \times 220{,}000 = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** This derivation shows that the **effective load-bearing area** of a fillet weld is only **70.7 %** of the apparent cross-section. This is why fillet welds are weaker than butt welds of the same plate thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bend Allowance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is bent through an angle of **90°** with an inside bend radius of **R = 4 mm**. The K-factor for mild steel is **0.45**. Calculate the bend allowance.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Bend Allowance Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (4 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Inside the Bracket

$$
R + K \cdot t = 4 + 0.45 \times 2 = 4 + 0.9 = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Angle Factor

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708
$$

### Step 6: Compute Final Result

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times 4.9 = 7.697 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{BA \approx 7.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation

If a designer wants a final **flat length of 100 mm** for the bent sheet (along the outer dimensions), the **flat blank length** required is:

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA
$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the straight portions before and after the bend.

**Example:** If each straight leg is 50 mm:

$$
L_{blank} = 50 + 50 + 7.70 = 107.70 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the **inside radius R** with the **neutral radius**. The neutral radius is $R + Kt$, NOT just $R$. Failing to add $Kt$ leads to an undersized blank and a part that is too short after bending.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Bend Radius for a Sheet

**Problem Statement:** A 1.5 mm thick aluminum sheet (UTS = 110 MPa) is to be bent at room temperature. The modulus of elasticity in tension on the outer fiber can be approximated by the strain-hardening modulus. Calculate the **minimum bend radius** to avoid surface cracking, using the rule of thumb $R_{min} \approx n \cdot t$ and verify with the theoretical formula.

### Step 1: Theoretical Strain Analysis

The maximum strain on the outer surface of a bent sheet is:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R + t} \approx \dfrac{t}{2R} \quad (\text{when } R \gg t)
$$

For aluminum, the **limiting strain** before cracking is approximately:

$$
\varepsilon_{limit} \approx 0.4 \quad (\text{for soft aluminum})
$$

### Step 2: Theoretical Formula

Setting $\varepsilon_{max} = \varepsilon_{limit}$:

$$
\dfrac{t}{2R_{min}} = \varepsilon_{limit}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{limit}} = \dfrac{1.5}{2 \times 0.4} = 1.875 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Rule of Thumb

For soft aluminum, $R_{min} \approx 1 \times t$:

$$
R_{min} = 1 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Safety Check

The practical rule gives a smaller radius (1.5 mm) than the theoretical (1.875 mm). For **safety**, we choose the larger value:

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = 1.875 \text{ mm} \approx 2 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 5: Design Decision

In practice, the designer would specify **R = 2 mm or higher** to ensure no surface cracking during bending. This is well within the "**R = t**" rule but provides a safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The relationship $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ is a favorite board question. The student is expected to (a) state the formula, (b) apply it with the limiting strain, and (c) interpret the result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Recommendation Mapping

| Defect Observed | Process | Design Recommendation That Prevents It |
|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage porosity at junction | Casting | Use **fillet radius** $r \geq (t_1+t_2)/4$; reduce section thickness ratio to $\leq 2$ |
| Hot tear at corner | Casting | Add **fillet and chill**; uniform cooling |
| Pattern damage on withdrawal | Casting | Provide **draft angle** $1°$ to $3°$ |
| Fold / lap in rib | Forging | Maintain $t_{web} \geq 0.15W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 t_{rib}$ |
| Die filling incomplete | Forging | Increase fillet radii; provide proper draft $5°$ to $7°$ |
| Cracking at weld root | Welding | Use **V-groove** for plates > 3 mm; full penetration |
| Distortion in welded plate | Welding | Use **symmetric joint**; intermittent welds; balanced sequence |
| Tearing at bend | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = t$ to $3t$ depending on material |
| Springback error | Sheet Metal | Overbend by $1°$ to $3°$; use lower K-factor |
| Edge tearing during piercing | Sheet Metal | Maintain $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Comparison: With vs Without DFMA

Consider a cast iron bracket with wall thickness 20 mm meeting a flange of 30 mm.

| Parameter | Without DFMA | With DFMA |
|---|---|---|
| Section ratio $t_2/t_1$ | 30/20 = 1.5 (acceptable) | — |
| Fillet radius | 0 (sharp corner) | $r \geq (20+30)/4 = 12.5$ mm |
| Rib thickness | Full 20 mm (hot spot) | $0.7 \times 20 = 14$ mm |
| Draft angle | $0°$ (pattern stuck) | $2°$ external, $3°$ internal |
| Expected defect | Shrinkage cavity, pattern damage | Defect-free casting |
| Cost impact | Rejection + rework | First-time-right |

This matrix is the **standard structure** for KTU 14-mark questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Explain the importance of draft angle in sand casting. State typical draft values for external and internal surfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Draft angle is the **taper provided on the vertical walls of a pattern** to enable its easy withdrawal from the sand mold without damaging the mold cavity.

- **External surfaces**: $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (pattern lifts easily)
- **Internal surfaces**: $\alpha = 2°$ to $5°$ (cores require more draft for removal)
- **Minimum draft for intricate patterns**: Up to $5°$

**Significance:** Without draft, the pattern rubs against sand walls, causing **mold erosion, dimensional inaccuracy, and surface defects**. Draft also accounts for **mold wall expansion** during pouring.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define K-factor. State the typical range of K-factor for mild steel and aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance of the **neutral axis** from the inner surface of the sheet to the **material thickness**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

- **Aluminum, soft brass**: $K = 0.33$
- **Mild steel**: $K = 0.40$ to $0.45$
- **Stainless steel, hard brass**: $K = 0.45$ to $0.50$

**Significance:** K-factor is essential for calculating **bend allowance** and predicting **flat blank length** before bending. It represents the location within the sheet thickness where neither stretching nor compression occurs during bending.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Casting + Forging Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A cast iron bracket has a uniform wall thickness of 25 mm. A boss of 40 mm diameter is to be added at one location for a bearing housing. As a design engineer, suggest modifications to:
1. Avoid shrinkage porosity at the boss-wall junction
2. Ensure easy pattern withdrawal
3. Maintain adequate strength

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Section thickness ratio check:**
The boss (40 mm) is essentially a thick cylinder attached to a 25 mm wall. Without modification, the ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6
$$

This is borderline. To make it safe, **reduce boss diameter** to 32–35 mm or add a **transition ring**.

**Step 2 — Add fillet radius:**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4} = \dfrac{25 + 40}{4} = 16.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r = 16$ mm at the boss-wall junction. This **reduces stress concentration** and **eliminates hot spot** at the junction, preventing shrinkage porosity.

**Step 3 — Add draft angle:**
For external boss surface: $\alpha = 2°$ (external)
For internal bore: $\alpha = 3°$ (internal)

**Step 4 — Boss thickness control:**
Boss wall thickness = $1.5 \times 25 = 37.5$ mm. Since boss OD is 40 mm, this means inner bore = 40 - 2(37.5) = negative. **Modify boss design**: Use a boss OD of 60 mm so that wall thickness = 10 mm (the inner bore of bearing fits) and $t_{boss} = 1.5 \times t_{wall} = 37.5$ mm is met by adjusting boss height/diameter.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Modification | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Fillet radius at junction | 16 mm | Reduce stress + hot spot |
| Section ratio | $\leq 2$ (achieved by reducing boss) | Avoid shrinkage |
| External draft | $2°$ | Pattern removal |
| Internal draft | $3°$ | Core removal |
| Rib at junction (if needed) | 17.5 mm thick | Strengthen without hot spot |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
Explain the key design recommendations for **drop forging** of a connecting rod-shaped component with maximum width $W = 60$ mm and depth $h = 25$ mm. Recommend suitable values for fillet radii, web thickness, and draft angle.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draft angle:**
For impression die forging: $\alpha = 5°$ to $7°$. Adopt $\alpha = 7°$ for safety.

**Step 2 — External fillet radius:**

$$
r_e \geq 0.1 \, h = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_e = 3$ mm.

**Step 3 — Internal fillet radius:**

$$
r_i \geq 0.15 \, h = 0.15 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_i = 4$ mm.

**Step 4 — Web thickness:**

$$
t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W = 0.15 \times 60 = 9 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{web} = 10$ mm.

**Step 5 — Rib thickness:**

$$
t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{rib} = 6$ mm.

**Step 6 — Maximum rib height:**

$$
h_{rib,max} = 5 \times t_{rib} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Parting line:**
Choose a **straight plane** through the **widest cross-section** ($W = 60$ mm plane) for simple die construction.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | $7°$ | Die separation |
| External fillet | $3$ mm | Material flow into corner |
| Internal fillet | $4$ mm | Die stress relief |
| Web thickness | $10$ mm | Avoid folds |
| Rib thickness | $6$ mm | Full die fill |
| Max rib height | $30$ mm | Avoid fold/lap |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding + Sheet Metal Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A fillet weld of leg size $s = 12$ mm is to be deposited along a joint of length $L = 250$ mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is $\tau_{allow} = 100$ MPa. Determine:
1. The throat thickness
2. The load-carrying capacity of the weld
3. The minimum recommended leg size if the thinner plate is 14 mm thick

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t = 0.707 \, s = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.484 \text{ mm} \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective shear area:**

$$
A = t \times L = 8.484 \times 250 = 2121 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Load capacity:**

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 100 \times 2121 = 212{,}100 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 212.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum recommended leg size:**

$$
s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min} = 0.7 \times 14 = 9.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the actual leg size $s = 12$ mm $> s_{min} = 9.8$ mm, the design is **acceptable**.

**Step 5 — Maximum recommended leg size check:**

For plate thickness 14 mm, the maximum leg size for full strength is $s_{max} \approx t_{plate} = 14$ mm. Since $s = 12$ mm $< 14$ mm, the weld is **within safe limits**.

**Step 6 — Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Throat thickness | 8.5 mm | ✓ |
| Weld area | 2121 mm² | ✓ |
| Load capacity | 212.1 kN | ✓ |
| Min leg required | 9.8 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |
| Max leg allowed | 14 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is to be bent at $90°$ with an inside bend radius of $R = 5$ mm. Calculate:
1. The bend allowance (use $K = 0.45$)
2. The flat blank length if each straight leg is 60 mm
3. The minimum recommended edge distance for a hole pierced 4 mm from the bend

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (5 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times (5 + 0.9) = 1.5708 \times 5.9
$$

$$
\boxed{BA = 9.268 \text{ mm} \approx 9.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Flat blank length:**

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA = 60 + 60 + 9.27
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{blank} = 129.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum edge distance for hole:**

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R = 1.5 \times 2 + 5 = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d_{edge} = 8$ mm from bend line to hole center.

**Step 5 — Verification of bend radius:**

For mild steel, $R_{min} \approx 2t = 2 \times 2 = 4$ mm.
Since $R = 5$ mm $> R_{min} = 4$ mm, the bend is **safe**.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Bend allowance | 9.27 mm | — |
| Blank length | 129.27 mm | — |
| Min edge distance | 8 mm | ✓ |
| Min bend radius | 4 mm (reqd) | ✓ (5 mm used) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Design Recommendations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-factor and Neutral Axis position.** K-factor is dimensionless; neutral axis position = $K \times t$ (in mm). Students writing $K = 0.45$ mm lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.707 in fillet weld throat.** Writing $t = s$ instead of $t = 0.707s$ loses 2 marks. Always show the derivation.
>
> 3. **Using inside radius $R$ instead of neutral radius $(R + Kt)$ in bend allowance.** This is the #1 error in sheet metal problems. Always write $(R + Kt)$ explicitly.
>
> 4. **Not specifying units in design recommendations.** "Add fillet radius of 5" is incomplete. "Add fillet radius of **5 mm** to reduce stress concentration" scores full marks.
>
> 5. **Giving vague justifications.** "It will be good for manufacturing" scores 0. "It eliminates hot spot and prevents shrinkage porosity" scores 2/2.
>
> 6. **Mixing up draft angles for casting vs forging.** Casting: $1°$ to $5°$. Forging: $5°$ to $7°$. Many students write $1°$ for forging, which is wrong.
>
> 7. **Ignoring minimum weld leg size formula.** $s_{min} = 0.7 t_{min}$ is mandatory to mention whenever weld design is asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Design Recommendations"**

- [x] **Casting — Section Ratio**: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ (always enforce uniform cooling)
- [x] **Casting — Fillet**: $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ (no sharp internal corners)
- [x] **Casting — Draft**: $1°$ to $3°$ external, $2°$ to $5°$ internal
- [x] **Casting — Rib**: $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ (avoid hot spots)
- [x] **Casting — Boss**: $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ (strength + machining allowance)
- [x] **Forging — Draft**: $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die)
- [x] **Forging — External Fillet**: $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Internal Fillet**: $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Web**: $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$
- [x] **Forging — Rib**: $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$
- [x] **Forging — Parting Line**: straight, through widest cross-section
- [x] **Welding — Throat**: $t = 0.707 \, s$ (single most important formula)
- [x] **Welding — Load**: $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$
- [x] **Welding — Min Leg**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$
- [x] **Welding — Avoid**: weld intersections and high-stress zones
- [x] **Sheet Metal — K-Factor**: Al = 0.33, MS = 0.45, SS = 0.50
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Bend Allowance**: $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \, \theta \, (R + Kt)$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Min Bend Radius**: $R_{min} = 1t$ to $3t$ (material dependent)
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Edge Distance**: $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Hole Pitch**: $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize

| Value | Meaning | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0.707** | Fillet weld throat factor | $t = 0.707s$ |
| **0.7** | Weld min leg factor | $s_{min} = 0.7t$ |
| **0.1** | Forging external fillet factor | $r_e = 0.1h$ |
| **0.15** | Forging internal fillet / web factor | $r_i = 0.15h$, $t_{web} = 0.15W$ |
| **2** | Max section ratio (casting) | $t_2/t_1 \leq 2$ |
| **¼** | Casting fillet factor | $r = (t_1+t_2)/4$ |
| **0.7** | Casting rib factor | $t_{rib} = 0.7t_{wall}$ |
| **1.5** | Casting boss factor | $t_{boss} = 1.5t_{wall}$ |
| **5** | Forging max rib height factor | $h_{rib} \leq 5t_{rib}$ |

### One-Line Definition Recall (for 2-mark definitions)

- **DFC** = Design for Casting — guidelines for uniform walls, fillets, draft, ribs
- **DFF** = Design for Forging — guidelines for material flow, die fill, draft, radii
- **DFW** = Design for Welding — guidelines for joint type, weld size, distortion control
- **DFSM** = Design for Sheet Metal — guidelines for bending, K-factor, blank size

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INJECTION MOULDING: INTRODUCTION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Injection Moulding** is a high-volume, closed-loop, cyclic polymer processing (and limited metal/ceramic) manufacturing process in which a **thermoplastic** or **thermosetting** material is heated to a molten/plasticized state, forced under high pressure into a closed, precision-engineered cavity (the *mould*), held under pressure during cooling/solidification, and finally ejected as a finished, dimensionally accurate part with minimal secondary operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4, Section 4.1):**
> Injection moulding is classified as a **primary shaping process** under the broader family of *material conversion processes* in Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA). The introductory module specifically focuses on the **process concept, machine architecture, cycle sequence, and its design-implication linkage** — students are expected to be able to identify why DFMA principles apply uniquely to injection-moulded parts (e.g., uniform wall thickness, draft angles, parting line placement, gate design).

| Formal Term | Engineering Definition |
|---|---|
| **Shot** | The total volume of material injected in one complete cycle. |
| **Cycle Time (t_c)** | The time elapsed between the start of two consecutive shots. |
| **Mould Cavity** | The negative geometric space inside the tool that defines the part. |
| **Clamping Force (F_c)** | The tonnage applied to keep the mould halves closed against injection pressure. |
| **Injection Pressure (P_i)** | The pressure applied by the screw/ram to push melt into the cavity. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in DFMA:**
> Approximately **30% of all polymer parts** in modern engineering (automotive dashboards, mobile phone housings, medical syringes, gear housings) are produced by injection moulding. Since the **tooling cost is very high** (a single-cavity mould can range from ₹2,00,000 to ₹50,00,000+), every design decision made on paper *before* cutting steel directly determines the part cost, cycle time, and assembly feasibility. This is the central pillar of DFM thinking.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Jello Mould" Analogy

Imagine you have a fancy kitchen mould in the shape of a star, and you want to make 5,000 identical jellies for a wedding.

- You take gelatin powder, mix it with hot water, and **melt it into a pourable liquid** → this is the **plasticization stage**.
- You pour the liquid into the star-shaped mould using a syringe to push it into every tiny corner → this is the **injection stage**.
- You put the mould in the fridge, the jelly **cools and hardens** taking the exact shape of the cavity → this is the **cooling/solidification stage**.
- You open the mould, gently push the jelly out, close the mould, and repeat → this is the **ejection stage** and the start of the **next cycle**.

**The injection moulding machine does exactly this**, except:
- The "syringe" is a heated **screw** capable of generating **>1000 bar** of pressure.
- The "fridge" is replaced by water/oil channels (**cooling lines**) machined inside the mould.
- The "jelly" is engineering-grade polymer (ABS, PP, Nylon, PC) heated to **200–320 °C**.
- The "5,000 jellies" are produced in **15–60 seconds each** with tolerances of ±0.05 mm.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:**
> Think **"P.I.C.H.E"** — the five sequential operations of any injection moulding cycle:
> **P**lasticize → **I**nject → **C**ool/Hold → **H**old (under pressure) → **E**ject.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Injection Moulding Processes

Injection moulding is not a single process — it is a **family of processes** differentiated by how the material is plasticized, the mould architecture, and the driving force:

| Sub-Process | Material | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional Injection Moulding (CIM)** | Thermoplastics | Reciprocating screw, 1–3 cavities | Consumer goods, toys |
| **Thermoset Injection Moulding** | Thermosets (PF, MF, UF) | Material cured by heat inside mould | Electrical switchgear, knobs |
| **Metal Injection Moulding (MIM)** | Metal powder + binder | Binder removed, sintered | Surgical implants, firearm parts |
| **Ceramic Injection Moulding (CIM-c)** | Ceramic powder + binder | Binder removal, sintering | Cutting tool inserts |
| **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** | Polyurethane, epoxy | Two liquid streams mix in mould | Automotive bumpers, panels |
| **Gas-Assisted Injection Moulding (GAIM)** | Thermoplastics + N₂ | Hollow sections created by gas | TV bezels, handles |
| **Micro-Injection Moulding (μIM)** | Engineering thermoplastics | Shot weight < 1 g, μ-scale features | Microfluidic chips, gears |
| **Insert Moulding** | Plastic over metal/component | Pre-placed insert overmoulded | Threaded bushings, contacts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> The examiner frequently tests the difference between **CIM (thermoplastics)** and **RIM (thermoset/PU)**. RIM uses *two reactive liquid monomers* that polymerize *inside* the mould — there is **no plasticizing screw**; the drive is a high-pressure impingement mix head operating at only **20–30 bar**.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE INJECTION MOULDING CYCLE

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases (Exam-Critical Sequence)

The injection moulding cycle is **deterministic and time-sequenced**. Every phase has a direct impact on part quality and cycle economics.

### Phase 1 — Plasticization (Melting + Metering)
- **Action:** Rotation of the reciprocating screw draws polymer granules from the hopper into the heated barrel. The shear from screw rotation combined with external barrel heaters (band heaters) melts the polymer.
- **Why it matters for DFM:** Poor plasticization → non-uniform melt → **weld lines, air entrapment, sink marks** in the part.
- **Design Lever:** Material selection (MFI — Melt Flow Index) and barrel temperature profile (rear → middle → nozzle, increasing).

### Phase 2 — Injection (Filling)
- **Action:** The screw acts as a plunger (axial translation, no rotation) and pushes the metered melt through the nozzle, sprue, runner, gate, and into the mould cavity.
- **Why it matters for DFM:** Cavity pressure must overcome the **flow resistance** of the gating system. Long runners, thin walls, and complex paths increase pressure demand.
- **Design Lever:** Wall thickness, runner layout (cold/hot), gate type (edge, pin, tunnel, fan).

### Phase 3 — Packing / Hold Pressure
- **Action:** As the part begins to cool, additional melt is forced in to compensate for volumetric shrinkage (~1–3% for most thermoplastics, up to 5% for semi-crystalline like PA, POM).
- **Why it matters for DFM:** Insufficient packing → **sink marks, voids, warpage**. Excessive packing → **flash, over-packed mould, higher internal stress**.

### Phase 4 — Cooling
- **Action:** Cooling channels carry water (or oil for high-temperature resins) at **20–80 °C**, extracting heat from the part. Cooling typically accounts for **50–80% of the total cycle time** — this is the bottleneck.
- **Why it matters for DFM:** Uniform cooling → uniform shrinkage → minimal warpage. Asymmetric cooling → differential shrinkage → part distortion.
- **Design Lever:** Wall thickness uniformity, cooling line layout, conformal cooling (in 3D-printed moulds).

### Phase 5 — Ejection
- **Action:** Mould opens, ejector pins push the part out. The part is then degated (runner removed) and collected.
- **Why it matters for DFM:** Inadequate draft angle → part sticks → **scoring, ejector pin push marks**. Sharp corners → **stress concentration → cracking on ejection**.

---

## 2.2 The Injection Moulding Machine — Anatomy

The machine has **two fundamental units** working in synchronized, opposing motions:

| Unit | Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Unit** | Melts, meters, and injects material | Hopper, heated barrel, reciprocating screw, nozzle, hydraulic motor |
| **Clamping Unit** | Opens/closes mould and holds it closed against injection pressure | Tie bars, platens (stationary + moving), toggle/ hydraulic clamp, mould |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trap — Clamp Tonnage vs. Injection Pressure:**
> Students often confuse the *injection pressure* (generated by the screw) with the *clamping force* (generated by the clamp unit). They are **independent and opposing** in the following sense: the injection pressure tries to *push the mould halves apart*, while the clamping force *resists this opening*. The machine is sized so that:
> $$\text{Clamp Tonnage (F}_c\text{)} \geq \text{Projected Cavity Area} \times \text{Cavity Pressure}$$

---

## 2.3 The Mould (Tool) — Architecture

A typical **two-plate mould** contains the following features:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cavity (A-side / Core side)** | Forms the external geometry of the part |
| **Core (B-side / Cavity side)** | Forms the internal geometry |
| **Sprue Bushing** | Entry channel from machine nozzle into mould |
| **Runner System** | Distributes melt from sprue to each cavity |
| **Gate** | Final restriction before cavity; controls flow, shear, and degatability |
| **Cooling Channels** | Bored passages for heat extraction |
| **Ejector System** | Pins, sleeves, stripper plates to push out the part |
| **Parting Line** | The line where A and B halves meet — critical for DFMA |
| **Vents** | Tiny channels (0.01–0.03 mm) for air escape |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **core to numerical problems in KTU University Exams (Part B, 14-mark questions)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Clamping Force Required:}\quad & F_c \;=\; P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n \\
\text{2. Shot Volume / Weight:}\quad & V_{shot} \;=\; n \times (V_{part} + V_{runner}) \\
\text{3. Material Shrinkage Allowance:}\quad & D_{cavity} \;=\; D_{part} \times (1 + S) \\
\text{4. Cycle Time:}\quad & t_c \;=\; t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{open/close} \\
\text{5. Cooling Time (rule of thumb):}\quad & t_{cool} \;\approx\; \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{8(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}\right) \\
\text{6. Injection Pressure (hydrodynamic):}\quad & P_i \;=\; \frac{12 \mu Q L}{w h^3} \quad \text{(for rectangular channel)} \\
\text{7. Cavity Pressure:}\quad & P_{cav} \;\approx\; 0.4 \text{ to } 0.7 \times P_{inj} \\
\text{8. Production Rate:}\quad & \dot{N} \;=\; \frac{3600}{t_c} \quad \text{[parts/hour]} \\
\text{9. Production Cost per Part:}\quad & C_{part} \;=\; \frac{C_{tool}/N + C_{material} + C_{cycle} \times t_c}{n} \\
\text{10. Rejection / Yield:}\quad & Y \;=\; \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Notation Legend (for the table above):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_c$ | Required clamping force | kN or tonnes |
| $P_{cav}$ | Cavity pressure | MPa (typical 30–80) |
| $A_{proj}$ | Projected area of cavity (incl. runner) | cm² or mm² |
| $n$ | Number of cavities | — |
| $V_{shot}$ | Total shot volume | cm³ |
| $V_{part}$ | Volume of one part | cm³ |
| $S$ | Material shrinkage (e.g., 0.015 = 1.5%) | dimensionless |
| $h$ | Wall thickness | mm |
| $\alpha$ | Thermal diffusivity of polymer | mm²/s |
| $\mu$ | Melt viscosity | Pa·s |
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | mm³/s |
| $L$ | Flow length | mm |
| $w$ | Channel width | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Symbol Confusion in Exam Scripts:**
> Do **not** interchange $P_{inj}$ (injection pressure at the screw nozzle, typically 700–1400 bar) with $P_{cav}$ (cavity pressure inside the mould, typically 300–800 bar). The pressure drop occurs across the **gate**, which acts as a deliberate flow restriction.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Injection Moulding is Chosen |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | High volume (>100,000/year), tight tolerances, multi-material overmoulding (soft-touch grips) |
| **Medical** | Sterile, single-use disposables (syringes, IV components) with biocompatible resins |
| **Electronics** | Precision features (snap-fits, threaded bosses) with dielectric insulation |
| **Packaging** | Thin-wall moulding (sub-1 mm walls), fast cycles (<3 s) for caps and closures |
| **Consumer Goods** | Aesthetic surface finishes, in-mould decoration, multi-color moulding |
| **Aerospace** | Lightweight composite parts (glass/carbon-filled resins) for interior panels |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cooling Time Equation (Schenk-Springer Approximation)

The **Schenk-Springer** empirical formula is universally used in KTU board exams to estimate cooling time. It assumes **1-D heat conduction** through a slab of wall thickness $h$, with instantaneous mould surface temperature.

### Step 1 — Define the Boundary Value Problem
A polymer slab of thickness $h$ initially at melt temperature $T_m$ is placed in contact with a mould wall at $T_{wall}$. We seek the time $t$ at which the **centreline** (mid-plane) of the slab reaches ejection temperature $T_{eject}$.

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Symmetry Condition
The geometry is symmetric about the centreline, so we solve for $0 \le x \le h/2$, with $\partial T/\partial x = 0$ at $x=0$ and $T(h/2, t) = T_{wall}$.

### Step 3 — Normalize the Temperatures
Define a dimensionless temperature $\theta$:

$$
\theta \;=\; \frac{T - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}}
$$

Initial condition: $\theta(x, 0) = 1$. Boundary condition: $\theta(h/2, t) = 0$.

### Step 4 — Solve via Separation of Variables + Fourier Series
The solution is an infinite cosine series. Truncating to the **first term** (sufficient for engineering accuracy):

$$
\theta(x, t) \;\approx\; \frac{4}{\pi} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{h}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{h^2}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate at the Centreline
At $x = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$
\theta(0, t) \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{h^2}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Definition of $\theta$
For ejection at $T = T_{eject}$:

$$
\frac{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_{cool}}{h^2}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $t_{cool}$
Rearranging:

$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_{cool}}{h^2}\right) \;=\; \frac{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}{4(T_m - T_{wall})}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_{cool}}{h^2} \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}{4(T_m - T_{wall})}\right)
$$

$$
t_{cool} \;=\; -\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}{4(T_m - T_{wall})}\right)
$$

### Step 8 — Note on the Coefficient
Some textbook variants use a slightly different coefficient ($\pi^2/4$ instead of $\pi^2$) depending on whether the **average** or **centreline** temperature is referenced. For KTU board purposes, retain the centreline form as it is conservative (longer cooling time = safer estimate).

$$
\boxed{\;t_{cool} \;=\; \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{4 (T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> Cooling time scales with the **square of the wall thickness** $h^2$. This is why a 4 mm wall takes **four times longer** to cool than a 2 mm wall — and why DFMA mandates **uniform, thin walls** for fast cycles.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]:**
> An injection moulded polypropylene (PP) part has a wall thickness of 2.5 mm. The melt temperature is 230 °C, the mould wall temperature is 40 °C, and the ejection temperature is 90 °C. The thermal diffusivity of PP melt is $\alpha = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the cooling time.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $h = 2.5 \text{ mm}$
- $T_m = 230 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{wall} = 40 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{eject} = 90 \text{ °C}$
- $\alpha = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature ratios**

$$
T_m - T_{wall} \;=\; 230 - 40 \;=\; 190 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{eject} - T_{wall} \;=\; 90 - 40 \;=\; 50 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the logarithm**

$$
\frac{4(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 190}{\pi \times 50} \;=\; \frac{760}{157.08} \;=\; 4.838
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm**

$$
\ln(4.838) \;=\; 1.576
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor**

$$
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \;=\; \frac{(2.5)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.09} \;=\; \frac{6.25}{0.888} \;=\; 7.04 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to get cooling time**

$$
t_{cool} \;=\; 7.04 \times 1.576 \;=\; 11.1 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Result:** The part requires approximately **11 seconds** of cooling before it can be safely ejected. The full cycle time will be $t_c = t_{cool} + t_{inj}(1.5) + t_{eject}(1) + t_{open/close}(2.5) \approx 16 \text{ s}$, giving a production rate of $3600/16 \approx 225$ parts/hour.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Clamp Tonnage Selection

The clamp must withstand the **separating force** generated by cavity pressure acting on the projected area.

$$
F_c \;=\; P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n
$$

**Derivation logic:**
- The cavity pressure $P_{cav}$ acts on every square millimetre of projected area.
- For a single cavity, $n=1$.
- For multi-cavity moulds, the pressure acts on *all* cavities simultaneously (they are filled through a common runner), so $n$ is the number of cavities.
- A **safety factor of 1.1 to 1.3** is usually applied to account for pressure spikes at the moment of fill completion.

$$
\boxed{\;F_{c, design} \;=\; 1.2 \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n\;}
$$

### Example
A 4-cavity mould produces parts with projected area $20 \text{ cm}^2$ each. Cavity pressure = 50 MPa. Find the required clamp tonnage.

$$
F_c \;=\; 1.2 \times 50 \times (4 \times 20) \;=\; 1.2 \times 50 \times 80 \;=\; 4800 \text{ N} \;\text{(check units: MPa}\times\text{cm}^2\text{)}
$$

Converting units: $50 \text{ MPa} = 500 \text{ bar} = 500 \text{ kgf/cm}^2$.

$$
F_c \;=\; 1.2 \times 500 \times 80 \;=\; 48{,}000 \text{ kgf} \;\approx\; 480 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check Rule:** For most thermoplastics, every $100 \text{ cm}^2$ of projected area requires roughly **50 tonnes** of clamp force. A hand-sized part (~$100 \text{ cm}^2$) fits comfortably in a 50–100 tonne press.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Injection Moulding Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Hopper - Granule Feed] --> B[Heated Barrel - Plasticization Zone]
    B --> C[Reciprocating Screw - Melting and Metering]
    C --> D[Nozzle - High Pressure Injection]
    D --> E[Sprue and Runner System - Flow Distribution]
    E --> F[Gate - Flow Restriction and Shear Control]
    F --> G[Mould Cavity - Shape Definition]
    G --> H[Packing and Hold Phase - Shrinkage Compensation]
    H --> I[Cooling Phase - Heat Extraction via Water Lines]
    I --> J[Mould Opening - Part Ejection]
    J --> K[Ejector Pins - Part Release]
    K --> L[Part Collection and Degating]
    L --> M{Cycle Repeat?}
    M -- Yes --> A
    M -- No --> N[Production Complete]
```

## 4.2 Machine Layout — Injection + Clamping Units

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INJ[Injection Unit - Right Side]
        HOP[Hopper] --> BAR[Heated Barrel]
        BAR --> SCR[Reciprocating Screw]
        SCR --> NOZ[Nozzle]
    end
    subgraph CLP[Clamping Unit - Left Side]
        TB1[Tie Bar] --> PLT1[Stationary Platen]
        PLT1 --> MLD[Mould - A and B Halves]
        MLD --> PLT2[Moving Platen]
        PLT2 --> TB2[Tie Bar]
        TB2 --> CLM[Toggle or Hydraulic Clamp]
    end
    NOZ -. mates with .-> MLD
    PLT1 --- MLD
    PLT2 --- MLD
    CLM --> PLT2
```

## 4.3 Cycle Phases — Time-Mapped Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 - Plasticization - Screw Rotates and Draws Melt] --> P2
    P2[Phase 2 - Injection - Screw Translates Forward - Fill Cavity] --> P3
    P3[Phase 3 - Packing - Hold Pressure Compensates Shrinkage] --> P4
    P4[Phase 4 - Cooling - Largest Time Share - 50 to 80 percent] --> P5
    P5[Phase 5 - Ejection - Mould Opens and Part is Pushed Out] --> P6
    P6[Cycle Reset - Mould Closes and Next Shot Begins] --> P1
```

## 4.4 Two-Plate Mould — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NOZ2[Machine Nozzle] --> SPR[Sprue Bushing]
    SPR --> RUN[Runner Channel]
    RUN --> GT[Gate]
    GT --> CV[Cavity - Defines External Geometry]
    CV --> CR[Core - Defines Internal Geometry]
    CR --> CL[Cooling Channels - Bored in Both Halves]
    CR --> EJP[Ejector Pin - Travels Through Backing Plate]
    EJP --> PRT[Finished Part]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Common Defects Linked to Process Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEF[Defect Detected] --> Q1{Where does it appear?}
    Q1 -- Surface --> Q2{Type?}
    Q2 -- Sink marks --> DFM1[Cause: Insufficient packing or thick wall - DFMA Fix: Uniform thickness and adequate hold pressure]
    Q2 -- Flow lines --> DFM2[Cause: Low melt temp or slow injection - DFMA Fix: Increase melt temp and shot speed]
    Q2 -- Weld line --> DFM3[Cause: Two flow fronts meet - DFMA Fix: Relocate gate to single flow path]
    Q1 -- Dimensional --> Q3{Type?}
    Q3 -- Warpage --> DFM4[Cause: Asymmetric cooling - DFMA Fix: Balanced cooling line layout]
    Q3 -- Flash --> DFM5[Cause: Insufficient clamp tonnage - DFMA Fix: Increase clamp force or reduce injection pressure]
    Q1 -- Structural --> Q4{Type?}
    Q4 -- Short shot --> DFM6[Cause: Insufficient material or venting - DFMA Fix: Increase shot size and add vents]
    Q4 -- Voids --> DFM7[Cause: Thick section - DFMA Fix: Core out thick areas]
```
