# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define injection moulding. List any four thermoplastic materials commonly processed by this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Injection moulding is a cyclic polymer processing method in which a thermoplastic material is plasticized in a heated barrel, injected under high pressure into a closed mould cavity, cooled to a solid state, and ejected as a finished part.
>
> **Four common thermoplastics:** (i) Polypropylene (PP), (ii) Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS), (iii) Polycarbonate (PC), (iv) Polyethylene (PE). *(Any four accepted — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each pair of materials.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Differentiate between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics in the context of injection moulding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Thermoplastics | Thermosetting Plastics |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular Structure** | Linear/branched chains, no cross-linking | 3D cross-linked network after curing |
| **Cycle Behaviour** | Reversible — can be remelted | Irreversible — cannot be remelted |
| **Mould Temperature** | 20–80 °C (cooled mould) | 150–200 °C (heated mould for cure) |
| **Plasticization** | In barrel by screw | In barrel for RIM, or by chemical reaction in mould |
| **Example Processes** | Conventional IM, GAIM, μIM | RIM, BMC, SMC |
| **Ejection Stage** | After cooling below softening point | After cure (exothermic reaction) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **injection moulding cycle** in detail. Discuss the significance of the **packing/holding phase** in determining part quality. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mould produces a polypropylene (PP) part with the following data: wall thickness = 3.0 mm, melt temperature = 240 °C, mould wall temperature = 35 °C, ejection temperature = 95 °C, and thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$. Determine the **cooling time** and the **production rate per hour** if the total cycle time (injection + cooling + ejection + mould open/close) is 18 s. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Injection Moulding Cycle — Block Diagram & Packing Phase Significance (7 marks)**

The cycle consists of **five sequential phases** as illustrated below:

1. **Plasticization (1.5 marks):** Polymer granules from the hopper are fed into the heated barrel, where a rotating screw shears and mixes the melt. The screw retracts as melt accumulates at its tip, metering the exact shot volume.

2. **Injection (1 mark):** The screw acts as a plunger, forcing the melt through the nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate into the mould cavity at high pressure (700–1400 bar) and high speed.

3. **Packing/Hold Pressure (1.5 marks):** As the part begins to cool, additional melt is forced into the cavity to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** (1–3% for amorphous, up to 5% for semi-crystalline polymers like PP, PA). This phase is critical because:
   - It prevents **sink marks** and **internal voids**.
   - It establishes the final **part density** and **dimensional accuracy**.
   - It compensates for the volumetric contraction as polymer chains reorganize during cooling.

4. **Cooling (1.5 marks):** Cooling channels remove heat from the part. The part must cool to ejection temperature before the mould opens. Cooling typically occupies **50–80%** of the cycle time.

5. **Ejection (0.5 mark):** The mould opens, ejector pins push the part out, and the cycle repeats.

**Significance of Packing Phase:**
The packing phase is often called the "**quality determinant phase**". Insufficient packing causes surface sink marks, voids, and poor dimensional control. Excessive packing causes flash, mould overloading, and high internal stress leading to warpage. A well-tuned packing profile (switch-over from velocity to pressure control at ~95–98% cavity fill) is the single most important parameter in producing a defect-free part.

> **Marking Scheme Note:** Diagram = 2 marks; 5 phases described = 3 marks; Packing significance = 2 marks.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $h = 3.0 \text{ mm}$
- $T_m = 240 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{wall} = 35 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{eject} = 95 \text{ °C}$
- $\alpha = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$
- $t_c = 18 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature ratio inside the log argument (2 marks)**

$$
T_m - T_{wall} \;=\; 240 - 35 \;=\; 205 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{eject} - T_{wall} \;=\; 95 - 35 \;=\; 60 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\frac{4(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi (T_{eject} - T_{wall})} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 205}{\pi \times 60} \;=\; \frac{820}{188.5} \;=\; 4.350
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the natural logarithm (1 mark)**

$$
\ln(4.350) \;=\; 1.470
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the prefactor (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \;=\; \frac{(3.0)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.09} \;=\; \frac{9.0}{0.888} \;=\; 10.13 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cooling time (1 mark)**

$$
t_{cool} \;=\; 10.13 \times 1.470 \;=\; 14.89 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 15 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the production rate (1 mark)**

$$
\dot{N} \;=\; \frac{3600}{t_c} \;=\; \frac{3600}{18} \;=\; 200 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Final Answer:** Cooling time ≈ **15 s**, Production rate = **200 parts/hour**.

> **Marking Scheme Note:** Step 1 = 2 marks; Step 2 = 1 mark; Step 3 = 2 marks; Step 4 = 1 mark; Step 5 = 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q1 Alt]**

**(a)** Explain the **clamping unit** and **injection unit** of an injection moulding machine with the help of a block diagram. Why is the **clamp tonnage** selection important, and what is its relationship with cavity pressure and projected area? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A two-cavity mould produces parts each with a projected area of 25 cm². The estimated cavity pressure is 50 MPa. Calculate the **minimum clamping force required**, and recommend the **machine clamp tonnage** if a 25% safety margin is applied. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Machine Architecture & Clamp Tonnage (7 marks)**

The injection moulding machine consists of two synchronized units:

**Injection Unit (3.5 marks):**
- **Hopper:** Stores and feeds polymer granules by gravity.
- **Heated Barrel:** Has 3–4 heating zones with progressively higher temperatures.
- **Reciprocating Screw:** Combines plasticization (rotation) and injection (axial translation). It is driven by a hydraulic motor.
- **Nozzle:** Seals against the mould sprue bushing; contains a shut-off valve for some designs.

**Clamping Unit (3.5 marks):**
- **Platen and Tie Bars:** Two parallel platens (one stationary, one moving) connected by four tie bars that maintain alignment and absorb clamp reaction.
- **Drive Mechanism:** Either a **toggle clamp** (mechanical advantage, fast, energy-efficient) or a **hydraulic clamp** (smoother, more controllable, used for large machines).
- **Mould Mounting:** Bolted to platens; half fitted with cavity, half with core.

**Clamp Tonnage Significance (within the 3.5 marks):**
The clamp tonnage must exceed the **separating force** generated by cavity pressure on the projected area, or the mould will "open" and produce flash. The relationship is:

$$
F_c \;\geq\; P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n
$$

If clamp force is too low → flash, over-packed parts, mould damage.
If clamp force is too high → energy wastage, slower clamp motion, excessive mould wear.

---

**(b) Clamp Force Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $A_{proj}$ per cavity = 25 cm²
- $n$ = 2 cavities
- $P_{cav}$ = 50 MPa

**Step 1 — Compute total projected area (1 mark)**

$$
A_{total} \;=\; n \times A_{proj} \;=\; 2 \times 25 \;=\; 50 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure to consistent units (2 marks)**

Convert MPa to kgf/cm²:
$$
1 \text{ MPa} \;=\; 10.197 \text{ kgf/cm}^2
$$

$$
P_{cav} \;=\; 50 \times 10.197 \;=\; 509.85 \text{ kgf/cm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute minimum clamp force (2 marks)**

$$
F_{c, min} \;=\; P_{cav} \times A_{total} \;=\; 509.85 \times 50 \;=\; 25{,}492.5 \text{ kgf}
$$

Converting to tonnes:
$$
F_{c, min} \;\approx\; 25.5 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 25% safety margin (2 marks)**

$$
F_{c, design} \;=\; 1.25 \times 25.5 \;=\; 31.875 \text{ tonnes} \;\approx\; 32 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Recommendation:** Select a standard machine of **35 tonnes** (next standard size up).

> **Marking Scheme Note:** Step 1 = 1 mark; Step 2 = 2 marks; Step 3 = 2 marks; Step 4 = 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Injection Moulding Answers (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Pressure Unit Confusion:** Writing "50 MPa" and "500 bar" interchangeably loses 1 mark. Be precise: $1 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar}$.
> 2. **Confusing $P_{inj}$ with $P_{cav}$:** The injection pressure at the nozzle is **not** the cavity pressure. The gate causes a pressure drop of 30–60%.
> 3. **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** Clamp tonnage calculations must include a **factor of 1.2 to 1.3** (sometimes explicitly stated in the question).
> 4. **Missing the Diagram:** In 7-mark theory questions, a **labelled block diagram is mandatory**. A textual description alone earns at most 4–5 marks.
> 5. **Schenk-Springer Coefficient Errors:** Using the wrong constant ($\pi^2$ vs. $\pi^2/4$) for centreline vs. average temperature. State your assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Cycle Time vs. Cooling Time:** The total cycle time **includes** cooling, injection, hold, ejection, and mould open/close. Do not equate them.
> 7. **Shrinkage Allowance Direction:** A part that must be 50 mm and shrinks 2% requires a **cavity of 51 mm**, not 49 mm. This is a classic trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Injection Moulding: Introduction**

- **Definition:** Cyclic, high-pressure polymer shaping process with melting, injection, packing, cooling, and ejection phases.
- **Primary Driver:** Reciprocating screw in a heated barrel (thermoplastics) or impingement mix head (RIM for thermosets).
- **Memorize the cycle mnemonic:** **"P.I.C.H.E"** = Plasticize → Inject → Cool/Hold → Hold → Eject.
- **Cooling dominates:** 50–80% of cycle time is cooling; $t_{cool} \propto h^2$.
- **Clamp force formula:** $F_c = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n$, with 1.2–1.3 safety factor.
- **Shrinkage allowance:** $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ — semi-crystalline polymers (PA, POM, PP) shrink more (1.5–3%) than amorphous (PS, ABS, PC) (0.3–0.7%).
- **DFMA golden rules for injection-moulded parts:**
  1. **Uniform wall thickness** (avoid thickness > 4 mm or thickness transitions > 1.5:1).
  2. **Generous draft angles** (1° for soft resins, 2° for stiff resins).
  3. **Generous corner radii** (≥ 0.5 × wall thickness, but ≥ 0.5 mm).
  4. **Single, balanced parting line.**
  5. **Gate at thickest section.**
  6. **Avoid undercuts** or use side-action sliders.
  7. **Design snap-fits with a uniform cross-section** (cantilever width / thickness ratio).
  8. **Self-aligning features** to reduce assembly tolerance stack-up.
- **Cycle time equation:** $t_c = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{open/close}$.
- **Production rate equation:** $\dot{N} = 3600 / t_c$ (parts/hour).
- **Common defects to recognize:**
  - *Sink marks* → under-packed thick section.
  - *Weld lines* → multi-gate layout or insert obstruction.
  - *Flash* → insufficient clamp tonnage or worn mould surfaces.
  - *Warpage* → asymmetric cooling or non-uniform wall.
  - *Short shot* → insufficient material or vent blockage.
  - *Voids* → thick section without core-out.
- **Typical operating window:**
  - Melt temperature: **200–320 °C** (thermoplastics).
  - Mould temperature: **20–120 °C**.
  - Injection pressure: **700–1400 bar**.
  - Cavity pressure: **300–800 bar**.
  - Cycle time: **5–120 s** depending on part size.
- **The Schenk-Springer cooling time formula** must be memorized as it is the single most frequently tested equation in KTU Part B questions for this module.
- **Connect to DFMA philosophy:** Always ask *"What is the mould cost? What is the cycle time? What is the assembly method?"* — every design decision must be justified by these three numbers.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Characteristics of Injection Moulded Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Characteristics of Injection Moulded Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Injection Moulding** is a high-volume, closed-loop, cyclic plastic manufacturing process in which molten thermoplastic (or thermosetting) material is injected under high pressure into a closed, temperature-controlled mould cavity, where it solidifies to form a net-shape (or near-net-shape) component. The geometric features, surface quality, and dimensional integrity of the resulting part are dictated by a tightly coupled set of physical phenomena: polymer rheology, transient heat transfer, cavity pressure evolution, volumetric shrinkage, and molecular orientation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET522 Module 4):**
> The "Typical Characteristics of Injection Moulded Parts" refers to the *design-inherent* geometric, mechanical, and surface features that are unavoidably created (or constrained) by the physics of the injection moulding process. These include — but are not limited to — wall thickness uniformity, draft angle requirements, rib–boss intersections, corner radii, gate scars, weld/meld lines, parting-line witness marks, ejection-pin witness marks, sink marks, void formation, flow lines, and frozen-in orientation-induced anisotropy.

These characteristics are *not defects* in the engineering sense — they are **systemic, process-imposed design constraints** that the DFMA engineer must internalize while conceptualising the plastic component. Failing to do so causes redesigns, mould rework, scrap rates exceeding acceptable limits (typically **<1%** in high-volume production), and shot-to-shot variability that breaches GD&T tolerances.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Water Balloon in a Cookie Cutter":**
> Imagine injecting a thick, hot chocolate paste into a hinged metal cookie cutter (the mould). The paste is forced into every corner through a tiny nozzle. As it cools, it *shrinks* (because hot liquid chocolate occupies more volume than cold solid chocolate). It also *sticks* slightly to the metal walls. Now, to get the cookie out, you must:
> 1. Tilt the cutter (this is the **draft angle**).
> 2. Not let the walls be *too* thick in some places and *too* thin in others (otherwise thick regions take longer to cool and cause **sink marks**).
> 3. Round the inner corners (sharp corners trap air and create stress concentrations).
> 4. Plan a place for the excess paste to enter (the **gate** — leaves a small scar).
> 5. Make sure two flow fronts meet smoothly (otherwise they leave a visible **weld line**).
>
> Every "characteristic" of the resulting chocolate cookie is a *direct fingerprint* of how the paste was injected, cooled, and ejected. The cookie is not designed "first" and then manufactured — it is **designed FOR the manufacturing process from day one**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Process Windows

The following metrics, when used as design guard-rails, are universally accepted in the plastics DFMA industry and form the numerical basis for the formulae presented in §2:

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear mould shrinkage (semi-crystalline) | **0.015 – 0.030** | mm/mm | Volume change on cooling |
| Linear mould shrinkage (amorphous) | **0.003 – 0.008** | mm/mm | Lower than semi-crystalline |
| Draft angle (standard) | **0.5° – 2°** | degrees | Ejectability |
| Draft angle (deep-draw / textured) | **2° – 5°** | degrees | Required for release |
| Minimum wall thickness (general) | **0.8 – 1.5** | mm | Process & flow limits |
| Maximum wall thickness (general) | **3.0 – 4.0** | mm | Beyond → sink marks, voids |
| Wall thickness ratio limit | **2 : 1** (ideal) | ratio | Uniform cooling |
| Minimum corner radius (inside) | **0.5 × wall thickness** | mm | Stress relief, flow |
| Cavity fill pressure | **35 – 140** | MPa | Pack/hold phase |
| Mould surface temperature | **20 – 120** | °C | Cycle-time driver |
| Typical ejection force budget | **< 50 N** (per pin) | N | Boss/pin design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The "**2 : 1 wall-thickness rule**" is the single most-asked DFMA guideline in KTU university exam papers on injection moulded parts. Memorise it verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primary Characteristics — Structured Logic

Below is the canonical breakdown used by KTU examiners and by industry DFMA checklists (e.g., the **Boothroyd–Dewhurst** and **Roth** design-for-injection-moulding heuristics).

### 2.1.1 Wall Thickness Uniformity

* **Why it matters:** A thicker section requires more mass to cool → longer cycle time → internal vacuum voids form as the outer skin solidifies first while the core is still molten. The outer skin is then pulled inward, producing a **sink mark** on the cosmetic surface.
* **How to enforce:** Maintain a **maximum 2:1 thickness ratio** between adjacent sections. Use **cored-out pockets** in any region that would otherwise be unnecessarily thick.
* **Design metric:** Volumetric shrinkage compensation factor, defined per Eq. (1) in §3.

### 2.1.2 Draft Angle (Taper)

* **Why:** As the part cools inside the cavity, it *shrinks* radially and *grips* the steel wall. Without taper, ejection force spikes and parts either get marked by the ejector pins or fail to release at all.
* **How:** Apply **0.5° – 2°** on all walls parallel to the mould opening direction; **1° per 25 mm of draw depth** as a safe general rule; **2° – 5°** for textured surfaces.
* **Caveat:** Draft is *opposite* to the perceived direction of shrinkage — meaning the cavity side is *larger* than the core side by the projected shrinkage amount.

### 2.1.3 Ribs, Bosses & Gussets (Stiffening Features)

* **Why:** Plain thick walls are *bad*. Instead, designers use ribs to add stiffness with minimal mass.
* **Design rule:** Rib thickness = **0.5 × nominal wall thickness** (some texts use 0.6×).
* **Why not equal-thickness ribs:** Equal-thickness ribs cause a **sink mark** directly opposite the rib root on the cosmetic surface (a "mirror-image sink").
* **Boss rule:** Boss outer diameter = **2 × hole diameter + wall thickness** (Rule of thumb from Fisher, see §3 derivation).
* **Knit-line / weld-line trap:** Place bosses *away* from the gate so the melt front does not have to split and rejoin around the boss OD (a weld line at a boss root is a chronic leak/fatigue failure site).

### 2.1.4 Corner Radii — Inside & Outside

* **Why:** Sharp inside corners create **(a)** stress concentration (Kt → infinity in linear-elastic theory), **(b)** a flow-restriction zone where the polymer stalls and overheats, and **(c)** a difficult-to-machine sharp radius on the steel mould.
* **Standard rule:** Inside radius $r_i \geq 0.5 \times t$ (where $t$ = wall thickness). Outside radius $r_o = r_i + t$ (geometric consequence of constant wall thickness).
* **Flow physics:** Rounded corners reduce effective flow-path length by $\approx 15\%$ for typical ribs.

### 2.1.5 Gate Location & Gate Scars

* **Why:** The gate is the *wound* left by the injection process. It is a small protrusion (or flush scar) on the part surface.
* **Design strategy:** Locate the gate on a **non-cosmetic surface** or on a surface hidden by a snap-fit, label, or mating part. Use **submarine (tunnel) gates** for automatic degating.
* **Gate types:** Edge gate, fan gate, diaphragm gate, pinpoint gate, hot-runner gate, valve gate.

### 2.1.6 Weld Lines (Meld Lines) & Flow Lines

* **Weld line:** Formed when two (or more) melt fronts meet *after* flowing around an obstruction (e.g., a hole or boss). Molecular chains have not inter-diffused → mechanical strength drops **30 – 70%** at the weld.
* **Flow line:** A visible streak formed by a stalled front re-accelerating; *not* a structural weakness but a cosmetic flaw.
* **Design rule:** Reposition the gate so the obstruction is fed from a single front; or use a *flow leader* to force the obstruction to be filled last.

### 2.1.7 Parting Line

* **The line** at which the two mould halves meet. It is always visible (a witness mark of $\approx$ 0.01 – 0.05 mm height).
* **Design strategy:** Place the parting line on a **silhouette edge** (the parting line should coincide with the part's largest projected cross-section) so the witness is hidden on the silhouette transition, not on a visible face.

### 2.1.8 Ejector Pin Marks & Undercuts

* **Ejector pins** push the part out of the cavity. They always leave a small witness — usually a **2 – 5 mm diameter circular depression**.
* **Design strategy:** Place ejector pins on **non-cosmetic** surfaces, on ribs (where the sink they would otherwise cause is *already* present), or on bosses.
* **Undercuts** (internal or external features that prevent direct draw) require **side-action cores**, **lifters**, or **collapsible cores** — each adds **₹ ₹ ₹** to mould cost. DFMA heuristic: **avoid undercuts entirely** if possible.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are **exam-grade**, mathematically verifiable, and drawn from standard DFMA / polymer-processing literature. They are the equations the KTU valuation key expects to see cited by name.

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{cavity} - V_{part}}{V_{part}} \times 100\%$ | $S_v$ = volumetric shrinkage, $V$ = volume | % | Cavity oversize design |
| 2 | $S_L \approx \dfrac{1}{3} S_v$ | $S_L$ = linear mould shrinkage | mm/mm | Linear dimension scaling |
| 3 | $D_{cavity} = D_{part}\,(1 + S_L)$ | $D$ = dimension | mm | Cavity dimension design |
| 4 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}\right)$ | $t$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T$ = temperature | s | Cycle-time estimation |
| 5 | $\theta_d = 1^\circ + \dfrac{H}{25}$ (approx) | $\theta_d$ = draft angle, $H$ = draw depth | °, mm | Draft-angle selection |
| 6 | $t_{rib} \leq 0.5\,t_{wall}$ | $t$ = thickness | mm | Anti-sink rib rule |
| 7 | $r_i \geq 0.5\,t_{wall}$ | $r_i$ = inside corner radius, $t$ = wall thickness | mm | Corner radius rule |
| 8 | $\sigma_{weld} \approx (0.3 \text{ to } 0.7)\,\sigma_{bulk}$ | $\sigma$ = tensile strength | MPa | Weld-line strength derating |
| 9 | $F_{eject} = \mu\,P_c\,A_{proj}$ | $F$ = force, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $P_c$ = cavity pressure, $A$ = projected area | N | Ejection force check |
| 10 | $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | $t$ = time | s | Total cycle time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table-formatting discipline:** All absolute-value / norm notations in this sheet use `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid corrupting the markdown pipe-delimited table syntax, as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These ten formulae and eight characteristics are the working vocabulary of every **DFMA engineer, mould designer, polymer technologist, and quality engineer** in the plastic-product industry — from medical-device housings (Becton Dickinson, Stryker) and automotive fascia (Toyota, Tata Motors) to consumer-electronics enclosures (Apple, Samsung). Cost-of-poor-quality (COPQ) studies in the automotive sector consistently rank *inadequate DFMA at the part-design stage* as the **#1 root cause of injection-mould tooling rework**, with average rework costs of **$25,000 – $250,000 per tool modification** in industrialised economies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Shrinkage to Linear Mould-Shrinkage Factor (Eq. 1 & 2)

**Physical Premise:** A polymer's volumetric coefficient of thermal expansion ($\beta$) is *not* equal to its linear coefficient ($\alpha$). For small strains, the isotropic, homogeneous-shrinkage assumption yields the classical thermoelasticity identity.

**Step 1 — Define the volume ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } V_0 &= \text{volume of the cavity at mould temperature } T_{mould} \\
V_{part} &= \text{volume of the solid part at room temperature } T_{room} \\
\therefore \frac{V_{part}}{V_0} &= 1 - S_v \quad \text{(definition of volumetric shrinkage ratio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply isotropic linear scaling.**

Because the part is *approximately* isotropic at the macroscopic level, each linear dimension $L$ scales by the same linear shrinkage factor $S_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{part} &= L_0\,(1 - S_L) \\
\therefore V_{part} &= V_0\,(1 - S_L)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and take the cube root.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - S_L)^3 &= 1 - S_v \\
1 - S_L &= (1 - S_v)^{1/3} \\
S_L &= 1 - (1 - S_v)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-strain Taylor expansion** ($S_v \ll 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - S_v)^{1/3} &\approx 1 - \frac{S_v}{3} - \frac{S_v^2}{9} - \dots \\
S_L &\approx \frac{S_v}{3} + \frac{S_v^2}{9} + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_v$ is typically $0.03$ to $0.09$ (i.e., $3\%$ – $9\%$ volumetric), the first-order approximation is more than sufficient for KTU-level calculations:

$$
\boxed{\,S_L \approx \dfrac{1}{3}\,S_v\,} \quad \text{(Eq. 2, verified)}
$$

**Step 5 — Cavity dimension design (Eq. 3).**

Because the part shrinks as it cools, the *cavity* must be made larger than the nominal part dimension by the linear shrinkage factor:

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part}\,(1 + S_L)
$$

For example, a nominal $D_{part} = 100$ mm feature in a polypropylene (PP) part with $S_L = 0.020$ requires a cavity of $D_{cavity} = 100 \times 1.020 = 102.0$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering judgment note:** $S_L$ is *not* a constant — it depends on the part's local thickness, the gate location, the cavity pressure during packing, and the fibre orientation (for fibre-filled grades). KTU examiners award marks for mentioning this dependency explicitly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rib Thickness Anti-Sink Rule (Eq. 6)

**Premise:** A rib whose root meets a cosmetic surface creates a *thick* region (rib + underlying wall) whose mass exceeds the *uniform* wall thickness. The thicker region solidifies later → volumetric contraction is greater → the cosmetic surface is pulled inward → visible **sink mark**.

**Geometric Setup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{rib} &= \text{rib thickness} \\
t_{wall} &= \text{nominal wall thickness} \\
h_{rib} &= \text{rib height} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Volumetric Local Thickening Ratio:** The effective local thickness directly above the rib root is

$$
t_{eff} \approx t_{wall} + t_{rib}
$$

**Anti-Sink Design Condition:** The sink mark depth is empirically (and analytically) proportional to $(t_{eff} - t_{wall}) / t_{wall}$. The threshold for *visually acceptable* sink depth (typically $< 0.025$ mm on a Class-A cosmetic surface) is reached when

$$
t_{rib} \leq 0.5 \, t_{wall}
$$

This is the **origin** of the "**rib = half wall**" rule — a KTU classic.

**Numerical Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{wall} &= 2.0 \text{ mm (typical ABS part)} \\
\therefore t_{rib,max} &= 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If the designer needs more stiffness than a 1-mm rib provides, the correct strategy is to **add a second, parallel rib** (with a minimum gap of $3 \times t_{wall}$ between rib centres), not to thicken the existing rib.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling-Time Approximation (Eq. 4)

**Premise:** Cooling time dominates the cycle (typically $60$ – $80\%$ of total cycle). The simplest analytical estimate comes from solving 1-D transient heat conduction in a slab of half-thickness $t/2$, with the classical lumped-capacitance / 1-D Fourier solution.

**Step 1 — Temperature field.** For a slab initially at melt temperature $T_m$, suddenly exposed to mould-wall temperature $T_{wall}$ on both faces, the 1-D transient temperature field is the Fourier series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{4}{(2n+1)\pi}\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{t}\right)\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{t^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Centreline temperature.** Setting $x = t/2$ (the *last* point to freeze, in a slab of total thickness $t$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_{centre}(t) - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha\,t_{cool}}{t^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for cooling time to ejection temperature** $T_{eject}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} &\approx \dfrac{4}{\pi}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha\,t_{cool}}{t^2}\right) \\
\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha\,t_{cool}}{t^2}\right) &\approx \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,\dfrac{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} \\
-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha\,t_{cool}}{t^2} &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\,\dfrac{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}}\right) \\
t_{cool} &= \dfrac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi}\,\dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The standard form reported in textbooks uses a slightly different constant inside the logarithm (the $8/\pi^2$ version arises from keeping only the $n=0$ term and adjusting for the average temperature). The end result is the working engineering formula:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}\right)\,}
$$

**Numerical Check (typical PP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{wall} &= 2.0 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m} \\
\alpha &\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \\
T_m &= 220 \degree C, \quad T_{wall} = 30 \degree C, \quad T_{eject} = 90 \degree C \\
t_{cool} &\approx \dfrac{(0.002)^2}{\pi^2 (1.0 \times 10^{-7})}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 (220-30)}{\pi^2 (90-30)}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{9.87 \times 10^{-7}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 190}{9.87 \times 60}\right) \\
&\approx 4.05 \times \ln(2.566) \\
&\approx 4.05 \times 0.943 \approx 3.82 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 2-mm PP wall in a $30 \degree C$ mould takes roughly **$3.8$ seconds to freeze** — a number well within the KTU textbook range ($2$ – $8$ s for thin-wall parts).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Boss Outer Diameter Rule (Fisher, Simplified)

**Premise:** A boss is a cylindrical feature designed to receive a self-tapping screw, an insert, or a mating pin. The boss must be (a) thick enough to carry the load, (b) hollow inside to receive the fastener, and (c) connected to the wall by a generous fillet.

**Standard Fisher Rule:**

$$
D_{boss} = 2\,d_{hole} + t_{wall}
$$

where $d_{hole}$ is the *minor* (inner) hole diameter and $t_{wall}$ is the nominal wall thickness to which the boss is attached.

**Worked example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{hole} &= 4.0 \text{ mm (M5 self-tapping screw pilot)} \\
t_{wall} &= 2.5 \text{ mm} \\
D_{boss} &= 2 \times 4.0 + 2.5 = 10.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU error:** Students frequently compute $D_{boss} = d_{hole} + t_{wall}$ (omitting the factor of 2). The factor of 2 arises because the boss *surrounds* the hole — it must provide hoop-material on **both** sides of the hole to support radial screw-in forces. Examiners deduct full marks for this mistake.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Cause-Effect-Design-Rule

| Phenomenon (Characteristic) | Root Cause | Design Rule (Heuristic) | Numeric Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sink mark | Local thick section, slow cool | Wall ratio $\leq 2:1$, rib $= 0.5\,t$ | Eq. (6) |
| Voids | Internal vacuum, thick section | Core-out, $t_{max} \leq 3$ mm | — |
| Weld line | Flow split + rejoin | Move gate, add flow leader | Eq. (8) |
| Ejector pin mark | Pin contact | Place on non-cosmetic, on rib | Eq. (9) |
| Warpage | Differential shrinkage | Symmetric wall, uniform cool | Eq. (2) |
| Short-shot | Incomplete fill | Increase pressure / temperature | — |
| Flash | Excess pressure / worn mould | $t_{min}$ per material spec | — |
| Burn marks | Air entrapment | Add vents at flow stagnation | — |
| Flow lines | Cold front, slow fill | Raise $T_{melt}$, $T_{mould}$ | — |
| Gate vestige | Gate freezing | Submarine / hot-runner gate | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — Part Design for Injection Moulding

This flowchart maps the *decision cascade* a DFMA engineer follows when designing (or reviewing) an injection-moulded component. Every characteristic in this module maps to one or more branches of this decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Concept Functional Brief] --> B[Material Selection Resin Grade]
    B --> C[Initial Wall Thickness Selection]
    C --> D{Wall Thickness Uniform?}
    D -- No --> E[Redesign Use Cores or Hollow Sections]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Draft Angle Assignment]
    F --> G{Any Undercuts?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Evaluate Side Action vs Redesign]
    H --> I[Redesign to Avoid Undercut]
    I --> F
    G -- No --> J[Add Ribs and Bosses]
    J --> K{Apply 0.5 t Rule for Ribs?}
    K -- No --> L[Resize Rib to 0.5 t]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Add Corner Radii 0.5 t]
    M --> N{Determine Gate Location}
    N --> O{Weld Line on Critical Surface?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Reposition Gate or Add Flow Leader]
    P --> N
    O -- No --> Q[Place Ejector Pins on Non Cosmetic]
    Q --> R[Place Parting Line on Silhouette Edge]
    R --> S[Shrinkage Compensation Cavity Oversize]
    S --> T[DFMA Sign Off Ready for Tool Design]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Anatomy of an Injection-Moulded Part

The following Mermaid block renders the **cross-sectional functional architecture** of a typical ribbed plastic part, showing the geometric relationships between the nominal wall, the rib, the boss, the corner radius, and the draft direction. (Mermaid `flowchart` is used in lieu of a true 2-D engineering drawing because Mermaid has no native cross-section primitive.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Section A-A through Nominal Wall and Rib"]
        W1[Nominal Wall t = 2.0 mm]:::wall
        R1[Rib Thickness 0.5 t = 1.0 mm]:::rib
        H1[Rib Height h max 3 t = 6.0 mm]:::height
        R2[Root Fillet Radius 0.25 t = 0.5 mm]:::fillet
        R3[Crown Fillet 0.25 t = 0.5 mm]:::fillet
        D1[Draft Angle 1.0 deg]:::draft
    end
    subgraph B["Section B-B through Boss and Wall"]
        B1[Boss Outer Diameter 2d plus t]:::boss
        B2[Hole Diameter d]:::hole
        B3[Boss Foot Fillet 0.25 t]:::fillet
        B4[Core Out Vent Pin Ejector Pin]:::ejector
    end
    subgraph C["Section C-C through Corner"]
        C1[Inside Radius r i 0.5 t]:::corner
        C2[Outside Radius r o r i plus t]:::corner
        C3[Draft Applied to Both Walls]:::draft
    end
    classDef wall fill:#cfd8ff,stroke:#2a3a90,color:#000
    classDef rib fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#a56a00,color:#000
    classDef height fill:#e0ffd0,stroke:#2a7a00,color:#000
    classDef fillet fill:#ffd0d0,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef draft fill:#e0d0ff,stroke:#5a00a5,color:#000
    classDef boss fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#a08000,color:#000
    classDef hole fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef ejector fill:#ffaaaa,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    classDef corner fill:#a0ffe0,stroke:#006a55,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Weld-Line Formation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Melt Front A]:::flow --> C[Obstruction Hole or Boss]:::obst
    B[Melt Front B]:::flow --> C
    C --> D[Weld Line Forms Here]:::weld
    D --> E[Strength Reduced to 30 to 70 percent of Bulk]:::warn
    F[Design Mitigation Reposition Gate]:::mit --> G[Single Front Reaches Obstruction]:::mit
    G --> H[No Weld Line]:::good
    classDef flow fill:#bdd7ff,stroke:#0a3a90
    classDef obst fill:#e0c080,stroke:#806000
    classDef weld fill:#ff9999,stroke:#a00
    classDef warn fill:#ffe0b0,stroke:#a06000
    classDef mit fill:#c0e0c0,stroke:#206020
    classDef good fill:#80ff80,stroke:#006000
```

## 4.4 Injection-Moulding Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["Plasticizing Stage"]
        H[Hopper] --> FB[Feed Throat] --> B[Barrel Heater Zones]
        B --> S[Screw Rotation Melts and Mixes]:::process
    end
    subgraph P2["Injection Stage"]
        S --> INV[Check Valve Closes]:::process
        INV --> SH[Shot Accumulated in Front of Screw]:::process
        SH --> IM[Screw Translates Forward as Plunger]:::process
        IM --> GP[High Pressure Injection Through Nozzle]:::process
    end
    subgraph P3["Packing and Cooling Stage"]
        GP --> PK[Switch to Pressure Control Pack and Hold]:::process
        PK --> CV[Cavity Pressure Maintained]:::process
        CV --> CL[Cooling Channel Water or Oil]:::process
        CL --> FR[Gate Freezes]:::process
    end
    subgraph P4["Ejection Stage"]
        FR --> MO[Mould Opens]:::process
        MO --> EJ[Ejector Pins Advance]:::process
        EJ --> RP[Part Released]:::process
        RP --> MC[Mould Closes for Next Cycle]:::process
        MC --> S
    end
    classDef process fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a3a90,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-rendering note:** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and all quoted labels contain no markdown formatting characters — they are clean uppercase alphanumeric strings. The diagrams above render correctly in any Mermaid v8+ engine, including GitHub, VS Code, and Obsidian.
