# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> Define *volumetric shrinkage* and *linear mould shrinkage* of an injection-moulded plastic part. Establish the mathematical relation $S_L \approx S_v / 3$ for isotropic shrinkage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Volumetric shrinkage $S_v$:** The fractional reduction in the volume of the polymer as it cools from the melt (cavity) temperature to the room (ejection) temperature, expressed as a percentage of the cavity volume. (1 mark)
- **Linear mould shrinkage $S_L$:** The fractional reduction in any single linear dimension of the part from cavity to room temperature. (1 mark)
- **Relation:** For isotropic, homogeneous shrinkage, $V_{part} = V_0(1 - S_L)^3$. Equating with $V_{part} = V_0(1 - S_v)$ and taking the cube root gives $S_L = 1 - (1 - S_v)^{1/3}$. Taylor expansion for small $S_v$ yields $S_L \approx S_v/3$. (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> State the **"rib = half wall"** rule in injection-moulded part design. Why is a rib of thickness equal to the wall thickness considered poor DFMA practice?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Rule statement:** $t_{rib} \leq 0.5\,t_{wall}$ (rib thickness should be at most half the nominal wall thickness). (1 mark)
- **Sink-mark mechanism:** A rib of equal thickness to the wall creates a local thickening of $\approx 2t$ at the rib root. The thicker region solidifies *later* than the surrounding wall; volumetric contraction pulls the cosmetic surface inward, producing a **visible sink mark** directly opposite the rib. (1 mark)
- **Correct alternative:** When additional stiffness is needed, use **multiple thinner ribs** spaced at minimum $3 t_{wall}$ between centres, not a single thicker rib. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Module-Internal Choice Option

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** List and briefly explain **any seven** typical characteristics that distinguish an injection-moulded plastic part from a metal-machined or die-cast part. For each, state the design rule a DFMA engineer should apply. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polypropylene (PP) container has a nominal wall thickness of $2.5$ mm and a nominal outer diameter of $120$ mm. The volumetric shrinkage of the grade is $S_v = 0.060$ (i.e., $6\%$). Determine:
>   1. The linear mould shrinkage factor $S_L$ using the small-strain approximation.
>   2. The required cavity diameter to produce a part of nominal $120$ mm at room temperature.
>   3. The minimum draft angle for a $40$ mm deep draw.
>   4. The maximum allowable rib thickness, and justify your answer with a sketch description. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — Seven Characteristics (7 marks)

| # | Characteristic | Origin / Mechanism | Design Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wall-thickness variability** | Differential cooling → sink marks, voids | $t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 2$; core out thick pockets |
| 2 | **Draft-angle requirement** | Part grips steel wall on cooling | $0.5°$ – $2°$ (textured: $2°$ – $5°$); $\approx 1°$ per 25 mm depth |
| 3 | **Rib–boss intersections** | Local thickening at root | Rib $= 0.5\,t$; boss $D = 2d + t$ |
| 4 | **Corner-radius requirement** | Stress concentration, flow restriction | $r_i \geq 0.5\,t$; $r_o = r_i + t$ |
| 5 | **Gate scar** | Material enters through a point witness | Place on non-cosmetic surface; use hot-runner / submarine gate |
| 6 | **Weld / meld lines** | Flow split + rejoin around an obstruction | Strength derated to $30$ – $70\%$; relocate gate or add flow leader |
| 7 | **Parting-line witness** | Mould halves meet along this contour | Place on silhouette edge, not on visible face |
| 8 | **Ejector-pin mark** | Pins push the part out of the cavity | Place on ribs, bosses, or hidden surfaces |

*(Any 7 well-explained — 1 mark per row including the rule; 7 marks.)*

### Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Part (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Linear shrinkage factor.** (1 mark)

$$
S_L = \frac{S_v}{3} = \frac{0.060}{3} = 0.020
$$

**Step 2 — Required cavity diameter.** (1.5 marks)

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part}\,(1 + S_L) = 120\,(1 + 0.020) = 120 \times 1.020 = 122.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum draft angle for 40 mm draw.** (1.5 marks)

Using the working rule $\theta_d \approx 1° + (H/25 \text{ mm})$:

$$
\theta_d = 1° + \frac{40}{25} = 1° + 1.6° = 2.6°
$$

(Round to **$2.5°$ or $3°$** for tool-room specification.) (1 mark for selection of formula, 0.5 for arithmetic.)

**Step 4 — Maximum rib thickness + sketch description.** (3 marks)

$$
t_{rib,max} = 0.5 \times t_{wall} = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Sketch description (verbal):** A transverse cross-section through the rib and adjacent wall shows the wall as a 2.5-mm-thick slab, with a rib of 1.25 mm projecting from its inner face. A fillet of radius $0.25\,t \approx 0.6$ mm is applied at the rib root (to reduce stress) and a similar small radius at the rib crown (to improve flow). The rib is tapered with $\approx 2°$ draft in the ejection direction to avoid drag marks.

*(2 marks for numerical result + 1 mark for sketch description.)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a clearly labelled block diagram, describe the **four stages** of the injection-moulding cycle and identify which stage most strongly influences (i) sink-mark formation, (ii) weld-line formation, and (iii) ejection-pin mark placement. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene (ABS) electrical enclosure has a nominal wall thickness of $2.0$ mm. The cavity-wall temperature is $60 \degree C$, the melt temperature is $240 \degree C$, and the ejection temperature is $95 \degree C$. The thermal diffusivity of the ABS grade is $\alpha = 1.1 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. Estimate the cooling time using the analytical slab formula. State two design actions you would take if the resulting cycle time was unacceptable in a high-volume production setting. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — Four Stages (7 marks)

**The Four Stages** (1.5 marks each = 6 marks; 1 mark for the influence mapping):

1. **Plasticising (Melting & Metering):** Granular resin is fed from the hopper through the feed throat into the heated barrel. The rotating screw melts the polymer by conduction from the barrel heaters *and* by viscous dissipation. A metered "shot" of melt is accumulated in front of the screw tip. The screw acts as a check valve (non-return ring) to prevent back-flow.
2. **Injection (Fill):** The screw stops rotating and translates forward as a plunger, injecting the melt through the nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate into the cavity at high pressure ($35$ – $140$ MPa). The fill phase ends when $\approx 90$ – $98\%$ of the cavity volume is filled.
3. **Packing & Cooling (Hold):** The machine switches from *velocity control* to *pressure control*. Additional melt is forced in to compensate for volumetric shrinkage. Once the gate freezes, no more melt can enter; the part continues to cool under residual cavity pressure. This is the longest stage of the cycle.
4. **Ejection (Demoulding):** The mould opens along the parting line. Ejector pins (or a stripper plate) push the solidified part out of the cavity. The mould then re-closes, and the cycle repeats.

**Influence mapping** (1 mark):

- **Sink marks** → controlled in stage (3) by *cavity pressure* and *gate-freeze time*. Insufficient pack pressure leaves the core under-packed.
- **Weld lines** → determined in stage (2) by the *flow geometry* (where the melt splits around obstructions) and the *melt temperature*.
- **Ejector-pin marks** → determined in stage (4) by the *ejector-pin layout*, which is fixed at tool design (a stage-(1) design-time decision).

### Model Solution B(b) — Cooling-Time Estimate (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify inputs.** (0.5 mark)

$$
t = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad
\alpha = 1.1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$
$$
T_m = 240 \degree C, \quad T_{wall} = 60 \degree C, \quad T_{eject} = 95 \degree C
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the analytical slab formula.** (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cool} &= \dfrac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 (T_{eject} - T_{wall})}\right) \\[2mm]
&= \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 (1.1 \times 10^{-7})}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(240 - 60)}{\pi^2 (95 - 60)}\right) \\[2mm]
&= \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.086 \times 10^{-5}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 180}{9.87 \times 35}\right) \\[2mm]
&= 0.3684 \times \ln(4.166) \\[2mm]
&= 0.3684 \times 1.427 \\[2mm]
&\approx 0.526 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the result.** (1 mark)

The estimate of $t_{cool} \approx 0.53$ s is *theoretically* very short, but the practical cycle time for a 2-mm ABS part is typically $8$ – $15$ s because the model neglects (a) heat transfer from the *steel mould* to the *coolant channels*, (b) the ejection time, and (c) the plasticising time. The analytical result is a *lower bound* — useful for relative comparison, not for absolute cycle-time planning.

**Step 4 — Two design actions to reduce cycle time.** (2 marks — 1 each)

1. **Use a high-thermal-conductivity mould insert** (e.g., a beryllium–copper or hardened-aluminium insert) in the thick-cored region to increase local heat extraction rate. This can reduce $t_{cool}$ by $30$ – $50\%$.
2. **Optimise the cooling-channel layout** to maintain a *uniform mould-wall temperature* by placing conformal cooling channels (made by 3-D printed mould inserts) within $3$ – $5$ mm of the cavity surface, particularly under thick sections. Additionally, switch from water to a high-heat-capacity coolant (e.g., water-glycol mixture) to allow the mould to run at a higher, more uniform temperature without sacrificing extraction rate.

*(Alternative acceptable actions: (i) reduce wall thickness where feasible (the $t^2$ dependence makes this powerful); (ii) select a faster-cycling resin grade with lower specific heat; (iii) use a hot-runner system to eliminate cold-runner waste and reduce re-melt time.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in cooling-time derivation:** KTU examiners deduct $1$ – $2$ marks if $t$ is used in mm while $\alpha$ is in m²/s. Convert *all* dimensions to metres before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing $S_v$ with $S_L$:** Students often use the volumetric value directly in the cavity-dimension formula. Always divide by 3 *first*.
> 3. **Forgetting that draft is in the *ejection* direction:** Draft tapers the part *as the mould opens* — i.e., the cavity is *larger* at the parting-line side, the core is *smaller*. Students frequently describe the direction backwards.
> 4. **Treating weld lines as cosmetic only:** KTU examiners award $2$ – $3$ marks for explicitly stating the *strength derating* of $30$ – $70\%$. A "cosmetic" answer alone scores only $1$ mark.
> 5. **Omitting the sink-mark mechanism:** Listing the rule ($t_{rib} \leq 0.5\,t$) without explaining *why* (differential cooling → volumetric contraction → cosmetic pull-in) scores only half marks. KTU expects the *physics*, not just the heuristic.
> 6. **Failing to convert $\ln$ in the cooling equation:** Some students confuse $\log_{10}$ and $\ln$. Check the *base* of the logarithm in the formula before computing.
> 7. **Mermaid-diagram mistakes in answer scripts:** In the **written** exam, students are not expected to draw Mermaid — but they *are* expected to draw clean labelled sketches. Practice drawing rib + wall + boss + draft + ejector-pin on a single cross-section.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Card — Memorise before exam day.**

- [ ] **Wall-thickness ratio:** $t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 2:1$ (the **single most-asked** DFMA number).
- [ ] **Linear shrinkage** $S_L \approx S_v / 3$ (isotropic small-strain).
- [ ] **Cavity design:** $D_{cavity} = D_{part}\,(1 + S_L)$.
- [ ] **Draft angle:** $0.5°$ – $2°$ standard, $1°$ per $25$ mm of draw depth, $2°$ – $5°$ textured.
- [ ] **Rib rule:** $t_{rib} \leq 0.5\,t_{wall}$; spacing $\geq 3\,t_{wall}$.
- [ ] **Boss rule (Fisher):** $D_{boss} = 2\,d_{hole} + t_{wall}$.
- [ ] **Corner radius:** $r_i \geq 0.5\,t$; $r_o = r_i + t$.
- [ ] **Weld-line strength:** $30$ – $70\%$ of bulk; relocate gate or add flow leader.
- [ ] **Cooling time:** $t_{cool} = (t^2 / \pi^2 \alpha)\,\ln(\ldots)$ — note the $t^2$ scaling (halving wall thickness reduces $t_{cool}$ by $4\times$).
- [ ] **Ejector-pin mark:** always on a non-cosmetic surface, ideally on a rib root.
- [ ] **Parting line:** place on the silhouette edge, not on a visible face.
- [ ] **Undercuts:** avoid — if unavoidable, they need side-action cores (₹₹₹).
- [ ] **Sink marks** appear opposite the thick region (rib root, boss foot, internal gusset junction).
- [ ] **Voids** form in the *core* of thick sections when the outer skin freezes before the core has finished contracting.
- [ ] **Gate types to remember:** edge, fan, pinpoint, submarine (tunnel), diaphragm, hot-runner, valve.
- [ ] **Flow lines** are *cosmetic only*; **weld lines** are *structural* — do not confuse them in the exam.
- [ ] **Cycle time:** $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ — $t_{cool}$ dominates.
- [ ] **Mould material hint:** use beryllium-copper or hardened-aluminium inserts locally where heat extraction is rate-limiting.
- [ ] **Demoulding safety check:** ensure $F_{eject} = \mu P_c A_{proj}$ stays within ejector-pin load rating (typically $< 50$ N per pin for standard pins).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Shrinkage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Shrinkage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrinkage** in the context of Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA) is defined as the **volumetric and linear contraction** exhibited by a material as it transitions from its molten / plasticized state to a fully solidified state at ambient room temperature. It is a **dimensional change phenomenon** that occurs in primary shaping processes such as **sand casting, investment casting, die casting, injection molding, and compression molding**.

Mathematically, **Linear Shrinkage (SL)** is expressed as the percentage reduction per unit length of the solidified part compared to the original mold or pattern cavity dimension:

$$SL = \frac{L_m - L_c}{L_m} \times 100\%$$

where $L_m$ is the original **mold cavity (or pattern)** dimension and $L_c$ is the **final cast/molded** dimension measured at room temperature.

**Volumetric Shrinkage (SV)** is similarly defined as:

$$SV = \frac{V_m - V_c}{V_m} \times 100\%$$

where $V_m$ denotes the **original molten/plasticized volume** and $V_c$ is the **final solidified volume**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:** Shrinkage is a **non-negotiable design consideration** under Module 4 (Casting & Molding Design Principles) because it directly governs the **pattern dimension, draft allowance, machining allowance, and final tolerance stack-up** of the manufactured component.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a **balloon filled with hot water**. As the water cools, the balloon visibly **shrinks and sags** — its outer surface contracts inward. Now imagine a **wet clay pot** drying under the sun: the rim diameter reduces, the height drops, and the entire body contracts. The same physics governs what happens inside a **foundry mold**.

Think of it this way:
- **Hot metal** = molecules vibrating wildly, spaced far apart (high energy, high volume).
- **Cool metal** = molecules tightly packed, vibrating less (low energy, low volume).
- The **transit** from hot to cold = **shrinkage**.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Intuition Rule:** If the material was "bigger" when hot, the part you measure at room temperature will be "smaller." The patternmaker must therefore build the mold **bigger** than the final desired part — this is called the **Shrinkage Allowance**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Value (Typical Range) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Unit of Shrinkage** | **% (percentage)** or **mm/m** |
| **Cast Iron Shrinkage** | **~ 1.0 %** |
| **Steel Casting Shrinkage** | **~ 2.0 %** |
| **Aluminum Alloy Shrinkage** | **~ 1.5 %** |
| **Brass / Bronze Shrinkage** | **~ 1.5 %** |
| **Zinc Alloy Die Casting** | **~ 2.5 %** |
| **Thermoplastics (e.g., PP, ABS)** | **1.0 – 2.5 %** |
| **Standard Ambient Reference Temperature** | **20 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Distinct Stages of Shrinkage

Shrinkage in **metallic casting** is not a single event; it unfolds in three sequential physical stages. Each stage must be considered separately when designing the pattern and gating system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 1 — Liquid Shrinkage (Liquid Contraction)**
> - Occurs **above the solidus temperature** while the metal is still in the molten state.
> - Caused purely by **thermal contraction** of the liquid as it cools.
> - Compensated by feeding **molten metal from the riser** into the casting cavity.
> - The **riser design** is critical here; if inadequate, **shrinkage cavities (porosity)** form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 2 — Solidification Shrinkage (Phase Change Contraction)**
> - Occurs at the **solidus temperature** during the **liquid → solid** phase transformation.
> - For most metals, the **solid phase is denser** than the liquid phase (water/cast iron being exceptions).
> - This is the **most damaging stage** in terms of internal defect formation (voids, centerline porosity).
> - Compensated by **proper riser sizing, chills, and feeders**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 3 — Solid Shrinkage (Solid Contraction)**
> - Occurs **below the solidus temperature** as the solid casting cools from the solidus to room temperature.
> - This is the stage the **DFMA designer is most concerned with**, because it directly determines the **final part dimension**.
> - Compensated by applying a **Shrinkage Allowance (SA)** to the pattern dimension.

---

## 2.2 How Shrinkage Affects Design — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the material** of the final part (e.g., cast iron, aluminum, ABS plastic).
2. **Look up the standard shrinkage percentage** from material handbooks (e.g., cast iron ≈ 1.0 %).
3. **Calculate the Shrinkage Allowance (SA)** for every critical linear dimension.
4. **Add the SA to the nominal dimension** when fabricating the pattern.
5. **Add the Machining Allowance (MA)** for surfaces that require post-casting machining.
6. **Add the Draft Allowance (DA)** to allow easy pattern withdrawal from the sand mold.
7. **Subtract or add these systematically** to generate the **final pattern drawing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in DFMA:** A 1 % shrinkage error on a 500 mm dimension is **5 mm** — more than the entire tolerance band of a typical precision casting. Ignoring shrinkage destroys **dimensional accuracy** and forces expensive re-tooling.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $SL = \dfrac{L_m - L_c}{L_m} \times 100$ | Linear Shrinkage Percentage | **%** |
| 2 | $SV = \dfrac{V_m - V_c}{V_m} \times 100$ | Volumetric Shrinkage Percentage | **%** |
| 3 | $SA = L \times S$ | Shrinkage Allowance (per dimension) | **mm** |
| 4 | $P = L + SA + MA$ | Final Pattern Dimension (no draft) | **mm** |
| 5 | $P = L + SA + MA + DA$ | Final Pattern Dimension (with draft) | **mm** |
| 6 | $SV \approx 3 \times SL$ | Approximate volumetric-to-linear relation (isotropic) | **—** |
| 7 | $C_d = C_f + S \times C_f$ | Compensation for die/mold cavity | **mm** |
| 8 | $\Delta L = \alpha \times L_0 \times \Delta T$ | Thermal contraction (linear) | **mm** |

**Where:**
- $L_m$ = Mold / pattern cavity dimension
- $L_c$ = Final cast dimension at room temperature
- $V_m$ = Original molten volume
- $V_c$ = Final cast volume
- $L$ = Nominal (drawing) dimension of final part
- $S$ = Shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.01 for 1 %)
- $SA$ = Shrinkage allowance
- $MA$ = Machining allowance
- $DA$ = Draft allowance
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature drop (solidus to ambient)

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Examiner Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, when asked to "design a pattern," students **must** show $P = L + SA + MA + DA$ explicitly. Omitting **DA** is a common reason for losing **2–3 marks** out of 14.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundry Industry:** Prevents miscast parts and reduces scrap rate from 8 % to under 2 %.
- **Injection Molding (Plastics):** Mold cavity is **oversized** to compensate for polymer shrinkage on cooling.
- **Investment Casting (Aerospace):** Tight tolerance ±0.1 mm demands **shrinkage compensation at the wax pattern stage**.
- **3D Printing (FFF/FDM):** Slicer software applies a **horizontal expansion factor** to counteract polymer shrinkage.
- **Forging Design:** Die cavity is dimensioned to account for **thermal contraction** during cooling in the die.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Shrinkage from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** As a molten metal cools from pouring temperature $T_p$ to room temperature $T_a$, every linear dimension contracts proportionally to the temperature drop.

**Step 1 — Define the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) $\alpha$** for the solid phase of the metal:

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \times \Delta T}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the temperature range over which solid contraction occurs:**
The solid forms at the **solidus temperature** $T_s$ and cools down to the **ambient temperature** $T_a$.
Therefore:

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a$$

**Step 3 — Express the dimensional change $\Delta L$ of the solidified casting:**
The contraction occurs in the solid state from $L_s$ (length at solidus) to $L_c$ (length at ambient):

$$\Delta L = L_s - L_c$$

**Step 4 — Apply the CTE relation to obtain $L_c$ in terms of $L_s$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \alpha \times L_s \times \Delta T \\
L_s - L_c &= \alpha \times L_s \times (T_s - T_a) \\
L_c &= L_s \left[1 - \alpha (T_s - T_a)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that the mold cavity was dimensioned to the liquid volume**, but the **pattern itself** is machined to allow the metal to **fill** the cavity fully. So the pattern dimension $L_p$ is the dimension the metal will **occupy at the solidus temperature** (i.e., $L_p = L_s$ in this simplified model).

**Step 6 — Define the linear shrinkage fraction $S$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{L_p - L_c}{L_p} \\
S &= \frac{L_s - L_s [1 - \alpha (T_s - T_a)]}{L_s} \\
S &= \frac{L_s \times \alpha (T_s - T_a)}{L_s} \\
S &= \alpha \times \Delta T
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Final Working Equation:**

$$\boxed{S = \alpha \times (T_s - T_a)}$$

This shows that **shrinkage fraction** is the **product** of the **linear CTE** and the **solidus-to-ambient temperature drop**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shrinkage Allowance Per Dimension

**Given:** A final part drawing dimension $L = 250$ mm and a material shrinkage of $S = 1.0 \% = 0.01$.

**Step 1 — State the Shrinkage Allowance formula:**

$$SA = L \times S$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$SA = 250 \times 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$SA = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — State the Pattern Dimension formula (no draft, no machining):**

$$P = L + SA$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$P = 250 + 2.5 = 252.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
The patternmaker must machine the pattern **2.5 mm larger** than the drawing dimension so that, after solidification and cooling, the casting settles at exactly **250 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric-to-Linear Shrinkage Relationship

**Given:** Isotropic material (uniform shrinkage in all directions).

**Step 1 — Express the volume of a cube of side $L_m$ (mold) and $L_c$ (cast):**

$$V_m = L_m^3, \quad V_c = L_c^3$$

**Step 2 — Express volumetric shrinkage:**

$$SV = \frac{V_m - V_c}{V_m} = 1 - \left(\frac{L_c}{L_m}\right)^3$$

**Step 3 — Express linear shrinkage:**

$$SL = \frac{L_m - L_c}{L_m} = 1 - \frac{L_c}{L_m}$$

**Step 4 — Let $k = 1 - SL = \dfrac{L_c}{L_m}$. Then:**

$$SV = 1 - k^3$$

**Step 5 — Apply binomial expansion $(1 - x)^3 = 1 - 3x + 3x^2 - x^3$ with $x = SL$:**

$$SV = 1 - (1 - SL)^3 = 3 \cdot SL - 3 \cdot SL^2 + SL^3$$

**Step 6 — For small $SL$ (typical case $SL \leq 0.025$), ignore higher-order terms:**

$$SV \approx 3 \cdot SL$$

**Step 7 — Final Approximation (commonly used in KTU numericals):**

$$\boxed{SV \approx 3 \times SL}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A cast iron gear blank is to be produced with a finished outer diameter of **200 mm** and a hub length of **80 mm**. The patternmaker uses a shrinkage allowance of **1 %**. Calculate:
> (a) The pattern outer diameter
> (b) The pattern hub length
> (c) The total volumetric shrinkage, assuming isotropy.

**Solution (a):** Pattern OD = $200 + (200 \times 0.01) = 200 + 2.0 = \mathbf{202.0 \text{ mm}}$

**Solution (b):** Pattern hub length = $80 + (80 \times 0.01) = 80 + 0.8 = \mathbf{80.8 \text{ mm}}$

**Solution (c):** $SV \approx 3 \times SL = 3 \times 1 = \mathbf{3.0 \%}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow: Shrinkage Compensation Chain

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **decision-based process** by which a DFMA designer compensates for shrinkage at the pattern design stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Part Drawing Received] --> B[Identify Material Type]
    B --> C[Lookup Material Shrinkage S percent]
    C --> D[Identify Critical Linear Dimensions]
    D --> E[Compute Shrinkage Allowance SA per dimension]
    E --> F[Add Machining Allowance MA to machined surfaces]
    F --> G[Add Draft Allowance DA to vertical faces]
    G --> H[Compute Final Pattern Dimension P = L + SA + MA + DA]
    H --> I{Dimension within Foundry Tolerance?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Release Pattern for Fabrication]
    I -->|No| K[Re-evaluate S and Recompute]
    K --> C
    J --> L[End: Pattern Ready]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Three-Stage Shrinkage Phenomenon

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physical state transitions** of a casting and the corresponding shrinkage contributions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1((Molten Metal at Pouring Temp Tp)) --> P2((Liquid Shrinkage above Solidus))
    P2 --> P3((Solidification Shrinkage at Solidus Ts))
    P3 --> P4((Solid Shrinkage below Solidus))
    P4 --> P5((Final Casting at Ambient Ta))
    P2 -.Compensated by.-> R1[Riser Sizing]
    P3 -.Compensated by.-> R2[Feeders and Chills]
    P4 -.Compensated by.-> R3[Pattern Shrinkage Allowance]
    P5 --> P6{Volume Compare}
    P6 --> P7[SV = delta V / Vm]
    P6 --> P8[SL = delta L / Lm]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Shrinkage Compensation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Shrinkage Compensation Methods] --> M1[Pattern Oversizing]
    M --> M2[Mold Cavity Adjustment]
    M --> M3[Process Parameter Control]
    M --> M4[Material Selection]
    M1 --> M1a[Add SA to pattern dimension]
    M1 --> M1b[Use shrinkage rule on patternmaker's ruler]
    M2 --> M2a[Enlarge die cavity for die casting]
    M2 --> M2b[Adjust core prints]
    M3 --> M3a[Control pouring temperature]
    M3 --> M3b[Control cooling rate]
    M3 --> M3c[Use chills to localize solidification]
    M4 --> M4a[Choose low-shrinkage alloy]
    M4 --> M4b[Use filled polymers to reduce plastic shrinkage]
```

## 4.4 Compensating vs. Non-Compensating Effects — Side-by-Side Block

| Aspect | Without Shrinkage Compensation | With Shrinkage Compensation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Final Dimension** | Smaller than drawing | Matches drawing |
| **Tolerance Compliance** | Fails (out of spec) | Passes |
| **Scrap Rate** | High (15–25 %) | Low (2–5 %) |
| **Cost of Re-tooling** | High (one-time) | Negligible |
| **Assembly Fit** | Loose / interference | Designed fit |
| **Customer Rejection** | Frequent | Minimal |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Shrinkage" as applied to casting. List the three stages of shrinkage with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Shrinkage is the **reduction in volume (and corresponding linear dimensions)** of a casting as it cools from pouring temperature to room temperature.

The three stages are:
1. **Liquid Shrinkage** — Contraction of the molten metal above the solidus temperature.
2. **Solidification Shrinkage** — Contraction occurring at the solidus temperature during the liquid-to-solid phase change.
3. **Solid Shrinkage** — Contraction of the solid metal as it cools from solidus temperature to room temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Cue (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, all 3 stages named = 1 mark, brief description of each = 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: A steel casting has a nominal length of 500 mm. Given the shrinkage for steel is 2 %, calculate the pattern length. Also state one engineering consequence if shrinkage is ignored.**

**Model Answer:**

$$SA = L \times S = 500 \times 0.02 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

$$P = L + SA = 500 + 10 = \mathbf{510 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Consequence (any one of the following):**
- Casting will be **undersized** (only 500 mm nominal = actual 490 mm, failing drawing spec).
- **Interference fit** at assembly stage → parts will not assemble.
- **High rejection / scrap rate**, increasing production cost.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks** split across two sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **three stages of shrinkage** in a metal casting process. For each stage, identify (i) the temperature range, (ii) the physical cause, and (iii) the design feature that compensates for it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron flange is to be produced with the following drawing dimensions:
- Outer diameter $D = 300$ mm
- Inner bore diameter $d = 150$ mm
- Thickness $t = 25$ mm

Given the linear shrinkage for cast iron is **1 %**, calculate the **pattern dimensions** (OD, bore, thickness) and the **volumetric shrinkage** assuming isotropic behavior. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):
1. **Liquid Shrinkage (2 marks):**
   - Temperature: Above solidus, between pouring temp $T_p$ and liquidus $T_L$.
   - Cause: Thermal contraction of the molten metal.
   - Compensated by: **Riser** — a reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting as it contracts.

2. **Solidification Shrinkage (2 marks):**
   - Temperature: At the solidus $T_s$, during liquid→solid transformation.
   - Cause: Density increase (solid is denser than liquid for most metals).
   - Compensated by: **Risers, feeders, and chills** that supply extra molten metal during phase change.

3. **Solid Shrinkage (3 marks):**
   - Temperature: Below solidus, between $T_s$ and ambient $T_a$.
   - Cause: Thermal contraction of the solid metal.
   - Compensated by: **Pattern Shrinkage Allowance** — the pattern is oversized so the cooled casting matches the drawing dimension.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):
Given $SL = 1\% = 0.01$.

**Pattern OD:**
$$P_{OD} = 300 + (300 \times 0.01) = 300 + 3 = \mathbf{303 \text{ mm}}$$

**Pattern Bore:**
$$P_{bore} = 150 + (150 \times 0.01) = 150 + 1.5 = \mathbf{151.5 \text{ mm}}$$

**Pattern Thickness:**
$$P_t = 25 + (25 \times 0.01) = 25 + 0.25 = \mathbf{25.25 \text{ mm}}$$

**Volumetric Shrinkage (using $SV \approx 3 \times SL$):**
$$SV = 3 \times 1\% = \mathbf{3\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often **forget to apply shrinkage to the bore dimension** (treating it as a hole and not a linear dimension). **Every linear dimension, internal or external, must be compensated.** Deduction: 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Derive the relationship between **linear shrinkage (SL)** and **volumetric shrinkage (SV)** for an isotropic material. State the final approximation used in practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminum alloy sand casting has a pouring temperature of **720 °C** and solidifies at **580 °C**. The ambient temperature is **20 °C**. The coefficient of thermal expansion (solid) is $\alpha = 24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$. If the final part dimension is **400 mm**, determine:
- (i) The shrinkage fraction $S$
- (ii) The required pattern dimension
- (iii) The expected final casting dimension (verify) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):
**Derivation (as in Section 3.3):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SV &= 1 - \left(\frac{L_c}{L_m}\right)^3 \\
SL &= 1 - \frac{L_c}{L_m} \\
\text{Let } k &= 1 - SL \\
SV &= 1 - (1 - SL)^3 \\
SV &= 1 - [1 - 3 SL + 3 SL^2 - SL^3] \\
SV &= 3 SL - 3 SL^2 + SL^3
\end{aligned}$$

For $SL \ll 1$ (typically $< 3\%$):

$$\boxed{SV \approx 3 \times SL}$$

This is the **standard approximation** used in foundry practice and in KTU numericals.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the temperature range for solid contraction:**
$$\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 580 - 20 = 560 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shrinkage fraction:**
$$S = \alpha \times \Delta T = (24 \times 10^{-6}) \times 560 = 0.01344 = \mathbf{1.344\%}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Shrinkage Allowance:**
$$SA = L \times S = 400 \times 0.01344 = 5.376 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Pattern Dimension:**
$$P = L + SA = 400 + 5.376 = \mathbf{405.376 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (final casting dimension):**
The metal cools from solidus to ambient in the mold. Length at solidus ≈ Pattern dimension = 405.376 mm.
Final length = $L_s [1 - \alpha (T_s - T_a)] = 405.376 \times (1 - 0.01344)$
$= 405.376 \times 0.98656 = \mathbf{400.00 \text{ mm}}$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently use the **pouring temperature (720 °C)** instead of the **solidus temperature (580 °C)** in the formula $S = \alpha \Delta T$. Only the **solid-state cooling** range contributes to the **pattern shrinkage allowance**. The liquid and solidification stages are compensated by **risers**, not by pattern oversizing. Deduction: 2–3 marks if confused.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing shrinkage stages** — Stage 1 and 2 are compensated by **risers/feeders**, NOT by pattern oversizing. Only Stage 3 (Solid Shrinkage) uses the shrinkage allowance on the pattern.
> 2. **Forgetting the draft allowance** when asked to design a complete pattern. Always write: $P = L + SA + MA + DA$.
> 3. **Using the wrong temperature** in the CTE formula. Use $T_s$ (solidus), not $T_p$ (pouring).
> 4. **Neglecting shrinkage on internal dimensions** (holes, bores, slots). All linear dimensions must be compensated.
> 5. **Ignoring units in the final answer.** KTU examiners award **0.5 mark** for proper SI units (mm, °C, %).
> 6. **Not writing the final boxed answer** in derivations. Box the final expression clearly using $\boxed{\;}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Effect of Shrinkage" (DFM Module 4):**
> - ✅ Shrinkage is the **dimensional contraction** from molten / plasticized state → solid at room temperature.
> - ✅ **Three stages:** Liquid → Solidification → Solid.
> - ✅ **Linear Shrinkage Formula:** $SL = \dfrac{L_m - L_c}{L_m} \times 100$
> - ✅ **Shrinkage Allowance Formula:** $SA = L \times S$
> - ✅ **Pattern Dimension Formula:** $P = L + SA + MA + DA$
> - ✅ **Volumetric vs. Linear:** $SV \approx 3 \times SL$ (isotropic, small SL).
> - ✅ **CTE-based Derivation:** $S = \alpha \times (T_s - T_a)$.
> - ✅ **Liquid & Solidification stages → compensated by RISERS.**
> - ✅ **Solid stage → compensated by PATTERN SHRINKAGE ALLOWANCE.**
> - ✅ **Typical values:** Cast Iron 1 %, Steel 2 %, Aluminum 1.5 %, Brass 1.5 %, Zinc 2.5 %.
> - ✅ **Internal dimensions (bores, slots) ALSO need shrinkage compensation.**
> - ✅ **For KTU 2024:** Always include units, box the final answer, and state assumptions (isotropy, ambient temperature = 20 °C).
> - ✅ **One mark for a labeled diagram** in long-answer questions — always draw the three-stage shrinkage schematic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suitable Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suitable Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suitable Materials in DFMA Context:** In Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA), *suitable materials* refers to the systematic selection and specification of engineering materials whose mechanical, physical, chemical, and economic properties align optimally with the chosen manufacturing processes, assembly methods, functional requirements, and service conditions of a product, while minimizing total lifecycle cost.

The selection of suitable materials is a **multi-criteria decision-making process** governed by the principles of concurrent engineering, where the material choice is finalized only after considering:

- **Function** (load, stress, environment)
- **Manufacturing** (process capability, formability, machinability)
- **Assembly** (joining methods, tolerance stack-up)
- **Cost** (raw material, processing, recycling)
- **Compliance** (regulations, sustainability, end-of-life)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Right Spice for the Dish":**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish. The choice of *suitable material* is like choosing the right spice. If you use cinnamon in a curry or turmeric in a dessert, the dish will fail — even if every other ingredient is perfect. Similarly, in DFMA, choosing titanium for a simple plastic toy (over-engineered) or using brittle cast iron where ductile steel is needed (functional failure) leads to a product that fails in manufacture, assembly, or service.
>
> **The Golden Rule:** *Material selection is not just a technical choice — it is a design decision that propagates through every stage of the product lifecycle.*

## 1.3 Key Material Property Metrics

The fundamental material metrics used in DFMA decision-making are:

- **Young's Modulus (E)** — measured in **GPa** — indicates stiffness.
- **Density (ρ)** — measured in **kg/m³** — affects weight and inertia.
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** — measured in **MPa** — determines load capacity.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_uts)** — measured in **MPa** — failure limit.
- **Elongation (%ε)** — dimensionless % — indicates ductility and formability.
- **Hardness (HV, HB, HRC)** — resistance to deformation/wear.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** — measured in **W/(m·K)** — heat dissipation.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, α)** — measured in **10⁻⁶/°C** — dimensional stability.
- **Melting Point (T_m)** — measured in **°C** — process temperature limits.
- **Corrosion Resistance** — qualitative + quantitative (mm/year).
- **Recyclability Index (RI)** — dimensionless 0–1.

## 1.4 Material Families in DFMA

> [!NOTE]
> The four primary families of engineering materials considered in DFMA are:
>
> 1. **Metals & Alloys** — Ferrous (steels, cast irons) and Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Ti, Mg, Ni).
> 2. **Polymers** — Thermoplastics, Thermosets, Elastomers.
> 3. **Ceramics & Glasses** — Oxides, Nitrides, Carbides.
> 4. **Composites** — Fibre-reinforced (CFRP, GFRP), Particle-reinforced (MMCs, PMCs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Selection Criteria Hierarchy in DFMA

The decision-making follows a hierarchical funnel from *must-have* to *nice-to-have* properties:

### Stage 1: Functional Constraints (Non-negotiable)
- Service load (static, dynamic, fatigue)
- Service environment (temperature, humidity, chemicals)
- Safety factor requirements
- Regulatory standards (FDA, ISO, ASTM)

### Stage 2: Manufacturing Constraints
- **Formability** (sheet metal bending, deep drawing, forging)
- **Castability** (fluidity, shrinkage, hot tearing tendency)
- **Machinability Index (MI%)** — relative ease of cutting
- **Weldability** (carbon equivalent, susceptibility to HAZ cracking)
- **Mouldability** (for polymers: melt flow index MFI)

### Stage 3: Assembly Constraints
- **Joinability** (welding, brazing, adhesive bonding, snap-fit, fasteners)
- **Tolerance compatibility** (CTE mismatch issues in mixed-material assemblies)
- **Galvanic compatibility** (avoiding dissimilar metal corrosion)

### Stage 4: Economic & Sustainability Constraints
- Raw material cost per kg
- Processing cost (energy, tooling)
- Recyclability and disassembly
- Carbon footprint of extraction & processing

## 2.2 The Ashby Material Selection Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Professor Michael F. Ashby's Method** is the industry-standard methodology for systematic material selection, forming the theoretical backbone of DFMA material choice.

### Step-by-Step Ashby Method:

1. **Translate** the design requirements into a *Function* (e.g., "must support a load F without excessive deflection").
2. **Define** the *Constraints* (e.g., maximum deflection δ ≤ 0.5 mm, length L fixed).
3. **Identify** the *Objective* (e.g., minimize mass *m*).
4. **Derive** the *Performance Index* (e.g., a combination of material properties that maximizes the objective while satisfying constraints).
5. **Screen** candidates using Ashby material selection charts.
6. **Rank** survivors using the performance index.
7. **Document** the final selection with supporting documentation.

### Example Performance Indices:

| Design Goal | Performance Index |
|---|---|
| Light, stiff tie (tension) | $P = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ |
| Light, stiff beam (bending) | $P = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ |
| Light, strong tie | $P = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ |
| Light, strong beam | $P = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ |
| Light, energy-absorbing spring | $P = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{E \cdot \rho}$ |
| Light, thermal insulator | $P = \dfrac{1}{k \cdot \rho}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equations for Material Selection in DFMA Examinations**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Stiffness | $E_s = E / \rho$ | $\text{MJ/kg} = \text{kPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Lightweight stiff design (aerospace) |
| 2 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_s = \sigma_y / \rho$ | $\text{kPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Lightweight strong design (automotive) |
| 3 | Strength-to-Weight (Beam) | $P_{b} = \sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$ | $\text{Pa}^{2/3}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Optimal beam cross-section |
| 4 | Stiffness-to-Weight (Beam) | $P_{b} = E^{1/2} / \rho$ | $\text{Pa}^{0.5}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Optimal beam cross-section |
| 5 | Spring Index (Energy Storage) | $P = \sigma_y^{2} / (E \cdot \rho)$ | $\text{Pa}/\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}$ | Springs, bumpers, crash structures |
| 6 | Linear Thermal Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | CTE mismatch in assemblies |
| 7 | Thermal Stress (Constrained) | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Bi-metallic joints |
| 8 | Machinability Rating | $MR = \dfrac{V_{cut}}{V_{ref}} \times 100$ | % | Relative ease of cutting |
| 9 | Cost-Per-Unit-Performance | $C_{eff} = C_{mat} \cdot \rho / P$ | currency unit | Economic optimization |
| 10 | Ashby Merit Index (General) | $M = \dfrac{P_1^a \cdot P_2^b}{P_3^c \cdot \rho^d}$ | various | Multi-attribute material ranking |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
>
> - **Aerospace:** Aluminium-lithium alloys selected over conventional Al for wing skins (specific stiffness drives the choice).
> - **Automotive:** Advanced high-strength steels (AHSS) replace mild steel in crash structures to meet crashworthiness with reduced gauge.
> - **Consumer Electronics:** Magnesium alloys used in laptop chassis to balance EMI shielding, stiffness, and weight.
> - **Medical Devices:** Ti-6Al-4V chosen for implants due to biocompatibility + specific strength.
> - **Packaging:** Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) selected for bottles due to clarity, food safety, and recyclability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Performance Index for a Light, Stiff Tie

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** Select a material of minimum mass *m* for a tie of length *L* that must carry a tensile load *F* without elongating by more than $\delta_{max}$.

### Step 1 — Express Functional Constraint
The stiffness requirement is governed by axial deformation under load:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E} \leq \delta_{max}
$$

Here, $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the tie, and $E$ is Young's Modulus.

### Step 2 — Solve for Required Cross-Sectional Area
Rearranging for the required area:

$$
A \geq \dfrac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}}
$$

This equation is the *area constraint* — the tie must be at least this large in cross-section.

### Step 3 — Express the Objective (Minimise Mass)
The mass of the tie is:

$$
m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho
$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the chosen material.

### Step 4 — Substitute the Area Constraint into the Mass Equation
Substituting the minimum area from Step 2 into the mass equation:

$$
m = \left( \dfrac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}} \right) \cdot L \cdot \rho
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{F \cdot L^2}{\delta_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{E}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Performance Index
The term $F \cdot L^2 / \delta_{max}$ depends only on the design (load, length, allowable deflection), not on the material. To **minimise mass**, we must **minimise** the material-dependent term:

$$
P = \dfrac{E}{\rho} = \text{Specific Stiffness}
$$

Therefore, the optimal material is the one with the **highest $E/\rho$** ratio. This ratio is called the **performance index** or **merit index** for a light, stiff tie.

### Step 6 — Numerical Example
Consider three candidate materials:

| Material | E (GPa) | ρ (kg/m³) | E/ρ (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel (AISI 1020) | 200 | 7850 | 25.5 |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 69 | 2700 | 25.6 |
| CFRP (Uni-directional) | 135 | 1600 | 84.4 |

**Conclusion:** CFRP gives a tie that is **~3.3× lighter** than steel or aluminium for the same stiffness, which is why CFRP is the preferred material for high-performance aerospace ties.

## 3.2 Derivation: CTE-Induced Thermal Stress in a Constrained Assembly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** Two materials A and B are rigidly bonded (e.g., a metal insert moulded inside a polymer housing). A temperature change $\Delta T$ occurs. Determine the induced thermal stress.

### Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain for Each Material
If the materials were unbonded, the free thermal strain would be:

$$
\varepsilon_A^{free} = \alpha_A \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\varepsilon_B^{free} = \alpha_B \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\alpha$ is the linear coefficient of thermal expansion.

### Step 2 — Strain Compatibility at the Bonded Interface
Because the assembly is rigid (constrained), both materials must deform by the same net strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\varepsilon_A^{mech} + \varepsilon_A^{free} = \varepsilon_B^{mech} + \varepsilon_B^{free} = \varepsilon
$$

The mechanical strains are the elastic responses to the internal stress:

$$
\varepsilon_A^{mech} = \dfrac{\sigma_A}{E_A}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_B^{mech} = \dfrac{\sigma_B}{E_B}
$$

### Step 3 — Force Equilibrium
Since the assembly is internally balanced (no external load), the forces in the two materials must be equal and opposite:

$$
\sigma_A \cdot A_A = -\sigma_B \cdot A_B
$$

### Step 4 — Solve Simultaneously
Solving the compatibility and equilibrium equations gives the interface shear/tensile stress:

$$
\sigma_A = \dfrac{(\alpha_B - \alpha_A) \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_A} + \dfrac{A_A}{A_B \cdot E_B}}
$$

For equal areas ($A_A = A_B$):

$$
\sigma_A = \dfrac{(\alpha_B - \alpha_A) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_A \cdot E_B}{E_A + E_B}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- **Large $\alpha$ mismatch** → high thermal stress → potential delamination or cracking.
- **Design mitigation:** Use materials with similar CTE (e.g., Al and CFRP are *not* a good pair; Invar + borosilicate glass *is* an excellent pair).
