# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define "Performance Index" in the context of material selection. Give two examples of performance indices for a light, stiff design.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A *Performance Index* (or *Merit Index*) is a combination of material properties that must be **maximized or minimized** to optimize a design objective while satisfying functional constraints. It is derived from translating the design requirements into a mathematical form involving material properties.

**Two examples:**

1. **Light, stiff tie (axial loading):**
$$P = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$$
2. **Light, stiff beam (bending):**
$$P = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$$

---

### **Question 2:** What is "Galvanic Corrosion"? Why is it a critical consideration in DFMA when selecting suitable materials for assembled products?

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Galvanic Corrosion** is the accelerated electrochemical corrosion that occurs when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte (e.g., moisture), because of the potential difference established between them. The more anodic metal corrodes preferentially.

**DFMA Significance:**
- In assembled products, fasteners, brackets, and structural members often combine dissimilar metals (e.g., steel bolts in aluminium housings).
- Without proper material selection (e.g., choosing metals close in the galvanic series, or using insulating washers), premature failure of the anodic component can occur.
- It directly affects *service life, maintenance cost, and warranty* — all DFMA cost metrics.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme provides an **Internal Choice** in Part B. Two alternative questions are provided below. Students answer either Question A or Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Ashby Material Selection Methodology** in detail. With suitable examples, show how performance indices are derived for:
    (i) A light, stiff **tie** (axial loading).
    (ii) A light, strong **beam** (bending loading).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A tie rod of length **L = 500 mm** must carry a tensile load of **F = 25 kN** with maximum allowable deflection of **δ = 0.25 mm**. Compare the masses of the tie if made from:
    • Aluminium 6061-T6 (E = 69 GPa, ρ = 2700 kg/m³)
    • Steel AISI 1020 (E = 200 GPa, ρ = 7850 kg/m³)
    • CFRP (E = 135 GPa, ρ = 1600 kg/m³)
State which material you would recommend and why.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Ashby Methodology and Performance Index Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Ashby Method Phases:**

1. **Translation:** Convert the design need into a *function* (e.g., "carry tensile load without excessive deflection").
2. **Constraints:** Identify the bounds (e.g., length fixed, max deflection specified).
3. **Objective:** Define the optimization goal (e.g., minimum mass).
4. **Derivation of Performance Index:** Use mechanics to express the objective in terms of material properties.
5. **Screening & Ranking:** Use Ashby charts to filter and rank.
6. **Documentation:** Final selection with supporting rationale.

**(i) Light, Stiff Tie (Axial Loading):**

The stiffness constraint is:
$$\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E} \leq \delta_{max}$$

Solving for area:
$$A = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}}$$

The mass is:
$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \dfrac{F \cdot L^2 \cdot \rho}{\delta_{max} \cdot E}$$

The design-dependent term is $\dfrac{F \cdot L^2}{\delta_{max}}$. To minimize *m*, the material term $\dfrac{\rho}{E}$ must be minimized, or equivalently, the **performance index** must be **maximized**:

$$P_{tie} = \dfrac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Stiffness)}$$

**(ii) Light, Strong Beam (Bending Loading):**

For a beam of square cross-section (side *b*) under bending load, the maximum stress is:
$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{6 \cdot M}{b^3}$$

With safety factor $N$:
$$b^3 = \dfrac{6 \cdot M \cdot N}{\sigma_y}$$

The mass is:
$$m = b^2 \cdot L \cdot \rho = \left( \dfrac{6 \cdot M \cdot N}{\sigma_y} \right)^{2/3} \cdot L \cdot \rho$$

The performance index becomes:
$$P_{beam} = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Strength, Beam Form)}$$

**(b) Numerical Comparison:**

**Given Data:**
- $F = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $\delta_{max} = 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

**Required Area (General Formula):**
$$A = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 0.5}{E \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{E} \text{ m}^2$$

**Mass (General Formula):**
$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{E} \times 0.5 \times \rho = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{7} \cdot \rho}{E}$$

**Material 1 — Aluminium 6061-T6:**
$$A_{Al} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{69 \times 10^9} = 7.246 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
$$m_{Al} = 7.246 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 2700 = 0.978 \text{ kg}$$

**Material 2 — Steel AISI 1020:**
$$A_{St} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{200 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
$$m_{St} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 7850 = 0.981 \text{ kg}$$

**Material 3 — CFRP:**
$$A_{CFRP} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{135 \times 10^9} = 3.704 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
$$m_{CFRP} = 3.704 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 1600 = 0.296 \text{ kg}$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Material | Mass (kg) | Mass Ratio (vs CFRP) |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 0.978 | 3.30× |
| Steel AISI 1020 | 0.981 | 3.31× |
| CFRP | 0.296 | 1.00× |

**Recommendation:** **CFRP** is the best choice when **mass minimization** is the primary objective (mass is ~70% less than metallic options). However, if **cost** is the dominant constraint, **Aluminium 6061-T6** is the practical choice (it has the lowest $E/\rho$ gap with steel but better corrosion resistance). For applications where both cost and mass matter, a tie-rod of steel is acceptable since its mass is essentially identical to aluminium here (the high E offsets the high ρ).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern, Alternative Set]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the suitability of **polymers, metals, and composites** as engineering materials in the DFMA context. Compare their relative advantages in terms of:
    (i) Manufacturability
    (ii) Assembly ease
    (iii) Cost and recyclability

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel shaft (E = 200 GPa, α = 12 × 10⁻⁶/°C) is fitted inside an aluminium hub (E = 70 GPa, α = 23 × 10⁻⁶/°C) at **20 °C**. The assembly is then heated to **120 °C** in service. The cross-sectional areas are equal ($A_{steel} = A_{Al} = 100 \text{ mm}^2$).
    (i) Determine the **interface stress** developed due to CTE mismatch.
    (ii) Suggest two design strategies to **mitigate** this stress.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison of Material Families in DFMA Context:**

| Criterion | Metals | Polymers | Composites |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturability** | Excellent — casting, forging, machining, forming, joining are all well-established. | Excellent for high-volume via injection moulding; limited for high-temperature or high-load. | Limited — specialised processes (RTM, autoclave); tooling expensive. |
| **Assembly Ease** | Welding, brazing, fasteners well-developed. Often requires multiple parts. | Snap-fits, ultrasonic welding, adhesives — enables **single-piece** designs, reducing assembly count. | Bolted or bonded joints; difficult to weld or form. |
| **Cost (Raw)** | Moderate (steel cheap; Ti expensive). | Low (commodity plastics very cheap). | High (CFRP can be 20–100× steel). |
| **Cost (Processing)** | Moderate–High (machining energy-intensive). | Low (injection moulding amortised over volume). | Very high (labour-intensive, long cure cycles). |
| **Recyclability** | High — well-established scrap streams. | Moderate — thermoplastics recyclable; thermosets difficult. | Low — most composites end up in landfill. |
| **Specific Strength** | Moderate (steel ~25 kN·m/kg; Al ~50 kN·m/kg). | Low (~30 kN·m/kg for top engineering plastics). | **Highest** (CFRP ~500 kN·m/kg). |
| **DFMA Verdict** | Default choice for general engineering. | **Best for high-volume consumer products** with low-to-moderate load. | **Best for performance-critical applications** (aerospace, motorsport). |

**Engineering Insight:**
- Polymers and metals together dominate ~80% of all manufactured goods.
- The DFMA trade-off is: *Polymers reduce part count* (often 5–10× part consolidation via snap-fits and integral hinges) but have lower performance envelopes.
- Composites offer the best **specific performance** but suffer from poor **recyclability** — a major concern in sustainable DFMA.

**(b) CTE Mismatch Analysis:**

**Given Data:**
- $E_{steel} = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $E_{Al} = 70 \text{ GPa} = 70 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$
- $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$
- $\Delta T = 120 - 20 = 100 \text{ °C}$
- $A_{steel} = A_{Al} = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain of Each Material:**

$$
\varepsilon_{steel}^{free} = \alpha_{steel} \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{Al}^{free} = \alpha_{Al} \cdot \Delta T = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 2.3 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Differential Free Strain (Driving Force for Stress):**

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{free} = \varepsilon_{Al}^{free} - \varepsilon_{steel}^{free} = (2.3 - 1.2) \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Composite Elastic Strain Compatibility:**

For equal areas, the interface stress is:

$$
\sigma_{interface} = \dfrac{\Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_{steel} \cdot E_{Al}}{E_{steel} + E_{Al}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{interface} = \dfrac{(23 - 12) \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 70 \times 10^9}{(200 + 70) \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\sigma_{interface} = \dfrac{11 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 14 \times 10^{18}}{270 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\sigma_{interface} = \dfrac{1.54 \times 10^{16}}{270 \times 10^9} = 57.04 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- The aluminium (higher CTE) is in **tension** (it wants to expand more than the steel allows).
- The steel (lower CTE) is in **compression** (constrained by the Al hub).
- This 57 MPa stress is **significant** — it can lead to plastic deformation in the aluminium, loss of interference fit, or fatigue cracking.

**Step 5 — Two Mitigation Strategies:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation Strategy 1 — Use a Compliant Interlayer:**
> Apply a thin layer of low-modulus material (e.g., a polymer adhesive or a rubber sleeve) between the steel and aluminium. This decouples the strain and absorbs the differential displacement, reducing the interface stress by ~70–90%.

> **Mitigation Strategy 2 — Use CTE-Matched Material Pair:**
> Replace the steel-aluminium pair with materials that have similar CTE values. For example, **Invar (Fe-Ni alloy, α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶/°C) paired with borosilicate glass** is a classical CTE-matched system used in precision instruments. Alternatively, use **titanium (α ≈ 8.6 × 10⁻⁶/°C)** instead of steel, which is closer to aluminium's 23 × 10⁻⁶/°C — the smaller gap reduces $\Delta\varepsilon_{free}$ proportionally.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Suitable Materials" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *density* with *specific gravity*.** Density has SI units of kg/m³, not dimensionless. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit errors.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify *which* performance index is being used.** A light, stiff *tie* uses $E/\rho$; a light, stiff *beam* uses $E^{1/2}/\rho$. Mixing them up costs full marks.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the *constraint* part of the question.** A common error is to compute the performance index without verifying that the material actually satisfies the constraint (e.g., yield strength, max temperature). Always check the *screening* step before ranking.
>
> 4. **Not writing the *units* in the final answer.** KTU examiners expect units in all numerical answers. A numerical answer without units = lose 0.5 marks minimum.
>
> 5. **Confusing *castability* with *formability*.** Castability is for molten material in a mould; formability is for solid material being shaped (e.g., sheet metal bending). Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 6. **Treating composites as "stronger than steel in all directions".** Composites are *anisotropic* — strong only in the fibre direction. Stating CFRP is "stronger than steel" without qualification is technically wrong and costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Suitable Materials" in DFMA**

### 🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize:
- **Ashby's 4-step method:** Translate → Constraints → Objective → Performance Index.
- **Performance indices:**
  - Light, stiff *tie*: $P = E / \rho$
  - Light, stiff *beam*: $P = E^{1/2} / \rho$
  - Light, strong *beam*: $P = \sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$
  - Energy-absorbing spring: $P = \sigma_y^2 / (E \cdot \rho)$
- **CTE stress equation (equal areas):**
$$\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_1 \cdot E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$

### 🧠 Material-Specific DFMA Rules of Thumb:
- **Steel** → default for high-strength, low-cost parts; *not* the lightest.
- **Aluminium** → excellent strength-to-weight; *good* corrosion resistance; *good* castability.
- **Magnesium** → lightest structural metal; *poor* corrosion resistance; *poor* ductility.
- **Titanium** → highest specific strength among metals; *very expensive*; *difficult to machine*.
- **Engineering polymers (PA, POM, PC)** → enable part consolidation via snap-fits; *low* temperature capability.
- **CFRP composites** → highest specific stiffness; *anisotropic*; *poor* recyclability; *high* cost.

### ⚙️ Process-Material Compatibility Quick Reference:
- *Casting:* Best for metals with low melting point and good fluidity (Al, Mg, cast iron).
- *Injection moulding:* Only for thermoplastics and some thermosets.
- *Sheet metal forming:* Ductile metals (low-carbon steel, Al, Cu).
- *Additive manufacturing:* Powder-based — works for most metals, polymers, and some ceramics.
- *Welding:* Limited for polymers (special techniques needed); poor for ceramics and composites.

### 🚫 Common DFMA Pitfalls in Material Choice:
- Choosing a material with high performance but **incompatible with the chosen process** (e.g., ceramics for casting).
- Ignoring **galvanic corrosion** in mixed-metal assemblies.
- Ignoring **CTE mismatch** in bonded/riveted joints.
- Selecting **over-engineered materials** (e.g., titanium for a non-critical cover).
- Neglecting **end-of-life recyclability** in modern sustainable design.

### 📐 Examiner-Expected Keywords:
Always use these exact terms in answers: *Performance Index, Merit Index, Specific Stiffness, Specific Strength, Ashby Charts, Material Index, Constraints, Free Variables, Design Space, Material Space, Property Space, Functional Requirements.*

### 🎯 Quick Numerical Conversions to Remember:
- 1 GPa = $10^9$ Pa = $10^3$ MPa
- 1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa = 1 N/mm²
- 1 kg/m³ = $10^{-3}$ g/cm³
- Density of water (reference) = 1000 kg/m³

> **END OF MODULE 4 — SUITABLE MATERIALS NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Design Recommendations" in DFMA?

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, the term **"Design Recommendations"** refers to a structured set of **empirical rules, geometric guidelines, tolerance principles, and material-process alignment strategies** that a designer must follow to ensure a component can be produced economically, accurately, and reliably using a specific manufacturing process. These recommendations form the bridge between the conceptual CAD model and a shop-floor manufacturable part.

Formally, design recommendations are **process-specific heuristics** derived from:
- **Dimensional capability curves** of machines
- **Tool wear and accessibility constraints**
- **Shrinkage, draft, and distortion behaviors**
- **Metallurgical phenomena** (grain flow, porosity, residual stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Design recommendations for **Casting, Forging, Welding, and Sheet Metal Working** form the core of this module. The emphasis is on translating *practical shop-floor constraints* into *upstream design decisions* so that cost of production, cycle time, and rejection rates are minimized **without sacrificing functional performance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

Imagine you are writing a recipe to bake a cake. The recipe says: *"use a round tin, do not fill more than 2/3rd, grease the sides lightly, bake at 180°C"*. You can ignore these rules and bake freely, but you will end up with a sticky, broken, or undercooked cake.

**Design recommendations are EXACTLY these "recipes" for manufacturing processes.**

- A *casting* needs a draft angle, just like a cake needs a greased tin — otherwise the part cannot be extracted.
- A *forging* needs generous fillet radii, just like a cake needs a smooth batter — sharp corners cause cracks.
- A *weld joint* needs proper edge preparation, just like a cake needs leveled batter — to avoid weak zones.
- A *sheet metal bend* needs a minimum bend radius, just like a cake needs proper proportions — to avoid tearing.

The genius of DFMA is that it shifts these "recipe rules" **upstream into the design stage**, so the product is *born manufacturable* rather than being "forced" into manufacture.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Design Recommendations?

| Problem in Industry | Root Cause | Recommendation That Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| Castings crack at sharp corners | Stress concentration + shrinkage restraint | Use generous **fillet radii** (≥ 3 mm) at all internal corners |
| Sheet metal tears at bends | Fiber strain > limiting strain | Maintain bend radius **r ≥ t (sheet thickness)** |
| Welds distort the assembly | Uneven heating/cooling | Use **symmetric joint design** and balanced welding sequence |
| Forgings develop folds/laps | Improper material flow | Provide **draft, radii, and proper web thickness** |
| Machined parts are rejected | Tolerance tighter than machine capability | Design within **process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Design Recommendations are process-specific geometric and tolerance guidelines formulated to ensure that a component is producible with minimum cost, minimum scrap, and consistent quality using the chosen manufacturing route."*

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Module 4

```
Module 4 - Design Recommendations
        │
        ├──► Pillar 1: Design for Casting (DFC)
        ├──► Pillar 2: Design for Forging (DFF)
        ├──► Pillar 3: Design for Welding (DFW)
        └──► Pillar 4: Design for Sheet Metal (DFSM)
```

Each pillar addresses:
1. **Process capability envelope**
2. **Geometric rules** (draft, radii, web/rib ratios)
3. **Tolerance guidance**
4. **Common defects and their design remedies**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Design for Casting (DFC)

Casting involves pouring molten metal into a mold. The designer must account for **fluid flow, solidification shrinkage, and pattern removal**.

### 2.1.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Wall Thickness** | As **uniform** as possible; ideal range **5–25 mm** for sand casting | Uneven walls cause **hot spots** → shrinkage porosity |
| **Section Thickness Ratio** | Adjacent sections: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2:1$ to $1.5:1$ | Prevents thermal gradient → reduces stress |
| **Fillet Radius** | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ at junctions | Avoids stress concentration |
| **Draft Angle** | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (external), $2°$ to $5°$ (internal) | Allows easy pattern withdrawal |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall}$ to $0.8 \, t_{wall}$ | Prevents hot spot at rib base |
| **Boss Thickness** | $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ | Adequate strength for fastening |

### 2.1.2 The "I-Section" Rule

For a flat plate requiring rigidity, instead of increasing thickness, use a **Tee or I-section**:

$$
t_{rib} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \times t_{wall}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks *"Suggest a design modification to eliminate shrinkage porosity in a cast bracket."* The model answer: *Replace uniform thick section with a T-section having rib thickness = 0.7 × wall thickness, and add fillet radii at all junctions.*

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Design for Forging (DFF)

Forging plastically deforms metal between dies. Material flow is paramount.

### 2.2.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Draft Angle** | $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die forging) | Die separation; punch clearance |
| **Fillet Radius (External)** | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ where $h$ = depth | Material flow into corner |
| **Fillet Radius (Internal)** | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Reduces die stress, prevents crack |
| **Web Thickness** | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ where $W$ = width | Avoid folding/lapping |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$ | Ensures full die fill |
| **Corner Radius Ratio** | $\dfrac{r_i}{h} \leq 0.15$ | Die life optimization |
| **Parting Line** | Should be a **straight plane** through widest cross-section | Simplifies die making |

### 2.2.2 Grain Flow Principle

In forging, **grain flow follows the contour** of the part. Design should ensure that:
- Fiber direction is **parallel to the principal stress** (tensile axis)
- Avoid abrupt changes in cross-section
- Avoid **sharp corners** that interrupt grain flow

$$
\text{Maximum allowable rib height} = 5 \times t_{rib}
$$

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Design for Welding (DFW)

Welding creates a **localized metallurgical bond** through fusion. Heat input, joint geometry, and accessibility govern weld quality.

### 2.3.1 Key Joint Design Rules

| Joint Type | Symbol | Strength Efficiency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Butt Joint (Square) | — | 70–80 % | Thin sheets (< 3 mm) |
| Butt Joint (V-groove) | V | 95–100 % | Plates > 3 mm |
| T-Joint (Fillet) | ⊥ | 60–70 % | Structural brackets |
| Lap Joint (Fillet) | ⫽ | 50–65 % | Sheet assemblies |
| Corner Joint | ⌐ | 70–80 % | Boxes, frames |

### 2.3.2 Fillet Weld Geometry

For a fillet weld of leg size $s$ and throat thickness $t$:

$$
t = 0.707 \, s
$$

> This is the **single most important formula** in welding design — KTU loves asking it.

### 2.3.3 Weld Strength Formula

For a fillet weld of length $L$, leg size $s$, in shear:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}
$$

where:
- $P$ = load capacity (N)
- $s$ = leg size (mm)
- $L$ = weld length (mm)
- $\tau_{allow}$ = allowable shear stress (MPa)

### 2.3.4 Critical Design Recommendations

- **Minimum weld leg size**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ where $t_{min}$ is the thinner plate
- **Maximum weld leg size**: For plates $\leq$ 6 mm, $s_{max} = t_{plate}$
- **Pitch of intermittent welds**: $p \leq 16 \, t$ for compression; $p \leq 24 \, t$ for tension (avoid)
- **Weld should not be located at high stress concentration zones**
- **Avoid weld intersection** — causes 4-direction stress

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Design for Sheet Metal Working (DFSM)

Sheet metal operations include **bending, drawing, piercing, blanking, and stretching**.

### 2.4.1 The K-Factor (Neutral Axis)

When a sheet is bent, the **outer surface stretches** and the **inner surface compresses**. The **neutral axis** lies somewhere between them, defined by the **K-factor**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

where $t_{neutral}$ is the distance from inner surface to neutral axis.

**Standard K-factor values:**

| Material | K-factor |
|---|---|
| Soft brass, aluminum | 0.33 |
| Mild steel | 0.40–0.45 |
| Stainless steel | 0.45–0.50 |
| Hard brass, bronze | 0.50 |

### 2.4.2 Bend Allowance (BA) — The Master Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

where:
- $\theta$ = bend angle (degrees)
- $R$ = inside bend radius
- $K$ = K-factor
- $t$ = sheet thickness

### 2.4.3 Minimum Bend Radius

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t \cdot E_{outer}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}} \quad \text{(theoretical, no safety factor)}
$$

**Practical rule** (used in industry and KTU exams):

$$
R_{min} \approx 1 \times t \quad \text{(for soft materials like aluminum)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 2 \times t \quad \text{(for mild steel)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 3 \times t \quad \text{(for stainless steel, hard brass)}
$$

### 2.4.4 Hole-to-Edge and Hole-to-Hole Distances

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum distance from hole center to edge)}
$$
$$
d_{hole} \geq 3 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum pitch between hole centers)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** If the edge distance is too small, the sheet **tears** during piercing. If holes are too close, the **inter-hole web distorts**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Process | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Casting | $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ | Adjacent thicknesses | mm |
| 2 | Casting | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1+t_2}{4}$ | Fillet radius | mm |
| 3 | Casting | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ | Draft angle | degrees |
| 4 | Forging | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ | External fillet | mm |
| 5 | Forging | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Internal fillet | mm |
| 6 | Forging | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ | Web thickness | mm |
| 7 | Welding | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | Fillet throat | mm |
| 8 | Welding | $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$ | Weld load | N |
| 9 | Welding | $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ | Min leg size | mm |
| 10 | Sheet Metal | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \theta (R + K t)$ | Bend allowance | mm |
| 11 | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = n \cdot t$ | Min bend radius | mm |
| 12 | Sheet Metal | $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ | Edge distance | mm |
| 13 | Sheet Metal | $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$ | Hole pitch | mm |
| 14 | Casting | $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ | Rib design | mm |
| 15 | Forging | $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$ | Rib height | mm |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These recommendations are not academic luxuries — they save **billions of dollars** annually:

- **Automotive industry**: An engine block with improper rib ratios can crack within weeks. A bracket with wrong fillet can fail in fatigue.
- **Aerospace**: Wing ribs in forged aluminum must follow $r \geq 0.1 \, h$ to maintain grain flow for fatigue life.
- **Pressure vessels**: Welding of LPG cylinders follows $s = 0.7 t$ to ensure full penetration without burn-through.
- **Consumer electronics**: Sheet metal laptop chassis use $K = 0.45$ for aluminum to predict bend behavior in stamping simulation.
- **Power transmission**: Cast transformer housings use section thickness ratio $\leq 2$ to prevent shrinkage porosity at junction of body and flange.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"How would you improve the manufacturability of this component?"*, the answer should ALWAYS quote at least one **numerical recommendation** with units, plus a one-line justification. Vague answers like "add fillets" score 1/7. "Add fillet radius ≥ (t1+t2)/4 to reduce stress concentration and shrinkage porosity" scores 4/7.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fillet Weld Throat Thickness

**Problem Statement:** A fillet weld of leg size **s = 10 mm** is deposited on a structural bracket. Derive the throat thickness and the load it can carry if the weld length is **L = 200 mm** and allowable shear stress is **$\tau_{allow}$ = 110 MPa**.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

A fillet weld has a triangular cross-section with two equal legs of length $s$ meeting at a right angle. The **throat** is the shortest distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (the weld face).

### Step 2: Apply Trigonometry

The throat $t$ is the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse. The hypotenuse length is:

$$
h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

For a right-angled isosceles triangle, the perpendicular from the right angle to the hypotenuse equals:

$$
t = \dfrac{s \cdot s}{h} = \dfrac{s^2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Rationalize the Denominator

$$
t = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, s
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Load Capacity

The weld is in shear along the throat plane of area $A = t \times L$:

$$
A = 7.07 \times 200 = 1414 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 110 \times 1414
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 155.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Step 6: Verification

Check using the master formula directly:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow} = 0.707 \times 10 \times 200 \times 110
$$
$$
P = 0.707 \times 220{,}000 = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** This derivation shows that the **effective load-bearing area** of a fillet weld is only **70.7 %** of the apparent cross-section. This is why fillet welds are weaker than butt welds of the same plate thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bend Allowance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is bent through an angle of **90°** with an inside bend radius of **R = 4 mm**. The K-factor for mild steel is **0.45**. Calculate the bend allowance.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Bend Allowance Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (4 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Inside the Bracket

$$
R + K \cdot t = 4 + 0.45 \times 2 = 4 + 0.9 = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Angle Factor

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708
$$

### Step 6: Compute Final Result

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times 4.9 = 7.697 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{BA \approx 7.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation

If a designer wants a final **flat length of 100 mm** for the bent sheet (along the outer dimensions), the **flat blank length** required is:

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA
$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the straight portions before and after the bend.

**Example:** If each straight leg is 50 mm:

$$
L_{blank} = 50 + 50 + 7.70 = 107.70 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the **inside radius R** with the **neutral radius**. The neutral radius is $R + Kt$, NOT just $R$. Failing to add $Kt$ leads to an undersized blank and a part that is too short after bending.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Bend Radius for a Sheet

**Problem Statement:** A 1.5 mm thick aluminum sheet (UTS = 110 MPa) is to be bent at room temperature. The modulus of elasticity in tension on the outer fiber can be approximated by the strain-hardening modulus. Calculate the **minimum bend radius** to avoid surface cracking, using the rule of thumb $R_{min} \approx n \cdot t$ and verify with the theoretical formula.

### Step 1: Theoretical Strain Analysis

The maximum strain on the outer surface of a bent sheet is:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R + t} \approx \dfrac{t}{2R} \quad (\text{when } R \gg t)
$$

For aluminum, the **limiting strain** before cracking is approximately:

$$
\varepsilon_{limit} \approx 0.4 \quad (\text{for soft aluminum})
$$

### Step 2: Theoretical Formula

Setting $\varepsilon_{max} = \varepsilon_{limit}$:

$$
\dfrac{t}{2R_{min}} = \varepsilon_{limit}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{limit}} = \dfrac{1.5}{2 \times 0.4} = 1.875 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Rule of Thumb

For soft aluminum, $R_{min} \approx 1 \times t$:

$$
R_{min} = 1 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Safety Check

The practical rule gives a smaller radius (1.5 mm) than the theoretical (1.875 mm). For **safety**, we choose the larger value:

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = 1.875 \text{ mm} \approx 2 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 5: Design Decision

In practice, the designer would specify **R = 2 mm or higher** to ensure no surface cracking during bending. This is well within the "**R = t**" rule but provides a safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The relationship $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ is a favorite board question. The student is expected to (a) state the formula, (b) apply it with the limiting strain, and (c) interpret the result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Recommendation Mapping

| Defect Observed | Process | Design Recommendation That Prevents It |
|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage porosity at junction | Casting | Use **fillet radius** $r \geq (t_1+t_2)/4$; reduce section thickness ratio to $\leq 2$ |
| Hot tear at corner | Casting | Add **fillet and chill**; uniform cooling |
| Pattern damage on withdrawal | Casting | Provide **draft angle** $1°$ to $3°$ |
| Fold / lap in rib | Forging | Maintain $t_{web} \geq 0.15W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 t_{rib}$ |
| Die filling incomplete | Forging | Increase fillet radii; provide proper draft $5°$ to $7°$ |
| Cracking at weld root | Welding | Use **V-groove** for plates > 3 mm; full penetration |
| Distortion in welded plate | Welding | Use **symmetric joint**; intermittent welds; balanced sequence |
| Tearing at bend | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = t$ to $3t$ depending on material |
| Springback error | Sheet Metal | Overbend by $1°$ to $3°$; use lower K-factor |
| Edge tearing during piercing | Sheet Metal | Maintain $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Comparison: With vs Without DFMA

Consider a cast iron bracket with wall thickness 20 mm meeting a flange of 30 mm.

| Parameter | Without DFMA | With DFMA |
|---|---|---|
| Section ratio $t_2/t_1$ | 30/20 = 1.5 (acceptable) | — |
| Fillet radius | 0 (sharp corner) | $r \geq (20+30)/4 = 12.5$ mm |
| Rib thickness | Full 20 mm (hot spot) | $0.7 \times 20 = 14$ mm |
| Draft angle | $0°$ (pattern stuck) | $2°$ external, $3°$ internal |
| Expected defect | Shrinkage cavity, pattern damage | Defect-free casting |
| Cost impact | Rejection + rework | First-time-right |

This matrix is the **standard structure** for KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Framework of Module 4

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Module 4: Design Recommendations] --> B[Pillar 1: Design for Casting]
    A --> C[Pillar 2: Design for Forging]
    A --> D[Pillar 3: Design for Welding]
    A --> E[Pillar 4: Design for Sheet Metal]
    
    B --> B1[Wall Thickness Uniformity]
    B --> B2[Fillet Radii]
    B --> B3[Draft Angles]
    B --> B4[Rib and Boss Design]
    
    C --> C1[Draft Angle 5 to 7 deg]
    C --> C2[Fillet Radii]
    C --> C3[Web and Rib Ratios]
    C --> C4[Parting Line Position]
    
    D --> D1[Joint Selection]
    D --> D2[Fillet Weld Geometry]
    D --> D3[Edge Preparation]
    D --> D4[Distortion Control]
    
    E --> E1[K-Factor and Neutral Axis]
    E --> E2[Minimum Bend Radius]
    E --> E3[Bend Allowance]
    E --> E4[Edge and Hole Distances]
    
    B2 --> X1[Reduces Stress Concentration]
    B2 --> X2[Reduces Shrinkage Porosity]
    C2 --> X3[Ensures Material Flow]
    C2 --> X4[Prevents Folds and Laps]
    D2 --> X5[Ensures Full Strength]
    D4 --> X6[Maintains Dimensional Accuracy]
    E2 --> X7[Prevents Surface Tearing]
    E3 --> X8[Predicts Blank Length]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Decision Tree for Process Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Component to be Manufactured] --> Q1{Strength Requirement?}
    Q1 -->|High, with grain flow| Forging[Choose Forging]
    Q1 -->|Complex shape, moderate strength| Q2{Production Volume?}
    Q1 -->|Sheet form, thin-walled| SheetMetal[Choose Sheet Metal]
    Q1 -->|Permanent joint needed| Welding[Choose Welding]
    
    Q2 -->|Low to medium| Casting[Choose Casting]
    Q2 -->|Very high| Q3{Material is plastic?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| InjectionMolding[Choose Injection Molding]
    Q3 -->|No| Casting
    
    Forging --> F1[Apply DFF Rules: radii, draft, ribs]
    Casting --> C1[Apply DFC Rules: uniform walls, fillets, draft]
    SheetMetal --> S1[Apply DFSM Rules: K-factor, R-min, edge distance]
    Welding --> W1[Apply DFW Rules: joint type, leg size, sequence]
    
    F1 --> Out[Manufacturable Part]
    C1 --> Out
    S1 --> Out
    W1 --> Out
    InjectionMolding --> Out
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Cross-Section of Fillet Weld with Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Plate 1] --- B[Weld Root]
    B --- C[Throat Thickness t = 0.707 s]
    C --- D[Weld Face Hypotenuse]
    D --- E[Plate 2]
    
    B --- F[Leg Size s]
    F --- D
    
    style C fill:#ffe4b5
    style F fill:#e0ffff
    style B fill:#ffb6c1
```

> **Note:** The above is a topological representation. In the actual physical cross-section, the weld metal forms an isosceles right triangle with legs `s` and hypotenuse forming the weld face. The throat is the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Flowchart for Bend Allowance Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Material, t, R, angle] --> B[Look up K-factor table]
    B --> C[Compute neutral radius RN = R + Kt]
    C --> D[Apply BA = pi by 180 times theta times RN]
    D --> E[Output: Bend Allowance]
    E --> F[Add to straight leg lengths]
    F --> G[Final Blank Length]
    
    style B fill:#fffacd
    style D fill:#e6e6fa
    style G fill:#90ee90
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Function Flow: Design Recommendation Implementation

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT[Design Phase]
        A1[Concept Sketch] --> A2[Material Selection]
        A2 --> A3[Process Selection]
    end
    
    subgraph APPLY[Apply Recommendations]
        A3 --> B1[Apply DFC if Casting]
        A3 --> B2[Apply DFF if Forging]
        A3 --> B3[Apply DFW if Welding]
        A3 --> B4[Apply DFSM if Sheet Metal]
    end
    
    subgraph VERIFY[Verification]
        B1 --> C1[Check wall ratios]
        B2 --> C2[Check fillet radii]
        B3 --> C3[Check weld throat]
        B4 --> C4[Check bend allowance]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Outcome]
        C1 --> D[Manufacturable Design]
        C2 --> D
        C3 --> D
        C4 --> D
    end
    
    style A3 fill:#ffebcd
    style D fill:#98fb98
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Explain the importance of draft angle in sand casting. State typical draft values for external and internal surfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Draft angle is the **taper provided on the vertical walls of a pattern** to enable its easy withdrawal from the sand mold without damaging the mold cavity.

- **External surfaces**: $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (pattern lifts easily)
- **Internal surfaces**: $\alpha = 2°$ to $5°$ (cores require more draft for removal)
- **Minimum draft for intricate patterns**: Up to $5°$

**Significance:** Without draft, the pattern rubs against sand walls, causing **mold erosion, dimensional inaccuracy, and surface defects**. Draft also accounts for **mold wall expansion** during pouring.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define K-factor. State the typical range of K-factor for mild steel and aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance of the **neutral axis** from the inner surface of the sheet to the **material thickness**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

- **Aluminum, soft brass**: $K = 0.33$
- **Mild steel**: $K = 0.40$ to $0.45$
- **Stainless steel, hard brass**: $K = 0.45$ to $0.50$

**Significance:** K-factor is essential for calculating **bend allowance** and predicting **flat blank length** before bending. It represents the location within the sheet thickness where neither stretching nor compression occurs during bending.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Casting + Forging Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A cast iron bracket has a uniform wall thickness of 25 mm. A boss of 40 mm diameter is to be added at one location for a bearing housing. As a design engineer, suggest modifications to:
1. Avoid shrinkage porosity at the boss-wall junction
2. Ensure easy pattern withdrawal
3. Maintain adequate strength

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Section thickness ratio check:**
The boss (40 mm) is essentially a thick cylinder attached to a 25 mm wall. Without modification, the ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6
$$

This is borderline. To make it safe, **reduce boss diameter** to 32–35 mm or add a **transition ring**.

**Step 2 — Add fillet radius:**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4} = \dfrac{25 + 40}{4} = 16.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r = 16$ mm at the boss-wall junction. This **reduces stress concentration** and **eliminates hot spot** at the junction, preventing shrinkage porosity.

**Step 3 — Add draft angle:**
For external boss surface: $\alpha = 2°$ (external)
For internal bore: $\alpha = 3°$ (internal)

**Step 4 — Boss thickness control:**
Boss wall thickness = $1.5 \times 25 = 37.5$ mm. Since boss OD is 40 mm, this means inner bore = 40 - 2(37.5) = negative. **Modify boss design**: Use a boss OD of 60 mm so that wall thickness = 10 mm (the inner bore of bearing fits) and $t_{boss} = 1.5 \times t_{wall} = 37.5$ mm is met by adjusting boss height/diameter.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Modification | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Fillet radius at junction | 16 mm | Reduce stress + hot spot |
| Section ratio | $\leq 2$ (achieved by reducing boss) | Avoid shrinkage |
| External draft | $2°$ | Pattern removal |
| Internal draft | $3°$ | Core removal |
| Rib at junction (if needed) | 17.5 mm thick | Strengthen without hot spot |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
Explain the key design recommendations for **drop forging** of a connecting rod-shaped component with maximum width $W = 60$ mm and depth $h = 25$ mm. Recommend suitable values for fillet radii, web thickness, and draft angle.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draft angle:**
For impression die forging: $\alpha = 5°$ to $7°$. Adopt $\alpha = 7°$ for safety.

**Step 2 — External fillet radius:**

$$
r_e \geq 0.1 \, h = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_e = 3$ mm.

**Step 3 — Internal fillet radius:**

$$
r_i \geq 0.15 \, h = 0.15 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_i = 4$ mm.

**Step 4 — Web thickness:**

$$
t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W = 0.15 \times 60 = 9 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{web} = 10$ mm.

**Step 5 — Rib thickness:**

$$
t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{rib} = 6$ mm.

**Step 6 — Maximum rib height:**

$$
h_{rib,max} = 5 \times t_{rib} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Parting line:**
Choose a **straight plane** through the **widest cross-section** ($W = 60$ mm plane) for simple die construction.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | $7°$ | Die separation |
| External fillet | $3$ mm | Material flow into corner |
| Internal fillet | $4$ mm | Die stress relief |
| Web thickness | $10$ mm | Avoid folds |
| Rib thickness | $6$ mm | Full die fill |
| Max rib height | $30$ mm | Avoid fold/lap |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding + Sheet Metal Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A fillet weld of leg size $s = 12$ mm is to be deposited along a joint of length $L = 250$ mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is $\tau_{allow} = 100$ MPa. Determine:
1. The throat thickness
2. The load-carrying capacity of the weld
3. The minimum recommended leg size if the thinner plate is 14 mm thick

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t = 0.707 \, s = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.484 \text{ mm} \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective shear area:**

$$
A = t \times L = 8.484 \times 250 = 2121 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Load capacity:**

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 100 \times 2121 = 212{,}100 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 212.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum recommended leg size:**

$$
s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min} = 0.7 \times 14 = 9.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the actual leg size $s = 12$ mm $> s_{min} = 9.8$ mm, the design is **acceptable**.

**Step 5 — Maximum recommended leg size check:**

For plate thickness 14 mm, the maximum leg size for full strength is $s_{max} \approx t_{plate} = 14$ mm. Since $s = 12$ mm $< 14$ mm, the weld is **within safe limits**.

**Step 6 — Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Throat thickness | 8.5 mm | ✓ |
| Weld area | 2121 mm² | ✓ |
| Load capacity | 212.1 kN | ✓ |
| Min leg required | 9.8 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |
| Max leg allowed | 14 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is to be bent at $90°$ with an inside bend radius of $R = 5$ mm. Calculate:
1. The bend allowance (use $K = 0.45$)
2. The flat blank length if each straight leg is 60 mm
3. The minimum recommended edge distance for a hole pierced 4 mm from the bend

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (5 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times (5 + 0.9) = 1.5708 \times 5.9
$$

$$
\boxed{BA = 9.268 \text{ mm} \approx 9.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Flat blank length:**

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA = 60 + 60 + 9.27
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{blank} = 129.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum edge distance for hole:**

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R = 1.5 \times 2 + 5 = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d_{edge} = 8$ mm from bend line to hole center.

**Step 5 — Verification of bend radius:**

For mild steel, $R_{min} \approx 2t = 2 \times 2 = 4$ mm.
Since $R = 5$ mm $> R_{min} = 4$ mm, the bend is **safe**.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Bend allowance | 9.27 mm | — |
| Blank length | 129.27 mm | — |
| Min edge distance | 8 mm | ✓ |
| Min bend radius | 4 mm (reqd) | ✓ (5 mm used) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Design Recommendations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-factor and Neutral Axis position.** K-factor is dimensionless; neutral axis position = $K \times t$ (in mm). Students writing $K = 0.45$ mm lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.707 in fillet weld throat.** Writing $t = s$ instead of $t = 0.707s$ loses 2 marks. Always show the derivation.
>
> 3. **Using inside radius $R$ instead of neutral radius $(R + Kt)$ in bend allowance.** This is the #1 error in sheet metal problems. Always write $(R + Kt)$ explicitly.
>
> 4. **Not specifying units in design recommendations.** "Add fillet radius of 5" is incomplete. "Add fillet radius of **5 mm** to reduce stress concentration" scores full marks.
>
> 5. **Giving vague justifications.** "It will be good for manufacturing" scores 0. "It eliminates hot spot and prevents shrinkage porosity" scores 2/2.
>
> 6. **Mixing up draft angles for casting vs forging.** Casting: $1°$ to $5°$. Forging: $5°$ to $7°$. Many students write $1°$ for forging, which is wrong.
>
> 7. **Ignoring minimum weld leg size formula.** $s_{min} = 0.7 t_{min}$ is mandatory to mention whenever weld design is asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Design Recommendations"**

- [x] **Casting — Section Ratio**: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ (always enforce uniform cooling)
- [x] **Casting — Fillet**: $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ (no sharp internal corners)
- [x] **Casting — Draft**: $1°$ to $3°$ external, $2°$ to $5°$ internal
- [x] **Casting — Rib**: $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ (avoid hot spots)
- [x] **Casting — Boss**: $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ (strength + machining allowance)
- [x] **Forging — Draft**: $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die)
- [x] **Forging — External Fillet**: $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Internal Fillet**: $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Web**: $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$
- [x] **Forging — Rib**: $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$
- [x] **Forging — Parting Line**: straight, through widest cross-section
- [x] **Welding — Throat**: $t = 0.707 \, s$ (single most important formula)
- [x] **Welding — Load**: $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$
- [x] **Welding — Min Leg**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$
- [x] **Welding — Avoid**: weld intersections and high-stress zones
- [x] **Sheet Metal — K-Factor**: Al = 0.33, MS = 0.45, SS = 0.50
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Bend Allowance**: $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \, \theta \, (R + Kt)$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Min Bend Radius**: $R_{min} = 1t$ to $3t$ (material dependent)
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Edge Distance**: $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Hole Pitch**: $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize

| Value | Meaning | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0.707** | Fillet weld throat factor | $t = 0.707s$ |
| **0.7** | Weld min leg factor | $s_{min} = 0.7t$ |
| **0.1** | Forging external fillet factor | $r_e = 0.1h$ |
| **0.15** | Forging internal fillet / web factor | $r_i = 0.15h$, $t_{web} = 0.15W$ |
| **2** | Max section ratio (casting) | $t_2/t_1 \leq 2$ |
| **¼** | Casting fillet factor | $r = (t_1+t_2)/4$ |
| **0.7** | Casting rib factor | $t_{rib} = 0.7t_{wall}$ |
| **1.5** | Casting boss factor | $t_{boss} = 1.5t_{wall}$ |
| **5** | Forging max rib height factor | $h_{rib} \leq 5t_{rib}$ |

### One-Line Definition Recall (for 2-mark definitions)

- **DFC** = Design for Casting — guidelines for uniform walls, fillets, draft, ribs
- **DFF** = Design for Forging — guidelines for material flow, die fill, draft, radii
- **DFW** = Design for Welding — guidelines for joint type, weld size, distortion control
- **DFSM** = Design for Sheet Metal — guidelines for bending, K-factor, blank size

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Process: Different Types of Welding Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding Process: Different Types of Welding Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Welding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET522 - Module 4)**
> **Welding** is a localized **permanent joining manufacturing process** in which two or more pieces of material (typically metals or thermoplastics) are coalesced (fused) together by applying **heat, pressure, or both**, with or without the addition of a filler material, to produce a metallurgical bond across the joint interface. The bond strength approaches that of the base material (parent metal) when executed with proper procedure specification (WPS) and qualified welders.

**Standards Reference:** Governed by **AWS (American Welding Society)**, **ISO 4063**, and **IS 814** for procedure qualification in KTU board examination problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chocolate Bar Bonding" Intuition

Imagine you have **two broken chocolate bars** and you want to rejoin them perfectly:

| Real-World Action | Welding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Warming chocolate with a hair dryer | Heat input (arc, flame, resistance) |
| Pressing the melted surfaces together | Application of forge pressure |
| Letting it cool to solidify | Solidification of weld pool |
| The chocolate reforms as one piece | Metallurgical fusion bond |

Just as chocolate *softens, flows, and resolidifies* without any "glue," welding **melts and re-solidifies the base material** (sometimes with extra filler metal like a "chocolate patch") to form a **continuous grain structure** across the joint. This is fundamentally different from **mechanical fastening** (bolts, rivets) or **adhesive bonding** (glue), where a third substance holds two separate pieces together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A common confusion is between *brazing*, *soldering*, and *welding*. Remember:
> - **Welding** → Melts the **base metal** (fusion temperature of parent material).
> - **Brazing** → Melts only the **filler** (above 450 °C, base metal does NOT melt).
> - **Soldering** → Melts only the **filler** (below 450 °C).

---

## 1.3 Why Welding is Critical in DFMA (Design for Manufacture and Assembly)

In the context of **PEMET522**, welding is not just a joining method — it is a **design decision**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to choose welding based on:

1. **Material compatibility** (steel vs. aluminum vs. stainless).
2. **Production volume** (one-off prototype vs. mass production).
3. **Joint accessibility** (can a torch/reach the joint?).
4. **Distortion tolerance** (thin sheets vs. thick plates).
5. **Post-processing needs** (grinding, painting, NDT inspection).

> [!TIP]
> The rule of thumb in **DFMA**: Welding is preferred when the part is **non-removable**, requires **high strength-to-weight ratio**, and has **simple joint geometry** (butt, lap, fillet). For parts that must be serviced/replaced, mechanical fasteners are preferred.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Welding Processes (ISO 4063)

```
WELDING PROCESSES
├── 1. ARC WELDING (Fusion)
│   ├── Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW / Stick)
│   ├── Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG)
│   ├── Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)
│   ├── Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)
│   ├── Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)
│   └── Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)
│
├── 2. GAS WELDING (Oxy-fuel)
│   ├── Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)
│   ├── Oxy-Hydrogen Welding
│   └── Air-Acetylene Welding
│
├── 3. RESISTANCE WELDING (Solid-State / Fusion)
│   ├── Spot Welding
│   ├── Seam Welding
│   ├── Projection Welding
│   ├── Butt (Upset) Welding
│   └── Flash Welding
│
├── 4. SOLID-STATE WELDING (No melting of base metal)
│   ├── Friction Welding (FW / FSW)
│   ├── Ultrasonic Welding (USW)
│   ├── Diffusion Welding
│   ├── Explosive Welding
│   ├── Cold Welding
│   └── Forge Welding
│
├── 5. HIGH-ENERGY BEAM WELDING
│   ├── Laser Beam Welding (LBW)
│   └── Electron Beam Welding (EBW)
│
└── 6. OTHER PROCESSES
    ├── Thermit Welding (Aluminothermic)
    ├── Electroslag Welding (ESW)
    ├── Electrogas Welding (EGW)
    └── Stud Welding
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — The "Three-Pillar" Model

Every welding process, regardless of type, can be broken down into **three fundamental pillars**:

1. **Heat Source** — How energy is delivered to the joint (electrical arc, flame, friction, beam).
2. **Shielding Mechanism** — How the molten weld pool is protected from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂) which cause porosity and embrittlement. Shielding can be:
   - **Flux coating** (e.g., SMAW electrode coating).
   - **Inert gas** (Argon, Helium in GTAW/GMAW).
   - **Slag/granular flux** (SAW).
   - **Vacuum** (EBW).
   - **Mechanical displacement** (solid-state processes).
3. **Filler Material Addition** — Optional. Some processes (autogenous GTAW, FSW) join without filler; others (SMAW, GMAW) require a consumable electrode/wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** The **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the region of base metal that has not melted but whose microstructure and mechanical properties were altered by the heat of welding. The **Weld Zone (WZ)** is the fully fused region. DFMA designers must consider both when specifying tolerances and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most frequently asked** in KTU PEMET522 University Exam questions on welding.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (Arc Welding)** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Controls HAZ size & distortion |
| 2 | **Heat Input (Resistance)** | $Q = I^{2} R t$ | Joules (J) | Resistance spot/seam welding |
| 3 | **Welding Speed (Travel)** | $S = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | mm/min or mm/s | Process time estimation |
| 4 | **Power (Arc)** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) or kW | Power supply sizing |
| 5 | **Weld Metal Deposition Rate** | $D_{R} = \dfrac{W_{wire}}{t} \cdot \eta$ | kg/hr | Productivity |
| 6 | **Weld Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{melt}}{Q_{input}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Process comparison |
| 7 | **Weld Dilution** | $D\% = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | % | Filler-base mix ratio |
| 8 | **Fusion Zone Area** | $A_{f} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot w \cdot p$ | mm² | For fillet welds |
| 9 | **Thermal Efficiency Factor** | $k = \dfrac{\text{Heat to workpiece}}{\text{Total heat generated}}$ | Decimal | Process characteristic |
| 10 | **Cooling Rate (t₈/₅)** | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{q / v}{d}$ | s | HAZ microstructure control |

> **Variable Legend:** $V$ = Arc Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $S$ = Travel speed (mm/min), $R$ = Electrical resistance (Ω), $t$ = Time (s), $L$ = Weld length, $w$ = Leg length, $p$ = Penetration depth, $q$ = Heat input, $d$ = Plate thickness.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **Heat Input (H)** is the **single most important design parameter**. KTU board problems often ask: *"Given V = 25 V, I = 200 A, travel speed = 300 mm/min, find heat input per unit length."*
- **Weld Dilution** matters in **dissimilar metal joining** (e.g., welding stainless steel to mild steel) — DFMA designer must specify filler chemistry to control dilution % and prevent brittle intermetallics.
- **Cooling Rate (t₈/₅)** determines whether the HAZ forms **martensite** (brittle, fast cooling) or **bainite/ferrite** (tough, slow cooling). Critical for **high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels**.

---

## 2.4 Process Selection Matrix (DFMA Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **must-memorize table** for KTU board exam. It allows students to **justify process selection** in 14-mark design questions.

| Process | Base Material | Thickness Range | Position | Production Volume | Distortion | Skill Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** | Carbon/Low-alloy steel | 3 – 25 mm | All | Low–Med | Medium | High |
| **GMAW (MIG)** | Steel, Al, SS | 0.8 – 25 mm | All | High | Low | Medium |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | SS, Al, Ti, Mg | 0.5 – 6 mm | All | Low | Very Low | Very High |
| **FCAW** | Carbon steel | 2 – 30 mm | All | High | Medium | Medium |
| **SAW** | Thick plates | 6 – 75 mm | Flat/Horiz. | Very High | Low | Medium |
| **PAW** | SS, Ti, refractory | 0.1 – 10 mm | All | Med–High | Very Low | High |
| **OAW** | Thin steel, Al, Cu | 0.5 – 6 mm | All | Low | High | High |
| **Spot RW** | Steel sheets | 0.5 – 3 mm | Restricted | Very High | Negligible | Low |
| **Seam RW** | Steel sheets | 0.5 – 3 mm | Flat/cyl. | Very High | Negligible | Low |
| **FSW** | Al, Mg, Cu | 3 – 50 mm | All | High | Very Low | Medium |
| **LBW** | All metals, plastics | 0.1 – 25 mm | All | Med–High | Very Low | High |
| **EBW** | Reactive metals (Ti, Zr) | 1 – 100 mm | Vacuum | Low | Minimal | Very High |
| **Stud Welding** | Steel studs | 3 – 25 mm Ø | All | High | Low | Medium |

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Solid-State Welding

Solid-state processes are increasingly demanded in KTU 2024 DFMA context because:

1. **No melting → No solidification defects** (no porosity, no cracks from shrinkage).
2. **No HAZ → Base metal properties preserved**.
3. **Lower distortion** (critical for aerospace DFMA).
4. **Join dissimilar metals** (e.g., Al-Cu, which is impossible with fusion welding due to brittle intermetallics).
5. **Energy efficient** (Friction Stir Welding uses ~2.5% of the energy of arc welding for the same joint).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in Arc Welding

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Definition

Heat input $H$ is the **energy delivered per unit length of weld** by the welding arc. It determines the **HAZ width**, **distortion magnitude**, and **mechanical properties** of the joint.

### 3.1.2 Dimensional Logic

- Power supplied by arc = $P = V \cdot I$ (W = J/s).
- For a weld of length $L$ welded in time $t$, energy supplied = $P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$.
- Travel speed $S = L / t$, therefore $t = L / S$.
- Total energy over length $L$: $E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S}$.
- **Heat input per unit length** = $E_{total} / L = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$.
- But this is in **J/mm only if** $S$ is in mm/s. Since KTU commonly gives $S$ in **mm/min**, we multiply by 60 (s/min) and convert to **kJ/mm** by dividing by 1000.

### 3.1.3 Final Derivation Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Power delivered by arc} \quad P &= V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{J/s}] \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Energy for length } L \quad E &= P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{Joules}] \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Travel time } t &= \dfrac{L}{S} \quad \text{where } S \text{ is in mm/min} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Substituting} \quad E &= V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Heat input per unit length} \quad H &= \dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \quad [\text{J/mm}] \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6: Convert to kJ/mm (KTU standard)} \quad H &= \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Example (Worked for KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A GMAW operation uses $V = 28$ V, $I = 250$ A, with travel speed $S = 400$ mm/min. Find the heat input per unit length.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{28 \times 250 \times 60}{400 \times 1000} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{420{,}000}{400{,}000} \\[6pt]
  &= 1.05 \; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $1.05$ kJ/mm is within the typical range for GMAW of mild steel (0.5 – 2.0 kJ/mm). ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Generated in Resistance Spot Welding

### 3.2.1 Foundation Equation

In resistance welding, heat is generated by **Joule heating** as current flows through the metal sheets held between two electrodes.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Ohm's Law} \quad V &= I \cdot R \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Power dissipated} \quad P &= V \cdot I = I^{2} R \quad [\text{W}] \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Energy generated over time } t \quad Q &= P \cdot t = I^{2} R t \quad [\text{J}] \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Substitute units} \quad Q &= I^{2} R t \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4a: For current in kA and time in cycles (60 Hz)} \quad Q &= k \cdot I^{2} R t \quad \text{where } k = \text{thermal efficiency}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

**Problem:** A spot welder uses $I = 8000$ A, $R = 200$ µΩ, for $t = 0.2$ s. Assuming $k = 0.85$, find heat generated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 200 \times 10^{-6} \; \Omega = 2 \times 10^{-4} \; \Omega \\[4pt]
Q &= k \cdot I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t \\[4pt]
  &= 0.85 \times (8000)^{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.2 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.85 \times 64 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.85 \times 2560 \\[4pt]
  &= 2176 \; \text{J} \approx 2.18 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For spot welding, the nugget diameter $d \approx 4\sqrt{t}$, where $t$ is sheet thickness. This is a frequently tested **empirical relation** in KTU papers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Selection Logic

| Selection Criterion | Best Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowest distortion on thin sheet** | **LBW / EBW** | Very narrow HAZ, high energy density |
| **Cheapest for field repair** | **SMAW** | Portable, no shielding gas required |
| **Welding of thick plates (shipbuilding)** | **SAW** | High deposition, no spatter, deep penetration |
| **Aerospace Al-alloy fuselage** | **FSW** | Solid-state, no defects, repeatable |
| **Auto body sheet steel mass production** | **Spot / Seam RW** | Fast (60–180 spots/min), automation friendly |
| **Reactive metal (Ti, Zr)** | **EBW in vacuum** | Prevents atmospheric contamination |
| **Joining Al to Cu (electrical busbar)** | **Friction / Cold Weld** | Avoids brittle intermetallics |

---

## 3.4 Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Width — Empirical Estimate

A KTU-asked concept is HAZ width prediction:

$$
\text{HAZ width} \approx 0.8 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H \cdot d}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c}}
$$

Where $d$ = plate thickness, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat. Students are **not expected to memorize** this, but **recognize the proportionality**:

$$
\text{HAZ width} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{k}}
$$

Higher heat input → wider HAZ. Higher thermal conductivity → narrower HAZ (heat dissipates faster).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
