<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Q1.** Distinguish clearly between **fusion welding** and **solid-state welding**. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Base metal state | Melts to form pool | Does NOT melt; stays below melting point |
| Bond mechanism | Solidification of molten pool | Diffusion / plastic deformation |
| Shielding required | Yes (gas/flux/vacuum) | Not always |
| HAZ | Present (significant) | Minimal or absent |
| Distortion | Higher | Much lower |
| Examples | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, OAW | Friction Welding (FW), Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic, Diffusion, Cold, Forge |

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
>
> **Q2.** What is **heat input** in arc welding? State its **significance** and the formula used in KTU board standards.

**Model Answer:**
Heat input $H$ is the energy supplied by the arc per unit length of weld, expressed in **kJ/mm** (KTU convention). It controls the **HAZ size, weld metal grain structure, and distortion**.

$$
H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min). Excessive heat input causes **coarse grains, wider HAZ, and increased distortion**, while too low input causes **lack of fusion and porosity**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Fusion Welding Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle and working of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)**. List its **four advantages** and **two limitations** compared to SMAW.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel plate welding uses GMAW with the following parameters: $V = 30$ V, $I = 280$ A, $S = 350$ mm/min, electrode diameter = 1.2 mm, wire feed rate = 8 m/min, density of wire = 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate:
1. **Heat input per unit length (kJ/mm)**
2. **Deposition rate (kg/hr)** assuming transfer efficiency = 95%.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Principle:** GMAW uses a **continuous solid wire electrode** fed automatically through a welding gun. An **electric arc** is established between the wire (electrode) and the workpiece. The arc melts the wire (which becomes filler) and the base metal, forming a weld pool. A **shielding gas** (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) is supplied through the gun nozzle to protect the pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.3):** Power Supply → Wire Feeder → Welding Gun → Arc → Weld Pool (shielded by gas) → Solidified Weld.

**Advantages of GMAW over SMAW:**
1. Continuous wire → higher productivity (no electrode changing).
2. Cleaner weld (no slag to chip off).
3. Better weld bead appearance and consistency.
4. Suitable for all positions and easily automated.

**Limitations of GMAW vs. SMAW:**
1. Requires external shielding gas → cannot be used in strong wind/outdoor without shelter.
2. More complex equipment and higher initial cost.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Part 1: Heat Input**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{30 \times 280 \times 60}{350 \times 1000} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{504{,}000}{350{,}000} \\[6pt]
  &= 1.44 \; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2: Deposition Rate**

Cross-section area of wire: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.2)^{2} = 1.131 \; \text{mm}^{2}$

Wire feed rate = 8 m/min = 8000 mm/min.

Volume feed rate: $V_{feed} = A \times \text{length/min} = 1.131 \times 8000 = 9048 \; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}$

Mass feed rate: $\dot{m} = V_{feed} \times \rho = 9048 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{cm}^{3}/\text{min} \times 7.85 \; \text{g/cm}^{3}$

$$
\dot{m} = 9048 \times 7.85 / 1000 \;\text{g/min} = 71.03 \; \text{g/min}
$$

Convert to kg/hr:

$$
\dot{m} = 71.03 \times 60 / 1000 = 4.26 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

Apply efficiency $\eta = 0.95$:

$$
\boxed{D_{R} = 4.26 \times 0.95 = 4.05 \; \text{kg/hr}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Solid-State Welding Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** with a diagram. Compare it with **conventional fusion welding** under the headings: (i) heat-affected zone, (ii) distortion, (iii) joining of dissimilar metals, (iv) energy consumption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A resistance spot welding operation joins two mild steel sheets, each **1.5 mm thick**, using a current of **9000 A** for **0.25 seconds**. The contact resistance is **180 µΩ** and thermal efficiency is **0.85**. Calculate:
1. **Total heat generated (Joules)**
2. **Nugget diameter (mm)** using the empirical relation $d_{n} = 4\sqrt{t}$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Principle of FSW:**
FSW is a **solid-state joining process** where a **non-consumable rotating tool** (with a shoulder and a pin) is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates. The **frictional heat** generated softens (but does not melt) the material, and the **rotational + translational motion** of the pin causes **plastic deformation** and **dynamic recrystallization**, which forges the two pieces into a single joint as the tool traverses along the seam. The shoulder provides additional forging pressure and confines the plasticized material.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | FSW | Fusion Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) HAZ** | Very small / negligible | Significant HAZ with grain coarsening |
| **(ii) Distortion** | Minimal | High (shrinkage, warping) |
| **(iii) Dissimilar metals** | Excellent (Al-Cu, Al-Mg) | Difficult (intermetallics) |
| **(iv) Energy consumption** | Very low (~2.5% of arc) | High |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Part 1: Heat Generated**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= k \cdot I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t \\[6pt]
  &= 0.85 \times (9000)^{2} \times (180 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25 \\[6pt]
  &= 0.85 \times 81 \times 10^{6} \times 180 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.25 \\[6pt]
  &= 0.85 \times 81 \times 180 \times 0.25 \\[6pt]
  &= 0.85 \times 3645 \\[6pt]
  &= 3098.25 \; \text{J} \approx 3.10 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2: Nugget Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{n} &= 4\sqrt{t} \quad \text{where } t = 1.5 \; \text{mm (sheet thickness)} \\[6pt]
      &= 4 \sqrt{1.5} \\[6pt]
      &= 4 \times 1.225 \\[6pt]
      &= 4.90 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit error:** Reporting heat input in **J/mm** instead of **kJ/mm** (board-mandated unit) costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency (k)** in resistance welding — full marks only with $k$ applied.
> 3. **Mixing up FSW with FRICTION WELDING (FW):** FSW is linear-traverse; FW is rotary between two parts.
> 4. **Heat Input formula must use S in mm/min** — KTU convention; substituting S in mm/s gives 60× wrong answer.
> 5. **Not labeling the HAZ and Weld Zone** in a cross-section sketch — loses 2 marks in 7-mark parts.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Welding** = permanent joining with/without filler, base metal may or may not melt.
- ✅ **Brazing** ≠ Welding — filler melts, base metal does NOT (T > 450 °C).
- ✅ **Heat Input Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm**.
- ✅ **Resistance Spot Welding:** $Q = k \cdot I^{2} R t$ (Joules).
- ✅ **Nugget Diameter (empirical):** $d_{n} = 4\sqrt{t}$ (mm).
- ✅ **Three Pillars of any welding process:** Heat Source + Shielding + Filler (optional).
- ✅ **Solid-state processes (FSW, FW, USW, Diffusion, Cold):** No melting → no HAZ → no solidification defects.
- ✅ **DFMA Selection Rule:** Low volume + outdoor → **SMAW**; high volume + automation → **GMAW/SAW**; aerospace Al → **FSW**; thin sheet mass production → **Spot RW**.
- ✅ **Weld Dilution %** = $\dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ — critical for dissimilar joints.
- ✅ **HAZ width** ∝ $\sqrt{H/k}$ — high heat input and low thermal conductivity → wide HAZ.
- ✅ **Cooling time t₈/₅** controls HAZ microstructure (martensite vs. bainite).
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** AWS D1.1 (steel), AWS D17.1 (aerospace), ISO 4063 (process codes), IS 814 (Indian electrode spec).
- ✅ **Embrittlement risk** in fusion welding of dissimilar metals — solved by **solid-state** processes.
- ✅ **For 14-mark design answers:** Always end with a **justified process selection table** (production volume, material, thickness, distortion, cost, position).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Board:** **"GAS-ARC-RESIST-SOLID-BEAM"** = the six major welding families in ISO 4063 order. Memorize this for any "Classify welding processes" question worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURE AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Design Recommendations" in DFMA?

In the context of **Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)**, the term **"Design Recommendations"** refers to a structured set of **empirical rules, geometric guidelines, tolerance principles, and material-process alignment strategies** that a designer must follow to ensure a component can be produced economically, accurately, and reliably using a specific manufacturing process. These recommendations form the bridge between the conceptual CAD model and a shop-floor manufacturable part.

Formally, design recommendations are **process-specific heuristics** derived from:
- **Dimensional capability curves** of machines
- **Tool wear and accessibility constraints**
- **Shrinkage, draft, and distortion behaviors**
- **Metallurgical phenomena** (grain flow, porosity, residual stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Design recommendations for **Casting, Forging, Welding, and Sheet Metal Working** form the core of this module. The emphasis is on translating *practical shop-floor constraints* into *upstream design decisions* so that cost of production, cycle time, and rejection rates are minimized **without sacrificing functional performance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

Imagine you are writing a recipe to bake a cake. The recipe says: *"use a round tin, do not fill more than 2/3rd, grease the sides lightly, bake at 180°C"*. You can ignore these rules and bake freely, but you will end up with a sticky, broken, or undercooked cake.

**Design recommendations are EXACTLY these "recipes" for manufacturing processes.**

- A *casting* needs a draft angle, just like a cake needs a greased tin — otherwise the part cannot be extracted.
- A *forging* needs generous fillet radii, just like a cake needs a smooth batter — sharp corners cause cracks.
- A *weld joint* needs proper edge preparation, just like a cake needs leveled batter — to avoid weak zones.
- A *sheet metal bend* needs a minimum bend radius, just like a cake needs proper proportions — to avoid tearing.

The genius of DFMA is that it shifts these "recipe rules" **upstream into the design stage**, so the product is *born manufacturable* rather than being "forced" into manufacture.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Design Recommendations?

| Problem in Industry | Root Cause | Recommendation That Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| Castings crack at sharp corners | Stress concentration + shrinkage restraint | Use generous **fillet radii** (≥ 3 mm) at all internal corners |
| Sheet metal tears at bends | Fiber strain > limiting strain | Maintain bend radius **r ≥ t (sheet thickness)** |
| Welds distort the assembly | Uneven heating/cooling | Use **symmetric joint design** and balanced welding sequence |
| Forgings develop folds/laps | Improper material flow | Provide **draft, radii, and proper web thickness** |
| Machined parts are rejected | Tolerance tighter than machine capability | Design within **process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Design Recommendations are process-specific geometric and tolerance guidelines formulated to ensure that a component is producible with minimum cost, minimum scrap, and consistent quality using the chosen manufacturing route."*

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Module 4

```
Module 4 - Design Recommendations
        │
        ├──► Pillar 1: Design for Casting (DFC)
        ├──► Pillar 2: Design for Forging (DFF)
        ├──► Pillar 3: Design for Welding (DFW)
        └──► Pillar 4: Design for Sheet Metal (DFSM)
```

Each pillar addresses:
1. **Process capability envelope**
2. **Geometric rules** (draft, radii, web/rib ratios)
3. **Tolerance guidance**
4. **Common defects and their design remedies**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Design for Casting (DFC)

Casting involves pouring molten metal into a mold. The designer must account for **fluid flow, solidification shrinkage, and pattern removal**.

### 2.1.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Wall Thickness** | As **uniform** as possible; ideal range **5–25 mm** for sand casting | Uneven walls cause **hot spots** → shrinkage porosity |
| **Section Thickness Ratio** | Adjacent sections: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2:1$ to $1.5:1$ | Prevents thermal gradient → reduces stress |
| **Fillet Radius** | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ at junctions | Avoids stress concentration |
| **Draft Angle** | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (external), $2°$ to $5°$ (internal) | Allows easy pattern withdrawal |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall}$ to $0.8 \, t_{wall}$ | Prevents hot spot at rib base |
| **Boss Thickness** | $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ | Adequate strength for fastening |

### 2.1.2 The "I-Section" Rule

For a flat plate requiring rigidity, instead of increasing thickness, use a **Tee or I-section**:

$$
t_{rib} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \times t_{wall}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks *"Suggest a design modification to eliminate shrinkage porosity in a cast bracket."* The model answer: *Replace uniform thick section with a T-section having rib thickness = 0.7 × wall thickness, and add fillet radii at all junctions.*

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Design for Forging (DFF)

Forging plastically deforms metal between dies. Material flow is paramount.

### 2.2.1 Key Geometric Rules

| Parameter | Recommendation | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Draft Angle** | $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die forging) | Die separation; punch clearance |
| **Fillet Radius (External)** | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ where $h$ = depth | Material flow into corner |
| **Fillet Radius (Internal)** | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Reduces die stress, prevents crack |
| **Web Thickness** | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ where $W$ = width | Avoid folding/lapping |
| **Rib Thickness** | $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$ | Ensures full die fill |
| **Corner Radius Ratio** | $\dfrac{r_i}{h} \leq 0.15$ | Die life optimization |
| **Parting Line** | Should be a **straight plane** through widest cross-section | Simplifies die making |

### 2.2.2 Grain Flow Principle

In forging, **grain flow follows the contour** of the part. Design should ensure that:
- Fiber direction is **parallel to the principal stress** (tensile axis)
- Avoid abrupt changes in cross-section
- Avoid **sharp corners** that interrupt grain flow

$$
\text{Maximum allowable rib height} = 5 \times t_{rib}
$$

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Design for Welding (DFW)

Welding creates a **localized metallurgical bond** through fusion. Heat input, joint geometry, and accessibility govern weld quality.

### 2.3.1 Key Joint Design Rules

| Joint Type | Symbol | Strength Efficiency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Butt Joint (Square) | — | 70–80 % | Thin sheets (< 3 mm) |
| Butt Joint (V-groove) | V | 95–100 % | Plates > 3 mm |
| T-Joint (Fillet) | ⊥ | 60–70 % | Structural brackets |
| Lap Joint (Fillet) | ⫽ | 50–65 % | Sheet assemblies |
| Corner Joint | ⌐ | 70–80 % | Boxes, frames |

### 2.3.2 Fillet Weld Geometry

For a fillet weld of leg size $s$ and throat thickness $t$:

$$
t = 0.707 \, s
$$

> This is the **single most important formula** in welding design — KTU loves asking it.

### 2.3.3 Weld Strength Formula

For a fillet weld of length $L$, leg size $s$, in shear:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}
$$

where:
- $P$ = load capacity (N)
- $s$ = leg size (mm)
- $L$ = weld length (mm)
- $\tau_{allow}$ = allowable shear stress (MPa)

### 2.3.4 Critical Design Recommendations

- **Minimum weld leg size**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ where $t_{min}$ is the thinner plate
- **Maximum weld leg size**: For plates $\leq$ 6 mm, $s_{max} = t_{plate}$
- **Pitch of intermittent welds**: $p \leq 16 \, t$ for compression; $p \leq 24 \, t$ for tension (avoid)
- **Weld should not be located at high stress concentration zones**
- **Avoid weld intersection** — causes 4-direction stress

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Design for Sheet Metal Working (DFSM)

Sheet metal operations include **bending, drawing, piercing, blanking, and stretching**.

### 2.4.1 The K-Factor (Neutral Axis)

When a sheet is bent, the **outer surface stretches** and the **inner surface compresses**. The **neutral axis** lies somewhere between them, defined by the **K-factor**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

where $t_{neutral}$ is the distance from inner surface to neutral axis.

**Standard K-factor values:**

| Material | K-factor |
|---|---|
| Soft brass, aluminum | 0.33 |
| Mild steel | 0.40–0.45 |
| Stainless steel | 0.45–0.50 |
| Hard brass, bronze | 0.50 |

### 2.4.2 Bend Allowance (BA) — The Master Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

where:
- $\theta$ = bend angle (degrees)
- $R$ = inside bend radius
- $K$ = K-factor
- $t$ = sheet thickness

### 2.4.3 Minimum Bend Radius

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t \cdot E_{outer}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}} \quad \text{(theoretical, no safety factor)}
$$

**Practical rule** (used in industry and KTU exams):

$$
R_{min} \approx 1 \times t \quad \text{(for soft materials like aluminum)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 2 \times t \quad \text{(for mild steel)}
$$
$$
R_{min} \approx 3 \times t \quad \text{(for stainless steel, hard brass)}
$$

### 2.4.4 Hole-to-Edge and Hole-to-Hole Distances

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum distance from hole center to edge)}
$$
$$
d_{hole} \geq 3 \, t + R \quad \text{(minimum pitch between hole centers)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** If the edge distance is too small, the sheet **tears** during piercing. If holes are too close, the **inter-hole web distorts**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Process | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Casting | $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ | Adjacent thicknesses | mm |
| 2 | Casting | $r \geq \dfrac{t_1+t_2}{4}$ | Fillet radius | mm |
| 3 | Casting | $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ | Draft angle | degrees |
| 4 | Forging | $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$ | External fillet | mm |
| 5 | Forging | $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$ | Internal fillet | mm |
| 6 | Forging | $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$ | Web thickness | mm |
| 7 | Welding | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | Fillet throat | mm |
| 8 | Welding | $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$ | Weld load | N |
| 9 | Welding | $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$ | Min leg size | mm |
| 10 | Sheet Metal | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \theta (R + K t)$ | Bend allowance | mm |
| 11 | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = n \cdot t$ | Min bend radius | mm |
| 12 | Sheet Metal | $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ | Edge distance | mm |
| 13 | Sheet Metal | $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$ | Hole pitch | mm |
| 14 | Casting | $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ | Rib design | mm |
| 15 | Forging | $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$ | Rib height | mm |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These recommendations are not academic luxuries — they save **billions of dollars** annually:

- **Automotive industry**: An engine block with improper rib ratios can crack within weeks. A bracket with wrong fillet can fail in fatigue.
- **Aerospace**: Wing ribs in forged aluminum must follow $r \geq 0.1 \, h$ to maintain grain flow for fatigue life.
- **Pressure vessels**: Welding of LPG cylinders follows $s = 0.7 t$ to ensure full penetration without burn-through.
- **Consumer electronics**: Sheet metal laptop chassis use $K = 0.45$ for aluminum to predict bend behavior in stamping simulation.
- **Power transmission**: Cast transformer housings use section thickness ratio $\leq 2$ to prevent shrinkage porosity at junction of body and flange.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"How would you improve the manufacturability of this component?"*, the answer should ALWAYS quote at least one **numerical recommendation** with units, plus a one-line justification. Vague answers like "add fillets" score 1/7. "Add fillet radius ≥ (t1+t2)/4 to reduce stress concentration and shrinkage porosity" scores 4/7.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fillet Weld Throat Thickness

**Problem Statement:** A fillet weld of leg size **s = 10 mm** is deposited on a structural bracket. Derive the throat thickness and the load it can carry if the weld length is **L = 200 mm** and allowable shear stress is **$\tau_{allow}$ = 110 MPa**.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

A fillet weld has a triangular cross-section with two equal legs of length $s$ meeting at a right angle. The **throat** is the shortest distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (the weld face).

### Step 2: Apply Trigonometry

The throat $t$ is the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse. The hypotenuse length is:

$$
h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

For a right-angled isosceles triangle, the perpendicular from the right angle to the hypotenuse equals:

$$
t = \dfrac{s \cdot s}{h} = \dfrac{s^2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Rationalize the Denominator

$$
t = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, s
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Load Capacity

The weld is in shear along the throat plane of area $A = t \times L$:

$$
A = 7.07 \times 200 = 1414 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 110 \times 1414
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 155.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Step 6: Verification

Check using the master formula directly:

$$
P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow} = 0.707 \times 10 \times 200 \times 110
$$
$$
P = 0.707 \times 220{,}000 = 155{,}540 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** This derivation shows that the **effective load-bearing area** of a fillet weld is only **70.7 %** of the apparent cross-section. This is why fillet welds are weaker than butt welds of the same plate thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bend Allowance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is bent through an angle of **90°** with an inside bend radius of **R = 4 mm**. The K-factor for mild steel is **0.45**. Calculate the bend allowance.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Bend Allowance Formula

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (4 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Inside the Bracket

$$
R + K \cdot t = 4 + 0.45 \times 2 = 4 + 0.9 = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Angle Factor

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708
$$

### Step 6: Compute Final Result

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times 4.9 = 7.697 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{BA \approx 7.70 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation

If a designer wants a final **flat length of 100 mm** for the bent sheet (along the outer dimensions), the **flat blank length** required is:

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA
$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the straight portions before and after the bend.

**Example:** If each straight leg is 50 mm:

$$
L_{blank} = 50 + 50 + 7.70 = 107.70 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the **inside radius R** with the **neutral radius**. The neutral radius is $R + Kt$, NOT just $R$. Failing to add $Kt$ leads to an undersized blank and a part that is too short after bending.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Bend Radius for a Sheet

**Problem Statement:** A 1.5 mm thick aluminum sheet (UTS = 110 MPa) is to be bent at room temperature. The modulus of elasticity in tension on the outer fiber can be approximated by the strain-hardening modulus. Calculate the **minimum bend radius** to avoid surface cracking, using the rule of thumb $R_{min} \approx n \cdot t$ and verify with the theoretical formula.

### Step 1: Theoretical Strain Analysis

The maximum strain on the outer surface of a bent sheet is:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R + t} \approx \dfrac{t}{2R} \quad (\text{when } R \gg t)
$$

For aluminum, the **limiting strain** before cracking is approximately:

$$
\varepsilon_{limit} \approx 0.4 \quad (\text{for soft aluminum})
$$

### Step 2: Theoretical Formula

Setting $\varepsilon_{max} = \varepsilon_{limit}$:

$$
\dfrac{t}{2R_{min}} = \varepsilon_{limit}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{limit}} = \dfrac{1.5}{2 \times 0.4} = 1.875 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Rule of Thumb

For soft aluminum, $R_{min} \approx 1 \times t$:

$$
R_{min} = 1 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Safety Check

The practical rule gives a smaller radius (1.5 mm) than the theoretical (1.875 mm). For **safety**, we choose the larger value:

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = 1.875 \text{ mm} \approx 2 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 5: Design Decision

In practice, the designer would specify **R = 2 mm or higher** to ensure no surface cracking during bending. This is well within the "**R = t**" rule but provides a safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The relationship $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ is a favorite board question. The student is expected to (a) state the formula, (b) apply it with the limiting strain, and (c) interpret the result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Recommendation Mapping

| Defect Observed | Process | Design Recommendation That Prevents It |
|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage porosity at junction | Casting | Use **fillet radius** $r \geq (t_1+t_2)/4$; reduce section thickness ratio to $\leq 2$ |
| Hot tear at corner | Casting | Add **fillet and chill**; uniform cooling |
| Pattern damage on withdrawal | Casting | Provide **draft angle** $1°$ to $3°$ |
| Fold / lap in rib | Forging | Maintain $t_{web} \geq 0.15W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 t_{rib}$ |
| Die filling incomplete | Forging | Increase fillet radii; provide proper draft $5°$ to $7°$ |
| Cracking at weld root | Welding | Use **V-groove** for plates > 3 mm; full penetration |
| Distortion in welded plate | Welding | Use **symmetric joint**; intermittent welds; balanced sequence |
| Tearing at bend | Sheet Metal | $R_{min} = t$ to $3t$ depending on material |
| Springback error | Sheet Metal | Overbend by $1°$ to $3°$; use lower K-factor |
| Edge tearing during piercing | Sheet Metal | Maintain $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Comparison: With vs Without DFMA

Consider a cast iron bracket with wall thickness 20 mm meeting a flange of 30 mm.

| Parameter | Without DFMA | With DFMA |
|---|---|---|
| Section ratio $t_2/t_1$ | 30/20 = 1.5 (acceptable) | — |
| Fillet radius | 0 (sharp corner) | $r \geq (20+30)/4 = 12.5$ mm |
| Rib thickness | Full 20 mm (hot spot) | $0.7 \times 20 = 14$ mm |
| Draft angle | $0°$ (pattern stuck) | $2°$ external, $3°$ internal |
| Expected defect | Shrinkage cavity, pattern damage | Defect-free casting |
| Cost impact | Rejection + rework | First-time-right |

This matrix is the **standard structure** for KTU 14-mark questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Explain the importance of draft angle in sand casting. State typical draft values for external and internal surfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Draft angle is the **taper provided on the vertical walls of a pattern** to enable its easy withdrawal from the sand mold without damaging the mold cavity.

- **External surfaces**: $\alpha = 1°$ to $3°$ (pattern lifts easily)
- **Internal surfaces**: $\alpha = 2°$ to $5°$ (cores require more draft for removal)
- **Minimum draft for intricate patterns**: Up to $5°$

**Significance:** Without draft, the pattern rubs against sand walls, causing **mold erosion, dimensional inaccuracy, and surface defects**. Draft also accounts for **mold wall expansion** during pouring.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define K-factor. State the typical range of K-factor for mild steel and aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance of the **neutral axis** from the inner surface of the sheet to the **material thickness**:

$$
K = \dfrac{t_{neutral}}{t_{material}}
$$

- **Aluminum, soft brass**: $K = 0.33$
- **Mild steel**: $K = 0.40$ to $0.45$
- **Stainless steel, hard brass**: $K = 0.45$ to $0.50$

**Significance:** K-factor is essential for calculating **bend allowance** and predicting **flat blank length** before bending. It represents the location within the sheet thickness where neither stretching nor compression occurs during bending.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Casting + Forging Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A cast iron bracket has a uniform wall thickness of 25 mm. A boss of 40 mm diameter is to be added at one location for a bearing housing. As a design engineer, suggest modifications to:
1. Avoid shrinkage porosity at the boss-wall junction
2. Ensure easy pattern withdrawal
3. Maintain adequate strength

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Section thickness ratio check:**
The boss (40 mm) is essentially a thick cylinder attached to a 25 mm wall. Without modification, the ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6
$$

This is borderline. To make it safe, **reduce boss diameter** to 32–35 mm or add a **transition ring**.

**Step 2 — Add fillet radius:**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4} = \dfrac{25 + 40}{4} = 16.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r = 16$ mm at the boss-wall junction. This **reduces stress concentration** and **eliminates hot spot** at the junction, preventing shrinkage porosity.

**Step 3 — Add draft angle:**
For external boss surface: $\alpha = 2°$ (external)
For internal bore: $\alpha = 3°$ (internal)

**Step 4 — Boss thickness control:**
Boss wall thickness = $1.5 \times 25 = 37.5$ mm. Since boss OD is 40 mm, this means inner bore = 40 - 2(37.5) = negative. **Modify boss design**: Use a boss OD of 60 mm so that wall thickness = 10 mm (the inner bore of bearing fits) and $t_{boss} = 1.5 \times t_{wall} = 37.5$ mm is met by adjusting boss height/diameter.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Modification | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Fillet radius at junction | 16 mm | Reduce stress + hot spot |
| Section ratio | $\leq 2$ (achieved by reducing boss) | Avoid shrinkage |
| External draft | $2°$ | Pattern removal |
| Internal draft | $3°$ | Core removal |
| Rib at junction (if needed) | 17.5 mm thick | Strengthen without hot spot |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
Explain the key design recommendations for **drop forging** of a connecting rod-shaped component with maximum width $W = 60$ mm and depth $h = 25$ mm. Recommend suitable values for fillet radii, web thickness, and draft angle.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draft angle:**
For impression die forging: $\alpha = 5°$ to $7°$. Adopt $\alpha = 7°$ for safety.

**Step 2 — External fillet radius:**

$$
r_e \geq 0.1 \, h = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_e = 3$ mm.

**Step 3 — Internal fillet radius:**

$$
r_i \geq 0.15 \, h = 0.15 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $r_i = 4$ mm.

**Step 4 — Web thickness:**

$$
t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W = 0.15 \times 60 = 9 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{web} = 10$ mm.

**Step 5 — Rib thickness:**

$$
t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t_{rib} = 6$ mm.

**Step 6 — Maximum rib height:**

$$
h_{rib,max} = 5 \times t_{rib} = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Parting line:**
Choose a **straight plane** through the **widest cross-section** ($W = 60$ mm plane) for simple die construction.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | $7°$ | Die separation |
| External fillet | $3$ mm | Material flow into corner |
| Internal fillet | $4$ mm | Die stress relief |
| Web thickness | $10$ mm | Avoid folds |
| Rib thickness | $6$ mm | Full die fill |
| Max rib height | $30$ mm | Avoid fold/lap |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding + Sheet Metal Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
A fillet weld of leg size $s = 12$ mm is to be deposited along a joint of length $L = 250$ mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is $\tau_{allow} = 100$ MPa. Determine:
1. The throat thickness
2. The load-carrying capacity of the weld
3. The minimum recommended leg size if the thinner plate is 14 mm thick

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t = 0.707 \, s = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.484 \text{ mm} \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective shear area:**

$$
A = t \times L = 8.484 \times 250 = 2121 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Load capacity:**

$$
P = \tau_{allow} \times A = 100 \times 2121 = 212{,}100 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 212.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum recommended leg size:**

$$
s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min} = 0.7 \times 14 = 9.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the actual leg size $s = 12$ mm $> s_{min} = 9.8$ mm, the design is **acceptable**.

**Step 5 — Maximum recommended leg size check:**

For plate thickness 14 mm, the maximum leg size for full strength is $s_{max} \approx t_{plate} = 14$ mm. Since $s = 12$ mm $< 14$ mm, the weld is **within safe limits**.

**Step 6 — Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Throat thickness | 8.5 mm | ✓ |
| Weld area | 2121 mm² | ✓ |
| Load capacity | 212.1 kN | ✓ |
| Min leg required | 9.8 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |
| Max leg allowed | 14 mm | ✓ (12 mm used) |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
A 2 mm thick mild steel sheet is to be bent at $90°$ with an inside bend radius of $R = 5$ mm. Calculate:
1. The bend allowance (use $K = 0.45$)
2. The flat blank length if each straight leg is 60 mm
3. The minimum recommended edge distance for a hole pierced 4 mm from the bend

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
\theta = 90°, \quad R = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad K = 0.45, \quad t = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Bend Allowance:**

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot t)
$$

$$
BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90 \times (5 + 0.45 \times 2)
$$

$$
BA = 1.5708 \times (5 + 0.9) = 1.5708 \times 5.9
$$

$$
\boxed{BA = 9.268 \text{ mm} \approx 9.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Flat blank length:**

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + BA = 60 + 60 + 9.27
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{blank} = 129.27 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum edge distance for hole:**

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \, t + R = 1.5 \times 2 + 5 = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d_{edge} = 8$ mm from bend line to hole center.

**Step 5 — Verification of bend radius:**

For mild steel, $R_{min} \approx 2t = 2 \times 2 = 4$ mm.
Since $R = 5$ mm $> R_{min} = 4$ mm, the bend is **safe**.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Bend allowance | 9.27 mm | — |
| Blank length | 129.27 mm | — |
| Min edge distance | 8 mm | ✓ |
| Min bend radius | 4 mm (reqd) | ✓ (5 mm used) |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Design Recommendations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-factor and Neutral Axis position.** K-factor is dimensionless; neutral axis position = $K \times t$ (in mm). Students writing $K = 0.45$ mm lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.707 in fillet weld throat.** Writing $t = s$ instead of $t = 0.707s$ loses 2 marks. Always show the derivation.
>
> 3. **Using inside radius $R$ instead of neutral radius $(R + Kt)$ in bend allowance.** This is the #1 error in sheet metal problems. Always write $(R + Kt)$ explicitly.
>
> 4. **Not specifying units in design recommendations.** "Add fillet radius of 5" is incomplete. "Add fillet radius of **5 mm** to reduce stress concentration" scores full marks.
>
> 5. **Giving vague justifications.** "It will be good for manufacturing" scores 0. "It eliminates hot spot and prevents shrinkage porosity" scores 2/2.
>
> 6. **Mixing up draft angles for casting vs forging.** Casting: $1°$ to $5°$. Forging: $5°$ to $7°$. Many students write $1°$ for forging, which is wrong.
>
> 7. **Ignoring minimum weld leg size formula.** $s_{min} = 0.7 t_{min}$ is mandatory to mention whenever weld design is asked.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Design Recommendations"**

- [x] **Casting — Section Ratio**: $\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} \leq 2$ (always enforce uniform cooling)
- [x] **Casting — Fillet**: $r \geq \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{4}$ (no sharp internal corners)
- [x] **Casting — Draft**: $1°$ to $3°$ external, $2°$ to $5°$ internal
- [x] **Casting — Rib**: $t_{rib} = 0.7 \, t_{wall}$ (avoid hot spots)
- [x] **Casting — Boss**: $t_{boss} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ (strength + machining allowance)
- [x] **Forging — Draft**: $5°$ to $7°$ (impression die)
- [x] **Forging — External Fillet**: $r_e \geq 0.1 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Internal Fillet**: $r_i \geq 0.15 \, h$
- [x] **Forging — Web**: $t_{web} \geq 0.15 \, W$
- [x] **Forging — Rib**: $t_{rib} \geq 0.10 \, W$; $h_{rib} \leq 5 \, t_{rib}$
- [x] **Forging — Parting Line**: straight, through widest cross-section
- [x] **Welding — Throat**: $t = 0.707 \, s$ (single most important formula)
- [x] **Welding — Load**: $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{allow}$
- [x] **Welding — Min Leg**: $s_{min} = 0.7 \, t_{min}$
- [x] **Welding — Avoid**: weld intersections and high-stress zones
- [x] **Sheet Metal — K-Factor**: Al = 0.33, MS = 0.45, SS = 0.50
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Bend Allowance**: $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \, \theta \, (R + Kt)$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Min Bend Radius**: $R_{min} = 1t$ to $3t$ (material dependent)
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Edge Distance**: $d_{edge} \geq 1.5t + R$
- [x] **Sheet Metal — Hole Pitch**: $d_{hole} \geq 3t + R$

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize

| Value | Meaning | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0.707** | Fillet weld throat factor | $t = 0.707s$ |
| **0.7** | Weld min leg factor | $s_{min} = 0.7t$ |
| **0.1** | Forging external fillet factor | $r_e = 0.1h$ |
| **0.15** | Forging internal fillet / web factor | $r_i = 0.15h$, $t_{web} = 0.15W$ |
| **2** | Max section ratio (casting) | $t_2/t_1 \leq 2$ |
| **¼** | Casting fillet factor | $r = (t_1+t_2)/4$ |
| **0.7** | Casting rib factor | $t_{rib} = 0.7t_{wall}$ |
| **1.5** | Casting boss factor | $t_{boss} = 1.5t_{wall}$ |
| **5** | Forging max rib height factor | $h_{rib} \leq 5t_{rib}$ |

### One-Line Definition Recall (for 2-mark definitions)

- **DFC** = Design for Casting — guidelines for uniform walls, fillets, draft, ribs
- **DFF** = Design for Forging — guidelines for material flow, die fill, draft, radii
- **DFW** = Design for Welding — guidelines for joint type, weld size, distortion control
- **DFSM** = Design for Sheet Metal — guidelines for bending, K-factor, blank size
